topics.title: Inventory Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inventory Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **INVENTORY ANALYSIS** is the systematic engineering procedure of identifying, classifying, and quantifying all hazardous chemicals (raw materials, intermediates, products, by-products, catalysts, and utilities) present within the boundary limits of a process plant or storage facility, including their **physical state, quantity, location, temperature, pressure, and storage conditions**, in order to determine the maximum credible release mass available for dispersion, fire, or explosion consequence modeling.

In the framework of the KTU 2024 Scheme PBSFT404 syllabus, inventory analysis forms the **first quantitative step** of consequence analysis. It precedes the calculation of source models (i.e., release rate, flash fraction, pool evaporation) and ultimately feeds dispersion (Module 2), fire, and explosion modeling. The phrase used in the syllabus is *"estimation of inventories"* and is grouped with the screening of process units, identification of hazardous materials, and estimation of failure frequencies.

The relevant regulatory references used to define what counts as a "hazardous inventory" are:
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** (Process Safety Management – PSM)
- **EPA 40 CFR Part 68** (Risk Management Plan – RMP)
- **CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis (CPQRA)**
- **AIChE/CCPS Dow's Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI)** – chemical exposure inventory limits

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Inventory analysis is treated as a *pre-requisite numerical exercise* for any QRA, HAZOP consequence review, or LOPA scenario. The examiner expects you to demonstrate the ability to convert operating/storage conditions into release-relevant units (kg, m³, kmol).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a chemical plant as a *medicine cabinet* in a hospital. Before a doctor (the risk analyst) can prescribe treatment (mitigation), the nurse (the inventory analyst) must first:

1. **List every bottle** (raw materials, intermediates, finished products).
2. **Read every label** – name, quantity, concentration, hazard pictogram.
3. **Note the storage location** – refrigerator (cryogenic tank), shelf (atmospheric drum), locked cabinet (pressurized cylinder).
4. **Identify the most dangerous items** – the *worst-case* bottles (large volume of highly toxic / flammable / reactive chemicals).
5. **Estimate the worst spillage** – "If I knocked this bottle over, how much can come out, and in what form (liquid splash, vapor cloud, two-phase spray)?"

**Inventory Analysis = the "label-reading and counting" stage of process safety.** You cannot model a release if you do not first know *what*, *how much*, *where*, and *under what conditions* the chemical is held. The output of inventory analysis is essentially a structured **Chemical Inventory Register (CIR)** plus the maximum *credible release mass* (`m_release`) that feeds all downstream models.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics, Constants & Classification

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Universal gas constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Atmospheric pressure | P_atm | **101 325 Pa** |
| Standard temperature | T_std | **298.15 K** (25 °C) |
| Normal boiling point | T_b | substance-specific (K) |
| Critical temperature | T_c | substance-specific (K) |
| Critical pressure | P_c | substance-specific (Pa) |
| Liquid density | ρ_L | kg/m³ |
| Vapor density | ρ_V | kg/m³ |
| Molecular weight | MW | kg/kmol |
| Vapor pressure at T | P_vap(T) | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three physical states that govern inventory analysis (CCPS / Crowl–Louvar):**
> 1. **Compressed Liquids** – stored below T_b, pressure > P_vap(T) → two-phase release on loss of containment.
> 2. **Refrigerated Liquids** – stored at T < T_b, pressure ≈ P_atm → one-phase liquid release that pools and evaporates.
> 3. **Gases under pressure** – stored as gas, supercritical or subcritical → vapor or two-phase release.
> 4. **Atmospheric liquids** – stored at T_b, P_atm → one-phase liquid pool formation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Inventory Analysis Workflow (Structured Logic)

Inventory analysis, when executed as a KTU-style problem, follows a fixed five-step methodology:

- **Step 1 — Material Identification (What is there?)**
  Enumerate all process streams using the **P&ID + PFD + Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS/SDS)** register. Group them by:
  - **Raw materials** entering the battery limit.
  - **Intermediates** in-process (in reactors, columns, drums, day tanks).
  - **Final products** in storage bullets, spheres, or drums.
  - **By-products and waste streams** (often neglected — common error).
  - **Utilities** (LPG for burners, diesel for generators, ammonia for refrigeration, hydrogen for hydrotreaters).

- **Step 2 — State & Condition Classification (Under what conditions?)**
  For each material record: phase (solid/liquid/gas), temperature, pressure, total quantity (mass *or* volume), location, and containment type (vessel, pipe, drum, cylinder, bullet).

- **Step 3 — Quantity Calculation (How much?)**
  Convert to a single engineering unit (kg or m³) using:
  - **Liquids** → mass = ρ_L × V
  - **Gases (ideal)** → m = (P · MW · V) / (R · T)
  - **Liquified gases under vapor pressure** → treat liquid mass as the inventory (vapor mass in the ullage is small and usually ignored in KTU problems).

- **Step 4 — Release Mode Assignment (How can it escape?)**
  Map each inventory to one of the **standard release categories** of the CCPS *Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis*:
  - **Catastrophic vessel rupture** → entire inventory released.
  - **Large-bore pipe rupture (full bore)** → contents of the affected line section released over the inventory discharge time.
  - **Small leak** → continuous release at hole-flow rate until isolation.
  - **Two-phase discharge** → flashing liquid + entrained vapor.

- **Step 5 — Worst-Case & Alternate-Case Scenario Definition**
  Following **EPA RMP / CCPS** convention, define:
  - **Worst-Case Release Scenario (WCRS):** total inventory released in 10 minutes under worst weather (F stability, 1.5 m/s wind) – used for emergency planning.
  - **Alternate-Case Release Scenario (ACRS):** a more realistic, probability-weighted release – used for facility layout and risk reduction.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these five equations — they cover 90 % of KTU 2024 inventory analysis problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Key | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Liquid mass from volume | $m = \rho_L \cdot V$ | $\rho_L$ = liquid density, V = vessel volume | kg, kg/m³, m³ |
| 2 | Liquid volume from mass | $V = m / \rho_L$ | same as above | m³ |
| 3 | Ideal gas mass from P, V, T | $m = \dfrac{P \cdot MW \cdot V}{R \cdot T}$ | P (Pa), V (m³), T (K), MW (kg/kmol) | kg |
| 4 | Vapor mass from liquid leak | $m_v = \rho_V \cdot Q_{leak} \cdot t$ | $\rho_V$ = vapor density, Q = volumetric flow, t = release time | kg |
| 5 | Flashing liquid vapor fraction | $f_v = \dfrac{c_p (T_s - T_b)}{H_v}$ | $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K), $H_v$ = latent heat of vaporization (J/kg) | dimensionless |

| # | Storage / Release Concept | Governing Equation | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6 | Liquid pool depth / spreading | $V_{pool} = \dfrac{m_{release}}{\rho_L}$ | Pool evaporation source term |
| 7 | Two-phase mass discharge | $\dot{m}_{2\phi} = C_d A \sqrt{2 \rho_m \Delta P}$ | $\rho_m$ = mixture density, $C_d$ = 0.6–0.85 |
| 8 | Total two-phase release mass | $m_{total} = m_{liq} + m_{vap} = m_{liq} (1 + f_v)$ | Worst-case release into atmosphere |
| 9 | Vapor pressure (Antoine form) | $\log_{10} P_{vap} = A - \dfrac{B}{C+T}$ | T in °C, P_vap in mmHg |
| 10 | Vessel gas ullage mass | $m_{ullage} = \dfrac{P \cdot MW \cdot V_{ullage}}{R \cdot T}$ | Compressed-gas tank residual vapor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule-of-Thumb (CCPS):** A liquid at its atmospheric boiling point has $\rho_V \approx \dfrac{MW}{22.4} \text{ kg/m}^3$ at STP. The vapor density is roughly **1 000 – 1 500 times smaller** than its liquid density at the same T. This is why KTU problems focus on *liquid mass* for atmospheric storage and *two-phase discharge* for pressurized storage.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inventory analysis is the **spinal column** of the following safety deliverables:

1. **Consequence Analysis** — the release mass `m` drives the dispersion (Pasquill–Gifford, DEGADIS, ALOHA), fire (pool fire, jet fire, BLEVE), and explosion (TNT-equivalent, TNO Multi-Energy) calculations.
2. **Facility Siting (API RP 752 / 753)** — buildings are placed such that occupants are safe against the worst credible release from each inventory.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — EPA RMP requires submission of regulated substance inventories; OSHA PSM triggers at threshold quantities (TQ).
4. **Insurance & Loss Prevention** — insurers (e.g., Marsh, Allianz) require inventory-based Maximum Foreseeable Loss (MFL) and Probable Maximum Loss (PML) estimates.
5. **Emergency Response Planning (ERP)** — determines foam stocks, water curtains, evacuation radii, and shelter-in-place zones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of an Ideal Gas in a Pressurized Vessel

**Given:** A spherical ammonia storage sphere of geometric volume V = 200 m³ is filled with **liquid ammonia** at a storage temperature of 30 °C. The vapor space (ullage) of the sphere is 8 % of the geometric volume and contains saturated ammonia vapor.

**Required:** Calculate the mass of ammonia vapor (kg) present in the ullage space.

**Data Table (Satyendra Singh / Perry's):**
- Storage temperature, T = 30 °C = 303.15 K
- Vapor pressure of ammonia at 30 °C, P_vap = 1 167 kPa (absolute)
- Molecular weight of NH₃, MW = 17.03 kg/kmol
- Universal gas constant, R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K) = 8.314 × 10³ J/(kmol·K)

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the ullage volume.**
The ullage is the vapor-space volume that exists above the liquid level inside the sphere.

$$
V_{ullage} = 0.08 \times V_{geometric} = 0.08 \times 200 \; m^3
$$

$$
V_{ullage} = 16.0 \; m^3
$$

**Step 2 — Choose the governing equation.**
Because the ullage is a *low-to-moderate-pressure vapor space*, the ideal gas law provides an engineering-acceptable first approximation. The mass of an ideal gas is given by

$$
m_v = \dfrac{P \cdot MW \cdot V}{R \cdot T}
$$

This is the *standard form* of the ideal gas law in which P, V, T and the molecular weight are combined to yield a mass.

**Step 3 — Convert units to SI consistently.**
Rearranging the equation in SI units, with P in Pa, V in m³, T in K, and MW in kg/kmol, we get

$$
P = 1\,167 \; kPa = 1\,167\,000 \; Pa
$$

$$
T = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \; K
$$

$$
MW = 17.03 \; kg/kmol
$$

$$
R = 8\,314 \; J/(kmol \cdot K) = 8\,314 \; Pa \cdot m^3 / (kmol \cdot K)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
m_v = \dfrac{1\,167\,000 \; Pa \times 17.03 \; kg/kmol \times 16.0 \; m^3}{8\,314 \; Pa \cdot m^3/(kmol \cdot K) \times 303.15 \; K}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 1\,167\,000 \times 17.03 \times 16.0
$$

$$
= 1\,167\,000 \times 272.48 = 317\,983\,960 \; Pa \cdot m^3 \cdot kg / kmol
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 8\,314 \times 303.15
$$

$$
= 2\,520\,429.9 \; Pa \cdot m^3 / kmol
$$

**Step 7 — Final division.**

$$
m_v = \dfrac{317\,983\,960}{2\,520\,429.9}
$$

$$
m_v = 126.16 \; kg
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Approximately **126 kg of ammonia vapor** is present in the sphere's ullage. This is *negligible* compared with the liquid ammonia mass — the reason CCPS guidance says "for saturated-liquid storage, treat the inventory as 100 % liquid." This conclusion is the foundation for the simplification used in **all** KTU problems on saturated-liquid bullets.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Mass Inventory of a Pressurized Liquid (Liquid + Vapor)

**Given:** The same ammonia sphere, filled to 92 % (by volume) with liquid ammonia at 30 °C.

**Data:** Liquid ammonia density at 30 °C, ρ_L = 595 kg/m³.

**Required:** Calculate the total inventory of ammonia (liquid + vapor) in the sphere in kg.

**Step 1 — Compute the liquid volume.**

$$
V_{liq} = 0.92 \times V = 0.92 \times 200 = 184.0 \; m^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the liquid mass.**

$$
m_{liq} = \rho_L \cdot V_{liq} = 595 \; kg/m^3 \times 184.0 \; m^3
$$

$$
m_{liq} = 109\,480 \; kg \approx 109.48 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the vapor mass from the previous derivation (126 kg).**

$$
m_{total} = m_{liq} + m_v = 109\,480 + 126.16
$$

$$
m_{total} = 109\,606.16 \; kg \approx 109.6 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**
The vapor-phase inventory is **0.12 %** of the total — i.e., 845 times smaller. For KTU calculations and any EPA-RMP submission, the liquid mass alone is sufficient.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Flash-Fraction for a Compressed Liquid Release

**Given:** A chlorine bullet (T_stored = 25 °C, P_stored = 8.6 bar gauge) ruptures catastrophically. The released chlorine flashes as the pressure drops to atmospheric.

**Data (Perry's / Chlorine Institute):**
- Storage temperature, T_s = 25 °C = 298.15 K
- Normal boiling point of Cl₂, T_b = -34.04 °C = 239.11 K
- Specific heat of liquid Cl₂, c_p ≈ 0.95 kJ/(kg·K) = 950 J/(kg·K)
- Latent heat of vaporization at T_b, H_v ≈ 287.4 kJ/kg = 287 400 J/kg

**Required:** Compute the vapor fraction $f_v$ immediately after release.

**Step 1 — Write the flash-fraction equation (CCPS / Crowl–Louvar).**

$$
f_v = \dfrac{c_p \, (T_s - T_b)}{H_v}
$$

This is derived from an **energy balance** on the decompressing liquid: the sensible heat available between T_s and T_b is converted into latent heat at atmospheric pressure, vaporising a fraction $f_v$ of the liquid.

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature difference.**

$$
\Delta T = T_s - T_b = 25 - (-34.04) = 59.04 \; K
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the equation.**

$$
f_v = \dfrac{950 \times 59.04}{287\,400}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$
950 \times 59.04 = 56\,088 \; J/kg
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the final fraction.**

$$
f_v = \dfrac{56\,088}{287\,400} = 0.1951 \approx 0.195
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret.**
Approximately **19.5 % of the released chlorine will flash to vapor** upon catastrophic rupture. The remaining 80.5 % forms a *cold, dense, two-phase aerosol* that is the source term for the dense-gas dispersion model in Module 2.

**Step 7 — Total two-phase release mass.**
If the bullet contains 50 tonnes of liquid chlorine, the released mass is

$$
m_{total} = m_{liq}(1 + f_v) = 50\,000 \times 1.195 = 59\,750 \; kg
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $f_v$ matters in KTU problems:** It is the *bridge* between a "liquid" inventory and a "two-phase" source term. Mistakenly using only the liquid mass (50 t) under-predicts the actual airborne cloud by ~20 %, which in turn under-predicts the toxic-load footprint. The CCPS / Crowl–Louvar textbook derivation is the standard answer KTU examiners expect.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Tool for Inventory Type

Use this matrix to decide which equation from §2.2 to apply in an exam problem:

| Storage condition | Phase inside vessel | Release phase after LOC | Governing equation | Key data needed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric, T ≥ T_b | Liquid | Liquid (pool) | m = ρ_L · V | ρ_L |
| Refrigerated, T < T_b, P_atm | Liquid (subcooled) | Liquid → evaporates | m = ρ_L · V; pool vapor flux from Module 2 | ρ_L, vapor pressure |
| Pressurized gas, T > T_c | Supercritical | Vapor | m = P·MW·V/(R·T) | P, T, MW |
| Pressurized gas, T < T_c | Compressed gas | Vapor (with possible condensation) | m = P·MW·V/(R·T) | P, T, MW |
| Saturated liquid, T < T_c, P = P_vap | Liquid + vapor ullage | Two-phase (flashing) | m_total = m_liq(1 + f_v) | c_p, T_s, T_b, H_v |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Inventory Analysis Workflow

This Mermaid block shows the *Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow* of the inventory analysis procedure as a KTU-process model. Each block is a stage; arrows show the data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Process Plant Battery Limit] --> B0[Step 1: Material Identification]
    B0 --> C0[Step 2: State and Condition Classification]
    C0 --> D0[Step 3: Quantity Calculation]
    D0 --> E0[Step 4: Release Mode Assignment]
    E0 --> F0[Step 5: Worst Case and Alternate Case]
    F0 --> G0[Chemical Inventory Register CIR]
    F0 --> H0[Source Term for Consequence Models]
    G0 --> I0[Module 2 Dispersion Modeling]
    H0 --> I0
    H0 --> J0[Module 3 Fire and Explosion Modeling]
    I0 --> K0[Risk Characterization and Mitigation]
    J0 --> K0
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The *Process Plant* (A0) is the source of all data.
- Steps 1–5 are the inventory analysis workflow.
- Two deliverables emerge: the **CIR (G0)** for regulatory filing, and the **Source Term (H0)** for consequence modeling.
- H0 and G0 feed the dispersion, fire, and explosion models of subsequent modules.
- Everything converges on **Risk Characterization & Mitigation (K0)** — the ultimate goal of PBSFT404.

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Type of Inventory

This Mermaid diagram is a decision tree that guides the analyst (and the student) in classifying each chemical in the plant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Chemical in Process] --> Q0{Is it a solid?}
    Q0 -- Yes --> R0[Skip: low volatility treat as fixed inventory]
    Q0 -- No --> S0{Stored at atmospheric pressure?}
    S0 -- Yes --> T0{T greater than Tb?}
    T0 -- Yes --> U0[Atmospheric Liquid: one phase pool]
    T0 -- No --> V0[Refrigerated Liquid: one phase pool plus evaporation]
    S0 -- No --> W0{Stored temperature T less than Tc?}
    W0 -- No --> X0[Supercritical Gas: vapor release]
    W0 -- Yes --> Y0{T less than Tb?}
    Y0 -- Yes --> Z0[Compressed Liquefied Gas: two phase flashing release]
    Y0 -- No --> A1[Compressed Gas below Tc: vapor release with possible condensation]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Every chemical in a plant falls into one of these five leaves.
- Each leaf corresponds to a *release mode*, which is the input to a specific source-term model.

## 4.3 Schematic — Two-Phase Release After Catastrophic Failure

A *Block-Level Functional Architecture* representation of the physical release event of a compressed liquefied gas such as chlorine.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SrcA[Bulleted Vessel 50 t liquid Cl2 T 25 C P 8.6 bar] --> EvtA[Catastrophic Rupture Event]
    EvtA --> SrcB[Liquid Mass mL 50 000 kg]
    EvtA --> SrcC[Flashed Vapor Mass mV fv times mL]
    SrcB --> Mix1[Two Phase Aerosol Pool and Cloud]
    SrcC --> Mix1
    Mix1 --> Out1[Dispersion Module 2]
    Mix1 --> Out2[Fire and Explosion Module 3]
    Mix1 --> Out3[EPA RMP Worst Case Report]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The single vessel event produces both a *liquid* stream and a *vapor* stream (via flash).
- They recombine as a **two-phase aerosol pool + cloud**, which is the canonical source term used by ALOHA, PHAST, and CANARY.
- The same source term feeds three downstream deliverables: dispersion, fire/explosion, and regulatory reporting.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks)  *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]*

> **Q:** Define *inventory analysis* in the context of process safety. List the *five* standard steps used to perform inventory analysis on a process plant.

**Model Answer:**

Inventory analysis is the systematic procedure of identifying, classifying, and quantifying all hazardous chemicals present within the battery limits of a process plant, including their physical state, quantity, location, temperature, pressure, and storage conditions, so as to determine the maximum credible release mass for consequence modeling.

The five standard steps are:

1. **Material Identification** — list all raw materials, intermediates, products, by-products, and utilities using PFD, P&ID, and SDS.
2. **State and Condition Classification** — record phase, T, P, quantity, and location for each material.
3. **Quantity Calculation** — convert all data to engineering units (kg, m³) using ρ_L, ideal gas law, or Antoine vapor pressure.
4. **Release Mode Assignment** — map each inventory to a CCPS release category (catastrophic rupture, large-bore rupture, small leak, two-phase discharge).
5. **Worst-Case and Alternate-Case Scenario Definition** — per EPA RMP convention, define the WCRS and ACRS for emergency planning and risk reduction.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the five steps.)*

### Question 2 (3 marks)  *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]*

> **Q:** A spherical propane bullet has a geometric volume of 100 m³, is filled 85 % with liquid propane, and operates at 25 °C. Given ρ_L (propane) = 493 kg/m³ and MW = 44.1 kg/kmol, calculate the mass of liquid propane in the bullet.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Liquid volume.**

$$
V_{liq} = 0.85 \times 100 = 85 \; m^3
$$

**Step 2 — Liquid mass.**

$$
m_{liq} = \rho_L \cdot V_{liq} = 493 \times 85
$$

$$
m_{liq} = 41\,905 \; kg \approx 41.9 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

*(3 marks: 1 for setup, 1 for V_liq, 1 for m_liq.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 1 Choice Instruction (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Answer either Question A or Question B. Each carries 14 marks and has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)  *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]*

> **Q (a) [7 marks]:** A 50 m³ horizontal cylindrical tank, lying on its side, is filled to 90 % of its volume with acetone at 25 °C and atmospheric pressure. The tank diameter is 3.0 m. Given the density of acetone at 25 °C is 784 kg/m³, calculate:
>  (i) the volume of liquid acetone in the tank,
>  (ii) the mass of liquid acetone in the tank, and
>  (iii) the depth of the vapour space (ullage) above the liquid.
>
> **Q (b) [7 marks]:** Discuss briefly the four storage-condition classes used in inventory analysis (atmospheric, refrigerated, pressurized gas, compressed liquefied gas). For each, state the **dominant release mode** following loss of containment.

#### Model Solution to Q (a)

**(i) Volume of liquid acetone:**

$$
V_{liq} = 0.90 \times V_{tank} = 0.90 \times 50 = 45 \; m^3
$$

**(ii) Mass of liquid acetone:**

$$
m_{liq} = \rho_L \times V_{liq} = 784 \; kg/m^3 \times 45 \; m^3 = 35\,280 \; kg \approx 35.3 \; t
$$

**(iii) Depth of the vapour space:**
The tank is a horizontal cylinder of diameter D = 3.0 m, so the radius R = 1.5 m. The cross-sectional area of the tank is

$$
A_{tank} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (3.0)^2}{4} = 7.0686 \; m^2
$$

The total cross-sectional area of the tank (one circular end) is 7.07 m². The tank length L is

$$
L = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{A_{tank}} = \dfrac{50}{7.0686} = 7.074 \; m
$$

The liquid depth h corresponds to a circular segment of area A_liq = V_liq / L:

$$
A_{liq} = \dfrac{V_{liq}}{L} = \dfrac{45}{7.074} = 6.361 \; m^2
$$

Using the standard formula for the area of a circular segment of radius R, depth h:

$$
A_{liq} = R^2 \cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{R - h}{R}\right) - (R - h)\sqrt{2Rh - h^2}
$$

Substituting R = 1.5 m, A_liq = 6.361 m², and solving iteratively:

$$
h \approx 1.36 \; m
$$

Vapour depth (ullage) is therefore

$$
h_{vap} = D - h = 3.0 - 1.36 = 1.64 \; m
$$

#### Model Solution to Q (b)

The four storage-condition classes are:

1. **Atmospheric liquids** — T ≈ T_b, P = P_atm. *Dominant release mode:* one-phase liquid pool that spreads and evaporates; vapor source term is a low-flux pool.
2. **Refrigerated liquids** — T < T_b, P ≈ P_atm. *Dominant release mode:* subcooled liquid spills as a one-phase pool; vapor flux controlled by heat-transfer from the ground and air, producing a slow but persistent cloud.
3. **Pressurized gases (T > T_c, supercritical)** — *Dominant release mode:* instantaneous vapor expansion at sonic velocity; jet/plume dispersion; no liquid phase.
4. **Compressed liquefied gases (T < T_c, P > P_atm)** — *Dominant release mode:* two-phase discharge. A fraction f_v of the released liquid flashes to vapor, the remainder forms a cold aerosol pool. This is the most dangerous category and the most heavily examined in KTU.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)  *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]*

> **Q (a) [7 marks]:** A bullet of liquid chlorine (MW = 70.9 kg/kmol) has a geometric volume of 80 m³ and is filled to 92 % by volume with liquid chlorine at 30 °C. The vapor pressure of chlorine at 30 °C is 1.16 MPa. Calculate the **total mass inventory** (liquid + vapor) in kg, taking ρ_L (Cl₂) = 1.41 × 10³ kg/m³.
>
> **Q (b) [7 marks]:** If this bullet suffers a catastrophic rupture, calculate the **flashing fraction f_v** and the **total two-phase release mass**, given: c_p = 0.95 kJ/(kg·K), T_s = 30 °C, T_b = -34.04 °C, and H_v = 287.4 kJ/kg.

#### Model Solution to Q (a)

**Step 1 — Liquid volume and mass.**

$$
V_{liq} = 0.92 \times 80 = 73.6 \; m^3
$$

$$
m_{liq} = \rho_L \cdot V_{liq} = 1410 \times 73.6 = 103\,776 \; kg \approx 103.8 \; t
$$

**Step 2 — Vapor ullage volume.**

$$
V_{ullage} = 0.08 \times 80 = 6.4 \; m^3
$$

**Step 3 — Vapor mass via ideal gas law.**

$$
m_v = \dfrac{P \cdot MW \cdot V}{R \cdot T}
$$

Convert units: P = 1.16 × 10⁶ Pa, T = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 K, R = 8 314 J/(kmol·K), MW = 70.9 kg/kmol.

$$
m_v = \dfrac{1.16 \times 10^6 \times 70.9 \times 6.4}{8\,314 \times 303.15}
$$

Numerator = 1.16 × 10⁶ × 70.9 × 6.4 = 5.265 × 10⁸
Denominator = 8 314 × 303.15 = 2.520 × 10⁶

$$
m_v = \dfrac{5.265 \times 10^8}{2.520 \times 10^6} = 208.93 \; kg
$$

**Step 4 — Total inventory.**

$$
m_{total} = m_{liq} + m_v = 103\,776 + 208.93 = 103\,985 \; kg \approx 104.0 \; t
$$

#### Model Solution to Q (b)

**Step 1 — Flash fraction.**

$$
\Delta T = T_s - T_b = 30 - (-34.04) = 64.04 \; K
$$

$$
f_v = \dfrac{c_p \Delta T}{H_v} = \dfrac{950 \times 64.04}{287\,400}
$$

$$
f_v = \dfrac{60\,838}{287\,400} = 0.2117 \approx 0.212
$$

**Step 2 — Total two-phase release mass.**

$$
m_{total,\,release} = m_{liq} (1 + f_v) = 103\,776 \times (1 + 0.212)
$$

$$
m_{total,\,release} = 103\,776 \times 1.212 = 125\,776 \; kg \approx 125.8 \; t
$$

**Step 3 — Vapor-only portion (for dispersion calculation in Module 2).**

$$
m_{vap,\,release} = f_v \cdot m_{liq} = 0.212 \times 103\,776 = 22\,000 \; kg
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ullage** — many students take inventory as 92 % × V × ρ_L and stop, losing ~1 mark for the vapor mass calculation.
> 2. **Unit confusion in the ideal gas law** — P must be in Pa (not kPa or bar) and R must be 8 314 J/(kmol·K). A common error is using R = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K) and forgetting to convert V to litres and P to atm.
> 3. **Flash fraction sign error** — T_s − T_b is *always* positive because chlorine is stored *above* its normal boiling point. A negative flash fraction indicates an error in the data.
> 4. **Mixing the two-phase release equation with the simple liquid release** — students sometimes write m_release = m_liq × f_v. The correct form is m_total = m_liq (1 + f_v), where the factor f_v accounts for the *additional* flashed vapor on top of the original liquid.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inventory analysis = count + classify + quantify + assign release mode + define worst/alternate case.** It is the *enabling step* for every consequence model in PBSFT404.
- **Always start with a P&ID/PFD walk-down** — you cannot quantify what you have not identified.
- **Convert everything to SI units** (Pa, m³, K, kg, kmol) *before* substituting into any formula; mismatched units are the most common cause of wrong answers in KTU exams.
- **The five master equations** are: (i) m = ρ_L·V (liquid), (ii) m = P·MW·V/(R·T) (ideal gas/vapor), (iii) f_v = c_p (T_s − T_b)/H_v (flash), (iv) m_total = m_liq (1 + f_v) (two-phase), (v) ΔT for flashing.
- **For atmospheric liquids** — use the *liquid mass only*; vapor in ullage is negligible (< 0.5 %).
- **For compressed liquefied gases** — the *two-phase release mass* is the correct source term; do not omit the flashed vapor fraction.
- **For supercritical gases** — treat the entire contents as a vapor at the storage T and P, then release as a gas jet.
- **EPA RMP convention:** *Worst-Case* = entire inventory in 10 min, F stability, 1.5 m/s wind. *Alternate-Case* = a more realistic hole size and duration.
- **CCPS standard release categories:** catastrophic rupture, full-bore pipe rupture, large leak, small leak, two-phase discharge.
- **Threshold Quantities (TQ) trigger PSM/RMP** for substances like chlorine (1 500 lb ≈ 680 kg), propane (10 000 lb ≈ 4 535 kg), and ammonia (10 000 lb ≈ 4 535 kg).
- **Numerical magnitude check:** liquid chlorine inventory in a 100 m³ bullet is of the order 10⁵ kg. If your answer is 10² kg or 10⁸ kg, you almost certainly have a unit or formula error.
- **Always interpret the result** in terms of downstream consequence (toxic load, fire radiation, overpressure) — that is what carries the *application* marks in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating of Process Plants - The Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating of Process Plants - The Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fire and Explosion Hazard Index — Formal Definition

A **Fire and Explosion (F&E) Hazard Index** is a numerical, relative ranking tool used in process safety engineering to objectively quantify the anticipated fire, explosion, and reactivity damage potential of a process unit or plant section. It converts subjective hazard data into a single, dimensionless number that allows safety engineers, designers, insurers, and regulators to prioritize resources, design adequate protection layers, and rank different processes on a common scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardized)**
> The Fire and Explosion Index is an "objective, numerical measure of the realistic maximum loss potential of a process plant section arising from fire, explosion, and chemical reactivity incidents, derived from material, process, and inventory parameters."

### 1.2 The Dow Fire and Explosion Index (DOW F&EI)

The **DOW Fire and Explosion Index (DOW F&EI)** is the most widely used F&E hazard index worldwide. It was originally developed by **The Dow Chemical Company** in 1964 under the leadership of Mr. Jack Wagner and his team. It is currently published by the **American Institute of Chemical Engineers (AIChE)** in the *Dow's Fire & Explosion Index Hazard Classification Guide*. The 7th Edition (1994) and the 8th Edition (2016, AIChE) are the most referenced versions. It is a **quantitative, prescriptive, and empirically derived** tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why DOW F&EI is mandatory study in KTU 2024 PBSFT404**
> - It is the **first formally codified** hazard index in chemical process safety.
> - It forms the basis of the **Loss Prevention Principle** in chemical plant design.
> - It is the **only** index that simultaneously yields: (i) ranking number, (ii) radius of exposure, (iii) dollar loss estimate, and (iv) business interruption.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you are a **home insurance underwriter** evaluating 100 houses. Instead of reading lengthy reports, you assign each house a **"Fire Risk Score"** between 0 and 300. The score combines:
- **Material combustibility** (wood vs. concrete = low vs. high score)
- **Storage quantity** (matchbox of petrol vs. 1000 L tank)
- **Handling conditions** (cool garage vs. hot sun)
- **Safety features** (smoke detector, fire extinguisher, sprinklers — these *reduce* the final score)

The **DOW F&EI works exactly the same way** for an industrial process plant. A small reactor holding a non-flammable solvent scores low (say, F&EI = 60), while a large naphtha storage tank scores high (F&EI = 200+). The index does NOT tell you the *probability* of a fire — it tells you the **magnitude of damage if one occurs**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for first-time learners: "M-G-S-D"**
> **M**aterial → **G**eneral Hazards → **S**pecial Hazards → **D**amage & Loss. This is the four-step logic the Dow index follows.

### 1.4 Objectives of the DOW F&EI (Hierarchy of Purpose)

1. **Quantify** the realistic maximum fire, explosion, and reactivity loss potential.
2. **Identify** the equipment/process most likely to be the origin of a major incident.
3. **Communicate** loss potential to management, insurers, and regulatory bodies.
4. **Compare** alternative process routes and sites on a single numerical scale.
5. **Prioritize** loss-prevention expenditures and design improvements.
6. **Trigger** the use of detailed quantitative risk analysis (QRA) when the F&EI exceeds a threshold (typically F&EI > 128).
7. **Estimate** dollar loss (Maximum Probable Property Damage — MPPD) and business interruption (BI).

### 1.5 Limitations (Briefly Noted in Section 5)

> [!WARNING]
> The DOW F&EI is **NOT** a frequency/probability tool. It assumes an incident has already occurred and estimates the consequence magnitude. It is also a **single-process-unit** index, not a plant-wide or societal risk tool.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The DOW F&EI Logic Flow (Top-Level Architecture)

The DOW F&EI calculation follows a **rigid, eight-stage sequential logic**:

**Stage 1** → Identify the **Process Unit** (a logical grouping of equipment, e.g., a reactor + condenser + receiver).
**Stage 2** → Determine the **Material Factor (MF)** of the most hazardous material in the unit.
**Stage 3** → Compute the **General Process Hazards (GPH)** factor.
**Stage 4** → Compute the **Special Process Hazards (SPH)** factor.
**Stage 5** → Calculate the **Process Unit Hazards Factor (PUHF) = GPH + SPH**.
**Stage 6** → Calculate the **Fire and Explosion Index = MF × PUHF**.
**Stage 7** → Determine the **Damage Factor (DF)** from the DOW Chart (DF depends on F&EI and the unit's Material Factor).
**Stage 8** → Apply **Loss Control Credit Factors (LCCF)** to obtain the **Actual MPPD**, **MPDO**, and **Business Interruption (BI)**.

### 2.2 Material Factor (MF) — The Foundation of the Index

The **Material Factor (MF)** is a measure of the **intrinsic energy release potential** (combustion energy + reactivity) of the most hazardous material present in the process unit in significant quantity.

It is determined from a **DOW lookup table** based on:
- **NFPA flammability rating** (0–4)
- **NFPA reactivity rating** (0–4)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Benchmark Values (Memorize these):**
> - **MF = 1** → Water, sand, inert gases (no combustion energy)
> - **MF = 10** → Light oils like diesel (low volatility, slow burning)
> - **MF = 16** → Toluene, xylene, gasoline (the **most commonly cited benchmark in KTU exams**)
> - **MF = 21** → Acetone, MEK (high volatility, low flash point)
> - **MF = 24** → Acetylene, hydrogen, ethylene oxide (extreme reactivity)
> - **MF = 29** → Hydrazine (NFPA 4-3-3 — flammable + reactive + toxic)

If the material is not in the table, MF is computed as:

$$
\text{MF} = \frac{\Delta H_c \times 1.8 \times 10^{-2}}{1000 \times (0.0010 \times 1000 \times d)}
$$

where $\Delta H_c$ is the heat of combustion (kJ/mol) and $d$ is the density (kg/m³), or more commonly, a **pilot-burn test-based** assignment is used.

### 2.3 General Process Hazards (GPH) — Six Sub-Factors

The **GPH** captures **routine operational** characteristics of the plant. It is the sum of six penalty terms, each starting with a **base value of 1.00**:

| # | GPH Sub-Factor | Typical Penalty Range |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Base factor** | **1.00** (always added) |
| 2 | **Inventory** (quantity of material released) | 0.00 – 1.50 |
| 3 | **Above ambient pressure** (and/or vacuum) | 0.30 – 0.90 |
| 4 | **Low temperature** (carbon-steel embrittlement) | 0.20 – 0.30 |
| 5 | **Corrosion & erosion** | 0.10 – 0.75 |
| 6 | **Joint & packing leaks** (pump/valve flange) | 0.10 – 1.50 |

> [!TIP]
> **Sum of typical GPH in a moderately hazardous unit: 2.0 to 5.0.** Anything above GPH = 6.0 is considered high.

### 2.4 Special Process Hazards (SPH) — Twelve Sub-Factors

The **SPH** captures **exceptional, non-routine** hazards. It has **twelve** sub-factors:

| # | SPH Sub-Factor | Typical Penalty Range |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Base factor** | **1.00** |
| 2 | **Oxidizing materials** | 0.10 – 0.50 |
| 3 | **Reactions (unstable / exothermic)** | 0.10 – 4.00 |
| 4 | **Dust accumulation / mist** | 0.20 – 2.00 |
| 5 | **Pressure relief** (thermal expansion) | 0.10 – 0.50 |
| 6 | **Low-pressure storage / handling** | 0.10 – 1.00 |
| 7 | **Quantity of material released** (inventory again) | 0.10 – 3.00 |
| 8 | **Corrosion** (sub-factor in SPH as well) | 0.10 – 0.75 |
| 9 | **Use near flammable liquids / hot oil** | 0.10 – 0.50 |
| 10 | **Equipment & machinery rotation** (pumps, fans) | 0.10 – 0.50 |
| 11 | **Hot surfaces** (> 0.6 × AIT) | 0.20 – 0.40 |
| 12 | **Emergency support** (drainage, dyking) | 0.10 – 0.50 |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Inventory appears in BOTH GPH and SPH** but with different scoring logic. GPH inventory deals with *normal* operating inventory; SPH inventory deals with *total* inventory including drums, pipework, and intermediate storage. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

### 2.5 The Master F&EI Formula (KTU High-Yield)

$$
\boxed{\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times \left( \text{GPH} + \text{SPH} \right)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PUHF} = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH}}
$$

### 2.6 Radius of Exposure and Area of Exposure

The **Radius of Exposure (R)** is the distance from the unit center within which damage is expected to occur if a major incident unfolds. It is a **linear function** of the F&EI:

$$
\boxed{R = 0.256 \times \text{F\&EI} \quad [\text{feet}]}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI} \quad [\text{meters}]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **0.256 ft** and **0.84 m** come from a correlation of historical incident data. The radius is essentially the **lethal / serious-damage zone**.

The **Area of Exposure (A)** is the circular area around the unit exposed to damage:

$$
\boxed{A = \pi \times R^2 \quad [\text{sq ft or sq m}]}
$$

### 2.7 Damage Factor (DF) — Empirical Chart

The **Damage Factor (DF)** is read from a **DOW proprietary chart** using the F&EI and the Material Factor. It ranges from **0.00 (no damage)** to **1.00 (total destruction)**. Typical values:

| F&EI Range | Typical DF |
|---|---|
| 0 – 50 | 0.00 – 0.10 |
| 50 – 100 | 0.10 – 0.40 |
| 100 – 150 | 0.40 – 0.70 |
| 150 – 200 | 0.70 – 0.85 |
| 200 – 300 | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| > 300 | 0.95 – 1.00 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A higher MF shifts the DF curve upward for the same F&EI, because a more reactive material causes more damage per unit area. **Read the chart in the 7th edition Dow manual.**

### 2.8 Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD)

$$
\boxed{\text{Base MPPD} = \text{DF} \times \text{Replacement Value of Unit}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Actual MPPD} = \text{Base MPPD} \times \text{LCCF}}
$$

where **LCCF** is the **Loss Control Credit Factor** = product of credit factors for:

- Process Control (C₁)
- Material Isolation (C₂)
- Fire Protection (C₃)

Each credit factor is between **0.84 (best)** and **1.00 (no credit)**. So Actual MPPD ≤ Base MPPD.

### 2.9 Maximum Probable Days Outage (MPDO) and Business Interruption (BI)

**MPDO** is read from a **DOW nomograph** (or computed via regression) based on Actual MPPD and **VPM (Value of Production per Month)**. The empirical correlation is:

$$
\boxed{\text{MPDO} \approx \frac{0.7 \times \text{Actual MPPD}}{\text{VPM} \times 0.04} \quad [\text{months}]}
$$

Or in days:

$$
\boxed{\text{MPDO}_{\text{days}} \approx 30 \times \frac{0.7 \times \text{Actual MPPD}}{0.04 \times \text{VPM}}}
$$

The **Business Interruption (BI)** loss is:

$$
\boxed{\text{BI} = 0.7 \times \text{VPM} \times \text{MPDO}_{\text{months}}}
$$

The factor **0.7** represents the **fixed-cost / contribution margin** portion of VPM.

### 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Comprehensive Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times (\text{GPH} + \text{SPH})$ | Master DOW F&EI |
| 2 | $R = 0.256 \times \text{F\&EI}$ (ft) | Radius of exposure |
| 3 | $R = 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI}$ (m) | Radius of exposure (SI) |
| 4 | $A = \pi R^2$ | Area of exposure |
| 5 | $\text{Base MPPD} = \text{DF} \times \text{Replace. Value}$ | Unmitigated loss |
| 6 | $\text{Actual MPPD} = \text{Base MPPD} \times \text{LCCF}$ | Loss after controls |
| 7 | $\text{MPDO} = \dfrac{0.7 \times \text{Actual MPPD}}{0.04 \times \text{VPM}}$ | Outage duration (months) |
| 8 | $\text{BI} = 0.7 \times \text{VPM} \times \text{MPDO}_{\text{months}}$ | Business interruption loss |
| 9 | $\text{LCCF} = C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3$ | Combined loss-control credit |
| 10 | Degree of Hazard: Light $\vert$ Moderate $\vert$ Heavy $\vert$ Severe | F&EI: 0–95 $\vert$ 96–127 $\vert$ 128–158 $\vert$ > 158 |

### 2.11 Severity Ranking (DOW F&EI → Degree of Hazard)

| F&EI Value | Degree of Hazard |
|---|---|
| 1 – 60 | Light |
| 61 – 95 | Moderate |
| 96 – 127 | Heavy |
| **≥ 128** | **Severe** (triggers QRA) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Important:** An F&EI ≥ 128 mandates a **detailed quantitative risk analysis (QRA)** in addition to the index. This is a frequently asked conceptual question.

### 2.12 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plant design phase:** Compare alternative technologies, layouts, and inventories.
- **Insurance underwriting:** Insurers use DOW F&EI to set property insurance premiums.
- **Regulatory compliance:** OSHA, EPA, and SEVESO Directive reference it for Safety Reports.
- **Plant modification management (MOC):** Quantify change impact.
- **Emergency planning:** Radius of exposure sets the muster-zone distance.
- **Insurance industry:** Used by **Industrial Risk Insurers (IRI)** as the global benchmark.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Logical Derivation of the Master F&EI Equation

The DOW F&EI is **not** derived from first principles of physics. It is an **empirical regression** on the historical loss data of the Dow Chemical Company. The **multiplicative form** arises from the following physical reasoning:

> A unit's loss potential is the **product** of:
> 1. The **intrinsic energy content of the material** (the Material Factor, MF).
> 2. The **degree to which the process amplifies that energy** (the Process Unit Hazards Factor, PUHF = GPH + SPH).

Mathematically:

$$
\text{Loss Potential} \propto \text{Intrinsic Energy} \times \text{Process Amplification}
$$

The constants 0.256 ft and 0.84 m are regression coefficients, while DF is an empirical polynomial interpolation. The 0.7 and 0.04 factors in MPDO/BI come from industrial-average fixed-cost and operating-cost fractions.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When Each Penalty Sub-Factor Applies (Decision Table)

| Sub-factor | Triggers Penalty If… | Applies to GPH/SPH | Maximum Penalty |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inventory | Total material in unit > 5000 kg | Both (different scoring) | GPH: 1.50, SPH: 3.00 |
| Pressure | Operating P > atmospheric (any) | GPH | 0.90 (up to 1000+ psi) |
| Low temperature | T < –29 °C (carbon steel) | GPH | 0.30 |
| Corrosion | T, P, fluid promote corrosion | Both | 0.75 |
| Joint leaks | Pumps > 75 hp, screwed unions | GPH | 1.50 |
| Oxidation | Oxidizer present (e.g., HNO₃, H₂O₂) | SPH | 0.50 |
| Exothermic reaction | ΔH_rxn < –200 kJ/kg | SPH | 4.00 |
| Dust/mist | Combustible dust cloud | SPH | 2.00 |
| Hot oil heater | Heat transfer fluid above AIT | SPH | 0.50 |
| Hot surface | T_surface > 0.6 × AIT | SPH | 0.40 |

### 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Example

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> A process unit handles **toluene** in a **5000 kg** inventory at an **operating pressure of 5 bar(g)** and **temperature of 60 °C**. The unit has a **pump** with flanged joints, mild corrosion, and an **exothermic reaction** with ΔH = –250 kJ/kg. No oxidizers, no dust. **Process control** is moderate (C₁ = 0.95), **isolation** is good (C₂ = 0.90), and **fire protection** is excellent (C₃ = 0.84). **Replacement value** of the unit is **₹ 5,00,00,000** (5 Crore) and **VPM = ₹ 80,00,000/month** (80 Lakh). Calculate the **F&EI**, **Radius of Exposure**, **Area of Exposure**, **MPPD**, **MPDO**, and **BI**.

#### Step 1: Determine Material Factor (MF)
Toluene → NFPA 3-0-0 (flammable, stable). From the DOW table, **MF for toluene = 16**.

$$
\text{MF} = 16
$$

> The most hazardous material in the unit is the basis for MF. Toluene is the only material here.

#### Step 2: Compute General Process Hazards (GPH)

| Sub-factor | Penalty | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Base | 1.00 | Always |
| Inventory (5000 kg) | 0.60 | Between 4500–6800 kg band |
| Above ambient pressure (5 barg) | 0.55 | 0.55 typical for low-pressure operation |
| Low temperature | 0.00 | T > 0 °C |
| Corrosion & erosion | 0.10 | Mild corrosion |
| Joints & packing leaks | 0.30 | Pump with flanged joints |

$$
\text{GPH} = 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.55 + 0.00 + 0.10 + 0.30 = 2.55
$$

#### Step 3: Compute Special Process Hazards (SPH)

| Sub-factor | Penalty | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Base | 1.00 | Always |
| Oxidizing materials | 0.00 | None |
| Reactions (exothermic) | 1.50 | ΔH = –250 kJ/kg, falls in 200–400 band |
| Dust / mist | 0.00 | None |
| Pressure relief (thermal) | 0.30 | Standard relief |
| Low-pressure storage | 0.00 | Operating pressure is moderate |
| Quantity of material released | 1.00 | 5000 kg, full inventory available |
| Corrosion (SPH sub) | 0.10 | Mild |
| Hot oil / flammable liquids | 0.50 | Hot oil heat transfer used |
| Rotating equipment | 0.50 | Pump and agitator |
| Hot surfaces | 0.20 | Surface > 0.6 × AIT |
| Emergency support (drainage) | 0.50 | Dyking available |

$$
\text{SPH} = 1.00 + 0.00 + 1.50 + 0.00 + 0.30 + 0.00 + 1.00 + 0.10 + 0.50 + 0.50 + 0.20 + 0.50 = 5.60
$$

#### Step 4: Compute PUHF

$$
\text{PUHF} = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH} = 2.55 + 5.60 = 8.15
$$

#### Step 5: Compute F&EI

$$
\boxed{\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times \text{PUHF} = 16 \times 8.15 = 130.4 \approx 130}
$$

> Since F&EI = 130 > 128, the unit is classified as **"Severe"** hazard. **QRA is mandated.**

#### Step 6: Radius of Exposure (in meters)

$$
R = 0.84 \times 130.4 = 109.5 \text{ m}
$$

In feet:

$$
R = 0.256 \times 130.4 = 33.4 \text{ ft}
$$

#### Step 7: Area of Exposure

$$
A = \pi \times R^2 = \pi \times (109.5)^2
$$

$$
A = 3.1416 \times 11,990.25 = 37,672 \text{ sq m}
$$

#### Step 8: Damage Factor (from DOW chart, MF = 16, F&EI = 130)

Reading the DOW chart: at MF = 16 and F&EI = 130, **DF ≈ 0.74**.

$$
\text{DF} = 0.74
$$

#### Step 9: Base MPPD

$$
\text{Base MPPD} = 0.74 \times 5,00,00,000 = 3,70,00,000 \text{ (₹ 3.70 Crore)}
$$

#### Step 10: Loss Control Credit Factor (LCCF)

$$
\text{LCCF} = C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3 = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.84
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LCCF} = 0.7182}
$$

#### Step 11: Actual MPPD

$$
\text{Actual MPPD} = 3,70,00,000 \times 0.7182 = 2,65,73,400 \text{ (₹ 2.66 Crore)}
$$

#### Step 12: Maximum Probable Days Outage (MPDO)

$$
\text{MPDO}_{\text{months}} = \frac{0.7 \times \text{Actual MPPD}}{0.04 \times \text{VPM}}
$$

$$
\text{MPDO}_{\text{months}} = \frac{0.7 \times 2,65,73,400}{0.04 \times 80,00,000}
$$

$$
\text{MPDO}_{\text{months}} = \frac{1,86,01,380}{3,20,000} = 58.13 \text{ months}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A value of 58 months is unrealistic and indicates a **very high** loss potential. In practice, the unit must be re-engineered. The KTU exam answer is mathematically valid but the student should comment on severity.

In days:

$$
\text{MPDO}_{\text{days}} = 58.13 \times 30 = 1,744 \text{ days}
$$

#### Step 13: Business Interruption (BI)

$$
\text{BI} = 0.7 \times \text{VPM} \times \text{MPDO}_{\text{months}}
$$

$$
\text{BI} = 0.7 \times 80,00,000 \times 58.13 = 32,55,28,000 \text{ (₹ 32.55 Crore)}
$$

### 3.4 Sensitivity Analysis Matrix

| Variable | Increase Direction | Effect on F&EI | Effect on MPPD | Effect on MPDO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| MF (more hazardous material) | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑ |
| GPH | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| SPH | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| LCCF (more credit) | ↑ | **No effect on F&EI** | ↓ | ↓ |
| Replacement value | ↑ | **No effect on F&EI** | ↑ | ↑ |
| VPM (higher production) | ↑ | **No effect on F&EI** | **No effect on MPPD** | ↓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is **examined directly** in KTU. Notice the **decoupling** of F&EI from LCCF and replacement value. F&EI is a **pre-control** design index; MPPD/MPDO are **post-control** loss indices.

### 3.5 Cross-Index Comparison Matrix (KTU Favourite)

| Index | Developer | Year | Output | Frequency Considered? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DOW F&EI** | Dow Chemical | 1964 | F&EI number, R, MPPD, BI | **No** |
| **Mond Index** | Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI) | 1974 | Modified F&EI with toxicity | No |
| **CEI (Chemical Exposure Index)** | Dow / AIChE | 1990s | Toxic exposure radius | No |
| **IFAL (Instantaneous Fractional Annual Loss)** | – | 1980s | Annualized loss ($/yr) | **Yes** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — DOW F&EI Computational Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[START: Identify Process Unit] --> stepB[Step 1: Identify Most Hazardous Material]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Look up Material Factor MF from DOW Table]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Compute General Process Hazards GPH]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Compute Special Process Hazards SPH]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 5: PUHF equals GPH plus SPH]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Calculate F and EI equals MF times PUHF]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 7: Classify Severity Light Moderate Heavy Severe]
    stepH --> stepI{F and EI greater than 128}
    stepI --> stepJ[Yes: Trigger Detailed QRA and Layer of Protection Analysis]
    stepI --> stepK[No: Continue to Loss Estimation]
    stepK --> stepL[Step 8: Read Damage Factor DF from DOW Chart]
    stepL --> stepM[Step 9: Compute Base MPPD equals DF times Replacement Value]
    stepM --> stepN[Step 10: Apply Loss Control Credit Factors C1 C2 C3]
    stepN --> stepO[Step 11: Compute Actual MPPD equals Base MPPD times LCCF]
    stepO --> stepP[Step 12: Estimate MPDO from Nomograph using Actual MPPD and VPM]
    stepP --> stepQ[Step 13: Calculate Business Interruption BI equals 0.7 times VPM times MPDO months]
    stepQ --> stepR[Step 14: Report Outputs: F and EI, R, A, MPPD, MPDO, BI]
    stepR --> stepS[END: Decision Support for Design, Insurance, MOC]
    stepJ --> stepS
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Penalty Sub-factor Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootA[Process Unit Hazards Factor PUHF] --> nodeB1[General Process Hazards GPH]
    rootA --> nodeB2[Special Process Hazards SPH]
    nodeB1 --> subB11[Base 1.00]
    nodeB1 --> subB12[Inventory Penalty]
    nodeB1 --> subB13[Pressure Penalty]
    nodeB1 --> subB14[Corrosion Penalty]
    nodeB1 --> subB15[Low Temperature Penalty]
    nodeB1 --> subB16[Joint Leak Penalty]
    nodeB2 --> subB21[Base 1.00]
    nodeB2 --> subB22[Oxidizer Penalty]
    nodeB2 --> subB23[Exothermic Reaction Penalty]
    nodeB2 --> subB24[Dust or Mist Penalty]
    nodeB2 --> subB25[Pressure Relief Penalty]
    nodeB2 --> subB26[Released Quantity Penalty]
    nodeB2 --> subB27[Hot Oil Penalty]
    nodeB2 --> subB28[Hot Surface Penalty]
    nodeB2 --> subB29[Rotating Equipment Penalty]
    nodeB2 --> subB30[Emergency Support Penalty]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Output Decision Tree (Degree of Hazard)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    idxA[Calculated F and EI] --> idxB{F and EI less than 60}
    idxB --> idxC[Light Hazard: Routine Process Safety Management]
    idxB --> idxD{F and EI 61 to 95}
    idxD --> idxE[Moderate Hazard: Apply Loss Prevention Code]
    idxD --> idxF{F and EI 96 to 127}
    idxF --> idxG[Heavy Hazard: Detailed Engineering Review]
    idxD --> idxH{F and EI greater than or equal to 128}
    idxH --> idxI[Severe Hazard: MANDATORY QRA plus LOPA plus SIL assignment]
    idxC --> idxZ[END: Document in Safety Report]
    idxE --> idxZ
    idxG --> idxZ
    idxI --> idxZ
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Loss Control Credit Factor Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    lccfA[Loss Control Credit Factor LCCF] --> lccfB[Process Control Credit C1]
    lccfA --> lccfC[Material Isolation Credit C2]
    lccfA --> lccfD[Fire Protection Credit C3]
    lccfB --> lccfB1[Emergency Power]
    lccfB --> lccfB2[Cooling System]
    lccfB --> lccfB3[Instrumentation]
    lccfB --> lccfB4[Computer Control]
    lccfB --> lccfB5[Inert Gas Purge]
    lccfB --> lccfB6[Explosion Suppression]
    lccfC --> lccfC1[Remote Valves]
    lccfC --> lccfC2[Block Valves]
    lccfC --> lccfC3[Drainage]
    lccfC --> lccfC4[Interlocks]
    lccfD --> lccfD1[Leak Detection]
    lccfD --> lccfD2[Fire Water Supply]
    lccfD --> lccfD3[Special Systems]
    lccfD --> lccfD4[Sprinklers]
    lccfD --> lccfD5[Water Curtain]
    lccfD --> lccfD6[Foam]
    lccfD --> lccfD7[Hand Extinguishers]
    lccfD --> lccfD8[Cable Protection]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Dow Fire and Explosion Index (DOW F&EI). State the master equation and classify the four degrees of hazard based on the F&EI value.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (DOW F&EI)** is an objective numerical measure developed by The Dow Chemical Company (1964) to quantify the realistic maximum loss potential of a process plant from fire, explosion, and chemical reactivity incidents. It converts material, process, and inventory characteristics into a single dimensionless number.

$$
\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times (\text{GPH} + \text{SPH})
$$

| F&EI Value | Degree of Hazard |
|---|---|
| 1 – 60 | Light |
| 61 – 95 | Moderate |
| 96 – 127 | Heavy |
| ≥ 128 | **Severe** (triggers QRA) |

> Mark split: **Definition (1), Equation (1), Classification (1).**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between General Process Hazards (GPH) and Special Process Hazards (SPH) as used in the DOW F&EI method. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**GPH (General Process Hazards)** account for **routine, everyday** operational characteristics of the process. The base value is **1.00**, and it includes six sub-factors.

*Examples of GPH:*
1. **Inventory** (the amount of combustible material in the unit).
2. **Above-ambient operating pressure** (and vacuum conditions).
3. **Joint and packing leaks** (pumps, valves, flanges).

**SPH (Special Process Hazards)** account for **exceptional, non-routine** process conditions that can amplify loss potential. The base value is **1.00**, and it includes twelve sub-factors.

*Examples of SPH:*
1. **Exothermic reaction** (releases heat beyond the cooling capacity).
2. **Oxidizing material** presence (e.g., HNO₃, H₂O₂, O₃).
3. **Hot surface** above 0.6 × AIT (auto-ignition temperature).

> Key distinction: **GPH is "everyday"** while **SPH is "exceptional"**. Mark split: **GPH definition + 2 examples (1.5), SPH definition + 2 examples (1.5).**

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 Scheme requires **internal choice** at the module level. Two alternative questions are provided; the student answers **one**.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q. A process unit stores 8000 kg of acetone in a pressurized vessel at 8 bar(g) and 35 °C. The unit has a centrifugal pump, mild corrosion, an exothermic polymerization reaction (ΔH = –300 kJ/kg) with peroxide initiator, and uses hot oil heating (oil at 200 °C, AIT of acetone = 465 °C). Process control is good (C₁ = 0.93), isolation is fair (C₂ = 0.88), and fire protection is excellent (C₃ = 0.84). Replacement value of the unit is ₹ 4 Crore. The VPM is ₹ 1 Crore/month. Calculate:**
**(a) The DOW F&EI, the Radius of Exposure, and the Area of Exposure. (7 Marks)**
**(b) The Damage Factor, Base MPPD, Actual MPPD, MPDO, and Business Interruption. (7 Marks)**

##### **Solution (Question A)**

**Given:**
- Material: Acetone (NFPA 3-0-0, MF = 21)
- Inventory: 8000 kg
- Pressure: 8 barg, T = 35 °C
- Pump (flanged), mild corrosion, exothermic polymerization, peroxide initiator
- Hot oil at 200 °C
- C₁ = 0.93, C₂ = 0.88, C₃ = 0.84
- Replacement = ₹ 4,00,00,000; VPM = ₹ 1,00,00,000/month

**Part (a) — F&EI, R, A**

**Step 1: MF** → Acetone → **MF = 21**

**Step 2: GPH**

| Sub-factor | Penalty | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Base | 1.00 | Always |
| Inventory (8000 kg) | 0.80 | 6800–9000 kg band |
| Above ambient pressure (8 barg) | 0.60 | 5–10 barg band |
| Low temperature | 0.00 | T > 0 °C |
| Corrosion | 0.20 | Mild |
| Joint leaks (pump) | 0.30 | Centrifugal pump |

$$
\text{GPH} = 1.00 + 0.80 + 0.60 + 0.00 + 0.20 + 0.30 = 2.90
$$

**Step 3: SPH**

| Sub-factor | Penalty | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Base | 1.00 | Always |
| Oxidizer (peroxide) | 0.20 | Trace peroxide initiator |
| Exothermic reaction | 2.00 | Polymerization, ΔH = –300 kJ/kg |
| Dust/mist | 0.00 | None |
| Pressure relief | 0.30 | Standard relief |
| Low-pressure storage | 0.00 | Pressurized |
| Released quantity | 1.20 | 8000 kg |
| Corrosion (SPH) | 0.20 | Mild |
| Hot oil | 0.50 | Used |
| Rotating equipment | 0.50 | Pump + agitator |
| Hot surface | 0.00 | T_oil (200 °C) < 0.6 × 465 = 279 °C |
| Emergency support | 0.50 | Dyking |

$$
\text{SPH} = 1.00 + 0.20 + 2.00 + 0.00 + 0.30 + 0.00 + 1.20 + 0.20 + 0.50 + 0.50 + 0.00 + 0.50 = 6.40
$$

**Step 4: PUHF and F&EI**

$$
\text{PUHF} = 2.90 + 6.40 = 9.30
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{F\&EI} = 21 \times 9.30 = 195.3 \approx 195}
$$

> Classification: **Severe** (since 195 > 128). **QRA is mandatory.**

**Step 5: Radius and Area of Exposure**

$$
R = 0.84 \times 195.3 = 164.05 \text{ m}
$$

$$
R = 0.256 \times 195.3 = 50.0 \text{ ft}
$$

$$
A = \pi \times (164.05)^2 = 3.1416 \times 26,912.4 = 84,548 \text{ sq m}
$$

**Part (b) — DF, MPPD, MPDO, BI**

**Step 6: Damage Factor (DOW chart, MF = 21, F&EI = 195)**
At MF = 21 and F&EI = 195, the chart gives **DF ≈ 0.86**.

**Step 7: Base MPPD**

$$
\text{Base MPPD} = 0.86 \times 4,00,00,000 = 3,44,00,000 \text{ (₹ 3.44 Crore)}
$$

**Step 8: LCCF**

$$
\text{LCCF} = 0.93 \times 0.88 \times 0.84 = 0.6876
$$

**Step 9: Actual MPPD**

$$
\text{Actual MPPD} = 3,44,00,000 \times 0.6876 = 2,36,53,440 \text{ (₹ 2.37 Crore)}
$$

**Step 10: MPDO**

$$
\text{MPDO}_{\text{months}} = \frac{0.7 \times 2,36,53,440}{0.04 \times 1,00,00,000} = \frac{1,65,57,408}{4,00,000} = 41.39 \text{ months}
$$

$$
\text{MPDO}_{\text{days}} = 41.39 \times 30 = 1242 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 11: Business Interruption**

$$
\text{BI} = 0.7 \times 1,00,00,000 \times 41.39 = 28,97,30,000 \text{ (₹ 28.97 Crore)}
$$

##### **Final Answer (Question A) — Summary Box**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| MF | 21 |
| GPH | 2.90 |
| SPH | 6.40 |
| F&EI | **195.3 (Severe)** |
| Radius of Exposure | 164.05 m (50.0 ft) |
| Area of Exposure | 84,548 sq m |
| DF | 0.86 |
| Base MPPD | ₹ 3.44 Crore |
| LCCF | 0.6876 |
| Actual MPPD | ₹ 2.37 Crore |
| MPDO | 41.39 months (1242 days) |
| BI | ₹ 28.97 Crore |

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q. Discuss in detail the procedure for the calculation of the Dow Fire and Explosion Index. Explain each component of the index with relevant sub-factors. (14 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution Outline (Question B)**

**Part (a) — Procedure for DOW F&EI Calculation (7 Marks)**

The DOW F&EI calculation is performed in **eight sequential stages**:

1. **Identify the Process Unit:** A process unit is a logical grouping of equipment (e.g., a reactor, condenser, pump, and receiver serving one operational function). The unit with the **highest F&EI** dictates the plant's overall fire hazard.

2. **Determine the Material Factor (MF):** The MF is the **intrinsic fire/explosion/reactivity potential** of the most hazardous material. It is read from a DOW table based on **NFPA flammability and reactivity ratings** (range 1 to 40). Methanol = 10, Toluene = 16, Acetone = 21, Hydrazine = 29, Hydrogen = 28, etc.

3. **Compute the General Process Hazards (GPH):** GPH represents the **routine operational** penalty. There are **six sub-factors** (base + inventory + pressure + low temperature + corrosion + joint leaks), each scored on a numerical penalty. Typical GPH range: 2.0–5.0.

4. **Compute the Special Process Hazards (SPH):** SPH represents the **exceptional** penalties. There are **twelve sub-factors** (base + oxidizers + exothermic reactions + dust/mist + thermal relief + low-pressure handling + released quantity + corrosion + hot oil + rotating equipment + hot surfaces + emergency support). Typical SPH range: 2.0–8.0.

5. **Compute the Process Unit Hazards Factor (PUHF):**
$$
\text{PUHF} = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH}
$$

6. **Compute the Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI):**
$$
\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times \text{PUHF}
$$

7. **Determine the Damage Factor (DF):** The DF is read from the **DOW proprietary chart** using F&EI and MF. It represents the fraction of the unit destroyed in a major incident (range 0.00 to 1.00).

8. **Estimate losses and outage:**
   - **Radius of Exposure:** $R = 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI}$ (in meters)
   - **Area of Exposure:** $A = \pi R^2$
   - **Base MPPD:** $\text{DF} \times \text{Replacement Value}$
   - **LCCF:** $C_1 \times C_2 \times C_3$ (Process Control, Material Isolation, Fire Protection)
   - **Actual MPPD:** $\text{Base MPPD} \times \text{LCCF}$
   - **MPDO:** Read from nomograph or calculated using $\frac{0.7 \times \text{Actual MPPD}}{0.04 \times \text{VPM}}$
   - **Business Interruption:** $\text{BI} = 0.7 \times \text{VPM} \times \text{MPDO}_{\text{months}}$

> Mark split: **8 stages explained clearly (5), flow-chart mentioned (2).**

**Part (b) — Components and Sub-factors Explained (7 Marks)**

| Component | Sub-factors | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **MF** | Single value (1–40) | Quantifies intrinsic material hazard |
| **GPH** | 6 sub-factors (base 1.00) | Captures routine operational hazards |
| **SPH** | 12 sub-factors (base 1.00) | Captures exceptional hazard amplifiers |
| **PUHF** | GPH + SPH | Aggregate process-driven penalty |
| **F&EI** | MF × PUHF | Master hazard index (1–400+) |
| **DF** | From chart | Fractional damage to unit |
| **R / A** | Geometric functions | Spatial extent of damage |
| **LCCF** | C₁ × C₂ × C₃ (each 0.84–1.00) | Risk reduction credit |
| **MPPD** | DF × Replace. × LCCF | Financial loss (₹) |
| **MPDO** | Function of MPPD, VPM | Outage duration (days) |
| **BI** | 0.7 × VPM × MPDO | Loss of profit (₹) |

> Mark split: **Table (4), explanatory paragraph on classification & QRA trigger at F&EI ≥ 128 (3).**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DOW F&EI Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the base value of 1.00** in both GPH and SPH (each loses 0.5 marks).
> 2. **Confusing GPH inventory with SPH inventory** — GPH is *normal operating* inventory; SPH is *total potential release* inventory.
> 3. **Using the wrong unit for radius** — examiners expect the SI answer in **meters** unless explicitly stated. The factor is **0.84 m**, NOT 0.256 m.
> 4. **Mixing up MPPD, MPDO, and BI** — MPDO is in **months** for the BI formula; in **days** for reporting to plant management.
> 5. **Applying LCCF to F&EI** — LCCF applies **only to MPPD**, NOT to F&EI itself. This is a classic 2-mark deduction trap.
> 6. **Reporting the wrong severity classification** — at F&EI = 128 the boundary is **inclusive** in the "Severe" category.
> 7. **Skipping the QRA recommendation** — if F&EI ≥ 128, the answer is incomplete without a QRA recommendation. Examiners allocate 1 mark for this.
> 8. **Ignoring the "no units" requirement** for F&EI itself — it is dimensionless, even though the calculation involves scores.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **DOW F&EI — 30-Second Recall Sheet**

- **Originator:** The Dow Chemical Company, 1964; published by AIChE.
- **Equation:** $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times (\text{GPH} + \text{SPH})$
- **MF:** NFPA-based, typical range 1 (inert) to 29 (hydrazine). Toluene = 16, Acetone = 21.
- **GPH sub-factors (6):** Base + Inventory + Pressure + Low T + Corrosion + Joint leaks.
- **SPH sub-factors (12):** Base + Oxidizer + Reaction + Dust + Pressure relief + Low pressure + Released quantity + Corrosion + Hot oil + Rotating + Hot surface + Emergency support.
- **PUHF = GPH + SPH.**
- **Radius of Exposure (m):** $R = 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI}$.
- **Area of Exposure:** $A = \pi R^2$.
- **Damage Factor (DF):** Read from the DOW chart (0.00–1.00).
- **Base MPPD = DF × Replacement Value.**
- **LCCF = C₁ × C₂ × C₃**, where each C is in [0.84, 1.00].
- **Actual MPPD = Base MPPD × LCCF.**
- **MPDO (months) = (0.7 × Actual MPPD) / (0.04 × VPM).**
- **BI = 0.7 × VPM × MPDO (months).**
- **Severity classes:** Light (1–60), Moderate (61–95), Heavy (96–127), **Severe (≥ 128).**
- **At F&EI ≥ 128 → Mandatory QRA / LOPA / SIL assignment.**
- **DOW F&EI is a CONSEQUENCE index — NOT a frequency / probability tool.**
- **DOW F&EI is per process unit — NOT plant-wide or societal risk.**
- **Inventory appears twice** (once in GPH, once in SPH) — DO NOT double-count; the scores are different.
- **LCCF applies to MPPD only — NOT to F&EI.**
- **0.7 factor** = fixed cost / contribution margin; **0.04 factor** = operating cost fraction in MPDO.
- **Related indices to know:** Mond Index (ICI, 1974, with toxicity), CEI (Chemical Exposure Index, toxic radius), IFAL (frequency-incorporating loss).
- **Limitations:** (1) No frequency data, (2) per-unit only, (3) empirically derived, (4) requires Dow lookup tables.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Mond Index
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Mond Index

# 🔬 The Mond Index — A KTU 2024 Scheme Engineering Study Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This topic falls under the cluster of **Fire & Explosion Indices** used in Process Safety Management. The Mond Index is a **second-generation hazard index** developed by the **Mond Corporation (formerly ICI)** as an advanced, scientifically rigorous extension of the **DOW Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)**. It carries **high weightage** in KTU university examinations as it bridges fundamental fire theory with modern probabilistic risk analysis.

---

## 📘 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> **The Mond Index**, also referred to as the **Mond Fire, Explosion and Toxicity Index**, is a quantitative hazard rating methodology used to evaluate the **overall process hazard potential** of chemical and petrochemical plant units. It quantifies the radius of the vulnerable zone around process equipment and assigns a monetary loss potential, while uniquely accounting for **toxicological effects**, **layout geometry**, and **inventory adjustments** that the Dow Index does not fully capture.

Mathematically, the Mond Index is anchored on a **Material Factor (M)**, which is then modified by hazard penalty terms to yield a single composite dimensionless number reflecting the intrinsic hazard of the operation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are grading students in a school. The **Dow Index** is like a teacher who only checks whether a student can pass an exam on math and English (fire & explosion). The **Mond Index**, however, is like a stricter, more modern school inspector who also grades **toxicity (poison potential)**, **classroom layout (proximity to others)**, **number of students (quantity of chemical)**, and even **housekeeping discipline (good engineering practice)**. This makes the Mond Index a more *realistic and complete* assessment of danger.

### 1.3 Historical Origin & Standard Metrics

- **Developed by:** The Mond Corporation (originally Imperial Chemical Industries — **ICI**, UK) during the late **1970s** and **1980s**.
- **Primary Standard:** Documented in the **Chemical Industry Safety, Health & Environment Council (CISHC)** guidelines and the **D.J. Lewis (ICI)** technical reports.
- **Key Constant:** Material Factor (**M**) is derived from the **NFPA flammability rating (N_f)** and is dimensionless.
- **Boundary Limits:** A Mond Index value of **D < 27** is considered *Tolerable*, while **D > 100** warrants immediate corrective action.

---

## 📙 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Philosophy

The Mond Index is constructed on a **multiplicative penalty philosophy**. The hazard is amplified with each worsening condition rather than added linearly.

**Step 1: Establish the Base Material Factor (M)**
The Material Factor represents the **inherent energy release potential** of the substance. A substance like **hydrogen (M ≈ 24)** is far more energetic than water.

**Step 2: Apply General Process Hazards (G)**
These are **operating-mode hazards** present whenever the material is processed. Examples:
- Inventory and handling
- Enclosed/unenclosed units
- Drainage and spill control

**Step 3: Apply Special Process Hazards (S)**
These are **situation-specific hazards** that magnify risk:
- High temperature/pressure operation
- Corrosion/erosion tendencies
- Reactive chemical incompatibilities
- Use of toxic materials

**Step 4: Calculate the Base Major Hazard Index (B)**

$$B = \left(1 + \frac{M}{100}\right) \times (G + S)$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Radius of Vulnerability (R) and Distance Factor (F)**

$$R = 10^{\frac{B}{2.647}} \quad \text{(meters)}$$

$$F = R \times 0.5$$

**Step 6: Apply Quantity & Layout Factors**
The Quantity Factor **Q** adjusts B based on the actual tonnage of material present, and the **Layout Factor (L)** accounts for the spatial configuration.

$$D = B \times Q \times L \times \text{(Toxicity Index correction)}$$

**Step 7: Final Classification**

| Mond Index Value (D) | Classification |
|---|---|
| **0 – 27** | **Mild** — Acceptable |
| **27 – 60** | **Moderate** — Needs monitoring |
| **60 – 100** | **Heavy** — Significant action |
| **> 100** | **Very Heavy / Extreme** |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **core formulas** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the ESE. Avoid using the vertical pipe `|` symbol within cells; mathematical absolute value is denoted as `\vert \cdot \vert`.

| **#** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Definition** | **Unit** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | M | NFPA-based Material Factor | dimensionless | Quantifies intrinsic energy |
| 2 | G | Sum of General Process Hazards | dimensionless | Operating penalties |
| 3 | S | Sum of Special Process Hazards | dimensionless | Scenario penalties |
| 4 | B | $\left(1 + \frac{M}{100}\right)(G+S)$ | dimensionless | Base Major Hazard Index |
| 5 | R | $10^{B / 2.647}$ | meters | Radius of vulnerability |
| 6 | F | $R \times 0.5$ | meters | Half-radius correction |
| 7 | Q | Quantity Adjustment Factor | dimensionless | Inventory scaling |
| 8 | L | Layout Factor | dimensionless | Spatial penalty |
| 9 | T | Toxicity Index | dimensionless | Health hazard scaling |
| 10 | D | Final Mond Index = $B \times Q \times L \times T$ | dimensionless | Overall Hazard Rating |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plant Layout Design:** Determining minimum separation distances between units.
- **Insurance Premiuming:** Used by re-insurers to quantify Process Risk.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Supports **OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119)** and **SEVESO III Directive** compliance audits in EU refineries.
- **HAZOP Pre-screening:** Acts as a **sieve** to prioritize units for detailed HAZOP studies.
- **Municipal Emergency Planning:** Identifies potential **toxic gas dispersion footprints** for off-site consequence analysis.

---

## 📗 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Example

### 3.1 Derivation of the Base Major Hazard Index (B)

The Mond Index philosophy recognizes that the **Material Factor alone is insufficient** because a dangerous material stored safely is far less hazardous than a moderately dangerous material under high-pressure reactive conditions.

Therefore, the Base Major Hazard Index is derived as a **product of a normalized material amplification term and a sum of hazard penalties:**

$$B = \underbrace{\left(1 + \frac{M}{100}\right)}_{\text{Energy Amplification}} \times \underbrace{(G + S)}_{\text{Hazard Penalties}}$$

**Justification of each term:**

- The term $\left(1 + \frac{M}{100}\right)$ ensures that even when $G + S = 0$, a non-zero base hazard exists corresponding to the material's energy release capability.
- The term $(G + S)$ aggregates penalties; both are tabulated multipliers ranging typically from **0.1 to 1.0** for each sub-hazard.
- This **multiplicative coupling** ensures hazards do not merely add — they **compound geometrically**, matching physical reality.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Engineering Style)

> **Problem:** A plant stores **10 tonnes of acetone** under the following conditions:
> - Material Factor (M) for acetone = **16**
> - General Process Hazards (G): Inventory penalty = 0.50, Enclosed unit = 0.30, Spillage = 0.20
> - Special Process Hazards (S): Pressure = 0.40, Corrosion = 0.30, Joints = 0.10
> - Quantity Factor Q = **1.2**
> - Layout Factor L = **1.1**
> - Toxicity Index T = **1.0**

**Step 1:** Compute $G$

$$G = 0.50 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 1.00$$

**Step 2:** Compute $S$

$$S = 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.10 = 0.80$$

**Step 3:** Compute $G + S$

$$G + S = 1.00 + 0.80 = 1.80$$

**Step 4:** Compute the energy amplification term

$$\left(1 + \frac{M}{100}\right) = \left(1 + \frac{16}{100}\right) = 1.16$$

**Step 5:** Compute Base Major Hazard Index (B)

$$B = 1.16 \times 1.80 = 2.088$$

**Step 6:** Compute Radius of Vulnerability (R)

$$R = 10^{\frac{2.088}{2.647}} = 10^{0.7888} = 6.15 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 7:** Compute Final Mond Index D

$$D = B \times Q \times L \times T = 2.088 \times 1.2 \times 1.1 \times 1.0 = 2.756$$

**Step 8:** Classification

Since **D < 27**, the plant section is rated **"Mild"** and is acceptable, though continuous monitoring is recommended.

---

## 📕 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mond Index Computational Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Identify Process Unit"] --> nodeB["Determine Material Factor M from NFPA"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Tabulate General Process Hazards G"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Tabulate Special Process Hazards S"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Compute G plus S"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Compute B equals 1 plus M over 100 times G plus S"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Compute Radius R equals 10 to the power B over 2.647"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Apply Quantity Factor Q"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Apply Layout Factor L"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Apply Toxicity Index T"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Compute D equals B Q L T"]
    nodeK --> nodeL{"Is D greater than 100?"}
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeM["Extreme Action Required"]
    nodeL -- No --> nodeN{"Is D greater than 60?"}
    nodeN -- Yes --> nodeO["Heavy Category"]
    nodeN -- No --> nodeP["Moderate or Mild"]
```

### 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — Dow vs Mond Indices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Dow["Dow Fire and Explosion Index"]
        d1["NFPA Rating"] --> d2["Material Factor MF"]
        d2 --> d3["General Process Hazards"]
        d3 --> d4["Special Process Hazards"]
        d4 --> d5["F and EI Score"]
    end

    subgraph Mond["Mond Index"]
        m1["NFPA plus Toxicity"] --> m2["Material Factor M"]
        m2 --> m3["General Hazards G"]
        m3 --> m4["Special Hazards S"]
        m4 --> m5["Base Index B"]
        m5 --> m6["Quantity Factor Q"]
        m6 --> m7["Layout Factor L"]
        m7 --> m8["Final Mond D"]
    end
```

---

## 📓 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Define the **Material Factor (M)** as used in the Mond Index. How is it determined for a substance?

> **Model Answer:**
> The Material Factor (**M**) is a dimensionless numerical rating that quantifies the **intrinsic energy release potential** of a chemical substance during a fire or explosion event. It is determined by referencing the **NFPA (National Fire Protection Association) flammability rating** $N_f$ of the substance through a standardized lookup table. For highly energetic substances like **hydrogen ($N_f = 4$)**, M approaches values of **21–24**, while for low-energy substances like water, M = 0.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** List **any four differences** between the **Dow Fire and Explosion Index** and the **Mond Index**.

> **Model Answer:**
> 1. The Mond Index explicitly incorporates a **Toxicity Index**, while the Dow Index does not.
> 2. The Mond Index uses a **Layout Factor (L)** to account for spatial arrangement, absent in Dow.
> 3. The Mond Index incorporates a **Quantity Factor (Q)** for inventory scaling.
> 4. The Mond Index expresses hazard via a **multiplicative penalty model**, whereas the Dow Index uses an additive offset model with an F-3 factor.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the aid of a neat schematic, explain the **step-by-step methodology** of computing the Mond Index for a process plant unit.

> **Model Solution:**
> **Step 1 — Identification:** Select the process unit (e.g., a naphtha storage tank).
> **Step 2 — Material Factor (M):** From NFPA tables, M for naphtha is typically **16**.
> **Step 3 — General Process Hazards (G):** Sum penalties from: storage mode, drainage, spillage, etc. Suppose G = 1.20.
> **Step 4 — Special Process Hazards (S):** Sum penalties from: pressure, temperature, corrosion, joints. Suppose S = 0.80.
> **Step 5 — Compute Base Index B:**
>
> $$B = \left(1 + \frac{M}{100}\right)(G + S) = \left(1 + \frac{16}{100}\right)(1.20 + 0.80) = 1.16 \times 2.00 = 2.32$$
>
> **Step 6 — Compute Radius of Vulnerability R:**
>
> $$R = 10^{B/2.647} = 10^{2.32/2.647} = 10^{0.8765} \approx 7.52 \, \text{m}$$
>
