> **Step 7 — Apply Quantity Factor Q and Layout Factor L.** For a 50-tonne storage with poor spacing: Q = 1.4, L = 1.2.
> **Step 8 — Compute Final Mond Index D:**
>
> $$D = B \times Q \times L = 2.32 \times 1.4 \times 1.2 = 3.898$$
>
> Since D < 27, the unit is classified as **Mild**, requiring only routine monitoring.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **engineering significance** of the Mond Index in **plant layout design and insurance risk evaluation**.

> **Model Solution:**
> In **plant layout design**, the Mond Index yields the **Radius of Vulnerability (R)**, which dictates the **minimum safe inter-unit spacing** to prevent domino effects during fire/explosion events. This distance is critical for compliance with **OSHA PSM** and the **NBC (National Building Code) of India** for hazardous industries.
>
> In **insurance evaluation**, re-insurers like **Lloyd's of London** use the Mond Index to estimate the **Maximum Probable Loss (MPL)**. A higher D value translates into elevated premium pricing and stricter safety audit mandates.
>
> Furthermore, the Mond Index is integrated into **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** workflows where it serves as a screening tool, identifying high-hazard units requiring detailed **HAZOP**, **LOPA**, or **fault tree analysis**.

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] A refinery stores **50 tonnes of toluene**. Given: **M = 16**, **G = 1.40**, **S = 0.60**, **Q = 1.3**, **L = 1.0**, **T = 1.1**. Compute the **final Mond Index** and classify the hazard.

> **Model Solution:**
> **Step 1:** Compute B
>
> $$B = \left(1 + \frac{16}{100}\right)(1.40 + 0.60) = 1.16 \times 2.00 = 2.32$$
>
> **Step 2:** Apply Q, L, T
>
> $$D = 2.32 \times 1.3 \times 1.0 \times 1.1 = 3.3176$$
>
> **Step 3:** Classification — D < 27 ⇒ **Mild Hazard**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Critically evaluate the **limitations** of the Mond Index in modern process safety practice.

> **Model Solution:**
> Despite its comprehensive structure, the Mond Index exhibits several limitations:
> 1. **Subjective Penalty Selection:** The G and S penalties are tabulated with broad ranges, allowing subjective interpretation that can yield inconsistent results across different analysts.
> 2. **Static Nature:** The index assumes **steady-state operation** and fails to model transient risks during start-up, shut-down, or maintenance.
> 3. **Limited Toxicological Granularity:** Although the Toxicity Index is included, it does not replace a rigorous **probabilistic toxic dispersion analysis**.
> 4. **Insensitivity to Human Factors:** Operator error, training, and procedural compliance are not captured.
> 5. **Outdated for Modern Risks:** Nanotechnology, hydrogen economy, and AI-controlled plants introduce risk profiles the original 1980s framework was never designed to evaluate.
>
> Hence, the Mond Index is best deployed as a **screening tool** rather than a definitive risk measure.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in R:** Students often forget the unit of **Radius R is meters**, not feet. KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for the unit.
> 2. **Formula mis-quoting:** Writing $B = (1 + M) \times (G + S)$ instead of $\left(1 + \frac{M}{100}\right)(G+S)$ loses a full mark in derivations.
> 3. **Skipping intermediate steps:** KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **step-by-step working** under **CO4 (Bloom: Apply/Analyze)**. Skipping from M directly to D costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring classification:** Always end with the **Mild/Moderate/Heavy/Extreme** classification — failing to do so results in deduction of 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing Q with quantity in tonnes:** The Quantity Factor Q is a **dimensionless multiplier**, not the tonnage itself.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Mond Index = Base Penalty Model** incorporating Material, General, and Special Hazards.
- 🔑 The **Material Factor M** is the cornerstone — always look it up via **NFPA flammability**.
- 🔑 The **multiplicative coupling** $\left(1 + \frac{M}{100}\right)$ makes the index **non-linear**.
- 🔑 **Radius of Vulnerability** uses the constant **2.647** in the exponent — memorize it.
- 🔑 Final value **D < 27 ⇒ Mild**, **27–60 ⇒ Moderate**, **60–100 ⇒ Heavy**, **>100 ⇒ Extreme**.
- 🔑 The Mond Index **uniquely includes Toxicity**, distinguishing it from the Dow Index.
- 🔑 Quantity Factor (Q) and Layout Factor (L) are **inventive additions** absent in Dow.
- 🔑 Used in **insurance underwriting, plant layout, and HAZOP pre-screening**.
- 🔑 Limitations include **subjectivity**, **static assumption**, and **inability to capture human factors**.
- 🔑 **Always quote units** of R in meters and conclude with classification.
- 🔑 **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** This topic addresses **CO2 (Apply hazard indices)** at Bloom Level **Apply/Analyze**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plant Layout and Unit Hazard Rating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plant Layout and Unit Hazard Rating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Plant Layout — Formal Definition

**Plant Layout** is the systematic physical arrangement of industrial facilities — including process equipment, storage tanks, piping, buildings, control rooms, utility systems, and emergency access routes — within a defined boundary, designed to achieve safe, efficient, and economical operation while minimizing the potential consequences of fire, explosion, and toxic release.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A safe plant layout is the **first line of defense** in inherent safety design. Even the best engineered controls (valves, relief systems, instruments) can fail; properly spaced units limit the propagation of damage (domino effect) and protect personnel and capital.

**Key Layout Objectives:**
- Minimization of accident propagation (domino effect)
- Safe separation between hazardous and non-hazardous units
- Adequate access for firefighting, emergency shutdown, and evacuation
- Compliance with regulatory spacing (OISD, NFPA, IS, TAC rules)
- Optimization of pipe runs, plot area, and operating cost

### 1.2 Unit Hazard Rating — Formal Definition

**Unit Hazard Rating** is a quantitative numerical score assigned to a process unit that expresses the *relative fire, explosion, and reactivity hazard* of that unit. The most universally used method is the **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)**, supplemented by the **Dow Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)** for toxic hazards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why quantify hazard?** A qualitative "high/medium/low" rating is too vague for risk-based decisions like insurance, spacing distances, and location of control rooms. A numerical F&EI allows direct comparison between units and prioritization of risk reduction measures.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

**Plant Layout Analogy — The Kitchen Analogy:**
Think of a **commercial restaurant kitchen**. The chef (operator) places the deep-fryer (high fire hazard) far from the ice-cream counter (low temperature hazard), the gas burners (heat source) are kept away from the flour-storage shelf (combustible dust), and the fire-extinguisher (mitigation) is mounted within arm's reach. Walkways are clear so a person can run if a pan catches fire. **A chemical plant follows the same logic — but with flammable hydrocarbons, toxic gases, and pressures of 50 bar instead of cooking oil.**

**Unit Hazard Rating Analogy — The Insurance Premium Analogy:**
When an insurance company insures your car, they don't just ask "is it a car?" — they ask *the make, model, engine size, safety features, and accident history*. The premium reflects a **numerical risk score**. The Dow F&EI does the same for process units: it rates the unit using a "hazard score" derived from the material, the process conditions, and the quantity involved. A higher score ⇒ a higher "insurance premium" in the form of stricter layout spacing, fire protection, and emergency planning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core metrics to remember:** Material Factor (**MF**), General Process Hazards (**GPH**), Special Process Hazards (**SPH**), Process Unit Hazard Factor (**F₃**), Fire and Explosion Index (**F&EI**), Damage Factor (**DF**), and Loss Control Credit Factor (**C₁**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Plant Layout — Types and Principles

#### 2.1.1 Types of Plant Layout

| Layout Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Process (Functional) Layout** | Equipment grouped by function (all reactors together, all storage together) | Multi-product batch plants, pharmaceuticals |
| **Product (Line) Layout** | Equipment arranged in sequence of process flow | Continuous petrochemical, refinery units |
| **Fixed-Position Layout** | Product stays in one place; equipment moves to it | Shipbuilding, very large fabrications |
| **Combined (Hybrid) Layout** | Mix of process and product layout | Most modern chemical/process plants |

#### 2.1.2 Principles of Safe Plant Layout (Kletz / OISD Guidelines)

1. **Separation of high-hazard units** from low-hazard, offices, and control rooms.
2. **Open-air (outdoor) construction** preferred over enclosed buildings for flammable materials.
3. **Dominant wind direction** — locate control rooms, fire stations, and personnel shelters **upwind** of major hazard units.
4. **Emergency access** — at least **two independent routes** into the plant.
5. **Drainage and spill containment** — graded floors leading to collection pits, dykes, and firewalls.
6. **Equipment spacing** — minimum inter-unit distances based on F&EI value and tank capacity.
7. **Fire-water reservoir and hydrant network** sized for the largest credible fire.
8. **Safe distance** for storage of liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), chlorine, ammonia per **OISD-118 / OISD-156**.
9. **Bund walls** around storage tanks of capacity **≥ 110% of the largest tank** within the dyke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key spacing reference (OISD-118 / NFPA 30):** For two adjacent atmospheric storage tanks of flammable liquids, the minimum inter-tank shell-to-shell distance is the **larger of** (a) the diameter of the larger tank, or (b) 10 m.

#### 2.1.3 Plant Layout Stages

1. **Block Diagram Layout** — major units shown as blocks; preliminary.
2. **General Layout** — actual equipment positions with rough dimensions.
3. **Detailed Layout** — pipe racks, cable trays, platforms, and instrument locations.
4. **Construction Layout** — foundation, column, and steel drawings.

### 2.2 Unit Hazard Rating — Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)

#### 2.2.1 Philosophy

The Dow F&EI was developed by the **Dow Chemical Company** in 1964 (last major revision: **Dow's Fire & Explosion Index Hazard Classification Guide, 7th/8th edition**). It rates the **relative fire and explosion hazard** of a process unit by combining material properties with process conditions.

#### 2.2.2 F&EI Calculation Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Identify the Process Unit**
Select the most hazardous unit (or section) to be evaluated. Process units are typically delineated by **major equipment boundaries** (e.g., a reactor + its feed drum + reflux drum + condenser).

**Step 2 — Determine the Material Factor (MF)**
A dimensionless number (typically **1 to 40**) based on the **NFPA 704 rating** (Nᵩ flammability, Nₕ reactivity) of the most hazardous material handled in significant quantity in the unit.

> [!NOTE]
> **MF selection rules:**
> - Use the material with the **highest MF**.
> - If a mixture, use the component with the highest MF.
> - For solids, use the MF corresponding to the dust layer or bulk solid rating.

**Step 3 — Calculate General Process Hazards (GPH)**
Six factors, each with a penalty range. Sum to obtain the **GPH factor** (typical range 1.0 – 6.0).

| GPH Factor | Penalty Range |
|---|---|
| 1. Exothermic chemical reactions | 0.30 – 1.25 |
| 2. Endothermic processes | 0.20 – 0.40 |
| 3. Material handling and transfer | 0.25 – 1.05 |
| 4. Enclosed or indoor process units | 0.25 – 0.90 |
| 5. Access (spill, emergency) | 0.20 – 0.35 |
| 6. Drainage and spill control | 0.25 – 0.50 |

**Step 4 — Calculate Special Process Hazards (SPH)**
Twelve factors, each with a penalty range. Sum to obtain the **SPH factor** (typical range 1.0 – 8.0).

| SPH Factor | Penalty Range |
|---|---|
| 1. Toxic materials | 0.20 – 0.80 |
| 2. Sub-atmospheric pressure | 0.20 – 0.50 |
| 3. Operation in or near flammable range | 0.20 – 1.50 |
| 4. Dust explosion | 0.20 – 2.00 |
| 5. Relief pressure (high pressure) | 0.10 – 1.20 |
| 6. Low temperature (brittle fracture) | 0.20 – 0.30 |
| 7. Quantity of flammable material | up to 5.00 |
| 8. Corrosion and erosion | 0.10 – 0.75 |
| 9. Leakage – joints and packing | 0.10 – 1.50 |
| 10. Use of fired heaters | 0.20 – 1.00 |
| 11. Hot oil heat exchange system | 0.15 – 1.15 |
| 12. Rotating equipment | 0.10 – 0.50 |

**Step 5 — Process Unit Hazard Factor (F₃)**
$$F_3 = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH}$$

**Step 6 — Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)**
$$\boxed{\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_3}$$

**Step 7 — Damage Factor (DF)**
Obtained from a chart correlating F&EI to DF (typical range **0.01 to 1.00**). Higher F&EI ⇒ higher DF.

**Step 8 — Radius of Exposure (R)**
The radius (in **feet**) of a circle within which property damage is expected if the worst-case event occurs:
$$R = 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI} \quad \text{(for F\&EI up to 100; for higher values a different coefficient applies)}$$

In metric: $R(\text{m}) = 0.256 \times \text{F\&EI}$ (approx.)

**Step 9 — Area of Exposure (A)**
$$A = \pi R^2$$

**Step 10 — Base Maximum Probable Property Damage (Base MPPD)**
$$\text{Base MPPD} = \text{Replacement value of equipment within Area of Exposure} \times \text{DF}$$

**Step 11 — Loss Control Credit Factor (C₁)**
$$C_1 = C_{\text{process control}} \times C_{\text{material isolation}} \times C_{\text{fire protection}}$$
Each sub-factor ranges from **0.84 to 0.98** (a credit of 2% to 16% per item).

**Step 12 — Actual Maximum Probable Property Damage (Actual MPPD)**
$$\text{Actual MPPD} = \text{Base MPPD} \times C_1$$

**Step 13 — Maximum Foreseeable Property Damage (MFPD)**
Includes damage outside the area of exposure and an escalation factor:
$$\text{MFPD} = \text{Actual MPPD} + 0.7 \times \text{Actual MPPD} = 1.7 \times \text{Actual MPPD}$$
(Some editions use a different factor; the 1.7 multiplier accounts for the 70% business-interruption / escalation allowance.)

**Step 14 — Days Outage**
Estimated business interruption time read from a chart correlating Actual MPPD ($) to days of plant shutdown.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Source | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| Material Factor | Read from NFPA 704 Nᵩ/Nₕ table | Dimensionless |
| Process Unit Hazard Factor | $F_3 = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH}$ | Dimensionless |
| **Fire and Explosion Index** | $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_3$ | Dimensionless |
| **Radius of Exposure (US units)** | $R = 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI}$ | **feet** |
| Radius of Exposure (metric) | $R = 0.256 \times \text{F\&EI}$ | **metres** |
| **Area of Exposure** | $A = \pi R^2$ | **m² or ft²** |
| **Base MPPD** | $\text{Base MPPD} = (\text{Value in area of exposure}) \times \text{DF}$ | **₹ / $** |
| **Loss Control Credit Factor** | $C_1 = C_{\text{PC}} \times C_{\text{MI}} \times C_{\text{FP}}$ | Dimensionless (≤ 1) |
| **Actual MPPD** | $\text{Actual MPPD} = \text{Base MPPD} \times C_1$ | **₹ / $** |
| **MFPD** | $\text{MFPD} = 1.7 \times \text{Actual MPPD}$ | **₹ / $** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **F&EI Severity Classification (Dow):**
> - F&EI = 1 – 60 → **Light**
> - F&EI = 61 – 96 → **Moderate**
> - F&EI = 97 – 127 → **Intermediate (Heavy)**
> - F&EI = 128 – 158 → **Heavy**
> - F&EI ≥ 159 → **Severe / Extra Heavy**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Insurance underwriting** (re-insurance companies like Munich Re, Swiss Re use Dow F&EI)
- **Plant layout decisions** (inter-unit distances are scaled with √F&EI in many codes)
- **Hazard classification of control-room location** (occupied buildings must lie outside the area of exposure)
- **Prioritization of risk-reduction projects** in process safety management (PSM)
- **Quantitative input** for Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) studies
- **Compliance** with OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119, SEVESO directives, and Indian Factories Act / Manufacture Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC, 1989)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

### 3.1 Worked Example: Dow F&EI Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem statement (typical KTU-style 14-mark numerical):**
> A **reactor process unit** handles **acetone** as the major flammable material. The total quantity of acetone in the unit is **6,800 kg**. The reactor operates at **6 bar (g)** and **50 °C** in an **open-air** structure. There is an **exothermic hydrogenation reaction**; **no fired heater**; mild **corrosion** by trace HCl; **gland packing** on the agitator shaft. Total **replacement value of equipment within the area of exposure = ₹ 50 crore**.
> **Loss control features:** Basic process control, Block valves, Fire water sprinkler + foam system.
> **Compute:** (a) Material Factor, (b) GPH, (c) SPH, (d) F&EI, (e) Radius of Exposure, (f) Base MPPD, (g) Actual MPPD, (h) MFPD.

---

#### Step 1 — Material Factor (MF)

Acetone NFPA 704 rating:
- Health (Nₕ) = 1
- Flammability (Nᵩ) = 3

From the Dow MF table (NFPA 704 lookup):

| Nᵩ / Nₕ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 |
| 1 | 4.00 | 4.00 | 4.00 | 4.00 | 4.00 |
| 2 | 10.00 | 10.00 | 10.00 | 10.00 | 10.00 |
| **3** | **16.00** | **16.00** | **16.00** | **16.00** | **16.00** |
| 4 | 21.00 | 21.00 | 21.00 | 21.00 | 21.00 |

For acetone, **MF = 16** (standard textbook value, also obtained from NFPA 704 lookup).

#### Step 2 — General Process Hazards (GPH)

| GPH Factor | Justification | Penalty |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Exothermic chemical reactions | Hydrogenation, exothermic | 0.50 |
| 2. Endothermic processes | None | 0.00 |
| 3. Material handling and transfer | Unloading acetone from tanker, transfer via pump | 0.50 |
| 4. Enclosed or indoor process units | **Open-air** structure | 0.00 |
| 5. Access | Adequate road access (≥ 2 ways) | 0.20 |
| 6. Drainage and spill control | Dyke & catch-pit provided | 0.25 |
| **Total GPH** | | **1.45** |

#### Step 3 — Special Process Hazards (SPH)

**Sub-step 3.1 — Quantity of flammable material (penalty from chart):**
Total mass of acetone = 6,800 kg. Heat of combustion = 28.5 MJ/kg. Total heat release = 6,800 × 28.5 = 1,93,800 MJ ≈ 1.94 × 10⁵ MJ.
From the Dow "heat of combustion × quantity" penalty curve → penalty ≈ **0.95**.

**Other SPH factors:**

| SPH Factor | Justification | Penalty |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Toxic materials | Acetone mildly toxic (TLV 750 ppm) | 0.20 |
| 2. Sub-atmospheric pressure | None | 0.00 |
| 3. Operation in/near flammable range | Acetone at 50 °C well below flash point (-20 °C), but operating above F.P. when released | 0.50 |
| 4. Dust explosion | Liquid only | 0.00 |
| 5. Relief pressure | 6 bar(g) → penalty | 0.30 |
| 6. Low temperature | None | 0.00 |
| 7. Quantity of flammable material | (calculated above) | 0.95 |
| 8. Corrosion and erosion | Mild HCl → < 0.5 mm/yr | 0.20 |
| 9. Leakage – joints and packing | Packed agitator shaft, flanged joints | 0.30 |
| 10. Use of fired heaters | None | 0.00 |
| 11. Hot oil heat exchange | None (steam heat) | 0.00 |
| 12. Rotating equipment | Centrifugal pump + agitator | 0.20 |
| **Total SPH** | | **2.65** |

#### Step 4 — Process Unit Hazard Factor (F₃)

$$F_3 = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH} = 1.45 + 2.65 = 4.10$$

#### Step 5 — Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{F\&EI} &= \text{MF} \times F_3 \\
&= 16 \times 4.10 \\
&= 65.6
\end{aligned}$$

**Severity classification:** F&EI = 65.6 → **Moderate** (range 61–96).

#### Step 6 — Radius of Exposure (R)

$$R = 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI} = 0.84 \times 65.6 = 55.1 \text{ feet}$$

In metric:
$$R = 0.256 \times 65.6 = 16.79 \text{ m}$$

#### Step 7 — Area of Exposure (A)

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi R^2 \\
&= \pi \times (16.79)^2 \\
&= \pi \times 281.9 \\
&= 885.5 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 8 — Damage Factor (DF)

From the Dow F&EI ↔ DF chart, for **F&EI = 65.6** → **DF ≈ 0.70** (read off the published chart).

#### Step 9 — Base MPPD

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Base MPPD} &= \text{Replacement value} \times \text{DF} \\
&= ₹ 50,00,00,000 \times 0.70 \\
&= ₹ 35,00,00,000 \; (₹ 35 \text{ crore})
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 10 — Loss Control Credit Factor (C₁)

| Credit Category | Items Provided | Credit Factor |
|---|---|---|
| Process Control (C_PC) | Basic DCS, alarms, interlocks, emergency shutdown | 0.88 |
| Material Isolation (C_MI) | Block valves, remote-operated isolation | 0.95 |
| Fire Protection (C_FP) | Hydrant + sprinkler + foam pourer + fire brigade | 0.90 |

$$C_1 = 0.88 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.7524$$

#### Step 11 — Actual MPPD

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual MPPD} &= \text{Base MPPD} \times C_1 \\
&= ₹ 35 \text{ crore} \times 0.7524 \\
&= ₹ 26.33 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 12 — Maximum Foreseeable Property Damage (MFPD)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MFPD} &= 1.7 \times \text{Actual MPPD} \\
&= 1.7 \times 26.33 \\
&= ₹ 44.77 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 13 — Days Outage (read from chart)

From the Actual MPPD = ₹ 26.33 crore → days of outage ≈ **15 days** (read off the Dow days-outage chart; typical KTU answer expected: state the method of reading from the chart).

---

### 3.2 Sample Plant Layout Spacing Calculation (Kletz Rule)

For two adjacent atmospheric storage tanks, the **minimum shell-to-shell distance** $D$ is:

$$D = \max\!\left(D_{\text{tank-1}},\, D_{\text{tank-2}},\, 10 \text{ m}\right)$$

where $D_{\text{tank-1}}, D_{\text{tank-2}}$ are the diameters of the two tanks.

**Example:** Tank A diameter = 12 m, Tank B diameter = 8 m.

$$D = \max(12, 8, 10) = 12 \text{ m}$$

Thus tanks must be separated by at least **12 m shell-to-shell**.

### 3.3 Plant Layout — Derivation of F&EI-based Separation Distance

If a process unit has **F&EI = X**, the conservative inter-unit distance to avoid domino effect is:

$$D_{\text{inter-unit}} \ge 0.256 \times X \; \text{(metres)} \quad \text{or} \quad D \ge 0.84 \times X \; \text{(feet)}$$

This is essentially the **radius of exposure** scaled to act as a separation distance — ensuring that the heat/explosion flux of one unit does not impinge on the adjacent unit's vessel wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering judgement:** Some codes (OISD-118) prescribe **fixed distances** that **override** the F&EI-based distances, especially for LPG and cryogenic storage. Always check the prevailing code first.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Block Layout of a Typical Process Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Raw Material Storage] --> A2[Feed Preparation]
    A2 --> A3[Reactor Section]
    A3 --> A4[Distillation Column]
    A4 --> A5[Product Storage]
    A3 -.emergency.-> A6[Safety Relief Flare]
    A4 -.vent.-> A6
    A5 --> A7[Loading Bay]
    C1[Control Room] -.upwind.-> A3
    C1 -.upwind.-> A4
    F1[Fire Water Reservoir] --> A3
    F1 --> A4
    F1 --> A5
    W1[ETP / Waste Treatment] -.downwind.-> A5
    subplot_line["Plot Boundary (fence)"]
    subplot_line -.- A1
    subplot_line -.- A3
    subplot_line -.- A7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Note the **dominating-wind direction** (assumed left → right). Control room (C1) is placed **upwind** of the reactor (A3) and column (A4). Fire-water reservoir (F1) is centrally located to minimize run length. Effluent treatment (W1) is **downwind**. This arrangement is the textbook "Kletz layout" for a hydrocarbon process plant.

### 4.2 Mermaid — Dow F&EI Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Select Process Unit] --> S1[Identify Most Hazardous Material]
    S1 --> S2[Read Material Factor MF from NFPA 704]
    S2 --> S3[Compute GPH sum of 6 factors]
    S3 --> S4[Compute SPH sum of 12 factors]
    S4 --> S5[F3 = GPH + SPH]
    S5 --> S6[F and EI = MF times F3]
    S6 --> S7[Read Damage Factor DF from F and EI chart]
    S7 --> S8[Compute Radius R = 0.84 times F and EI]
    S8 --> S9[Area of Exposure A = pi R squared]
    S9 --> S10[Base MPPD = Equipment Value times DF]
    S10 --> S11[Loss Control Credit C1]
    S11 --> S12[Actual MPPD = Base MPPD times C1]
    S12 --> S13[MFPD = 1.7 times Actual MPPD]
    S13 --> S14[Read Days Outage from chart]
    S14 --> S15[End: Risk Report]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Plant Layout Hierarchy (Block → Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Block Diagram Layout] --> L1[General Layout]
    L1 --> L2[Detailed Layout]
    L2 --> L3[Construction Drawing]
    L0 --- note1["Major units as rectangles; relative scale; spacing on grid 0-5 m"]
    L1 --- note2["Exact equipment footprints; pipe rack corridor; roadways"]
    L2 --- note3["Pipe rack elevations; cable trays; platforms; instruments"]
    L3 --- note4["Foundation, column, beam, RCC details"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid — Unit Hazard Rating Decision Tree (Severity Bands)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V0{F and EI value?} --> V1[1 to 60 Light]
    V0 --> V2[61 to 96 Moderate]
    V0 --> V3[97 to 127 Heavy]
    V0 --> V4[128 to 158 Very Heavy]
    V0 --> V5[Greater than or equal to 159 Severe]
    V1 --> R1[Normal precautions]
    V2 --> R2[Additional fire protection]
    V3 --> R3[Dedicated fire wall, larger spacing]
    V4 --> R4[Reactor blast resistant control room]
    V5 --> R5[Relocation or major risk reduction mandatory]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid — Loss Control Credit Factor Composition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Loss Control Credit Factor C1] --> C1A[Process Control CPC 0.84 to 0.98]
    C0 --> C1B[Material Isolation CMI 0.84 to 0.98]
    C0 --> C1C[Fire Protection CFP 0.84 to 0.98]
    C1A --> C1D[Example: 0.88]
    C1B --> C1E[Example: 0.95]
    C1C --> C1F[Example: 0.90]
    C1D --> C1G[C1 = 0.7524]
    C1E --> C1G
    C1F --> C1G
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term *Material Factor (MF)* as used in the Dow Fire and Explosion Index method. How is it determined for a process unit?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Material Factor (MF)** is a dimensionless number (ranging from **1 to 40**) that represents the intrinsic **fire and explosion energy** released by the most hazardous material handled in significant quantity within a process unit.

**Determination procedure:**
1. Identify the most hazardous material (highest Nᵩ flammability, Nₕ reactivity, and Nₛ instability ratings under NFPA 704).
2. For a mixture, use the component with the **highest MF**.
3. Read the MF directly from the **Dow NFPA 704 lookup table** (e.g., acetone Nᵩ = 3, Nₕ = 1 ⇒ MF = 16; gasoline ⇒ MF = 16; propane ⇒ MF = 21).
4. For dust-handling units, use the **dust explosion class** to determine MF.

> **Key point:** MF is the *only* factor in F&EI that depends purely on material properties — it is the foundation of the entire hazard score.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Differentiate between *General Process Hazards (GPH)* and *Special Process Hazards (SPH)* in the Dow F&EI method. List any three factors from each category.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | GPH | SPH |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Hazards that are **common to most process operations** and arise from the *general* nature of the unit | Hazards that are **specific** to the particular process, equipment design, or operating conditions |
| Number of factors | **6 factors** | **12 factors** |
| Typical range of penalty sum | 1.0 to 6.0 | 1.0 to 8.0 |
| Examples | Exothermic reaction, endothermic, material handling, enclosed unit, access, drainage | Toxicity, sub-atmospheric pressure, dust explosion, relief pressure, low temperature, corrosion, leakage, fired heater, hot oil, rotating equipment |

**Any three GPH factors (as above):** Exothermic reaction, Material handling, Enclosed unit.
**Any three SPH factors (as above):** Toxic materials, Dust explosion, Corrosion.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Adapted]**
**(a)** Describe the **principles of safe plant layout** as recommended by **OISD and Kletz**. List and explain **any five** of these principles. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A process unit handles **ethyl alcohol** as the major flammable material. Total inventory in the unit = **8,500 kg**. Operating pressure = **5 bar(g)**, temperature = **78 °C**, open-air construction. There is an exothermic esterification reaction, mild corrosion, gland-packed agitator, and centrifugal transfer pump. Total replacement value of equipment in the area of exposure = **₹ 60 crore**. Loss-control features: DCS + interlocks, block valves, hydrant + foam system. Calculate: **(i)** MF, **(ii)** GPH, **(iii)** SPH, **(iv)** F&EI, **(v)** Radius of Exposure (m), **(vi)** Base MPPD, **(vii)** Actual MPPD, **(viii)** MFPD. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Question A)

**(a) Principles of Safe Plant Layout — OISD / Kletz (7 marks):**

1. **Segregation of high-hazard units from low-hazard units** — Flammable storage, reactors, and compressors must be separated from offices, canteens, and the control room by a minimum inter-unit distance (Kletz: at least the radius of exposure of the larger unit).
2. **Open-air construction preferred** — Avoid enclosed buildings for flammable processes; outdoor layout disperses leaks and prevents vapour cloud accumulation.
3. **Dominant wind direction** — Locate the **control room, fire station, and personnel assembly points upwind** of hazardous units, and locate the flare, ETP, and **storage downwind**.
4. **Multiple emergency access roads** — At least **two independent routes** must enter each process area to allow fire engines and ambulances in case one is blocked.
5. **Bund walls and dykes** — All bulk storage tanks for flammable liquids must be inside a **bund (dyke)** whose capacity is **at least 110% of the largest tank's volume** within the dyke.
6. **Adequate fire-water network** — Ring-main hydrants spaced every 30 m near process units, with water reservoir sized for **4–6 hours** of the largest credible fire.
7. **Safe distance from populated areas** — As per OISD-118 / IS 1446, minimum safe distances for LPG, ammonia, and chlorine storage from public buildings.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Material Factor:**
Ethanol NFPA 704 → Nᵩ = 3, Nₕ = 0 (or 1) ⇒ **MF = 16** (standard Dow value for ethanol).

**(ii) GPH:**

| GPH Factor | Penalty |
|---|---|
| 1. Exothermic reaction (esterification) | 0.50 |
| 2. Endothermic | 0.00 |
| 3. Material handling & transfer (tanker → pump → reactor) | 0.50 |
| 4. Enclosed (open-air) | 0.00 |
| 5. Access (adequate) | 0.20 |
| 6. Drainage / dyke provided | 0.25 |
| **Total GPH** | **1.45** |

**(iii) SPH:**

| SPH Factor | Penalty |
|---|---|
| 1. Toxic (ethanol, mild) | 0.20 |
| 3. Operation near flammable range (78 °C, F.P. = 13 °C, releases flash) | 0.50 |
| 5. Relief pressure 5 bar(g) | 0.30 |
| 7. Quantity penalty (8,500 kg ethanol → ~ 2.4 × 10⁵ MJ) | 1.00 |
| 8. Corrosion (mild) | 0.20 |
| 9. Leakage (gland packing, flanges) | 0.30 |
| 12. Rotating equipment (pump, agitator) | 0.20 |
| All others | 0.00 |
| **Total SPH** | **2.70** |

**(iv) F&EI:**
$$F_3 = 1.45 + 2.70 = 4.15$$
$$\text{F\&EI} = 16 \times 4.15 = 66.4 \; \Rightarrow \; \textbf{Moderate}$$

**(v) Radius of Exposure:**
$$R = 0.256 \times 66.4 = 17.0 \text{ m}$$

**(vi) Base MPPD:**
Read DF from chart for F&EI = 66.4 ⇒ **DF ≈ 0.71**
$$\text{Base MPPD} = 60 \text{ crore} \times 0.71 = 42.6 \text{ crore}$$

**(vii) Loss Control Credit:**
$$C_1 = 0.88 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.7524$$
$$\text{Actual MPPD} = 42.6 \times 0.7524 = 32.05 \text{ crore}$$

**(viii) MFPD:**
$$\text{MFPD} = 1.7 \times 32.05 = 54.49 \text{ crore}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper Adapted]**
**(a)** Explain the **various types of plant layouts** with neat sketches. Compare their **advantages and limitations** for chemical process industries. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **step-by-step procedure** for calculating the **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)**. State the **severity classification** of F&EI values. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Question B)

**(a) Types of Plant Layouts (7 marks):**

1. **Process (Functional) Layout** — Equipment grouped by *function* (all reactors in one block, all columns in another). Suited to **multi-product batch plants** (pharma, specialty chemicals). **Advantage:** flexibility for product changeover. **Limitation:** longer pipe runs, more inventory in transit, higher material loss.

2. **Product (Line) Layout** — Equipment arranged in *sequence of process flow*. Suited to **continuous high-volume plants** (refineries, petrochemicals). **Advantage:** short pipe runs, low work-in-process inventory, easy material balance. **Limitation:** inflexible; one equipment failure halts the entire line.

3. **Fixed-Position Layout** — The *product* remains in one place; tools, machines, and labour are brought to it. Suited to **ship-building, large fabrications**. **Advantage:** no movement of bulky item. **Limitation:** very limited applicability to chemical plants.

4. **Combined (Hybrid) Layout** — A pragmatic mix of process and product layouts. Most **modern chemical plants** adopt this. **Advantage:** balances flexibility with throughput. **Limitation:** more complex to design and maintain.

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3 for a hierarchical view of how the layout evolves from block to construction drawings.)*

**(b) Dow F&EI Procedure (7 marks):**

The stepwise procedure (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2) is:

1. **Select process unit** for evaluation.
2. **Identify the most hazardous material** and read the **Material Factor (MF)** from NFPA 704.
3. **Compute GPH** (sum of 6 penalty factors).
4. **Compute SPH** (sum of 12 penalty factors).
5. **Process Unit Hazard Factor** $F_3 = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH}$.
6. **Fire and Explosion Index** $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_3$.
7. **Read Damage Factor (DF)** from the F&EI ↔ DF chart.
8. **Compute Radius of Exposure** $R = 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI}$ (feet) or $0.256 \times \text{F\&EI}$ (metres).
9. **Compute Area of Exposure** $A = \pi R^2$.
10. **Base MPPD** = (Replacement value of equipment within area of exposure) × DF.
11. **Loss Control Credit Factor** $C_1 = C_{\text{PC}} \times C_{\text{MI}} \times C_{\text{FP}}$.
12. **Actual MPPD** = Base MPPD × $C_1$.
13. **MFPD** = 1.7 × Actual MPPD.
14. **Days Outage** = read from chart correlating Actual MPPD to business interruption days.

**Severity Classification of F&EI (Dow):**

| F&EI Range | Severity |
|---|---|
| 1 – 60 | **Light** |
| 61 – 96 | **Moderate** |
| 97 – 127 | **Heavy (Intermediate)** |
| 128 – 158 | **Very Heavy** |
| ≥ 159 | **Severe / Extra Heavy** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where students typically lose marks:**
> 1. **Using the wrong MF** — Students often pick the highest *Health* rating material instead of the highest combined Nᵩ + Nₕ (Material Factor basis).
> 2. **Confusing GPH with SPH** — "Quantity of flammable material" is an **SPH** factor, **not** a GPH factor. Mixing them up is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting to add the "1.0 base"** — In some Dow editions, the GPH and SPH subtotals **include an implicit 1.0 base**. Students must check which edition the question refers to.
> 4. **Wrong radius formula** — $0.84 \times \text{F\&EI}$ gives feet; **not metres**. For metres, use $0.256 \times \text{F\&EI}$. Mixing the two is a common 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Skipping the loss-control credit step** — Many students compute Base MPPD and stop. KTU explicitly asks for **Actual MPPD and MFPD** — a complete answer needs all three MPPD values.
> 6. **Reporting F&EI as "severe" without a chart reference** — Always state the **F&EI range** (e.g., "Moderate, as 61 ≤ 66.4 ≤ 96"), not a subjective adjective.
> 7. **Plant layout answers lacking direction-of-wind consideration** — A 7-mark layout answer that omits **dominant wind direction** and **domino-effect spacing** loses at least 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Plant Layout = first layer of inherent safety.** Even if every instrument fails, a good layout contains the damage.
- [ ] **Four types of layout:** Process, Product, Fixed-Position, Combined. Chemical plants usually use **Combined**.
- [ ] **OISD-118** governs hydrocarbon storage spacing; **OISD-156** governs LPG; **NFPA 30** governs flammable liquids.
- [ ] **Bund (dyke) capacity ≥ 110% of largest tank** within the dyke.
- [ ] **Minimum 2 emergency access roads** to every process area.
- [ ] **Control room, fire station = upwind**; **flare, ETP, storage = downwind**.
- [ ] **Inter-tank shell-to-shell distance = max(diameter of larger tank, 10 m)**.
- [ ] **Material Factor (MF)** is the *only* factor dependent on material alone — read from **NFPA 704** (Nᵩ flammability, Nₕ reactivity).
- [ ] **GPH has 6 factors; SPH has 12 factors.** Both are *penalties* (additive).
- [ ] **F₃ = GPH + SPH; F&EI = MF × F₃.** Memorize this two-line formula.
- [ ] **F&EI severity bands:** 1–60 Light, 61–96 Moderate, 97–127 Heavy, 128–158 Very Heavy, ≥159 Severe.
- [ ] **Radius of Exposure:** $R = 0.84 \times \text{F\&EI}$ (ft) **or** $0.256 \times \text{F\&EI}$ (m).
- [ ] **Area of Exposure:** $A = \pi R^2$.
- [ ] **Base MPPD = (Value in area of exposure) × DF.** **Actual MPPD = Base MPPD × C₁.** **MFPD = 1.7 × Actual MPPD.**
- [ ] **Loss control credit** $C_1 = C_{\text{PC}} \times C_{\text{MI}} \times C_{\text{FP}}$ (each between 0.84 and 0.98).
- [ ] **Days of Outage** is read from a chart — not calculated. Always state the method.
- [ ] **CEI (Chemical Exposure Index)** is the *companion* method for **toxic** hazards — use it **alongside** F&EI.
- [ ] **Mond Index** is the *variant* for *non-specific* multi-product plants — but F&EI is still the **default** for KTU answers.
- [ ] **In KTU exams:** always show **units** (m or ft for R, ₹ or $ for MPPD), always show the **F&EI severity band**, and always state the **assumptions** made for each GPH/SPH penalty.

> **One-line memory hook:** *"MF × (GPH + SPH) ⇒ F&EI ⇒ DF × Value ⇒ MPPD ⇒ multiply by C₁ ⇒ Actual MPPD ⇒ × 1.7 ⇒ MFPD."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preliminary Hazard Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Preliminary Hazard Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is a structured, qualitative, inductive (bottom-up) hazard identification and risk assessment technique performed during the **early conceptual or preliminary design phase** of a system, facility, process, or product. It systematically identifies potential hazards, their causal mechanisms, worst-case outcomes, severity levels, probability of occurrence, and recommended corrective actions *before* detailed design information becomes available.

The PHA method was originally developed for the U.S. Army (Military Standard – MIL-STD-882) and adopted by aerospace, nuclear, chemical, and process industries worldwide as the **first-pass safety review tool** of any engineering project.

**Key Standard Reference Terminology:**
- **Hazard** – Any real or potential condition that can cause injury, illness, death, or damage to equipment/property/environment.
- **Cause** – The initiating event or condition that produces the hazard.
- **Effect / Consequence** – The worst credible outcome resulting from the hazard.
- **Severity (I–IV)** – A qualitative measure of the worst credible mishap consequence.
- **Probability (A–E)** – A qualitative measure of the likelihood of occurrence.
- **Risk** – A combined function of **Severity** and **Probability**.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping: PHA is covered under **CO1 – Demonstrate the fundamental concepts of Hazard Identification.** It forms the *anchor concept* upon which HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, and What-If Analysis are layered in later modules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are about to cook a new, complex recipe for the first time in your kitchen. Before touching the stove, you mentally walk through every step and ask yourself:
- *"What can burn me?"* (stove, hot oil, steam)
- *"What can cut me?"* (sharp knives, blender blades)
- *"What can poison me?"* (raw meat, allergens, expired items)
- *"What can cause a fire?"* (grease, unattended gas)
- *"How bad would each be?"* (minor burn vs. major fire)
- *"How likely is it?"* (rare vs. very likely)

You mentally list each hazard, its cause, its worst outcome, and how dangerous it might be — **without yet having detailed measurements, flow rates, or thermostat calibrations**. This early, "rough-cut", checklist-style mental screening is exactly what **Preliminary Hazard Analysis** does for engineering systems.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor:** Think of PHA as the *"First-aid pre-check"* of system safety — fast, cheap, broad, and done when the design is still a **sketch on paper**, not a finished blueprint.

### 1.3 Governing Standards & Standardized Metrics (Bold Emphasis)

Per **MIL-STD-882E** (the global benchmark for PHA categorization adopted in industry and frequently cited in KTU board exams):

| Metric | Standardized Categories |  |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard Severity Categories** | **I – Catastrophic**, **II – Critical**, **III – Marginal**, **IV – Negligible** |  |
| **Hazard Probability Levels** | **A – Frequent**, **B – Probable**, **C – Occasional**, **D – Remote**, **E – Improbable** |  |
| **Risk Assessment Code (RAC)** | **RAC 1 (Highest)** through **RAC 5 (Lowest)** |  |
| **Hazard Resolution Timeline** | **Catastrophic (I)** must be eliminated before operational use; **Critical (II)** before operational use; **Marginal (III)** acceptable with management approval |  |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind PHA – Engineering Rationale

Engineering projects have a fundamental economic reality: **the cost of fixing a hazard increases roughly tenfold with each design phase it passes through**. A hazard caught at the conceptual stage might cost **₹1,000** to redesign; the same hazard caught after plant commissioning can cost **₹1 Crore or more** to retrofit.

PHA exists because:
1. **Earlier = Cheaper** – PHA operates at the conceptual phase when design changes are nearly free.
2. **Broader than detailed analyses** – HAZOP needs P&IDs; FMEA needs component lists; FTA needs known top events. PHA needs *only the system description*.
3. **Sets the safety baseline** – The RAC (Risk Assessment Code) matrix generated in PHA becomes the *acceptance threshold* against which later detailed analyses are judged.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of PHA

The PHA procedure follows a strictly sequential logic, typically executed across **3–5 working sessions** by a multidisciplinary team:

- **Step 1 – Define the System Boundary & Scope.**
  *Why:* PHA cannot proceed until engineers agree on *what is being studied* (a reactor, a UAV, a pharmaceutical plant batch process). Boundary drawings and functional descriptions are prepared.
- **Step 2 – Assemble a Multidisciplinary Team.**
  *Why:* Safety blind spots come from single-discipline thinking. Typical team: design engineer + process/systems engineer + safety engineer + operations/maintenance expert + human-factors specialist.
- **Step 3 – Identify Hazards (Brainstorming + Checklist + System Walkthrough).**
  *Why:* Hazards are listed by considering *energy sources* (electrical, mechanical, thermal, chemical, radiation, kinetic, potential, biological), *environmental factors*, and *human factors*.
- **Step 4 – Determine Causes and Worst-Credible Effects.**
  *Why:* Each hazard is decomposed into (a) initiating cause and (b) terminal consequence using the *Why–Then–So What* logic chain.
- **Step 5 – Assign Severity (I–IV) and Probability (A–E).**
  *Why:* These two qualitative descriptors feed the Risk Assessment Matrix.
- **Step 6 – Compute the Risk Assessment Code (RAC).**
  *Why:* The RAC consolidates severity and probability into a single actionable priority number.
- **Step 7 – Recommend Corrective / Mitigation Actions.**
  *Why:* Every hazard with RAC ≤ 3 (i.e., higher risk) must receive an explicit mitigation action, owner, and verification method.
- **Step 8 – Document in the PHA Worksheet.**
  *Why:* The PHA worksheet is the legal, auditable deliverable.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Quantity | Expression or Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk (Qualitative PHA) | $R = f(S,\;P)$ where $S$ = Severity, $P$ = Probability | Dimensionless RAC index |
| 2 | Severity Categories (MIL-STD-882E) | I – Catastrophic, II – Critical, III – Marginal, IV – Negligible | Ordinal scale |
| 3 | Probability Categories (MIL-STD-882E) | A – Frequent, B – Probable, C – Occasional, D – Remote, E – Improbable | Ordinal scale |
| 4 | Risk Assessment Code (RAC) Lookup | RAC 1 = $\vert S \times P \vert$ highest combined; RAC 5 = lowest | Discrete code 1–5 |
| 5 | Hazard Resolution Priority | Higher Severity ⇒ Earlier Resolution (I before II before III) | Pre-operational requirement |
| 6 | Cost-of-Change Rule-of-Thumb | $C_{n+1} \approx 10 \times C_n$ per design phase | Engineering heuristic |
| 7 | Team Size Heuristic | Typically **5–8** subject-matter experts | Cross-functional |
| 8 | PHA Duration Heuristic | 2–6 workshops × 2–4 hours each | Phase: Conceptual Design |
| 9 | Forms of Energy Checklist | Electrical, Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical, Radiation, Kinetic, Potential, Biological | KTA / KTU mnemonic |
| 10 | Output Deliverable | PHA Worksheet (Hazard, Cause, Effect, Severity, Probability, RAC, Recommendation) | Auditable document |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PHA is the **mandatory first step** in:
- **Aerospace:** Pre-flight safety review of any new UAV or spacecraft subsystem (NASA, ISRO, DRDO follow MIL-STD-882 derivations).
- **Nuclear Power:** Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR) — a regulatory precursor to operational licensing.
- **Chemical Process Industries:** Pre-HAZOP screening; required by OSHA 1910.119 (PSM) and Indian Factories Act for new plants.
- **Automotive & EV Design:** ISO 26262 early hazard screening of functional safety concepts.
- **Pharmaceutical & Biotech:** Pre-design GMP facility safety review before commissioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Note:** When examiners ask *"Why is PHA done before HAZOP?"*, the model answer must emphasize **(a) earlier stage = cheaper fix, (b) HAZOP requires P&ID maturity that does not yet exist, (c) PHA establishes the baseline risk acceptability criteria.** Missing any one of these three costs marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Construction of the Standard 5×4 Risk Assessment Matrix (RAM)

The standard PHA Risk Assessment Matrix (also called the **Hazard Risk Matrix**) is a 4-column (Severity) × 5-row (Probability) lookup table. We will derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 – Define the Severity Axis (Columns).**
MIL-STD-882E defines four discrete, mutually exclusive severity classes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{IV} &: \text{Negligible} \quad \rightarrow \text{Minor injury, negligible equipment damage} \\
S_{III} &: \text{Marginal} \quad \rightarrow \text{Minor injury, minor system damage, recoverable} \\
S_{II} &: \text{Critical} \quad \rightarrow \text{Severe injury, severe system damage, mission abort} \\
S_{I} &: \text{Catastrophic} \quad \rightarrow \text{Death, loss of system, unacceptable loss}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Define the Probability Axis (Rows).**
Five ordered qualitative likelihood bands:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{E} &: \text{Improbable} \quad \rightarrow \text{Such that it may never occur in system lifetime} \\
P_{D} &: \text{Remote} \quad \rightarrow \text{Unlikely but possible to occur in lifetime} \\
P_{C} &: \text{Occasional} \quad \rightarrow \text{Likely to occur several times in lifetime} \\
P_{B} &: \text{Probable} \quad \rightarrow \text{Likely to occur often} \\
P_{A} &: \text{Frequent} \quad \rightarrow \text{Likely to occur frequently, almost expected}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Compose the Matrix via the Risk Function.**
Risk is a monotonically increasing function of both severity and probability. The convention adopted by MIL-STD-882E is the discrete Risk Assessment Code:

$$
\text{RAC}(S,P) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } S \in \{I\} \;\text{or}\; (S \in \{II\} \wedge P \in \{A,B\}) \\ 2 & \text{if } S = II \wedge P = C \;\text{or}\; S = III \wedge P = A \\ 3 & \text{if } S = II \wedge P = D \;\text{or}\; S = III \wedge P = B \\ 4 & \text{if } S = III \wedge P = C,D \;\text{or}\; S = IV \wedge P = A,B \\ 5 & \text{if } S = III \wedge P = E \;\text{or}\; S = IV \wedge P = C,D,E \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 – The Standard 5×4 PHA Risk Assessment Matrix.**

| Probability ↓ \ Severity → | I – Catastrophic | II – Critical | III – Marginal | IV – Negligible |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A – Frequent**    | **RAC 1** | **RAC 1** | RAC 2 | RAC 3 |
| **B – Probable**     | **RAC 1** | **RAC 1** | RAC 2 | RAC 3 |
| **C – Occasional**   | **RAC 1** | RAC 2 | RAC 3 | RAC 4 |
| **D – Remote**       | RAC 2 | RAC 3 | RAC 4 | RAC 5 |
| **E – Improbable**   | RAC 3 | RAC 4 | RAC 5 | RAC 5 |

**Step 5 – Decision Rules for Each RAC.**

$$
\text{Decision}(\text{RAC}) = \begin{cases}
\text{Must eliminate} & \text{if RAC} = 1 \\
\text{Must mitigate before operational use} & \text{if RAC} = 2 \\
\text{Desirable to mitigate; accept with review} & \text{if RAC} = 3 \\
\text{Accept; manage by SOP} & \text{if RAC} = 4 \\
\text{Accept; no action} & \text{if RAC} = 5
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example – PHA of a Domestic LPG Cylinder System

We construct one complete PHA row so that the full template is memorizable for the exam.

**System:** Domestic LPG cylinder + regulator + rubber tube + gas stove.

**Step 1 – Boundary:** From cylinder valve to burner nozzle.

**Step 2 – Team:** Safety engineer, gas distributor, civil user, design engineer.

**Step 3–6 – Hazard row:**

| Hazard | Cause | Effect | Severity | Probability | RAC | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LPG leakage | Tube crack / valve failure | Fire / explosion | **I** | **C** | **1** | Install excess-flow valve + leak detector + auto-shut-off |
| Burner backfire | Clogged jet | Hand burn | **III** | **B** | **2** | Periodic jet cleaning SOP |
| Incomplete combustion | Poor ventilation | CO poisoning | **I** | **D** | **2** | Mandatory kitchen vent; CO detector |
| Cylinder drop | Improper handling | Mechanical injury | **III** | **C** | **3** | Wall-mounted clamp + SOP |

**Logical conversion:** Severity column is read first (I beats III) ⇒ RAC priority propagates accordingly. Notice how *LPG leakage* receives the highest priority RAC 1 because both its consequence and likelihood are unacceptably high.

### 3.3 Theoretical Justification of the 10× Cost-of-Change Rule

$$
C_{n+1} = 10 \times C_n \quad \text{where } n = 0,1,2,\ldots
$$

Let $C_0$ = cost to fix at conceptual phase. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 10 \times C_0 \quad (\text{detailed design phase}) \\
C_2 &= 10 \times C_1 = 10^2 \times C_0 \quad (\text{construction phase}) \\
C_3 &= 10 \times C_2 = 10^3 \times C_0 \quad (\text{commissioning phase}) \\
C_4 &= 10 \times C_3 = 10^4 \times C_0 \quad (\text{operational phase})
\end{aligned}
$$

This exponential growth is the *quantitative justification* for conducting PHA as early as possible. If $C_0$ = ₹1,000, then $C_4$ = ₹1,00,00,000 — a 10,000-fold penalty for late hazard discovery.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End PHA Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start PHA Study"] --> B["Define System Boundary and Scope"]
    B --> C["Assemble Multidisciplinary Team 5 to 8 members"]
    C --> D["Gather System Description and Energy Inventory"]
    D --> E["Identify Hazards via Brainstorm Checklist Walkthrough"]
    E --> F["Determine Cause and Worst Credible Effect per Hazard"]
    F --> G["Assign Severity I to IV per MIL STD 882"]
    G --> H["Assign Probability A to E per MIL STD 882"]
    H --> I["Lookup Risk Assessment Code RAC 1 to 5"]
    I --> J{"RAC 1 or 2?"}
    J -- Yes --> K["Mandatory Mitigation Required"]
    J -- No --> L["Accept with Review or SOP"]
    K --> M["Define Recommendation Owner and Verification"]
    L --> M
    M --> N["Document in PHA Worksheet"]
    N --> O["Management Sign Off and Closure"]
    O --> P["End PHA Study"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture – Position of PHA in the Safety Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Conceptual Design"] --> B["PHA Module 1 KTU"]
    B --> C["Preliminary Design"]
    C --> D["HAZOP Module 2 KTU"]
    C --> E["FMEA Module 3 KTU"]
    D --> F["Detailed Design"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["FTA Module 4 KTU"]
    G --> H["Construction and Commissioning"]
    H --> I["Operational Phase and Audits"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram – PHA Worksheet Column Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["Column 1 Hazard ID"] --> C2["Column 2 Cause"]
    C2 --> C3["Column 3 Effect"]
    C3 --> C4["Column 4 Severity I to IV"]
    C4 --> C5["Column 5 Probability A to E"]
    C5 --> C6["Column 6 RAC 1 to 5"]
    C6 --> C7["Column 7 Recommendation"]
    C7 --> C8["Column 8 Verification Owner"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram – Energy-Source Hazard Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E["Energy Sources in PHA Checklist"] --> E1["Electrical"]
    E --> E2["Mechanical"]
    E --> E3["Thermal"]
    E --> E4["Chemical"]
    E --> E5["Radiation"]
    E --> E6["Kinetic"]
    E --> E7["Potential Gravitational"]
    E --> E8["Biological"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA). State when in a project lifecycle PHA is conducted.*

**Model Answer:**
> **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is a qualitative, inductive hazard identification and risk assessment technique conducted during the **early conceptual or preliminary design phase** of a system, when detailed design data such as P&IDs or component lists are not yet available. PHA identifies potential hazards, their causes, worst-credible effects, severity (I–IV), probability (A–E), Risk Assessment Code (RAC), and recommends corrective actions — all documented in a structured PHA worksheet as per **MIL-STD-882E**.

**(3 marks – definition 2 marks, phase 1 mark)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*List the four severity categories and five probability categories used in PHA as per MIL-STD-882E.*

**Model Answer:**
> **Severity Categories:** I – Catastrophic, II – Critical, III – Marginal, IV – Negligible.
> **Probability Categories:** A – Frequent, B – Probable, C – Occasional, D – Remote, E – Improbable.
> The Risk Assessment Code (RAC 1–5) is obtained by combining these two axes in the standard 5×4 Risk Assessment Matrix.

**(3 marks – severity 1½ marks, probability 1½ marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure for conducting a Preliminary Hazard Analysis. Mention the team composition and the information inputs required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct the standard 5×4 PHA Risk Assessment Matrix as per MIL-STD-882E. For each Risk Assessment Code (RAC 1–5), state the recommended decision action. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step PHA Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Define System Boundary and Scope (1 mark):** Clearly state the functional limits of the system under study. Include inputs, outputs, interfaces, operating modes, and environmental envelopes.

2. **Assemble a Multidisciplinary Team (1 mark):** Typical team size is **5–8 members** drawn from design engineering, process engineering, safety engineering, operations and maintenance, and human-factors. A facilitator leads the PHA.

3. **Gather Preliminary Information (1 mark):** System description, intended use, energy sources, materials, applicable codes and standards, prior incident history.

4. **Identify Hazards (1 mark):** Use brainstorming guided by checklist of **energy sources (electrical, mechanical, thermal, chemical, radiation, kinetic, gravitational, biological)** and review of similar past systems.

5. **Determine Cause and Worst-Credible Effect (1 mark):** For each hazard, perform the *Why–Then–So What* chain: Why does it exist? Then what initiates it? So what is the worst credible outcome?

6. **Assign Severity (I–IV) and Probability (A–E) (1 mark):** As per MIL-STD-882E standardized descriptors.

7. **Compute RAC and Document Recommendations (1 mark):** Populate the PHA worksheet columns: Hazard ID, Cause, Effect, Severity, Probability, RAC, Recommendation, Owner, Verification.

**(b) Risk Assessment Matrix and RAC Decision Rules (7 marks):**

| Probability ↓ \ Severity → | I – Catastrophic | II – Critical | III – Marginal | IV – Negligible |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A – Frequent**    | **RAC 1** | **RAC 1** | RAC 2 | RAC 3 |
| **B – Probable**     | **RAC 1** | **RAC 1** | RAC 2 | RAC 3 |
| **C – Occasional**   | **RAC 1** | RAC 2 | RAC 3 | RAC 4 |
| **D – Remote**       | RAC 2 | RAC 3 | RAC 4 | RAC 5 |
| **E – Improbable**   | RAC 3 | RAC 4 | RAC 5 | RAC 5 |

**Decision Rules (3 marks for these):**
- **RAC 1:** Must be eliminated or controlled to RAC 2/3 before operational use.
- **RAC 2:** Must be mitigated before operational use; senior management acceptance required.
- **RAC 3:** Desirable to mitigate; acceptable with management approval and SOP.
- **RAC 4:** Accept with routine procedures; track through audits.
- **RAC 5:** Accept; no action required beyond documentation.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Discuss the advantages and limitations of Preliminary Hazard Analysis. Compare PHA with HAZOP and FMEA on at least five parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A proposed chemical process uses **hydrogen gas at 30 bar pressure and 200 °C**. Conduct a PHA and identify at least **six hazards**, classify them using MIL-STD-882E severity (I–IV) and probability (A–E), determine the RAC, and recommend mitigation. Present the answer in the form of a PHA worksheet. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PHA Advantages, Limitations, and Comparison (7 marks):**

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- Conducted early → cheapest to fix hazards.
- Requires minimum design data → can begin at conceptual stage.
- Provides baseline risk criteria for all later detailed analyses.
- Inexpensive and fast (typically 2–6 workshops).
- Encourages cross-functional safety thinking.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- Qualitative only — no quantitative probability or risk numbers.
- Highly dependent on team experience (subjectivity).
- Cannot replace detailed methods (HAZOP, FMEA, FTA).
- May miss hazards not on the brainstorm checklist.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | PHA | HAZOP | FMEA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase** | Conceptual / preliminary | Detailed design | Detailed design |
| **Type** | Inductive (bottom-up) | Inductive, keyword-based | Inductive, component-level |
| **Data needed** | System description | P&IDs, line specs | Component list, failure rates |
| **Output** | Hazard list + RAC | Deviations + causes + consequences | Failure modes + RPN |
| **Team size** | 5–8 | 6–10 | 4–8 |
| **Qualitative/Quantitative** | Qualitative | Qualitative | Semi-quantitative (with RPN) |

**(b) PHA Worksheet for Hydrogen System (7 marks):**

| # | Hazard | Cause | Effect | Severity | Probability | RAC | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | H₂ leakage | Pipe flange failure | Fire / explosion | **I** | **C** | **1** | Use welded joints + hydrogen leak detectors + flame arrestors |
| 2 | H₂–air mixture explosion | Valve misalignment | Vessel rupture | **I** | **D** | **2** | Double-block-and-bleed valves; inert purge before startup |
| 3 | High-temperature burns | Uninsulated 200 °C pipe | Personnel burn injury | **III** | **B** | **2** | Install thermal insulation + warning signage |
| 4 | Over-pressurization (30 bar) | Failed PSV | Vessel rupture | **I** | **D** | **2** | Redundant PSV + rupture disc + SIL-rated pressure transmitter |
| 5 | Embrittlement of steel | Long H₂ exposure | Crack propagation | **III** | **C** | **3** | Use austenitic stainless steel; periodic NDT |
| 6 | Static electricity ignition | Inadequate grounding | H₂ ignition | **I** | **D** | **2** | Bonding + grounding straps + intrinsically safe instruments |
| 7 | Asphyxiation in confined space | H₂ leak in enclosed room | Personnel asphyxia | **II** | **D** | **3** | Continuous H₂ + O₂ detectors; ventilation redundancy |

*(Examiner note: awarding full 7 marks requires at least six rows, each row containing all eight columns. Half mark deducted per incomplete column.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in PHA Questions:**
> 1. **Stating the wrong phase.** PHA is done at *conceptual/preliminary design*, NOT during detailed design or operations. Wrong phase = −1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing PHA with HAZOP.** PHA does NOT use guidewords (No/More/As Well As…). Using guidewords in a PHA answer signals conceptual confusion.
> 3. **Omitting Probability category.** Many students write only Severity and RAC. The Probability column (A–E) is mandatory and carries separate marks.
> 4. **Writing RAC without action.** RAC alone is incomplete; examiners expect the *decision rule* (eliminate/mitigate/accept) attached to each RAC code.
> 5. **Single-discipline team.** Stating "the safety engineer does PHA alone" is wrong — PHA is fundamentally *multidisciplinary*.
> 6. **Quantitative claims.** PHA is qualitative; quoting numerical failure rates is a category error. Save numbers for FTA.
> 7. **Skipping the "Recommendation Owner".** Each mitigation action must have a named owner and verification method.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PHA = Preliminary Hazard Analysis**, an **inductive, qualitative** hazard identification method.
- Conducted in the **conceptual / preliminary design phase**, before P&IDs are finalized.
- Governed by the **MIL-STD-882E** standard (most-cited reference in KTU answers).
- Severity: **I Catastrophic | II Critical | III Marginal | IV Negligible**.
- Probability: **A Frequent | B Probable | C Occasional | D Remote | E Improbable**.
- Output: **Risk Assessment Code (RAC 1 to 5)** from the 5×4 matrix.
- RAC 1 → eliminate; RAC 2 → mitigate before use; RAC 3 → accept with approval; RAC 4 → manage by SOP; RAC 5 → accept.
- Team: **multidisciplinary, 5–8 members**, facilitated by a safety engineer.
- Energy-source checklist: **Electrical, Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical, Radiation, Kinetic, Gravitational, Biological**.
- Deliverable: **PHA worksheet** with 8 columns (Hazard ID, Cause, Effect, Severity, Probability, RAC, Recommendation, Verification Owner).
- **Cost-of-change rule:** $C_{n+1} \approx 10 \times C_n$ — earlier PHA saves exponential cost.
- PHA **does NOT use guidewords** (unlike HAZOP) and is **NOT semi-quantitative** (unlike FMEA with RPN).
- **Best exam mantra:** *"PHA = First, Fast, Qualitative, Multidisciplinary."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a systematic, structured, and rigorous team-based qualitative risk analysis technique used to identify potential hazards and operability problems in process systems. It systematically examines every part of a process or operation to identify how deviations from the design intent can occur, and whether such deviations can lead to safety, health, environmental, or operational consequences.

The term was originally coined by the **Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI)** in the United Kingdom in the **1960s**, and it was further formalized by the **Chemical Industries Association (CIA)** in **1974** and later by the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) Standard 61882** in **2016**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, HAZOP is defined as a **"brainstorming-based, multi-disciplinary team examination of a process plant or system using guide words applied to process parameters to identify deviations from design intent."**

### Key Conceptual Distinctions

| Term | Meaning in HAZOP Context |
|------|--------------------------|
| **Hazard** | A physical situation with potential to cause harm (injury, damage, loss) |
| **Operability** | The ability of a plant/system to function as intended without operational disturbances |
| **Deviation** | Departure from the original design intent (e.g., "No Flow", "High Pressure") |
| **Node** | A specific section of the process where parameters and guide words are applied |
| **Cause** | The reason a deviation may occur (equipment failure, human error, external event) |
| **Consequence** | The outcome of a deviation (fire, explosion, production loss, environmental release) |
| **Action** | A recommendation to eliminate, reduce, or control the deviation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** HAZOP is fundamentally a **qualitative technique** — it identifies *what can go wrong*, *why it can go wrong*, and *what can be done about it*, but it does **NOT** quantify the frequency or probability of occurrence on its own (this is done by LOPA or QRA in later modules).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Road Trip Checklist" Intuition

Imagine you are planning a long road trip from **Kochi to Delhi** in your car. The *Design Intent* is: "Reach Delhi safely, on time, with full fuel and no breakdowns."

Before starting, you and your friends (the *HAZOP team*) sit down and brainstorm:
- What if there is **NO fuel** in the tank? (Cause: forgot to refuel; Consequence: stranded on highway; Action: refuel before starting)
- What if there is **MORE traffic** than expected? (Cause: accident ahead; Consequence: delay; Action: take alternate route)
- What if the **temperature is HIGHER** than the engine's tolerance? (Cause: radiator failure; Consequence: engine seizure; Action: check coolant, drive at night)
- What if there is **LESS oxygen** in the cabin at high altitude? (Cause: thinning air; Consequence: driver fatigue; Action: take breaks)

This is **exactly** what HAZOP does for a chemical plant. Instead of "no fuel", the team examines "no flow" in a pipe. Instead of "high temperature", the team examines "high temperature" in a reactor. The deviations, causes, consequences, and recommended actions follow the same structured pattern.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Mnemonic:** Think of HAZOP as **"What-If × Guide Words × Process Sections"** — it is essentially a *systematic what-if analysis* where the "what" is standardized through guide words.

---

## 1.3 The Origin and Evolution of HAZOP

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1960s** | Developed by **ICI (Imperial Chemical Industries)** in the UK, originally as **Critical Examination (CE)** |
| **1974** | Formalized by **Chemical Industries Association (CIA)** as a guide for the UK process industry |
| **1977** | First public release of HAZOP guidelines |
| **1992** | Expanded into **HAZOP** (Hazard **and** Operability) — operability was added to address productivity issues, not just safety |
| **2001** | **IEC 61882** published as the first international standard |
| **2016** | **IEC 61882:2016** updated with modern terminology, team roles, and procedural steps |
| **2020s** | Integration with **CHAZOP** (Computer HAZOP), **HAZOP-LOPA** combined methodologies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** For KTU 2024 examinations, students are expected to be familiar with the **IEC 61882:2016** framework, the **CIA Guide**, and the **CCPS Guidelines** (Center for Chemical Process Safety, AIChE).

---

## 1.4 When is HAZOP Used? (Application Scope)

HAZOP is specifically applied during the **design phase** and at **critical operational milestones** in a process plant's life cycle:

1. **Basic Engineering Design (BED)** stage — when P&IDs are 60–70% complete
2. **Detailed Engineering Design (DED)** stage — final review before construction
3. **Pre-commissioning** stage — review of the as-built plant
4. **Periodic Safety Reviews** — typically every 5 years
5. **Modification Management (MOC)** — whenever a significant process change is proposed
6. **Decommissioning Stage** — for safe plant shutdown

### Industries Where HAZOP is Applied

- **Oil and Gas** (refineries, offshore platforms, LNG terminals)
- **Petrochemicals** (ethylene plants, polymer units)
- **Pharmaceuticals** (API manufacturing, batch reactors)
- **Pulp and Paper** (digester systems)
- **Power Generation** (boiler systems, cooling water)
- **Nuclear Processing** (fuel fabrication, reprocessing)
- **Food and Beverage** (fermentation, pasteurization)
- **Water Treatment** (chlorination, desalination)

> [!WARNING]
> HAZOP is **NOT** suitable for:
> - Pure software/systems (use FMEA or FTA instead)
> - Early conceptual design (information is too incomplete)
> - Pure mechanical/structural analysis (use FEA or stress analysis)
> - Single component failure studies (use FMEA)
> - However, **CHAZOP** (Computer HAZOP) is used for control systems and **HAZOP-LOPA** is used for safety instrumented systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation of HAZOP

HAZOP is built upon three foundational pillars that distinguish it from other risk analysis techniques:

### Pillar 1: The Design Intent
Every process section has a **design intent** — a clear statement of how that section is supposed to operate under normal conditions. This includes:
- The intended function (e.g., "transfer 100 m³/h of benzene from Tank T-101 to Reactor R-201")
- The operating parameters (e.g., temperature = 80°C, pressure = 2 barg, flow = 100 m³/h)
- The state of the material (e.g., liquid, sub-cooled, single-phase)

### Pillar 2: Deviations from Design Intent
The deviations are constructed by combining a **Guide Word** (e.g., NO, MORE, LESS) with a **Process Parameter** (e.g., FLOW, TEMPERATURE). Each combination represents a *possible* way in which the system may deviate from its intended state.

### Pillar 3: Causes and Consequences
For each deviation, the team must identify:
- **Causes** — What could cause this deviation? (Equipment failure, human error, external event)
- **Consequences** — What happens if this deviation occurs? (Fire, explosion, environmental release, production loss)
- **Safeguards** — Existing protective systems (alarms, interlocks, relief valves, procedures)
- **Recommendations** — Additional measures needed to reduce risk

---

## 2.2 The Standard HAZOP Guide Words

The **seven standard guide words** (as per IEC 61882:2016) are the heart of the HAZOP methodology. Each guide word has a precise meaning when applied to a process parameter:

| # | Guide Word | Meaning | Example Applied to "FLOW" |
|---|------------|---------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **NO / NONE / NOT** | Complete negation of the design intent | No flow in line L-101 |
| 2 | **MORE / HIGH / LARGE** | Quantitative increase | More flow than designed (100 → 150 m³/h) |
| 3 | **LESS / LOW / SMALL** | Quantitative decrease | Less flow than designed (100 → 50 m³/h) |
| 4 | **AS WELL AS / MORE THAN** | Additional activity or component present | Two-phase flow instead of single-phase (vapor + liquid) |
| 5 | **PART OF / PARTIAL** | Only some of the design intent is achieved | Partial flow (only 60% of design flow) |
| 6 | **REVERSE / INVERSE** | Opposite of design intent | Reverse flow (flow goes backward through pump) |
| 7 | **OTHER THAN / WRONG** | Complete substitution; entirely different event | Wrong fluid in line (water instead of oil) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The guide words **MUST** be applied to **process parameters**, not to the entire process. A common student mistake is to write "No safety" or "More accidents" — these are not valid HAZOP deviations. Always pair a guide word with a specific parameter like "No Flow", "High Temperature", "Reverse Flow".

---

## 2.3 Standard Process Parameters Used in HAZOP

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Application |
|---|-----------|--------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Flow** | F | Liquid, gas, or solid flow in pipes |
| 2 | **Pressure** | P | Vessel, line, or system pressure |
| 3 | **Temperature** | T | Process temperature at a point |
| 4 | **Level** | L | Liquid level in a vessel |
| 5 | **Composition / Concentration** | C | Chemical composition, phase, purity |
| 6 | **Phase** | Ph | Single-phase vs multi-phase |
| 7 | **Reaction / Mixing** | R | Chemical reaction, blending |
| 8 | **Time / Sequence** | t | Start-up, shutdown, batch sequencing |
| 9 | **Sampling / Maintenance** | S | Operator actions, sampling procedures |
| 10 | **Service / Utility** | U | Cooling water, steam, instrument air, electricity |
| 11 | **Stability / Vibration** | V | Mechanical stability, vibration |
| 12 | **Viscosity / Density** | ρ / μ | Material property changes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specification:** The syllabus specifically emphasizes **Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, and Composition** as the *core five parameters*. Students should be prepared to generate deviations using these five parameters, but they should also know that additional parameters (Phase, Time, Service) may be applied for more comprehensive studies.

---

## 2.4 The HAZOP Team Composition

A successful HAZOP study requires a **multi-disciplinary team**. According to IEC 61882:2016, the following roles must be filled:

| Role | Responsibility | Typical Background |
|------|----------------|---------------------|
| **HAZOP Leader / Chairman** | Facilitates the study, ensures methodology is followed, maintains focus | Senior Process Safety Engineer (10+ years experience) |
| **Scribe / Recorder** | Documents all discussions, deviations, and actions in real-time | Junior engineer with HAZOP training |
| **Process Engineer** | Explains design intent, parameters, operating conditions | Plant/process design engineer |
| **Operations Expert** | Provides operating experience, startup/shutdown considerations | Plant operations manager, shift supervisor |
| **Instrumentation & Control Engineer** | Reviews interlocks, alarms, control loops | I&C / Automation engineer |
| **Mechanical/Static Equipment Engineer** | Reviews vessels, pumps, heat exchangers | Mechanical engineer |
| **Safety Engineer** | Assesses consequences, risk ranking | Process safety specialist |
| **Maintenance Engineer** | Reviews maintenance procedures, equipment reliability | Maintenance supervisor |
| **Specialist (as needed)** | Specific technical input (corrosion, HSE, etc.) | Domain expert invited for specific nodes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Team Size Rule:** A typical HAZOP team has **6–10 members**. Smaller teams (less than 4) lack diversity of thought; larger teams (more than 12) become inefficient and bogged down in discussions.

---

## 2.5 The HAZOP Node and Study Section Concept

A **node** is a specific point or section of the process where the design intent is constant. The team divides the P&ID into logical sections, and each section becomes a **study node** (or simply "node"). Common node definitions include:

- **Pipe segments** between two equipment items
- **A specific vessel** (reactor, separator, tank)
- **A heat exchanger** with both shell and tube sides
- **A pump or compressor** with suction and discharge
- **A control loop** with sensor, controller, and control valve

### Example: A Pump with Suction and Discharge
- **Node 1:** Pump suction line (from Tank T-101 to Pump P-101)
- **Node 2:** Pump P-101 itself
- **Node 3:** Pump discharge line (from Pump P-101 to header)

For each node, the **design intent** is documented before applying guide words.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — HAZOP Risk Matrix (for Consequence Ranking)

Although HAZOP is qualitative, many teams use a **Risk Matrix** to rank the severity and likelihood of consequences. This is a *supporting tool*, not part of the core HAZOP methodology, but it is widely used and **frequently tested in KTU exams**.

### Risk Matrix (4×4 Standard)

| Severity \ Likelihood | **L1 — Rare** | **L2 — Unlikely** | **L3 — Possible** | **L4 — Frequent** |
|------------------------|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **S1 — Negligible** (minor injury, no lost time) | Low | Low | Low | Medium |
| **S2 — Marginal** (first aid injury, minor loss) | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **S3 — Critical** (serious injury, major loss) | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **S4 — Catastrophic** (fatality, plant destruction) | Medium | High | Very High | Very High |

### Risk Score Equation (for Quantitative HAZOP extensions)

$$R = S \times L \times F_{exposure}$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk Score** (dimensionless or in monetary units)
- $S$ = **Severity** of consequence (rated 1–4 or 1–5)
- $L$ = **Likelihood** of occurrence (rated 1–4 or 1–5)
- $F_{exposure}$ = **Exposure Frequency** factor (number of personnel exposed, typically 1–10)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The HAZOP risk matrix is used by **ONGC, BPCL, IOCL, Reliance Industries, Saudi Aramco, Shell, and BP** in their process safety management systems. The risk score helps prioritize which HAZOP recommendations to implement first.

---

## 2.7 HAZOP vs Other Risk Analysis Techniques (Comparison Table)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** This comparison is a **highly expected 7-mark question** in Part B. Memorize the differences.

| Aspect | HAZOP | FMEA | FTA | What-If | Checklist |
|--------|-------|------|-----|---------|-----------|
