| **Type** | Qualitative | Qualitative/Semi-quantitative | Quantitative | Qualitative | Qualitative |
| **Approach** | Guide Words + Parameters | Component failure modes | Boolean logic (AND/OR gates) | Open-ended questions | Pre-defined list |
| **Application Stage** | Design (detailed) | Design/Operation | Complex systems | Conceptual/Early design | All stages |
| **Best For** | Process plants (continuous) | Mechanical/electrical | Complex interdependent systems | Quick screening | Routine operations |
| **Team Required** | Yes (multi-disciplinary) | Single discipline | Specialized | Single/Small | Single |
| **Output** | Deviation list + actions | RPN (Risk Priority Number) | Minimal cut sets | Brainstorming list | Yes/No responses |
| **Time Required** | High (days to weeks) | Medium (days) | High (specialist) | Low (hours) | Very Low (hours) |
| **Standards** | IEC 61882 | IEC 60812 | IEC 61025 | No formal standard | No formal standard |

---

## 2.8 The HAZOP Worksheet Format (Standard Documentation)

The HAZOP worksheet is the formal output of the study. According to IEC 61882:2016, it should contain the following columns:

| Column | Content |
|--------|---------|
| 1 | **Node Number & Description** (e.g., "Node 3: Pump P-101 discharge line") |
| 2 | **Design Intent** (e.g., "Transfer 100 m³/h benzene at 2 barg") |
| 3 | **Guide Word** (e.g., "NO") |
| 4 | **Parameter** (e.g., "FLOW") |
| 5 | **Deviation** (e.g., "NO FLOW from P-101 to header") |
| 6 | **Possible Causes** (e.g., "Pump failure, blocked strainer, closed discharge valve") |
| 7 | **Consequences** (e.g., "Loss of feed to reactor, overheating") |
| 8 | **Existing Safeguards** (e.g., "Low flow alarm FAL-101, pump trip on low suction pressure") |
| 9 | **Risk Level** (Low / Medium / High) |
| 10 | **Recommendations / Actions** (e.g., "Install additional high-temperature trip on R-201") |
| 11 | **Action By / Target Date** (e.g., "Process Eng / 30-June-2025") |
| 12 | **Status** (Open / Closed / In Progress) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Filling the worksheet without clearly stating the **design intent** first. The design intent is the *foundation* — without it, deviations are meaningless. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for missing design intent.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Worked Example

## 3.1 The Standard HAZOP Procedure (10-Step Methodology)

The complete HAZOP procedure, as standardized by IEC 61882:2016, consists of the following steps. Each step is **mandatory** and is frequently tested in KTU exams.

### **Step 1: Define Scope, Objectives, and Boundaries**

The first step is to clearly define:
- **What** is being studied (e.g., "HAZOP of the new benzene storage and transfer section")
- **What is excluded** (e.g., "Civil, structural, and electrical systems are excluded")
- **Boundaries** (e.g., "From Tank T-101 inlet flange to Pump P-101 discharge flange")
- **Objectives** (e.g., "Identify safety, health, environmental, and operability issues")
- **Type of study** (e.g., "Design-stage HAZOP for green-field project")

> **Why this step?** Without a clear scope, the study can become unfocused, time-consuming, and may miss critical issues. The scope statement is usually a one-page document approved by the project manager.

### **Step 2: Assemble the HAZOP Team**

Select a multi-disciplinary team as described in Section 2.4. Ensure:
- The **HAZOP Leader** is independent (not part of the project team) to maintain objectivity
- The team has **read access** to all relevant documents before the sessions begin
- All members are **trained in HAZOP methodology** (minimum 2-day workshop)

### **Step 3: Gather Information and Documentation**

Collect all relevant design and operational documents:
- **P&IDs (Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams)** — the primary working document
- **PFDs (Process Flow Diagrams)** — for overall process understanding
- **Equipment data sheets** (vessels, pumps, heat exchangers, instruments)
- **Operating procedures** (normal, startup, shutdown, emergency)
- **Cause and Effect diagrams** (for control and safety systems)
- **Layout drawings** (plot plans, equipment arrangements)
- **Hazardous area classification drawings**
- **Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS)**
- **Previous incident reports** (if plant is operational)

### **Step 4: Divide the Plant into Study Nodes (Sections)**

Logically partition the process into manageable sections. The team should:
- Start from the **inlet** of the process and work sequentially
- Define nodes such that **design intent is constant** within each node
- For continuous processes, nodes typically follow **pipe segments** between equipment
- For batch processes, nodes may follow **operational steps or reactor charges**
- Mark node boundaries clearly on the P&ID using a **node mark-up sheet**

> **Example Node Breakdown for a Benzene Storage Facility:**
> - Node 1: Tank T-101 (storage)
> - Node 2: Tank outlet line to Pump P-101
> - Node 3: Pump P-101
> - Node 4: Pump discharge line to header

### **Step 5: For Each Node, State the Design Intent**

Before applying any guide words, the team must **explicitly state** the design intent of each node. This is documented on the HAZOP worksheet.

> **Example for Node 3 (Pump P-101):**
> *"Design Intent: Transfer liquid benzene from Tank T-101 to the reactor header at a flow rate of 100 m³/h, discharge pressure 3 barg, ambient temperature."*

### **Step 6: Apply Guide Words to Parameters — Generate Deviations**

For each node, systematically apply **all relevant guide words × parameters** combinations. The combinations that do not apply (e.g., "Reverse Composition") are noted as **"Not Applicable (NA)"** with a brief reason.

> **Example Deviations for Node 3 (Pump P-101):**
> - NO + FLOW = "No Flow"
> - LESS + FLOW = "Low Flow"
> - MORE + FLOW = "High Flow"
> - NO + PRESSURE = "No Pressure" (or "Loss of Suction Pressure")
> - MORE + PRESSURE = "High Discharge Pressure"
> - REVERSE + FLOW = "Reverse Flow"
> - OTHER THAN + COMPOSITION = "Wrong Fluid" (e.g., water instead of benzene)

### **Step 7: Identify Causes, Consequences, and Existing Safeguards**

For each deviation, the team brainstorms:
- **Causes** (e.g., "Pump mechanical failure", "Strainer blockage", "Valve closed")
- **Consequences** (e.g., "Reactor feed loss → reaction temperature runaway")
- **Existing Safeguards** (e.g., "Low flow alarm FAL-101", "Pump auto-trip on low NPSH")

### **Step 8: Develop Recommendations (Actions)**

If the risk from a deviation is **unacceptable** (Medium or High per the risk matrix), the team develops **specific recommendations**. These are:
- **Actionable** (e.g., "Install high-temperature interlock on R-201", not "improve safety")
- **Assigned** to a specific person/team
- **Time-bound** (target completion date)
- **Closed out** through follow-up meetings

### **Step 9: Document the Study**

All findings are recorded in the **HAZOP Register** (or HAZOP Report). The report includes:
- Cover page, scope, team list
- Node definitions
- Completed HAZOP worksheets
- Risk matrix used
- Action register
- Assumptions and limitations
- Signature page

### **Step 10: Follow-up and Action Closure**

The HAZOP report is distributed to the design/operations team. Each action must be:
- **Tracked** in an action register
- **Implemented** in the design or procedure
- **Verified** in subsequent HAZOP or P&ID revisions
- **Closed** formally by the HAZOP leader

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** Many students confuse the steps of HAZOP with FMEA. The correct sequence is **"Scope → Team → Information → Nodes → Design Intent → Deviations → Causes/Consequences → Recommendations → Documentation → Follow-up"**. Memorize this 10-step sequence — it is a guaranteed 7-mark question.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — HAZOP of a Centrifugal Pump Section

Let us work through a **complete HAZOP example** for a centrifugal pump transferring a flammable solvent.

### Process Description

A centrifugal pump (P-101) transfers a flammable hydrocarbon solvent from atmospheric storage tank T-101 to a downstream reactor. The pump has a suction strainer, a discharge check valve, and a flow transmitter (FT-101) on the discharge line.

**Design Intent for Node 3 (Pump P-101):**
> *"Transfer liquid hydrocarbon from T-101 to the reactor header at a flow rate of 80 m³/h, discharge pressure 4 barg, temperature 30°C, NPSH available = 6 m."*

### Worked Deviations Table

| # | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Existing Safeguards | Recommendations |
|---|-----------|--------|--------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **NO FLOW** | (a) Pump mechanical failure<br>(b) Strainer blocked<br>(c) Discharge valve closed<br>(d) Loss of prime (NPSH failure)<br>(e) Pipe rupture downstream | (a) Loss of feed to reactor → temperature runaway<br>(b) Pump cavitation → seal damage → fire hazard | (i) Low flow alarm FAL-101<br>(ii) Low-low flow trip → pump shutdown<br>(iii) Low suction pressure switch PSL-102 | (1) Install redundant low flow switch<br>(2) Add high-temperature interlock on reactor (independent of flow) |
| 2 | **LESS FLOW** | (a) Strainer partially blocked<br>(b) Cavitation<br>(c) Pump speed reduced (VFD failure)<br>(d) Control valve partially closed | (a) Reduced feed to reactor → reaction rate drop<br>(b) Pump overheating | (i) Flow indicator FI-101<br>(ii) Low flow alarm FAL-101 | (1) Verify VFD failure mode on loss of signal<br>(2) Specify minimum flow bypass line |
| 3 | **MORE FLOW** | (a) Pump oversized (design error)<br>(b) Wrong pump installed<br>(c) Control valve failure (open) | (a) Reactor overfeed → exothermic runaway<br>(b) Pipe vibration → fatigue failure | (i) High flow alarm FAH-101<br>(ii) Flow control loop FIC-101 | (1) Install high flow trip independent of controller<br>(2) Verify sizing of downstream control valve |
| 4 | **REVERSE FLOW** | (a) Check valve failure<br>(b) Pump shutdown with pressurized reactor<br>(c) Wrong valve installation | (a) Hot fluid back-flow into tank → flashing, vapor cloud<br>(b) Pump reverse rotation → seal damage | (i) Check valve CV-101<br>(ii) Pump minimum flow line | (1) Install double check valve or RFO valve<br>(2) Add thermal relief on discharge line |
| 5 | **HIGH PRESSURE** | (a) Blocked discharge<br>(b) Control valve failure (open downstream, closed downstream)<br>(c) Thermal expansion (line blocked with cold fluid) | (a) Pipe rupture → major flammable release → fire<br>(b) Equipment damage | (i) Pressure transmitter PT-101<br>(ii) Pressure safety valve PSV-101 set at 6 barg | (1) Verify PSV-101 sizing<br>(2) Install redundant pressure transmitter |
| 6 | **HIGH TEMPERATURE** | (a) Pump cavitation<br>(b) Loss of cooling (jacket failure)<br>(c) Ambient temperature rise | (a) Seal degradation → leak<br>(b) Vapour formation in suction → cavitation | (i) Bearing temperature monitor<br>(ii) Seal pot temperature alarm | (1) Install seal failure detection<br>(2) Specify maximum ambient temperature for pump |
| 7 | **WRONG COMPOSITION** | (a) Cross-connection in header<br>(b) Operator error during line-up<br>(c) Tank contamination | (a) Incompatible chemical reaction → explosion<br>(b) Product quality loss | (i) Procedural line-up checklist<br>(ii) Material verification log | (1) Install double block and bleed on common header<br>(2) Add composition analyzer (FTIR) on discharge |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** A typical Part B question provides a **P&ID, design intent, and a list of deviations** and asks the student to **complete the HAZOP worksheet** by identifying causes, consequences, and recommendations. The above table format is the **expected answer structure**.

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Analysis — Risk Score Calculation for HAZOP Deviations

Although HAZOP is qualitative, a quantitative risk ranking is often attached. The **risk score** is calculated as:

$$R = S \times L \times C_{factor}$$

Where:
- $R$ = Risk Score (dimensionless)
- $S$ = Severity rating (1 = Negligible, 2 = Minor, 3 = Major, 4 = Catastrophic, 5 = Catastrophic with multiple fatalities)
- $L$ = Likelihood rating (1 = Rare < 10⁻⁶/yr, 2 = Unlikely 10⁻⁴/yr, 3 = Possible 10⁻²/yr, 4 = Likely 10⁻¹/yr, 5 = Frequent > 1/yr)
- $C_{factor}$ = Consequence clustering factor (1 = single, 2 = domino, 3 = cascade)

### Worked Numerical Example

**Scenario:** Deviation "NO FLOW from Pump P-101" with the following characteristics:
- Severity: Catastrophic (potential reactor runaway, possible fatality) → $S = 4$
- Likelihood: Possible (1 in 100 per year) → $L = 3$
- No cascade to other units → $C_{factor} = 1$

Applying the formula:

$$R = S \times L \times C_{factor}$$

$$R = 4 \times 3 \times 1 = 12$$

**Interpretation:** Per the risk matrix threshold (typically $R \geq 8$ requires action), this deviation requires **mandatory additional safeguards**. The team recommends:
- High-temperature interlock on reactor (SIL-2 rated)
- Redundant low-flow switch

> [!NOTE]
> **Extension to Quantitative Risk Analysis (QRA):** The likelihood values used in HAZOP can be linked to **failure rate databases** such as **OREDA (Offshore and Onshore Reliability Data)** or **IEEE 500** for more rigorous QRA in subsequent risk assessment stages (Module 3 of PBSFT404).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Guide Word × Parameter Combinations

The following matrix shows **all valid guide word × parameter combinations** for a typical HAZOP study. Cells marked with **✓** are common; cells marked with **(✓)** are occasionally used; cells marked with **✗** are not applicable.

| Guide Word \ Parameter | Flow | Pressure | Temperature | Level | Composition | Phase | Time |
|------------------------|------|----------|-------------|-------|-------------|-------|------|
| **NO / NONE** | No Flow ✓ | No Pressure ✓ | No Temperature ✗ | Empty ✓ | No Composition ✗ | No Phase ✗ | No Time ✗ |
| **MORE / HIGH** | High Flow ✓ | High Pressure ✓ | High Temp ✓ | High Level ✓ | High Concentration ✓ | Two-Phase (✓) | Late Start (✓) |
| **LESS / LOW** | Low Flow ✓ | Low Pressure ✓ | Low Temp ✓ | Low Level ✓ | Low Concentration ✓ | (✓) | Early Start (✓) |
| **AS WELL AS** | Two-Phase Flow ✓ | Over-Pressurization (✓) | Hot Spot ✓ | Foam/Slug ✓ | Contaminant Present ✓ | Vapor in Liquid ✓ | Step Skipped ✗ |
| **PART OF** | Partial Flow ✓ | Partial Pressure (✓) | Stratification ✓ | Partial Fill ✓ | Dilution ✓ | Partial Phase (✓) | Partial Sequence (✓) |
| **REVERSE** | Reverse Flow ✓ | Vacuum (✓) | Cold Spot (✓) | Roll-Over (✓) | Wrong Mix (✓) | Phase Inversion (✓) | Reverse Order ✗ |
| **OTHER THAN** | Wrong Fluid ✓ | Wrong Pressure Source (✓) | Wrong Material (✓) | Wrong Liquid ✓ | Wrong Chemical ✓ | Wrong Phase ✓ | Wrong Step ✓ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recap:** The **7 guide words × 7 parameters = 49 combinations** framework is the most common exam question. Students should be able to **state at least 5 combinations** with examples.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HAZOP Process Flow Diagram — Complete Methodology

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete HAZOP methodology workflow**, from project initiation to action closure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation & Scope Definition] --> B[Assemble Multi-Disciplinary HAZOP Team]
    B --> C[Gather Documentation: P&IDs, PFDs, Datasheets]
    C --> D[Train Team on HAZOP Methodology]
    D --> E[Divide Plant into Logical Nodes]
    E --> F[For Each Node: Define Design Intent]
    F --> G[Apply Guide Words to Parameters]
    G --> H{Valid Deviation?}
    H -->|No| I[Mark as Not Applicable NA]
    H -->|Yes| J[Identify Possible Causes]
    J --> K[Assess Consequences]
    K --> L[Review Existing Safeguards]
    L --> M{Is Risk Acceptable?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Document and Close]
    M -->|No| O[Develop Recommendations]
    O --> P[Assign Action with Target Date]
    P --> N
    I --> Q{Move to Next Node?}
    N --> Q
    Q -->|Yes| F
    Q -->|No| R[Compile Final HAZOP Report]
    R --> S[Distribute Report to Project Team]
    S --> T[Track and Close Actions]
    T --> U[Periodic HAZOP Re-Review]
```

```mermaid
    Node3 --> Node4

    Node1 -.HAZOP Review.-> W1[Deviations: No Flow, Low Flow, High Temp]
    Node2 -.HAZOP Review.-> W2[Deviations: No Pressure, Cavitation, Seal Failure]
    Node3 -.HAZOP Review.-> W3[Deviations: High Pressure, Reverse Flow, Two-Phase]
    Node4 -.HAZOP Review.-> W4[Deviations: Valve Failure, Loss of Control Signal]
```

## 4.5 HAZOP Risk Ranking Decision Tree (for Consequence Assessment)

This decision tree shows how the team determines whether a deviation requires additional action or can be closed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deviation Identified] --> B[Causes Brainstormed]
    B --> C[Consequences Identified]
    C --> D[Severity Rated: S1, S2, S3, S4]
    D --> E[Likelihood Rated: L1, L2, L3, L4]
    E --> F[Risk Score R = S x L]
    F --> G{R Score}
    G -->|1 to 3| H[LOW - Acceptable, Close Out]
    G -->|4 to 6| I[MEDIUM - Document Safeguards, May Recommend]
    G -->|7 to 9| J[HIGH - Additional Safeguards Required]
    G -->|10 to 16| K[VERY HIGH - Mandatory Action, Independent Protection Layer]
    I --> L[Implement Additional Recommendations]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Update P&ID and Procedures]
    M --> N[Close Action in HAZOP Register]
    H --> N
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions test **direct recall, definitions, and concise explanations**. Each answer should be **3–5 sentences with bullet points** for clarity.

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Define HAZOP. List the standard guide words used in a HAZOP study with one example each.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**HAZOP Definition (1 Mark):**
HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) is a **systematic, qualitative, team-based risk analysis technique** used to identify potential hazards and operability problems in process plants by examining deviations from the design intent using standardized guide words applied to process parameters. It was developed by Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI) in the 1960s and is now standardized under IEC 61882:2016.

**Standard Guide Words (2 Marks):**

| Guide Word | Meaning | Example (Applied to Flow) |
|------------|---------|---------------------------|
| **NO / NONE** | Complete negation | No flow from P-101 |
| **MORE / HIGH** | Quantitative increase | High flow to reactor (150 m³/h) |
| **LESS / LOW** | Quantitative decrease | Low flow (30 m³/h) |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional component present | Two-phase flow (vapor + liquid) |
| **PART OF** | Only partial fulfillment | Partial flow (60% of design) |
| **REVERSE** | Opposite of intent | Reverse flow to tank |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | Wrong fluid in line |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**State any three differences between HAZOP and FMEA.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Aspect | HAZOP | FMEA |
|--------|-------|------|
| **1. Methodology** | Uses guide words applied to process parameters to identify deviations (1 Mark) | Uses component-level failure modes (e.g., bearing seizure, seal leak) (1 Mark) |
| **2. Application** | Best suited for continuous process plants with detailed P&IDs (0.5 Mark) | Best suited for mechanical, electrical, and discrete-component systems (0.5 Mark) |
| **3. Output** | List of deviations with causes, consequences, and recommendations (0.5 Mark) | Risk Priority Number (RPN = S × O × D) for each failure mode (0.5 Mark) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions follow a strict **two-sub-part structure** (typically 7 marks each). Students must answer **either the full Question A or the full Question B** as an internal choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**A centrifugal pump P-201 transfers a flammable hydrocarbon from a storage tank T-201 to a reactor R-201. The pump has a suction strainer, a discharge check valve, a flow transmitter FT-201, and a pressure safety valve PSV-201 set at 7 barg. The design intent is: 100 m³/h flow, 5 barg discharge pressure, 30°C, NPSHa = 8 m.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Identify at least FIVE (5) deviations using HAZOP guide words and parameters. For each deviation, list the possible causes, consequences, and existing safeguards.**

#### Model Answer (7 Marks):

**Design Intent of Pump Section (1 Mark):**
> *"Transfer 100 m³/h of liquid hydrocarbon from T-201 to R-201 at 5 barg discharge pressure and 30°C, with NPSHa = 8 m, ensuring no cavitation."*

**Deviations Table (6 Marks):**

| # | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Existing Safeguards |
|---|-----------|--------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **NO FLOW** (1.5 M) | (a) Pump failure (impeller, seal, bearing)<br>(b) Strainer fully blocked<br>(c) Discharge valve mistakenly closed<br>(d) Pipe rupture downstream | (a) Loss of feed to reactor → exothermic runaway<br>(b) Pump dry running → seal damage → leak → fire hazard | (i) Low flow alarm FAL-201<br>(ii) Low flow trip (FALL-201)<br>(iii) Suction pressure switch PSL-201 |
| 2 | **HIGH PRESSURE** (1.5 M) | (a) Discharge line blocked<br>(b) Thermal expansion in dead-end line<br>(c) Valve misalignment (open/closed in wrong sequence)<br>(d) PSV-201 set point drift | (a) Pipe/vessel rupture → flammable release → fire/explosion<br>(b) Equipment over-stress | (i) PT-201 pressure transmitter<br>(ii) PAH-201 high pressure alarm<br>(iii) PSV-201 set at 7 barg |
| 3 | **REVERSE FLOW** (1.0 M) | (a) Check valve CV-201 fails open<br>(b) Reactor at higher pressure than pump discharge<br>(c) Wrong valve installed | (a) Hot reactor fluid backflows → flashing in suction line → vapor cloud explosion<br>(b) Pump reverse rotation → seal damage | (i) Check valve CV-201<br>(ii) Minimum flow line with orifice |
| 4 | **HIGH TEMPERATURE** (1.0 M) | (a) Pump cavitation (NPSHa loss)<br>(b) Loss of seal flush fluid<br>(c) Bearing failure → heat conduction | (a) Seal degradation → leak<br>(b) Vapor formation in suction → cavitation | (i) Bearing temperature monitor<br>(ii) Seal pot temperature indicator |
| 5 | **WRONG COMPOSITION** (1.0 M) | (a) Cross-connection in header<br>(b) Operator error during line-up<br>(c) Tank contamination (water, incompatible chemical) | (a) Incompatible mixing → uncontrolled reaction → explosion<br>(b) Product contamination | (i) Procedural line-up checklist<br>(ii) Material verification log<br>(iii) Color-coded piping |
| 6 | **TWO-PHASE FLOW** (0.5 M) (BONUS) | (a) Vapor formation in suction due to high temperature or low pressure<br>(b) Cavitation | (a) Pump damage, vibration, loss of flow | (i) NPSH margin check<br>(ii) Suction temperature alarm |

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**For the deviations identified in part (a), develop appropriate HAZOP recommendations. Also explain the role of the HAZOP team leader and the importance of documentation.**

#### Model Answer (7 Marks):

**Recommendations for Each Deviation (4 Marks):**

1. **NO FLOW Recommendation:** Install a **redundant low-low flow switch** (independent of FAL-201) that trips the pump. Add a **high-temperature interlock on R-201** that is independent of flow signal. Implement a **strainer differential pressure indicator** with high alarm to detect blockage.

2. **HIGH PRESSURE Recommendation:** Verify **PSV-201 sizing** against API 520/521 for the maximum credible flow case. Install a **second independent pressure transmitter** on discharge. Add a **mechanical pressure gauge** for operator verification. Implement **block valve isolation** procedure for maintenance.

3. **REVERSE FLOW Recommendation:** Replace single check valve with **double check valve (DCV)** or a **reverse-flow preventer**. Install **thermal relief valve** on the discharge line to handle thermal expansion. Add a **flame arrester** in the pump suction line to prevent flashback.

4. **HIGH TEMPERATURE Recommendation:** Install a **seal failure detector** (e.g., SelaCam or optical sensor). Implement **predictive maintenance** for bearings (vibration analysis every 3 months). Add **cooling water flow switch** if seal flush is water-cooled.

5. **WRONG COMPOSITION Recommendation:** Install **double block and bleed (DBB)** on the common header. Add a **composition analyzer** (e.g., FTIR or density meter) on the discharge. Implement **permit-to-work system** for line-up operations.

**Role of HAZOP Team Leader (2 Marks):**
- **Facilitates** the study using the standard HAZOP methodology, ensuring consistency across nodes
- **Maintains focus** and keeps the team on schedule; prevents digression into design debates
- **Guides discussion** to identify deviations, causes, and consequences
- **Resolves conflicts** within the team; ensures all voices are heard
- **Verifies completeness** of the study and reviews the final report
- **Must be independent** of the project design team to maintain objectivity

**Importance of Documentation (1 Mark):**
- Provides a **legal record** of safety decisions for regulatory compliance (OSHA, EPA, OISD)
- **Enables tracking** of action items to closure
- **Supports** future HAZOP re-validations when plant is modified
- **Captures institutional knowledge** for training new engineers

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2022]`

**Explain the complete HAZOP procedure step-by-step. Discuss in detail the team composition, the role of the scribe, and the standard documentation format.**

#### Model Answer (14 Marks):

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: HAZOP Procedure (10 Steps)**

| Step | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **1. Define Scope & Objectives** (0.7 M) | Clearly state the boundaries of the study, what is included/excluded, and the study type (design, pre-commissioning, etc.) |
| **2. Assemble HAZOP Team** (0.7 M) | Form a multi-disciplinary team of 6–10 members with relevant expertise; ensure HAZOP leader is independent |
| **3. Gather Information** (0.7 M) | Collect P&IDs, PFDs, equipment datasheets, operating procedures, MSDS, Cause & Effect diagrams |
| **4. Train Team** (0.7 M) | Conduct a 1–2 day workshop on HAZOP methodology, guide words, parameters, and worksheet format |
| **5. Divide into Nodes** (0.7 M) | Logically partition the plant into study nodes (e.g., pump, heat exchanger, control loop) |
| **6. Define Design Intent** (0.7 M) | For each node, explicitly state the intended function and operating parameters |
| **7. Apply Guide Words** (0.7 M) | Systematically apply 7 guide words × relevant parameters to generate deviations |
| **8. Identify Causes, Consequences, Safeguards** (0.7 M) | For each valid deviation, brainstorm causes, assess consequences, list existing safeguards |
| **9. Develop Recommendations** (0.7 M) | For high-risk deviations, propose specific, actionable, time-bound recommendations |
| **10. Document & Follow-up** (0.7 M) | Compile the HAZOP report, distribute, track actions to closure, and schedule re-review |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Team Composition, Scribe Role, Documentation**

**HAZOP Team Composition (3 Marks):**
- **HAZOP Leader (Chairman):** Independent facilitator, senior process safety engineer, guides discussion, ensures methodology adherence
- **Scribe (Recorder):** Documents all discussions in real-time, must be fluent in HAZOP terminology, typing speed important
- **Process Engineer:** Explains design intent, P&ID details, process parameters
- **Operations Expert:** Provides operating experience, startup/shutdown considerations, past incident insights
- **Instrumentation Engineer:** Reviews control loops, alarms, interlocks, safety instrumented systems
- **Mechanical Engineer:** Reviews equipment integrity, failure modes, maintenance requirements
- **Safety Engineer:** Assesses consequences, risk ranking, regulatory compliance
- **Maintenance Engineer:** Provides input on equipment reliability, maintenance procedures
- **Specialist (as needed):** Corrosion engineer, environmental specialist, civil/structural engineer

**Role of the Scribe (2 Marks):**
- **Real-time documentation** of all deviations, causes, consequences, and actions during HAZOP sessions
- Uses a **standard HAZOP worksheet format** (electronic or paper-based)
- **Captures verbatim** the team discussions, especially the rationale for recommendations
- **Cross-checks** with the HAZOP leader for accuracy before moving to the next node
- Maintains **confidentiality** of preliminary findings
- Compiles the **final HAZOP report** after the sessions are complete

**Standard HAZOP Documentation Format (2 Marks):**

The HAZOP report typically includes the following sections:
1. **Cover Page** — Project name, study type, date, signature
2. **Scope & Objectives** — Boundaries, inclusions, exclusions
3. **Team List** — Names, roles, affiliations, signatures
4. **Reference Documents** — P&IDs, PFDs, datasheets used
5. **Node Definitions** — Description and design intent of each node
6. **HAZOP Worksheets** — Completed worksheets with all columns filled
7. **Risk Matrix** — Severity, likelihood, and risk rating criteria used
8. **Action Register** — List of all recommendations, owners, target dates
9. **Assumptions & Limitations** — Any constraints or unverified information
10. **Conclusion & Sign-off** — Summary of findings, signatures of team and management

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing Design Intent (–2 marks):** Students often skip the design intent before applying guide words. Always state the design intent first — it is the *foundation* of HAZOP.
> 2. **Invalid Deviation Construction (–2 marks):** Writing deviations like "More Safety" or "No Accident" is **wrong**. A deviation is ALWAYS a guide word + a process parameter.
> 3. **Confusing HAZOP with FMEA (–3 marks):** Do not write component failure modes (e.g., "bearing seizure") as HAZOP deviations. HAZOP is parameter-based, not component-based.
> 4. **Listing causes but not consequences (–1 mark):** A deviation is incomplete without both causes AND consequences.
> 5. **Vague recommendations (–2 marks):** Writing "improve safety" or "increase maintenance" is **not actionable**. Recommendations must be **specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound** (SMART).
> 6. **Forgetting safeguard assessment (–1 mark):** Existing safeguards must be listed to demonstrate that the team has reviewed the current protection layers.
> 7. **Single-discipline team (–1 mark):** HAZOP requires a multi-disciplinary team. A team of only process engineers is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision (10 minutes before exam):** Go through this checklist. Each point is a high-frequency KTU exam topic.

### **A. Core HAZOP Concepts (Definitions to Memorize)**
- ✅ HAZOP = **H**azard **and Op**erability Study (note: it is "Operability", not "Operation")
- ✅ Origin: **ICI (Imperial Chemical Industries), 1960s, UK**
- ✅ Standard: **IEC 61882:2016**
- ✅ Type: **Qualitative, team-based, systematic**
- ✅ Primary Document: **P&ID (Piping and Instrumentation Diagram)**
- ✅ Primary Output: **HAZOP Worksheet + Action Register**

### **B. The 7 Guide Words (Memorize with Examples)**
- ✅ **NO / NONE** → No Flow, Empty, Vacuum
- ✅ **MORE / HIGH** → High Pressure, High Temperature, High Level
- ✅ **LESS / LOW** → Low Flow, Low Pressure, Low Temperature
- ✅ **AS WELL AS** → Two-Phase Flow, Contaminant Present
- ✅ **PART OF** → Partial Flow, Dilution, Stratification
- ✅ **REVERSE** → Reverse Flow, Phase Inversion
- ✅ **OTHER THAN** → Wrong Fluid, Wrong Step, Wrong Material

### **C. The Core 5 Process Parameters**
- ✅ **Flow (F)**
- ✅ **Pressure (P)**
- ✅ **Temperature (T)**
- ✅ **Level (L)**
- ✅ **Composition (C)**

### **D. HAZOP Team (6–10 Members, Multi-Disciplinary)**
- ✅ Leader (independent), Scribe, Process, Operations, I&C, Mechanical, Safety, Maintenance

### **E. HAZOP Procedure (10 Steps in Order)**
- ✅ Scope → Team → Info → Train → Nodes → Design Intent → Deviations → Causes/Consequences → Recommendations → Documentation → Follow-up

### **F. HAZOP Worksheet Columns (Mandatory)**
- ✅ Node → Design Intent → Guide Word → Parameter → Deviation → Causes → Consequences → Safeguards → Risk → Recommendations → Action By → Date → Status

### **G. Key Differences (Frequently Asked)**
- ✅ HAZOP vs FMEA (parameter-based vs component-based)
- ✅ HAZOP vs What-If (structured guide words vs open questions)
- ✅ HAZOP vs FTA (qualitative vs quantitative Boolean)

### **H. Important Risk Matrix Formulas**
- ✅ $R = S \times L$ (basic risk score)
- ✅ $R = S \times L \times C_{factor}$ (extended with cluster factor)
- ✅ Risk categories: Low (1–3), Medium (4–6), High (7–9), Very High (10–16)

### **I. Common Applications (Industries)**
- ✅ Oil & Gas, Petrochemical, Refinery, Pharmaceutical, Power, Nuclear, Food, Water Treatment

### **J. Limitations to Remember**
- ❌ Not for pure software, conceptual design, or single-component analysis
- ❌ Time-consuming and requires experienced facilitator
- ❌ Does not quantify probability (use LOPA/QRA for that)
- ❌ Dependent on P&ID completeness and team expertise

---

> **End of Module 1 — HAZOP Topic Notes**

topics.title: What If Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** What If Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WHAT-IF ANALYSIS (WIA)** is a structured, scenario-based, qualitative hazard identification and risk evaluation technique in which a multidisciplinary team systematically generates and examines hypothetical deviations ("What if...?") from the intended design, operation, or environment of a process, system, or facility, in order to identify potential hazards, evaluate their consequences, and recommend preventive or mitigative safeguards.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PBSFT404), What-If Analysis is classified under **Module 1 – Hazard Identification Techniques**, sitting alongside HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study), FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis), and Checklist Analysis. It is most commonly applied during the **conceptual and early engineering design stages** of chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, pharmaceutical, and manufacturing facilities, as well as for **modifications (Management of Change – MOC)** of existing plants.

The technique is formally documented in:
- **AIChE/CCPS** *Guidelines for Hazard Evaluation Procedures* (3rd Edition, 2008)
- **IEC 61882:2016** – *Hazard and operability studies (HAZOP studies) – Application guide* (also covers WIA as a precursor)
- **OSHA 1910.119** – Process Safety Management (PSM) lists What-If as an accepted PHA technique

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of What-If Analysis as a "Fire Drill for the Mind".** Imagine you are the head chef of a busy industrial kitchen. Before service begins, instead of just cooking, you pause and ask yourself:
> - *"What if the gas cylinder leaks?"*
> - *"What if the deep-fryer thermostat fails?"*
> - *"What if a new untrained helper slips on the wet floor?"*
> - *"What if the fire-suppression system is OFF during peak hours?"*
>
> You are not waiting for a real accident. You are **imagining the worst plausible scenarios** *before* they occur, brainstorming the consequences, and writing down precautions (training, alarms, SOPs). That mental exercise—**asking "what if" before something breaks**—is exactly what a What-If Analysis session does, but in a formal, documented, team-based setting inside a process plant.

The technique is **scenario-driven, not deviation-parameter driven** (unlike HAZOP, which uses *Guide Words + Parameters*). It is essentially a **structured brainstorming session** anchored to the P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram) or process flow diagram.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Meaning in WIA Context |
|---|---|
| **Node** | A logical section of the process under review (e.g., a reactor, storage tank, transfer line) |
| **What-If Question** | A hypothetical deviation phrased in plain English: *"What if the cooling water fails?"* |
| **Hazard** | A chemical or physical condition with potential to cause harm |
| **Consequence** | The outcome if the hazard is realized (fire, explosion, toxic release) |
| **Severity** | Categorical ranking of harm (typically **Catastrophic / Major / Moderate / Minor / Negligible**) |
| **Likelihood** | Probability category (**Frequent / Probable / Occasional / Remote / Improbable**) |
| **Risk Ranking** | Combination of Severity × Likelihood, often plotted on a **5×5 Risk Matrix** |
| **Recommendation** | Engineered or procedural safeguard suggested by the team |
| **Action Item** | A specific, time-bound, owner-assigned follow-up task |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Metrics Used in WIA:**
> - **Risk Matrix Dimensions:** **5 × 5** (Severity on Y-axis, Likelihood on X-axis) – *bolded because it is the most commonly used and the KTU-recommended default.*
> - **Typical Session Duration:** **2 to 4 hours per major process unit** for a focused WIA.
> - **Team Size:** **4 to 8 specialists** (process, mechanical, instrumentation, safety, operations).
> - **Documentation Output:** A *What-If Worksheet* with columns: **Question, Hazard, Consequence, Severity, Likelihood, Risk, Recommendations, Action Owner**.

## 1.4 Why is What-If Analysis Important?

- **Early-stage identification** of process hazards *before* detailed engineering.
- Complements other PHAs (HAZOP, FMEA, Checklist) by addressing **external events** (e.g., natural disasters, utility failures, human factors, security threats) that may not be captured by guide-word-based methods.
- **Flexible, less rigid** than HAZOP – suitable for non-process systems (e.g., warehousing, logistics, laboratories).
- **Cost-effective** – requires less preparation time than a full HAZOP.
- Supports **regulatory compliance** (OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, Seveso Directive).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The What-If Analysis is not a single calculation—it is a **structured, multi-stage qualitative procedure**. The KTU examiner frequently asks students to *list the steps* or *explain the methodology* (often a 7-mark sub-question). Below is the rigorous, stepwise logic.

### Stage 1 — Define Scope, Boundaries & Objectives

- The **Leader (PHA Facilitator)** establishes:
  - Physical and operational boundaries of the study.
  - Reason for the study (new project, MOC, periodic revalidation, incident investigation).
  - Desired output (list of hazards, risk-ranked recommendations, compliance report).
- **Why:** A poorly defined scope leads to missed hazards (e.g., utilities, off-site) or wasted effort on out-of-scope items.

### Stage 2 — Assemble the Multidisciplinary Team

A balanced WIA team includes:

- **Team Leader / Facilitator** – experienced in PHA methodology, neutral, guides discussion.
- **Process Engineer** – knows design intent and operating philosophy.
- **Operations / Production Supervisor** – knows real-world operating quirks and near-misses.
- **Maintenance / Mechanical Engineer** – knows equipment failure history.
- **Instrumentation & Control Engineer** – knows interlock logic and DCS architecture.
- **Safety / HSE Specialist** – applies consequence analysis and regulatory lens.
- **Scribe / Recorder** – captures every question, answer, and recommendation in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a team and not a single expert?** Single-expert reviews suffer from **cognitive fixation** and **blind-spot bias**. Diverse perspectives surface hazards that no individual would anticipate (e.g., the operations guy remembers that a particular pump vibrates at high load).

### Stage 3 — Gather Reference Information

The team must have access to:

- **P&IDs (Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams)**
- **Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)**
- **Equipment datasheets & material specifications**
- **Operating procedures & SOPs**
- **Plot plans & layout drawings**
- **Utility flow diagrams** (steam, air, nitrogen, cooling water, electrical)
- **Past incident reports, near-miss logs, MOC records**
- **Hazardous area classification drawings**
- **Emergency response plans**

### Stage 4 — Divide the Process into Logical Sections (Nodes)

- Break the facility into **manageable chunks**: feed system, reactor, separator, storage, loading, utilities, fire protection, etc.
- **Why:** Keeps each discussion focused; prevents the team from getting lost in the entire plant at once.

### Stage 5 — Brainstorm "What-If" Questions

The facilitator walks the team through each node, prompting hypothetical deviations. Question categories include:

| Category | Example What-If Questions |
|---|---|
| **Equipment Failure** | What if the centrifugal pump seal fails? What if a heat-exchanger tube ruptures? |
| **Utility Failure** | What if cooling water is lost? What if instrument air pressure drops to zero? |
| **Human Error** | What if the operator opens the wrong valve? What if startup sequence is skipped? |
| **Process Deviation** | What if feed flow surges to 2× design? What if reactor temperature exceeds 300 °C? |
| **External Events** | What if a 1-in-100-year flood occurs? What if an external fire spreads to the tank farm? |
| **Maintenance / MOC** | What if a bypass line is left open during maintenance? |
| **Instrumentation & Control** | What if the level transmitter gives a false-high reading? What if a solenoid valve fails closed? |
| **Security / Sabotage** | What if an unauthorized person accesses the control room? |
| **Emergency Systems** | What if the firewater pump fails to start? |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb (KTU-favorite):** Generate **at least 1 What-If question per major piece of equipment**, plus **at least 5–10 questions per utility system**.

### Stage 6 — Identify Hazards & Consequences

For **each** What-If question, the team discusses:
- *What could go wrong?* (Hazard)
- *How bad could it be?* (Consequence – fire, explosion, toxic release, environmental release, business interruption, injury, fatality)

### Stage 7 — Assess Existing Safeguards

The team lists **what is already in place** to prevent or mitigate the scenario:
- Engineered safeguards: relief valves, dikes, interlocks, alarms, trips, fire suppression, explosion-proof equipment, secondary containment.
- Procedural safeguards: SOPs, permits-to-work, training, inspection, emergency drills.

### Stage 8 — Estimate Risk (Severity × Likelihood)

The team assigns a **qualitative category** to each scenario using the **5 × 5 Risk Matrix**. This produces a **Risk Rating** (Low / Medium / High / Very High).

> [!WARNING]
> In WIA, risk is **qualitative**, not quantified. There is no specific QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment) in a WIA. The team agrees on a category by consensus, not by exact frequency calculation. The KTU examiner will *not* expect a numerical formula beyond the matrix logic.

### Stage 9 — Develop Recommendations

For every **Medium, High, or Very High** risk, the team generates **specific, actionable recommendations**:
- *Add a high-high level interlock on TK-101.*
- *Provide secondary containment under the chemical storage shed.*
- *Update SOP to include step-by-step emergency shutdown procedure.*

### Stage 10 — Document & Distribute the Report

A formal **What-If Report** is produced, signed by the team, and circulated to:
- Plant management
- Engineering design team
- Maintenance & operations departments
- Regulatory auditors (if applicable)

### Stage 11 — Resolve & Close-Out

Each recommendation is assigned to an **Action Owner** with a **target date**. A **follow-up review** (often a second WIA or a revalidation HAZOP) confirms closure.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> What-If Analysis is **qualitative**, so there are no heavy engineering formulas. However, the **Risk Matrix scoring logic** is mathematical and frequently tested.

| # | Concept | Formula / Logic | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Rating (qualitative)** | $R = S \times L$ | Dimensionless categorical index; $S$ = Severity (1–5), $L$ = Likelihood (1–5) |
| 2 | **Numerical Risk Score (optional)** | $R_{score} = S \times L$ | Range 1 to 25 (purely a sorting aid, not a probability) |
| 3 | **Severity Scale (CCPS typical)** | 1=Negligible, 2=Minor, 3=Moderate, 4=Major, 5=Catastrophic | Unitless |
| 4 | **Likelihood Scale** | 1=Improbable, 2=Remote, 3=Occasional, 4=Probable, 5=Frequent | Unitless |
| 5 | **Risk Category Threshold** | $R_{score} \geq 15$ → **High / Very High**, $R_{score} \in [8,14]$ → **Medium**, $R_{score} \leq 7$ → **Low** | Industry convention; KTU-accepted default |
| 6 | **Total Number of What-If Questions (planning heuristic)** | $N_{q} \approx N_{equip} + k \cdot N_{utility}$ | $N_{equip}$ = major equipment count, $N_{utility}$ = utility systems, $k$ = 5 to 10 |
| 7 | **Estimated Team-Hours (planning heuristic)** | $T_{hr} = 0.5 \cdot N_{P\&ID} + 2$ | $N_{P\&ID}$ = number of P&IDs in scope; $T_{hr}$ in hours |
| 8 | **ALARP Reduction Check (post-WIA)** | Residual Risk $R_{res} \leq R_{tolerable}$ | Used after recommendations are implemented |
| 9 | **Hazard Frequency Categorization (qualitative)** | $f \in [\text{less than } 10^{-6}/\text{yr},\ \text{more than } 10^{-1}/\text{yr}]$ | Range used to map to Likelihood 1–5 |
| 10 | **Consequence Distance Reference** | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot C_{t}}}$ | Not part of WIA itself, but referenced in consequence discussion (toxic gas dispersion) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Notation Used (No Pipes in Tables):**
> - $S$ = Severity (integer 1 to 5)
> - $L$ = Likelihood (integer 1 to 5)
> - $R$ = Risk Rating (integer 1 to 25, or qualitative Low/Medium/High/Very High)
> - $N_{q}$ = Number of What-If questions generated
> - $T_{hr}$ = Estimated team-hours required
> - $R_{tolerable}$ = Tolerable risk threshold (organizational or regulatory)
> - $r$ = Downwind hazard distance (m) when discussing toxic dispersion consequences

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas:** Upstream platforms use WIA to assess helicopter crashes, dropped objects, ship collisions, blowouts.
- **Chemical Plants:** Early identification of runaway reaction scenarios.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** WIA on solvent recovery, clean-in-place (CIP) systems.
- **Warehousing & Logistics:** What-If of forklift accidents, pallet-rack collapse, lithium battery thermal runaway.
- **Renewable Energy:** Battery energy storage systems (BESS) – thermal runaway propagation scenarios.
- **Emergency Planning:** What-If of simultaneous multi-utility failures during a cyclone/flood.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — Risk Scoring Logic Used in WIA

The numerical risk score is *derived* from the qualitative risk matrix as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define ordinal scales} \quad & S \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}, \quad L \in \{1,2,3,4,5\} \\
\text{Step 2: Compute raw risk score} \quad & R_{score} = S \times L \\
\text{Step 3: Map to risk category} \quad & R_{cat} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Very High} & \text{if } R_{score} \geq 15 \\
\text{High} & \text{if } 10 \leq R_{score} \leq 14 \\
\text{Medium} & \text{if } 5 \leq R_{score} \leq 9 \\
\text{Low} & \text{if } R_{score} \leq 4
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner often tests whether students can:
> 1. Justify the scale values.
> 2. Plot the 5×5 matrix.
> 3. Apply the threshold logic to a given scenario.

### Example Worked Problem (KTU-style)

**Scenario:** A reactor operates at 250 °C with a runaway exotherm risk. The team estimates:
- Severity = **5** (Catastrophic – potential vessel rupture and fatality)
- Likelihood = **3** (Occasional – one thermal event expected every 5 years)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{score} &= S \times L \\
&= 5 \times 3 \\
&= 15
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the threshold:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{score} = 15 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad R_{cat} = \text{Very High} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Recommendation: Install a high-high temperature interlock,} \\
&\quad \text{independent pressure relief, and a quench system.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Strength vs. Weakness of WIA

| Dimension | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|
| **Flexibility** | Can address any type of hazard (process, human, external, security) | Highly dependent on team experience — may miss hazards if team is weak |
| **Speed** | Faster than HAZOP; ~2 hrs per major unit | May be *too* quick, leading to superficial analysis |
| **Documentation** | Clear, simple worksheet format (Question / Hazard / Consequence / Safeguards / Recommendations) | Less structured than HAZOP, no standard guide-word library |
| **Cost** | Low preparation cost (no node-by-node deviation catalogue needed) | Risk of *false sense of completeness* |
| **Scope Coverage** | Excellent for utilities, external events, and human factors | Weaker for tight process-deviation logic (HAZOP is better) |
| **Suitability for Modifications** | Ideal for MOC reviews | Not suitable for complex interlock logic (FMEA is better) |

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — WIA vs. HAZOP vs. FMEA vs. Checklist

| Feature | What-If | HAZOP | FMEA | Checklist |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Scenario brainstorming | Guide-word + parameter | Equipment failure mode | Yes/No questions vs. list |
| **Driver** | Imagination of team | Standard deviation set | Component failure | Pre-made list |
| **Best for** | Early design, MOC, external events | Detailed process design | Mechanical / electrical | Compliance review |
| **Output** | Recommendations | Causes, consequences, actions | RPN (Risk Priority Number) | Compliance gaps |
| **Time** | Low–Medium | High | Medium | Low |
| **Rigor** | Low–Medium | High | High | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-favorite question pattern:** *"Compare What-If Analysis with HAZOP."* Memorize the 6-row table above; it carries 7 marks easily.

## 3.4 Worked Example — End-to-End WIA Worksheet Entry

**Process Node:** Sodium Hypochlorite Storage Tank (TK-201)

| What-If Question | Hazard | Consequence | Existing Safeguards | S | L | R | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| What if the tank is overfilled during tank-truck unloading? | Loss of containment of corrosive oxidizer | Toxic Cl₂ release, operator burn injury, environmental release | Level indicator, high-level alarm, SOP for unloading | 4 | 3 | 12 (High) | Add independent high-high level switch + automatic shutoff valve |
| What if the unloading hose ruptures? | Mechanical failure | Spill of 5 m³ NaOCl, soil contamination, slip hazard | Dike wall, spill kit, PPE | 3 | 3 | 9 (Medium) | Add quick-coupling with dry-break, monthly hose integrity test |
| What if the vent line is blocked? | Pressure build-up | Tank rupture, projectile | Pressure-vacuum relief valve, routine inspection | 5 | 2 | 10 (High) | Install redundant relief device, alarm on vent pressure |
| What if ambient temperature exceeds 45 °C? | Decomposition of NaOCl | O₂ release, pressure rise, potential rupture | Vent, dyke | 3 | 2 | 6 (Medium) | Add temperature sensor + cooling spray or shade structure |

> [!NOTE]
> This is the exact format the KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark answer for *"Conduct a What-If Analysis on the given process (PFD or scenario)"*.

## 3.5 Quantitative Heuristic — Number of Questions Planning

For a plant with **$E$ major equipment** and **$U$ utility systems**, a planning estimate for question generation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{q,min} &= E + 5U \\
N_{q,max} &= 2E + 10U \\
N_{q,target} &= \frac{N_{q,min} + N_{q,max}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** If $E = 12$ equipment and $U = 4$ utility systems,

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{q,min} &= 12 + 5(4) = 32 \\
N_{q,max} &= 24 + 40 = 64 \\
N_{q,target} &= \frac{32 + 64}{2} = 48 \text{ questions}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU may ask: *"Estimate the number of What-If questions for a system with 10 major equipment and 3 utility systems."* Use the formula above.

## 4.1 What-If Analysis Methodology Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Scope and Objectives] --> B[Assemble Multidisciplinary Team 4 to 8 members]

    C --> D[Divide Plant into Logical Nodes Sections]
    D --> E[For Each Node: Brainstorm What If Questions]
    E --> F[Identify Hazard and Consequence for each question]
    F --> G[List Existing Safeguards Engineered and Procedural]
    G --> H[Assign Severity 1 to 5 and Likelihood 1 to 5]
    H --> I[Compute Risk Score R equals S times L]
    I --> J{Risk Category}
    J -->|Very High or High| K[Develop Specific Recommendations]
    J -->|Medium| L[Consider Recommendations]
    J -->|Low| M[Accept with Documentation]
    K --> N[Assign Action Owner and Target Date]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compile Final WIA Report and Worksheet]
    O --> P[Distribute to Management Engineering and Operations]
    P --> Q[Follow up and Verify Action Closure]
    Q --> R[End of WIA Cycle]
```
