# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define What-If Analysis. Mention any two situations where it is preferred over HAZOP. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **What-If Analysis (WIA)** is a structured, team-based hazard identification technique in which a multidisciplinary group systematically generates hypothetical "What if…?" questions about a process, system, or facility to identify potential hazards, evaluate their consequences, and recommend safeguards.
>
> **Two situations where WIA is preferred over HAZOP:**
> 1. **Early conceptual / feasibility stage** – when detailed P&IDs and node-by-node deviation logic are not yet available.
> 2. **External events and human factors assessment** – e.g., natural disasters, utility failures, security threats, where HAZOP's guide-word approach is too rigid.
> 3. *(Bonus point)* Management of Change (MOC) reviews for small modifications where a full HAZOP is disproportionate.

---

### Q2. List the typical columns of a What-If Analysis worksheet. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A standard What-If worksheet contains the following columns:

1. **What-If Question** – the hypothetical deviation being examined.
2. **Hazard / Cause** – the source of potential harm.
3. **Consequence** – outcome if the hazard is realized.
4. **Existing Safeguards** – engineered (alarms, trips, dikes) and procedural (SOPs, training).
5. **Severity (S)** – categorical rating, typically 1 to 5.
6. **Likelihood (L)** – categorical rating, typically 1 to 5.
7. **Risk Rating (R = S × L)** – with risk category (Low / Medium / High / Very High).
8. **Recommendations** – specific, actionable safeguards.
9. **Action Owner & Target Date** – person responsible and closure deadline.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often write *only* the steps of WIA without mentioning **team composition** (3-mark penalty).
> - Students confuse WIA with HAZOP and start using **guide words** (deviation, parameter, node) wrongly – KTU deducts 2 marks.
> - When the question asks to *conduct* a WIA on a PFD, students forget to **include severity, likelihood, and risk rating columns** (lose 2–3 marks).
> - Do **not** write Python / code in this subject's answer sheet. WIA is qualitative, descriptive, and tabular.
> - Always end the answer with a **risk matrix** or at least a **risk categorization** to score full marks.

---

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step methodology of conducting a What-If Analysis** for a process plant. Include team composition, scope definition, question generation, and documentation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare **What-If Analysis with HAZOP** in terms of approach, application stage, flexibility, team size, time, documentation, and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**1. Define Scope, Boundaries & Objectives (1 mark)**

The **PHA Facilitator** clearly defines:
- The **physical boundaries** of the study (e.g., battery limit of a tank farm, or feed system to product storage).
- The **operational phase** being studied (normal operation, startup, shutdown, maintenance, emergency).
- The **purpose** of the study: new design, MOC, periodic revalidation, incident follow-up, or compliance with OSHA PSM / EPA RMP.

**2. Assemble the Multidisciplinary Team (1 mark)**

A typical WIA team has **4 to 8 members**:
- **Team Leader / Facilitator** – neutral, experienced in PHA methodology.
- **Process Engineer** – design intent and material balance.
- **Operations Supervisor** – practical operating experience and near-miss history.
- **Maintenance Engineer** – equipment failure history.
- **Instrumentation & Control Engineer** – DCS, interlocks, ESD logic.
- **HSE / Safety Specialist** – regulatory and consequence analysis.
- **Scribe** – real-time documentation.

**3. Gather Reference Documents (1 mark)**

The team must be equipped with:
- **Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)**
- **Process Flow Diagrams (P&Ds)**
- **Operating procedures (SOPs)**
- **Equipment datasheets and material specifications**
- **Plot plans, hazardous area classification drawings**
- **Past incident reports, near-miss logs, MOC records**

**4. Divide the Process into Nodes (1 mark)**

The plant is broken into **logical, manageable nodes** (e.g., feed system, reactor, separator, storage, loading, utilities, fire water network, control room).

**5. Brainstorm "What-If" Questions (1 mark)**

For each node, the team poses hypothetical questions. **Question categories include:**

- **Equipment failure:** What if pump seal leaks?
- **Utility failure:** What if cooling water is lost?
- **Human error:** What if operator opens the wrong valve?
- **Process deviation:** What if feed surges to 2× design?
- **External events:** What if flood level exceeds dyke?
- **Security / sabotage:** What if cyber-attack disables DCS?

**6. Identify Hazard, Consequence, and Existing Safeguards (1 mark)**

For each question, the team identifies:
- The **hazard** (source of harm).
- The **consequence** (fire, explosion, toxic release, fatality, environmental damage).
- The **existing safeguards** (alarms, trips, relief valves, dikes, SOPs, training).

**7. Assign Severity, Likelihood & Compute Risk (1 mark)**

Using a **5 × 5 Risk Matrix**:
- $R_{score} = S \times L$
- Categorize as **Low (≤4) / Medium (5–9) / High (10–14) / Very High (≥15)**.

**8. Develop Recommendations & Action Items (0.5 mark)**

For every **High or Very High** scenario, the team proposes specific engineered or procedural safeguards, with **action owner** and **target closure date**.

**9. Documentation & Reporting (0.5 mark)**

A **What-If Report** with the completed worksheet, signed by the team, is circulated to management, design, operations, and (if applicable) regulatory auditors.

**10. Follow-up & Closure (0.5 mark – bonus)**

A revalidation meeting (or a follow-up WIA) confirms that all action items are closed.

> **Key Point for Full Marks:** Always mention that WIA is **scenario-based**, **less rigid than HAZOP**, and **excellent for early-stage and MOC reviews**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b): Comparison Table (WIA vs. HAZOP) **[7 Marks]**

| S. No. | Feature | What-If Analysis | HAZOP |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Approach** | Scenario-based brainstorming ("What if…?") | Guide-word + parameter (e.g., No Flow, High Pressure) |
| 2 | **Application Stage** | Early conceptual / feasibility / MOC | Detailed engineering / late design / pre-startup |
| 3 | **Flexibility** | High – can address any hazard | Rigid – only process deviations |
| 4 | **Team Size** | 4 to 8 members | 5 to 8 members (similar) |
| 5 | **Time Required** | Low – ~2 hours per major unit | High – 2 to 4 hours per node |
| 6 | **Documentation** | Free-form What-If worksheet | Standardized HAZOP worksheet with deviations, causes, consequences |
| 7 | **Best Suited For** | External events, utilities, human factors, security | Process deviations, operating parameter excursions |
| 8 | **Limitations** | Highly dependent on team experience; less systematic | Time-consuming; not suitable for early stage with no P&IDs |

> *(Each row above carries ~0.75 mark. Cover 8 rows for 6 marks + 1 mark for a concluding sentence on when to choose WIA over HAZOP.)*

---

### QUESTION B — (14 Marks, ALTERNATIVE) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** A **sodium hypochlorite storage facility** has the following equipment: 1 storage tank (TK-201, 20 m³), 1 transfer pump (P-201), 1 unloading hose from tank-truck, 1 vent line, 1 dyke, and a level indicator. **Conduct a What-If Analysis** for at least 6 scenarios. Present your answer in a tabular worksheet format with columns: What-If, Hazard, Consequence, Existing Safeguards, Severity, Likelihood, Risk, and Recommendation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of What-If Analysis. When would you *not* recommend its use? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

| # | What-If Question | Hazard | Consequence | Existing Safeguards | S | L | R | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | What if the tank is overfilled during truck unloading? | Loss of containment of oxidizer | NaOCl spill, Cl₂ release, operator burn, environmental contamination | Level gauge, high-level alarm, SOP for unloading | 4 | 3 | 12 (High) | Add high-high level switch with automatic shutoff solenoid valve |
| 2 | What if the unloading hose ruptures? | Mechanical failure of hose | 5 m³ NaOCl spill, slip hazard, soil contamination | Dyke (110% containment), spill kit, PPE | 3 | 3 | 9 (Medium) | Use dry-break coupling, monthly hydrostatic test, logbook for hose age |
| 3 | What if the vent line is blocked (e.g., by ice, debris, or polymer build-up)? | Pressure build-up inside tank | Tank rupture, projectile injury, NaOCl aerosol release | Pressure-vacuum relief valve, visual inspection schedule | 5 | 2 | 10 (High) | Install redundant relief device, alarm on vent-line pressure drop |
| 4 | What if the transfer pump P-201 seal fails? | Loss of prime, leakage of NaOCl | Localized spill, vapor release, pump damage | Mechanical seal, drip tray, area ventilation | 3 | 3 | 9 (Medium) | Upgrade to double mechanical seal with barrier fluid |
| 5 | What if instrument air fails during unloading? | Loss of pneumatic control valves | Uncontrolled flow, overfill, spill | Manual bypass valve, SOP for manual control | 4 | 2 | 8 (Medium) | Add air receiver with 30-min backup, install air-pressure low alarm |
| 6 | What if the dyke is breached by external impact (e.g., vehicle collision)? | Loss of secondary containment | Uncontrolled pool spread, environmental release | Dyke wall, vehicle wheel stopper, security barrier | 4 | 2 | 8 (Medium) | Reinforce dyke with crash barrier, periodic integrity test |
| 7 | What if the operator connects the truck to the wrong tank? | Cross-contamination / wrong chemical | Violent reaction, tank rupture, toxic gas | Color-coded hoses, line-up checklist, label verification | 5 | 2 | 10 (High) | Add keyed quick-connectors unique to each chemical service, mandatory verification log |
| 8 | What if a cyclone/flood submerges the dyke? | External natural event | Wash-out of NaOCl to nearby water body | Dyke height, drainage valve (normally closed) | 5 | 2 | 10 (High) | Raise dyke height, install flood monitoring with SMS alert |

> *(Each row = ~0.75 mark. Provide 6–8 rows for full 7 marks. Severity and Likelihood ratings must be justified.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b): Advantages & Limitations **[7 Marks]**

**Advantages (3.5 marks):**

1. **Flexibility:** Can address any type of hazard — process, utility, human, external, security, cyber.
2. **Speed & Low Cost:** Requires only 2–4 hours per major unit; no elaborate preparation of deviation catalogues.
3. **Ideal for Early Stages & MOC:** Applicable even when P&IDs are not fully developed.
4. **Captures External Events:** Excellent for natural disasters, security threats, multi-utility failures (often missed by HAZOP).
5. **Simple Documentation:** Plain-English questions, easy for non-specialists to understand.
6. **Encourages Free Thinking:** Promotes team discussion and surfaces "what was never thought of."

**Limitations (3.5 marks):**

1. **Highly Dependent on Team:** If the team lacks experience, hazards may be missed.
2. **Not Rigorous / Less Systematic:** No standard guide-word library; quality varies.
3. **No Quantitative Output:** Cannot directly feed into LOPA / SIL determination (HAZOP is better for that).
4. **Inconsistent Documentation:** Free-form worksheets may not align with regulatory checklists.
5. **Overlapping Scenarios:** Different team members may pose the same question in different words, leading to inefficiency.
6. **Not Suitable for Complex Interlock Logic:** FMEA / Fault Tree is better for detailed instrumented systems.

**When NOT to Recommend WIA:**

- For **detailed process safety studies** of a complex chemical reactor where **SIL determination** is required.
- For **complex instrumented protective systems** (FMEA is more appropriate).
- When **regulatory compliance mandates a specific technique** (e.g., HAZOP as per COMAH / OSHA PSM in some jurisdictions).
- When the **team has limited experience** and cannot brainstorm adequately.
- For **post-incident root cause analysis** (use TapRooT / Why-Tree instead).

> *(Wrap up with a 1-line summary: "WIA is best used as a *complementary* technique alongside HAZOP, FMEA, and Checklist, not as a stand-alone PHA for high-hazard facilities.")*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- **Definition:** What-If Analysis = structured, team-based, scenario ("What if…?") brainstorming for hazard identification.
- **Origin / Standard:** CCPS *Guidelines for Hazard Evaluation Procedures* (AIChE, 3rd Ed., 2008); referenced by OSHA PSM and EPA RMP.
- **Team Size:** 4 to 8 members; **must include a Facilitator + Scribe**.
- **Team Members:** Process, Operations, Maintenance, Instrumentation, HSE, plus Scribe.
- **Methodology (11 Steps):** Define scope → Assemble team → Gather documents → Divide into nodes → Brainstorm What-If questions → Identify hazard & consequence → List existing safeguards → Score S × L → Develop recommendations → Document report → Follow up & close.
- **Risk Matrix:** 5 × 5; $R = S \times L$; ranges 1 to 25.
- **Risk Thresholds:** Low (≤4), Medium (5–9), High (10–14), Very High (≥15).
- **Severity Scale:** 1 = Negligible, 2 = Minor, 3 = Moderate, 4 = Major, 5 = Catastrophic.
- **Likelihood Scale:** 1 = Improbable, 2 = Remote, 3 = Occasional, 4 = Probable, 5 = Frequent.
- **Question Categories:** Equipment, Utility, Human Error, Process Deviation, External Events, Security, Emergency Systems.
- **Best Used For:** Early conceptual design, MOC reviews, external events, human factors, security.
- **Not Suitable For:** Detailed SIL determination, complex interlock logic, regulated HAZOP-required studies.
- **WIA vs. HAZOP:** WIA is flexible & fast; HAZOP is systematic & rigorous. WIA = "what if" questions; HAZOP = guide-word + parameter deviations.
- **Planning Formula:** $N_{q,target} = \dfrac{(E + 5U) + (2E + 10U)}{2}$.
- **Time Estimate:** $T_{hr} \approx 0.5 \cdot N_{P\&ID} + 2$ hours.
- **Output Deliverable:** *What-If Worksheet* with 8–9 columns (Question, Hazard, Consequence, Safeguards, S, L, R, Recommendation, Action Owner & Date).
- **Follow-up:** Re-WIA or HAZOP revalidation to confirm closure of action items.
- **Complementarity:** WIA complements HAZOP, FMEA, and Checklist — never replaces them in high-hazard industries.
- **Examiner Traps:** (i) Using HAZOP guide words in a WIA answer; (ii) Forgetting severity/likelihood columns; (iii) Not mentioning team composition; (iv) Writing code/Python.
- **Regulatory Anchors:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM); EPA 40 CFR Part 68 (RMP); Seveso III Directive (EU); ISO 31000 (risk management family).
- **Quick Mnemonic for Question Categories:** **"E-U-H-P-E-S-M"** → **E**quipment, **U**tility, **H**uman, **P**rocess, **E**xternal, **S**ecurity, e**M**ergency.
- **Golden Rule of WIA:** *"If you cannot think of a question, you have not invited the right person to the room."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Case Studies" in the HIRA Context?

In **Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA)**, **Case Studies** are in-depth, retrospective investigations of real-world industrial, chemical, petrochemical, and process safety disasters. They are not merely historical accounts — they are systematic post-mortem analyses that allow safety engineers to:

- **Reconstruct** the chain of events (causal chain) that led to a major accident
- **Identify** the latent failures (management, design, maintenance) and immediate triggers
- **Quantify** the human, environmental, and economic consequences
- **Derive** engineering, regulatory, and procedural lessons that strengthen future HIRA frameworks
- **Calibrate** risk assessment tools (HAZOP, FMEA, LOPA, Bow-Tie, QRA) against real failure data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A *case study* in safety engineering is a documented, evidence-based retrospective analysis of a major hazard incident, which is used as a learning instrument to validate hazard identification methodologies, quantify failure frequencies, and demonstrate the practical application of the hierarchy of controls (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE).

### 1.2 Why Study Disasters? — The Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Black Box of an Airplane":**
> Think of a case study like reading the **black box flight recorder** of a crashed aircraft. The flight data alone does not bring passengers back, but studying it teaches the industry how to design the *next* aircraft so that the same crash never happens again. Similarly, a disaster is a "black box" for process safety. Each Bhopal, Flixborough, Piper Alpha, or Seveso is a painful but priceless experiment that nature has run for us — we must extract every drop of learning from it.

The five "W's" of case study analysis in HIRA are:

| Question | Purpose in HIRA |
|---|---|
| **What** happened? | Establish the factual sequence of events |
| **Who** was affected? | Quantify human/environmental cost (fatalities, injuries, displacement) |
| **Why** did it happen? | Trace root causes (technical + organizational) |
| **How** could it have been prevented? | Map barriers that failed (Swiss Cheese model) |
| **What** must be done now? | Formulate new regulations, standards, and HIRA methodologies |

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Case Studies

When KTU examiners expect you to *quantify* a disaster, you must use the following standard safety engineering metrics (always expressed in **bold** units):

- **Fatalities (F)** — number of immediate + long-term deaths
- **Injuries (I)** — categorized as minor, serious, or critical
- **Economic Loss (₹ / $)** — direct + indirect
- **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)** — number of fatalities per $10^8$ man-hours
- **Individual Risk (IR)** — probability of fatality per person per year
- **Societal Risk** — expressed via **F-N Curves** showing cumulative frequency (F) of events causing N or more fatalities
- **Displacement / Evacuation (people)** — indicator of off-site impact severity

### 1.4 The Taxonomy of Case Studies Relevant to KTU Module 1

The KTU 2024 syllabus (S6 Safety & Fire Technology) lists the following canonical case studies for Module 1:

1. **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)** — Methyl Isocyanate release
2. **Flixborough Disaster (1974)** — Caprolactam plant explosion
3. **Texas City Disaster (1947)** — Ammonium nitrate ship detonation
4. **Seveso Disaster (1976)** — Dioxin (TCDD) release
5. **Chernobyl Disaster (1986)** — Nuclear reactor explosion
6. **Piper Alpha Disaster (1988)** — Offshore oil platform fire

These six are the **"Big Six"** of process safety pedagogy and form the backbone of every HIRA textbook.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Analytical Framework Used to Study a Case Study

Every KTU-quality case study analysis must follow a **structured 7-step framework**:

1. **Incident Synopsis** — Date, location, plant, and one-line summary
2. **Hazard Identification** — Identify the hazardous material, condition, or energy source
3. **Causal Chain Reconstruction** — Build the event tree (what happened, in what order)
4. **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** — Apply techniques such as 5-Why, Fishbone (Ishikawa), or HFACS
5. **Barrier / Swiss Cheese Analysis** — Identify which independent protection layers (IPLs) failed
6. **Consequence Quantification** — Estimate fatalities, injuries, economic loss, environmental impact
7. **Lessons Learned & Mitigation** — Recommendations to prevent recurrence, mapped to hierarchy of controls

### 2.2 The Three Universal Lenses of Case Study Analysis

Every disaster can be examined through three complementary lenses:

> **A. Technical Lens** — What equipment, instrument, or process failed?
> Examples: BPCS failure, relief valve stuck, pipe corrosion, gasket rupture
>
> **B. Human & Organizational Lens** — What decisions, omissions, or training deficits contributed?
> Examples: Poor Permit-to-Work, lack of Management of Change, operator fatigue, communication failure
>
> **C. Systemic / Cultural Lens** — What regulatory, economic, or cultural pressure allowed the hazard to exist?
> Examples: Cost-cutting, regulatory capture, normalization of deviance, regulatory gaps

The **Swiss Cheese Model** (James Reason, 1990) is the universal conceptual tool used in HIRA to represent how multiple defense layers must all fail simultaneously for a disaster to occur.

### 2.3 The Bow-Tie Model — A Compact Risk Visualization

The **Bow-Tie Diagram** is a KTU-favourite representation combining a **fault tree** (left side: causes) and an **event tree** (right side: consequences) with **prevention barriers** on the left and **mitigation barriers** on the right.

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Threats}, \text{Consequences}, \text{Barriers})$$

Mathematically:

$$R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (F_i \times C_i \times \prod_{j=1}^{m} (1 - B_{ij}))$$

Where:
- $F_i$ = frequency of initiating event *i* (per year)
- $C_i$ = consequence magnitude of outcome *i*
- $B_{ij}$ = probability of failure of barrier *j* for event *i*
- $n$ = number of initiating events
- $m$ = number of barriers

### 2.4 The ALARP Principle

All HIRA frameworks ultimately apply the **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** principle:

> A risk must be reduced until the cost of further reduction is "grossly disproportionate" to the residual risk benefit.

Mathematically:

$$\text{Continue reducing risk while} \quad \frac{\Delta \text{Risk Reduction}}{\Delta \text{Cost}} \geq \frac{1}{\text{Disproportionality Factor } (\beta)}$$

If the ratio drops below $1/\beta$, the risk is considered **ALARP** and further reduction is not mandatory.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination-Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All the following formulas, constants, and units are examinable. Memorize them.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fundamental Risk Equation** | $R = F \times C$ | $F$ = Frequency (yr⁻¹), $C$ = Consequence (fatalities or ₹) |
| 2 | **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)** | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{N_f \times 10^{8}}{T_h}$ | $N_f$ = fatalities, $T_h$ = total man-hours |
| 3 | **Individual Risk (IR)** | $\text{IR} = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot p(f \mid i)$ | Per person per year |
| 4 | **F-N Curve Cumulative Frequency** | $F(N) = \sum_{N_i \geq N} f_i$ | $F$ = events per year causing $\geq N$ fatalities |
| 5 | **ALARP Disproportionality Test** | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{\Delta C} \geq \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ | $\beta$ is the industry-accepted disproportionality factor (typically 2–10 for major hazards) |
| 6 | **Probit for Toxic Load** | $Y = a + b \ln(V^{n} \cdot t)$ | $V$ = concentration (ppm), $t$ = exposure time (min) |
| 7 | **TNT Equivalency for Explosions** | $W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{\text{TNT}}}$ | $M$ = mass released, $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion, $E_{\text{TNT}} = 4680$ kJ/kg, $\eta$ = yield factor (1–10%) |
| 8 | **VCE Overpressure (TNT method)** | $Z_e = \dfrac{R}{(W_{\text{TNT}})^{1/3}}$ | $R$ = distance, $Z_e$ = scaled distance (m/kg^(1/3)) |
| 9 | **Pool Fire Heat Flux** | $q'' = \dfrac{\dot{m}'' \cdot \Delta H_c}{4 \pi r^2}$ | For point-source approximation |
| 10 | **Jet Fire Length (API RP 521)** | $L_j = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot \sqrt{\tau}}{\sqrt{\rho_a}}$ | $\tau$ = stoichiometric air-fuel ratio |
| 11 | **Hazard Rate from Operating Data** | $\lambda = \dfrac{N_{\text{failures}}}{N_{\text{components}} \cdot t}$ | Failures per component-hour |
| 12 | **Layer of Protection (LOPA) Probability** | $P_{\text{midway}} = \prod_{k=1}^{K} PFD_k$ | $PFD$ = Probability of Failure on Demand per layer |

### 2.6 Why Case Studies Matter in Real-World Engineering

Case studies are not academic luxuries — they directly inform:

- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** databases (e.g., OREDA, HSE UK Failure Rate Database)
- **Land-use planning** (safety distance calculations around Seveso-type establishments)
- **Regulatory design** (Seveso Directive I/II/III in EU, OSHA PSM, Indian SMPV Rules)
- **Insurance underwriting** (Major Hazard Installation insurance premiums)
- **Emergency preparedness** (Off-site Emergency Plans under Indian EP Act 1986)
- **Corporate Safety Case** development (post-Piper Alpha in UK offshore)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

This section presents **exhaustive, board-quality analyses** of the most frequently examined KTU case studies. Each analysis follows the 7-step HIRA framework.

---

### 3.1 CASE STUDY 1: BHOPAL GAS TRAGEDY (1984)

**Incident Synopsis (Step 1):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Date** | Night of 2–3 December 1984 |
| **Location** | Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh, India |
| **Plant** | Union Carbide India Limited (UCIL) pesticide plant |
| **Hazardous Material** | **Methyl Isocyanate (MIC)** — formula CH₃NCO |
| **Quantity Released** | ~40 tonnes of MIC over 1–2 hours |
| **Official Fatalities** | ~3,800 immediate deaths |
| **Estimated Long-Term Deaths** | 15,000–25,000+ (activist estimates) |
| **Injuries** | 500,000+ exposed, 100,000+ chronically affected |
| **Economic Loss** | ~$470 million (1984) settlement; estimated total cost >$1 billion |

**Hazard Identification (Step 2):**

- **MIC Physical Properties** (KTU-critical):
  - Boiling Point = **39.1 °C**
  - Vapour Density = **2× air** (heavier than air, hugs ground)
  - Odour Threshold = low (poor warning property)
  - TLV-TWA = **0.02 ppm**
  - IDLH = **3 ppm**
  - Highly **flammable** (LEL = 5.3%, UEL = 26%)
  - Reacts violently with **water** (hydrolyses to MIC + CO₂ + heat)

**Causal Chain Reconstruction (Step 3):**

The event tree (each bullet is a node):

1. **MIC Storage Tank E610** held 41 tonnes of MIC
2. Water ingress (estimated 450–1000 kg) via faulty valve or scrubber pipe
3. **Runaway exothermic reaction** began:
   $$\text{MIC} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{Methylamine} + \text{Heat}$$
4. Temperature rose from ~15 °C to over 200 °C in tank
5. Pressure surged; relief valve opened to vent gas
6. **Vent Gas Scrubber (VGS)** — meant to neutralize MIC — was **out of service** for maintenance
7. **Flare Tower** — meant to burn vented MIC — was **also out of service**
8. Refrigeration system had been **shut down** to save nitrogen blanketing cost
9. MIC vapor escaped into atmosphere, drifted over densely populated slum
10. Toxic cloud covered 8 km² of Bhopal, primarily affecting sleeping residents

**Root Cause Analysis (Step 4) — Multi-Layer Failure:**

| Root Cause Category | Specific Failure |
|---|---|
| **Technical** | Refrigeration off, VGS offline, flare offline, poor tank design (no water-tight isolation) |
| **Human/Operational** | Maintenance staff absent at night, valve not locked out, no real-time gas monitoring |
| **Organizational** | Cost-cutting post-1982 production slowdown, skeleton crew at night |
| **Regulatory** | Inadequate Indian safety regulations pre-1984, no enforced PSM standard |
| **Cumulative Acts of Omission** | "Normalization of Deviance" — multiple safety systems simultaneously degraded |

**Barrier / Swiss Cheese Analysis (Step 5):**

The Swiss Cheese model shows that **every barrier was compromised** (Bhopal is the textbook case of a "Swiss Cheese with all holes aligned"):

| Barrier Layer | Status at Bhopal |
|---|---|
| **Layer 1: Design Inherently Safe** | ❌ Failed — MIC was not replaced by less hazardous alternative |
| **Layer 2: Basic Process Control (BPCS)** | ⚠️ Partially — pressure/temperature alarms not alerted to night staff |
| **Layer 3: Critical Alarms & Operator Action** | ❌ Failed — no night operator qualified on MIC |
| **Layer 4: Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** | ❌ Failed — interlocks/isolation valves not functional |
| **Layer 5: Mechanical Relief Devices** | ❌ Failed — relief valve vented to defective scrubber |
| **Layer 6: Physical Protection (Bund, Dikes)** | ⚠️ — no off-site protection |
| **Layer 7: Emergency Response (On-site)** | ❌ Failed — siren delayed, no community awareness |
| **Layer 8: Emergency Response (Off-site)** | ❌ Failed — hospitals unprepared for MIC exposure |

**Consequence Quantification (Step 6):**

Using the **Probit equation for toxic exposure** (Examination-critical derivation):

$$Y = a + b \ln(V^{n} \cdot t)$$

For MIC, published probit constants (WHO/IPCS):

$$Y = -17.1 + 0.905 \ln(V^{2} \cdot t)$$

where $V$ is concentration in **mg/m³** and $t$ is exposure time in **minutes**.

**Derivation — Sample Calculation:**

Assume downwind concentration $V = 30$ mg/m³ and exposure $t = 30$ min:

$$Y = -17.1 + 0.905 \ln(30^{2} \times 30)$$

$$Y = -17.1 + 0.905 \ln(27000)$$

$$Y = -17.1 + 0.905 \times 10.203$$

$$Y = -17.1 + 9.234$$

$$Y = -7.866$$

Convert probit to percentage using standard probit table: Probit $-7.866$ corresponds to mortality $< 1\%$ at this point, but the **integrated dose** over a wide population and 2-hour exposure pushed mortality into the thousands.

**Lessons Learned (Step 7) → Regulatory Impact:**

| Post-Bhopal Reform | Year | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC)** | 1989 | India's first comprehensive major hazard rules |
| **Environment Protection Act (EP Act)** strengthening | 1986, amended | Off-site emergency plans mandatory |
| **Public Liability Insurance Act** | 1991 | ₹5–₹50 crore insurance for victims |
| **National Disaster Management Act** | 2005 | DM framework, NDMA, SDMA |
| **SMPV Rules under Factories Act** | Strengthened | Pressure vessel code enforcement |
| **OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM)** | 1992 (USA, parallel) | 14 elements of PSM influenced by Bhopal |
| **Responsible Care (Chemical Industry)** | 1985 | Voluntary global initiative post-Bhopal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked about Bhopal, examiners expect you to mention: (a) MIC properties, (b) the three failed safety systems (Refrigeration, VGS, Flare), (c) the Swiss Cheese alignment, and (d) the regulatory aftermath (MSIHC Rules 1989). Missing any one costs 2–3 marks.

---

### 3.2 CASE STUDY 2: FLIXBOROUGH DISASTER (1974)

**Incident Synopsis:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Date** | Saturday, 1 June 1974 |
| **Location** | Flixborough, Lincolnshire, UK |
| **Plant** | Nypro (UK) caprolactam plant |
| **Process** | Cyclohexane oxidation to cyclohexanone → caprolactam (nylon-6 precursor) |
| **Fatalities** | **28 killed** |
| **Injuries** | 36 injured (off-site) |
| **Damage** | Entire plant destroyed, 1,800+ nearby buildings damaged |
| **Crater** | ~30 m diameter, ~6 m deep |

**Hazard Identification:**

- **Hazardous substance:** ~50 tonnes of **hot cyclohexane** (~150 °C, 9.4 bar gauge)
- **Reactor volume:** 20 m³ each, operated in series (6 reactors)
- **Hot, flammable liquid** — released and formed a vapour cloud

**Causal Chain:**

1. Reactor 5 developed a leak in a **cyclohexane feed line** (mild steel, known hairline crack)
2. To continue production, engineers installed a **20-inch temporary bypass pipe (dog-leg / "Gooseneck" pipe)**
3. The temporary pipe was supported by **rubber hoses wrapped in scaffolding clamps** (not engineered support)
4. The bypass pipe was never subjected to **formal stress analysis, pressure testing, or management of change review**
5. The pipe **failed** under thermal and pressure stress
6. ~50 tonnes of hot cyclohexane released, vaporized instantly, formed a **vapour cloud (VCE — Vapour Cloud Explosion)**
7. VCE ignited (likely from furnace ~30 m away); massive explosion equivalent to ~15–45 tonnes TNT
8. Domino effect: damaged control room, offices, and entire plant

**TNT Equivalency Derivation:**

$$W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{\text{TNT}}}$$

Step-by-step:
- $M$ = mass of cyclohexane = **50,000 kg**
- $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion of cyclohexane = **43,900 kJ/kg**
- $E_{\text{TNT}}$ = energy of TNT per kg = **4,680 kJ/kg**
- $\eta$ = explosion yield factor for VCE = **0.05** (5% — conservative for VCE)

$$W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 50000 \times 43900}{4680}$$

$$W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{1.0975 \times 10^{8}}{4680}$$

$$W_{\text{TNT}} \approx 23{,}460 \text{ kg TNT} \approx 23.5 \text{ tonnes TNT}$$

This matches the historical estimate of **15–45 tonnes TNT** — validated by the **TNT method**.

**Scaled Distance (Overpressure Calculation):**

At distance $R = 100$ m from epicentre:

$$Z_e = \dfrac{R}{(W_{\text{TNT}})^{1/3}} = \dfrac{100}{(23460)^{1/3}}$$

$$(23460)^{1/3} = 28.66$$

$$Z_e = \dfrac{100}{28.66} \approx 3.49 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

From TNT overpressure curves, $Z_e = 3.49$ corresponds to peak overpressure ~ **50 kPa (0.5 bar)** — sufficient to destroy typical buildings (a typical brick wall collapses at ~20 kPa).

**Swiss Cheese Analysis:**

| Barrier | Status at Flixborough |
|---|---|
| **Inherent Safety** | ❌ Failed — temporary bypass violates basic piping code |
| **Engineering Design** | ❌ Failed — no formal stress analysis, no support design |
| **Management of Change (MOC)** | ❌ Failed — bypass installed without MOC review (this is THE key lesson) |
| **Pressure Testing** | ❌ Failed — bypass was not pressure-tested |
| **Hazard Study** | ❌ Failed — process hazard analysis not performed for modification |
| **Emergency Response** | ⚠️ Partial — main control room destroyed |

**Lessons Learned → Flixborough Report & ASES:**

- **Flixborough Report (HMFI, 1975)** — UK government inquiry
- **Establishment of **Health and Safety Executive (HSE)**, 1975
- **6 CIMAH Regulations** (Control of Industrial Major Accident Hazards, 1984) → evolved into **COMAH Regulations 2015**
- **Birth of **HAZOP** as a regulatory requirement** (widely used in process industries worldwide)
- **Strengthened ASME B31.3 Process Piping Code**
- **"Management of Change" became a mandated element of PSM**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Flixborough is the canonical case for teaching **Management of Change (MOC)** failure. Examiners reward students who identify the temporary "20-inch dog-leg" pipe and emphasize that NO engineering review was conducted — the modification bypassed every safety layer.

---

### 3.3 CASE STUDY 3: TEXAS CITY DISASTER (1947)

**Incident Synopsis:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Date** | 16 April 1947 |
| **Location** | Texas City, Texas, USA |
| **Ship** | SS Grandcamp (French), also SS High Flyer |
| **Substance** | ~2,300 tons of **ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃)** in paper sacks |
| **Fatalities** | **~581 (some sources: 468 + dock casualties)** |
| **Injuries** | ~5,000+ |
| **Damage** | ~1,000 buildings destroyed, port paralyzed |

**Causal Chain:**

1. SS Grandcamp loading with NH₄NO₃ fertilizer-grade bags (regulated but with sub-optimal packaging)
2. Hold fire detected; crew sealed hatches and applied steam
3. Steam likely decomposed NH₄NO₃ → **exothermic self-sustaining decomposition**:
   $$\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{N}_2\text{O} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (\Delta H = -36 \text{ kJ/mol})$$
4. Explosion occurred, killing longshoremen, firefighters, spectators
5. Domino effect: flying debris punctured adjacent **Monsanto styrene plant** and other chemical facilities
6. **SS High Flyer** (with ~960 tons NH₄NO₃) caught fire 16 hours later; exploded next day
7. Total blast ~ **2.7 kT** equivalent

**TNT Equivalency:**

$$W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 2{,}300{,}000 \times \Delta H_c(\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3)}{4680}$$

With $\Delta H_c(\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3) \approx 3{,}370$ kJ/kg:

$$W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 2{,}300{,}000 \times 3370}{4680}$$

$$W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{3.8755 \times 10^{8}}{4680} \approx 82{,}800 \text{ kg} \approx 83 \text{ tonnes TNT}$$

**Lessons Learned:**

- **NH₄NO₃** added to hazardous materials regulations
- **Wharf / dock** storage standards
- **Buffer zones** between ammonium nitrate and other chemicals
- **Crowd control** at incident scenes
- **Lessons** directly applicable to **2001 Toulouse disaster** (also NH₄NO₃)

---

### 3.4 CASE STUDY 4: SEVESO DISASTER (1976)

**Incident Synopsis:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Date** | 9 July 1976 |
| **Location** | Meda (near Seveso), Lombardy, Italy |
| **Plant** | ICMESA chemical plant (Givaudan group) |
| **Substance** | **2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD / "dioxin")** |
| **Quantity** | ~2–5 kg released |
| **Immediate Fatalities** | 0 (rare for a major accident) |
| **Affected** | 37,000 people exposed, 3,300+ animals died |
| **Long-term** | Increased cancer incidence, chloracne cases |

**Causal Chain:**

1. Chemical reactor producing **2,4,5-trichlorophenol (TCP)** — intermediate in herbicide manufacture
2. Reaction was **run out of control** due to a temperature drop allowing the exotherm to stall and then surge
3. Pressure relief valve opened, releasing the reactor contents
4. Contents passed through a **burst disc vent** that discharged directly into the open atmosphere
5. The discharge plume of TCDD settled on surrounding farmland
6. Animals (rabbits, chickens) died within days
7. Local residents developed **chloracne** (TCDD signature symptom)

**Key HIRA Lessons:**

- **Inherent Safety** principles — runaway reaction must be impossible by design
- **Vent scrubbing** is mandatory
- **Community right-to-know** (passive public notification)
- **Land-use planning** in industrial zones
- **Dioxin** became the new symbol of chemical hazard

**Regulatory Outcome → "Seveso Directive":**

| Version | Year | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| **Seveso I Directive (82/501/EEC)** | 1982 | First EU major hazard directive |
| **Seveso II Directive (96/82/EC)** | 1996 | Added **Safety Management System (SMS)** requirements |
| **Seveso III Directive (2012/18/EU)** | 2012 | Aligned with CLP/GHS classification |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Seveso is the case for "**low-consequence high-toxicity persistent pollutants**" — the test is to discuss the *chronic* (not acute) impact and the birth of the *Seveso Directive*.

---

### 3.5 CASE STUDY 5: PIPER ALPHA DISASTER (1988)

**Incident Synopsis:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Date** | 6 July 1988 |
| **Location** | North Sea, UK continental shelf (120 miles NE of Aberdeen) |
| **Platform** | Piper Alpha (Oceanic/Chevron/BNOC) |
| **Operation** | Oil + gas production |
| **Fatalities** | **167 (all on platform, including 2 rescuers)** |
| **Total destroyed** | One of the world's worst offshore disasters |

**Causal Chain:**

1. A **condensate pump** had its **pressure safety relief valve removed** for maintenance
2. The open end of the pipe was blanked with a **vulnerable blind flange** (not rated for hydrocarbon service)
3. **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** system failed — the night shift was not informed of the open-ended condition
4. Pump was started; condensate leaked, formed a flammable cloud
5. The condensate cloud was ignited by a faulty starting-air line or by the platform's flare
6. First explosion ruptured the **gas riser** (large pipeline from subsea)
7. Second, larger explosion followed once the riser's contents ignited
8. Fire engulfed the platform; **emergency isolation valves (EIV)** failed or were not operated
9. The accommodation block caught fire, leading to mass casualties
10. The **CMA** (Mereen firewater pump) and **fire deluge** system also failed

**Swiss Cheese Analysis — Four Holes Aligned:**

| Barrier | Status |
|---|---|
| **PTW System** | ❌ Failed (night crew uninformed) |
| **Blind Flange** | ❌ Failed (not hydrocarbon-rated) |
| **Gas Detection** | ❌ Failed (no automatic isolation) |
| **EIV** | ❌ Failed (manual, not auto, not operable in fire) |
| **Fire Water System** | ❌ Failed (electric pumps lost in fire) |
| **Deluge / Passive Fire Protection** | ❌ Failed (steel jacket exposed) |
| **Temporary Safe Refuge (TSR)** | ❌ Failed (no fire/blast protection) |
| **Escape & Evacuation** | ❌ Failed (helideck exposed, lifeboats failed in fire) |

**Lessons → Cullen Report (1990):**

- **Safety Case** now mandatory for UK offshore installations
- **COMAH Regulations 2015** (UK onshore analogue)
- **Performance Standards** for all safety-critical elements (a HIRA framework for assets)
- **ALARP** formally codified in UK safety law
- **Independent verification** of safety systems

---

### 3.6 CASE STUDY 6: CHERNOBYL DISASTER (1986)

**Incident Synopsis:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Date** | 26 April 1986 |
| **Location** | Chernobyl, Ukrainian SSR (now Ukraine) |
| **Plant** | RBMK-1000 Reactor No. 4 |
| **Substance** | Radioactive isotopes (¹³¹I, ¹³⁷Cs, ⁹⁰Sr, Pu-239) |
| **Fatalities** | 31 immediate (operators/firefighters); estimated 4,000–60,000 long-term (controversial) |
| **Evacuation** | 350,000+ evacuated from 30 km zone |
| **INES Level** | **7 (Maximum)** |

**Causal Chain:**

1. A **safety test** was scheduled to study residual turbine rundown
2. Operators **violated procedures** to delay the test, leaving the reactor in a low-power, unstable state
3. **Positive void coefficient** + xenon poisoning created a highly unstable core
4. **Control rods** were fully withdrawn — the reactor was in a forbidden state
5. Reactor power surged to ~30 GW (10× nominal) in 4 seconds
6. Fuel pellets shattered, steam exploded, graphite moderator caught fire
7. Roof blown off; **graphite fire** burned for 10 days, releasing fission products
8. USSR attempted cover-up; international monitoring via Sweden detected the release

**Lessons Learned:**

- **Reactor safety culture** overhaul
- **IAEA Convention on Nuclear Safety (1994)**
- **Western reactor design** (PWR, BWR) hardened with passive safety
- **Operator training and procedure adherence** made paramount
- **Transparency** in reporting

### 3.7 Comparative Analytical Matrix — "Big Six" Case Studies

| Parameter | Bhopal | Flixborough | Texas City | Seveso | Piper Alpha | Chernobyl |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Year** | 1984 | 1974 | 1947 | 1976 | 1988 | 1986 |
| **Hazard** | Toxic MIC | Flammable VCE | Explosive NH₄NO₃ | Toxic dioxin | Flammable + fire | Nuclear |
| **Initial Event** | Water ingress into tank | Pipe bypass failure | Hold fire | Runaway reaction | Pump restart | Test violation |
| **Killed** | 3,800+ | 28 | ~581 | 0 immediate | 167 | 31 (immediate) |
| **Primary Lesson** | Multiple safety systems offline | Management of Change | Hazardous material storage | Land-use planning | Permit-to-Work | Safety culture |
| **Regulatory Outcome** | MSIHC Rules 1989 | HSE, HAZOP mandated | NH₄NO₃ regulations | Seveso Directive | Safety Case (Cullen) | IAEA Convention |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The 7-Step Case Study Analysis Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Incident Synopsis<br/>Date, Location, Plant, Substance"] --> B["Step 2: Hazard Identification<br/>Material, Energy, Conditions"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Causal Chain<br/>Event Tree Reconstruction"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Root Cause Analysis<br/>5-Why, Fishbone, HFACS"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Swiss Cheese Analysis<br/>Identify Failed IPLs"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Consequence Quantification<br/>Probit, TNT eqn, F-N Curve"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Lessons and Mitigation<br/>Hierarchy of Controls, Regs"]
    G --> H["Feedback to HIRA Methodology<br/>Update Databases, Standards"]
    style A fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style H fill:#90EE90,color:#000
```

### 4.2 The Swiss Cheese Model Applied to Bhopal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H["HAZARD<br/>MIC under pressure"] --> L1["Layer 1:<br/>Inherent Safety<br/>✗ Use safer chemical"]
    L1 --> L2["Layer 2:<br/>BPCS Alarms<br/>✗ Night operators absent"]
    L2 --> L3["Layer 3:<br/>Operator Action<br/>✗ Untrained crew"]
    L3 --> L4["Layer 4:<br/>SIS Interlocks<br/>✗ Valves not closed"]
    L4 --> L5["Layer 5:<br/>Relief Devices<br/>✗ Vent to dead scrubber"]
    L5 --> L6["Layer 6:<br/>Bund/Dike<br/>✗ No offsite barrier"]
    L6 --> L7["Layer 7:<br/>Onsite Emergency<br/>✗ Siren delayed"]
    L7 --> L8["Layer 8:<br/>Offsite Emergency<br/>✗ Hospitals unprepared"]
    L8 --> DM["DISASTER<br/>3800+ deaths"]

    style DM fill:#8B0000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Bow-Tie Diagram — Generic Major Hazard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT["LEFT SIDE - THREATS"]
        T1["Threat 1:<br/>Pipe Rupture"]

    end
    T1 --> PB1 --> CE1
    T2 --> PB2 --> CE1
    T3 --> PB3 --> CE1
    CE1 --> MB1 --> C1
    CE1 --> MB2 --> C2
    CE1 --> MB3 --> C3

    style MB3 fill:#90EE90,color:#000
```

### 4.4 HIRA Methodology Flowchart with Case Study Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Hazard Source<br/>(Chemical/Energy/Process)"] --> B["Hazard Identification<br/>(HAZOP, FMEA, Checklist)"]
    B --> C["Frequency Estimation<br/>(Historical Data, Frequencies)"]
    C --> D["Consequence Analysis<br/>(Probit, TNT, Dispersion)"]
    D --> E["Risk Estimation<br/>(R = F x C)"]
    E --> F{"Risk vs Criteria?"}
    F -->|Acceptable| G["Operate with Controls"]
    F -->|ALARP Region| H["Apply Cost-Benefit<br/>ALARP Test"]
    F -->|Unacceptable| I["Apply Hierarchy<br/>of Controls"]
    H --> J{"Cost Justified?"}
    J -->|Yes| I
    J -->|No| G
    I --> K["Residual Risk Re-assessed"]
    K --> L["Case Studies Database<br/>(Lessons Archived)"]
    L -.-> B
    style F fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style I fill:#FF6347,color:#fff
    style L fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS<br/>(Most to Least Effective)"] --> B["1. ELIMINATION<br/>Remove the hazard entirely"]
    B --> C["2. SUBSTITUTION<br/>Replace with less hazardous material"]
    C --> D["3. ENGINEERING CONTROLS<br/>BPCS, SIS, Relief Valves, Bunds"]
    D --> E["4. ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS<br/>PTW, SOPs, Training, Signage"]
    E --> F["5. PPE<br/>Respirators, Suits, Helmets"]
    style A fill:#000080,color:#fff
    style B fill:#006400,color:#fff

    style F fill:#FF6347,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

> "List any six essential pieces of information that must be captured in the 'Incident Synopsis' (Step 1) of a case study analysis in HIRA. Use the Bhopal Gas Tragedy to illustrate your answer."

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1.5 marks per set):**

The six essential elements of an Incident Synopsis are:
1. **Date and Time** of the incident (Bhopal: night of 2–3 December 1984)
2. **Location** (Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh, India)
3. **Plant / Facility** (Union Carbide India Limited — UCIL)
4. **Hazardous Material / Process Involved** (Methyl Isocyanate — MIC)
5. **Magnitude of Release** (40+ tonnes of MIC vapor)
6. **Consequences** (~3,800 immediate deaths, 500,000+ exposed)

*(Any 6 points with Bhopal illustration: full 3 marks. If 4 points: 2 marks. If only generic: 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 Marks]**

> "Explain the 'Swiss Cheese Model' of accident causation. Why is Bhopal Gas Tragedy considered the textbook example of a total Swiss Cheese failure?"

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Swiss Cheese Model** (James Reason, 1990) represents an organization as a series of **defensive layers** (cheese slices), each with **holes** (latent failures). A disaster occurs when holes in *all* layers momentarily align, allowing the hazard to reach the victim.

At Bhopal, **every single protective layer** had its hole aligned simultaneously:
- Inherent safety not applied (MIC could have been replaced)
- BPCS alarms not monitored by trained night operators
- **Vent Gas Scrubber offline** for maintenance
- **Flare Tower offline** for maintenance
- Refrigeration system shut down to save costs
- Siren sounded 90+ minutes late
- Hospitals unprepared for MIC exposure

Because holes in *all 8* layers aligned, Bhopal is the **canonical "Swiss Cheese with all holes aligned" disaster**. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING:**
> - In case-study answers, **always quantify** (give numbers: fatalities, mass, pressure, distance) — vague answers lose 2–4 marks.
> - **Always draw the Swiss Cheese or Bow-Tie diagram** (at least 3 marks reserved for diagram).
> - Mention **at least two regulatory outcomes** post-disaster (e.g., MSIHC Rules 1989 for Bhopal, HSE/COMAH for Flixborough, Seveso Directive for Seveso).
> - Do **not** confuse TCE (trichloroethylene) with TCP (trichlorophenol) in Seveso.
> - Do **not** state the Bhopal plant as "Union Carbide USA" — it is **Union Carbide India Limited (UCIL)**.
> - For Flixborough, do **not** call the bypass a "20-inch hose" — it is a **20-inch diameter temporary bypass pipe / dog-leg pipe**.

---

#### **PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** With the help of a **Bow-Tie diagram**, analyse the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)** under the framework of Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment. Discuss the failed barriers, root causes, and the regulatory aftermath in India. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **TNT-equivalent yield** and the **scaled distance** for a 50-tonne cyclohexane release with yield factor 5%, and comment on the destructive potential at 100 m and 250 m from the epicentre. Comment on its relevance to the Flixborough disaster. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Component | Marks | Expected Content |
|---|---|---|
| **Bow-Tie diagram** (drawn or described) | 2 | Threats (water ingress, runaway), Prevention barriers (none functional), Top event (MIC release), Mitigation barriers (all failed), Consequences (3800+ deaths) |
| **Hazard identification of MIC** | 1 | Mention boiling point 39.1 °C, vapour density 2× air, TLV 0.02 ppm, water-reactive |
| **Root cause analysis** | 2 | Technical (VGS off, flare off, refrigeration off); Organizational (cost-cutting, skeleton crew); Regulatory (no PSM in India pre-1984) |
| **Swiss Cheese reference** | 1 | List of 4–6 failed layers |
| **Regulatory aftermath** | 1 | MSIHC Rules 1989, EP Act 1986 amendments, Public Liability Insurance Act 1991 |

---

**Model Answer to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — TNT Equivalent Yield:**

$$W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{\text{TNT}}}$$

Substitute: $\eta = 0.05$, $M = 50{,}000$ kg, $\Delta H_c = 43{,}900$ kJ/kg (cyclohexane), $E_{\text{TNT}} = 4680$ kJ/kg

$$W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 50{,}000 \times 43{,}900}{4680}$$

$$W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{1.0975 \times 10^{8}}{4680}$$

$$W_{\text{TNT}} \approx 23{,}460 \text{ kg} = 23.46 \text{ tonnes TNT}$$

**Step 2 — Scaled Distance at 100 m:**

$$Z_e = \dfrac{R}{(W_{\text{TNT}})^{1/3}} = \dfrac{100}{(23{,}460)^{1/3}}$$

$$(23{,}460)^{1/3} = 28.66$$

$$Z_e = \dfrac{100}{28.66} = 3.49 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

From TNT overpressure chart, $Z_e = 3.49$ corresponds to peak side-on overpressure ~ **50 kPa (≈ 0.5 bar)** — destroys typical brick buildings.

**Step 3 — Scaled Distance at 250 m:**

$$Z_e = \dfrac{250}{28.66} = 8.72 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

Peak overpressure ~ **15 kPa (≈ 0.15 bar)** — windows shatter, light injuries.

**Step 4 — Relevance to Flixborough (1 mark):**

At Flixborough, ~50 tonnes of cyclohexane released and exploded. The estimated yield was 15–45 tonnes TNT, matching our 23.46 t calculation. Buildings within 100 m were destroyed; windows shattered within 250 m, exactly as predicted by the TNT method — validating the TNT method as a useful (though conservative) tool in QRA.

---

#### **PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** Compare and contrast the **Flixborough Disaster (1974)** and the **Piper Alpha Disaster (1988)** in terms of: (i) initiating event, (ii) failed Permit-to-Work / Management of Change systems, and (iii) the regulatory outcomes that followed. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A plant stores 1,000 tonnes of anhydrous ammonia at 10 °C. A catastrophic release forms a pool of 1.5 m radius (assume instantaneous release, dispersion using Pasquill-Gifford class D stability, wind speed 3 m/s). Estimate the downwind concentration at 500 m using the **Gaussian Plume model** and determine if the **AEGL-2 (30 ppm for 1 hour)** threshold is exceeded. Comment on the implication for emergency planning. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Dimension | Flixborough (1974) | Piper Alpha (1988) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Initiating Event** | 20-inch temporary bypass pipe failed under thermal stress, releasing 50 tonnes of hot cyclohexane → Vapour Cloud Explosion | Condensate pump started with relief valve removed; blind flange failed; condensate cloud ignited → 2 explosions, one from gas riser rupture |
| **(ii) Failed PTW / MOC** | Bypass pipe installed **without Management of Change (MOC)** review — no stress analysis, no pressure test, no engineering sign-off | Permit-to-Work issued for relief valve removal but **night shift not informed**; pump restarted with the line still open-ended |
| **(iii) Regulatory Outcome** | **Flixborough Report 1975** → creation of **HSE (UK)**; HAZOP mandated; **ASME B31.3** strengthened; CIMAH Regulations | **Cullen Report 1990** → **Safety Case** mandatory for offshore installations; **COMAH Regulations 2015**; Performance Standards codified |

(Draw either a comparison table or Swiss Cheese diagram: +1 mark; Cite specific regulations: +1 mark)

---

**Model Answer to Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Gaussian Plume Equation:**

$$\chi(x,y,0) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^{2}}\right) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^{2}}{2\sigma_z^{2}}\right)$$

For ground-level centreline ($y = 0$, $H = 0$):

$$\chi(x,0,0) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate Q (ammonia release rate):**

A 1,500 mm radius pool of NH₃ evaporating. Standard NH₃ evaporation rate from EPA ALOHA: ~0.05 kg/m²·s for a calm pool. Pool area = $\pi r^2 = \pi \times 1.5^2 = 7.07$ m²

$$Q = 0.05 \times 7.07 = 0.354 \text{ kg/s}$$

Convert to mg/s:

$$Q = 354 \text{ mg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Dispersion coefficients at 500 m, Class D:**

Pasquill-Gifford Class D (neutral stability, low wind):

$$\sigma_y(500) \approx 30 \text{ m}, \quad \sigma_z(500) \approx 18 \text{ m}$$

(These are tabulated look-up values — students may use ISC or AERMOD equivalents.)

**Step 4 — Concentration at 500 m, centreline, ground-level:**

$$\chi = \dfrac{354}{2 \pi \times 3 \times 30 \times 18}$$

$$\chi = \dfrac{354}{10{,}178.76}$$

$$\chi \approx 0.0348 \text{ mg/m}^{3}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to ppm:**

For NH₃, molecular weight $M = 17$ g/mol, at 25 °C, 1 atm: 1 ppm = $\dfrac{M}{24.45} = \dfrac{17}{24.45} \approx 0.695$ mg/m³

$$\chi_{ppm} = \dfrac{0.0348}{0.695} \approx 0.05 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with AEGL-2 (30 ppm for 1 hour):**

$$0.05 \text{ ppm} \ll 30 \text{ ppm AEGL-2}$$

**Conclusion:** AEGL-2 is **not** exceeded at 500 m for this scenario. However, this is a steady-state estimate — for a large release the instantaneous concentration could spike 10–100× higher. Emergency planning must still consider **isolation zones of 1–2 km** for catastrophic ammonia releases. (1 mark for discussion)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and revise 30 minutes before the KTU exam.**

#### **A. Must-Know Six Case Studies**

- ✅ **Bhopal 1984** — MIC; 3,800+ deaths; 3 safety systems offline; MSIHC Rules 1989
- ✅ **Flixborough 1974** — Cyclohexane VCE; 28 deaths; temporary 20-inch bypass pipe; MOC failure; HSE + HAZOP
- ✅ **Texas City 1947** — NH₄NO₃ ship; ~581 deaths; wharf storage reforms
- ✅ **Seveso 1976** — Dioxin TCDD; 0 immediate deaths; Seveso Directive
- ✅ **Piper Alpha 1988** — Offshore platform; 167 deaths; PTW failure; Cullen Report → Safety Case
- ✅ **Chernobyl 1986** — Nuclear; INES Level 7; 31 immediate deaths; safety culture reform

#### **B. Must-Know Numerical Formulas**

- ✅ $R = F \times C$
- ✅ $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{N_f \times 10^8}{T_h}$
- ✅ $W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{\text{TNT}}}$ with $E_{\text{TNT}} = 4680$ kJ/kg
- ✅ $Z_e = R / (W_{\text{TNT}})^{1/3}$
- ✅ Gaussian Plume: $\chi = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}$
- ✅ Probit: $Y = a + b \ln(V^n \cdot t)$

#### **C. Must-Quote Constants & Properties**

- ✅ MIC: B.P. = 39.1 °C, Vapour density = 2× air, TLV = 0.02 ppm
- ✅ Cyclohexane: $\Delta H_c = 43{,}900$ kJ/kg
- ✅ NH₄NO₃: $\Delta H_c \approx 3{,}370$ kJ/kg
- ✅ TNT energy: 4,680 kJ/kg
- ✅ AEGL-2 NH₃ (1 hr) = 30 ppm

#### **D. Must-Use Frameworks**

- ✅ **7-step Case Study Analysis** (Synopsis → Hazard ID → Causal Chain → RCA → Swiss Cheese → Consequence → Lessons)
- ✅ **Swiss Cheese Model** (James Reason, 1990)
- ✅ **Bow-Tie Diagram** (Threats | Prevention | Event | Mitigation | Consequences)
- ✅ **ALARP Principle** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls** (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE)

#### **E. Must-Avoid Pitfalls (Repeated Examiner Warnings)**

- ❌ Confusing TCE with TCP in Seveso (it's **2,4,5-trichlorophenol → TCDD**)
- ❌ Stating Bhopal plant as "Union Carbide USA" (it's **UCIL, India**)
- ❌ Using "20-inch hose" for Flixborough (it's a **20-inch diameter pipe bypass**)
- ❌ Omitting the **diagram** in 14-mark answers (loses ~2 marks)
- ❌ Vague answers without numbers (always **quantify**)

#### **F. Must-Cite Regulations (Indian + International)**

- ✅ Indian: **MSIHC Rules 1989**, **EP Act 1986**, **Public Liability Insurance Act 1991**, **Factories Act 1948**, **SMPV Rules**
- ✅ UK: **HSE**, **COMAH 2015**, **CIMAH 1984**
- ✅ EU: **Seveso I/II/III Directives**
- ✅ USA: **OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119)**
- ✅ International: **IAEA Convention on Nuclear Safety**, **ILO C170 Chemicals Convention**

#### **G. Quick Last-Minute Memory Aid — "BIG-6" Mnemonic**

> **"Bhopal, Flixborough, Texas, Seveso, Piper, Chernobyl"** — remember the order, year, and one-line lesson:
> 1. **B**hopal — **B**ypass of safety systems
> 2. **F**lixborough — **F**ailure of bypass pipe
> 3. **T**exas City — **T**onnage of NH₄NO₃
> 4. **S**eveso — **S**ubstitution (Seveso Directive)
> 5. **P**iper Alpha — **P**TW failure
> 6. **C**hernobyl — **C**ulture failure

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 — CASE STUDIES NOTES**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) | Outcome-Based Education | Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Levels: L1 (Remember) → L6 (Create)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plant Availability and Process Reliability: Ways of Improving Plant Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Plant Availability and Process Reliability: Ways of Improving Plant Availability # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Plant Availability — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plant Availability (A)** is defined as the *probability that a system, equipment, or plant is operationally functional and available to perform its intended duty at any randomly chosen instant of time during its scheduled operating period.* In quantitative terms, Plant Availability is a dimensionless ratio (often expressed as a **percentage**) that compares the time a plant is actually *ready and running* against the total time it was *required* to be operational. $$A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} = \frac{\text{Time Plant is Operational}}{\text{Total Calendar Time (or Scheduled Time)}}$$ A high-availability plant (e.g., **A = 0.95** or **95 %**) means that, on average, the facility is producing **95 %** of the time and is offline only **5 %**. --- ## 1.2 Process Reliability — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Process Reliability (R)** is the probability that a process (or component) will perform its required function without failure under stated conditions, for a specified period of time, **t**. It is a *time-dependent* function, denoted as **R(t)**, and for the widely used exponential reliability model: $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ where: - **R(t)** = reliability at time t (dimensionless, $0 \le R \le 1$) - **λ** = constant failure rate (failures per unit time, e.g., per hour) - **t** = mission or operating time (hours) --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Car Imagine your family car. **Reliability** asks: *"What is the chance my car will start tomorrow without breaking down?"* It is a future-looking probability. **Availability**, however, asks a different question: *"Right now, in the garage, is the car ready to be driven?"* It depends not just on reliability (it doesn't break down) but also on **how fast you can repair it** when it does break down. > [!TIP] > **The "Reliability vs. Availability" Distinction:** > - A system can have **high reliability** (rarely fails) but **low availability** (takes ages to repair). > - A system can have **low reliability** (fails often) but **high availability** (repairs are instant). > - **Best practice:** pursue *both* — design for reliability AND fast maintainability. In a refinery, petrochemical complex, or offshore platform, **Plant Availability** directly determines the *revenue-generating capacity* and *profitability*. Even a **1 %** drop in availability on a large plant can translate to **millions of dollars in lost production** annually. --- ## 1.4 Importance in the Context of Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment In the broader theme of **PBSFT404**, availability and reliability are critical for: | Aspect | Connection to HIRA | | :--- | :--- | | **Loss Prevention** | Higher reliability = fewer hazardous process upsets, leaks, fires. | | **Safety Integrity** | Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) rely on proof-test intervals tied to availability. | | **Risk Reduction** | High process availability implies low frequency of LOPA-triggering failures. | | **Asset Integrity** | Reliability underpins the mechanical integrity programs required by OSHA 1910.119. | | **Business Continuity** | Risk assessment evaluates loss of containment / loss of production scenarios. | --- ## 1.5 The Key Performance Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three standard definitions of Availability used in industry:** > 1. **Inherent Availability (A_i)** — only considers *corrective* downtime. > 2. **Achieved Availability (A_a)** — considers corrective + *preventive* downtime. > 3. **Operational Availability (A_o)** — considers *all* downtime (administrative, logistics, supply delays). These are the foundational metrics that any KTU exam question will test. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Reliability Block Diagram Concept A plant is composed of components arranged either: - **In Series** — failure of *any one* component stops the entire plant. - **In Parallel** (redundant) — the plant runs as long as *at least one* path is alive. For a series system of *n* independent components: $$R_{\text{system}}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) \cdots R_n(t)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** Series systems have *lower* reliability than any single component. To improve reliability, either upgrade components (raise R_i) or introduce **redundancy** (parallel paths). For two identical components in parallel: $$R_{\text{parallel}}(t) = 1 - [1 - R_1(t)][1 - R_2(t)] = 2R(t) - R^2(t)$$ --- ## 2.2 MTBF, MTTR, and MTTF — The Maintenance Triangle Three acronyms form the bedrock of reliability engineering: | Term | Full Form | Meaning | Typical Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | Average operating time between two consecutive failures (for *repairable* systems) | hours | | **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure | Average lifetime before failure (for *non-repairable* components like bulbs, bearings) | hours | | **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | Average time to restore a failed system to working state | hours | > [!WARNING] > **Common mistake:** Students often confuse MTBF with MTTF. Remember — MTBF applies to **repairable** systems (pumps, motors, vessels) while MTTF applies to **non-repairable** items (ball bearings, fuses, capacitors). Mathematically: $$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} \quad \text{(approximately, for repairable systems)}$$ --- ## 2.3 The Failure Rate (λ) and the "Bathtub Curve" The **constant failure rate** assumption (used in exponential reliability) corresponds to the *useful life* region of the classical **Bathtub Curve**: $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$$ | Phase | Failure Rate Trend | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **I — Infant Mortality** | Decreasing | Manufacturing defects, commissioning issues. | | **II — Useful Life** | Constant (λ) | Random failures; exponential model valid. | | **III — Wear-out** | Increasing | Aging, corrosion, fatigue. | > [!TIP] > For the KTU exam, the **exponential distribution** is the default reliability model unless otherwise specified. It is the *only* distribution with the **memoryless property**: $R(t+s \mid t) = R(s)$. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the *high-yield formula bank* for this topic. Memorize these — they appear in **every** KTU question paper on this module. | # | Formula | Description | Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Inherent Availability | Repairable equipment | | 2 | $A_a = \frac{\text{MTBM}}{\text{MTBM} + \text{MADR}}$ | Achieved Availability | With PM downtime | | 3 | $A_o = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}}$ | Operational Availability | All losses included | | 4 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability (exponential) | Constant failure rate | | 5 | $\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Failure rate | Reciprocal of MTBF | | 6 | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Unreliability (CDF) | Probability of failure by time t | | 7 | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability density of failure | Used for hazard rate | | 8 | $h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \lambda$ | Hazard rate (constant) | For exponential | | 9 | $R_{\text{series}} = \prod R_i$ | Series reliability | All must work | | 10 | $R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ | Parallel reliability | At least one works | | 11 | $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$ | Mean life | General formula | | 12 | $d = 1 - A$ | Unavailability (downtime fraction) | Used in SIL calcs | | 13 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \frac{\lambda \cdot T}{2}$ | Avg. Probability of Failure on Demand | For low-demand SIS | | 14 | $\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | Safety system metric | > [!NOTE] > In all formulas above, **PFD** stands for *Probability of Failure on Demand* and **RRF** for *Risk Reduction Factor*, which are the *lingua franca* of IEC 61511 safety integrity level (SIL) calculations. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields - **Petrochemical / Refinery**: Availability figures of **92 %–97 %** are typical targets; every extra **0.1 %** can be worth **\$100,000s/year** for large units. - **Power Plants**: Forced outage rates directly map to grid reliability indices (LOLE, EUE). - **Aerospace**: Reliability must be **> 0.9999999** ("seven nines") for flight-critical systems. - **Process Safety**: SIL-2 requires **PFD between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-1}$**; SIL-3 needs **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$**. --- ## 2.6 The Hidden Cost of Unreliability — Loss Equation A useful way KTU examiners frame questions is through the **cost of unavailability**: $$\text{Loss} = (1 - A) \cdot T_{\text{total}} \cdot C_{\text{per\_hour}} + N_f \cdot C_{\text{per\_failure}}$$ where $N_f$ is the number of failures and $C_{\text{per\_failure}}$ includes repair labor, spare parts, environmental cleanup, and HSE penalties. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Linking Reliability, Failure Rate, and MTTF We start with the survival (reliability) function $R(t)$ and the cumulative failure distribution $F(t)$: $$F(t) = 1 - R(t)$$ **Step 1 — Define the probability density function of failure.** The density is the time-derivative of the CDF: $$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 2 — Define the hazard (failure) rate.** It is the conditional probability of failure in the next instant, *given* survival up to that instant: $$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 3 — Solve the differential equation assuming constant h(t) = λ.** This is the *exponential* model: $$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda\,dt$$ Integrating both sides from 0 to t (with R(0) = 1): $$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t \quad \Rightarrow \quad R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ **Step 4 — Derive the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF).** For any non-negative random variable, the expected value is: $$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$$ Substituting $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$: $$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$ Evaluating the limits: $$\text{MTTF} = 0 - \left( -\frac{1}{\lambda} \right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$ Hence, for a constant failure rate, the mean life is simply the **reciprocal of the failure rate**, which is the formal justification of formula #5 in the cheat sheet. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — A Reactor Coolant Pump > **Problem:** A centrifugal pump has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures/hour. The maintenance team has an average MTTR of 8 hours. Compute: > 1. The reliability for a 720-hour (30-day) mission. > 2. The MTBF and Inherent Availability. > 3. The expected number of failures in 1 year (8760 hours). ### Step (a) — Compute R(720) Using the exponential model with $\lambda = 0.0002$ per hour and $t = 720$ h: $$\lambda t = 0.0002 \times 720 = 0.144$$ $$R(720) = e^{-0.144}$$ $$R(720) \approx 0.8659 \;\; (\text{or } 86.59\%)$$ > **Interpretation:** The pump has an **86.59 %** chance of operating continuously for 30 days without failure. ### Step (b) — Compute MTBF and Inherent Availability $$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0002} = 5000 \text{ hours}$$ $$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{5000}{5000 + 8} = \frac{5000}{5008}$$ $$A_i \approx 0.9984 \;\; (\text{or } 99.84\%)$$ ### Step (c) — Expected Failures in 1 Year $$N_f = \frac{T_{\text{year}}}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{8760}{5000} = 1.752 \text{ failures/year}$$ Equivalently, using $N_f = \lambda t$: $$N_f = 0.0002 \times 8760 = 1.752 \;\; \text{failures}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of MTTR Reduction on Availability The following table illustrates how shrinking MTTR (e.g., through better maintenance practices) improves availability dramatically, *without* changing the underlying reliability. | MTBF (h) | MTTR (h) | $A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$ | Annual Downtime (h) | Annual Downtime (days) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 5000 | 50 | 0.9901 | 87.6 | 3.65 | | 5000 | 20 | 0.9960 | 35.0 | 1.46 | | 5000 | 8 | 0.9984 | 14.0 | 0.58 | | 5000 | 2 | 0.9996 | 3.5 | 0.15 | > [!TIP] > **Insight for the exam:** The *biggest gains* in availability often come from reducing MTTR (maintainability) rather than increasing MTBF (reliability), because MTBF is typically much larger than MTTR. A small change in MTTR produces a *larger* fractional change in availability. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Series System Reliability > **Problem:** A processing line has three critical instruments in series: Flow Transmitter (R₁ = 0.99), Temperature Sensor (R₂ = 0.98), Pressure Sensor (R₃ = 0.995), for a 1000-hour mission. Find system reliability. ### Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Identify that the system fails if *any* instrument fails** (series configuration). **Step 2 — Multiply individual reliabilities:** $$R_{\text{system}} = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 = 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.995$$ $$R_{\text{system}} = 0.99 \times 0.98 = 0.9702$$ $$R_{\text{system}} = 0.9702 \times 0.995 = 0.96535$$ > **Result:** $R_{\text{system}}(1000\,\text{h}) \approx 0.9654$ or **96.54 %**. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Even though each component has reliability > 0.98, the *system* reliability drops to ~0.965. This multiplicative penalty is why engineers use **redundancy** for safety-critical measurements. --- ## 3.5 Worked Example — Parallel (1-out-of-2) Redundant System > **Problem:** Two identical pumps operate in parallel (1-out-of-2 redundancy). Each pump has reliability R = 0.95 for a 1-year mission. Find the parallel-system reliability. ### Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Compute probability of *one* pump failing:** $$P(\text{one fails}) = 1 - R = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$$ **Step 2 — Compute probability that *both* pumps fail simultaneously** (independent failures): $$P(\text{both fail}) = (1 - R)^2 = (0.05)^2 = 0.0025$$ **Step 3 — Subtract from 1 to get the parallel-system reliability:** $$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975$$ > **Result:** $R_{\text{parallel}} = 0.9975$ or **99.75 %** — versus 95 % for a single pump. Redundancy increased reliability from 0.95 to 0.9975, a **50×** improvement in unreliability reduction. $$\text{Unreliability}_{\text{single}} = 0.05 \quad ; \quad \text{Unreliability}_{\text{parallel}} = 0.0025$$ The unreliability was reduced by a factor of **20×**, demonstrating the power of redundancy in safety instrumented systems. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Availability Drivers ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Plant Performance] --> B[Reliability] A --> C[Availability] B --> D[Failure Rate lambda] C --> E[MTBF / MTTR] D --> F[MTBF = 1 / lambda] E --> G[Inherent Availability] E --> H[Achieved Availability] E --> I[Operational Availability] G --> J[Improved Process Uptime] H --> J I --> J J --> K[Reduced HIRA Risk] J --> L[Higher Profitability] K --> M[Safer Plant] L --> M ``` > **Figure 1:** High-level cause-effect chain linking reliability and availability metrics to safety and business outcomes. --- ## 4.2 The "Six Pillars" of Improving Plant Availability ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Pillar 1
Preventive Maintenance] P2[Pillar 2
Predictive Maintenance] P3[Pillar 3
Reliability Centered Maintenance] P4[Pillar 4
Root Cause Failure Analysis] P5[Pillar 5
Spare Parts Inventory] P6[Pillar 6
Operator Training] A[Plant Availability] --> P1 A --> P2 A --> P3 A --> P4 A --> P5 A --> P6 P1 --> OUT[Higher A and Lower Risk] P2 --> OUT P3 --> OUT P4 --> OUT P5 --> OUT P6 --> OUT ``` > **Figure 2:** Six engineering practices that *directly* raise plant availability. --- ## 4.3 Reliability Block Diagram — Series vs. Parallel Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR IN[Process Input] --> S1[Component A] S1 --> S2[Component B] S2 --> S3[Component C] S3 --> OUT[Process Output] PIN[Process Input] --> MUX{Junction} MUX --> PA[Component A] MUX --> PB[Component B] PA --> POUT[Process Output] PB --> POUT ``` > **Figure 3:** Top — *Series* configuration (single line). Bottom — *Parallel* (1-out-of-2) configuration offering redundancy. --- ## 4.4 Maintenance Strategy Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Reactive Maintenance
Run to Failure] --> PM[Preventive Maintenance
Time or Cycle Based] PM --> PdM[Predictive Maintenance
Condition Based] PdM --> RCM[Reliability Centered Maintenance
RCM Analysis] RCM --> TPM[Total Productive Maintenance
TPM Autonomous Care] ``` > **Figure 4:** Evolution of maintenance philosophy from purely reactive (bottom of the reliability pyramid) to fully integrated TPM at the top. --- ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Maintenance Management Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD SENS[Condition Sensors
Vibration, Temperature] --> CM[Condition Monitoring Module] CM --> DEC[Threshold Check and Decision Logic] DEC --> ACT{Action Required?} ACT -->|No| MON[Continue Monitoring] ACT -->|Yes| WO[Generate Work Order] WO --> SCH[Schedule Maintenance Task] SCH --> EXEC[Execute Repair or Replacement] EXEC --> LOG[Update Failure Database] LOG --> RCFA[Root Cause Failure Analysis] RCFA --> FEED[Feedback to Design] FEED --> SENS ``` > **Figure 5:** Closed-loop architecture of a modern predictive maintenance program, showing how sensor data ultimately feeds back into design improvements. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Apr 2024]** ### **Q1.** Define Plant Availability. Differentiate between Inherent Availability and Operational Availability. **Model Answer:** **Plant Availability** is the fraction of the total time that a plant or equipment is in an operable state to perform its intended function. It is given by: $$A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}}$$ | Aspect | Inherent Availability ($A_i$) | Operational Availability ($A_o$) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Formula** | $A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | $A_o = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}}$ | | **Downtime considered** | Only corrective maintenance | All downtimes — corrective, preventive, administrative, supply delays | | **Use** | Design-stage evaluation | Operating-stage management | --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** ### **Q2.** Explain the concept of "Bathtub Curve" in reliability engineering. **Model Answer:** The **Bathtub Curve** is a graphical representation of the failure rate of a component as a function of time. It has three distinct regions: 1. **Infant Mortality Region (Burn-in):** Failure rate *decreases* with time due to removal of manufacturing defects, commissioning errors, and weak components. 2. **Useful Life Region:** Failure rate is *constant* and low; failures are random in nature. The exponential reliability model $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ applies here. 3. **Wear-out Region:** Failure rate *increases* sharply due to aging, corrosion, fatigue, and degradation. **Significance:** Preventive maintenance is most effective when scheduled *just before* the wear-out region begins, thereby intercepting the rising failure rate. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Define Process Reliability. A pressure transmitter in a Safety Instrumented Function has a constant failure rate of $5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour.
