Calculate the probability that the transmitter will operate without failure for (i) 1 year, and (ii) 5 years. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the various ways of improving Plant Availability in a process industry with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to (a) **Definition:** Process Reliability is the probability that a process or component will perform its required function under stated conditions for a specified period of time *t*, without failure. For the constant failure rate, we use the exponential model: $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ where $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. **(i) For t = 1 year = 8760 hours:** $$\lambda t = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 0.0438$$ $$R(8760) = e^{-0.0438} \approx 0.9571$$ > **Answer:** $R \approx 0.9571$ or **95.71 %**. **(ii) For t = 5 years = 5 × 8760 = 43,800 hours:** $$\lambda t = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 43800 = 0.219$$ $$R(43800) = e^{-0.219} \approx 0.8034$$ > **Answer:** $R \approx 0.8034$ or **80.34 %**. **Observation:** Over 5 years, the reliability drops by roughly **15 percentage points** — a clear demonstration of *time-dependent degradation* of reliability. --- #### Solution to (b) **Ways of Improving Plant Availability:** **1. Preventive Maintenance (PM):** Scheduled interventions (e.g., changing pump bearings every 8000 hours) prevent in-service failures. *Example:* Quarterly overhaul of a gas turbine. **2. Predictive Maintenance (PdM):** Use of condition-monitoring techniques — vibration analysis, thermography, oil analysis, ultrasonic testing — to detect incipient failure and intervene *just in time*. *Example:* Vibration sensors on a motor predict bearing failure 30 days in advance. **3. Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM):** A systematic methodology (SAE JA1011) that determines the optimal maintenance strategy for each asset based on its failure modes and consequences. **4. Root Cause Failure Analysis (RCFA):** After every failure, perform 5-Why or fishbone analysis to eliminate the *root cause* — not just the symptom. **5. Spare Parts Inventory Optimization:** Maintain a critical spares list (CSL) so that MTTR is not bottlenecked by procurement delays. Use 2-bin kanban systems for fast-movers. **6. Operator Training and Competency:** Well-trained operators detect abnormal conditions earlier, reducing the severity of failures and the time to isolate them. **7. Design for Reliability (DfR):** Specify equipment with low λ (e.g., Class A vs. Class B instruments), use de-rating, redundancy (1oo2, 2oo3 voting), and proven materials. **8. Total Productive Maintenance (TPM):** An organization-wide philosophy where operators perform daily autonomous care (cleaning, lubrication, inspection), freeing maintenance crew for technical work. **9. Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS):** Software that schedules PM, tracks failures, manages spares, and generates KPIs (MTBF, MTTR, availability). **10. Process Improvements:** Reduce process variability to lower the stress on equipment (e.g., smoother start-up procedures, surge control for compressors). > **Conclusion:** A *holistic* program combining all ten strategies yields the largest gains. The greatest *single* lever, in many cases, is reducing **MTTR** through better spares, training, and condition-based diagnostics. --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)** **(a)** Two centrifugal pumps operate in **parallel** (1-out-of-2 redundancy). Each pump has a constant failure rate of $2 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hour and the system must operate for a 5000-hour mission. Calculate: (i) The reliability of a single pump. (ii) The reliability of the parallel system. (iii) The unreliability of the parallel system, and comment on the benefit of redundancy. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the relationship between reliability, availability, and maintainability. How does this relationship impact Safety Instrumented System (SIS) design? **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to (a) Given: $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour, $t = 5000$ hours. **(i) Single-pump reliability:** $$\lambda t = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 5000 = 1.0$$ $$R_{\text{single}} = e^{-1.0} \approx 0.3679$$ > **Answer:** $R_{\text{single}} \approx 0.368$ or **36.79 %**. **(ii) Parallel (1oo2) reliability:** $$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - R_{\text{single}})^2$$ $$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.3679)^2 = 1 - (0.6321)^2$$ $$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - 0.3996 = 0.6004$$ > **Answer:** $R_{\text{parallel}} \approx 0.6004$ or **60.04 %**. **(iii) Unreliability and commentary:** $$U_{\text{single}} = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321$$ $$U_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - 0.6004 = 0.3996$$ The **unreliability was reduced by 36.8 %** simply by adding a parallel pump. > **Comment:** Redundancy (1oo2) raised system reliability from 36.79 % → 60.04 %, demonstrating the *substantial benefit* of duplication for safety-critical and mission-critical systems. In SIS design, this corresponds to a *risk reduction factor (RRF)* of: $$\text{RRF} = \frac{U_{\text{single}}}{U_{\text{parallel}}} = \frac{0.6321}{0.3996} \approx 1.58$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **For SIL-2 / SIL-3 safety loops, 1oo2 or 2oo3 architectures achieve the required RRF of 10–1000, as per IEC 61511.** --- #### Solution to (b) **Relationship between R, A, and M:** | Property | Definition | Affects | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Reliability (R)** | Probability of *no failure* in a time interval | Failure rate λ | | **Maintainability (M)** | Probability of *restoring* a failed system in a time interval | Repair rate μ | | **Availability (A)** | Fraction of time the system is *operational* | Both R and M combined | **Mathematical Link:** $$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$ > **Insight:** Availability is a *function of both failure rate (reliability) and repair rate (maintainability)*. You cannot discuss availability without invoking both. **Impact on SIS Design:** - **Reliability** determines the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** of a safety instrumented function. For low-demand systems: $$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$$ where $\lambda_D$ is the dangerous failure rate and $T_{proof}$ is the proof-test interval. - **Maintainability (proof testing)** is *as important as reliability*: a low-λ sensor that is never tested may have a high *average* PFD, since undetected dangerous failures can persist for years. - **Availability vs. Spurious Trip Rate:** While high availability is desirable, SIS must be designed to balance *safe failure fraction (SFF)* and *hardware fault tolerance* so that spurious trips (false alarms) do not erode plant availability. > **Conclusion:** In SIS design, you must *simultaneously* engineer for low dangerous failure rate (high R), short proof-test intervals (high M), and architectural constraints (redundancy) — all of which combine to deliver the target **availability** that the process safety function demands. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks:** > 1. **Unit mismatch** in $\lambda$ — converting "per year" to "per hour" incorrectly. Always convert to a *consistent* time base (e.g., hours). > 2. **Confusing MTBF and MTTF.** For repairable systems use MTBF; for non-repairable items use MTTF. > 3. **Adding reliabilities for series systems** — this is wrong! Series systems *multiply*: $R_s = R_1 R_2 R_3$, they do not add. > 4. **Forgetting to subtract from 1 for parallel systems.** The standard parallel formula is $1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)\cdots$, not $R_1 + R_2$. > 5. **In availability problems**, students often quote $A$ as a fraction but fail to convert to *percentage* when the question asks for "percent availability." > 6. **Skipping units in the final answer.** Always write "h" for hours, "h⁻¹" for failure rate, and "%" for availability. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plant Availability & Process Reliability** - ☐ **Plant Availability** = Uptime / (Uptime + Downtime). Always express as a fraction or percentage. - ☐ **Three Availability types:** Inherent ($A_i$, only CM), Achieved ($A_a$, with PM), Operational ($A_o$, all losses). - ☐ **Reliability** is a *probability* of survival over time $t$, not a single point metric. - ☐ **Exponential model:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — valid in the *useful life* region of the bathtub curve, where $\lambda$ is constant. - ☐ **MTBF = 1/λ** for constant failure rate (units: hours). - ☐ **MTTR** is repair time; **MTTF** is failure time for non-repairable items. - ☐ **Series system:** $R_s = \prod R_i$ — system is only as reliable as its *weakest link*. - ☐ **Parallel system (1oo2):** $R_p = 1 - (1-R)^2$ — redundancy *dramatically* improves reliability. - ☐ **Greatest gain in availability** often comes from reducing **MTTR**, not increasing MTBF, because MTTR is a small number in the denominator. - ☐ **SIS design** balances dangerous failure rate ($\lambda_D$), proof-test interval ($T_{proof}$), and redundancy (1oo2, 2oo3) to achieve target **PFD** and **SIL**. - ☐ **Ways to improve availability** — PM, PdM, RCM, RCFA, spares management, training, DfR, TPM, CMMS, and process improvements. - ☐ **Cost of unavailability** scales with production rate × hours of lost operation; even a 1 % drop can cost millions. - ☐ For KTU, remember the **conversion factor**: 1 year = **8760 hours**, 1 day = **24 hours**. > **Final Tip:** When solving numerical KTU problems, always write the **formula first**, then **substitute the values with units**, then **compute step-by-step**, and finally **box the answer with units**. This is exactly the pattern followed in the KTU model answer key, and earns full marks.

topics.title: MTBF and MTTF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** MTBF and MTTF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):**
MTTF is the **arithmetic mean of the expected lifetime of a non-repairable component or system** before it fails. It represents the average operational time of an item that, once failed, is replaced rather than repaired (e.g., fuses, light bulbs, ball bearings, electronic resistors, capacitors).

Mathematically:
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time of Units}}{\text{Number of Units Observed to Fail}}$$

**MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):**
MTBF is the **arithmetic mean of the time intervals between consecutive failures of a repairable system**. It applies to systems that can be restored to operational service after a failure through maintenance or repair (e.g., turbines, vehicles, manufacturing machines, aircraft engines).

Mathematically:
$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operational Time}}{\text{Number of Failures Observed}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Distinction:** MTTF is used for **NON-REPAIRABLE** items. MTBF is used for **REPAIRABLE** systems. Confusing these two is a common KTU valuation pitfall that costs students 1–2 marks every exam cycle.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Disposable Pen (MTTF):**
> Imagine a box of 100 disposable pens. You hand them to 100 students and ask them to write until the ink runs dry. The *average writing time per pen* is the MTTF. Once a pen fails, you **throw it away** and pick a new one — you never repair it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The College Bus (MTBF):**
> A KTU college bus operates daily. Each time it breaks down, the mechanic **repairs it**, and it returns to service. The *average time between two consecutive breakdowns* is the MTBF. Here, downtime (repair time) is called **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — The Human Body (Combining Both):**
> A red blood cell is **non-repairable** → use **MTTF** (~120 days). A human heart is **repairable** (with surgery, pacemakers) → use **MTBF**. Engineers choose the metric based on whether failure means *replacement* or *restoration*.

---

### 1.3 Standard Reliability Metrics (Highlighted Constants)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Failure Rate | **λ** | failures/hour | Frequency of failure events |
| MTTF | τ | hours | Avg. life of non-repairable item |
| MTBF | T̄ | hours | Avg. uptime between failures |
| MTTR | μ | hours | Avg. downtime for repair |
| Availability | A | dimensionless (0–1) | Fraction of time system is operational |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The relationship **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR** holds for *all* repairable systems and is one of the **most frequently tested 2-mark definitions** in PBSFT404 university exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why MTTF and MTBF Exist

In safety engineering and risk assessment, a *hazard* can only be quantified if we know **how often** a component fails. MTTF and MTBF convert qualitative failure data into **quantitative risk metrics** that feed into:
- **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**
- **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**
- **SIL (Safety Integrity Level) determination** per IEC 61508
- **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** calculations

The logic flow of reliability theory follows these steps:

- **Step 1 — Define the population:** Choose N identical components or one repairable system.
- **Step 2 — Record failure times:** Note the exact time of every failure event.
- **Step 3 — Compute the average:** Apply the arithmetic mean across all observed lifetimes or intervals.
- **Step 4 — Cross-check with failure rate (λ):** For constant failure rate regions (the "useful life" of the bathtub curve), λ = 1/MTBF.
- **Step 5 — Use in risk equation:** R(t) = e^(–λt) gives the survival probability at time t.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the exponential distribution matters:** For electronic components and many mechanical systems operating in their "useful life" phase, failures occur randomly in time. The exponential reliability function R(t) = e^(–λt) is the **only continuous distribution with a constant hazard rate**, making it the cornerstone of KTU-level reliability mathematics.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N}$ | Mean life of N non-repairable items | hours | Non-repairable only |
| 2 | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{\text{operational}}}{n_f}$ | Mean uptime between failures | hours | Repairable systems |
| 3 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Total cycle time | hours | Always true for repairable |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Constant failure rate | failures/hour | Exponential distribution only |
| 5 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t / \text{MTBF}}$ | Reliability (survival) function | dimensionless | $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$ |
| 6 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Cumulative failure (CDF) | dimensionless | $0 \leq F(t) \leq 1$ |
| 7 | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability density of failure | per hour | Exponential PDF |
| 8 | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBR}}$ | Steady-state availability | dimensionless | $0 \leq A \leq 1$ |
| 9 | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum t_{\text{repair},i}}{n_f}$ | Mean repair time | hours | Repairable only |
| 10 | $\text{Unavailability} = 1 - A = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Downtime fraction | dimensionless | $0 \leq U \leq 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU LaTeX Note:** Absolute values in formulas (e.g., $R \leq \vert 1 \vert$) are written using $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking markdown table pipes.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas Refineries (KTU-relevant industry):** MTTF of relief valves and pressure transmitters is used to compute **PFDavg** for **SIL-1 to SIL-3** loops in Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS).
- **Aerospace:** MTBF of hydraulic pumps on aircraft (often >50,000 hours) drives scheduled maintenance intervals.
- **Manufacturing:** MTBF of CNC spindles (~20,000 hours) is used in **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** calculations.
- **Power Plants:** MTTR of turbine trip systems (target: <8 hours) directly affects plant availability factor.
- **Electronics:** MTTF of electrolytic capacitors (typically 7,000–10,000 hours at 105 °C) governs **MTBF prediction per MIL-HDBK-217** or **Telcordia SR-332**.

---

### 2.4 The "Bathtub Curve" Context (Why MTTF Varies with Time)

Reliability engineers universally use the **bathtub curve** to visualize how failure rate changes over an asset's life:

| Phase | Name | Failure Behavior | Hazard Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| I — Infant Mortality | Burn-in | Decreasing λ | Manufacturing defects |
| II — Useful Life | Random Failures | **Constant λ** | Random overloads |
| III — Wear-out | End of Life | Increasing λ | Fatigue, corrosion |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The exponential model R(t) = e^(–λt) and the formula MTBF = 1/λ are **strictly valid only in Phase II**. Examiners often award bonus marks to students who mention this limitation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — MTTF for the Exponential Distribution

We start with the Probability Density Function (PDF) of the exponential distribution:
$$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

The **expected value** (mean) of any continuous distribution is defined as:
$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

Substituting the exponential PDF:
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

We solve this using **integration by parts**. Let:
- $u = t \;\Rightarrow\; du = dt$
- $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt \;\Rightarrow\; v = -e^{-\lambda t}$

Applying the integration-by-parts formula $\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du$:
$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ t \cdot (-e^{-\lambda t}) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\lambda t}) \, dt$$

Evaluate the boundary term:
$$\left[ -t e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \lim_{t \to \infty} (-t e^{-\lambda t}) - 0 = 0$$

(The term $e^{-\lambda t}$ decays to zero faster than $t$ grows, so the limit is 0.)

The remaining integral becomes:
$$\text{MTTF} = 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

Evaluate:
$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Therefore, the **fundamental result** is:
$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When the failure rate λ is constant (random-failure regime), the average lifetime is exactly the reciprocal of the failure rate. If λ = 0.0001 failures/hour, then MTTF = 10,000 hours.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reliability Function from Hazard Rate

The **hazard function** h(t) is defined as the conditional probability of failure in the next instant, given survival up to time t:
$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

The reliability function is:
$$R(t) = 1 - F(t) = 1 - \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau) \, d\tau$$

Differentiating the cumulative failure function:
$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Substitute into the hazard function:
$$h(t) = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

This can be rewritten as:
$$h(t) \, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

Integrate both sides from 0 to t:
$$\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau) \, d\tau = -\int_{R(0)}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)$$

Since R(0) = 1 (the item is working at time zero), ln R(0) = 0:
$$\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau) \, d\tau = -\ln R(t)$$

Solving for R(t):
$$R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau) \, d\tau}$$

For the **exponential distribution**, h(t) = λ = constant, so the integral becomes λt:
$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t / \text{MTTF}}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — MTBF Decomposition for Repairable Systems

For a repairable system, one complete operational cycle consists of:
- **Up-time** (working): duration $T_{\text{up}}$
- **Down-time** (failed, being repaired): duration $T_{\text{down}}$

The **MTBR (Mean Time Between Repairs)** is the full cycle time:
$$\text{MTBR} = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}$$

The **MTBF** is the up-time component only, so:
$$\text{MTBF} = E[T_{\text{up}}]$$

The **MTTR** is the down-time component:
$$\text{MTTR} = E[T_{\text{down}}]$$

By linearity of expectation:
$$\text{MTBR} = E[T_{\text{up}}] + E[T_{\text{down}}] = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\text{MTBF} = \text{MTBR} - \text{MTTR} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

The **steady-state availability** A is the long-run fraction of time the system is operational:
$$A = \frac{E[T_{\text{up}}]}{E[T_{\text{up}}] + E[T_{\text{down}}]}$$

Substituting the expected values:
$$\boxed{A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter in a refinery SIS loop is tested and found to have a constant failure rate of λ = 5 × 10⁻⁶ failures/hour. The repair team takes on average 4 hours to restore the system after a trip.

**Find:**
- (i) MTTF
- (ii) MTBF
- (iii) Reliability after 1 year (t = 8,760 hours)
- (iv) Steady-state availability

**Solution:**

**(i) MTTF:**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 200{,}000 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) MTBF:**
$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 200{,}000 + 4 = 200{,}004 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Reliability after 1 year:**
$$R(8760) = e^{-8760 / 200{,}000} = e^{-0.0438}$$
$$R(8760) = 0.9572 \;\;\text{(or 95.72%)}$$

**(iv) Availability:**
$$A = \frac{200{,}004}{200{,}004 + 4} = \frac{200{,}004}{200{,}008} = 0.99998$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** A is dimensionless and **always between 0 and 1**. Many students mistakenly write "A = 99.998%" — KTU examiners accept both, but writing 0.99998 is the more rigorous, board-preferred form.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Reliability Lifecycle Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: System Commissioned] --> B[Operational Phase Begins]
    B --> C{Failure Event?}
    C -- No --> D[Continue Operation, Increment Uptime Clock]
    D --> C
    C -- Yes --> E[System Fails: Log Failure Time]
    E --> F[Repair Crew Dispatched]
    F --> G[Active Repair: Increment Downtime Clock]
    G --> H[System Restored to Service]
    H --> I[Record Total Uptime MTBF and Downtime MTTR]
    I --> B
    B --> J[Compute Aggregate Metrics]
    J --> K[MTTF equals 1 over lambda for non repairable items]
    J --> L[MTBF equals MTTF plus MTTR for repairable systems]
    J --> M[Availability A equals MTBF over MTBF plus MTTR]
    J --> N[Reliability Function R of t equals exp of minus t over MTBF]
    K --> O[Output Reliability Block Diagram]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Logic: MTTF vs MTBF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Component or System?] --> Q{Is it Repairable after Failure?}
    Q -- No, Replace the Item --> R[Use MTTF: Mean Time To Failure]
    R --> S[Examples: Light Bulb, Capacitor, Fuse, Bearing]
    Q -- Yes, Restore via Maintenance --> T[Use MTBF: Mean Time Between Failures]
    T --> U[Examples: Turbine, Pump, Vehicle, Aircraft Engine]
    R --> V[Compute Lambda equals 1 over MTTF]
    T --> W[Compute MTBF equals MTTF plus MTTR]
    V --> X[Compute Reliability R of t]
    W --> X
    X --> Y[Compute Availability A for Repairable Systems]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Bathtub Curve Phases & MTBF Validity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Phase I: Infant Mortality] --> B[Phase II: Useful Life]
    B --> C[Phase III: Wear Out]
    A --> A1[Lambda Decreasing: Defects Dominate]
    B --> B1[Lambda Constant: Random Failures]
    C --> C1[Lambda Increasing: Aging and Fatigue]
    B1 --> D[MTBF and MTTF Valid Here]
    A1 --> E[MTBF NOT Valid in Phase I]
    C1 --> F[MTBF NOT Valid in Phase III]
    D --> G[Apply Exponential Model R of t equals exp of minus lambda times t]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define MTBF and MTTF. State the fundamental relationship between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** It is the average expected operating time of a **non-repairable** component before it fails (1 mark).
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** It is the average time interval between two consecutive failures of a **repairable** system (1 mark).
- **Relationship:** For a repairable system, MTBF = MTTF + MTTR, where MTTR is the mean time to repair (1 mark).

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**A system has MTBF = 10,000 hours and MTTR = 8 hours. Calculate the steady-state availability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 8} = \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}008}$$
$$A = 0.9992 \;\;\text{(or 99.92%)}$$
- Formula (1 mark), substitution (1 mark), final numerical answer (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice (OR). Solve **either** Option A **or** Option B completely. Each carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define MTTF, MTBF, and MTTR. Derive the relationship MTBF = MTTF + MTTR for a repairable system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-repairable electronic sensor used in a fire-detection system has a constant failure rate of λ = 2 × 10⁻⁴ failures per hour. Calculate: (i) MTTF, (ii) Reliability after 1000 hours, (iii) Probability of failure within the first 500 hours, (iv) The time at which reliability drops to 90%. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions and Derivation (7 marks):**

- **MTTF** (Mean Time To Failure): Average lifetime of a non-repairable component before failure (1 mark).
- **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures): Average time between two consecutive failures of a repairable system (1 mark).
- **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair): Average time taken to restore a failed system to operational condition (1 mark).

Derivation: A repairable system experiences an alternating cycle of **operational time** and **repair time**. Let $T_{\text{up},i}$ be the i-th operational period and $T_{\text{down},i}$ be the i-th repair period. Over N cycles:
$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{\text{up},i}}{N}, \quad \text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{\text{down},i}}{N}$$

The total cycle time (MTBR) per cycle is:
$$\text{MTBR} = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}$$

Taking expectations (3 marks):
$$\text{MTBR} = E[T_{\text{up}}] + E[T_{\text{down}}] = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}$$

Equivalently: $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTBR} - \text{MTTR} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ (1 mark for the final boxed relationship).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: λ = 2 × 10⁻⁴ failures/hour, t₁ = 1000 h, t₂ = 500 h, R(t₃) = 0.90.

**(i) MTTF (2 marks):**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 5000 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Reliability after 1000 hours (2 marks):**
$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187 \;\;\text{(81.87%)}$$

**(iii) Probability of failure within 500 hours (1.5 marks):**
$$F(500) = 1 - e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4})(500)} = 1 - e^{-0.1} = 1 - 0.9048 = 0.0952 \;\;\text{(9.52%)}$$

**(iv) Time for R = 0.90 (1.5 marks):**
$$0.90 = e^{-\lambda t_3} \;\Rightarrow\; \ln(0.90) = -\lambda t_3$$
$$t_3 = \frac{-\ln(0.90)}{\lambda} = \frac{0.10536}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 526.8 \text{ hours}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of availability. Derive the expression for steady-state availability of a repairable system in terms of MTBF and MTTR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A control valve in a chemical reactor has MTBF = 50,000 hours and MTTR = 6 hours. Determine: (i) Failure rate λ, (ii) Probability that the valve will operate without failure for 10,000 hours, (iii) Unavailability U, (iv) Expected number of failures in 1 year (8760 hours). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Availability Concept and Derivation (7 marks):**

- **Definition:** Availability is the probability that a system is operational and able to perform its intended function **at a given instant of time** (instantaneous availability) or **as a long-run fraction of time** (steady-state availability) (1 mark).

- For a repairable system, the timeline alternates between **uptime** and **downtime** states. Over a long observation period T_total:
$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}$$

- Steady-state availability is defined as:
$$A = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$$

- During a large number N of failure–repair cycles, the average uptime per cycle is MTBF and the average downtime per cycle is MTTR (2 marks):
$$A = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N \cdot \text{MTBF}}{N \cdot \text{MTBF} + N \cdot \text{MTTR}}$$

- Cancel N:
$$\boxed{A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}}$$

(3 marks for derivation steps and final boxed expression.)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: MTBF = 50,000 h, MTTR = 6 h, t = 10,000 h, T_year = 8,760 h.

**(i) Failure rate λ (2 marks):**
$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{50{,}000} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ failures/hour}$$

**(ii) Reliability at 10,000 hours (2 marks):**
$$R(10{,}000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-5})(10{,}000)} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187 \;\;\text{(81.87%)}$$

**(iii) Unavailability U (1.5 marks):**
$$A = \frac{50{,}000}{50{,}006} = 0.99988$$
$$U = 1 - A = 1 - 0.99988 = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(iv) Expected failures per year (1.5 marks):**
$$N_f = \lambda \times T_{\text{year}} = (2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 8760 = 0.1752 \text{ failures/year}$$

This is roughly 1 failure every 5.7 years.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing MTTF with MTBF** in the formula (–1 mark): MTTF is for *non-repairable* items, MTBF for *repairable* systems. Writing "MTBF = 1/λ" for a non-repairable item is technically accepted but loses the conceptual mark.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (–0.5 mark): Always write "hours" or "failures/hour".
> 3. **Writing 1 – R(t) when asked for R(t)** (–1 mark): Read the question carefully — "operate without failure" means R(t), not F(t).
> 4. **Mixing up availability and reliability** (–1 mark): R(t) depends on mission time t; A is a long-run steady-state value independent of t.
> 5. **Not boxing the final relation** MTBF = MTTF + MTTR: KTU examiners reward a clean boxed final expression (–0.5 mark if missing).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — MTBF & MTTF (KTU PBSFT404 Module 2)**

- ✅ MTTF = **mean lifetime** of a **non-repairable** component = **1/λ** for exponential model.
- ✅ MTBF = **mean uptime between failures** of a **repairable** system.
- ✅ MTBF = **MTTF + MTTR** (always true for repairable systems).
- ✅ Reliability function: **R(t) = e^(–λt) = e^(–t/MTBF)**; valid only in **Phase II (useful life)** of the bathtub curve.
- ✅ Cumulative failure probability: **F(t) = 1 – e^(–λt)**.
- ✅ Steady-state availability: **A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)**; ranges in **[0, 1]**.
- ✅ Unavailability: **U = 1 – A = MTTR / (MTBF + MTTR)** — used in **PFDavg** for SIL calculations.
- ✅ Expected number of failures in time T: **N_f = λ × T = T / MTBF**.
- ✅ Time for reliability to drop to R: **t = –(ln R) / λ**.
- ✅ Distinguish: **R(t)** is mission-time-dependent; **A** is a steady-state long-run metric.
- ✅ Exponential distribution is the **only** continuous distribution with a **constant hazard rate** h(t) = λ.
- ✅ **MTTR reduction** improves availability — drives preventive maintenance strategy in PBSFT404 risk mitigation.
- ✅ KTU exam weightage: 1–2 marks (definition) in Part A, 7–14 marks (numerical) in Part B — high-yield topic.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions** (constant λ, exponential model, independent failures) before solving any problem.
- ✅ Always **box the final formula and final numerical answer** for full KTU marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Reliability Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Reliability Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability Function (R(t))** is formally defined as the conditional probability that a system, component, or item will perform its intended function **without failure** for a specified period of time `t`, under **stated operating conditions**, given that it was operational at time `t = 0`.

Mathematically, the Reliability Function is expressed as:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

where:
- `T` = Random variable representing the **Time-To-Failure (TTF)** of the system or component.
- `t` = A specified mission time, operating duration, or service life.

The complementary function, known as the **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of failure**, is given by:

$$F(t) = P(T \leq t) = 1 - R(t)$$

where `F(t)` is also called the **Unreliability Function**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The LED Bulb Scenario:**
> Imagine purchasing a packet of 1000 brand-new LED bulbs. The manufacturer claims each bulb has a reliability `R(1000) = 0.95` at `1000 hours`. This means: **out of 1000 bulbs, 950 are statistically expected to still be glowing after 1000 hours of continuous use**. The remaining 50 may have burned out (failed). The Reliability Function simply gives you the *survival probability* of the bulb over a time horizon.

## 1.3 Core Properties of the Reliability Function

The following properties are foundational for KTU examinations:

| Property | Mathematical Form | Plain English Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Initial Value** | `R(0) = 1` | A brand-new, non-defective item is **100% reliable** at time zero. |
| **Terminal Value** | `R(∞) = 0` | Given infinite time, **every component eventually fails**. |
| **Monotonicity** | `R(t₁) ≥ R(t₂)` for `t₁ < t₂` | Reliability can only **decrease or stay the same**, never increase with time. |
| **Complementarity** | `R(t) + F(t) = 1` | An item is either working or failed — **no middle state**. |
| **Non-Negativity** | `0 ≤ R(t) ≤ 1` | Reliability is always a **valid probability**. |

## 1.4 Physical & Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment**, `R(t)` is the primary probabilistic metric used to:
> 1. Quantify **inherent safety** of process equipment (pressure vessels, valves, reactors).
> 2. Schedule **predictive maintenance** and replacement cycles.
> 3. Compute **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** for availability analysis.
> 4. Feed into **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** and **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** calculations under IEC 61511.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Reliability-Failure Density Relationship

The **Probability Density Function (PDF) of failure**, denoted `f(t)`, represents the unconditional instantaneous rate of failure per unit time:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

This means the area under the PDF curve between times `t₁` and `t₂` gives the probability of failure in that interval:

$$P(t_1 < T \leq t_2) = F(t_2) - F(t_1) = R(t_1) - R(t_2)$$

## 2.2 The Hazard Function (Failure Rate Function)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Hazard Function** `h(t)` (also called the **instantaneous failure rate** or **force of mortality**) is the conditional probability density of failure at time `t`, **given survival up to time `t`**.

The mathematical relationship is:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Rearranging by separating variables:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -h(t)\,dt$$

Integrating both sides from `0` to `t`:

$$\ln[R(t)] - \ln[R(0)] = -\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du$$

Since `R(0) = 1`, we arrive at the **Master Equation of Reliability Theory**:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du\right)}$$

This is arguably the **most important equation** in reliability engineering and is a high-yield KTU exam item.

## 2.3 The Cumulative Hazard Function

The **Cumulative Hazard Function** `H(t)` is simply the integral of the hazard function:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du$$

Which allows us to compactly write:

$$R(t) = \exp(-H(t))$$

## 2.4 Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)

The expected operational life of a non-repairable system is:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$$

The second equality is derived using integration by parts and the boundary condition `t \cdot F(t) \to 0` as `t \to \infty`.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **every formula** you must memorize for `R(t)`-related KTU problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ | Dimensionless probability |
| 2 | Unreliability | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Cumulative failure probability |
| 3 | Failure Density | $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$ | Probability per unit time, `1/hr` |
| 4 | Hazard Function | $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ | Conditional failure rate, `1/hr` |
| 5 | Cumulative Hazard | $H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | **Master Equation** | $R(t) = \exp(-H(t))$ | **Most critical formula** |
| 7 | MTTF (non-repairable) | $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$ | Hours, cycles, km, etc. |
| 8 | Exponential R(t) | $R(t) = \exp(-\lambda t)$ | $\lambda$ = constant failure rate |
| 9 | Weibull R(t) | $R(t) = \exp(-(t/\eta)^{\beta})$ | $\eta$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape |
| 10 | Weibull h(t) | $h(t) = (\beta/\eta)(t/\eta)^{\beta-1}$ | Shape dictates curve type |
| 11 | Weibull MTTF | $\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ | Uses Gamma function |
| 12 | Exponential MTTF | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | Special case of Weibull $\beta=1$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Process Safety**: In chemical plants, `R(t)` of safety instrumented systems (SIS) determines the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**.
> - **Aerospace**: Predicting fatigue failure of turbine blades using Weibull `R(t)`.
> - **Civil Engineering**: Estimating the lifespan of bridges and concrete structures.
> - **Oil & Gas**: Scheduling **Risk-Based Inspection (RBI)** for pressure vessels and piping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Failure Density to Reliability Function

**Goal:** Show that $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of unreliability.**
By definition, the cumulative probability of failure up to time `t` is:

$$F(t) = P(T \leq t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(u)\,du$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Leibniz Rule for differentiation under the integral sign.**
Differentiating both sides with respect to `t`:

$$\frac{dF(t)}{dt} = f(t)$$

**Step 3 — Use the complementarity identity $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$.**
Substituting:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\bigl[1 - R(t)\bigr] = f(t)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**
The derivative of the constant `1` is zero, leaving:

$$-\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = f(t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}}$$

This is the **fundamental link** between reliability and failure density.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Master Equation $R(t) = \exp(-H(t))$

**Goal:** Derive the central relationship between `R(t)` and `h(t)`.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of hazard function.**
By definition:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expression for `f(t)` from Derivation 1.**

$$h(t) = \frac{-\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Separate the variables.**
Multiply both sides by `dt` and divide by the negative sign:

$$-h(t)\,dt = \frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from time `0` to time `t`.**
On the right side, `R(0) = 1` (initial reliability of a brand-new system):

$$\int_{0}^{t} -h(u)\,du = \int_{R(0)=1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the right-hand integral.**

$$-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du = \ln[R(t)] - \ln(1) = \ln[R(t)]$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate both sides.**

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du\right) = \exp(-H(t))$$

This is the **Master Equation** — the most powerful single formula in reliability analysis.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: MTTF for an Exponential Distribution

**Given:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, where $\lambda$ is a constant failure rate.

**Step 1 — Use the integral definition of MTTF.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral using $\int e^{at}dt = e^{at}/a$.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[\frac{e^{-\lambda t}}{-\lambda}\right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the limits.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{e^{-\lambda \cdot \infty}}{-\lambda} - \frac{e^{0}}{-\lambda} = 0 + \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: MTTF for a Weibull Distribution

**Given:** $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right)$, with scale $\eta$ and shape $\beta$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the MTTF integral.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right)dt$$

**Step 2 — Use the substitution** $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$, so $du = \frac{\beta}{\eta^{\beta}} t^{\beta-1}\,dt$, hence $t = \eta u^{1/\beta}$ and $dt = \frac{\eta}{\beta} u^{(1/\beta)-1} du$.

**Step 3 — The integral transforms into the standard Gamma function form:**

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u} \cdot \frac{\eta}{\beta} u^{(1/\beta)-1}\,du = \frac{\eta}{\beta} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{(1/\beta)-1} e^{-u}\,du$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Gamma function** $\Gamma(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{s-1} e^{-u}\,du$ with $s = 1 + 1/\beta$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{\eta}{\beta}\,\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_{\text{Weibull}} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)}$$

For the special case $\beta = 1$ (exponential): $\Gamma(2) = 1! = 1$, reducing correctly to $\text{MTTF} = \eta = 1/\lambda$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Distribution Selection Guide

| Shape Parameter $\beta$ | Hazard Behavior | Real-World Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $\beta < 1$ | Decreasing hazard (infant mortality) | Electronic burn-in, software defects |
| $\beta = 1$ | Constant hazard (random failures) | Exponential regime, complex components |
| $\beta > 1$ | Increasing hazard (wear-out) | Mechanical fatigue, corrosion, bearings |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Relationship Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **interconnected mathematical architecture** of all reliability functions discussed above.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Random Variable T - Time To Failure"] --> B["PDF f of t = Failure Density"]
    A --> C["CDF F of t = Unreliability = 1 minus R of t"]
    A --> D["Reliability R of t = P T greater than t"]
    A --> E["Hazard h of t = f of t divided by R of t"]
    A --> F["Cumulative Hazard H of t = integral of h u du"]

    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G["Master Equation R of t = exp minus H of t"]
    D --> H["MTTF = integral 0 to infinity R of t dt"]
    H --> I["Exponential MTTF = 1 by lambda"]
    H --> J["Weibull MTTF = eta times Gamma 1 plus 1 by beta"]
```

## 4.2 Reliability Curve Classification by Shape Parameter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S{"Shape Parameter beta"}
    S -- beta less than 1 --> I["Infant Mortality - Decreasing hazard - R drops fast initially then flattens"]
    S -- beta equals 1 --> R["Random Failures - Constant hazard - Pure exponential decay"]
    S -- beta greater than 1 --> W["Wear Out Phase - Increasing hazard - R stays high then drops sharply"]
```

## 4.3 Bathtub Curve Conceptual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Phase 1 Infant Mortality - beta less than 1 - Burn-in screening eliminates weak units"]
    P2["Phase 2 Useful Life - beta equals 1 - Constant lambda - Random failures dominate"]
    P3["Phase 3 Wear Out - beta greater than 1 - Preventive replacement scheduled"]
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Bathtub Curve** is a graphical representation of the hazard function `h(t)` versus time `t`. It visually justifies why **reliability engineering splits the lifecycle** of equipment into three distinct management strategies: **burn-in**, **random-failure mitigation**, and **preventive replacement**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Reliability Function `R(t)`. State any four of its fundamental mathematical properties.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark) Definition:** The Reliability Function $R(t)$ is the probability that a component or system performs its intended function without failure for a specified time `t`, under stated conditions. $R(t) = P(T > t)$.
- **(2 Marks) Four Properties:**
  1. **Initial condition:** $R(0) = 1$ — a new component is fully reliable.
  2. **Terminal condition:** $R(\infty) = 0$ — every component eventually fails.
  3. **Monotonically non-increasing:** $R(t_1) \geq R(t_2)$ for $t_1 < t_2$.
  4. **Complementarity:** $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$ and $R(t) + F(t) = 1$.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the Reliability Function `R(t)` and the Hazard Function `h(t)` with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Aspect | Reliability `R(t)` | Hazard `h(t)` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Unconditional survival probability up to time `t` | Conditional failure rate at time `t`, given survival |
| **Mathematical Form** | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ | $h(t) = f(t) / R(t)$ |
| **Units** | Dimensionless | `1/time` (e.g., per hour) |
| **Example** | 0.95 of 1000 LEDs survive 1000 hr | 0.0001 failures/hr per surviving pump |
| **Trend** | Monotonically decreasing | May increase, decrease, or stay constant |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **Master Equation** relating the Reliability Function `R(t)` to the Cumulative Hazard Function `H(t)`. Show every step clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a Weibull-distributed system with shape parameter $\beta = 2$ and scale parameter $\eta = 5000$ hours, compute:
- (i) The reliability at `t = 2000 hours`.
- (ii) The MTTF of the system.

Use $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.8862$. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Start from the definition of hazard function.

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Substitute $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$.

$$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Separate variables.

$$-h(t)\,dt = \frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Integrate from `0` to `t`, with `R(0) = 1`.

$$-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du = \int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = \ln[R(t)] - \ln(1) = \ln[R(t)]$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Exponentiate both sides.

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du\right)$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Recognize the integral as $H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du$.

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp(-H(t))}$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** State the engineering significance — this is the **Master Equation** linking survival probability to the cumulative hazard, valid for *any* lifetime distribution.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the Weibull reliability expression.

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Substituting $\beta = 2$, $\eta = 5000$ hr, $t = 2000$ hr:

$$R(2000) = \exp\!\left(-\left(\frac{2000}{5000}\right)^{2}\right) = \exp(-(0.4)^{2}) = \exp(-0.16)$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Numerical evaluation.

$$R(2000) = e^{-0.16} \approx 0.8521 \quad \text{(i.e., 85.21% reliability)}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** State the Weibull MTTF formula.

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Substitute $\beta = 2$, $\eta = 5000$, and use $\Gamma(1.5) = 0.8862$.

$$\text{MTTF} = 5000 \times \Gamma(1 + 0.5) = 5000 \times \Gamma(1.5) = 5000 \times 0.8862$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} \approx 4431 \text{ hours}}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` *(Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **Hazard Function** `h(t)`. Show how it relates to `R(t)` and `f(t)`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A non-repairable pump has an exponential failure rate $\lambda = 0.0002$ per hour. Determine:
- (i) The probability of failure within the first 500 hours.
- (ii) The probability of survival beyond 1500 hours.
- (iii) The MTTF of the pump.

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Concept Explanation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** The Hazard Function $h(t)$ is the **instantaneous rate of failure at time `t`, conditional on survival up to that time**. It answers the question: *"Given the system has survived until time `t`, what is the rate at which it is failing now?"*

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Mathematical derivation of the relationship:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)}\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Three classical regimes of $h(t)$:
1. **Decreasing hazard** ($h$ falls with $t$): infant-mortality phase, $\beta < 1$ in Weibull.
2. **Constant hazard** ($h = \lambda$): useful-life phase, exponential distribution, $\beta = 1$.
3. **Increasing hazard** ($h$ rises with $t$): wear-out phase, $\beta > 1$ in Weibull.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Real-world example: Bearings exhibit **increasing hazard** due to fatigue; electronic components during **early life** show **decreasing hazard**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the exponential reliability expression: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Part (i) — Probability of failure within first 500 hr:

$$F(500) = 1 - R(500) = 1 - e^{-0.0002 \times 500} = 1 - e^{-0.1} = 1 - 0.9048 = 0.0952$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Part (ii) — Probability of survival beyond 1500 hr:

$$R(1500) = e^{-0.0002 \times 1500} = e^{-0.3} \approx 0.7408$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Part (iii) — MTTF for exponential distribution:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0002} = 5000 \text{ hours}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in `R(t)` Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary condition** $R(0) = 1$ during integration — costs **1 full mark** in derivations.
> 2. **Confusing `R(t)` and `F(t)`** in numerical problems — `F(t) = 1 - R(t)`, NOT the other way.
> 3. **Sign error** when writing $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$ — the negative sign is mandatory; losing it gives the wrong sign of $f(t)$.
> 4. **Wrong units for `h(t)`** — examiners expect `1/time` (e.g., per hour, per cycle). Writing just "per hour" for $R(t)$ is also wrong — it is dimensionless.
> 5. **MTTF vs MTBF confusion** — MTTF is for *non-repairable* items; MTBF is for *repairable* systems. Using the wrong term costs a mark in viva.
> 6. **Forgetting the Gamma function identity** $\Gamma(2) = 1! = 1$ when checking the exponential Weibull special case.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for `The Reliability Function`:**

- **Core Definition:** $R(t) = P(T > t)$ — probability of survival past time `t`.
- **Complementarity:** $R(t) + F(t) = 1$, where $F(t)$ is the CDF of failure.
- **Failure Density:** $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$.
- **Hazard Function:** $h(t) = f(t) / R(t)$ — *conditional* failure rate.
- **Master Equation (★★★★):** $R(t) = \exp(-H(t)) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du\right)$.
- **MTTF:** $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$.
- **Exponential Distribution:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$, constant hazard.
- **Weibull Distribution:** $R(t) = \exp(-(t/\eta)^{\beta})$, $\text{MTTF} = \eta\,\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.
- **Shape Parameter $\beta$ Decides:**
  - $\beta < 1$: Decreasing hazard → infant mortality.
  - $\beta = 1$: Constant hazard → useful life (exponential).
  - $\beta > 1$: Increasing hazard → wear-out.
- **Properties:** $R(0) = 1$, $R(\infty) = 0$, monotonically non-increasing, $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$.
- **Engineering Use:** PFD calculation in LOPA, RBI scheduling, MTTF estimation, SIL verification under IEC 61511.
- **Common Pitfall:** Always remember the **negative sign** in $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$ — this is the single most-frequently-lost mark in KTU derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Failure Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Failure Rate

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA), the **Failure Rate (λ)** is formally defined as the *conditional probability per unit time* that a system, component, or piece of equipment will fail, **given that it has survived up to the current time** *t*. It is a fundamental reliability parameter used in quantitative risk assessment (QRA), Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA), and probabilistic safety assessment (PSA).

Mathematically, the failure rate is expressed as the **instantaneous rate of change** of unreliability, normalized by the survival probability:

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t}$$

Where:
- $T$ = the random variable representing the time-to-failure
- $\lambda(t)$ = failure rate (instantaneous hazard rate) at time $t$
- Units: **failures per hour**, **failures per year (f/yr)**, or **per million hours (FIT)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Failure rate is the cornerstone parameter for calculating the *frequency of initiating events* in process plants. It directly feeds into the failure probability calculations used in **LOPA** and **SIL (Safety Integrity Level) determination** as per **IEC 61511**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Lightbulb Test"

Imagine you have a brand-new LED bulb in your hostel room.

- **Right after purchase (early life):** There is a *small chance* it fails almost immediately due to manufacturing defects (poor solder joint, weak filament). Once it survives the first 10 hours, that risk drops sharply.
- **After 5,000 hours (useful life):** The bulb has settled into a predictable "steady performance zone." The chance of it failing in the *next* hour is roughly constant — say, 1 in 50,000. This is its **constant failure rate**.
- **After 50,000 hours (wear-out):** The internal components have physically degraded. The risk of failure *in the next hour* starts to climb steeply. Eventually, it will burn out.

This three-stage behaviour is the famous **"Bathtub Curve"** of reliability engineering, and it gives intuitive meaning to failure rate. The failure rate is essentially *"given that the bulb is still working right now, what is the probability it will fail in the next instant?"*

## 1.3 Why Failure Rate Matters in HIRA

In process industries, the failure rate of equipment like:
- Pressure Safety Valves (PSVs)
- Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valves
- Pumps and compressors
- Sensors and transmitters
- Relief devices
- Human operators (Human Error Rate — HER)

…is used to compute the **frequency of loss of containment events**, **demand rate on protection layers**, and the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** of safety instrumented functions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Data Sources:** Industry-accepted failure rate databases used by KTU-exam-relevant industries include **OREDA (Offshore and Onshore Reliability Data)**, **CCPS PERD (Process Equipment Reliability Database)**, and **TNO Green Book**. These provide generic failure rates that are adjusted for service conditions via modification factors.

## 1.4 Types of Failure Rate (Classification)

| Classification | Description | Common Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant Failure Rate (CFR)** | λ is independent of time; observed during the useful life period | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ per hour |
| **Decreasing Failure Rate (DFR)** | λ decreases with time; observed during early life / burn-in | Early-life period |
| **Increasing Failure Rate (IFR)** | λ increases with time; observed during wear-out | End-of-life period |
| **Hazard Rate (instantaneous)** | Generic term; same as failure rate in reliability context | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probability Foundation — Setting the Stage

To fully grasp failure rate, three foundational probability functions must be understood:

**1. Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of failure — F(t)**
$$F(t) = P(T \leq t)$$
Probability that failure occurs *on or before* time $t$.

**2. Reliability (Survival) Function — R(t)**
$$R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)$$
Probability that the component survives *beyond* time $t$.

**3. Probability Density Function (PDF) of failure — f(t)**
$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$
The "density" of failure occurrences around time $t$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — How the Failure Rate is Constructed

The derivation flows in five logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Start with conditional probability:** The probability that a component fails in the tiny window $[t, t+\Delta t]$, *given* that it has already survived to time $t$, is:
$$P(t \leq T < t+\Delta t \mid T \geq t) = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{1 - F(t)} = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)}$$

- **Step 2 — Take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$:** This converts the discrete probability into an *instantaneous rate*:
$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t \cdot R(t)} = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dF(t)}{dt}$$

- **Step 3 — Substitute $f(t)$ for the derivative:** Since $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$, we obtain the canonical definition:
$$\boxed{\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}}$$

- **Step 4 — Derive the cumulative hazard function:** Integrate the failure rate from 0 to $t$:
$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du$$

- **Step 5 — Express reliability in terms of the hazard:** Using $f(t) = -dR/dt$ and the integral above, the most powerful reliability formula emerges:
$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du\right)}$$

## 2.3 The Three Regimes of the Bathtub Curve

| Phase | Time Region | Failure Rate Behaviour | Physical Cause | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in)** | $0 \leq t < t_1$ | **Decreasing** (DFR) | Manufacturing defects, installation errors, poor commissioning | Factory burn-in, debugging, run-in testing |
| **Phase II — Useful Life (Normal Operation)** | $t_1 \leq t < t_2$ | **Constant** (CFR), $\lambda = \lambda_0$ | Random, statistically independent failures | Preventive maintenance, redundancy |
| **Phase III — Wear-out (Aging)** | $t \geq t_2$ | **Increasing** (IFR) | Fatigue, corrosion, erosion, wear | Replacement, refurbishment, life extension |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** In the **constant failure rate regime**, the *useful life period*, all probability calculations become very simple. The exponential distribution governs, and a single number (λ) characterizes the entire system. This is why most textbook problems and industry LOPA sheets assume constant λ.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Instantaneous failure rate | General definition, all regimes |
| 2 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability under constant λ | Useful life, exponential distribution |
| 3 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Unreliability under constant λ | Useful life, exponential distribution |
| 4 | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | PDF of failure under constant λ | Useful life, exponential distribution |
| 5 | $H(t) = \lambda t$ | Cumulative hazard under constant λ | Useful life, exponential distribution |
| 6 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure | Constant λ, non-repairable |
| 7 | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Constant λ, repairable |
| 8 | $R(t_1 + t_2 \mid t_1) = e^{-\lambda t_2}$ | Memoryless property | Constant λ, exponential |
| 9 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_I}{2}$ | Probability of Failure on Demand | LOPA, IEC 61511 (low demand mode) |
| 10 | $f_{\text{IE}} = \lambda \cdot t_{\text{active}}$ | Initiating event frequency | Process safety, QRA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Exam Favourite):** *MTTF* is used for **non-repairable** components (e.g., a rupture disc, a one-shot initiator). *MTBF* is used for **repairable** systems (e.g., a pump, a control valve). Numerically, $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ (Mean Time To Repair).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Process Safety & LOPA:** Failure rates of initiators (e.g., pump deadhead, valve passing) are multiplied by operating time to obtain *initiating event frequencies*. These are then compared against company risk tolerance criteria.
- **SIL Determination (IEC 61511):** PFD of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is calculated from the failure rates of its sensors, logic solver, and final elements.
- **Inspection & Maintenance Planning:** API 510, API 570, and RBI (Risk-Based Inspection) frameworks use failure rate trends to optimize inspection intervals.
- **Insurance & Risk Engineering:** Failure rate data feed into property damage and business interruption probability models.
- **Human Reliability Analysis (HRA):** Human Error Rates (e.g., THERP method, $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ per demand) are the "failure rate" of operator actions on safety layers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1 — The Constant Failure Rate Exponential Law

**Statement to Prove:** When $\lambda(t) = \lambda = $ constant, the reliability function is $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Step 1 — Begin with the general relationship.**
From the definition of failure rate:
$$\lambda = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$.**
Since $f(t)$ is the rate of failure and $R(t)$ is survival, the rate of *decrease* in survival equals the rate of *increase* in failure:
$$\lambda = \frac{-\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}}{R(t)}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange the equation.**
$$\lambda = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply.**
$$\lambda \cdot dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$.**
At $t = 0$, $R(0) = 1$ (a brand-new component is certainly working at time zero):
$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda \, du = -\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the right-hand side integral.**
$$-\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -[\ln R]_{1}^{R(t)} = -(\ln R(t) - \ln 1) = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the left-hand side integral.**
$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda \, du = \lambda t$$

**Step 8 — Equate and solve.**
$$\lambda t = -\ln R(t)$$
$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t$$
$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$

This is the **single most important equation in reliability engineering**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2 — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) for Constant λ

**Statement to Prove:** $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ under constant failure rate.

**Step 1 — Definition of MTTF.**
MTTF is the expected (mean) time-to-failure:
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the exponential PDF.**
For constant λ: $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Use integration by parts.**
Let $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$. Then $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.
$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ t \cdot (-e^{-\lambda t}) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\lambda t}) \, dt$$
$$\text{MTTF} = -t e^{-\lambda t} \Big|_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the boundary term.**
As $t \to \infty$: $t e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ (exponential decay wins). At $t = 0$: $0 \cdot e^{0} = 0$.
So the boundary term is $0 - 0 = 0$.

**Step 5 — Evaluate the remaining integral.**
$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 6 — Conclude.**
$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation #3 — Reliability from a Known Failure Count

**Scenario:** A pressure transmitter has been in service for 4 years (35,040 hours) and has experienced 2 failures. Compute the (constant) failure rate, the reliability for an additional 1 year, and the MTTF.

**Step 1 — Compute the empirical (average) failure rate.**
The simplest estimator of λ is *number of failures / cumulative operating time*:
$$\lambda = \frac{n}{T_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numbers.**
$$\lambda = \frac{2 \text{ failures}}{4 \text{ years}} = 0.5 \text{ failures/year}$$

Equivalently, in hours: $\lambda = \dfrac{2}{35{,}040} = 5.71 \times 10^{-5}$ failures/hour.

**Step 3 — Compute the reliability for an additional 1 year ($t = 8760$ hours).**
$$R(1 \text{ yr}) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.5 \times 1} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**Interpretation:** There is a **60.65% probability** the transmitter will survive the next year without failure.

**Step 4 — Compute the MTTF.**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2 \text{ years}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check.** The MTTF (2 years) is *less than* the actual survival time (4 years), which is consistent — the equipment was on its "last legs" having already had 2 failures.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation #4 — PFD of a Single Proof-Tested Component (LOPA)

**Scenario:** A low-demand safety instrumented function (SIF) consists of a sensor with dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. Proof-test interval is $T_I = 1$ year = 8760 hours. The SIF operates in low-demand mode (one demand at a random time in the interval).

**Step 1 — Recall the PFD formula for constant failure rate, low demand, with perfect proof testing and perfect repair.**
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} (1 - e^{-\lambda_D \cdot t})\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Use the approximation valid for very small $\lambda_D T_I$.**
When $\lambda_D T_I \ll 1$, $1 - e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx \lambda_D t$:
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \frac{1}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} \lambda_D t \, dt = \frac{\lambda_D}{T_I} \cdot \frac{T_I^{2}}{2} = \frac{\lambda_D T_I}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numbers.**
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})(8760)}{2} = \frac{0.01752}{2} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret in SIL terms.**
- $\text{PFD} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$ falls in the range $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2})$, corresponding to **SIL 2** (per IEC 61511).

**Step 5 — Reduce the test interval to improve SIL.**
If $T_I$ is halved to 6 months (4380 hours):
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})(4380)}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Still SIL 2}$$

If $T_I$ is reduced to 1 month (730 hours):
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})(730)}{2} = 7.30 \times 10^{-4} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{SIL 3}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Halving the failure rate or halving the proof-test interval *both* halve the PFD. The most effective path to a higher SIL is therefore a combination of more reliable components **and** more frequent proof testing.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Failure Rate by Component Type (Typical Industry Values)

| Component | Mode | Generic $\lambda$ (per hour) | Data Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Centrifugal pump (process) | Failure to run | $1 \times 10^{-5}$ to $5 \times 10^{-5}$ | OREDA |
| Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) | Failure to open on demand | $1 \times 10^{-5}$ to $1 \times 10^{-3}$ | CCPS |
| Check valve | Failure to close (passing) | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ to $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | OREDA |
| Pressure transmitter | Dangerous failure | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ to $5 \times 10^{-6}$ | OREDA |
| Solenoid valve (ESD) | Failure to operate | $1 \times 10^{-5}$ to $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | CCPS |
| Human operator (simple action) | Error of omission | $1 \times 10^{-3}$ to $1 \times 10^{-1}$ per demand | THERP / NUREG |
| Rupture disc | Spurious operation | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ per year | CCPS |
| Logic solver (PLC, redundant) | Dangerous failure | $1 \times 10^{-7}$ to $1 \times 10^{-6}$ | OREDA |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Exams:** Using a *single point value* (e.g., $10^{-5}$/h) for all components without acknowledging the *data uncertainty bounds*. Industry practice demands either a **point estimate with error factor** or a **probability distribution** (e.g., lognormal) for proper QRA.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Define failure rate. Explain its significance in the context of Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA).**

**Model Answer:**

Failure rate $\lambda(t)$ is the conditional probability per unit time that a component fails, given that it has survived to time $t$. It is mathematically defined as:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

where $f(t)$ is the probability density of failure and $R(t)$ is the reliability at time $t$. Under a constant failure rate (useful life period), this reduces to a single value $\lambda$, and reliability follows the exponential law $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Significance in LOPA:** Failure rate is the primary input used to compute the *frequency of initiating events* and the *Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)* of each independent protection layer (IPL). For example, the failure rate of a check valve (failure to close) is multiplied by the process demand time to obtain the initiator frequency. The PFD of an IPL is then calculated as $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \lambda_D T_I / 2$ for proof-tested components. The product of the initiator frequency and the PFDs of all IPLs between the initiating event and the consequence yields the *mitigated event frequency*, which is compared to the company's risk tolerance. Thus, failure rate is the **fundamental quantitative parameter** without which LOPA cannot be performed.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Sketch and explain the bathtub curve. Identify the dominant failure mechanism in each region.**

**Model Answer:**

The bathtub curve plots the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ as a function of operating time and exhibits three distinct regions:

- **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** Failure rate is *high but decreasing*. Dominant mechanism: manufacturing defects, material impurities, installation errors, and commissioning mistakes. Engineering action: factory burn-in, run-in testing, debugging.

- **Region II — Useful Life (Normal Operation):** Failure rate is *constant and minimum*. Dominant mechanism: random, statistically independent, chance failures (e.g., random overload, random material variability). Engineering action: preventive maintenance, redundancy, condition monitoring.

- **Region III — Wear-out (Aging):** Failure rate is *increasing*. Dominant mechanism: fatigue, corrosion, erosion, creep, wear, and chemical degradation. Engineering action: replacement, refurbishment, life extension programs.

The constant failure rate of Region II is the most commonly assumed in reliability calculations for process equipment.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024, ESE Module 2]

**(a)** A centrifugal pump in a refinery has been in continuous service for 3 years. During this period, it has failed 4 times. Assuming the pump operates under a constant failure rate regime:

  **(i)** Calculate the average failure rate in failures per year and failures per hour.
  **(ii)** Determine the reliability of the pump for the next 2 years of operation.
  **(iii)** Compute the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF).
  **(iv)** What is the probability that the pump will fail *during* the next 2 years?

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the significance of the constant failure rate assumption in process safety studies. Discuss the conditions under which this assumption is valid and the consequences of misapplying it.

**(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Operating time: $T = 3$ years
- Number of failures: $n = 4$
- Constant failure rate assumed.

**Step (i) — Compute the average failure rate.**

The empirical failure rate (Maximum Likelihood Estimator for constant λ) is:
$$\lambda = \frac{n}{T} = \frac{4 \text{ failures}}{3 \text{ years}} = 1.333 \text{ failures/year}$$

Convert to per-hour basis. Use $1 \text{ year} = 8760$ hours:
$$\lambda = \frac{1.333}{8760} = 1.522 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}$$

**Step (ii) — Reliability for the next 2 years.**

For $t = 2$ years, under the exponential law:
$$R(2) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-1.333 \times 2} = e^{-2.6667}$$

Evaluate:
$$R(2) = 0.0697 \approx 6.97\%$$

**Interpretation:** There is only a **6.97% chance** the pump will run without failure for the next 2 years. The pump is clearly in its wear-out phase or has very poor reliability for this service.

**Step (iii) — Mean Time To Failure.**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{1.333} = 0.75 \text{ years} \approx 6570 \text{ hours}$$

**Step (iv) — Probability of failure during the next 2 years.**

This is the unreliability at $t = 2$ years:
$$F(2) = 1 - R(2) = 1 - 0.0697 = 0.9303 = 93.03\%$$

**Alternative verification using the unreliability formula:**
$$F(2) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} = 1 - e^{-1.333 \times 2} = 0.9303 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The pump has a **93% probability** of failing within the next 2 years. This is a strong signal for **immediate overhaul, root-cause analysis, or replacement**.

---

#### Part (b) — Significance of Constant Failure Rate (7 Marks)

**Definition Recap:** A constant failure rate $\lambda$ means the probability of failure in the next instant does not depend on how long the equipment has already operated. This is the **memoryless property** of the exponential distribution.

**Why it is significant in process safety:**

1. **Mathematical Simplicity:** Only one parameter (λ) is needed to characterize the entire reliability behaviour. Reliability is simply $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, and PFD reduces to $\lambda T_I / 2$. This enables quick LOPA calculations.

2. **Data Availability:** Most generic failure rate databases (OREDA, CCPS PERD, TNO) report point estimates of λ, which are only valid under the constant failure rate assumption.

3. **SIL Calculations:** IEC 61511 PFD formulas are derived assuming constant failure rates. The PFD of a SIF is the sum of contributions from each subsystem, each computed as $\lambda_D T_I / 2$.

4. **Industry Acceptance:** Standards like API 581 (RBI) and IEC 61511 rely on this assumption for base-case calculations.

**Conditions for Validity:**

- The equipment must be in its **useful life period** (Region II of the bathtub curve).
- Failures must be **statistically independent** and not driven by systematic degradation.
- A **burn-in** program has eliminated early-life defects.
- The component is operated **within design limits** (no over-pressure, over-temperature, etc.).
- **Proof testing** is performed to identify and reset wear-out failures.

**Consequences of Misapplication:**

- If the equipment is in the **wear-out phase** but λ is treated as constant, the predicted reliability will be **optimistic and unsafe** — leading to underestimation of PFD and incorrect SIL.
- If it is in the **infant mortality phase**, the constant-λ calculation will be **pessimistic**, leading to overdesign.
- For **human reliability**, the constant failure rate assumption is **fundamentally wrong** — human error probability depends on training, fatigue, time pressure, and procedure quality, not on a single time-independent rate.

**Engineering Recommendation:** Always assess the phase of the bathtub curve before applying the constant-λ model. For non-constant behaviour, use **Weibull distribution** (two-parameter: shape $\beta$ and scale $\eta$) which can represent decreasing, constant, or increasing failure rates depending on $\beta$.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024, ESE Module 2]

**(a)** A low-demand Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is required to bring a reactor to a safe state upon high-pressure detection. The sensor has a dangerous failure rate of $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The proof-test interval is $T_I = 2$ years.

  **(i)** Calculate the average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) of the sensor.
  **(ii)** Identify the SIL achieved by this sensor alone.
  **(iii)** If the consequence severity requires SIL 3 (PFD between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-2}$), what is the maximum permissible proof-test interval?
  **(iv)** What alternative strategy could achieve SIL 3 without changing the test interval?

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **MTTF** and **MTBF**. Why is this distinction important when dealing with a *rupture disc* versus a *centrifugal pump* in a process plant?

**(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Dangerous failure rate: $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour
- Proof-test interval: $T_I = 2$ years $= 2 \times 8760 = 17{,}520$ hours

**Step (i) — Compute the average PFD.**

Using the standard IEC 61511 formula for low-demand mode, constant failure rate, perfect proof test, perfect repair:
$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \frac{\lambda_D T_I}{2} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(17{,}520)}{2} = \frac{0.0876}{2} = 0.0438$$

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = 4.38 \times 10^{-2}}$$

**Step (ii) — Identify the SIL.**

Per IEC 61511, the SIL is determined by the PFD range:

| SIL | PFD Range |
|---|---|
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |

Since $\text{PFD} = 4.38 \times 10^{-2}$ lies in the range $[10^{-2}, 10^{-1})$, this single sensor alone provides **SIL 1**.

**Step (iii) — Maximum $T_I$ for SIL 3.**

For SIL 3, we require $\text{PFD} \leq 10^{-3}$. Setting the boundary:
