```mermaid
    G2 --> T1
    T1 --> R[Final Risk = P_top × C]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) Probability Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Process Design - Inherently Safer] --> P2[BPCS Control Loops]
    P2 --> P3[Alarms and Operator Intervention]
    P3 --> P4[Safety Instrumented Function - SIF]
    P4 --> P5[Physical Protection - Relief Valves, Dikes]
    P5 --> P6[Emergency Response - Plant, Community]
    P6 --> P7[Mitigated Event Frequency Target]
    P1 -->|PFD1| M1[Multiply f_initiating]
    P2 -->|PFD2| M1
    P3 -->|PFD3| M1
    P4 -->|PFD4| M1
    P5 -->|PFD5| M1
    P6 -->|PFD6| M1
    M1 --> P7
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Failure Rate vs Time (Bathtub Curve Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X[Time Axis]
    T0[Commissioning t=0] --> T1[Useful Life Starts]
    T1 --> T2[Wear-out Begins]
    end
    A1[Region 1: Infant Mortality] -.lambda decreasing.-> A2[Region 2: Useful Life - Constant lambda]
    A2 -.lambda constant.-> A3[Region 3: Wear-out - lambda increasing]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block: Risk Matrix Application of Probability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Consequence Severity C] --> S2[Catastrophic]
    S1 --> S3[Major]
    S1 --> S4[Moderate]
    S1 --> S5[Minor]
    P1[Probability P] --> P2[Frequent > 0.1 per year]
    P1 --> P3[Probable 0.01 to 0.1]
    P1 --> P4[Occasional 0.001 to 0.01]
    P1 --> P5[Remote 0.0001 to 0.001]
    P1 --> P6[Improbable less than 0.0001]
    S2 --> R1[Risk Rating: HIGH]
    P2 --> R1
    R1 --> AC1[Immediate Action Required - HALT]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "Probability" as applied to hazard identification and risk assessment. State Kolmogorov's axioms of probability.**

**Model Answer:**

In HIRA, **probability** is a numerical measure (ranging from 0 to 1) of the likelihood that a hazardous event or failure will occur within a defined time period or operational scenario. It is the quantitative input to the risk equation $R = P \times C$.

**Kolmogorov's Three Axioms:**
1. **Non-negativity:** For any event $A$, $0 \leq P(A) \leq 1$
2. **Normalization:** $P(S) = 1$ where $S$ is the entire sample space
3. **Additivity:** For mutually exclusive events $A$ and $B$, $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of "Hazard Rate" $\lambda(t)$. What is the significance of a constant hazard rate in HIRA?**

**Model Answer:**

The **hazard rate** $\lambda(t)$ is the instantaneous rate of failure of a component at time $t$, conditional on its survival up to that time. It is mathematically defined as:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$$

A **constant hazard rate** ($\lambda(t) = \lambda$) implies that the component is in its **useful life phase** (random failure region of the bathtub curve). In HIRA, this allows the use of the **exponential distribution**, which has the simple reliability form $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — enabling tractable calculations in fault tree analysis, event tree analysis, and LOPA.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **bathtub curve** of failure rate. Discuss the significance of each region with respect to hazard identification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pressure safety valve in a refinery has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ per year. Determine:
  (i) The reliability after 3 years of operation
  (ii) The probability of failure in the 5th year of operation
  (iii) The MTTF
  (iv) Comment on the safety implications if the consequence of failure is classified as "Catastrophic" with a target tolerable risk of $10^{-6}$ per year. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bathtub Curve Explanation:**

The bathtub curve plots the **failure rate $\lambda(t)$** on the y-axis against **time $t$** on the x-axis. It consists of three distinct regions:

- **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** Failure rate is high at $t=0$ and decreases with time. Failures here are due to **manufacturing defects, installation errors, material flaws, or poor workmanship**. HIRA implication: Quality control and pre-service testing reduce this region.

- **Region II — Useful Life:** Failure rate is **approximately constant** at the minimum value $\lambda$. Failures are **stochastic and random** — independent of age. HIRA implication: This is the operating region assumed in most reliability models (exponential distribution); failure prediction uses historical data.

- **Region III — Wear-out:** Failure rate **increases sharply** with time. Failures are due to **fatigue, corrosion, erosion, and aging**. HIRA implication: Preventive maintenance and replacement schedules are designed to keep operation within Region II.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ per year

**(i) Reliability at $t = 3$ years:**
$$R(3) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-3})(3)} = e^{-0.015}$$
$$R(3) = 0.9851 \text{ or } 98.51\%$$

**(ii) Probability of failure in the 5th year (between $t=4$ and $t=5$):**
$$P(4 < T \leq 5) = F(5) - F(4) = (1 - e^{-0.025}) - (1 - e^{-0.020})$$
$$= e^{-0.020} - e^{-0.025}$$
$$= 0.9802 - 0.9753 = 0.0049 \text{ or } 0.49\%$$

**(iii) Mean Time To Failure:**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \text{ years}$$

**(iv) Safety Implications:**

The probability of failure per year (for the year of interest) is roughly $P \approx 5 \times 10^{-3}$ per year. If consequence is **Catastrophic** and target tolerable risk is $R_{\text{target}} = 10^{-6}$/year, then:

$$R = P \times C \Rightarrow 5 \times 10^{-3} \times C \leq 10^{-6}$$

The required probability reduction factor is:
$$\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-6}} = 5000$$

The system would require **at least 3-4 additional independent protection layers** (e.g., SIL-3 SIF, BPCS, and physical relief) to bring the mitigated probability into the tolerable range, demonstrating **ALARP** is not satisfied without further safeguards.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]

**(a)** Explain the **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** methodology. How is probability used to determine if the residual risk is within tolerable limits? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reactor has an initiating event (loss of cooling) with frequency $f_I = 0.05$ per year. The following Independent Protection Layers (IPLs) are present:

  - IPL-1: BPCS Control Loop, PFD = $10^{-1}$
  - IPL-2: Safety Instrumented Function (SIL-1), PFD = $10^{-2}$
  - IPL-3: Pressure Relief Valve, PFD = $10^{-2}$

  Calculate:
  (i) The mitigated event frequency
  (ii) Compare with target frequency of $10^{-5}$ per year and recommend further action if required
  (iii) If the consequence is "3 fatalities per event," compute the individual risk per year **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) LOPA Methodology:**

**Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** is a semi-quantitative risk assessment method that applies **probability of failure on demand (PFD)** values to each Independent Protection Layer (IPL) to estimate the **mitigated frequency** of a hazardous scenario.

**Steps in LOPA:**
1. Identify the **scenario** (initiating event + consequence)
2. Determine the **initiating event frequency** $f_I$
3. Identify all **IPLs** in the safeguard chain
4. Assign **PFD values** to each IPL (from industry standard tables like IEC 61511)
5. Calculate the **mitigated event frequency:**
$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_I \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i$$
6. Compare with the **tolerable target frequency** for that consequence severity
7. If $f_{\text{mitigated}} > f_{\text{target}}$, recommend **additional IPLs** (e.g., higher SIL-rated SIF)

LOPA uses probability as the **central metric** to determine whether the residual (mitigated) risk falls within the organization's risk tolerance criteria, and if not, additional protection layers must be added.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $f_I = 0.05$/year, $\text{PFD}_1 = 10^{-1}$, $\text{PFD}_2 = 10^{-2}$, $\text{PFD}_3 = 10^{-2}$

**(i) Mitigated event frequency:**
$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_I \times \text{PFD}_1 \times \text{PFD}_2 \times \text{PFD}_3$$
$$= 0.05 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-2}$$
$$= 0.05 \times 10^{-5} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per year}$$

**(ii) Comparison with Target:**

Target frequency: $f_{\text{target}} = 10^{-5}$ per year

Since $5 \times 10^{-7} < 10^{-5}$, the mitigated frequency is **lower** than the target — the system meets the risk tolerance criterion. **No further action is required.** However, the engineer should document an **ALARP demonstration** to show that the cost of additional protection is grossly disproportionate to the marginal safety benefit.

**(iii) Individual Risk Calculation:**

$$\text{IR} = f_{\text{mitigated}} \times N_{\text{fatalities}}$$
$$= 5 \times 10^{-7} \times 3 = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ fatalities/year}$$

For an operator working 24/7 in the control room, this IR is **well below** the widely accepted tolerable individual risk of $10^{-4}$ fatalities/year (HSE upper limit) and also below the $10^{-6}$/year ALARP region threshold — confirming the design is **safe**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Always state the **time basis** for probability (e.g., "per year" or "per flight hour"). The examiner will deduct marks if you write $P = 0.05$ without specifying "/year".
>
> 2. **Independence assumption:** The AND/OR gate formulas ($P_{\text{AND}} = \prod P_i$ and $P_{\text{OR}} = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$) are valid **only if events are statistically independent**. In dependent events, use conditional probability. Many students lose marks by ignoring this prerequisite.
>
> 3. **Fault tree vs Event tree confusion:** Fault trees use **AND/OR** gates and calculate top event probability. Event trees use **branching paths** and calculate consequence frequency. Mixing up the two methods is a common error.
>
> 4. **MTTF vs MTBF:** MTTF is for **non-repairable** components; MTBF is for **repairable** systems. Use the correct term.
>
> 5. **LOPA calculation order:** Compute $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_I \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$ first, then compare with target. Students often divide by target instead of multiplying PFDs.
>
> 6. **Final answer with units and conclusion:** Always end numerical problems with a **commentary sentence** interpreting the result (e.g., "The mitigated frequency is within the tolerable range, hence ALARP is satisfied").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Application to Probability**

- ✅ **Risk Equation:** $R = P \times C$ — the single most important formula in HIRA
- ✅ **Reliability:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for constant failure rate (exponential distribution)
- ✅ **Unreliability:** $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$
- ✅ **MTTF:** $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ — average life of a non-repairable component
- ✅ **Hazard Rate:** $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$
- ✅ **Bathtub Curve:** Three regions — Infant Mortality, Useful Life (constant $\lambda$), Wear-out
- ✅ **Fault Tree AND Gate:** $P = \prod P_i$ (independent events only)
- ✅ **Fault Tree OR Gate:** $P = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$
- ✅ **Series System:** $R_s = \prod R_i$ (all components must work)
- ✅ **Parallel System:** $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ (at least one must work)
- ✅ **LOPA Formula:** $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_I \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$
- ✅ **Individual Risk (IR):** $IR = f_{\text{event}} \times P_{\text{being present}} \times P_{\text{fatality given event}}$
- ✅ **Societal Risk:** F-N curve; tolerability boundary $F \cdot N^2 = 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$
- ✅ **Kolmogorov Axioms:** Non-negativity, normalization, additivity — foundational to all probability
- ✅ **ALARP Principle:** Risk must be reduced until further reduction is "grossly disproportionate" in cost
- ✅ **Target Frequencies (industry standard):** Catastrophic $\sim 10^{-6}$/year; Major $\sim 10^{-5}$/year; Significant $\sim 10^{-4}$/year
- ✅ **Always state units** — probability per year, fatalities per year, etc.
- ✅ **Always check independence** before applying AND/OR gate formulas

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boolean Manipulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boolean Manipulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boolean Manipulation** in the context of Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment refers to the systematic application of **Boolean algebra** to model, combine, simplify, and analyze logical relationships between hazard-initiating events, fault conditions, and top-level undesired events (Catastrophic Failures). It forms the mathematical backbone of techniques like **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**, **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)**, and **Bow-Tie Analysis**, where system failure logic is expressed using logical operators **AND ($\cdot$)**, **OR ($+$)**, and **NOT ($\overline{A}$)**.

In safety engineering, every hazard pathway is reduced to a logical proposition: *"The accident occurs IF and ONLY IF a specific combination of basic events occurs."* Boolean Manipulation provides the formal language to express, minimize, and quantify these propositions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **fire alarm system** in a building. The alarm rings (Top Event) when **ANY ONE** of the following happens:
- A smoke detector triggers **OR**
- A heat sensor triggers **OR**
- A manual call point is pressed

In Boolean terms: $T = S_d + S_h + S_c$ (where $+$ means OR).

Now consider a **safety interlock**: A reactor shuts down **ONLY IF** (temperature exceeds limit **AND** pressure exceeds limit) **OR** (operator presses emergency stop).

$$T = (T_E \cdot P_E) + E_s$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Real Power of Boolean Manipulation**: It allows safety engineers to find the **Minimum number of component failures** required to cause a disaster. This is the foundation of calculating system unavailability, risk prioritization, and SIL (Safety Integrity Level) determination per **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**.

## 1.3 Variables in Boolean Algebra (Fault Tree Context)

| Symbol | Meaning in Logic | Hazard Context |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A, B, C \ldots$ | Basic Event | A single component failure (e.g., pump fails) |
| $T$ | Top Event | Undesired hazard event (e.g., reactor meltdown) |
| $\cdot$ (AND) | Intersection | Both conditions must occur simultaneously |
| $+$ (OR) | Union | Either condition can independently cause failure |
| $\overline{A}$ | NOT / Complement | Event A does NOT occur |
| $1$ | Certain Event | Guaranteed to happen |
| $0$ | Impossible Event | Cannot happen |

## 1.4 Why Boolean Manipulation is Critical for Safety Engineers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Simplification**: Reduces redundant logic paths in fault trees, making the analysis manageable.
> - **Minimal Cut Set Identification**: Finds the smallest combinations of failures that cause the top event.
> - **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**: Enables calculation of top event probability using inclusion-exclusion principles.
> - **Vulnerability Identification**: Highlights single-point failures (cut sets of order 1) that require immediate attention.
> - **Common Cause Failure Analysis**: Boolean methods help reveal coupled failure paths.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Boolean Laws (Applicable to Fault Trees)

The following axioms and theorems form the **mathematical toolkit** for simplifying any fault tree expression:

### 2.1.1 Identity Laws
$$A + 0 = A \qquad \text{(OR with impossible event = event itself)}$$
$$A \cdot 1 = A \qquad \text{(AND with certain event = event itself)}$$

### 2.1.2 Null (Dominance) Laws
$$A + 1 = 1 \qquad \text{(If a certain event occurs, the OR is certain)}$$
$$A \cdot 0 = 0 \qquad \text{(AND with impossible event = impossible)}$$

### 2.1.3 Idempotent Laws
$$A + A = A$$
$$A \cdot A = A$$

### 2.1.4 Complement Laws
$$A + \overline{A} = 1$$
$$A \cdot \overline{A} = 0$$

### 2.1.5 Commutative Laws
$$A + B = B + A$$
$$A \cdot B = B \cdot A$$

### 2.1.6 Associative Laws
$$A + (B + C) = (A + B) + C$$
$$A \cdot (B \cdot C) = (A \cdot B) \cdot C$$

### 2.1.7 Distributive Laws
$$A \cdot (B + C) = A \cdot B + A \cdot C$$
$$A + (B \cdot C) = (A + B) \cdot (A + C)$$

### 2.1.8 De Morgan's Theorems (Most Used in Safety Logic)
$$\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$$
$$\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety Significance of De Morgan's**: It allows conversion between **failure logic** and **success logic**. For example, "system does NOT fail" = "ALL subsystems do NOT fail."

### 2.1.9 Absorption Laws (Critical for Minimal Cut Set Reduction)
$$A + A \cdot B = A$$
$$A \cdot (A + B) = A$$

### 2.1.10 Simplification (Combining) Theorems
$$A \cdot B + A \cdot \overline{B} = A$$
$$(A + B) \cdot (A + \overline{B}) = A$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Theorem / Law | OR Form ($+$) | AND Form ($\cdot$) | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Identity | $A + 0 = A$ | $A \cdot 1 = A$ | Baseline reference |
| Null / Dominance | $A + 1 = 1$ | $A \cdot 0 = 0$ | Failure dominates |
| Idempotent | $A + A = A$ | $A \cdot A = A$ | Redundancy collapse |
| Complement | $A + \overline{A} = 1$ | $A \cdot \overline{A} = 0$ | Mutual exclusivity |
| Commutative | $A + B = B + A$ | $A \cdot B = B \cdot A$ | Order independence |
| Associative | $(A+B)+C = A+(B+C)$ | $(A\cdot B)\cdot C = A\cdot(B\cdot C)$ | Grouping flexibility |
| Distributive | $A+BC = (A+B)(A+C)$ | $A(B+C) = AB+AC$ | Factoring tool |
| De Morgan's | $\overline{A+B} = \overline{A}\cdot\overline{B}$ | $\overline{A\cdot B} = \overline{A}+\overline{B}$ | Logic inversion |
| Absorption | $A + AB = A$ | $A(A+B) = A$ | Cut set reduction |
| Combining | $AB + A\overline{B} = A$ | $(A+B)(A+\overline{B}) = A$ | Path elimination |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**: Reduces complex multi-gate trees to Minimal Cut Sets (MCS).
2. **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**: Logic check of failure combinations.
3. **SIL Determination (IEC 61508)**: Quantitative probability calculation uses Boolean structure.
4. **Reliability Block Diagrams (RBD)**: Series-parallel system logic maps directly to Boolean.
5. **Hazard and Operability (HAZOP)**: Logic verification of deviation propagation.
6. **Bow-Tie Analysis**: Left side (threats) and right side (consequences) use AND/OR Boolean gates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Simplifying a Fault Tree Expression

**Problem:** A pressure vessel rupture (Top Event $T$) occurs based on the following logic:
- Event $A$: Pressure relief valve fails to open
- Event $B$: Pressure exceeds design limit  
- Event $C$: Operator fails to respond
- Event $D$: Backup cooling fails

The fault tree gives: $T = AB + ACD + BC$

**Objective:** Simplify using Boolean algebra and identify minimal cut sets.

### Step-by-Step Simplification

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= A \cdot B + A \cdot C \cdot D + B \cdot C \quad \text{(Expand product notation for clarity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= A \cdot B + B \cdot C + A \cdot C \cdot D \quad \text{(Reorder for factoring - Commutative Law)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= B(A + C) + A \cdot C \cdot D \quad \text{(Factor out } B \text{ using Distributive Law)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= B \cdot A + B \cdot C + A \cdot C \cdot D \quad \text{(Re-expand for further factoring)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= A \cdot B + A \cdot C \cdot D + B \cdot C \quad \text{(Apply Absorption: } AB + ACD = AB + ACD \text{ - no absorption yet)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= B(A + C) + A \cdot C \cdot D \quad \text{(Check if } AB + BC = B(A+C) \text{ absorbs } ACD\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= B(A + C) + A \cdot C \cdot D \quad \text{(No further absorption - expression is in standard Sum-of-Products form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Minimal Cut Sets Identification

The expression $T = AB + BC + ACD$ gives three minimal cut sets:

| Cut Set | Order (Number of Events) | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\{A, B\}$ | 2 | Pressure relief fails AND Pressure exceeds |
| $\{B, C\}$ | 2 | Pressure exceeds AND Operator fails |
| $\{A, C, D\}$ | 3 | All three backup systems fail |

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Applying De Morgan's Theorem

**Problem:** A safety system succeeds (no top event) only when ALL three barriers ($X$, $Y$, $Z$) succeed. Express the failure of the safety system in terms of failures of individual barriers.

Let $S$ = Safety system succeeds, $\overline{S}$ = Safety system fails.
Let $\overline{X}, \overline{Y}, \overline{Z}$ = Individual barrier failures.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= X \cdot Y \cdot Z \quad \text{(Safety succeeds if ALL barriers work)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{S} &= \overline{X \cdot Y \cdot Z} \quad \text{(Take complement of both sides)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{S} &= \overline{X} + \overline{Y} + \overline{Z} \quad \text{(Apply De Morgan's Theorem repeatedly)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation**: The safety system fails if **ANY ONE** of the three barriers fails. This is the **OR-gate failure logic** that contradicts the "defense-in-depth" principle when barriers are not truly independent.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Absorption Law Application

**Problem:** Simplify $F = A + AB + AC + ABC$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A + AB + AC + ABC \quad \text{(Original expression)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A(1 + B) + AC(1 + B) \quad \text{(Factor using Distributive Law)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A(1 + B) + AC(1 + B) \quad \text{(Apply } 1 + B = 1 \text{ Null Law)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A \cdot 1 + AC \cdot 1 \quad \text{(Identity Law)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A + AC \quad \text{(Reorder)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A(1 + C) \quad \text{(Distributive Law)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A \cdot 1 \quad \text{(Null Law)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A \quad \text{(Identity Law - Final Result)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Quantitative Probability using Inclusion-Exclusion

> [!NOTE]
> **Probability Quantification Principle**: For events with small probabilities ($\le 0.1$), the rare-event approximation is used: $P(A \cup B) \approx P(A) + P(B)$.

For $T = AB + BC + AC$ with independent basic event probabilities $P(A), P(B), P(C)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= P(AB) + P(BC) + P(AC) - 2 \cdot P(ABC) \quad \text{(Inclusion-Exclusion for 3 terms)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T) &= P(A)P(B) + P(B)P(C) + P(A)P(C) - 2P(A)P(B)P(C) \quad \text{(Independence assumption)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the foundation for **quantitative fault tree evaluation** in risk assessment.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Boolean Operations and Their Safety Meaning

| Operation | Mathematical Symbol | Fault Tree Gate | Safety Meaning | Typical Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| AND | $A \cdot B$ | AND Gate | Both must fail together | Dual failure pathway |
| OR | $A + B$ | OR Gate | Either failure causes top event | Redundant components failing |
| NOT | $\overline{A}$ | Inhibit / NOT gate | Event must NOT occur | Inhibited protection |
| NAND | $\overline{A \cdot B}$ | NAND Gate | Not both fail simultaneously | Fail-safe voting logic |
| NOR | $\overline{A + B}$ | NOR Gate | Neither fails | High-integrity system |
| XOR | $A \oplus B$ | Exclusive OR | Exactly one fails | 2-out-of-2 voting |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fault Tree to Boolean Expression Mapping (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Identify Top Event T]
    nodeB[Identify Basic Events A, B, C, D]
    nodeC[Map each Gate to Boolean Operator]
    nodeD["AND Gate → MULTIPLICATION (·)"]
    nodeE["OR Gate → ADDITION (+)"]
    nodeF["NOT Gate → OVERLINE / Complement"]
    nodeG[Write Boolean Expression T = f(A,B,C,D)]
    nodeH[Apply Boolean Laws to Simplify]
    nodeI["Identity, Null, Idempotent"]
    nodeJ["Distributive Law"]
    nodeK["Absorption Law"]
    nodeL["De Morgan's Theorem"]
    nodeM[Obtain Sum-of-Products Form]
    nodeN[Identify Minimal Cut Sets MCS]
    nodeO[Calculate Top Event Probability]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeL
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Boolean-Based Risk Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer["INPUT LAYER - Basic Events"]
        evA["Event A: Pump Failure P=0.01"]
        evB["Event B: Valve Failure P=0.02"]
        evC["Event C: Sensor Failure P=0.005"]
        evD["Event D: Controller Failure P=0.008"]
    end

    subgraph logicLayer["LOGIC LAYER - Boolean Gates"]
        gate1["AND Gate: A AND B"]
        gate2["OR Gate: A OR C"]
        gate3["AND Gate: B AND D"]
    end

    subgraph processLayer["BOOLEAN MANIPULATION LAYER"]
        expr["Expression: T = AB + (A+C)BD"]
        simp["Simplified: T = AB + ABD + BCD"]
        absorb["After Absorption: T = AB + BCD"]
    end

    subgraph outputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER - Risk Metrics"]
        mcs["Minimal Cut Sets: {A,B}, {B,C,D}"]
        prob["Top Event Probability"]
        sil["SIL Classification"]
    end

    evA --> gate1
    evB --> gate1
    evA --> gate2
    evC --> gate2
    gate2 --> gate3
    evB --> gate3
    evD --> gate3
    gate1 --> expr
    gate3 --> expr
    expr --> simp
    simp --> absorb
    absorb --> mcs
    mcs --> prob
    prob --> sil
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy of Boolean Operations in Hazard Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph level1["LEVEL 1: PRIMITIVE EVENTS"]
        l1a["Component Failures"]
        l1b["Human Errors"]
        l1c["External Events"]
    end

    subgraph level2["LEVEL 2: BOOLEAN OPERATORS"]
        l2a["AND - Multi-cause Convergence"]
        l2b["OR - Multi-pathway Alternative"]
        l2c["NOT - Conditional Inhibition"]
    end

    subgraph level3["LEVEL 3: INTERMEDIATE EVENTS"]
        l3a["Subsystem Failure Modes"]
    end

    subgraph level4["LEVEL 4: TOP EVENT"]
        l4a["Catastrophic Hazard Realization"]
    end

    l1a --> l2a
    l1b --> l2b
    l1c --> l2c
    l2a --> l3a
    l2b --> l3a
    l2c --> l3a
    l3a --> l4a
```

## 4.4 Truth Table Representation for a 3-Input Hazard Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph tt["TRUTH TABLE: T = A·B + C"]
        t1["A=0, B=0, C=0 → T=0"]
        t2["A=0, B=0, C=1 → T=1"]
        t3["A=0, B=1, C=0 → T=0"]
        t4["A=0, B=1, C=1 → T=1"]
        t5["A=1, B=0, C=0 → T=0"]
        t6["A=1, B=0, C=1 → T=1"]
        t7["A=1, B=1, C=0 → T=1"]
        t8["A=1, B=1, C=1 → T=1"]
    end

    t1 --> t2 --> t3 --> t4 --> t5 --> t6 --> t7 --> t8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Truth Table**: Each row represents a unique system state. In safety engineering, the rows where $T = 1$ define the **failure combinations (cut sets)** that must be prevented or mitigated.
