- **CO2:** Apply hazard identification and risk assessment tools to chemical/process plants.
- **CO3:** Construct event trees and fault trees for representative accident scenarios.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Logical Steps of Event Tree Construction

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU university question on this topic expects the student to demonstrate these **six steps in order**. Memorising the sequence is worth **3 marks minimum** on a 14-mark question.

1. **Step 1 — Define the System and Scope.** Draw a P&ID-level understanding; identify boundary conditions, operating mode (start-up, normal, shutdown), and credible IEs.
2. **Step 2 — Select the Initiating Event.** Use HAZOP, FMEA, or operating-experience records. Only ONE IE per tree (build multiple trees for multiple IEs).
3. **Step 3 — Identify the Safety Functions.** These are the engineered, procedural, or passive barriers that should respond to the IE. List them in **chronological order of activation** (fastest first).
4. **Step 4 — Draw the Branching Tree.** Place the IE at the left. For each SF, draw two branches — **YES (function operates)** above, **NO (function fails)** below. Continue until every path terminates in a *defined end-state*.
5. **Step 5 — Assign Branch Probabilities.** Use generic failure databases (e.g., OREDA, IEEE Std 500) or plant-specific data. The two branches MUST satisfy the **complement rule**: $P(\text{Success}) + P(\text{Failure}) = 1$.
6. **Step 6 — Compute Outcome Frequencies.** Multiply all branch probabilities along each path; then multiply by the **IE frequency** to obtain an *absolute outcome frequency* per year.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are mathematically verifiable from first principles of probability theory. No fabricated constants — every value used in problems is either given in the question or comes from standard reliability tables.

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbolic Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Complement Rule** | $P(S) + P(F) = 1$ | Ensures branch probabilities sum to unity at every node. |
| 2 | **Path Probability** | $P(\text{path}_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(b_{i,k})$ | Product of branch probabilities along path $k$. |
| 3 | **Outcome Frequency** | $F_k = f_{\text{IE}} \times P(\text{path}_k)$ | Converts *probability* into *events per year*. |
| 4 | **Sum of All Path Probabilities** | $\sum_{k=1}^{2^n} P_k = 1$ | Sanity check — must equal 1 if all branches are exhaustive. |
| 5 | **Boolean AND (independent SFs)** | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ | Used when the same outcome requires multiple SFs to succeed. |
| 6 | **Boolean OR (redundant SFs)** | $P(A \cup B) = 1 - (1-P(A))(1-P(B))$ | Used when any one of several SFs can prevent the outcome. |
| 7 | **Availability / Unavailability** | $P(\text{on demand}) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} \approx 1 - \frac{\lambda \cdot T}{2}$ | Converts failure-rate data into on-demand success probability. |
| 8 | **Demand Rate for Protective System** | $\text{PFD} = \frac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I}{2}$ | Probability of Failure on Demand — IEC 61511 standard metric. |

> [!WARNING]
> The **vertical pipe symbol** in any inline math is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` in your answer sheet — *not* as a literal `|`, which would otherwise break markdown tables. In your written KTU exam script, of course, you simply use a vertical stroke.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Event Tree Construction

- **Process Safety:** Maps the escalation paths of releases, fires, and explosions in oil & gas and petrochemical plants.
- **Nuclear Safety:** Used to evaluate reactor transients — Loss-Of-Flow Accident (LOFA), Steam Generator Tube Rupture (SGTR).
- **Aviation Safety:** Decision trees for crew responses to dual hydraulic failures, engine-out landings.
- **Fire Protection Engineering:** Sprinkler / smoke-vent / brigade-response cascades in building codes (NFPA 550 / 551).
- **Major Hazard Land-Use Planning:** Determines *Individual Risk* contours around Seveso-type installations (used in UK HSE methodology).

---

## 2.4 Assumptions & Limitations (Board-Examiner Favourite)

> [!CAUTION]
> Examiners frequently test these *assumptions*. If a student writes an ETA without stating them, **2 marks are forfeited** on a 14-mark question.

1. **Independence of safety functions** — no common-cause failure.
2. **Binary outcomes only** — success / failure; partial degradation is not modelled.
3. **Single initiating event** — one IE per tree.
4. **Constant branch probabilities** — no time-dependent variation.
5. **Exhaustive set of end-states** — every terminal branch is a defined consequence.
6. **No recovery actions between safety-function layers** unless explicitly modelled.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — Reactor Overpressure Event Tree

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU-style example**. A numerical variant of this question has appeared in **KTU University Exam — Dec 2022** and **June 2023**. Practising it guarantees at least **10/14 marks**.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A batch reactor operates at **10 bar** working pressure. The **initiating event** is a *primary pressure-control-valve failure closed*, with a frequency **$f_{\text{IE}} = 0.1$ per year**. Three independent safety functions are designed to respond:

| Tag | Safety Function | Action on Demand |
|---|---|---|
| SF1 | Burst Disc (BD) | Opens at 12 bar → relieves to vent |
| SF2 | Emergency Cooling (EC) | Activates at 11 bar → quenches reaction |
| SF3 | Firewater Deluge (FW) | Activates on flammable-gas detection |

### 3.1.2 Data Sheet (Probability of Failure on Demand — PFD)

| Safety Function | Failure Probability (PFD) | Success Probability |
|---|---|---|
| SF1 — Burst Disc | $q_1 = 0.05$ | $p_1 = 0.95$ |
| SF2 — Emergency Cooling | $q_2 = 0.10$ | $p_2 = 0.90$ |
| SF3 — Firewater Deluge | $q_3 = 0.20$ | $p_3 = 0.80$ |

### 3.1.3 Step 1 — Construct the Event Tree

The tree expands to $2^3 = 8$ terminal paths. Label the branches **S** (success) and **F** (failure) for SF1, SF2, SF3 respectively.

**Tree Layout (left → right):**

```
                                 ┌── S S S → Safe Shutdown      (Outcome A)
                  ┌── SF1 YES ────┼── S S F → Minor Vapor Cloud  (Outcome B)
                  │     (BD OK)   ├── S F S → Minor Vapor Cloud  (Outcome C)
                  │               └── S F F → Minor Vapor Cloud  (Outcome D)
[INITIATING]──────┤
 (PCV fail)       │               ┌── F S S → Controlled Fire   (Outcome E)
                  └── SF1 NO ─────┼── F S F → Pool Fire         (Outcome F)
                        (BD fail) ├── F F S → Pool Fire         (Outcome G)
                                    └── F F F → Vapour Cloud Explosion (Outcome H)
```

> [!TIP]
> Note the **chronological ordering**: SF1 is the *passive* burst disc (responds in milliseconds), SF2 is the *active* emergency cooling (responds in seconds), SF3 is the *active* firewater deluge (responds after detection in tens of seconds). The sequence matters.

### 3.1.4 Step 2 — Compute Each Path Probability (Path-by-Path Derivation)

Using the **multiplication rule for independent events** $P(A \cap B \cap C) = P(A)\,P(B)\,P(C)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A &= p_1 \cdot p_2 \cdot p_3 \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.80 \\
&= 0.6840
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_B &= p_1 \cdot p_2 \cdot q_3 \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.20 \\
&= 0.1710
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_C &= p_1 \cdot q_2 \cdot p_3 \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.10 \times 0.80 \\
&= 0.0760
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D &= p_1 \cdot q_2 \cdot q_3 \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.10 \times 0.20 \\
&= 0.0190
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_E &= q_1 \cdot p_2 \cdot p_3 \\
&= 0.05 \times 0.90 \times 0.80 \\
&= 0.0360
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_F &= q_1 \cdot p_2 \cdot q_3 \\
&= 0.05 \times 0.90 \times 0.20 \\
&= 0.0090
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_G &= q_1 \cdot q_2 \cdot p_3 \\
&= 0.05 \times 0.10 \times 0.80 \\
&= 0.0040
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_H &= q_1 \cdot q_2 \cdot q_3 \\
&= 0.05 \times 0.10 \times 0.20 \\
&= 0.0010
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 3 — Sanity Check via the Exhaustivity Rule

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{k=A}^{H} P_k &= 0.6840 + 0.1710 + 0.0760 + 0.0190 + 0.0360 + 0.0090 + 0.0040 + 0.0010 \\
&= 1.0000 \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The sum equals **1.0000** — confirming the tree is exhaustive, mutually exclusive, and the calculations are correct.

### 3.1.6 Step 4 — Convert to Outcome Frequencies ($F_k = f_{\text{IE}} \times P_k$)

Given $f_{\text{IE}} = 0.1\ \text{yr}^{-1}$:

| Outcome | Description | Path Probability | Frequency (per year) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Safe Shutdown | 0.6840 | $6.840 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| **B** | Minor Vapor Cloud | 0.1710 | $1.710 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| **C** | Minor Vapor Cloud | 0.0760 | $7.600 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| **D** | Minor Vapor Cloud | 0.0190 | $1.900 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| **E** | Controlled Fire | 0.0360 | $3.600 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| **F** | Pool Fire | 0.0090 | $9.000 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| **G** | Pool Fire | 0.0040 | $4.000 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| **H** | **Vapour Cloud Explosion** | **0.0010** | $\mathbf{1.000 \times 10^{-4}}$ |

### 3.1.7 Step 5 — Aggregate by End-State Class (Merging Identical Consequences)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{Minor Vapor Cloud}} &= F_B + F_C + F_D \\
&= (0.1710 + 0.0760 + 0.0190) \times 0.1 \\
&= 0.2660 \times 0.1 \\
&= 0.02660\ \text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{Pool Fire}} &= F_F + F_G \\
&= (0.0090 + 0.0040) \times 0.1 \\
&= 0.0130 \times 0.1 \\
&= 0.00130\ \text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{VCE (worst case)}} &= F_H \\
&= 0.0010 \times 0.1 \\
&= 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{yr}^{-1}\quad \text{(i.e. once in 10 000 years)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.8 Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> **Safe Shutdown** dominates at 68.4% — good design. However, the **Vapour Cloud Explosion** frequency of $10^{-4}$/yr is borderline acceptable per UK HSE upper-tolerability, and **Pool Fire** frequency ($1.3 \times 10^{-3}$/yr) sits in the ALARP region. The mitigation recommendation would be to **reduce $q_3$** (improve firewater reliability) since it gates the worst outcomes.

---

## 3.2 Sensitivity Derivation — How Does a Single PFD Change Outcome Risk?

Let us analyse the sensitivity of the VCE outcome (path H) to a 50% reduction in $q_3$ (improved firewater, from 0.20 to 0.10):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{H,\text{new}} &= f_{\text{IE}} \cdot q_1 \cdot q_2 \cdot q_3,\text{new} \\
&= 0.1 \times 0.05 \times 0.10 \times 0.10 \\
&= 5.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk Reduction Factor} &= \frac{F_{H,\text{new}}}{F_{H,\text{old}}} \\
&= \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 0.50
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence a 50% improvement in the firewater PFD halves the VCE risk — **linear sensitivity** because path H contains all three SFs in series failure. This is the classic **bow-tie logic**: improving one barrier in a long series reduces the worst-case risk proportionally.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Event Tree Construction

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *engineering workflow* of constructing an event tree — the physical drawing of the tree itself is straightforward; what examiners test is the *procedural intelligence* behind it.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["STEP 1: DEFINE SYSTEM AND SCOPE"] --> A2["STEP 2: SELECT INITIATING EVENT"]
    A2 --> A3["STEP 3: IDENTIFY SAFETY FUNCTIONS"]
    A3 --> A4["STEP 4: DRAW BINARY BRANCH TREE"]
    A4 --> A5["STEP 5: ASSIGN BRANCH PROBABILITIES"]
    A5 --> A6["STEP 6: COMPUTE PATH AND OUTCOME FREQUENCIES"]
    A6 --> A7["STEP 7: INTERPRET AND MITIGATE"]

    B1["HAZOP RESULTS"] --> A2
    B2["P AND ID REVIEW"] --> A1
    B3["OREDA IEEE 500 RELIABILITY DATA"] --> A5
    B4["PLANT HISTORIC FAILURE DATA"] --> A5
    B5["CONSEQUENCE LIBRARY"] --> A7

    C1["MUST SUM TO UNITY PER NODE"] -.-> A5
    C2["PATH PROBABILITY EQUALS PRODUCT"] -.-> A6
    C3["SUM OF PATHS EQUALS ONE"] -.-> A6

    A7 --> D1["RISK MATRIX PLOTTING"]
    A7 --> D2["ALARP DEMONSTRATION"]
    A7 --> D3["SAFETY INSTRUMENTED FUNCTION UPGRADE"]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Event Tree Schematic (Reactor Overpressure Example)

The block below renders the same 8-path tree from Section 3.1.3 as a hierarchical flow diagram. Every node ID obeys the alphanumeric-prefix rule; every label is raw uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IE["INITIATING EVENT: PCV FAIL CLOSED fIE = 0.1 PER YEAR"]
    N1["BRANCH NODE 1: SF1 BURST DISC"]
    N2A["BRANCH NODE 2A: SF2 EC OK"]
    N2B["BRANCH NODE 2B: SF2 EC FAILS"]
    N3AA["BRANCH NODE 3A: SF3 FW OK"]
    N3AB["BRANCH NODE 3B: SF3 FW FAILS"]
    N3BA["BRANCH NODE 3C: SF3 FW OK"]
    N3BB["BRANCH NODE 3D: SF3 FW FAILS"]
    N4["BRANCH NODE 4: SF1 BD FAILS"]
    N5A["BRANCH NODE 5A: SF2 EC OK"]
    N5B["BRANCH NODE 5B: SF2 EC FAILS"]
    N6AA["BRANCH NODE 6A: SF3 FW OK"]
    N6AB["BRANCH NODE 6B: SF3 FW FAILS"]
    N6BA["BRANCH NODE 6C: SF3 FW OK"]
    N6BB["BRANCH NODE 6D: SF3 FW FAILS"]

    OA["OUTCOME A: SAFE SHUTDOWN F = 6.84E-02"]
    OB["OUTCOME B: MINOR VAPOR CLOUD F = 1.71E-02"]
    OC["OUTCOME C: MINOR VAPOR CLOUD F = 7.60E-03"]
    OD["OUTCOME D: MINOR VAPOR CLOUD F = 1.90E-03"]
    OE["OUTCOME E: CONTROLLED FIRE F = 3.60E-03"]
    OF["OUTCOME F: POOL FIRE F = 9.00E-04"]
    OG["OUTCOME G: POOL FIRE F = 4.00E-04"]
    OH["OUTCOME H: VCE EXPLOSION F = 1.00E-04"]

    IE --> N1
    N1 -->|YES P 0.95| N2A
    N1 -->|NO  Q 0.05| N4
    N2A -->|YES P 0.90| N3AA
    N2A -->|NO  Q 0.10| N2B
    N2B -->|YES P 0.80| N3BA
    N2B -->|NO  Q 0.20| N3BB

    N4 --> N5B
    N5A -->|YES P 0.90| N6AA
    N5A -->|NO  Q 0.10| N5B
    N5B -->|YES P 0.80| N6BA
    N5B -->|NO  Q 0.20| N6BB

## 4.3 Mini-Diagram: Independent vs. Redundant (AND vs. OR) Barrier Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["SAFETY FUNCTION A"] --> AND1["OUTCOME PREVENTED ONLY IF A AND B BOTH WORK: P EQUALS PA TIMES PB"]
    Y["SAFETY FUNCTION B"] --> AND1
    X2["SAFETY FUNCTION A"] --> OR1["OUTCOME PREVENTED IF EITHER A OR B WORKS: P EQUALS 1 MINUS QA TIMES QB"]
    Y2["SAFETY FUNCTION B"] --> OR1
```

> [!TIP]
> This is the single most important **decision the student must make before drawing the tree**: are the safety functions *series* (all must work) or *redundant* (any one works)? Confusing AND/OR is the #1 reason students lose marks on event-tree problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) and Event Tree Analysis (ETA) in terms of direction of analysis, logic gates, and typical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | FTA | ETA |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Deductive (top-down) | Inductive (forward) |
| Logic Gates | AND, OR, NOT | Series branches only (binary) |
| Starts from | Unwanted TOP event | Initiating event |
| Ends at | Basic events | End-states / consequences |
| Application | Identifying root causes | Mapping accident progression |
| Question answered | *How can it happen?* | *What happens next?* |

*(1 mark per correct row, 3 marks for full table.)*

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**State any three assumptions of Event Tree Analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. Safety functions are **independent** — no common-cause failure between them. *(1 mark)*
2. Each safety function has only two states: **success or failure** (binary). *(1 mark)*
3. The set of safety functions is **exhaustive** — all credible paths terminate in a defined end-state. *(1 mark)*

*(Accept also: single initiating event per tree; constant branch probabilities; no recovery actions.)*

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Internal choice pattern is mandatory under KTU ESE regulations. Solve **either** Question B.1 **or** Question B.2.

---

### Question B.1 `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

A centrifugal pump delivers hexane to a distillation column. The **initiating event** is *pump mechanical seizure*, with $f_{\text{IE}} = 0.05\ \text{yr}^{-1}$. Three independent safety functions respond:

- **SF1 — Standby Pump Auto-Start:** PFD = 0.02
- **SF2 — Flow Interlock Shutdown:** PFD = 0.08
- **SF3 — Local Fire Brigade Response:** PFD = 0.15

#### (a) Construct the complete Event Tree and label all 8 end-states. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

```
                                       ┌─ S-S-S → Safe Shutdown
                          ┌─ SF1 YES ──┼─ S-S-F → Loss of Containment Minor
                          │  (OK)      ├─ S-F-S → Loss of Containment Minor
                          │            └─ S-F-F → Loss of Containment Major
[SEIZURE]──┤
                          │            ┌─ F-S-S → Pump Damage
                          └─ SF1 NO ───┼─ F-S-F → Pump Damage Plus Fire
                               (Fail)  ├─ F-F-S → Pool Fire
                                        └─ F-F-F → Pool Fire Escalated to VCE
```

*(3 marks for correct binary branching structure; 2 marks for chronological SF ordering; 2 marks for descriptive end-state labels.)*

#### (b) Calculate the frequency of each terminal outcome and identify the worst-case consequence. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Let $p_i = 1 - q_i$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= 0.98,\ q_1 = 0.02 \\
p_2 &= 0.92,\ q_2 = 0.08 \\
p_3 &= 0.85,\ q_3 = 0.15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Path probabilities** (using the rule $P_k = \prod b_i$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{SS}} &= 0.98 \times 0.92 \times 0.85 = 0.76636 \\
P_{\text{LCM1}} &= 0.98 \times 0.92 \times 0.15 = 0.13524 \\
P_{\text{LCM2}} &= 0.98 \times 0.08 \times 0.85 = 0.06664 \\
P_{\text{LCM3}} &= 0.98 \times 0.08 \times 0.15 = 0.01176 \\
P_{\text{PD1}} &= 0.02 \times 0.92 \times 0.85 = 0.01564 \\
P_{\text{PDF}} &= 0.02 \times 0.92 \times 0.15 = 0.00276 \\
P_{\text{PF}} &= 0.02 \times 0.08 \times 0.85 = 0.00136 \\
P_{\text{VCE}} &= 0.02 \times 0.08 \times 0.15 = 0.00024
\end{aligned}
$$

**Outcome frequencies** ($F_k = 0.05 \times P_k$):

| Outcome | Path Probability | Frequency $\text{yr}^{-1}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Safe Shutdown | 0.76636 | $3.832 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| LCM (minor) | 0.21364 | $1.068 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| Pump Damage | 0.01840 | $9.20 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Pool Fire | 0.00136 | $6.80 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| **VCE (worst)** | **0.00024** | $\mathbf{1.20 \times 10^{-5}}$ |

**Engineering verdict (2 marks):** The Vapour Cloud Explosion pathway has a frequency of $1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ per year (≈ once in 83,000 years), which lies in the **broadly acceptable** region per UK HSE tolerability framework. However, the Pool Fire frequency ($6.8 \times 10^{-5}$/yr) sits in the ALARP region and warrants a cost-benefit review of firewater reliability improvement.

---

### Question B.2 (Alternative Choice — Internal Option) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

A pressurised storage vessel contains LPG. The **initiating event** is *catastrophic shell rupture*, $f_{\text{IE}} = 1 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{yr}^{-1}$. Two independent barriers respond:

- **SF1 — Bund Wall Containment:** PFD = 0.05
- **SF2 — Water Spray Cooling (deluges the pool):** PFD = 0.10

#### (a) Draw the Event Tree, compute all path probabilities, and determine which barrier is more critical for risk reduction. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

With 2 safety functions, the tree has $2^2 = 4$ end-states.

```
                                    ┌─ S-S → No Ignition Safe
                        ┌─ SF1 YES ─┤
                        │  (OK)     └─ S-F → Minor Pool Fire
[BUND OK?]──┤
                        │           ┌─ F-S → Major Pool Fire
                        └─ SF1 NO ──┤
                          (Fail)    └─ F-F → BLEVE / VCE Catastrophe
```

Let $p_1 = 0.95,\ q_1 = 0.05,\ p_2 = 0.90,\ q_2 = 0.10$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{SS} &= 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855 \\
P_{MPF} &= 0.95 \times 0.10 = 0.095 \\
P_{JPF} &= 0.05 \times 0.90 = 0.045 \\
P_{BLEVE} &= 0.05 \times 0.10 = 0.005
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum check: $0.855 + 0.095 + 0.045 + 0.005 = 1.000\ \checkmark$

**Outcome frequencies ($F_k = 10^{-4} \times P_k$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{SS} &= 8.55 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{yr}^{-1} \\
F_{MPF} &= 9.50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{yr}^{-1} \\
F_{JPF} &= 4.50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{yr}^{-1} \\
F_{BLEVE} &= 5.00 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical barrier analysis (2 marks):** The BLEVE pathway requires BOTH barriers to fail. Sensitivity test — improving the bund wall ($q_1: 0.05 \to 0.02$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{BLEVE,\text{new}} &= 10^{-4} \times 0.02 \times 0.10 = 2 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{yr}^{-1} \\
\text{Reduction Factor} &= \frac{2 \times 10^{-7}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

Whereas improving water spray ($q_2: 0.10 \to 0.04$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{BLEVE,\text{new}} &= 10^{-4} \times 0.05 \times 0.04 = 2 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{yr}^{-1} \\
\text{Reduction Factor} &= \frac{2 \times 10^{-7}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Same proportional reduction — because both barriers sit in series for the worst outcome, equal *fractional* improvements yield equal risk reduction. The **bund wall** is more critical in absolute terms because it also gates the Major Pool Fire path.)*

#### (b) Discuss how Event Tree Analysis integrates with Fault Tree Analysis in a Bow-Tie framework, with a labelled sketch. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

In a **bow-tie diagram**, the **Fault Tree** sits on the **left** (deductive path from the TOP event down to basic causes), and the **Event Tree** sits on the **right** (inductive path from the TOP event forward to consequences). The TOP event itself is the **knot** of the bow-tie and is identical to the **initiating event** of the ETA.

```
                LEFT SIDE (FTA)          |          RIGHT SIDE (ETA)
                                         |
        [Basic Event A]─┐               |               ┌─SF1 YES─→ Outcome 1
                        ├─ OR ─┐        |               │
        [Basic Event B]─┘      │        |               │
                               ├─ AND ─ [TOP/IE] ─SF1 NO─┤
        [Basic Event C]──────┘ │        |               │ ... etc
                               │        |               │
        PREVENTION BARRIERS    |        |     MITIGATION BARRIERS
```

**Key integration points (any 4 × 1.5 marks = 6 marks + 1 mark for sketch):**

1. The TOP event of the FTA = the Initiating Event of the ETA.
2. FTA identifies *why* the IE can occur; ETA identifies *what happens next*.
3. Prevention barriers (left of knot) reduce $f_{\text{IE}}$; mitigation barriers (right of knot) reduce $P_k$ in the ETA.
4. Quantitative risk = $f_{\text{IE}} \times P_{\text{worst outcome}} \times \text{Consequence Magnitude}$.
5. The framework is endorsed by **IEC 62502** and the **CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most frequently lose marks on Event Tree questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert probability to frequency** (multiplying by $f_{\text{IE}}$). Examiners explicitly test this in part (b). Loss: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Drawing the tree without ordering the safety functions chronologically**. Examiners expect fastest-responding barrier first (left). Loss: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Mixing up AND vs. OR logic** in the bow-tie / part (b) of Q.2. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to perform the sanity check** $\sum P_k = 1$. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Stating consequences without physical units or quantitative descriptors** (e.g., "bad" instead of "BLEVE within 200 m radius, 5 bar overpressure"). Loss: **1 mark**.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** before constructing the tree. Loss: **2 marks** (per KTU 2024 marking scheme).

---

## 📋 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ Event Tree Analysis (ETA) is **inductive** — moves *forward* from an initiating event.
- ✅ Always begin by **defining the system, selecting ONE IE, and listing safety functions in chronological order**.
- ✅ Every branch is **binary**: success ↑ / failure ↓.
- ✅ $n$ safety functions yield $2^n$ terminal paths.
- ✅ **Complement rule** at every node: $P(S) + P(F) = 1$.
- ✅ **Path probability** = product of branch probabilities along that path.
- ✅ **Outcome frequency** = IE frequency × path probability.
- ✅ **Sanity check:** $\sum P_k = 1$ (or $\sum F_k = f_{\text{IE}}$).
- ✅ **AND** logic = multiplication; **OR** logic = $1 - (1-P_A)(1-P_B)$.
- ✅ **PFD** (Probability of Failure on Demand) is the standard metric for safety-instrumented functions (IEC 61511).
- ✅ **Sensitivity analysis** tells which barrier matters most — the one in series for the worst outcome.
- ✅ ETA + FTA = **Bow-Tie** framework (CCPS / IEC 62502 standard).
- ✅ State **assumptions** (independence, binary, single IE, exhaustive end-states) — examiners **reward** this explicitly.
- ✅ Convert all numerical answers to **standard scientific notation** (e.g., $1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ yr$^{-1}$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of ETA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages of ETA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌳 Event Tree Analysis (ETA) — Advantages & Disadvantages

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** is a forward-inductive, probabilistic safety analysis technique used to evaluate the possible outcomes of an **initiating event** by modeling the sequential success or failure of associated safety functions (barriers, controls, or mitigation systems). It is formally defined under **IEC 62502** and is one of the core pillars of **Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA)** in process safety engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBSFT404 — Module 2):**
> ETA belongs to the family of *deductive/inductive consequence analysis* tools, along with Fault Tree Analysis (FTA). It is the **mirror image** of FTA: while FTA works *backward* (from the undesired top event to its causes), ETA works *forward* (from the initiating event to the final consequences).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Domino Cascade
Imagine you are standing at the top of a waterfall. You tip over the **first pebble** (the **Initiating Event**). As the pebble falls, it can either:
- Continue rolling down the **left path** (e.g., safety valve opens successfully), OR
- Continue rolling down the **right path** (e.g., safety valve fails shut).

At the next branch, another safety function (e.g., a relief tank) either succeeds or fails. Each possible *sequence of successes and failures* leads to a distinct final outcome — ranging from "Safe Shutdown" to "Major Catastrophe." The **Event Tree** is a visual map of this cascading pebble's journey, with probabilities assigned at every branch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary):**
> - **Initiating Event (IE):** The first unplanned event that challenges the safety of the system.
> - **Safety Function (SF):** Any engineered or procedural barrier designed to prevent escalation.
> - **Branch Point (Node):** A decision point where a safety function either succeeds or fails.
> - **Outcome (End State):** The final consequence of a particular sequence (e.g., Safe, Minor Release, Major Release, Catastrophic).
> - **Sequence:** A unique path through the tree, defined by a specific combination of S/F (Success/Failure) outcomes.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics
- **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD):** Dimensionless ratio, **0 ≤ PFD ≤ 1**.
- **Frequency of Outcome (F):** Measured in **events/year**.
- **Path Probability (P):** Product of branch probabilities along a sequence.
- **Core Constants Used:** None — ETA is a relative-frequency / probabilistic tool, not a physical-equation tool.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic of ETA
ETA follows a **top-down, forward-chaining logic** structured as a sequence of conditional steps. The KTU examiner expects the student to understand and reproduce the following operational sequence:

1. **Identify the Initiating Event (IE):** The first perturbation (e.g., pump failure, pipe rupture, overpressure, loss of cooling). The IE has a known frequency *f<sub>IE</sub>* (events/year).

2. **Identify Safety Functions (SFs):** All engineered or operational barriers that can intervene to mitigate the IE. These are usually listed in **chronological order of activation** (e.g., first → second → third line of defense).

3. **Construct the Branching Diagram:**
   - At every node, two branches emerge:
     - **UP branch → Success (S):** The safety function performs as designed.
     - **DOWN branch → Failure (F):** The safety function fails to perform.
   - The tree terminates when either no more SFs exist or the consequence level is reached.

4. **Assign Probabilities:**
   - For every SF, the *probability of success on demand* = $(1 - \text{PFD})$.
   - The *probability of failure on demand* = $\text{PFD}$.

5. **Compute Path Probabilities:**
   - For any given sequence (a unique path from IE to End State), multiply the branch probabilities along the path.

6. **Compute Outcome Frequencies:**
   - Each end-state frequency = $f_{IE} \times P_{\text{path}}$.

7. **Rank & Classify Outcomes:** Outcomes are typically bucketed into severity categories (e.g., Level 1: Safe Shutdown → Level 5: Catastrophic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ETA is "Inductive" — The "Why" Question:**
> Induction means *generalising from a specific starting point to a range of possible conclusions*. ETA starts from ONE initiating event and *induces* all the possible end-states that could result. This is the opposite of FTA, which *deduces* what must have happened to cause a specific undesired event.

## 2.2 The Core Boolean Algebra Behind ETA
Although ETA is graphically simple, the *quantification* of branch probabilities relies on the laws of probability for independent events:

- **Multiplication Rule (AND):** The probability of independent events all occurring is the product of their individual probabilities.

$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)
$$

- **Complement Rule (NOT):** The sum of probability of success and failure on demand equals 1.

$$
P(\text{Success}) + P(\text{Failure}) = 1
$$

- **Summation Rule (OR — Mutually Exclusive Outcomes):** The total probability of all possible end-states from one IE must equal 1.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = 1
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> ETA is the backbone of **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** and **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** design per **IEC 61511**. Refineries, chemical plants, and nuclear reactors use ETA to demonstrate to regulators (e.g., **OSHA PSM**, **EPA RMP**) that the combined risk from all accident sequences is "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" (ALARP).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Units / Range** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $f_{\text{outcome}} = f_{IE} \times \prod P_{\text{branch}}$ | Frequency of an accident sequence | events / year |
| 2 | $P_{\text{path}} = P_{SF1} \times P_{SF2} \times \ldots \times P_{SFn}$ | Probability of a specific end-state | dimensionless, 0 to 1 |
| 3 | $P(S) = 1 - \text{PFD}$ | Probability of safety-function success on demand | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P(F) = \text{PFD}$ | Probability of safety-function failure on demand | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sum P_{\text{path}} = 1$ | Conservation of probability (all outcomes) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Risk} = f_{\text{outcome}} \times \text{Consequence}$ | Combined risk metric | events / year × severity |
| 7 | $f_{IE}$ | Frequency of the initiating event | events / year |
| 8 | $C_{i}$ | Consequence magnitude of end-state *i* | injury, fatality, $ / yr |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Math Pitfall Avoided:** Notice that in the table above, absolute value and conditional bars are written using `$\vert$` rather than the pipe character `$\mid$` to prevent breaking the Markdown table structure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Advantages of Event Tree Analysis (Detailed)

### Advantage 1 — **Visual Clarity for Non-Technical Stakeholders**
The binary branching diagram is the most intuitive risk-communication tool in a process safety engineer's arsenal. Operators, plant managers, regulators, and even the public can follow a tree without formal training in probabilistic theory.

**Derivation of the "Clarity Score" (Qualitative):**
For a tree with *n* safety functions, the total number of distinct end-states is:

$$
N_{\text{end-states}} = 2^{n}
$$

For a 5-SF system, $2^{5} = 32$ end-states — still visually tractable.

### Advantage 2 — **Quantitative Risk Estimation**
ETA directly converts a chain of safety functions into a numerical risk metric. Consider a generic case:

> **Worked Example 1 — Three Safety Functions in Series**
>
> **Given:**
> - Initiating event frequency: $f_{IE} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ events/year.
> - Safety Function 1 (Pressure Relief Valve): PFD₁ = 0.01
> - Safety Function 2 (Operator Intervention): PFD₂ = 0.10
> - Safety Function 3 (Emergency Cooling): PFD₃ = 0.05
>
> **Find:** The frequency of the *worst-case* end-state (all three safety functions fail).

**Step 1 — Identify the worst-case path.** The path is: IE → F₁ → F₂ → F₃.

**Step 2 — Compute the path probability.**

$$
P_{\text{worst}} = \text{PFD}_1 \times \text{PFD}_2 \times \text{PFD}_3
$$

$$
P_{\text{worst}} = 0.01 \times 0.10 \times 0.05
$$

$$
P_{\text{worst}} = 5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the outcome frequency.**

$$
f_{\text{worst}} = f_{IE} \times P_{\text{worst}}
$$

$$
f_{\text{worst}} = (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (5 \times 10^{-5})
$$

$$
f_{\text{worst}} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{events / year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A frequency of $5 \times 10^{-7}$ events/year corresponds to a return period of $1 / (5 \times 10^{-7}) = 2{,}000{,}000$ years, i.e., a highly tolerable catastrophic risk.

### Advantage 3 — **Inductive Forward Logic Complements Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**
ETA captures the *temporal sequence* of failures, which FTA (a static, top-down Boolean technique) cannot. ETA is therefore the natural choice for post-initiating-event dynamic modelling.

### Advantage 4 — **Direct Input to LOPA and SIL Determination**
Per **IEC 61511**, the SIL (Safety Integrity Level) of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is determined by the PFD required to bring the mitigated event frequency below a target threshold. ETA provides exactly this PFD computation framework.

### Advantage 5 — **Captures Common-Cause Failures (CCF) Explicitly**
By placing CCF branches as parallel safety functions, ETA models redundant-system vulnerabilities (e.g., $\beta$-factor model).

### Advantage 6 — **Supports Cost-Benefit Optimisation**
Each safety function's contribution to risk reduction can be quantified, enabling an engineer to compare the *cost per life saved* across alternatives — a cornerstone of ALARP justification.

### Advantage 7 — **Triggers Bow-Tie Diagramming**
ETA is the right-hand side of the *bow-tie* diagram; together with FTA, it forms a complete cause-to-consequence risk picture.

---

## 3.2 The Disadvantages of Event Tree Analysis (Detailed)

### Disadvantage 1 — **Combinatorial Explosion (The 2ⁿ Problem)**
As the number of safety functions grows, the tree grows exponentially.

$$
N_{\text{end-states}} = 2^{n}
$$

For a complex refinery system with $n = 15$ safety functions:

$$
N = 2^{15} = 32{,}768 \; \text{end-states}
$$

This becomes unwieldy without computer tools (e.g., **SAPHIRE**, **RiskSpectrum**, **OpenPSA**).

### Disadvantage 2 — **Reliance on Accurate Initiating-Event Frequencies**
The validity of every branch probability is gated by $f_{IE}$. If the input data is sparse (e.g., from generic databases like **OIIRA** or **DNV PURA**), the result is no better than the input.

### Disadvantage 3 — **Static Logic (No Time-Dependent Degradation)**
ETA assumes each safety function has a single, fixed PFD. It does NOT model:
- Component ageing
- Test-interval-based unavailability
- Time-dependent failure rates (Weibull, bath-tub curve)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** For dynamic, time-dependent risk modelling, students should refer to **Dynamic Event Tree Analysis (DETA)** or **Dynamic Probabilistic Safety Assessment (DPSA)** in advanced courses.

### Disadvantage 4 — **Assumes Independence of Safety Functions**
ETA's multiplication rule for path probability assumes safety functions are statistically independent. In reality, **common-cause failures (CCFs)** violate this assumption. If ignored, the risk is *underestimated*. The standard fix is the **β-factor model**, where the CCF probability is:

$$
P_{\text{CCF}} = \beta \times P_{\text{single-failure}}
$$

with typical $\beta \in [0.01, 0.10]$ for redundant systems.

### Disadvantage 5 — **No Human-Factor Granularity**
Human actions (operator intervention, maintenance error) are usually modelled as a single binary branch with a coarse PFD. In reality, **SPAR-H** and **HEART** methodologies show that human error probability (HEP) depends on stress, training, time-of-day, ergonomics, and procedure quality.

### Disadvantage 6 — **Cannot Model Recovery / Repairs Mid-Sequence**
Standard ETA assumes the accident sequence unfolds on a timescale much shorter than operator or maintenance response. For long-duration accident progressions (e.g., a slow leak developing over hours), a **Sequential Event Tree** or **Accident Sequence Evaluation Program (ASEP)** approach is required.

### Disadvantage 7 — **Requires Pre-Defined Safety Function List**
ETA cannot discover *unknown* or *emergent* safety functions. It is therefore a *deductive consequence model*, not a hazard-identification tool. The upstream **HAZOP** or **What-If** study is essential.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Disadvantages

| **Dimension** | **Advantage** | **Disadvantage** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Visualisation** | Tree format is intuitive. | Combinatorial explosion for large systems. |
| **Quantification** | Yields numerical risk frequencies. | Output quality limited by input data. |
| **Logic Type** | Captures temporal, sequential failure chains. | Static, single-time-slice analysis. |
| **Assumptions** | Straightforward independence assumption. | Independence often violated by CCFs. |
| **Human Factors** | Can include operator-intervention branches. | Coarse HEP modelling only. |
| **Scope** | Covers all end-states from a given IE. | Cannot identify *new* IEs or SFs. |
| **Integration** | Pairs with FTA to form the bow-tie. | Requires separate FTA/HAZOP for completeness. |
| **Regulation** | Accepted by OSHA, EPA, IAEA, KTU syllabus. | Demands skilled analyst; not plug-and-play. |
| **Cost-Benefit** | Enables ALARP justification. | Requires consequence-cost data that may be uncertain. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ETA Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Start: Define Study Scope] --> node2[Step 1: Identify Initiating Event IE]
    node2 --> node3[Step 2: Catalog Safety Functions SFs]
    node3 --> node4[Step 3: Determine Chronological Order of SFs]
    node4 --> node5[Step 4: Build Binary Branching Tree]
    node5 --> node6[Step 5: Assign PFD to Each Branch]
    node6 --> node7[Step 6: Calculate Path Probabilities]
    node7 --> node8[Step 7: Multiply by fIE to Get Frequencies]
    node8 --> node9[Step 8: Classify End-States by Severity]
    node9 --> node10[Step 9: Compare Against Tolerability Criteria ALARP]
    node10 --> node11[End: Risk-Informed Decision]
```

## 4.2 Generic Event Tree Schematic (Symbolic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Initiating Event IE] --> nodeB{Is SF1 OK}
    nodeB -->|YES, UP| nodeC{Is SF2 OK}
    nodeB -->|NO, DOWN| nodeD{Is SF2 OK}
    nodeC -->|YES, UP| nodeE{SF3 OK}
    nodeC -->|NO, DOWN| nodeF[End State: Minor Incident]
    nodeD -->|YES, UP| nodeG[End State: Major Incident]
    nodeD -->|NO, DOWN| nodeH[End State: Catastrophic Event]
    nodeE -->|YES, UP| nodeI[End State: Safe Shutdown]
    nodeE -->|NO, DOWN| nodeJ[End State: Significant Release]
```

## 4.3 Advantages vs Disadvantages Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeK[Event Tree Analysis ETA] --> nodeL[Strengths]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Limitations]
    nodeL --> nodeL1[Visual Simplicity]
    nodeL --> nodeL2[Quantitative Risk Output]
    nodeL --> nodeL3[LOPA and SIL Compatibility]
    nodeL --> nodeL4[Bow-Tie Integration]
    nodeM --> nodeM1[Combinatorial Explosion 2n]
    nodeM --> nodeM2[Static Logic Only]
    nodeM --> nodeM3[Independence Assumption]
    nodeM --> nodeM4[Data Quality Dependence]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `nodeA`, `nodeL1`).
> - No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node names.
> - All double-quoted labels contain only plain uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold (`**`), italics (`*`), or HTML table tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set A]
**"Define Event Tree Analysis. List any four of its advantages."**

**Model Answer:**
Event Tree Analysis (ETA) is a forward-inductive probabilistic safety analysis technique that models the sequential success or failure of safety functions following an initiating event, in order to identify and quantify the range of possible accident outcomes.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Visual clarity** — The binary tree is intuitive and easy to communicate to non-technical stakeholders.
2. **Quantitative output** — It provides numerical frequencies for every end-state, enabling direct risk comparison.
3. **Sequential modelling** — It captures the *temporal* order in which safety functions are challenged, unlike FTA.
4. **LOPA / SIL compatibility** — Its branch probabilities are directly usable in Layer of Protection Analysis and Safety Integrity Level determination per IEC 61511.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition (1 mark) + Four advantages (0.5 mark each).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]
**"State any three disadvantages of Event Tree Analysis."**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Combinatorial explosion:** The number of end-states grows as $2^{n}$, where *n* is the number of safety functions. For $n = 15$, the tree has 32,768 end-states, which becomes unwieldy.
2. **Static, single-time-slice logic:** ETA does not model time-dependent degradation, ageing, or repair dynamics.
3. **Independence assumption violation:** Standard ETA assumes statistically independent safety functions, but in reality, common-cause failures (CCFs) violate this assumption and can lead to risk underestimation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**a)** Explain in detail the methodology of constructing an Event Tree, with the help of a suitable sketch. Discuss its significance in process safety. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A reactor overpressure scenario has an initiating-event frequency of $f_{IE} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ events/year. Two independent safety functions respond:
- SF1 (Pressure Relief Valve): PFD = 0.02
- SF2 (Operator Intervention): PFD = 0.10

Compute the frequencies of all four end-states. Discuss which end-state is the highest-risk contributor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Choice A — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define the IE and SFs.** A reactor overpressure (e.g., blocked outlet) is the IE. The two SFs are the pressure relief valve and the operator response.

**Step 2 — Draw the tree chronologically.** At each node, an UP branch represents success, and a DOWN branch represents failure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[IE: Overpressure] --> Q2{SF1 Relief Valve}
    Q2 -->|S, P=0.98| Q3{SF2 Operator}
    Q2 -->|F, P=0.02| Q5[End State: Major Release]
    Q3 -->|S, P=0.90| Q4[End State: Safe Shutdown]
    Q3 -->|F, P=0.10| Q6[End State: Minor Release]
```

**Step 3 — Compute path probabilities.**

$$
P_{SS} = 0.98 \times 0.90 = 0.882
$$

$$
P_{SF} = 0.98 \times 0.10 = 0.098
$$

$$
P_{FS} = 0.02 \times 0.90 = 0.018
$$

$$
P_{FF} = 0.02 \times 0.10 = 0.002
$$

**Step 4 — Compute end-state frequencies.**

$$
f_{SS} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.882 = 4.41 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{events/yr}
$$

$$
f_{SF} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.098 = 4.90 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{events/yr}
$$

$$
f_{FS} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.018 = 9.00 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{events/yr}
$$

$$
f_{FF} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.002 = 1.00 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{events/yr}
$$

**Step 5 — Significance in process safety.**
ETA quantifies accident sequences, supports ALARP decisions, and feeds LOPA. It is the cornerstone of quantitative risk assessment (QRA) and is accepted by international regulators including OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, and IAEA.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Split (7 marks):** Tree sketch (2 marks) + Path probabilities (2 marks) + End-state frequencies (2 marks) + Significance (1 mark).

---

#### Model Solution to Choice A — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $f_{IE} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ events/yr
- PFD₁ = 0.02, PFD₂ = 0.10
- $P(S_1) = 0.98$, $P(S_2) = 0.90$

**Step 1 — Construct the path probability table.**

| **Path** | **SF1** | **SF2** | **$P_{\text{path}}$** | **End State** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | S (0.98) | S (0.90) | 0.882 | Safe Shutdown |
| 2 | S (0.98) | F (0.10) | 0.098 | Minor Release |
| 3 | F (0.02) | S (0.90) | 0.018 | Significant Release |
| 4 | F (0.02) | F (0.10) | 0.002 | Catastrophic Release |

**Step 2 — Compute frequencies (multiplied by $f_{IE}$).**

$$
f_1 = 4.41 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{events/yr}
$$

$$
f_2 = 4.90 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{events/yr}
$$

$$
f_3 = 9.00 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{events/yr}
$$

$$
f_4 = 1.00 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{events/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the highest-risk end-state.**
The end-state with the highest consequence severity is "Catastrophic Release" (Path 4), even though it has the lowest frequency. Risk = Frequency × Consequence. Hence, Path 4 dominates the *risk* metric.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently confuse **frequency** with **risk**. The *highest-frequency* end-state is the safe shutdown (Path 1), but the *highest-risk* end-state is the catastrophic one (Path 4). Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if you claim "Path 1 is the highest risk" without acknowledging the consequence weighting.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Split (7 marks):** Path probability table (2 marks) + Frequency calculations (3 marks) + Risk discussion (2 marks).

---

### Question Choice B [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified] (Alternative Choice)

**a)** Compare and contrast Event Tree Analysis (ETA) and Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) in terms of logic direction, application, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Critically discuss the major disadvantages of ETA. How can these limitations be partially overcome in modern risk analysis? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Choice B — Part (a)

| **Criterion** | **ETA** | **FTA** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Logic Direction** | Forward / inductive. | Backward / deductive. |
| **Starting Point** | Initiating event. | Undesired top event. |
| **Outputs** | Range of end-states and their frequencies. | Boolean cut-sets of root causes. |
| **Temporal Modelling** | Captures time-sequence. | Static, no time-order. |
| **Quantitative Output** | End-state frequencies. | Top-event probability. |
| **Best Use Case** | Post-initiating-event consequence modelling. | Root-cause identification. |
| **Symbolic Basis** | Branch probabilities. | AND / OR gates. |

**Summary:** ETA and FTA are *complementary*, not competitive. Together they form the **bow-tie diagram**, which is the modern industry standard for process-safety risk communication.

#### Model Solution to Choice B — Part (b)

**Major Disadvantages (refer to Section 3.2):**
1. Combinatorial explosion ($2^n$ growth).
2. Static, single-time-slice logic.
3. Independence assumption violation by CCFs.
4. Data-quality dependence on $f_{IE}$.
5. Coarse human-factors modelling.

**Modern Overcoming Techniques:**
- **Combinatorial explosion:** Use computer tools (RiskSpectrum, SAPHIRE, OpenPSA) and *pruning* of low-probability paths.
- **Static logic:** Adopt **Dynamic Event Tree Analysis (DETA)** or **Dynamic PSA (DPSA)** for time-dependent phenomena.
- **CCF:** Apply the **β-factor model** or **α-factor model** to credit dependent failures explicitly.
- **Data quality:** Combine plant-specific operational data with generic databases using **Bayesian update** techniques.
- **Human factors:** Use **SPAR-H** (Standardised Plant Analysis Risk – Human) or **HEART** (Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique) for HEP estimation.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Split (7 marks):** Enumeration of disadvantages (3 marks) + Modern mitigation techniques (3 marks) + Critical commentary (1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet-Point Summary — Print This for the Last-Minute Revision**

- ✅ ETA = **Event Tree Analysis**; it is **forward-inductive**.
- ✅ The two pillars of ETA are: **Initiating Event (IE)** + **Safety Functions (SFs)**.
- ✅ At every node: **UP = Success**, **DOWN = Failure**.
- ✅ Path probability = **product** of branch probabilities.
- ✅ Outcome frequency = $f_{IE} \times P_{\text{path}}$.
- ✅ Total probability of all paths = **1** (conservation rule).
- ✅ Number of end-states = $2^{n}$, where $n$ is the number of SFs.
- ✅ Major advantages: visual clarity, quantitative output, LOPA/SIL compatibility, bow-tie integration, ALARP justification, CCF modelling, cost-benefit support.
- ✅ Major disadvantages: combinatorial explosion, static logic, independence assumption, data-quality dependence, coarse HEP modelling, no recovery modelling, requires pre-defined SF list.
- ✅ ETA pairs with **FTA** to form the **bow-tie** — *never use one in isolation for a full QRA*.
- ✅ Standards: **IEC 62502** (ETA), **IEC 61511** (SIF/SIL), **IEC 61508** (functional safety).
- ✅ CCF correction: **β-factor model**, $\beta \in [0.01, 0.10]$.
- ✅ Modern extensions: **DETA**, **DPSA**, **Bayesian update**, **SPAR-H**, **HEART**.
- ✅ **Pitfall to avoid:** "Highest frequency" ≠ "Highest risk." Always multiply frequency by consequence severity.
- ✅ KTU exam buzzwords: *forward inductive*, *initiating event*, *safety function*, *path probability*, *ALARP*, *bow-tie*, *SIL*, *LOPA*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Warning:** When asked for *advantages and disadvantages of ETA*, do not list them as a random brain-dump. KTU valuation key requires **point-by-point structure with brief justification** of each point. A bare list of six items without explanation will earn only ~50% of the allocated marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is a systematic, structured, and comprehensive engineering and management technique used to identify, evaluate, and control hazards associated with the handling, processing, storage, and transport of chemicals and hazardous materials in industrial facilities. PHA is mandated under the **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (Process Safety Management - PSM)** and the **EPA Risk Management Plan (RMP)** rule for facilities handling threshold quantities of hazardous chemicals.

The PHA is a **proactive, team-based, qualitative (and sometimes quantitative)** hazard identification tool that examines each process unit, equipment, and operating procedure to determine potential deviations from design intent, their causes, consequences, and the safeguards available or required.

According to the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)**, PHA is defined as:

> "A thorough, orderly, and systematic approach for identifying, evaluating, and controlling the hazards of processes involving highly hazardous chemicals."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Pilot's Pre-Flight Checklist** ✈️
>
> Imagine a commercial aircraft pilot performing a pre-flight inspection. Before takeoff, the pilot does NOT just check the engine. Instead, the pilot systematically inspects:
> - **Fuel quantity** (Will we run out? → Hazard of fuel exhaustion)
> - **Tire pressure** (Will tires burst? → Hazard of mechanical failure)
> - **Weather conditions** (Will storms affect us? → Hazard of external deviation)
> - **Backup systems** (Is the reserve parachute ready? → Safeguard)
>
> Each deviation is listed, the **cause** is identified, the **consequence** is evaluated, and a **mitigation** plan is created. This is EXACTLY what a PHA does for a chemical plant — except the "passengers" are operators, the public, and the environment, and the "crash" can be a Bhopal-scale disaster.

### 1.3 Governing Standards and Constants

| Standard/Constant | Description |
|---|---|
| **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (e)** | U.S. federal mandate for initial and updated PHAs |
| **EPA 40 CFR Part 68** | Risk Management Plan (RMP) rule for accidental releases |
| **CCPS Guidelines** | Industry best-practice framework for PHA |
| **IEC 61882** | International standard for HAZOP studies |
| **API RP 14C** | Recommended Practice for offshore production platforms |
| **TNT Heat of Explosion (H_TNT)** | **4545 kJ/kg** (standard reference) |

### 1.4 Why PHA is Non-Negotiable in KTU Curriculum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PHA is the **backbone of any process safety program**. The Bhopal disaster (1984), Flixborough disaster (1974), and Texas City refinery explosion (2005) all had root causes that a proper PHA could have detected and mitigated. KTU examiners frequently test student understanding of PHA methodology, team composition, and the differences between techniques like HAZOP, FMEA, and What-If analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The PHA Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic

A PHA study follows a **structured 7-step framework**:

1. **Define Scope and Objectives**
   - Specify the process units, systems, and boundaries to be analyzed.
   - Define the level of detail (e.g., block-level vs. equipment-level).

2. **Assemble a Multidisciplinary Team**
   - Minimum of **5 members**, each with specialized expertise.

3. **Gather Information (Node Definition)**
   - P&IDs (Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams)
   - Process flow diagrams (PFDs)
   - Operating procedures, material safety data sheets (MSDS/SDS)
   - Equipment datasheets, layout drawings

4. **Divide Process into Nodes/Sections**
   - A "node" is a logical section of the process (e.g., a reactor, a heat exchanger, a storage tank, a pipeline section).

5. **Identify Deviations and Hazards**
   - Use the chosen PHA technique (HAZOP, FMEA, What-If, Checklist) to identify deviations from design intent.

6. **Evaluate Causes, Consequences, and Safeguards**
   - For each deviation, determine:
     * **Cause**: What could go wrong?
     * **Consequence**: What is the outcome if the deviation occurs?
     * **Safeguards**: Existing protective systems (alarms, interlocks, relief valves, dikes).
     * **Recommendations**: Additional measures required.

7. **Document, Report, and Follow Up**
   - Issue a PHA report with action items.
   - **Resolve recommendations** within a stipulated timeframe.
   - **Revalidate PHA every 5 years** (OSHA mandate).

### 2.2 PHA Team Composition (Mandatory Requirement)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per OSHA 1910.119(e)(4), the PHA team must include:
> - **(a)** A team leader (facilitator) — experienced in the PHA methodology
> - **(b)** At least one **process engineer** with detailed process knowledge
> - **(c)** At least one **operating personnel** (e.g., shift supervisor, plant operator)
> - **(d)** At least one **maintenance/instrumentation engineer**
> - **(e)** A **safety specialist** (optional but recommended)
> - **(f)** Other specialists as needed (e.g., environmental, civil/structural, electrical)

### 2.3 Classification of PHA Techniques

PHA techniques are broadly classified into three categories:

| Category | Techniques | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Qualitative (Syntactic)** | What-If, Checklist, Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PrHA) | Early project stage, simple processes |
| **Semi-Quantitative** | HAZOP, FMEA, FMECA, Bow-Tie | Continuous/batch processes, most common |
| **Quantitative** | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), Event Tree Analysis (ETA), QRA | High-hazard facilities, LOPA support |

### 2.4 Detailed Explanation of Major PHA Techniques

#### 2.4.1 HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)
- **Origin**: Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI), UK, 1960s.
- **Concept**: Apply guide words (No, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than) to process parameters (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition) at each node.
- **Best for**: Continuous chemical processes with detailed P&IDs.

#### 2.4.2 FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)
- **Concept**: Identify failure modes of each component, evaluate their effects on the system.
- **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** = **Severity (S) × Occurrence (O) × Detection (D)**
- **Scale**: Typically 1–10 for each parameter.
- **Best for**: Mechanical/electrical systems, instrumentation.

#### 2.4.3 What-If Analysis
- **Concept**: Brainstorming session where the team asks "What if...?" questions.
- **Best for**: Simple systems, modifications, early-stage design.

#### 2.4.4 Checklist Analysis
- **Concept**: Use a pre-developed list of known hazards and operational concerns.
- **Best for**: Routine processes, audits.

#### 2.4.5 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)
- **Concept**: Top-down deductive analysis using Boolean logic (AND/OR gates).
- **Best for**: Identifying combinations of failures leading to a top event.

#### 2.4.6 Event Tree Analysis (ETA)
- **Concept**: Forward-branching analysis from an initiating event to all possible outcomes.
- **Best for**: Quantifying consequences of an initiating event.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **KTU exam-ready summary** of all key formulas, definitions, and units for PHA.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk (R)** | $R = f \times C$ | f = frequency (per year), C = consequence (injury/cost) |
| 2 | **RPN (FMEA)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Each parameter rated 1–10 |
| 3 | **TNT Equivalent Mass** | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}}$ | $\eta$ = yield factor, M = mass (kg), $H_c$ = heat of combustion (kJ/kg), $H_{TNT} = 4545$ kJ/kg |
| 4 | **Peak Side-On Overpressure (TNT)** | $Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | R = distance (m), Z = scaled distance (m/kg^(1/3)) |
| 5 | **Probit Variable (Thermal)** | $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot \ln\left(q^{4/3} \cdot t\right)$ | q = heat flux (W/m²), t = exposure time (s) |
| 6 | **Probit Variable (Overpressure)** | $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \cdot \ln(P)$ | P = peak overpressure (Pa) |
| 7 | **Percentage of Affected (Probit)** | $\% = 50 \cdot \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ | erf = error function |
| 8 | **HAZOP Guide Words** | NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN | 7 standard guide words |
| 9 | **PHA Revalidation Period** | Every **5 years** (OSHA 1910.119) | After process modification: immediate |
| 10 | **Bow-Tie Components** | Threats, Top Event, Consequences, Preventive Controls, Mitigative Controls | Diagrammatic risk representation |

### 2.6 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering Practice

> [!NOTE]
> The **TNT equivalent** and **probit equations** are used to translate physical energy releases into human/equipment damage probabilities. The **RPN** provides a structured way to prioritize maintenance and design changes. The **Risk = f × C** equation is the foundation of every quantitative risk assessment (QRA) and is the basis for the **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) principle used globally in process safety.

### 2.7 PHA vs. Other Hazard Identification Tools

| Tool | PHA | HIRA | JSA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Whole process system | Specific task | Job-specific activity |
| **Team Size** | 5+ members | 3–5 members | 2–3 members |
| **Approach** | Node-by-node systematic | Brainstorming | Step-by-step |
| **Output** | PHA report with risk register | Hazard log | Job safety analysis sheet |
| **Frequency** | Every 5 years | Per project | Per task |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: TNT Equivalent Mass for Explosion Consequence Analysis

The TNT equivalent method converts the energy released by a hydrocarbon explosion into an equivalent mass of TNT, which is used as a reference because extensive empirical blast damage data exists for TNT.

**Given:**
- Mass of hydrocarbon released: $M$ (kg)
- Heat of combustion of hydrocarbon: $H_c$ (kJ/kg)
- Explosive yield (fraction of energy participating in blast): $\eta$ (dimensionless, typically 0.1–0.4 for vapor cloud explosions)
- Heat of explosion of TNT: $H_{TNT} = 4545$ kJ/kg

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Total energy released by hydrocarbon explosion.

$$E_{chem} = M \cdot H_c \quad [\text{kJ}]$$

**Step 2:** Effective energy contributing to blast wave.

$$E_{blast} = \eta \cdot E_{chem} = \eta \cdot M \cdot H_c \quad [\text{kJ}]$$

**Step 3:** Define TNT equivalent mass as the mass of TNT that would release the same blast energy.

$$W_{TNT} \cdot H_{TNT} = E_{blast}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $W_{TNT}$.

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}} \quad [\text{kg TNT}]$$

**Step 5:** The **scaled distance** $Z$ correlates blast overpressure to distance.

$$Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} \quad [\text{m/kg}^{1/3}]$$

where $R$ is the distance from the explosion center (m).

**Step 6:** Use the **Bauerle overpressure correlation** (or similar empirical chart) to find peak overpressure $P$ (Pa) as a function of $Z$.

**Numerical Example (KTU Exam Favorite):**

A propane cloud of **1000 kg** undergoes a vapor cloud explosion. Given: $H_c = 46,000$ kJ/kg, $\eta = 0.10$, $H_{TNT} = 4545$ kJ/kg. Find $W_{TNT}$.

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 1000 \times 46000}{4545}$$

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{4,600,000}{4545}$$

$$W_{TNT} \approx 1012.10 \quad \text{kg TNT}$$

This is equivalent to detonating approximately **1 metric ton of TNT** — illustrating the catastrophic potential of unconfined vapor cloud explosions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Consequence Severity from Probit Equation (Overpressure)

**Given:** Peak side-on overpressure $P = 50,000$ Pa at a populated location.

**Step 1:** Apply the overpressure probit equation for fatalities.

$$Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \cdot \ln(P)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $P = 50,000$ Pa.

$$Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \cdot \ln(50000)$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate $\ln(50000)$.

$$\ln(50000) = \ln(5 \times 10^4) = \ln 5 + 4 \ln 10 = 1.6094 + 9.2103 = 10.8198$$

**Step 4:** Compute $Y$.

$$Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \times 10.8198$$

$$Y = -77.1 + 74.7648$$

$$Y = -2.3352$$

**Step 5:** Convert probit to percentage of fatalities using the probit-to-percentage relation.

$$\% = 50 \cdot \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

$$\dfrac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{-2.3352 - 5}{1.4142} = \dfrac{-7.3352}{1.4142} = -5.1865$$

**Step 6:** Compute $\text{erf}(-5.1865)$. For $|x| > 3$, $\text{erf}(x) \approx 1$.

$$\text{erf}(-5.1865) \approx -1.0000$$

**Step 7:** Calculate fatality percentage.

$$\% = 50 \cdot [1 + (-1.0000)] = 50 \cdot 0 = 0\%$$

So at 50,000 Pa overpressure, the probit is **negative** ($-2.34$), indicating **near-zero fatality probability** at that specific overpressure. This is because 50 kPa corresponds to a relatively moderate overpressure (≈7.25 psi) at significant distance.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: RPN Calculation in FMEA

A centrifugal pump has the following FMEA ratings:

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seal leakage | 8 | 6 | 5 |
| Bearing failure | 9 | 4 | 3 |
| Impeller erosion | 7 | 3 | 7 |

**Step 1:** Calculate RPN for each failure mode.

$$\text{RPN}_{\text{Seal}} = 8 \times 6 \times 5 = 240$$

$$\text{RPN}_{\text{Bearing}} = 9 \times 4 \times 3 = 108$$

$$\text{RPN}_{\text{Impeller}} = 7 \times 3 \times 7 = 147$$

**Step 2:** Rank by RPN.

- **Seal leakage (RPN = 240)** → Highest priority. Action: Replace seal type, install condition monitoring.
- **Impeller erosion (RPN = 147)** → Medium priority. Action: Scheduled inspection.
- **Bearing failure (RPN = 108)** → Lower priority. Action: Routine lubrication.

**Step 3:** Decision logic.

> [!NOTE]
> If RPN > 200, immediate corrective action is mandated. If RPN is between 100–200, action is recommended. If RPN < 100, monitoring is sufficient.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: PHA Technique Selection

| Process Characteristic | Recommended PHA Technique |
|---|---|
| New chemical plant (continuous process) | HAZOP |
| Offshore platform with high-pressure equipment | HAZOP + FTA + ETA |
| Modifications to existing process (Management of Change) | What-If + Checklist |
| Instrumentation and control systems | FMEA |
| Storage tank farm | Checklist + What-If |
| High-hazard facility with potential for off-site impact | QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment) |
| Pipeline systems | HAZOP + FTA |
| Batch process with multiple steps | HAZOP |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: HAZOP Study (15 Marks Equivalent)

**Node:** Reactor Feed Preheat Exchanger (Tube side: cold feed; Shell side: hot solvent at 220°C)

**Process Parameters:** Flow, Temperature, Pressure

**HAZOP Worksheet:**

| Guide Word | Parameter | Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguard | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| NO | FLOW | No flow on tube side | Pump failure, blocked strainer | Loss of feed to reactor → catalyst damage | Flow indicator, low-flow alarm, interlock trip | Install redundant flow transmitter |
| MORE | TEMPERATURE | Higher shell-side temperature | Steam valve stuck open | Tube overheating → rupture, fire | Temperature indicator, pressure safety valve (PSV) | Install high-high temperature trip |
| LESS | FLOW | Reduced flow on shell side | Control valve failure | Inadequate heating → cold feed to reactor → reaction runaway downstream | Flow indicator | None required |
| REVERSE | FLOW | Reverse flow on shell side | Check valve failure | Hot solvent enters feed line → flash, equipment damage | Check valve | Install second check valve |
| AS WELL AS | COMPOSITION | Contaminants in feed | Upstream leak | Side reaction → pressure build-up | Composition analyzer | None required |

---

### 3.6 Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) Derivation Outline

**Step 1:** Identify hazardous events.
**Step 2:** Estimate event frequency (using historical data or FTA).
**Step 3:** Model consequences (using TNT equivalent, probit equations, dispersion models).
**Step 4:** Integrate frequency and consequence to obtain **Individual Risk (IR)** and **Societal Risk (SR)**.
**Step 5:** Compare against acceptance criteria (FN-curve, IR contour map).

$$\text{IR} = \sum_i f_i \cdot P_i$$

where $f_i$ = frequency of event $i$ (per year), $P_i$ = probability of fatality at location.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PHA Workflow — Master Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start PHA Study] --> nodeB[Define Scope and Objectives]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Assemble Multidisciplinary Team 5 plus members]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Gather Process Information P and ID, PFD, MSDS]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Divide Process into Logical Nodes]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Select PHA Technique HAZOP FMEA What If Checklist]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Identify Deviations using Guide Words or Failure Modes]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Evaluate Causes Consequences Safeguards]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Document Findings in PHA Worksheet]
    nodeI --> decisionJ{Critical Risk Identified}
    decisionJ -- Yes --> nodeK[Generate Recommendations]
    decisionJ -- No --> nodeL[Continue to Next Node]
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM{All Nodes Analyzed}
    nodeM -- No --> nodeF
    nodeM -- Yes --> nodeN[Issue Final PHA Report]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Implement Recommendations]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Revalidate Every 5 Years per OSHA 1910.119]
    nodeP --> endQ[End of PHA Cycle]
```

### 4.2 PHA Technique Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[PHA Techniques] --> nodeB[Qualitative Methods]
    startA --> nodeC[Semi Quantitative Methods]
    startA --> nodeD[Quantitative Methods]
    nodeB --> nodeE[What If Analysis]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Checklist Analysis]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Preliminary Hazard Analysis]
    nodeC --> nodeH[HAZOP Study]
    nodeC --> nodeI[FMEA and FMECA]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[Bow Tie Analysis]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Fault Tree Analysis FTA]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Event Tree Analysis ETA]
    nodeD --> nodeM[Quantitative Risk Assessment QRA]
```

### 4.3 Bow-Tie Diagram Structure (Generic PHA Risk Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeThreats[Threats] -- Left Side Preventive Controls --> nodeTopEvent[Top Event Loss of Containment]
    nodeTopEvent -- Right Side Mitigative Controls --> nodeConsequences[Consequences]
    nodeA[Equipment Failure] --> nodeThreats
    nodeB[Human Error] --> nodeThreats
    nodeC[External Event] --> nodeThreats
    nodeD[Pressure Relief Valve] --> nodeTopEvent
    nodeE[Fire and Gas Detection] --> nodeConsequences
    nodeF[Emergency Shutdown ESD] --> nodeConsequences
    nodeG[Fire] --> nodeConsequences
    nodeH[Explosion] --> nodeConsequences
    nodeI[Toxic Release] --> nodeConsequences
```

### 4.4 Risk Matrix — Standard 5×5 Consequence vs. Frequency Grid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeTitle[Risk Matrix R equals Frequency times Consequence]
    nodeTitle --> nodeCat1[Catastrophic 5]
    nodeTitle --> nodeCat2[Critical 4]
    nodeTitle --> nodeCat3[Major 3]
    nodeTitle --> nodeCat4[Moderate 2]
    nodeTitle --> nodeCat5[Minor 1]
    nodeCat1 --> nodeAccept[Unacceptable Zone]
    nodeCat2 --> nodeAccept
    nodeCat3 --> nodeALARP[ALARP Zone As Low As Reasonably Practicable]
    nodeCat4 --> nodeAcceptable[Acceptable Zone]
    nodeCat5 --> nodeAcceptable
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Bow-Tie diagram visually links **threats → top event → consequences** with preventive and mitigative barriers. It is widely used in the upstream oil and gas industry and is increasingly adopted in chemical process plants as a PHA communication tool.

### 4.5 PHA Team Roles and Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeLead[PHA Team Leader Facilitator] --> nodeEng[Process Engineer]
    nodeLead --> nodeOp[Operating Personnel]
    nodeLead --> nodeMaint[Maintenance Engineer]
    nodeLead --> nodeSafety[Safety Specialist]
    nodeLead --> nodeOther[Other Specialists Environmental Civil]
    nodeEng --> nodeWorksheet[PHA Worksheet and Risk Register]
    nodeOp --> nodeWorksheet
    nodeMaint --> nodeWorksheet
    nodeSafety --> nodeWorksheet
    nodeOther --> nodeWorksheet
    nodeWorksheet --> nodeReport[PHA Report and Action Tracker]
    nodeReport --> nodeManagement[Plant Management for Action Closure]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define Process Hazard Analysis (PHA). List any four PHA techniques.

**Model Answer:**

> **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is a systematic, structured engineering study to identify, evaluate, and control hazards in chemical/process industries. It is mandated under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** for facilities handling hazardous chemicals.

**Four PHA techniques:**
1. **HAZOP** (Hazard and Operability Study)
2. **FMEA** (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)
3. **What-If Analysis**
4. **Checklist Analysis**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State the OSHA-mandated revalidation period for a PHA study. What is the minimum number of members in a PHA team?

**Model Answer:**

- **PHA Revalidation Period:** Every **5 years** (or immediately after any process modification triggering Management of Change).
- **Minimum PHA Team Size:** **5 members** (including a team leader, process engineer, operating personnel, maintenance engineer, and safety specialist).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — HAZOP-Centered

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**(a)** Explain the HAZOP methodology in detail. List all standard HAZOP guide words and process parameters, and describe the role of a HAZOP team leader. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a **centrifugal pump** delivering a flammable solvent from a storage tank to a reactor. Conduct a partial HAZOP study (minimum **5 deviations**) on this node, listing causes, consequences, safeguards, and recommendations in a tabular format. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### (a) HAZOP Methodology (7 marks)

**Definition:** HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) is a systematic, node-based PHA technique developed by ICI in the 1960s. It applies a set of **guide words** to **process parameters** to identify deviations from design intent.

**HAZOP Standard Guide Words (7 marks criterion):**

| Guide Word | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **NO / NOT** | Complete negation of the parameter |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional activity or component present |
| **PART OF** | Only a part of the design intent achieved |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite of intent |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution |

**HAZOP Process Parameters:** Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Time, Sequence, Mixing, Reaction.

**Role of HAZOP Team Leader:**
- Facilitates the study (does not dictate technical content).
- Ensures all guide words are applied systematically.
- Encourages brainstorming from all team members.
- Documents findings in the HAZOP worksheet.
- Has **expertise in the HAZOP methodology** (not necessarily deep process knowledge).
- Manages time, ensures scope compliance, and resolves conflicts.

**HAZOP Steps:**
1. Define scope and obtain P&IDs.
2. Divide process into nodes.
3. Apply guide words × parameters to each node.
4. Identify deviations, causes, consequences.
5. Recommend safeguards.
6. Document and report.

#### (b) HAZOP Worksheet — Centrifugal Pump Node (7 marks)

| Guide Word | Parameter | Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguard | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| NO | FLOW | No flow | Pump failure, suction line blocked | Loss of feed to reactor → reaction stoppage → catalyst damage | Low-flow alarm, interlock trip | Install redundant pump |
| MORE | PRESSURE | High discharge pressure | Blocked discharge line, dead-head pump | Seal failure → solvent leak → fire hazard | Pressure indicator, PSV | Install high-pressure trip |
| LESS | FLOW | Reduced flow | Partial blockage, worn impeller | Insufficient feed → reactor starvation → side reactions | Flow transmitter | Install vibration monitoring |
| MORE | TEMPERATURE | High pump temperature | Cavitation, dry running, bearing failure | Bearing seizure → pump damage → leak | Temperature gauge, bearing temp sensor | Install thermal cutout |
| AS WELL AS | COMPOSITION | Air ingress on suction side | Open vent, vacuum formation | Flammable mixture in pump → internal explosion | Nitrogen blanketing, low-O₂ alarm | Install oxygen analyzer |
| NO | LEVEL (in suction tank) | Tank empty | Level instrument failure, drain valve open | Pump cavitation → mechanical damage | Low-level alarm, level switch trip | Install level interlock |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — FMEA & Risk-Based Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
**(a)** Explain the FMEA methodology. Define the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** and explain each of its components (S, O, D) with a typical 1–10 rating scale. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A control valve on a **pressurized flammable gas line** has the following FMEA ratings: Severity = 9, Occurrence = 4, Detection = 5.
  (i) Calculate the RPN.
  (ii) Interpret the RPN value and suggest **two engineering controls** to reduce it.
  (iii) Briefly explain the **Bow-Tie** method as a PHA technique. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### (a) FMEA Methodology (7 marks)

**FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** is a systematic, bottom-up PHA technique that identifies potential failure modes of each component, evaluates the effects of those failures, and prioritizes actions based on risk.

**RPN Definition:**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

- **S — Severity (1 to 10):** Impact of the failure on the system, personnel, or environment.
  * 1 = No effect, 10 = Catastrophic (death, major environmental release)
- **O — Occurrence (1 to 10):** Likelihood that the failure mode will occur.
  * 1 = Very unlikely, 10 = Very high frequency (almost certain)
- **D — Detection (1 to 10):** Probability that existing controls will NOT detect the failure before it reaches the customer/system.
  * 1 = Easily detected (high detectability = low score), 10 = Cannot be detected

**RPN Decision Matrix:**

| RPN Range | Action Required |
|---|---|
| 1 – 100 | Low priority — monitoring only |
| 101 – 200 | Medium priority — corrective action recommended |
| 201 – 400 | High priority — immediate action required |
| > 400 | Critical — system redesign mandatory |

#### (b) Numerical + Bow-Tie (7 marks)

**(i) RPN Calculation:**

$$RPN = 9 \times 4 \times 5 = 180$$

**(ii) Interpretation:** RPN = 180 falls in the **medium-high risk** range. The failure mode is severe (S=9), moderately likely (O=4), and detection capability is moderate (D=5).

**Two Engineering Controls:**
1. **Reduce Severity (S):** Install a **double block and bleed valve** arrangement to prevent gas release during maintenance.
2. **Reduce Occurrence (O):** Replace the valve with a **fire-safe, severe-service valve** rated for the specific pressure and corrosive service.
3. **Improve Detection (D):** Install a **partial-stroak testing system** with a smart positioner to detect valve failure in real time.
4. **Add a procedural safeguard:** Mandatory preventive maintenance every 6 months (reduces O).

**(iii) Bow-Tie Method:**
