The **Bow-Tie** method is a graphical PHA technique that combines a **Fault Tree (left side)** and an **Event Tree (right side)** linked by a **top event** (e.g., loss of containment). It clearly visualizes:
- **Threats** that can cause the top event
- **Preventive controls** that stop threats from reaching the top event
- **Mitigative controls** that reduce consequences after the top event occurs
- **Escalation factors** that degrade barrier effectiveness

The Bow-Tie is widely used in the **oil and gas industry** (especially in the North Sea under COMAH/Safety Case regulations) because it provides a clear, visual, and auditable risk representation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PHA Exam Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PHA with HIRA or JSA:** Many students write generic hazard identification when the question specifically asks about PHA. PHA is a **systematic, team-based, document-intensive** study — mention team composition, OSHA mandate, and revalidation.
>
> 2. **Incomplete HAZOP tables:** Examiners expect a **minimum of 4–5 guide words applied to process parameters** in a tabular format. Skipping columns (e.g., omitting "Recommendation") results in **−1 to −2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to state units in numerical problems:** For TNT equivalent, always state $H_{TNT} = 4545$ kJ/kg explicitly. For overpressure, state $P$ in Pa.
>
> 4. **Confusing RPN thresholds:** Some textbooks use 1–5 or 1–8 scales. KTU prefers the **1–10 standard CCPS scale**.
>
> 5. **Writing the Bow-Tie description as FTA or ETA:** Bow-Tie is a **hybrid** visualization — explicitly mention threats, top event, preventive controls, and mitigative controls.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the 5-year revalidation:** This is a **favorite 1-mark question** by KTU examiners.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **PHA = Systematic hazard identification + risk evaluation** for chemical/process industries.
- [x] **Governing Standard:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119; CCPS Guidelines; EPA RMP Rule.
- [x] **PHA Team:** Minimum 5 members; must include operating personnel, process engineer, maintenance engineer, and team leader.
- [x] **Revalidation:** Every 5 years OR after any process modification (Management of Change).
- [x] **Key PHA Techniques:** HAZOP, FMEA, What-If, Checklist, FTA, ETA, Bow-Tie, QRA.
- [x] **HAZOP Guide Words (7):** NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN.
- [x] **FMEA Formula:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$ (each rated 1–10).
- [x] **TNT Equivalent:** $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}}$ with $H_{TNT} = 4545$ kJ/kg.
- [x] **Probit (Overpressure, fatalities):** $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \cdot \ln(P)$.
- [x] **Probit (Thermal radiation):** $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot \ln(q^{4/3} \cdot t)$.
- [x] **Risk = Frequency × Consequence** ($R = f \times C$).
- [x] **Bow-Tie:** Threats → Top Event → Consequences, with preventive/mitigative controls.
- [x] **FTA** uses Boolean logic (AND/OR gates); **ETA** uses forward-branching.
- [x] **ALARP Principle:** Risk must be reduced to "As Low As Reasonably Practicable."
- [x] **PHA Report** must include risk register, action items, responsibility, and target closure date.
- [x] **Selection Rule:** HAZOP for continuous processes; FMEA for instrumentation; What-If for modifications; FTA/ETA for high-hazard events.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for HAZOP Guide Words — "No, More, Less, A, P, R, O":**
> **N**o, **M**ore, **L**ess, **A**s Well As, **P**art Of, **R**everse, **O**ther Than
>
> **Mnemonic for PHA Team Roles — "L-P-O-M-S":**
> **L**eader, **P**rocess engineer, **O**perator, **M**aintenance, **S**afety specialist

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA) - Methodology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA) - Methodology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, proactive, and inductive engineering methodology used to identify, evaluate, and prioritize potential failure modes of a product, process, or system. For each identified failure mode, the FMEA methodology documents the **Effect** (consequence on the system/customer), the **Cause** (root mechanism leading to failure), and assigns three numerical risk metrics — **Severity (S)**, **Occurrence (O)**, and **Detection (D)** — to compute the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** FMEA is an inductive bottom-up analytical technique that maps component-level failure modes to system-level effects, enabling structured prioritization of risk mitigation activities in alignment with the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle of ISO 31000 and the requirements of **IEC 60812:2006**.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of FMEA Scoring

| Metric | Symbol | Range | Question It Answers |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Severity** | **S** | **1 – 10** | "How bad is the effect on the customer or system?" |
| **Occurrence** | **O** | **1 – 10** | "How frequently does the cause occur?" |
| **Detection** | **D** | **1 – 10** | "How likely is the failure to be detected *before* reaching the customer?" |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Mechanic's Notebook"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine you are a senior mechanic responsible for **500 cars** leaving your workshop every month. You don't have time to fix *every* minor rattle, so you need a notebook to:
> 1. List **every possible way** a part could fail ("Brake pad may wear out" = *Failure Mode*).
> 2. Describe **what would happen** ("Car fails to stop" = *Effect*).
> 3. Guess **how often** it happens ("Every 6 months" = *Occurrence*).
> 4. Judge **how severe** the consequence is ("Fatal accident" = *Severity*).
> 5. Determine **how easily** your inspection catches it ("Only after road-test" = *Detection*).
>
> You then multiply S × O × D to get a **priority score**. Whichever failure has the highest score, you fix **first**. That is exactly what FMEA does — but on an industrial scale, for aircraft engines, nuclear reactors, chemical plants, and software systems.

## 1.4 Evolution of FMEA — Types Recognized in KTU 2024 Syllabus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Recognition:** The 2024 Scheme acknowledges four principal variants of FMEA. Students must distinguish them for full credit in Module 2 questions.
> - **System FMEA** — examines high-level system architecture.
> - **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** — focuses on product/component design.
> - **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** — focuses on manufacturing/assembly processes.
> - **Service FMEA** — focuses on customer-facing service functions.
>
> When ambiguity exists, FMEA defaults to **DFMEA** in KTU board questions unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ten Sequential Steps of FMEA Methodology

The FMEA methodology is executed as a **structured 10-step workflow**. Each step has a definite deliverable and a designated *Responsibility Matrix* (which is normally tabled in the final FMEA worksheet).

### Step 1 — Assemble the Cross-Functional Team
FMEA is **never** a solo exercise. A team of 4–8 experts from design, manufacturing, quality, reliability, and service functions is required.

### Step 2 — Define the Scope, Boundaries, and Assumptions
The **FMEA Project Charter** specifies:
- Item under analysis (system/sub-system/component).
- Level of analysis (top-down or bottom-up).
- Assumptions (operating conditions, environment).
- Customer of the analysis (internal/external).

### Step 3 — Develop the Block Diagram
A topological block diagram is drawn showing the **inter-relationships** between system elements.

### Step 4 — Identify Functions of Each Element
Functions must be quantified using **Performance Metrics** (e.g., flow rate, pressure, voltage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Function-Anchoring Principle:** A failure mode is only meaningful if it is anchored to a *function*. KTU board answers that link failure modes directly to functions always score higher.

### Step 5 — Determine Failure Modes for Each Function
The methodology asks: *"In what way could this element fail to deliver its function?"* Common grammar: **"fails to..."**, **"intermittent..."**, **"degraded..."**, **"unintended..."**.

### Step 6 — Determine Effects of Each Failure Mode
Effects cascade upward to the next assembly level. Effects must be described in **customer-perceivable terms** ("Lamp flickers" rather than "voltage fluctuation").

### Step 7 — Determine Causes of Each Failure Mode
Causes are identified at the **lowest possible decomposition level** and are usually categorized into: design deficiency, material defect, manufacturing defect, operational error, environmental stress.

### Step 8 — Assign Severity (S), Occurrence (O), Detection (D) Ratings
Using standardized **1–10 rating tables** (typically derived from **SAE J1739**, **AIAG-VDA 2019**, or **IEC 60812**).

### Step 9 — Calculate Risk Priority Number (RPN) and Recommend Actions
RPN is computed and the highest-priority items are targeted for corrective action.

### Step 10 — Document, Implement Actions, and Re-Rate
After actions are taken, **new S, O, D values** are assigned and a **new RPN** is computed. The cycle repeats until RPN targets are met.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental FMEA Equation:**
>
> $$\boxed{\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D}$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $S \in \{1, 2, 3, ..., 10\}$ — *Severity Rating*
- $O \in \{1, 2, 3, ..., 10\}$ — *Occurrence Rating*
- $D \in \{1, 2, 3, ..., 10\}$ — *Detection Rating*
- $\therefore \text{RPN}_{\min} = 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1$
- $\therefore \text{RPN}_{\max} = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$

**Action Priority (AP) — Modern AIAG-VDA 2019 Alternative:**
Instead of pure RPN, the **Action Priority Matrix** classifies risk into **H (High), M (Medium), L (Low)**. KTU may accept either method.

### 2.2.1 Severity Rating Scale (KTU Standard)

| Rating | Effect | KTU-Board Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **10** | Hazardous — safety failure without warning | Loss of life / total system loss |
| **9** | Hazardous — with warning | Major injury / mission failure |
| **8** | Major loss of primary function | Vehicle cannot operate |
| **7** | Significant degradation | Primary function degraded |
| **6** | Loss of secondary function | Secondary system failure |
| **5** | Significant inconvenience | Customer dissatisfaction |
| **4** | Minor inconvenience | Repair required soon |
| **3** | Minor effect | Noticeable to few customers |
| **2** | Minimal effect | Barely noticeable |
| **1** | None | No effect |

### 2.2.2 Occurrence Rating Scale

| Rating | Failure Rate | KTU-Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **10** | $\geq 1 \text{ in } 2$ | Very high — almost inevitable |
| **9** | $1 \text{ in } 8$ | High — frequent |
| **8** | $1 \text{ in } 80$ | High — repeated failure |
| **7** | $1 \text{ in } 800$ | Moderate — frequent failure |
| **6** | $1 \text{ in } 15{,}000$ | Moderate |
| **5** | $1 \text{ in } 150{,}000$ | Moderate |
| **4** | $1 \text{ in } 1{,}500{,}000$ | Low |
| **3** | $1 \text{ in } 15{,}000{,}000$ | Low |
| **2** | $1 \text{ in } 150{,}000{,}000$ | Remote |
| **1** | Failure eliminated by design | Almost impossible |

### 2.2.3 Detection Rating Scale

| Rating | Detection Probability | KTU-Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **10** | Almost impossible to detect | No inspection method |
| **9** | Very remote chance | Very low detection capability |
| **8** | Remote chance | Low detection capability |
| **7** | Very low | Low-to-moderate |
| **6** | Low | Moderate |
| **5** | Moderate | Moderate |
| **4** | Moderately high | Moderate-to-high |
| **3** | High | High |
| **2** | Very high | Very high (control with error-proofing) |
| **1** | Almost certain | Control prevents occurrence |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where FMEA is Used:** Aerospace (FAA Part 25 mandated), Automotive (AIAG-VDA), Nuclear (NUREG-1852), Medical Devices (ISO 14971), Semiconductor manufacturing, Oil & Gas (API 581), Pharmaceutical (ICH Q9), and increasingly in **cyber-security risk** (NIST SP 800-30). KTU emphasizes this method because it satisfies both **Hazard Identification (HAZID)** and **Risk Assessment** in a single structured deliverable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of RPN and Decision Thresholds

### 3.1.1 From Failure Probability to Numerical Rating

Let the *actual* probability of failure of a component during its mission be **$p_f$**. The KTU-accepted Occurrence rating **$O$** is derived from $p_f$ through a discrete mapping table. However, we can derive a **continuous approximation** useful for sensitivity analysis:

$$
O \approx - \log_{10}(p_f) + 1
$$

For example, if $p_f = 10^{-3}$ (1 in 1000), then $O \approx -(-3) + 1 = 4$.

### 3.1.2 Severity as a Severity-Index Function

Severity is **not probabilistic** but is treated as an ordinal weighting. Mathematically, the *severity index* $S$ is treated as a rank variable satisfying:

$$
S : \{\text{Effect}\} \longrightarrow \{1, 2, ..., 10\}
$$

with the *strict monotone* property:

$$
\text{Effect}_A \succ \text{Effect}_B \implies S_A > S_B
$$

### 3.1.3 Detection as a Conditional Probability

Detection **$D$** is the probability that the failure **escapes** to the customer. Mathematically:

$$
D \approx P(\text{Failure escapes to customer} \mid \text{Failure has occurred})
$$

Therefore **$D$ is inversely related to inspection capability**: better detection systems yield **lower** $D$ values, which reduces RPN — a desirable outcome.

### 3.1.4 The Risk Priority Number — Algebraic Derivation

Starting from the definition:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

Substituting the bounded ranges:

$$
\text{RPN} = \underbrace{S}_{\in [1,10]} \cdot \underbrace{O}_{\in [1,10]} \cdot \underbrace{D}_{\in [1,10]}
$$

Expanding with the boundary analysis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\min} &= 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1 \\
\text{RPN}_{\max} &= 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000 \\
\therefore \text{RPN} &\in [1, 1000] \subset \mathbb{Z}^{+}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 KTU-Board Acceptable Action Threshold

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Threshold (used in KTU numericals):** Any failure mode with **RPN > 100** OR **Severity ≥ 9** is flagged for **mandatory corrective action**, regardless of RPN value. This is called the **"Critical-Char Rule"** and is enforced by KTU board examiners.

Mathematically:

$$
\text{Action Required} = \begin{cases} \text{YES}, & \text{if } \text{RPN} > 100 \\ \text{YES}, & \text{if } S \geq 9 \\ \text{NO}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example

> [!TIP]
> **Worked KTU-Style Example:** Consider the **brake system** of a passenger car. A DFMEA team identifies the following failure mode for the *brake pedal arm*:

| Item | Function | Failure Mode | Effect | Cause | **S** | **O** | **D** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brake Pedal Arm | Transmit driver foot force to master cylinder | **Pedal arm bends** | Loss of braking — accident risk | Insufficient cross-section at weld | **9** | **4** | **6** |

### Step 1 — Calculate RPN

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D = 9 \times 4 \times 6
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN} &= 9 \times 4 \times 6 \\
&= 9 \times 24 \\
&= 216
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Decision Check

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN} &= 216 > 100 \quad \checkmark \\
S &= 9 \geq 9 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Both criteria trigger mandatory corrective action. The design team must **redesign** the cross-section (reducing $S$ is impossible, but $O$ can be reduced through better material, and $D$ through strain-gauged in-line testing).

### 3.2.1 Post-Action Re-Rating

After implementing corrective actions (e.g., adding reinforcement rib + 100% ultrasonic inspection):

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{new}} = 9 \times 2 \times 3 = 54
$$

The RPN dropped from **216 → 54**, a reduction of **75%**, satisfying the KTU-typical "≥ 50% reduction" target.

## 3.3 The Risk Matrix — Severity × Occurrence Visualization

> [!NOTE]
> **Risk Matrix Construction:** FMEA severity and occurrence ratings can be cross-plotted in a **10 × 10 risk matrix**, where the *colour-code* indicates action priority.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk Level} &= f(S, O) \text{ evaluated as:} \\
\text{Low} &:\; S \times O \leq 25 \\
\text{Moderate} &:\; 25 < S \times O \leq 70 \\
\text{High} &:\; 70 < S \times O \leq 100 \\
\text{Critical} &:\; S \times O > 100
\end{aligned}
$$

This 2D representation is often preferred over RPN because it does not suffer from **RPN masking** — a phenomenon where two failure modes with identical RPN (e.g., 3-3-3 = 27 vs. 1-9-3 = 27) carry vastly different risk profiles.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FMEA Methodology — Complete Process Flowchart

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (prefixed with letters), all labels use raw uppercase alphanumeric text, no markdown formatting inside node labels, and no reserved keywords are used as node names.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START FMEA Project] --> A2[Step 1 Assemble Cross Functional Team]
    A2 --> A3[Step 2 Define Scope Boundaries and Assumptions]
    A3 --> A4[Step 3 Develop System Block Diagram]
    A4 --> A5[Step 4 Identify Functions of Each Element]
    A5 --> A6[Step 5 Determine Failure Modes for Each Function]
    A6 --> A7[Step 6 Determine Effects of Each Failure Mode]
    A7 --> A8[Step 7 Determine Causes of Each Failure Mode]
    A8 --> A9[Step 8 Assign S O and D Ratings]
    A9 --> A10[Step 9 Calculate RPN]
    A10 --> A11{RPN Greater Than 100 OR Severity Greater Than Equal 9}
    A11 -->|YES| A12[Step 10a Recommend Corrective Actions]
    A12 --> A13[Implement Actions and Re Rate S O D]
    A13 --> A14[Calculate New RPN]
    A14 --> A15{New RPN Less Than Equal 100 AND Severity Less Than 9}
    A15 -->|YES| A16[Document and Close]
    A15 -->|NO| A12
    A11 -->|NO| A16
    A16 --> A17[END FMEA Deliverable]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — FMEA Worksheet Structure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram represents the canonical **FMEA Worksheet** as mandated by SAE J1739 / IEC 60812. Each column has a specific data type and review responsibility.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Item Function] --> B2[Failure Mode]
    B2 --> B3[Effect]
    B3 --> B4[Cause]
    B4 --> B5[S Rating 1 to 10]
    B5 --> B6[O Rating 1 to 10]
    B6 --> B7[D Rating 1 to 10]
    B7 --> B8[RPN Calculator S times O times D]
    B8 --> B9{Decision RPN over 100 or S over 9}
    B9 -->|YES| B10[Recommended Action]
    B10 --> B11[Action Owner]
    B11 --> B12[Target Completion Date]
    B12 --> B13[New S]
    B13 --> B14[New O]
    B14 --> B15[New D]
    B15 --> B16[New RPN]
    B9 -->|NO| B17[Accept Risk and Document]
```

## 4.3 Detection-Loss Causality Chain (Failure-Mode Cascade)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Root Cause e.g. material defect] --> C2[Failure Mode e.g. shaft fatigue crack]
    C2 --> C3[Local Effect e.g. excessive vibration]
    C3 --> C4[System Effect e.g. gearbox shutdown]
    C4 --> C5[Customer Effect e.g. production loss]
    C5 --> C6{Detection System}
    C6 -->|Detected at level 1| C7[Detection Rating Low i.e. 2]
    C6 -->|Detected at level 2| C8[Detection Rating Mid i.e. 5]
    C6 -->|Not Detected| C9[Detection Rating High i.e. 10]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram:** The diagram visually confirms the **cascade property** of FMEA — each failure "telescopes" upward. The **further downstream** a failure is detected (closer to customer), the **higher** the D rating, and therefore the **higher** the RPN.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions test **CO2 (Apply FMEA methodology)** at the *Remember/Understand* cognitive level of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define FMEA. List the three rating parameters used in FMEA with their standard range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, inductive, bottom-up engineering methodology used to identify potential failure modes of a system, evaluate their effects on system performance, and prioritize corrective actions based on a quantitative risk score.

The three rating parameters are:
1. **Severity (S)** — measures the seriousness of the failure effect, ranging from **1 to 10**.
2. **Occurrence (O)** — measures the likelihood of the failure cause occurring, ranging from **1 to 10**.
3. **Detection (D)** — measures the probability that the failure will escape detection before reaching the customer, ranging from **1 to 10**.

These three parameters are multiplied to compute the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Differentiate between Design FMEA (DFMEA) and Process FMEA (PFMEA).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | DFMEA | PFMEA |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Product/component design | Manufacturing/assembly process |
| **Failure Source** | Design inadequacy | Process variability |
| **Timing** | Conducted during product development | Conducted during process planning |
| **Cause Categories** | Material, geometry, tolerance | Machine, method, manpower |
| **Detection Methods** | Design reviews, FEA, prototyping | SPC, gauges, control plans |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B Module-2 questions are typically framed at the *Apply/Analyze* level. Each carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. **Internal Choice** must be provided as two completely independent alternatives. Both are presented below.

### ── Question A (14 Marks) ──

#### Part (a) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **(7 marks)**
**Explain the FMEA methodology in detail with its step-by-step procedure. How is the Risk Priority Number (RPN) calculated?**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

FMEA is executed as a structured **10-step methodology**:

1. **Assemble the cross-functional team** of design, manufacturing, quality, and reliability experts.
2. **Define scope and boundaries** through a Project Charter (item, level, assumptions).
3. **Develop the block diagram** showing inter-relationships of system elements.
4. **Identify functions** of each element with quantitative performance metrics.
5. **Determine failure modes** — ways in which each function may fail.
6. **Determine effects** — consequences cascading upward to the customer.
7. **Determine causes** — root mechanisms at the lowest decomposition level.
8. **Assign Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D)** ratings on a 1–10 scale using standardized tables.
9. **Calculate RPN and recommend actions** — see equation below.
10. **Implement actions, re-rate, and document** until RPN targets are met.

**RPN Calculation:**

$$
\boxed{\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D}
$$

The RPN ranges from 1 to 1000. Any failure mode with **RPN > 100** or **Severity ≥ 9** triggers mandatory corrective action.

#### Part (b) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **(7 marks)**
**A brake disc has a Severity rating of 8, an Occurrence rating of 5, and a Detection rating of 7. Calculate the RPN. If a corrective action reduces Occurrence to 2 and Detection to 4, calculate the percentage reduction in RPN.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Initial RPN Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{initial}} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 8 \times 5 \times 7 \\
&= 40 \times 7 \\
&= 280
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial RPN = 280** *(≥ 100 → corrective action required)*

**Post-Action RPN Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{new}} &= 8 \times 2 \times 4 \\
&= 16 \times 4 \\
&= 64
\end{aligned}
$$

**New RPN = 64**

**Percentage Reduction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} &= \frac{\text{RPN}_{\text{initial}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{new}}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{initial}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{280 - 64}{280} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{216}{280} \times 100 \\
&= 0.7714 \times 100 \\
&= 77.14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A **77.14% reduction in RPN** was achieved, well above the KTU-typical 50% improvement target. The new RPN of 64 falls below the 100-threshold, so the action is **closed successfully**.

### ── Question B (14 Marks) ──

#### Part (a) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **(7 marks)**
**Discuss the limitations of using RPN alone for risk prioritization. Explain the Action Priority (AP) approach as an alternative.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The RPN method has several well-documented limitations:

1. **RPN Masking:** Two failure modes can produce the same RPN (e.g., $S=9, O=1, D=3 = 27$ vs. $S=3, O=9, D=1 = 27$) despite having radically different risk profiles.
2. **Inconsistent weighting:** Multiplying S, O, and D assumes equal weighting, which is rarely true in safety-critical applications.
3. **Subjectivity:** Ratings are assigned by experts and can vary across teams.
4. **Ordinal misuse:** Ratings are ordinal but treated as interval/ratio data in the multiplication.
5. **Single-number distortion:** A low severity may mask high occurrence, producing false prioritization.

**Action Priority (AP) Alternative (AIAG-VDA 2019):**

The AP approach uses a **decision matrix** based on Severity, Occurrence, and Detection. Instead of a single RPN, the team assigns a label:

- **H (High Priority)** — must take action; typically safety-related.
- **M (Medium Priority)** — should take action.
- **L (Low Priority)** — may take action.

**Key Advantages of AP over RPN:**
- Eliminates RPN masking.
- Severity is given primary importance (aligning with safety priorities).
- Easier to communicate to non-technical stakeholders.
- Compliant with **IATF 16949** automotive QMS requirements.

#### Part (b) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **(7 marks)**
**For a chemical reactor system, identify any three failure modes with their effects, causes, and S/O/D ratings. Compute RPN and rank them.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Failure Mode Table — Chemical Reactor:**

| # | Failure Mode | Effect | Cause | S | O | D | RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure relief valve stuck | Reactor over-pressure → explosion risk | Corrosion deposits | 10 | 3 | 4 | 120 |
| 2 | Temperature sensor drift | Wrong temperature reading → exothermic runaway | Sensor calibration loss | 9 | 4 | 5 | 180 |
| 3 | Cooling jacket blockage | Loss of cooling → thermal runaway | Impurities in coolant | 9 | 5 | 6 | 270 |

**Calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_1 &= 10 \times 3 \times 4 = 120 \\
\text{RPN}_2 &= 9 \times 4 \times 5 = 180 \\
\text{RPN}_3 &= 9 \times 5 \times 6 = 270
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ranking (Highest to Lowest):**

1. **Cooling jacket blockage (RPN = 270)** — Top priority.
2. **Temperature sensor drift (RPN = 180)** — Second priority.
3. **Pressure relief valve stuck (RPN = 120)** — Third priority (but $S = 10$, so **Critical-Char Rule** also applies → mandatory action).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not** write only the RPN number without showing the multiplication step-by-step — KTU examiners award 1 mark per arithmetic step. **(−1 mark for skipping)**
> 2. **Always** re-state the Critical-Char Rule for $S \geq 9$ cases — failure to do so costs **2 marks** in Process FMEA questions.
> 3. **Never** write RPN as a decimal — it is always an **integer** in KTU valuations. **(−0.5 mark)**
> 4. **Avoid** confusion between **Severity of Effect** vs. **Severity of Cause** — KTU strictly evaluates the Effect, not the Cause.
> 5. **Always** quote at least **one limitation of RPN** in any 7-mark question — examiners check for this as a discriminator between average and high-scoring answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — FMEA Methodology**

### Core Concepts
- FMEA stands for **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis** — an inductive, bottom-up risk methodology.
- It is standardized under **IEC 60812:2006** and **SAE J1739**.
- Four types exist: **System, Design, Process, Service FMEA**.

### The Critical Equation
- $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ with each parameter in $\{1, 2, ..., 10\}$.
- $\text{RPN} \in [1, 1000]$ — strictly integer.

### Decision Rules (Memorize for KTU)
- **Action trigger:** $\text{RPN} > 100$ OR $S \geq 9$ → mandatory corrective action.
- **Critical-Char Rule:** $S \geq 9$ overrides low RPN; safety always takes precedence.
- **Closure target:** Achieve ≥ 50% RPN reduction and $S < 9$.

### Rating Anchors
- **S = 10** = Hazardous failure without warning (loss of life possible).
- **O = 10** = Failure rate ≥ 1 in 2 (almost certain).
- **D = 10** = No inspection method available (impossible to detect).

### Limitations to Quote
- **RPN Masking** — same RPN, different risk profiles.
- **Subjectivity** of ratings.
- **Equal weighting** assumption.
- **Ordinal-vs-ratio** data misuse.

### Modern Alternative
- **Action Priority (AP)** — H / M / L classification (AIAG-VDA 2019).
- Increasingly preferred in **automotive IATF 16949** audits.

### KTU Memory Hooks
- **"SOD" → RPN** — Severity, Occurrence, Detection.
- **"100 or 9"** — the two magic numbers that trigger action.
- **"IEC 60812"** — the KTU-favoured standard reference.

### Common Confusions to Avoid
- FMEA ≠ **FMEA-RCA**: FMEA evaluates *what can fail*, RCA investigates *why it did fail* (reactive).
- FMEA ≠ **HAZOP**: FMEA is inductive (bottom-up), HAZOP is deductive (top-down via guide words).
- FMEA is **proactive** (predictive), not **reactive** (historical).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Criticality Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Criticality Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Criticality Analysis

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Criticality Analysis** is a systematic, structured, and quantitative engineering methodology used within Process Safety Management (PSM) to evaluate, rank, and prioritize identified hazards, failure modes, or process deviations based on their combined **severity of consequence** and **likelihood of occurrence**. It is the logical step that follows Hazard Identification, where every potential upset condition is assigned a numerical "criticality value" to determine which scenarios demand immediate engineering control, procedural mitigation, or further detailed study.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety) guidelines, Criticality Analysis is defined as:

> The act of determining the *relative importance* of an identified hazard or failure mode to the safe, reliable, and economical operation of a process plant — combining the magnitude of the worst credible consequence with the expected frequency of that event.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$C = S \times P$$

where $C$ is the **Criticality**, $S$ is the **Severity**, and $P$ is the **Probability** (or frequency) of the event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Criticality Analysis forms the *quantitative backbone* of techniques such as **FMEA, FMECA, HAZOP, Dow's Fire & Explosion Index, and the Mond Index**. In KTU university exams, students are frequently asked to compute the Risk Priority Number (RPN) or build a Risk/Criticality Matrix — making this a guaranteed high-weightage topic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Light of Risk"

Imagine a doctor examining 100 patients arriving at an emergency ward. The doctor does *not* treat all patients equally. Instead:

- **Patient 1**: A mild headache (Low severity, High frequency → Green zone — no urgent action).
- **Patient 2**: A suspected heart attack (High severity, Moderate frequency → Red zone — immediate ICU admission).
- **Patient 3**: A common cold (Low severity, Low frequency → Green zone — paracetamol only).

The doctor performs a **Criticality Analysis** — assigning each patient a priority based on *how bad the consequence would be* (severity) and *how likely it is to happen if untreated* (probability). Criticality Analysis does the same for an industrial plant: it sorts the hundreds of alarms, deviations, and failure modes into a "traffic light" so management knows **which red flags to fix first**.

## 1.3 Why is it Called "Criticality" and Not Just "Risk"?

In KTU exam answers, examiners often differentiate:

| Term | Focus |
|------|-------|
| **Risk** | Combination of consequence + likelihood (broad umbrella term). |
| **Hazard** | The inherent *source* of harm (e.g., flammable liquid). |
| **Criticality** | The *degree of importance* or *priority ranking* of that hazard relative to others. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Criticality is the OUTPUT of a Risk Assessment — it is the ranked, prioritized list that drives engineering decisions.** Without criticality ranking, a plant has hazards but no *order of treatment*.

## 1.4 The Four Foundational Inputs of Criticality

Every criticality calculation, regardless of the specific method (FMEA, Dow, Mond, HAZOP), depends on four universal inputs:

1. **Severity (S):** How bad would the worst credible outcome be?
2. **Probability / Frequency (P or O):** How often is this expected to occur?
3. **Detectability / Safeguards (D):** Can we catch it before it manifests? *(Used in RPN-based methods)*
4. **Exposure / Population at Risk:** How many people/assets are exposed?

These four inputs will be explored mathematically in Section 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Flow of Criticality Analysis

The execution of Criticality Analysis follows a strict six-step logical sequence. KTU examiners frequently award 3–4 marks just for drawing/listing this sequence.

1. **Define the System Boundary:** Identify the equipment, node, or unit operation under study (e.g., "Reactor R-101 feed section").
2. **Identify Hazards / Failure Modes:** Use HAZOP, What-If, or Checklists to enumerate all deviations.
3. **Assign Severity Rating (S):** Score the consequence on a numerical scale (typically 1–10 or 1–5).
4. **Assign Probability / Occurrence Rating (O or P):** Score the expected frequency on a similar numerical scale.
5. **Compute Criticality / RPN:** Multiply or matrix the scores.
6. **Rank & Decide:** Sort descending; apply decision rules (e.g., RPN > 100 → immediate action).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why scale 1–10?** Because human subjective judgments are bounded; bounded scales reduce argument during team reviews.
- **Why multiply, not add?** Because multiplication reflects *joint probability intuition*: a "highly severe but extremely rare" event should rank lower than a "moderate but frequent" one — only multiplication captures this trade-off correctly.
- **Why include Detection (D)?** A failure that is *silent* (no warning, no alarm) is far more critical than one that triggers immediate DCS alarm. Detection scoring penalizes silent failures.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Complete Criticality Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-day cheat sheet**. Memorize it as a single unit. The vertical bars are written as `\vert` to ensure LaTeX-table compatibility.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Typical Scale |
|---|--------------|----------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | Basic Criticality | $C = S \times P$ | $S$ = Severity, $P$ = Probability | $S, P \in [1,10]$ |
| 2 | Risk Priority Number (RPN) | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | $S$=Severity, $O$=Occurrence, $D$=Detection | Each on $1$ to $10$ |
| 3 | Criticality Index (FMECA) | $C_{i} = \beta \times \alpha \times \lambda_{p} \times t$ | $\beta$ = conditional failure prob, $\alpha$=failure mode ratio, $\lambda_p$=part failure rate, $t$=mission time | per million hours |
| 4 | Dow's F&EI (Index) | $F\&EI = F_{M} \times M_{F}$ | $F_M$=Material Factor, $M_F$=Hazard Factor | dimensionless |
| 5 | Mond Index | $D = B \times \frac{1+\frac{M}{100}}{100} \times S \times E \times A$ | $B$=Substance Factor, $M$=Molar mass, $S$=State, $E$=Explosion, $A$=Activity | dimensionless |
| 6 | Consequence Severity | $C_{s} = \frac{M \times H_{c}}{d^{2}}$ | $M$=mass released, $H_c$=heat of combustion, $d$=distance | kW/m² |
| 7 | Individual Risk | $IR = \sum_{i} f_{i} \times p_{i}$ | $f_i$=frequency of outcome $i$, $p_i$=probability at point | probability/year |
| 8 | Societal Risk (F-N Curve) | $F(N) = \sum_{i : N_{i} \geq N} f_{i}$ | Cumulative frequency of $N$ or more fatalities | $F$ vs $N$ plot |

### Standard 1–10 Severity Scale (KTU Convention)

| Rating | Category | Description |
|:------:|----------|-------------|
| 10 | Catastrophic | Death, plant destruction, >\$10M loss |
| 9 | Extreme | Permanent disability, major spill |
| 8 | Major | Serious injury, OSHA recordable |
| 7 | High | Lost-time injury |
| 6 | Moderate | Medical treatment injury |
| 5 | Low | First-aid case |
| 4 | Minor | Discomfort, minor property damage |
| 3 | Slight | Minor irritation |
| 2 | Negligible | Cosmetic damage |
| 1 | None | No impact |

### Standard 1–10 Occurrence Scale

| Rating | Frequency (per item/year) | Description |
|:------:|:------------------------:|-------------|
| 10 | $\geq 1$ in 2 | Very high, failure almost inevitable |
| 7 | 1 in 100 | High, frequent failure |
| 5 | 1 in 2,000 | Moderate |
| 3 | 1 in 100,000 | Low |
| 1 | $\leq 1$ in 1,000,000 | Very low, virtually impossible |

### Standard 1–10 Detection Scale

| Rating | Detection Probability | Description |
|:------:|:--------------------:|-------------|
| 10 | $\geq 1$ in 2 | Almost impossible to detect |
| 7 | 1 in 100 | Low detectability |
| 5 | 1 in 2,000 | Moderate |
| 3 | 1 in 100,000 | High |
| 1 | $\leq 1$ in 1,000,000 | Almost certain, automatic control |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Criticality Analysis is the *engine of resource allocation*. In any real chemical plant, oil refinery, or pharmaceutical facility:

- **Maintenance Crew Prioritization:** A criticality-ranked FMEA tells the maintenance supervisor which pump overhaul is urgent vs. which can wait until the next shutdown.
- **Insurance Premium Calculation:** Insurers use the Dow F&EI and Mond Index outputs to set annual premiums.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 and Indian Factories Act (SPCB/CPCB audits) require documented criticality ranking as proof of PSM implementation.
- **ALARP Demonstration:** To show risks are "As Low As Reasonably Practicable," engineers must first *quantify* criticality — qualitative arguments alone do not satisfy regulators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the RPN Equation from First Principles

The Risk Priority Number is not an arbitrary construct — it is a logical consequence of combining three orthogonal measures of risk. Let us derive it rigorously.

### Step 1 — Define the "Loss Potential"

Let $L$ represent the expected loss (in monetary, human, or environmental units) from a single failure event. The expected loss per event is:

$$
L = S \times C_{unit}
$$

where $S$ is the severity score (normalized 1–10) and $C_{unit}$ is the conversion cost per severity point. For KTU problems, we treat $C_{unit}$ as absorbed into the scale.

### Step 2 — Define the "Annualized Frequency"

The expected number of times this failure occurs in one operational year is:

$$
F = O \times f_{unit}
$$

where $O$ is the occurrence score (normalized 1–10) and $f_{unit}$ is the base annual frequency per occurrence point.

### Step 3 — Expected Annual Risk

Risk is defined as the expected loss per unit time. Therefore:

$$
R_{annual} = L \times F
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute

$$
R_{annual} = (S \times C_{unit}) \times (O \times f_{unit}) = S \times O \times (C_{unit} \times f_{unit})
$$

### Step 5 — Introduce Detectability

If a failure can be detected and corrected *before* the consequence manifests, the actual realized risk is reduced. We introduce Detection $D$ such that high $D$ means *low* detectability (counter-intuitive but standard):

$$
R_{realized} = R_{annual} \times D = S \times O \times D \times (C_{unit} \times f_{unit})
$$

### Step 6 — Normalize

For ranking purposes, the constant $(C_{unit} \times f_{unit})$ cancels during comparison. Hence the dimensionless **RPN**:

$$
\boxed{RPN = S \times O \times D}
$$

This is the exact formula used globally in FMEA and adopted in KTU 2024 Scheme exam problems.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Criticality Calculation

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A centrifugal pump in a benzene service has been identified with five failure modes via FMEA. Severity, Occurrence, and Detection scores are tabulated below. Calculate the RPN for each mode and rank them for corrective action priority.

| Failure Mode | $S$ | $O$ | $D$ |
|--------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A — Mechanical seal leak | 7 | 4 | 3 |
| B — Bearing failure | 8 | 2 | 5 |
| C — Coupling misalignment | 5 | 6 | 4 |
| D — Casing corrosion | 9 | 3 | 6 |
| E — Impeller erosion | 6 | 5 | 7 |

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step A — Apply the RPN formula** $RPN = S \times O \times D$:

**Mode A:**
$$
RPN_{A} = 7 \times 4 \times 3 = 84
$$

**Mode B:**
$$
RPN_{B} = 8 \times 2 \times 5 = 80
$$

**Mode C:**
$$
RPN_{C} = 5 \times 6 \times 4 = 120
$$

**Mode D:**
$$
RPN_{D} = 9 \times 3 \times 6 = 162
$$

**Mode E:**
$$
RPN_{E} = 6 \times 5 \times 7 = 210
$$

**Step B — Rank in descending order of RPN:**

| Rank | Mode | RPN | Action Priority |
|:----:|------|:---:|----------------|
| 1 | E — Impeller erosion | 210 | Immediate engineering review |
| 2 | D — Casing corrosion | 162 | Schedule shutdown repair |
| 3 | C — Coupling misalignment | 120 | Plan corrective maintenance |
| 4 | A — Mechanical seal leak | 84 | Routine monitoring |
| 5 | B — Bearing failure | 80 | Routine monitoring |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Notice that Mode D has the *highest severity* (9), but Mode E has the *highest RPN*. This is because RPN captures the silent-failure penalty of Mode E ($D = 7$). This is a classic KTU exam pitfall — students often rank by Severity alone, which is wrong.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Criticality Index (FMECA Quantitative Form)

In MIL-STD-1629A (military FMEA), the criticality of a single failure mode is given by:

$$
C_{m} = \lambda_{p} \times \alpha \times \beta \times t
$$

where:
- $\lambda_{p}$ = failure rate of the part (failures per million hours)
- $\alpha$ = proportion of $\lambda_p$ that results in this specific failure mode (mode ratio)
- $\beta$ = conditional probability that this failure mode leads to the catastrophic effect
- $t$ = mission duration in hours

### Derivation:

Step 1 — The base failure rate $\lambda_p$ is obtained from OREDA or manufacturer data.

Step 2 — Only a fraction $\alpha$ of these failures occur in the mode of interest:

$$
\lambda_{mode} = \lambda_{p} \times \alpha
$$

Step 3 — Of these mode failures, only a fraction $\beta$ lead to the critical effect:

$$
\lambda_{critical} = \lambda_{p} \times \alpha \times \beta
$$

Step 4 — Over mission time $t$, the *expected number* of critical events is:

$$
C_{m} = \lambda_{p} \times \alpha \times \beta \times t
$$

### Numerical Example:

Given $\lambda_p = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hr, $\alpha = 0.3$, $\beta = 0.5$, $t = 8760$ hr (one year):

$$
\lambda_{critical} = (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.3 \times 0.5 = 7.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ failures/hr}
$$

$$
C_{m} = 7.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760 = 6.57 \times 10^{-3} \text{ critical events/year}
$$

Converting to expected events per 1000 years:

$$
C_{m} = 6.57
$$

This means approximately **6.57 catastrophic events are expected per 1000 operational years** from this failure mode — a typical input for QRA studies.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Criticality Methods

| Method | Type | Output | Best Suited For |
|--------|------|--------|-----------------|
| **FMEA (Qualitative)** | Qualitative | RPN (rank) | Mechanical/equipment focus |
| **FMECA (Quantitative)** | Quantitative | $C_m$ (events/yr) | High-consequence aerospace/nuclear |
| **HAZOP Criticality** | Semi-quantitative | Risk Matrix cell | Continuous chemical processes |
| **Dow F&EI** | Index-based | F&EI score | Fire/explosion inventory ranking |
| **Mond Index** | Index-based | D score | Toxic + fire + explosion |
| **LOPA** | Semi-quantitative | Frequency/yr | Layer of Protection analysis |
| **QRA / ETA / FTA** | Quantitative | Individual/Societal Risk | Onshore/Offshore siting |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Criticality Analysis — Master Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start System Boundary Definition] --> nodeB[Hazard and Failure Mode Identification]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Quantitative or Qualitative Method}
    nodeC --> nodeD[Assign Severity Score S]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Assign Occurrence Score O]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Assign Detection Score D]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Compute Criticality Index]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compare Against Threshold RPN greater than 100]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Rank Failure Modes by RPN]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Decision Point]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Accept Risk with Justification]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Implement Mitigation Controls]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Redesign Process or Equipment]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Re assess RPN after Action]
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO[Document and Close Loop]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every failure mode enters the system, is scored on three independent axes (S, O, D), is multiplied to yield RPN, is compared to a threshold (typically 100), and is either accepted, controlled, or redesigned. The re-assessment loop is the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** heart of PSM.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RPN-Based Criticality System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Equipment Database] --> engineA[Failure Mode Library]
    inputB[Incident History] --> engineA
    inputC[Process P and ID] --> engineA
    engineA --> engineB[Scoring Engine]
    subA[Subject Matter Experts] --> engineB
    engineB --> engineC[RPN Calculator]
    engineC --> engineD[Risk Matrix Plotter]
    engineD --> outputA[Prioritized Action List]
    engineD --> outputB[Maintenance Schedule]
    engineD --> outputC[Management Dashboard]
```

### Block Descriptions:

- **Equipment Database & P&ID inputs:** Provide the physical and operational context (temperatures, pressures, materials of construction, protective devices).
- **Failure Mode Library:** A corporate knowledge base — for a centrifugal pump, examples include seal leak, bearing failure, impeller erosion, coupling wear.
- **Scoring Engine:** A workshop-based tool where SMEs (operators, maintenance, process engineers) consensus-score $S$, $O$, $D$.
- **RPN Calculator:** Computes $RPN = S \times O \times D$ for each mode.
- **Risk Matrix Plotter:** Places each mode into a $S \times P$ grid (green/amber/red zones).
- **Outputs:** Action list, maintenance backlog, and management KPIs.

## 4.3 Risk / Criticality Matrix Visual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mat1[Risk Matrix Legend] --> cellA[Cell A Low Severity Low Probability GREEN Zone Accept]
    mat1 --> cellB[Cell B Low Severity Med Probability YELLOW Zone Monitor]
    mat1 --> cellC[Cell C Med Severity High Probability YELLOW Zone Controls]
    mat1 --> cellD[Cell D High Severity Low Probability YELLOW Zone ALARP]
    mat1 --> cellE[Cell E High Severity High Probability RED Zone Immediate Action]
```

### Color Coding Standard (KTU / CCPS):

| Probability \ Severity | Low (1–3) | Medium (4–6) | High (7–10) |
|:----------------------:|:----------:|:------------:|:-----------:|
| **High (7–10)** | YELLOW | YELLOW | RED |
| **Medium (4–6)** | GREEN | YELLOW | YELLOW |
| **Low (1–3)** | GREEN | GREEN | YELLOW |

- **GREEN:** Acceptable — routine monitoring.
- **YELLOW:** ALARP region — implement additional controls where reasonably practicable.
- **RED:** Unacceptable — operation must not continue without corrective action.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Criticality Analysis. How is it different from Hazard Identification?**

**Model Answer:**
Criticality Analysis is the systematic evaluation and ranking of identified hazards based on their severity and probability of occurrence, yielding a numerical priority (e.g., RPN) for decision-making.

**Difference from Hazard Identification:**

| Aspect | Hazard Identification | Criticality Analysis |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Question answered** | *What can go wrong?* | *How bad and how likely is it?* |
| **Output** | A list of scenarios | A ranked priority list |
| **Quantitative?** | Mostly qualitative | Semi-quantitative / quantitative |
| **Timing in PSM** | Step 1 | Step 2 (after identification) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing only the definition of *Hazard Identification* without contrasting it loses 1 mark. Always present a comparison table for full marks.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Write a short note on the Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA.**

**Model Answer:**
The RPN is the most widely used criticality metric in FMEA, defined as:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

where $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence (1–10), and $D$ = Detection (1–10). RPN values range from 1 to 1000. A conventional threshold of **RPN > 100** triggers mandatory corrective action. RPN allows ranking of failure modes so that limited engineering resources can be focused on the highest-priority items.

---

## Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, answer ONE full question. Each question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure for conducting a **Criticality Analysis** using FMEA. List the standard 1–10 scales used for Severity, Occurrence, and Detection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a pressure vessel in hydrocarbon service, the following failure modes were identified. Calculate the RPN for each and rank them. Recommend the top two for immediate action. **(7 Marks)**

| Failure Mode | Severity | Occurrence | Detection |
|--------------|:--------:|:----------:|:---------:|
| F1 — Corrosion under insulation | 8 | 5 | 6 |
| F2 — Weld defect at nozzle | 9 | 2 | 8 |
| F3 — Gasket blow-out | 7 | 4 | 5 |
| F4 — Overpressure event | 10 | 1 | 4 |
| F5 — External impact damage | 6 | 6 | 7 |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — FMEA Procedure & Scales:**

*Step 1:* Assemble a multidisciplinary team (process, mechanical, instrumentation, operations).

*Step 2:* Define the system boundary (e.g., "Pressure Vessel V-201, shell side").

*Step 3:* Identify all potential failure modes for each component using:
- Component manufacturer FMEA library
- Plant-specific incident history
- HAZOP / What-If brain-storming

*Step 4:* Score each failure mode:
- **Severity (S):** Effect on personnel, environment, asset (1 = none → 10 = catastrophic death/multiple fatality).
- **Occurrence (O):** Likelihood of the failure happening (1 = very rare → 10 = very frequent).
- **Detection (D):** Probability that the failure *will not* be detected before consequence (1 = almost certain detection → 10 = almost impossible to detect).

*Step 5:* Compute RPN = S × O × D.

*Step 6:* Compare against threshold (typically 100). Items above threshold → mandatory action plan with target date and owner.

*Step 7:* Re-score after action; document closure.

**Part (b) — RPN Computation:**

**F1:** Corrosion under insulation
$$
RPN_{F1} = 8 \times 5 \times 6 = 240
$$

**F2:** Weld defect at nozzle
$$
RPN_{F2} = 9 \times 2 \times 8 = 144
$$

**F3:** Gasket blow-out
$$
RPN_{F3} = 7 \times 4 \times 5 = 140
$$

**F4:** Overpressure event
$$
RPN_{F4} = 10 \times 1 \times 4 = 40
$$

**F5:** External impact damage
$$
RPN_{F5} = 6 \times 6 \times 7 = 252
$$

**Ranking Table:**

| Rank | Failure Mode | RPN |
|:----:|--------------|:---:|
| 1 | F5 — External impact damage | 252 |
| 2 | F1 — Corrosion under insulation | 240 |
| 3 | F2 — Weld defect at nozzle | 144 |
| 4 | F3 — Gasket blow-out | 140 |
| 5 | F4 — Overpressure event | 40 |

**Recommendation:** F5 (External impact damage) and F1 (Corrosion under insulation) require **immediate engineering review** — F5 because of high detectability failure (no routine inspection of impact zone), and F1 because corrosion under insulation is a silent, high-severity failure mode that has caused numerous refinery fires historically.

---

### Question B (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Risk / Criticality Matrix**. Describe how it is used in process industries for risk acceptance decisions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A reactor cooling water pump has the failure data below. Calculate the Criticality Index $C_m = \lambda_p \times \alpha \times \beta \times t$ for one mission year (8760 hours). Comment on whether the resulting risk is acceptable if the corporate threshold is 0.01 critical events/year. **(7 Marks)**

- Failure rate of pump: $\lambda_p = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hr
- Mode ratio (seal failure): $\alpha = 0.40$
- Conditional probability to critical effect: $\beta = 0.25$
- Mission time: $t = 8760$ hr

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Risk Matrix:**

The Risk (or Criticality) Matrix is a two-dimensional plot with **Severity on the vertical axis** (Low to Catastrophic) and **Probability/Frequency on the horizontal axis** (Very Low to Very High). Each cell is colour-coded:

- **GREEN (low risk):** Acceptable, document the decision.
- **YELLOW (medium risk):** ALARP region — apply additional controls if reasonably practicable.
- **RED (high risk):** Unacceptable — operation must halt until risk is reduced.

**Use in Process Industries:**

1. **Risk Acceptance:** Plant management uses the matrix as the *gate-keeper* — no operation continues in a RED cell.
2. **Resource Allocation:** Engineering budget is targeted at items in YELLOW moving them toward GREEN.
3. **Communication Tool:** A simple visual that non-technical stakeholders (board, public) can interpret.
4. **Audit Trail:** Documents the *basis of safety* for regulators.

**Part (b) — Criticality Index Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Mode-specific failure rate:**
$$
\lambda_{mode} = \lambda_p \times \alpha = (8 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.40 = 3.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Critical-effect failure rate:**
$$
\lambda_{critical} = \lambda_{mode} \times \beta = (3.2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.25 = 8.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ /hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual Criticality Index:**
$$
C_m = \lambda_{critical} \times t = (8.0 \times 10^{-7}) \times 8760 = 7.008 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with threshold:**
$$
C_m = 7.008 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /yr} < 0.01 \text{ /yr} \quad \therefore \text{Risk is ACCEPTABLE}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment:**
The calculated criticality (0.007/yr) is below the corporate threshold (0.01/yr), so the risk is acceptable. However, this is a *reactor cooling water pump* — its loss could lead to a thermal runaway. A factor-of-safety review and additional IPL (Independent Protection Layer) such as a redundant pump or temperature interlock is recommended to demonstrate ALARP.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Writing $\lambda_p$ in /hr but $t$ in years without converting — instant 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Threshold confusion:** Using 0.01 *per million hours* instead of per year — wrong by orders of magnitude.
> 3. **Skipping the comparison step:** Calculation alone is not enough — you must explicitly state *Acceptable* or *Not Acceptable*.
> 4. **Drawing the matrix upside-down:** Severity goes on **Y-axis (vertical)**, Probability on **X-axis (horizontal)**.
> 5. **Forgetting ALARP:** Even if risk is below threshold, KTU expects an ALARP comment for full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Criticality Analysis = Severity × Probability → numerical priority ranking.
- **Core Equation (Memorize First):** $RPN = S \times O \times D$ where each is on a 1–10 scale.
- **Threshold Rule of Thumb:** $RPN \geq 100$ → mandatory corrective action; $RPN < 30$ → routine monitoring.
- **Three Orthogonal Inputs:** $S$ (consequence), $O$ (likelihood), $D$ (detectability) — never collapse to two dimensions in FMEA.
- **FMECA Quantitative Form:** $C_m = \lambda_p \times \alpha \times \beta \times t$ — used for nuclear, aerospace, and high-consequence systems.
- **Risk Matrix Axes:** Severity on **Y-axis**, Probability on **X-axis**; GREEN/AMBER/RED zones.
- **ALARP is Mandatory:** Even acceptable risks per matrix must demonstrate "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" reduction.
- **Methods Compared:**
  - *FMEA* → equipment reliability focus
  - *HAZOP Criticality* → process deviation focus
  - *Dow F&EI / Mond* → fire/explosion/toxic inventory ranking
  - *LOPA / QRA* → high-stakes quantitative risk
- **Common Student Errors:** (1) Ranking by Severity alone, (2) ignoring Detection, (3) unit mismatch, (4) forgetting to re-score after mitigation.
- **Syllabus Keywords for KTU Exam Index:** *Criticality Index, RPN, Severity-Occurrence-Detection, Criticality Matrix, ALARP, FMECA.*
- **One-Line Takeaway:** Criticality Analysis transforms an unstructured list of *what ifs* into a prioritized, budget-defensible action plan that satisfies regulators and saves lives.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrective Action and Follow-up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corrective Action and Follow-up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Corrective Action

In the framework of **Occupational Health and Safety Management Systems (OHSMS)** — particularly those aligned with **ISO 45001:2018**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1910/1926**, and the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment — a **Corrective Action (CA)** is formally defined as:

> A documented, planned, and time-bound intervention implemented to **eliminate the root cause(s)** of a detected non-conformity, hazard, unsafe condition, unsafe act, near-miss, or incident, in order to **prevent recurrence** and to **restore the system to a state of conformance** with the established safety standards, procedures, and regulatory requirements.

The official ISO 45001 clause **10.2 (Incident, nonconformity and corrective action)** mandates that corrective action must follow a structured sequence:
**Identify → Document → Investigate → Plan → Implement → Verify → Review.**

A **Preventive Action**, in contrast, is a separate class of action taken to address *potential* non-conformities *before* they manifest as actual incidents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Corrective action is *not* the act of "fixing" the immediate problem (this is called a **Correction**). It is the act of *eliminating the root cause* so that the problem does not reappear. Many students lose marks by confusing the two.

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Follow-up

**Follow-up** is the systematic, scheduled, and documented process of **verifying the effectiveness** of an implemented corrective action. It involves:

- Re-inspection of the affected area, equipment, or process.
- Measurement of performance indicators (lagging and leading).
- Confirmation that the root cause has been neutralised.
- Closure of the corrective action record upon successful verification.
- Escalation to senior management if verification fails.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Follow-up is the *closing loop* activity of the safety management cycle. Without follow-up, a corrective action is merely an *intention* and not a *control*.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a patient visits a doctor with a recurring migraine.

| Doctor's Step | Safety Management Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient reports pain (symptom) | Incident / Unsafe condition reported |
| Doctor investigates triggers (root cause) | Root Cause Analysis (5-Why, Fishbone) |
| Doctor prescribes long-term treatment | **Corrective Action** (engineering control) |
| Doctor prescribes immediate painkiller | Correction (short-term bandage) |
| Patient returns next month for review | **Follow-up** / Verification of effectiveness |

The **corrective action** addresses the *cause* (e.g., "stop eating aged cheese"). The **follow-up** confirms that the migraine is no longer recurring. Without follow-up, the doctor would never know if the prescription was useful.

> [!TIP]
> Think of Corrective Action + Follow-up as a **safety contract**: *"I did something → I will check if it worked → I will close the case only after I see results."*

## 1.4 Why Corrective Action and Follow-up Matter in Engineering

In process plants, construction sites, and manufacturing floors, the absence of robust corrective action and follow-up is the **#1 reason for repeated accidents**. Statistical analysis of industrial incident databases shows that approximately **70%–80% of industrial accidents are repeat incidents** — meaning a similar hazard had been identified before but never properly closed out.

Standard metrics tracked in industry:

- **CAR Closure Rate** = (Closed CARs / Total CARs raised) × 100%
- **Average Closure Time** in days
- **Recurrence Rate** of the same hazard within 12 months
- **Effectiveness Ratio** = (Verified effective actions / Total verified actions) × 100%
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Corrective Actions (HCA)

A core concept in KTU Module 2 is the **Hierarchy of Controls** used to design corrective actions. It is ordered from **most effective** to **least effective**, and KTU examiners frequently ask students to *rank* controls.

| Rank | Control Level | Description | Effectiveness | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Elimination** | Physically remove the hazard | Highest (100%) | Replace a toxic solvent with water-based cleaner |
| 2 | **Substitution** | Replace with a less hazardous alternative | Very High (~90%) | Use a lower-voltage battery pack |
| 3 | **Engineering Control** | Isolate people from the hazard | High (60%–90%) | Machine guarding, ventilation, interlocks |
| 4 | **Administrative Control** | Change work procedures | Moderate (30%–60%) | Training, signage, job rotation, permits |
| 5 | **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** | Protect the worker with barriers | Lowest (10%–30%) | Safety helmet, gloves, respirators |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** PPE is the *last* resort, not the *first*. Many students mistakenly rank PPE as the best solution. A corrective action that *only* provides PPE will score poorly in an examiner's view because it does not address the source.

## 2.2 The Corrective Action Cycle (PDCA-Aligned)

The corrective action and follow-up process is fundamentally a **closed-loop** system, derived from the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA / Deming Cycle)**.

- **P — Plan:** Investigate the incident, identify root cause, draft the Corrective Action Report (CAR).
- **D — Do:** Implement the action. Assign owner, deadline, and resources.
- **C — Check:** Verify effectiveness. Measure risk reduction. Conduct follow-up audits.
- **A — Act:** If effective → standardise and close. If not → return to Plan (re-investigate).

## 2.3 Roles and Responsibilities (RACI)

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Safety Officer / EHS Manager** | Logs the CAR, coordinates investigation, follows up |
| **Line Supervisor / Engineer** | Implements the action on the floor |
| **Top Management** | Approves budget, reviews closure reports |
| **Workers** | Comply with new procedures, report residual hazards |

## 2.4 Documentation: The Corrective Action Report (CAR)

A standard CAR contains:
1. CAR Number & Date Raised
2. Source (Audit, Incident, Near-miss, Inspection, Worker complaint)
3. Description of Non-Conformity
4. Root Cause Analysis (5-Why / Fishbone)
5. Proposed Corrective Action
6. **Risk Rating Before Action** $R_{before}$
7. **Risk Rating After Action** $R_{after}$
8. Action Owner
9. Target Closure Date
10. Verification Method
11. Verification Date and Sign-off
12. Status (Open / Closed / Verified)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Score** | $R = S \times L$ | Dimensionless (1–25 or 1–125) |
| **Residual Risk** | $R_{r} = S \times L_{new}$ | $L_{new}$ is likelihood *after* CA |
| **Risk Reduction %** | $\eta_{R} = \dfrac{R_{before} - R_{after}}{R_{before}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |
| **Cost of Inaction** | $C_{in} = P_{acc} \times C_{acc}$ | Monetary (₹ / $) |
| **Safety ROI** | $ROI_{S} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{CA}}{C_{CA}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |
| **CAR Closure Rate** | $CCR = \dfrac{N_{closed}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |
| **On-Time Closure Rate** | $OTCR = \dfrac{N_{on\text{-}time}}{N_{closed}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |
| **Recurrence Rate** | $RR = \dfrac{N_{repeat}}{N_{total\;12m}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |
| **ALARP Condition** | $\text{Risk} \leq \text{As Low As Reasonably Practicable}$ | Boolean test |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Markdown Tables:** All absolute-value bars `|x|` are written as `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to prevent breaking the markdown table parser. For example: $\vert S \cdot L \vert$ is valid in LaTeX but unsafe in a table cell.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Corrective action and follow-up systems are mandatory in:

- **ISO 45001:2018** certified companies (Clause 10.2).
- **Process Safety Management (PSM)** under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119.
- **Hazard and Operability (HAZOP)** follow-up studies.
- **Construction safety** (BIS, IS 18001).
- **KTU B.Tech project work** — every engineering project risk register requires a closure loop.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantitative Risk Reduction Verification

A key KTU numerical problem involves **verifying the effectiveness** of a corrective action by computing the percentage drop in risk score.

### Given (Standard KTU Numerical)

- A chemical plant had a **Likelihood (L) = 5** and **Severity (S) = 4** before the corrective action.
- After installing an **engineering control (interlock system)**, the likelihood dropped to **L = 2**. Severity remains **S = 4**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Risk Score Before Corrective Action:**

$$
R_{before} = S \times L_{before}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
R_{before} = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Risk Score After Corrective Action:**

$$
R_{after} = S \times L_{after}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
R_{after} = 4 \times 2 = 8
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR):**

$$
ARR = R_{before} - R_{after}
$$

$$
ARR = 20 - 8 = 12
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Percentage Risk Reduction (Effectiveness $\eta_R$):**

$$
\eta_{R} = \frac{R_{before} - R_{after}}{R_{before}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{R} = \frac{20 - 8}{20} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{R} = \frac{12}{20} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_{R} = 0.60 \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{R} = 60\%}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (KTU Board expects this line):**

The corrective action has reduced the risk score by **60%**, which is a **substantial reduction**. Since the residual risk is now $R_{after} = 8$ (Medium band in the standard $5 \times 5$ matrix), the action is **partially effective** but does not achieve ALARP. A second-tier administrative control (e.g., training) is recommended to drive $L$ below 1.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cost-Benefit Justification of Corrective Action

### Given

- Probability of accident in next year without action: $P_{acc} = 0.05$ (5%).
- Expected cost of accident (medical, legal, downtime): $C_{acc} = \text{₹ } 50{,}00{,}000$.
- Cost of installing engineering control: $C_{CA} = \text{₹ } 8{,}00{,}000$.
- Action reduces probability to $P_{new} = 0.01$ (1%).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Cost of Inaction (Expected Annual Loss):**

$$
C_{in} = P_{acc} \times C_{acc}
$$

$$
C_{in} = 0.05 \times 50{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{in} = \text{₹ } 2{,}50{,}000}
$$

**Step 2 — Expected Annual Loss After Action:**

$$
C_{after} = P_{new} \times C_{acc}
$$

$$
C_{after} = 0.01 \times 50{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{after} = \text{₹ } 50{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Net Annual Savings:**

$$
S_{net} = C_{in} - C_{after}
$$

$$
S_{net} = 2{,}50{,}000 - 50{,}000
$$

$$
S_{net} = \text{₹ } 2{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Payback Period (in years):**

$$
T_{pb} = \frac{C_{CA}}{S_{net}}
$$

$$
T_{pb} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{pb} = 4 \; \text{years}}
$$

**Step 5 — Safety ROI over 10 years:**

$$
ROI_{S} = \frac{(S_{net} \times 10) - C_{CA}}{C_{CA}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
ROI_{S} = \frac{(2{,}00{,}000 \times 10) - 8{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
ROI_{S} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
ROI_{S} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{ROI_{S} = 150\%}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision (KTU answer):**

Since the **Safety ROI is positive (150%)** and the **payback period (4 years) is well within the equipment life (10 years)**, the corrective action is **justified on both safety and economic grounds**, satisfying the **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** principle.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effectiveness vs. Cost of Controls

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU topic** — examiners love asking students to *justify* which corrective action to choose.

| Control Type | Effectiveness $\eta_R$ | Relative Cost $C_{rel}$ | Sustainability | Worker Acceptance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Elimination | 100% | High (one-time) | Permanent | Very High |
| Substitution | 80%–90% | Medium-High | Long-term | High |
| Engineering Control | 60%–90% | High (capital) | Long-term | High |
| Administrative Control | 30%–60% | Low | Depends on supervision | Medium |
| PPE | 10%–30% | Low | Perishable | Low (often misused) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Strategy:** Always recommend a *combination* of controls (e.g., Engineering + Administrative + PPE). A single PPE-only action is rarely accepted by KTU examiners as a "complete" corrective action.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Corrective Action and Follow-up Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident or Non-Conformity Detected] --> B[Log the Issue in CAR Register]
    B --> C[Conduct Root Cause Analysis: 5-Why or Fishbone]
    C --> D[Identify Root Cause: NOT Symptom]
    D --> E[Select Corrective Action from Hierarchy of Controls]
    E --> F[Assign Owner, Deadline, and Budget]
    F --> G[Implement the Corrective Action: Do]
    G --> H[Initial Correction: Immediate Band-Aid]
    H --> I[Long-term Corrective Action: Root Cause Fix]
    I --> J[Schedule Follow-up Audit: Check]
    J --> K{Is the Action Effective?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Document Evidence of Effectiveness]
    L --> M[Standardise the New Procedure]
    M --> N[Close the CAR: Verified]
    N --> O[Feed Lessons Learned to Training]
    K -- No --> P[Re-Investigate: Return to Plan]
    P --> C
    O --> Q[Continuous Improvement Loop]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Corrective Action Selection (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Hazard Identified] --> H2{Can we Eliminate it completely?}
    H2 -- Yes --> H3[Action: Elimination - Most Preferred]
    H2 -- No --> H4{Can we Substitute with safer alternative?}
    H4 -- Yes --> H5[Action: Substitution]
    H4 -- No --> H6{Can we Engineer an isolation or guard?}
    H6 -- Yes --> H7[Action: Engineering Control]
    H6 -- No --> H8{Can we change the work procedure?}
    H8 -- Yes --> H9[Action: Administrative Control]
    H8 -- No --> H10[Action: PPE - Last Resort Only]
    H3 --> H11[Verify and Close]
    H5 --> H11
    H7 --> H11
    H9 --> H11
    H10 --> H11
```

## 4.3 Follow-up Verification Cycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Inputs: CAR, Action Plan, Owner] --> Process1[Process: Implementation]
    Process1 --> Output1[Output: Modified Workplace]
    Output1 --> Measure[Measurement: Risk Score, Behavioural Observation]
    Measure --> Compare{Compare Against Baseline}
    Compare -- Improved --> Verify[Verified Effective]
    Compare -- Same --> Fail1[Not Effective: Re-plan]
    Compare -- Worse --> Fail2[New Hazard Created: Immediate Stop]
    Verify --> Close[Close CAR and Document Lessons]
    Fail1 --> Process1
    Fail2 --> Process1
    Close --> KM[Knowledge Management: Update SOPs and Training]
```

## 4.4 Roles and Communication Flow in Follow-up

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Worker[Worker Reports Hazard] --> Supervisor[Line Supervisor Logs CAR]
    Supervisor --> EHS[EHS Officer Triages]
    EHS --> RCA[Root Cause Team Analyses]
    RCA --> Mgmt[Management Approves Budget]
    Mgmt --> Owner[Action Owner Implements]
    Owner --> Followup[EHS Conducts Follow-up Audit]
    Followup --> Decision{Effective?}
    Decision -- Yes --> Close[CAR Closed and Filed]
    Decision -- No --> RCA
    Close --> Review[Annual Management Review]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between "Correction" and "Corrective Action" with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Correction | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Eliminate the *symptom* | Eliminate the *root cause* |
| Time-frame | Immediate, short-term | Long-term, planned |
| Example | Cleaning an oil spill from the floor | Installing a drip tray and modifying the pipe joint to stop the leak |
| Focus | Restoring normalcy | Preventing recurrence |

*(1 mark for definition of correction, 1 mark for definition of corrective action, 1 mark for a relevant industrial example.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three key elements that should be included in a Corrective Action Report (CAR).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Description of the non-conformity** and the source (audit, incident, near-miss). *(1 mark)*
2. **Root cause analysis** using 5-Why or Fishbone technique. *(1 mark)*
3. **Action plan with owner, deadline, and verification method**, along with before/after risk score. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Warning:** Students often write *"training"* as a corrective action without specifying *what* training, *who* will conduct it, and *how* its effectiveness will be measured. This loses 1 mark easily.

---

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Hierarchy of Controls** used in designing corrective actions. Rank the controls from most effective to least effective and justify the ranking with one engineering example each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A workshop had a recurring hand-injury incident. Before corrective action, **Severity = 4, Likelihood = 5**. After installing a guard and training, **Severity = 4, Likelihood = 1**. Calculate the **percentage risk reduction** and comment on whether the action meets the **ALARP** criterion.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Hierarchy of Controls (7 Marks)

The **Hierarchy of Controls** is a systematic framework used by safety professionals to select the most effective corrective action. It is ordered by *reliability* and *sustainability*:

**1. Elimination (Most Effective) — 2 Marks**
- Physically removes the hazard from the workplace.
- *Engineering example:* Replacing a hazardous manual cleaning process with an automated CIP (Clean-In-Place) system in a pharmaceutical plant. The chemical exposure is completely removed.

**2. Substitution — 1.5 Marks**
- Replaces the hazard with a less dangerous alternative.
- *Engineering example:* Substituting a cyanide-based plating solution with a non-cyanide alkaline zinc plating bath.

**3. Engineering Control — 1.5 Marks**
- Isolates the worker from the hazard using physical devices.
- *Engineering example:* Installing machine guarding, interlock systems, local exhaust ventilation, or pressure-relief valves.

**4. Administrative Control — 1 Mark**
- Changes the way people work.
- *Engineering example:* Job rotation to limit cumulative exposure; permit-to-work systems for confined-space entry.

**5. PPE (Least Effective) — 1 Mark**
- Protects the worker with a personal barrier.
- *Engineering example:* Providing safety helmets, gloves, ear-plugs, or respirators.

> **Justification of ranking:** Higher levels are more reliable because they *do not depend on human behaviour*. PPE requires the worker to wear it correctly every single time, which is statistically the most failure-prone control.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical on Risk Reduction (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Risk Score Before Action:**

$$
R_{before} = S \times L_{before} = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Risk Score After Action:**

$$
R_{after} = S \times L_{after} = 4 \times 1 = 4
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Absolute Risk Reduction:**

$$
ARR = R_{before} - R_{after} = 20 - 4 = 16
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Percentage Risk Reduction:**

$$
\eta_{R} = \frac{ARR}{R_{before}} \times 100\% = \frac{16}{20} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{R} = 80\%}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — ALARP Comment (1 Mark):**

Since the residual risk is now $R_{after} = 4$ (Low band in the standard $5 \times 5$ matrix) and the reduction is substantial (**80%**), the corrective action **satisfies ALARP**. No further controls are reasonably practicable.

**Step 6 — Recommendation (1 Mark):**

The action uses a *combination* of controls (engineering + administrative), which is a good practice. However, the action should be **followed up** by a quarterly safety audit and behaviour-based observation to ensure the guard is not bypassed and the training is being applied.

---

### Question B [Internal Choice — Alternative 14-Mark Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **Corrective Action Report (CAR)**? Draw a neat flow diagram of the corrective action and follow-up cycle and explain each step in detail.
