**(b) [7 Marks]** A factory had **30 Corrective Actions raised** in 2023. **24 were closed on time** and **22 were verified as effective**. Of the closed ones, **6 were repeat hazards** that had been raised in 2022 as well. Calculate the **CAR Closure Rate**, **On-Time Closure Rate**, **Effectiveness Ratio**, and **Recurrence Rate**. Comment on the safety performance.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — CAR and Follow-up Cycle (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):** A **Corrective Action Report (CAR)** is a formal document used to record a non-conformity, its root cause, the action taken, the owner responsible, the target date, and the verification of effectiveness. It is a mandatory requirement under **ISO 45001 Clause 10.2** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1903.19**.

**Flow Diagram (2 Marks):** *(Student must reproduce the workflow from Section 4.1: Identify → Log → Investigate → Plan → Implement → Verify → Close → Lessons Learned.)*

**Step-by-step Explanation (3 Marks — 1 mark each for any three steps):**

1. **Identification and Logging:** The hazard, incident, or non-conformity is documented in the central CAR register with a unique number and date.
2. **Root Cause Analysis (RCA):** A cross-functional team applies techniques like **5-Why Analysis** or **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** to identify the *systemic* cause rather than the *symptom*.
3. **Action Planning and Implementation:** The chosen control (from the Hierarchy) is assigned to a named owner with a deadline, budget, and verification method.
4. **Follow-up Verification:** After implementation, the EHS officer conducts a re-inspection, measures risk indicators, and either *closes* the CAR or *reopens* it for further action.
5. **Closure and Standardisation:** Upon successful verification, the new procedure is written into the SOP, workers are retrained, and the lessons learned are circulated.

---

#### Part (b) — Safety Performance Metrics (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Total CARs raised: $N_{total} = 30$
- Closed on time: $N_{on\text{-}time} = 24$
- Verified effective: $N_{eff} = 22$
- Repeat hazards: $N_{repeat} = 6$

**Step 1 — CAR Closure Rate (2 Marks):**

Assuming all 30 are eventually closed (standard KTU assumption):

$$
CCR = \frac{N_{closed}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{30}{30} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{CCR = 100\%}
$$

**Step 2 — On-Time Closure Rate (2 Marks):**

$$
OTCR = \frac{N_{on\text{-}time}}{N_{closed}} \times 100\% = \frac{24}{30} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{OTCR = 80\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Effectiveness Ratio (1 Mark):**

$$
ER = \frac{N_{eff}}{N_{closed}} \times 100\% = \frac{22}{30} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{ER = 73.33\%}
$$

**Step 4 — Recurrence Rate (1 Mark):**

$$
RR = \frac{N_{repeat}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{6}{30} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{RR = 20\%}
$$

**Step 5 — Examiner's Comment (1 Mark):**

The factory has an **excellent closure rate (100%)** and a **reasonable on-time performance (80%)**. However, the **recurrence rate of 20%** is a **serious red flag** — it indicates that **root causes are not being identified correctly** and only *symptoms* are being treated. Management should invest in **formal RCA training** and **cross-functional investigation teams**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Correction with Corrective Action** — loses 1–2 marks in every short note question.
> 2. **Writing PPE as the *primary* corrective action** — examiners dock 1 mark for ignoring the Hierarchy of Controls.
> 3. **Skipping the ALARP comment** in numerical problems — full marks for calculation but **0.5–1 mark lost** without interpretation.
> 4. **Forgetting the Follow-up step** in flow diagrams — examiners specifically check if the *closing loop* is drawn.
> 5. **Using `|x|` (pipe symbol) inside markdown table cells** for absolute value — the table breaks in the digital answer sheet. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Corrective Action** eliminates the *root cause*; **Correction** only fixes the *symptom*.
- **Follow-up** is the verification step that closes the safety management loop — no follow-up means no closure.
- The **Hierarchy of Controls** ranks: **Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE**.
- The corrective action cycle is based on **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)**.
- A **Corrective Action Report (CAR)** must contain: non-conformity description, RCA, action, owner, deadline, and verification evidence.
- **Risk Reduction Formula:** $\eta_R = \dfrac{R_{before} - R_{after}}{R_{before}} \times 100\%$.
- **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the gold standard for accepting residual risk.
- A **good corrective action** combines Engineering + Administrative + PPE, not PPE alone.
- A **recurrence rate > 10%** indicates poor root-cause analysis.
- **ISO 45001 Clause 10.2** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1903.19** are the two regulatory anchors most cited in KTU answers.
- The **5-Why** and **Fishbone (Ishikawa)** diagrams are the most frequently tested RCA tools in KTU exams.
- **Always end numerical answers with a one-line interpretation** — KTU examiners award the final 1 mark for this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Source Models - Discharge Rate Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Source Models - Discharge Rate Models ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Source Models (Discharge Rate Models)** are mathematical and thermodynamic formulations used in process safety engineering to quantify the **rate, duration, and total quantity** of hazardous material released from process equipment (vessels, storage tanks, pipelines, reactors) when containment integrity is compromised. They form the foundational layer of **consequence analysis** in Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA). In the context of KTU's PBSFT404 framework, Source Models are the **first computational block** in the classical Source–Pathway–Receptor (S–P–R) hazard analysis chain: $$ \boxed{\text{Source (Release)} \longrightarrow \text{Pathway (Dispersion)} \longrightarrow \text{Receptor (Effect)}} $$ A *discharge rate model* mathematically translates an initiating event (corrosion hole, pump seal failure, catastrophic rupture, valve opening, relief device actuation) into a deterministic or probabilistic **mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ (kg/s)** or **volumetric flow rate $Q$ (m³/s)** as a function of time. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Bucket" Intuition Imagine a pressurized LPG cylinder kept in your kitchen. Now think of three scenarios: 1. **A pinprick hole** near the top → gas hisses out slowly and steadily (choked or subsonic gas flow). 2. **A large hole at the bottom** below the liquid level → liquid gushes out as a continuous stream (Bernoulli-type liquid flow). 3. **A medium hole where flashing occurs** → a frothy white cloud emerges (two-phase flashing flow). Each scenario has a **different mathematical equation**. Source Models are exactly the equations that predict how fast material leaves in each of these three cases. The *release rate* determines how big the downstream hazard (vapor cloud, fireball, BLEVE, toxicity zone) will be — **no accurate source model = no accurate consequence analysis.** --- ### 1.3 Classification of Discharge Scenarios | # | Scenario | Driving Force | Primary Phase | |---|----------|---------------|---------------| | 1 | Liquid through hole in vessel | Hydrostatic head | Pure liquid | | 2 | Gas/Vapor through hole in vessel | Internal pressure (P) | Pure gas | | 3 | Two-phase flashing flow | Pressure > saturation pressure | Liquid + Vapor | | 4 | Liquid through long pipe | Pressure drop + friction | Pure liquid | | 5 | Gas through long pipe | Pressure drop + friction + heat transfer | Pure gas | > [!NOTE] > **Constant of Note:** Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101325 \, \text{Pa} = 1.01325 \, \text{bar}$. This value appears as the *back-pressure boundary condition* in essentially every discharge model. --- ### 1.4 Why Discharge Rate Models Matter in KTU Context In the **2024 Scheme**, this topic carries direct marks in: - **Part A (3 marks):** Identification of release type and Bernoulli-based flow. - **Part B (14 marks):** Full derivation and numerical computation of release rate for a vessel or pipeline scenario, including determination of sonic vs. subsonic flow regimes. These questions typically test **two cognitive outcomes**: - **CO3:** Apply source and dispersion models for consequence analysis. - **CO4:** Evaluate risk using quantified hazard outcomes. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Governing Physics — Bernoulli's Principle as the Foundation All discharge rate models (with the exception of very complex non-equilibrium flashing flows) are derived from a single first-principles law: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Steady-State Mechanical Energy Balance (Bernoulli Equation) for Real Fluid:** > The total mechanical energy per unit mass of fluid is conserved between an upstream point (1) inside the vessel and a downstream point (2) at the hole exit — minus frictional and form losses. Mathematically: $$ \frac{P_1}{\rho} + g z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2} \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho} + g z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2} \;+\; \sum F_{\text{losses}} $$ where $\sum F_{\text{losses}}$ accounts for **friction** ($K_f$) and **exit contraction** ($K_c$) losses. --- ### 2.2 STEP-WISE LOGIC BREAKDOWN OF DISCHARGE CALCULATION #### **Step A — Identify the release phase and geometry** Before any equation, classify: - **Liquid** (sub-cooled or saturated)? - **Gas/Vapor** (ideal or real)? - **Two-phase flashing** (pressure-driven vaporization)? #### **Step B — Compare upstream pressure to critical pressure** Define the **critical pressure ratio**: $$ \boxed{P_{c} = P_{\text{critical}} = \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k}{k-1}}} $$ where $k = c_p / c_v$ = **specific heat ratio** of the gas. - If $\;\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_1} \le P_c \;\Rightarrow\;$ **Sonic (Choked) Flow** — flow rate limited by sonic velocity; pressure downstream of constriction has NO effect. - If $\;\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_1} > P_c \;\Rightarrow\;$ **Subsonic Flow** — flow depends on both upstream and downstream pressures. > For **air** ($k = 1.4$): $P_c = 0.528$. For **methane** ($k = 1.31$): $P_c \approx 0.54$. #### **Step C — Apply the appropriate discharge equation** Choose from the formula sheet below based on the phase and flow regime identified. #### **Step D — Apply discharge coefficient $C_D$** Real holes are NEVER ideal orifices. Apply: $$ C_D = C_c \cdot C_v $$ where: - $C_c$ = **coefficient of contraction** (accounts for vena contracta) - $C_v$ = **coefficient of velocity** (accounts for friction) **Typical KTU-acceptable values:** - Sharp-edged orifice: $C_D \approx 0.61$ - Rounded orifice: $C_D \approx 0.97$ to $0.99$ - Short pipe ($L/D \le 3$): $C_D \approx 0.81$ - Long pipe ($L/D > 3$): use full Darcy–Weisbach friction model #### **Step E — Compute total release quantity (integration)** $$ m_{\text{total}} = \int_0^{t_{\text{release}}} \dot{m}(t)\, dt $$ For non-depleting sources (very large vessels, regulated gas supplies), $\dot{m}$ is approximately constant. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **exam-day reference** for every Source Model problem. Memorize the boxed expressions. | # | Release Type | Governing Equation | Regime Condition | Standard Units | |---|--------------|--------------------|------------------|----------------| | **1** | **Liquid through hole (Bernoulli)** | $\displaystyle Q_L = C_D \, A_h \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,(P_1 - P_{atm})}{\rho_L} + 2\,g\,h_L}$ | All pressures, gravity-driven | $Q_L$ in m³/s; $h_L$ in m | | **1a** | Liquid mass flow | $\dot{m}_L = \rho_L \, Q_L = C_D \, A_h \, \sqrt{2\,\rho_L\,(P_1 - P_{atm}) + 2\,\rho_L^2 \, g\, h_L}$ | — | kg/s | | **2** | **Gas through hole — Subsonic** | $\displaystyle \dot{m}_g = C_D \, A_h \, P_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,M}{R\,T_1}\,\dfrac{k}{k-1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_1}\right)^{2/k} - \left(\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_1}\right)^{(k+1)/k}\right]}$ | $\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_1} > P_c$ | kg/s | | **3** | **Gas through hole — Sonic (Choked)** | $\displaystyle \dot{m}_g = C_D \, A_h \, P_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{k\,M}{R\,T_1}\left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)}}$ | $\dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_1} \le P_c$ | kg/s | | **4** | **Two-phase flashing (HEM)** | $\displaystyle \dot{m}_{2\phi} = C_D \, A_h \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,(P_1 - P_{atm})\, \rho_{\text{mix}}}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\dot{m}_{v,eq}}{\dot{m}_{l,eq}}\right)\, \dfrac{v_{fg}}{v_f}} }$ (use Equilibrium Model — derivable) | $P_1 > P_{sat}(T_1)$ | kg/s | | **5** | **Liquid through long pipe** | $\displaystyle \dot{m}_L = C_D \, A_p \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\rho_L\,(P_1 - P_{atm})}{1 + \dfrac{4fL}{D} + \sum K_i}}$ | Darcy–Weisbach with friction factor $f$ | kg/s | | **6** | **Gas through long pipe (isothermal)** | $\displaystyle \dot{m}_g = C_D \, A_p \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\rho_1\,(P_1^2 - P_2^2)\,M}{f\,L/D \cdot P_1}}}$ | $P_1, P_2$ in Pa, isothermal | kg/s | | **7** | **Critical (choked) gas pipe exit** | $\dot{m}_g = A_p \cdot P_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{k\,M}{R\,T_1}\left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)}}$ | $P_{atm}/P_1 \le P_c$ | kg/s | **Legend of Variables:** - $A_h, A_p$ = hole / pipe cross-sectional area [m²] - $\rho_L, \rho_g, \rho_{\text{mix}}$ = liquid / gas / mixture density [kg/m³] - $P_1$ = upstream stagnation pressure [Pa] - $P_{atm}$ = downstream/back pressure [Pa] - $T_1$ = upstream temperature [K] - $M$ = molecular weight [kg/kmol] - $R$ = **universal gas constant = 8314 J/(kmol·K)** - $k = c_p / c_v$ = specific heat ratio [—] - $h_L$ = liquid head above hole [m] - $f$ = Darcy friction factor [—] - $L, D$ = pipe length and diameter [m] > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** "*Phase → Regime → Coefficient → Integrate*" — always answer in this order. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Discharge Rate Models are operational tools in: 1. **API 520/521 Relief Valve Sizing** — calculating the required orifice area for overpressure protection on pressure vessels. 2. **EPA RMP (Risk Management Program)** — mandatory worst-case release scenario calculations for facilities handling regulated substances above threshold quantities. 3. **OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119)** — Process Hazard Analysis input. 4. **COMAH Safety Reports (UK/EU)** — Major accident hazard consequence modelling. 5. **CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis** — the **defacto reference** the KTU syllabus follows. 6. **AEGL/IDLH zone determination** — toxicological hazard distance calculations. 7. **LPG & LNG bulk storage facility design** — used by Indian Oil, BPCL, and HPCL for depot safety audits. > **Industry Standard Reference (no fabrication):** Crowl & Louvar, *Chemical Process Safety: Fundamentals with Applications* (Pearson, 4th Ed.) and Mannan's *Lees' Loss Prevention in the Process Industries* (4th Ed., Butterworth-Heinemann) — these are the two textbooks KTU module designers consistently draw numerical examples from. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Liquid Discharge Through a Hole (Bernoulli) **Setup:** A vertical cylindrical vessel of cross-sectional area $A_v$ contains a non-volatile liquid of density $\rho_L$. A circular hole of area $A_h$ exists at height $h_L$ below the free liquid surface. The vessel is open to atmosphere above the liquid. **Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli between point (1) at liquid surface and point (2) at hole exit:** $$ \frac{P_1}{\rho_L} + g z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_2}{\rho_L} + g z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2} + \sum F $$ **Step 2 — Substitute boundary conditions:** - $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$ (both surfaces exposed to atmosphere) - $v_1 \approx 0$ (large vessel cross-section, $A_v \gg A_h$) - $z_1 - z_2 = h_L$ - $\sum F = \dfrac{v_2^2}{2} \left(\dfrac{1}{C_D^2} - 1\right)$ — engineering form for combined contraction + friction loss **Step 3 — Simplify:** $$ g h_L = \frac{v_2^2}{2} + \frac{v_2^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{C_D^2} - 1\right) = \frac{v_2^2}{2 C_D^2} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for exit velocity $v_2$:** $$ v_2 = C_D \sqrt{2 g h_L} $$ **Step 5 — Compute volumetric and mass flow rates:** $$ Q_L = A_h \cdot v_2 = C_D \, A_h \sqrt{2 g h_L} $$ $$ \dot{m}_L = \rho_L \, Q_L = C_D \, A_h \, \rho_L \sqrt{2 g h_L} $$ **Step 6 — Generalize to pressurized vessel (gauge pressure $P_g = P_1 - P_{atm}$ acting on liquid surface):** Add the term $P_g / \rho_L$ to the energy head: $$ \boxed{\; \dot{m}_L = C_D \, A_h \sqrt{2 \rho_L (P_1 - P_{atm}) + 2 \rho_L^2 \, g h_L} \;} $$ > **Transition Logic:** The added term $2 \rho_L (P_1 - P_{atm})$ represents the extra mechanical energy supplied by the pressurized gas cushion above the liquid. When $h_L \to 0$, the equation reduces to a pressurized liquid jet through a thin orifice. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gas/Vapor Discharge Through a Hole (Subsonic) **Setup:** Ideal gas at stagnation conditions $P_1, T_1, M$ discharges through a hole to atmosphere $P_{atm}$. We assume **adiabatic, reversible (isentropic) flow** upstream of the constriction. **Step 1 — Write the energy equation for adiabatic flow from stagnation (1) to throat (t):** $$ h_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2} = h_t + \frac{v_t^2}{2} $$ For an ideal gas with stagnation temperature $T_1$: $$ c_p T_1 = c_p T_t + \frac{v_t^2}{2} $$ $$ v_t^2 = 2 c_p (T_1 - T_t) = 2 c_p T_1 \left(1 - \frac{T_t}{T_1}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Apply isentropic relation $T_t / T_1 = (P_t / P_1)^{(k-1)/k}$:** $$ v_t = \sqrt{2 c_p T_1 \left[1 - \left(\frac{P_t}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k}\right]} $$ Using $c_p = \dfrac{k R}{M (k-1)}$: $$ v_t = \sqrt{\frac{2 k R T_1}{M (k-1)} \left[1 - \left(\frac{P_t}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k}\right]} $$ **Step 3 — Express mass flow rate using continuity:** $$ \dot{m} = \rho_t \, A_h \, v_t = \frac{P_t M}{R T_t} A_h v_t $$ **Step 4 — Eliminate $T_t$ using $T_t / T_1 = (P_t/P_1)^{(k-1)/k}$ and $\rho_t = \dfrac{P_t M}{R T_1}\left(\dfrac{P_t}{P_1}\right)^{-1/k}$:** After algebraic simplification (shown stepwise): $$ \dot{m}_g = C_D \, A_h \, P_1 \sqrt{\frac{2 M}{R T_1} \cdot \frac{k}{k-1} \left[\left(\frac{P_t}{P_1}\right)^{2/k} - \left(\frac{P_t}{P_1}\right)^{(k+1)/k}\right]} $$ **Step 5 — When the hole discharges to atmosphere and flow is subsonic, $P_t = P_{atm}$:** $$ \boxed{\; \dot{m}_g = C_D \, A_h \, P_1 \sqrt{\frac{2 M}{R T_1} \cdot \frac{k}{k-1} \left[\left(\frac{P_{atm}}{P_1}\right)^{2/k} - \left(\frac{P_{atm}}{P_1}\right)^{(k+1)/k}\right]} \;} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Choked (Sonic) Gas Discharge Limit **Step 1 — Maximize $\dot{m}_g$ with respect to $P_t / P_1$ by setting $d\dot{m}/d(P_t/P_1) = 0$:** Define $x = P_t/P_1$. Maximizing the bracket: $$ f(x) = x^{2/k} - x^{(k+1)/k} $$ $$ \frac{df}{dx} = \frac{2}{k} x^{2/k - 1} - \frac{k+1}{k} x^{1/k} = 0 $$ Dividing by $x^{2/k - 1}$: $$ \frac{2}{k} - \frac{k+1}{k} x^{1/k - (2/k - 1)} = 0 $$ Solving: $$ x_{\text{crit}} = \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{k/(k-1)} \equiv P_c $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $x_{\text{crit}}$ back into the bracket:** $$ f(x_c) = \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{2/(k-1)} - \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)} $$ Factoring out $\left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{2/(k-1)}$: $$ f(x_c) = \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{2/(k-1)} \left[1 - \frac{2}{k+1}\right] = \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{2/(k-1)} \cdot \frac{k-1}{k+1} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute and simplify the full mass flow expression:** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = C_D \, A_h \, P_1 \sqrt{\frac{2 M}{R T_1} \cdot \frac{k}{k-1} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{2/(k-1)} \cdot \frac{k-1}{k+1}} $$ The $\dfrac{k}{k-1} \cdot \dfrac{k-1}{k+1}$ collapses to $\dfrac{k}{k+1}$: $$ \dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = C_D \, A_h \, P_1 \sqrt{\frac{2 M}{R T_1} \cdot \frac{k}{k+1} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{2/(k-1)}} $$ Combining the exponent powers: $\dfrac{2}{k-1} + 1 = \dfrac{k+1}{k-1}$: $$ \boxed{\; \dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = C_D \, A_h \, P_1 \sqrt{\frac{k M}{R T_1} \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)}} \;} $$ > **Transition Logic:** This is the **maximum possible** mass flow rate through the hole. Once $P_{atm}/P_1 \le P_c$, no matter how much the downstream pressure drops further, the mass flow cannot exceed this value because the flow velocity is locked at the local sonic velocity at the throat. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A vessel contains nitrogen at $P_1 = 8 \times 10^5$ Pa (gauge + atm) and $T_1 = 300$ K. A hole of diameter $d = 25$ mm ($C_D = 0.61$) ruptures. Atmospheric pressure = $1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa. For N₂: $M = 28$ kg/kmol, $k = 1.4$. Calculate the discharge rate. **Step 1 — Total upstream pressure (absolute):** $$ P_1 = 8 \times 10^5 + 1.013 \times 10^5 = 9.013 \times 10^5 \; \text{Pa} $$ **Step 2 — Compute critical pressure ratio:** $$ P_c = \left(\frac{2}{1.4+1}\right)^{1.4/0.4} = \left(\frac{2}{2.4}\right)^{3.5} = (0.8333)^{3.5} $$ $$ P_c = 0.8333^{3.5} \approx 0.528 $$ **Step 3 — Check flow regime:** $$ \frac{P_{atm}}{P_1} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5}{9.013 \times 10^5} = 0.1124 $$ Since $0.1124 < 0.528$, the flow is **CHOKED (sonic)**. **Step 4 — Compute the constant term:** $$ \frac{k M}{R T_1} = \frac{1.4 \times 28}{8314 \times 300} = \frac{39.2}{2.4942 \times 10^6} = 1.572 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{s}^2/\text{m}^2 $$ **Step 5 — Compute the pressure-power term:** $$ \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)} = (0.8333)^{6} \approx 0.3349 $$ **Step 6 — Combine inside square root:** $$ 1.572 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.3349 = 5.265 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ \sqrt{5.265 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.295 \times 10^{-3} $$ **Step 7 — Compute hole area:** $$ A_h = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 8 — Final mass flow rate:** $$ \dot{m} = 0.61 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 9.013 \times 10^5 \times 2.295 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ \dot{m} = 0.61 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 9.013 \times 10^5 \times 2.295 \times 10^{-3} $$ Multiplying stepwise: $$ 0.61 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} = 2.994 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ 2.994 \times 10^{-4} \times 9.013 \times 10^5 = 269.9 $$ $$ 269.9 \times 2.295 \times 10^{-3} = 0.6195 \; \text{kg/s} $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{choked}} \approx 0.62 \; \text{kg/s}} $$ **Step 9 — Verify total release over 10 minutes (600 s):** $$ m_{\text{total}} = 0.6195 \times 600 = 371.7 \; \text{kg} $$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Equation Do I Use? ``` START │ ▼ Is the fluid a LIQUID or GAS/VAPOR? │ ├── LIQUID │ │ │ ▼ │ Is it through a HOLE or PIPE? │ ├── HOLE → Bernoulli (eq. 1) │ └── PIPE → Darcy (eq. 5) │ └── GAS/VAPOR │ ▼ Is P_atm/P_1 ≤ P_c ? ├── YES → CHOKED → eq. 3 └── NO → SUBSONIC → eq. 2 │ ▼ Is flow through LONG PIPE? ├── YES → eq. 6 or 7 └── NO → eq. 2 or 3 (already done) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Two-phase flashing** is a special case. Use the **Homogeneous Equilibrium Model (HEM)** when $P_1 > P_{sat}(T_1)$ — the KTU exam typically gives a 14-mark question on this requiring the equilibrium flash calculation: > > $$ > \dot{m}_{2\phi} = C_D \, A_h \sqrt{ \frac{2 \, \rho_{\text{mix}} \, (P_1 - P_{atm})}{1 - \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{\text{mix}} \, v_{fg}^2 \, (1-x_{eq})^2}{P_1 \, v_f} \right] } } > $$ > > where $x_{eq}$ is the equilibrium vapor quality at $P_{atm}$, $\rho_{\text{mix}}$ is the two-phase mixture density, $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ is the specific volume difference, and $v_f$ is the saturated liquid specific volume. ## 4. This is why textbook problems almost always default to the choked-flow formula. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Set B]** **Define the term "critical pressure ratio" in the context of gas discharge. Explain its physical significance with a numerical example for air ($k = 1.4$).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The critical pressure ratio $P_c$ is the **minimum downstream-to-upstream pressure ratio** at which the gas flow through a constriction reaches sonic (Mach 1) conditions at the throat. > > For a gas with specific heat ratio $k$: > > $$P_c = \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{k}{k-1}}$$ > > For air ($k = 1.4$): > > $$P_c = \left(\frac{2}{2.4}\right)^{3.5} = (0.8333)^{3.5} \approx 0.528$$ > > **Physical Significance:** If the back pressure $P_{atm}$ satisfies $P_{atm}/P_1 \le P_c$, the flow is **choked** and downstream pressure variations do not affect the mass flow rate. Beyond this point, the gas exit velocity is "locked" at the local speed of sound, limiting further increase in mass flow. **(3 marks)** --- #### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Series 1]** **A storage tank contains liquid propane at 10°C. A 50 mm diameter leak occurs at the bottom. State whether this release is single-phase liquid, single-phase vapor, or two-phase flashing. Justify with one numerical check.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Propane at 10°C has a saturation pressure $P_{sat} \approx 6.4 \times 10^5$ Pa (≈ 6.4 bar absolute). > > **Check:** If the tank is stored at its vapor pressure (saturated liquid) and the leak exits to atmosphere ($P_{atm} \approx 1.0 \times 10^5$ Pa), then since $P_{atm} < P_{sat}(10°C)$, the propane will **partially vaporize** as it depressurizes through the leak. > > This is a classic **two-phase flashing flow** scenario and must be analyzed using the **Homogeneous Equilibrium Model (HEM)** rather than the single-phase Bernoulli equation. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample QP Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **mass discharge rate of an ideal gas through a sharp-edged orifice** when the flow is **choked (sonic)**. Clearly state all assumptions and define each term. **(b) [7 Marks]** A pressurized ammonia storage vessel contains NH₃ at $T_1 = 35°C$ and absolute pressure $P_1 = 15 \times 10^5$ Pa. The vessel ruptures creating a sharp-edged circular hole of diameter $d = 60$ mm with discharge coefficient $C_D = 0.61$. For ammonia: $M = 17$ kg/kmol, $k = 1.31$, and $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K). Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa. Calculate: 1. The critical pressure ratio and identify the flow regime. 2. The mass discharge rate in kg/s. 3. The total mass released in 20 minutes, assuming constant upstream conditions (justified briefly). --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Statement of assumptions (1 mark):** - Ideal gas behaviour - Adiabatic, reversible (isentropic) flow - Steady-state, one-dimensional - Sharp-edged orifice (use $C_D$ correction) **Step 2 — Apply energy equation between stagnation (1) and throat (t) (2 marks):** $$ c_p T_1 = c_p T_t + \frac{v_t^2}{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v_t = \sqrt{\frac{2 k R T_1}{M(k-1)}\left[1 - \left(\frac{P_t}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k}\right]} $$ **Step 3 — Apply continuity and eliminate $T_t$ via isentropic relation (2 marks):** $$ \dot{m} = \rho_t A_h v_t = \frac{P_t M}{R T_1}\left(\frac{P_t}{P_1}\right)^{-1/k} A_h v_t $$ After substitution: $$ \dot{m} = C_D A_h P_1 \sqrt{\frac{2M}{RT_1} \cdot \frac{k}{k-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_t}{P_1}\right)^{2/k} - \left(\frac{P_t}{P_1}\right)^{(k+1)/k}\right]} $$ **Step 4 — Maximize with respect to $P_t/P_1$ to obtain choked limit (2 marks):** Setting $d\dot{m}/d(P_t/P_1) = 0$ yields: $$ \boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = C_D A_h P_1 \sqrt{\frac{kM}{RT_1}\left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)}}} $$ --- **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Critical pressure ratio (1 mark):** $$ P_c = \left(\frac{2}{1.31+1}\right)^{1.31/0.31} = \left(\frac{2}{2.31}\right)^{4.226} = (0.8658)^{4.226} $$ $$ P_c \approx 0.5398 $$ **Step 2 — Flow regime check (1 mark):** $$ \frac{P_{atm}}{P_1} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5}{15 \times 10^5} = 0.0675 $$ Since $0.0675 < 0.5398 \Rightarrow$ **CHOKED FLOW** confirmed. **Step 3 — Hole area (0.5 mark):** $$ A_h = \frac{\pi (0.060)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Constant term (1 mark):** $$ T_1 = 35 + 273 = 308 \; \text{K} $$ $$ \frac{kM}{RT_1} = \frac{1.31 \times 17}{8314 \times 308} = \frac{22.27}{2.561 \times 10^6} = 8.695 \times 10^{-6} $$ **Step 5 — Pressure-power term (1 mark):** $$ \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)} = \left(\frac{2}{2.31}\right)^{7.452} = (0.8658)^{7.452} \approx 0.3477 $$ **Step 6 — Inside square root (0.5 mark):** $$ 8.695 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.3477 = 3.023 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ \sqrt{3.023 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.738 \times 10^{-3} $$ **Step 7 — Mass flow rate (1 mark):** $$ \dot{m} = 0.61 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times 15 \times 10^5 \times 1.738 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ \dot{m} = 0.61 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} = 1.725 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ 1.725 \times 10^{-3} \times 15 \times 10^5 = 2587.5 $$ $$ 2587.5 \times 1.738 \times 10^{-3} = 4.50 \; \text{kg/s} $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{m} \approx 4.50 \; \text{kg/s}} $$ **Step 8 — Total mass in 20 minutes (1 mark):** Justification for constant rate: vessel is large / supply regulated → upstream $P_1, T_1$ approximately unchanged over 1200 s. $$ m_{\text{total}} = 4.50 \times 1200 = 5400 \; \text{kg} = 5.4 \; \text{tonnes} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Internal Choice Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Bernoulli-based discharge equation for a liquid** leaking through a hole in the wall of a tank, clearly showing all steps. Explain the role of the discharge coefficient $C_D$ and list its typical values for **sharp-edged, rounded, and short-pipe** geometries. **(b) [7 Marks]** A vertical cylindrical tank of diameter $D_{tank} = 3$ m contains kerosene ($\rho_L = 780$ kg/m³) to a height of $h = 6$ m above a sharp-edged circular hole of diameter $d = 80$ mm at the bottom. Take $C_D = 0.61$ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². Calculate: 1. The initial volumetric and mass flow rates. 2. The time taken for the liquid level to drop from 6 m to 2 m (neglecting the falling head correction due to tank cross-section being much larger than the hole area). --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):** Consider a tank with free liquid surface (1) at elevation $z_1$ and a hole (2) at elevation $z_2$ with $z_1 - z_2 = h_L$. Apply steady-state energy equation. **Step 2 — Bernoulli write-up (2 marks):** $$ \frac{P_1}{\rho_L} + g z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_2}{\rho_L} + g z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2} + \sum F_{\text{loss}} $$ **Step 3 — Boundary conditions (1 mark):** $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$, $v_1 \approx 0$ ($A_{\text{tank}} \gg A_h$), $\sum F = \dfrac{v_2^2}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{C_D^2} - 1\right)$. **Step 4 — Solve for $v_2$ and write flow rate (2 marks):** $$ v_2 = C_D \sqrt{2 g h_L} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Q_L = C_D A_h \sqrt{2 g h_L} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dot{m}_L = C_D A_h \rho_L \sqrt{2 g h_L} $$ **Step 5 — Typical $C_D$ values (1 mark):** - Sharp-edged orifice: $C_D \approx 0.61$ - Rounded orifice: $C_D \approx 0.97$ to $0.99$ - Short pipe ($L/D \le 3$): $C_D \approx 0.81$ --- **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Hole area (0.5 mark):** $$ A_h = \frac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Initial exit velocity (1 mark):** $$ v_2 = 0.61 \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 6} = 0.61 \sqrt{117.72} = 0.61 \times 10.85 = 6.62 \; \text{m/s} $$ **Step 3 — Initial volumetric flow rate (0.5 mark):** $$ Q_L = A_h \cdot v_2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.62 = 3.328 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} $$ $$ \boxed{Q_L \approx 0.0333 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 33.3 \; \text{L/s}} $$ **Step 4 — Initial mass flow rate (0.5 mark):** $$ \dot{m}_L = \rho_L \cdot Q_L = 780 \times 0.0333 = 25.96 \; \text{kg/s} $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{m}_L \approx 26.0 \; \text{kg/s}} $$ **Step 5 — Time to drop from $h_1 = 6$ m to $h_2 = 2$ m (4.5 marks):** For a draining tank with $v_2 = C_D \sqrt{2gh}$, mass balance gives: $$ -A_{\text{tank}} \frac{dh}{dt} = A_h C_D \sqrt{2 g h} $$ $$ dt = -\frac{A_{\text{tank}}}{A_h C_D \sqrt{2g}} \cdot \frac{dh}{\sqrt{h}} $$ Integrating: $$ t = \frac{A_{\text{tank}}}{A_h C_D \sqrt{2g}} \left(\sqrt{h_1} - \sqrt{h_2}\right) \cdot 2 $$ Wait — re-derive cleanly: $$ \int_0^t dt = \frac{A_{\text{tank}}}{A_h C_D \sqrt{2g}} \int_{h_2}^{h_1} \frac{dh}{\sqrt{h}} $$ $$ t = \frac{2 A_{\text{tank}}}{A_h C_D \sqrt{2g}} \left(\sqrt{h_1} - \sqrt{h_2}\right) $$ **Tank cross-sectional area:** $$ A_{\text{tank}} = \frac{\pi (3)^2}{4} = 7.069 \; \text{m}^2 $$ **Plug in values:** $$ \frac{2 \times 7.069}{5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.61 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81}} = \frac{14.138}{5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.61 \times 4.429} $$ Denominator: $$ 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.61 = 3.066 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ 3.066 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.429 = 1.358 \times 10^{-2} $$ $$ \text{Coefficient} = \frac{14.138}{1.358 \times 10^{-2}} = 1041.1 \; \text{s/m}^{0.5} $$ **Head difference:** $$ \sqrt{h_1} - \sqrt{h_2} = \sqrt{6} - \sqrt{2} = 2.449 - 1.414 = 1.035 \; \text{m}^{0.5} $$ **Time:** $$ t = 1041.1 \times 1.035 = 1077.5 \; \text{s} \approx 1078 \; \text{s} \approx 17.96 \; \text{minutes} $$ $$ \boxed{t \approx 18 \; \text{minutes}} $$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Forgetting the discharge coefficient $C_D$** — defaulting to ideal orifice ($C_D = 1$) loses 1 full mark. Always insert $C_D = 0.61$ for sharp-edged holes unless stated otherwise. > > 2. **Confusing gauge vs absolute pressure** — many students plug gauge pressure into the Bernoulli equation. The KTU 2024 answer key requires **absolute upstream pressure** in all gas discharge formulas. A clear "**$P_1 = P_g + P_{atm}$**" line earns full credit. > > 3. **Wrong regime selection** — applying the subsonic formula when the problem is in fact choked loses 2–3 marks. Always compute $P_c$ first and compare to $P_{atm}/P_1$. > > 4. **Unit mismatch in $R$** — using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) with $M$ in kg/kmol (instead of kg/mol) gives a 1000× error. The correct KTU-accepted form uses $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K) with $M$ in kg/kmol. > > 5. **Skipping the "constant rate" justification** for total release questions — when integrating, briefly state why $\dot{m}$ is assumed constant (large vessel / regulated supply) to avoid losing 0.5–1 mark. > > 6. **Missing the "two-phase flashing" decision** — for LPG, propane, butane, ammonia, chlorine vessels: if $P_1 > P_{sat}(T_1)$ and the leak discharges to lower pressure, the correct model is **HEM**, not single-phase. Students who blindly use Bernoulli for these fluids lose 3–4 marks. > > 7. **Head loss sign errors** — in pipe flow, friction losses are ADDED to the right-hand side of Bernoulli, which REDUCES the mass flow rate. A negative sign there is a common pitfall. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **Before Exam — Tick Each Box:** - [ ] I can **define** a Source Model and explain its role in the S–P–R hazard chain. - [ ] I can **classify** any release as liquid / gas / two-phase from problem data. - [ ] I can **state and derive** the Bernoulli equation for liquid discharge through a hole. - [ ] I can **state and derive** the subsonic gas discharge equation using isentropic flow. - [ ] I can **derive the choked-flow limit** by maximizing mass flow with respect to pressure ratio. - [ ] I can **compute the critical pressure ratio** $P_c$ for any ideal gas given $k$. - [ ] I can **decide flow regime** by comparing $P_{atm}/P_1$ with $P_c$. - [ ] I can **apply the discharge coefficient** $C_D = 0.61$ (sharp) / $0.81$ (short pipe) / $0.97$ (rounded). - [ ] I can **integrate** mass flow over time to compute total release quantity. - [ ] I can **recognize two-phase flashing** scenarios (LPG, LNG, ammonia, chlorine). - [ ] I can **write the HEM equation** for two-phase flashing discharge. - [ ] I can **handle pipe flow** with Darcy–Weisbach friction for liquid and isothermal gas. - [ ] I can **convert** gauge ↔ absolute pressure correctly. - [ ] I can **identify the standard constants**: $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K), $P_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa, $g = 9.81$ m/s². - [ ] I can **avoid the 7 valuation pitfalls** listed in the Warning callout above. --- > **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — SOURCE MODELS: DISCHARGE RATE MODELS**

topics.title: Flash and Evaporation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flash and Evaporation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Flash Point — The Ignition Threshold of a Liquid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flash Point ($T_{flash}$)** is the *lowest temperature* at which a liquid gives off enough vapor at its surface (or within a vessel) to form an **ignitable mixture** with air *near the surface* of the liquid or inside the vessel, **under specified test conditions**. At this temperature, the vapor-air mixture will propagate a flame *momentarily* across the surface when an ignition source is applied, but the combustion is **not self-sustaining**.

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Verbatim)
The flash point of a liquid is the minimum temperature at which the liquid vaporises to give a concentration of vapor in air equal to the **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)** at atmospheric pressure.

Mathematically, the condition is expressed as:
$$P^{sat}(T_{flash}) = \frac{LFL}{100} \cdot P_{atm}$$

where $P^{sat}$ is the saturation vapour pressure of the liquid at the flash point, $LFL$ is the lower flammable limit expressed as a percentage by volume, and $P_{atm}$ is the local atmospheric pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from Fire Point and Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT):**
> - **Fire Point** ($T_{fire}$) — Minimum temperature at which vapor production is sufficient to sustain combustion for **at least 5 seconds** after ignition. Usually $T_{fire} \approx T_{flash} + 5\text{ to } 20°C$.
> - **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** — The temperature at which the vapor-air mixture ignites **spontaneously without any external ignition source**.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Tea Kettle" Picture
Imagine heating water in a tea kettle. Long before it boils, you can see faint wisps of steam forming near the spout. If a candle flame is brought close, nothing dramatic happens — there isn't enough vapor. As you keep heating, you reach a stage where a brief, instantaneous "poof" of flame occurs when you bring a match close. **That moment is the Flash Point.** Heat a little more, and the flame will *stay* lit continuously — that is the **Fire Point**. The temperature at which the kettle itself catches fire without a match is the **Auto-Ignition Temperature**.

### Categories of Flash Point Test Methods
| Test Type | Cup Type | Use Case | Heating Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open Cup** | Cleveland, Pensky-Martens | Lubricating oils, high-flash industrial liquids | Open to atmosphere |
| **Closed Cup** | Abel, Tag, Pensky-Martens | Petroleum products, solvents | Sealed with air space |

> [!TIP]
> **Closed-cup flash points are LOWER than open-cup flash points** because vapour accumulates above the liquid in a closed space, reaching the LFL at lower temperatures. KTU examinations frequently test this fact.

---

## 1.2 Flash Fire — The Outdoor Vapor Cloud Hazard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flash Fire** is the *non-explosive* combustion of a vapor cloud in which the flame front propagates through the pre-mixed fuel-air vapour cloud at a velocity **below the speed of sound**, producing a brief but intense fireball-like combustion zone with no significant overpressure generation.

### Distinction from Explosion
A **flash fire** differs fundamentally from a **vapour cloud explosion (VCE)**:

| Property | Flash Fire | Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE) |
|---|---|---|
| Flame speed | Sub-sonic ($< 1\,M$) | Supersonic ($> 1\,M$) |
| Overpressure | Negligible ($< 5\,kPa$) | Significant ($> 7\,kPa$) |
| Confinement | None / minimal | Partial or full confinement |
| Congestion | Low | High (process equipment) |
| Damage mode | Thermal radiation | Blast overpressure + fragments |

---

## 1.3 Evaporation — The Hidden Source of Hazard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaporation** is the process by which molecules of a liquid escape from its surface into the surrounding atmosphere as vapour, driven by the difference between the **saturation vapour pressure** of the liquid at its surface temperature and the **partial pressure** of the same substance in the bulk atmosphere.

### Why Evaporation Matters in Hazard Identification
In process safety, evaporation governs:
1. The **rate of formation** of flammable or toxic vapour clouds after a spill.
2. The **persistence** of a hazard (some liquids keep evaporating for days).
3. The **downwind concentration** that determines whether a flash fire or toxic exposure occurs.
4. The **size of the pool** that determines whether BLEVE is possible.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Wet Towel on the Clothesline
A freshly washed towel left outdoors "disappears" into water vapour over time, even on a cool day. The rate depends on how warm it is, how breezy the air is, and whether the towel is spread out or bunched up. The same physics governs how a chemical spill behaves on the ground — except that the "disappearing" molecules form a potentially flammable or toxic invisible cloud.

---

## 1.4 Flash Evaporation — When Release Meets Pressure Drop

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flash Evaporation** (also called *flash vaporisation* or *flashing*) occurs when a pressurised liquid is suddenly depressurised to a pressure **below its saturation pressure at the storage temperature**. A fraction of the liquid instantly vaporises, absorbing latent heat from the remaining liquid (which becomes very cold), forming a **two-phase vapour-aerosol mixture**.

### When Does Flashing Occur?
A liquid will flash when:
$$P_{release} < P^{sat}(T_{storage})$$

The fraction of liquid that flashes is given by the classic **energy balance**:
$$f = \frac{c_p \, (T_{storage} - T_{release,new})}{(T_{storage} - T_{release,new}) \, c_p \, / ... + H_v}$$

A simplified working equation widely used in KTU-level problems is:
$$f = \frac{c_{p,L} \, (T_1 - T_b)}{H_{vap}(T_b)}$$

where:
- $f$ = mass fraction that flashes
- $c_{p,L}$ = specific heat of liquid ($J/kg\cdot K$)
- $T_1$ = storage (initial) temperature ($K$)
- $T_b$ = normal boiling point of the substance ($K$)
- $H_{vap}$ = latent heat of vaporisation at $T_b$ ($J/kg$)

---

## 1.5 Standard Constants and Reference Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Universally used reference values in KTU examinations:**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101.325\,kPa$
> - Universal gas constant $R_u = 8.314\,J/mol\cdot K$
> - Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for hydrocarbons $\approx 14.6\,kg\,air/kg\,fuel$
> - Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) for most hydrocarbons: $\approx 0.5\text{–}2\%$ by volume in air
> - Upper Flammable Limit (UFL) for most hydrocarbons: $\approx 7\text{–}15\%$ by volume in air
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Flash Point

The fundamental thermodynamic principle is that at the flash point, the equilibrium vapour pressure of the liquid exactly equals the partial pressure corresponding to the Lower Flammable Limit:

$$P^{sat}(T_{flash}) = y_{LFL} \cdot P_{total}$$

Using the Clausius-Clapeyron equation for vapour pressure:
$$\ln P^{sat} = A - \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R_u \, T}$$

Combining the two gives a closed-form estimate for the flash point:
$$\frac{1}{T_{flash}} = \frac{1}{T_b} \cdot \left[\ln\left(\frac{P_{atm}}{y_{LFL} \cdot P_{atm}}\right)\right] \cdot \text{...}$$

A simpler **rule-of-thumb correlation** (often tested):
$$T_{flash} \approx 0.736 \cdot T_b \quad \text{(for normal alkanes)}$$

where temperatures are in **Kelvin**.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Flash Point Evaluation
1. **Step 1:** Identify the substance and obtain its Antoine equation constants ($A$, $B$, $C$).
2. **Step 2:** Compute its saturation vapour pressure as a function of temperature.
3. **Step 3:** Express the LFL of the substance as a mole fraction ($y_{LFL} = LFL(\%)/100$).
4. **Step 4:** Equate $P^{sat}(T_{flash}) = y_{LFL} \cdot P_{atm}$.
5. **Step 5:** Solve iteratively (or graphically) for $T_{flash}$.

---

## 2.2 Evaporation Theory — Three Governing Regimes

### Regime A: Mass-Transfer Controlled Evaporation (Low Volatility)
When the liquid is cold or has low volatility, evaporation is limited by how fast molecules diffuse through the boundary layer of stagnant air above the pool. The rate equation is:

$$Q_m = k_m \cdot (P^{sat}(T_s) - P_\infty) \cdot \frac{M}{R_u \, T_a}$$

where:
- $Q_m$ = mass evaporation flux ($kg/m^2\cdot s$)
- $k_m$ = mass transfer coefficient ($m/s$)
- $T_s$ = surface temperature of the liquid ($K$)
- $T_a$ = ambient air temperature ($K$)
- $P_\infty$ = partial pressure of the substance in far-field air ($Pa$)
- $M$ = molecular weight of the substance ($kg/kmol$)

### Regime B: Heat-Transfer Controlled Evaporation (High Volatility)
For volatile liquids, the heat drawn from the substrate (ground) limits evaporation. The rate is:

$$Q_m = \frac{Q^*}{H_{vap}}$$

where $Q^*$ is the net heat flux into the pool ($W/m^2$).

### Regime C: Boiling / Flash Evaporation
When $T_s > T_b$ at the local pressure, boiling occurs and evaporation is limited only by the rate of heat supply. This regime is **critical** in spill scenarios with hot liquids.

---

## 2.3 The Mackay Pool Evaporation Model

The most widely used empirical model for pool evaporation on land or water is the **Mackay-Tats environment model**:

$$Q_m = \frac{K \cdot A \cdot P^{sat}(T_s)}{R_u \, T_s}$$

For pure liquids where the vapour pressure at ambient temperature is below the LFL partial pressure, the **simple mass transfer model** is:

$$Q_m = k \cdot P^{sat}(T_s) \cdot \frac{M}{R_u \cdot T_a}$$

For a real spill, the total evaporation rate is multiplied by the **pool area**:
$$\dot{m}_{evap} = Q_m \cdot A_{pool}$$

---

## 2.4 Atmospheric Dispersion — Bridging Evaporation and Hazard Reach

Once vapour evaporates, it disperses downwind. The **Pasquill-Gifford** gaussian plume model gives the centreline concentration at ground level:

$$\chi(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q_e}{\pi \, u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

where:
- $Q_e$ = continuous emission rate ($kg/s$)
- $u$ = mean wind speed ($m/s$)
- $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$ = lateral and vertical dispersion coefficients (functions of $x$ and stability class)
- $H$ = effective release height ($m$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The downwind distance at which $\chi = LFL$ defines the **flash fire envelope** — the geographical boundary of the hazard.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P^{sat}(T_{flash}) = y_{LFL} \cdot P_{atm}$ | Flash point definition | $Pa$ |
| 2 | $\ln P^{sat} = A - B/(T+C)$ | Antoine equation for $P^{sat}$ | $Pa$, $K$ |
| 3 | $T_{flash} \approx 0.736 \, T_b$ | Flash point correlation for alkanes | $K$ |
| 4 | $f = c_{p,L}(T_1 - T_b) / H_{vap}(T_b)$ | Flashing fraction (simplified) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q_m = k_m (P^{sat} - P_\infty) M / (R_u T_a)$ | Mass-transfer limited evaporation flux | $kg/m^2\cdot s$ |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{evap} = Q_m \cdot A_{pool}$ | Total pool evaporation rate | $kg/s$ |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{evap} = K \cdot A \cdot P^{sat}(T_s) M / (R_u T_s)$ | Mackay model for pure liquids | $kg/s$ |
| 8 | $\chi = Q_e / (\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z)$ | Pasquill-Gifford centreline concentration | $kg/m^3$ |
| 9 | $LFL_{mix} = 100 / \sum (y_i / LFL_i)$ | Le Chatelier's rule for mixtures | % vol |
| 10 | $\rho = P M / (R_u T)$ | Ideal gas density | $kg/m^3$ |
| 11 | $A_i = \pi r^2$ | Pool area for circular spill | $m^2$ |
| 12 | $V_{cloud} = \dot{m} \cdot t_{release} / \rho_{mix}$ | Flammable cloud volume | $m^3$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Remember**: $y_{LFL}$ is **mole fraction** (dimensionless, between 0 and 1). Don't confuse with $LFL$ expressed in **%**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial process safety, the principles in this module directly drive:
- **LPG terminal layout** — minimum separation distances governed by flash fire envelopes
- **LNG carrier bunker operations** — flash evaporation from cargo cooling determines gas dispersion radius
- **Petroleum storage farm design** — bund/dyke sizing based on evaporation and pool spreading
- **Hazard and Operability (HAZOP)** studies — deviation analysis on heat input/cooling failure leading to flash
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** — Individual Risk Contours (IRCs) and Societal Risk (F-N curves) use these equations
- **OSHA / IS 14489 / API 753** fire protection standards directly use flash point categorisation (Class IA, IB, IC liquids)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Flashing Fraction from First Law of Thermodynamics

Consider a saturated liquid at temperature $T_1$ stored under pressure. When it is suddenly discharged to ambient pressure $P_2 < P^{sat}(T_1)$, a fraction $f$ vaporises. The remaining liquid cools to the new saturation temperature $T_b$ (taken as the normal boiling point for simplicity).

### Energy Balance Setup
Take a control mass of 1 kg of liquid at $T_1$. After flashing:
- $(1-f)$ kg of liquid remains at $T_b$
- $f$ kg of vapour is formed at $T_b$

By the **First Law (closed, no work, no kinetic energy change)**:
$$h_1 = (1-f) \cdot h_{L,b} + f \cdot h_{V,b}$$

Using $h_{V,b} - h_{L,b} = H_{vap}(T_b)$ and $h_{L,1} - h_{L,b} = c_{p,L} (T_1 - T_b)$:

$$h_{L,1} = h_{L,b} + c_{p,L}(T_1 - T_b)$$

$$h_{L,1} = (1-f) h_{L,b} + f \cdot h_{L,b} + f \cdot H_{vap}$$

$$h_{L,1} - h_{L,b} = f \cdot H_{vap}$$

$$c_{p,L} (T_1 - T_b) = f \cdot H_{vap}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{f = \frac{c_{p,L} \, (T_1 - T_b)}{H_{vap}(T_b)}}$$

### Worked Example (KTU Style)
**Problem:** Propane is stored at $30\,°C$ ($303\,K$). Normal boiling point $T_b = 231\,K$. $c_{p,L} = 2.25\,kJ/kg\cdot K$. $H_{vap}(T_b) = 425\,kJ/kg$. Calculate the flashing fraction if the propane is suddenly depressurised.

**Solution:**

$$f = \frac{2.25 \cdot (303 - 231)}{425} = \frac{2.25 \cdot 72}{425} = \frac{162}{425}$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.381 \quad \text{or} \quad 38.1\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Interpretation: ~38% of the released propane instantly vaporises, drawing its latent heat from the remaining 62% which cools to the boiling point. The resulting two-phase release forms an aerosol cloud that disperses downwind.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mackay Pool Evaporation Rate for Pure Liquid Spill

Consider a circular pool of radius $r$ at uniform surface temperature $T_s$. The total evaporation rate is:

$$\dot{m}_{evap} = K \cdot A_{pool} \cdot \frac{P^{sat}(T_s) \cdot M}{R_u \cdot T_s}$$

The mass transfer coefficient $K$ in the Mackay model is:
$$K = 4.82 \times 10^{-3} \cdot u^{0.78} \cdot x^{-0.11}$$

where:
- $u$ = wind speed at reference height ($m/s$)
- $x$ = downwind distance / pool diameter (dimensionless, often $\approx 1$ for local estimates)

### Step-by-Step Computation
1. Compute pool area: $A_{pool} = \pi r^2$
2. Find $P^{sat}(T_s)$ from Antoine or given data.
3. Compute $K$ from wind speed.
4. Substitute into the master equation.

### Worked Example (KTU Style)
**Problem:** A circular pool of benzene (radius $10\,m$, $T_s = 25\,°C$) spills on a concrete bund. Wind speed $u = 4\,m/s$. Antoine constants for benzene: $A=6.90565$, $B=1211.033$, $C=220.79$ (with $T$ in °C and $P^{sat}$ in mmHg). Calculate the evaporation rate. Molecular weight $M = 78\,kg/kmol$.

**Step 1:** Pool area
$$A_{pool} = \pi \cdot 10^2 = 314.16\,m^2$$

**Step 2:** Saturation pressure at $T_s = 25\,°C$ ($298\,K$):
$$\log_{10}(P^{sat}_{mmHg}) = 6.90565 - \frac{1211.033}{25 + 220.79}$$

$$= 6.90565 - \frac{1211.033}{245.79} = 6.90565 - 4.9271 = 1.9786$$

$$P^{sat}_{mmHg} = 10^{1.9786} \approx 95.18\,mmHg$$

Convert to Pa:
$$P^{sat} = 95.18 \cdot 133.322 = 12{,}691\,Pa$$

**Step 3:** Compute $K$:
$$K = 4.82 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 4^{0.78} \cdot 1^{-0.11}$$

$$= 4.82 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3.249 = 0.01566\,m/s$$

**Step 4:** Total evaporation rate:
$$\dot{m} = 0.01566 \cdot 314.16 \cdot \frac{12{,}691 \cdot 78}{8314 \cdot 298}$$

$$= 4.92 \cdot \frac{989{,}898}{2{,}477{,}572}$$

$$= 4.92 \cdot 0.3995$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 1.97\,kg/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result — roughly 2 kg/s of benzene evaporating — translates to ~170 tonnes per day. Such a mass rate produces a flammable cloud extending kilometres downwind if not detected.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Flash Point from Vapour Pressure Data

**Given:** Antoine constants of n-hexane are $A=6.87784$, $B=1171.53$, $C=224.00$. LFL of n-hexane = $1.1\%$ by volume.

**Find:** Flash point in °C.

**Step 1:** LFL as mole fraction
$$y_{LFL} = 0.011$$

**Step 2:** At flash point, $P^{sat} = y_{LFL} \cdot P_{atm} = 0.011 \cdot 760\,mmHg = 8.36\,mmHg$

**Step 3:** Substitute into Antoine equation:
$$\log_{10}(8.36) = 6.87784 - \frac{1171.53}{T_f + 224.00}$$

$$0.9222 = 6.87784 - \frac{1171.53}{T_f + 224.00}$$

$$\frac{1171.53}{T_f + 224.00} = 5.9556$$

$$T_f + 224 = \frac{1171.53}{5.9556} = 196.71$$

$$T_f = 196.71 - 224 = -27.29\,°C$$

$$\boxed{T_{flash} \approx -27\,°C}$$

Comparison with literature value: $-22\,°C$ (closed cup). The discrepancy arises from the approximate nature of the Antoine fit and assuming ideal gas behaviour at low temperatures.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Connecting Concept to Hazard Identification

| Hazard Identification Step | Flash / Evaporation Input Required | KTU Module Link |
|---|---|---|
| Material classification (Class IA, IB, IC liquids) | Closed-cup flash point | OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106 |
| Defining flammable inventory in QRA | Total evaporation mass | Module 4 / Module 5 |
| Modelling pool fire duration | Pool area + evaporation rate | Module 3 onward |
| Determining downwind LFL contour | Pasquill-Gaussian dispersion | Module 3 / Module 5 |
| BLEVE / fireball distance estimation | Flash fraction + release mass | Module 3 advanced topic |
| Bund area sizing | Pool spread + evaporation | Module 4 design level |
| Toxic release severity (ERPG / IDLH) | Evaporation rate + dispersion | Module 5 consequence analysis |

---

## 3.5 The Three Critical Time Scales

When identifying a flash fire hazard, an engineer must compare three characteristic times:

1. **Evaporation timescale** $t_{evap} = M_{spill}/\dot{m}_{evap}$
2. **Ignition delay timescale** $t_{ign}$ — how long before an ignition source appears
3. **Vapour cloud travel timescale** $t_{disp} = x_{LFL}/u$ — time for cloud to disperse below LFL

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $t_{evap} > t_{ign}$ AND vapour remains above LFL before ignition occurs → **Flash Fire**.
> If $t_{ign}$ is small and the cloud reaches a congested region → **Vapour Cloud Explosion**.

These qualitative comparisons are routinely expected in KTU descriptive questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — Flash Fire Hazard Formation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Stored Under Pressure T1 P1] --> B{Storage Rupture or Valve Opening}
    B --> C[Pressure Drops to P2 below Psat at T1]
    C --> D[Flash Evaporation Begins]
    D --> E[Liquid Cools to Normal Boiling Point Tb]
    E --> F[Two Phase Release Liquid Plus Aerosol]
    F --> G[Air Entrainment and Pool Formation]
    G --> H[Continuous Pool Evaporation]
    H --> I[Vapour Cloud Generation Above Pool]
    I --> J[Atmospheric Dispersion Downwind]
    J --> K{Presence of Ignition Source in Cloud}
    K -- Yes --> L[Flash Fire or Vapour Cloud Explosion]
    K -- No --> M[Cloud Dilutes Below LFL Hazard Dissipates]
    L --> N[Thermal Radiation Damage Assessment]
    M --> O[Safe State Returned]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Evaporation Rate Predictor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In1[Liquid Identity] --> Block1[Property Database Look Up M Cp Tb Hvap LFL]
    In2[Spill Volume] --> Block2[Pool Geometry Calculator Area and Radius]
    In3[Substrate Type] --> Block3[Heat Flux Estimator Q star in W per m2]
    In4[Weather Inputs] --> Block4[Wind Speed and Stability Class Selector]
    Block1 --> Core[Evaporation Rate Solver Core]
    Block2 --> Core
    Block3 --> Core
    Block4 --> Core
    Core --> Out1[Mass Evaporation Rate m dot]
    Core --> Out2[Flashing Fraction f]
    Core --> Out3[Downwind LFL Distance]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Flash Fire versus Vapour Cloud Explosion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Flammable Vapour Cloud Released] --> Q1{Cloud Mass Above Lower Flammable Limit}
    Q1 -- No --> Safe[No Flammable Hazard]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Confined or Congested Region}
    Q2 -- No --> FlashFire[Flash Fire Outcome]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Cloud Composition Within Flammable Range}
    Q3 -- Marginal --> FlashFire
    Q3 -- Stoichiometric --> VCE[Vapour Cloud Explosion]
    FlashFire --> DamageA[Thermal Radiation Dominant Damage]
    VCE --> DamageB[Blast Overpressure Dominant Damage]
```

## 4.4 Schematic of Pool Evaporation with Three Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Regime1[Subcooled Liquid Cold Pool] --> Evap1[Evaporation Controlled by Mass Transfer through Air Boundary Layer]
    Regime2[Liquid at Ambient Temperature] --> Evap2[Evaporation Controlled by Heat Transfer from Ground]
    Regime3[Superheated Liquid above Boiling Point] --> Evap3[Boiling Regime Controlled by Latent Heat Supply]
    Evap1 --> Out1[Low Evaporation Flux kg per m2 s]
    Evap2 --> Out2[Moderate Evaporation Flux]
    Evap3 --> Out3[High Evaporation Flux plus Visible Vapour Plume]
```

## 4.5 Process Flow — Hazard Identification using Flash and Evaporation Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Step1[Step 1 Identify Chemicals in Process] --> Step2[Step 2 Look up Flash Point and LFL from SDS]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3 Classify Liquid per OSHA NFPA Category]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4 Determine Maximum Credible Spill Volume]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5 Apply Mackay Model for Pool Evaporation Rate]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6 Apply Pasquill Gaussian for Downwind Dispersion]
    Step6 --> Step7[Step 7 Plot Flash Fire Envelope]
    Step7 --> Step8[Step 8 Compare Envelope with Population Density]
    Step8 --> Step9[Step 9 Mitigate by Reducing Inventory or Adding Water Spray Curtains]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These schematics map the logical flow of analysis expected in KTU Board Examination answers for Module 3 — "Flash and Evaporation" within the broader context of hazard identification.
