<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question Paper]

**(a)** Define flash point of a liquid. How is it different from the fire point?

**Model Answer:**
The flash point of a liquid is the lowest temperature at which it gives off sufficient vapour to form an ignitable mixture with air near the liquid surface, producing a momentary flash when an ignition source is applied.

**Key distinctions:**
- **Flash point:** momentary flame propagation, ignition *not* self-sustaining.
- **Fire point:** continuous flame for at least 5 seconds, vapour production rate is high enough to sustain combustion.

Numerically, $T_{fire}$ is typically $5$–$20\,°C$ higher than $T_{flash}$ for the same liquid.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**(b)** A pool of acetone (molecular weight $58\,kg/kmol$) of diameter $15\,m$ spills at $25\,°C$. Vapour pressure of acetone at $25\,°C$ is $30\,kPa$. Wind speed $u = 5\,m/s$. Estimate the evaporation rate using the Mackay model. ($K = 4.82 \times 10^{-3} \cdot u^{0.78}$).

**Model Answer:**
- Pool area: $A = \pi \cdot (7.5)^2 = 176.71\,m^2$
- $K = 4.82 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 5^{0.78} = 4.82 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3.717 = 0.01792\,m/s$
- $T_s = 298\,K$, $P^{sat} = 30{,}000\,Pa$
- $\dot{m} = K \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{P^{sat} \cdot M}{R_u \cdot T_s}$
- $\dot{m} = 0.01792 \cdot 176.71 \cdot \dfrac{30{,}000 \cdot 58}{8314 \cdot 298}$
- $\dot{m} = 3.166 \cdot \dfrac{1{,}740{,}000}{2{,}477{,}572} = 3.166 \cdot 0.702 = \mathbf{2.22\,kg/s}$

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Sample 2024]

**(a)** Define the following terms with relevance to hazard identification:
- (i) Flash point
- (ii) Fire point
- (iii) Auto-ignition temperature
- (iv) Flash fire
- (v) Flashing fraction

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Flash Point:** The minimum temperature at which a liquid produces enough vapour to form a flammable mixture with air, evidenced by a momentary flash when an ignition source is applied. It is governed by the equation $P^{sat}(T_{flash}) = y_{LFL} \cdot P_{atm}$.

**Fire Point:** The minimum temperature at which the liquid produces enough vapour that combustion, once initiated by an external source, sustains itself for at least 5 seconds. Numerically, $T_{fire} > T_{flash}$ by a few degrees.

**Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT):** The minimum temperature at which a substance ignites spontaneously without any external flame or spark. It is determined purely by chemical kinetics (radical chain-branching rate exceeding termination rate) and depends strongly on the test apparatus geometry.

**Flash Fire:** The combustion of a flammable vapour cloud in which flame propagation is sub-sonic and confined within the cloud envelope. Pressure rise is negligible; damage is primarily thermal.

**Flashing Fraction:** The mass fraction of a pressurised liquid that instantaneously vaporises when depressurised below its saturation pressure at storage temperature. It is calculated as $f = c_{p,L}(T_1 - T_b)/H_{vap}(T_b)$.

---

**(b)** A 1000 kg storage of propane is released instantaneously from a pressurised vessel at $25\,°C$ through a rupture. The normal boiling point of propane is $-42\,°C$ ($231\,K$), the specific heat of liquid propane is $2.5\,kJ/kg\cdot K$, and the latent heat of vaporisation at the boiling point is $425\,kJ/kg$. The LFL of propane is $2.1\%$ by volume.
- (i) Calculate the mass of propane that flashes immediately.
- (ii) Compute the volume of the resulting propane-air cloud at LFL concentration. (Assume ambient $25\,°C$, $1\,atm$, ideal gas, $M_{propane} = 44\,kg/kmol$, $M_{air} = 29\,kg/kmol$.)

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i): Flashing fraction**

Initial temperature $T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298\,K$, $T_b = 231\,K$.

$$f = \frac{c_{p,L} (T_1 - T_b)}{H_{vap}} = \frac{2.5 \times (298 - 231)}{425}$$

$$f = \frac{2.5 \times 67}{425} = \frac{167.5}{425} = 0.3941$$

Mass flashed $= 0.3941 \times 1000 = \mathbf{394.1\,kg}$

**Part (ii): Cloud volume at LFL**

At LFL $= 2.1\%$, the mole fraction of propane is $y_p = 0.021$.

Moles of propane in cloud: $n_p = 394.1 / 44 = 8.957\,kmol$

Total moles in cloud: $n_{total} = n_p / y_p = 8.957 / 0.021 = 426.5\,kmol$

Moles of air: $n_{air} = 426.5 - 8.957 = 417.5\,kmol$

Average molecular weight of cloud:
$$\bar{M} = y_p \cdot M_p + (1 - y_p) \cdot M_{air} = 0.021 \cdot 44 + 0.979 \cdot 29 = 0.924 + 28.39 = 29.31\,kg/kmol$$

Density of cloud at $298\,K$, $1\,atm$ ($\approx 101.325\,kPa$):
$$\rho = \frac{P \bar{M}}{R_u T} = \frac{101{,}325 \times 29.31}{8314 \times 298} = \frac{2{,}969{,}840}{2{,}477{,}572} = 1.199\,kg/m^3$$

Total cloud mass: $m_{cloud} = n_{total} \cdot \bar{M} = 426.5 \times 29.31 = 12{,}503\,kg$

Volume: $V_{cloud} = m_{cloud} / \rho = 12{,}503 / 1.199 = \mathbf{10{,}428\,m^3}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Zone:**
> 1. Do NOT confuse LFL in **% volume** (here 2.1%) with **mole fraction** (0.021) when substituting into equations.
> 2. The flashing mass fraction must be calculated using **Kelvin** temperatures and **consistent SI units** (J/kg, not kJ/kg — the latent heat of vaporisation must be in $J/kg$ if $c_p$ is also in $J/kg\cdot K$, OR both in kJ equivalents). Most students lose marks by mixing units.
> 3. When asked for cloud volume, you must account for air + propane combined; do not give just the propane volume.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Sample 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Mackay pool evaporation model. A circular pool of toluene (molecular weight $92\,kg/kmol$) of diameter $20\,m$ is formed on a flat concrete surface at $30\,°C$ ($303\,K$). Vapour pressure of toluene at $30\,°C$ is $4{,}890\,Pa$. Wind speed $u = 3\,m/s$. Estimate the steady-state evaporation rate. Use $K = 4.82 \times 10^{-3} u^{0.78}$ ($x \approx 1$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Theory:** The Mackay model applies to pure liquid pools where mass transfer through the boundary layer of stagnant air is rate-limiting. The empirical mass transfer coefficient $K$ incorporates wind speed and pool size effects.

$$K = 4.82 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (3)^{0.78} = 4.82 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.379 = 0.01147\,m/s$$

Pool area: $A = \pi \cdot (10)^2 = 314.16\,m^2$

$$\dot{m} = K \cdot A \cdot \frac{P^{sat} \cdot M}{R_u \cdot T_s}$$

$$= 0.01147 \cdot 314.16 \cdot \frac{4{,}890 \cdot 92}{8314 \cdot 303}$$

$$= 3.602 \cdot \frac{449{,}880}{2{,}519{,}142}$$

$$= 3.602 \cdot 0.1786 = \mathbf{0.643\,kg/s}$$

**Discussion:** This corresponds to about 55.5 tonnes per day — a serious flammable hazard if not contained.

---

**(b)** Discuss the role of flash point and evaporation in hazard identification and risk assessment (HIRA) of a petroleum storage terminal. List the major mitigation measures adopted in industry to control flash and evaporation hazards. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role in HIRA:**

1. **Material Classification** — Flash point categorises liquids as Class IA ($<22.8\,°C$), Class IB ($<37.8\,°C$), Class IC ($22.8$–$37.8\,°C$), Class II ($37.8$–$60\,°C$), Class IIIA ($60$–$93\,°C$), Class IIIB ($>93\,°C$) as per OSHA/NFPA. This determines storage, handling, and area classification.

2. **Source Modelling** — Evaporation rate (via Mackay) provides the continuous emission rate $Q_e$ for dispersion calculations.

3. **Consequence Analysis** — LFL contour from Pasquill-Gaussian dispersion defines the geographical extent of flash fire hazard.

4. **Risk Quantification** — Integrating consequence with population density and ignition probability gives individual and societal risk.

**Mitigation Measures:**

- **Vapour Recovery Units (VRUs)** — capture evaporated vapour from storage tanks (internal floating roof tanks, vapour balancing systems).
- **Inert Gas Blanketing** — nitrogen blanketing prevents flammable mixture formation in storage headspace.
- **Dyke Bunding** — concrete bunds around tanks limit pool spread.
- **Water Spray / Deluge Curtains** — cool vapour clouds and prevent ignition.
- **Water Mist / Foam Systems** — suppress pool evaporation rate by orders of magnitude.
- **Flame Arrestors** — prevent flame propagation back into the tank.
- **Safe Separation Distances** — based on API 753 / IS 14489 minimum spacing.
- **Vent Conservation** — pressure/vacuum relief valves sized for fire exposure.
- **Secondary Containment** — double-walled tanks, spill containment basins.
- **Process Control** — reduce storage temperatures, lower inventory, use intrinsically safe equipment.
- **Hazardous Area Classification (Zone 0, 1, 2)** — electrical equipment ratings per IS/IEC 60079.
- **Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD)** — isolate inventory upon leak detection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** When asked to "discuss", students often write only definitions and skip **practical mitigation**. You must connect theory to **actual industrial control measures** to score full marks. Always link each mitigation back to the physics you explained in the first half of the answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency Recall Items for KTU Module 3 — Flash and Evaporation:**

- **Flash Point = $P^{sat}(T_{flash}) = y_{LFL} \cdot P_{atm}$** — this is the *definition* you must quote in definition questions.
- **Closed-cup flash point < Open-cup flash point** — always a two-mark question favourite.
- **Fire point is typically 5–20 °C above flash point.**
- **AIT is unrelated to flash point** — purely chemical kinetic phenomenon.
- **Flash Fire ≠ Vapour Cloud Explosion.** The former has negligible overpressure; the latter requires partial confinement/congestion and is supersonic.
- **Flashing fraction** $f = c_{p,L}(T_1 - T_b)/H_{vap}(T_b)$. Always use **Kelvin** temperatures and **consistent energy units**.
- **Mackay model** for pool evaporation: $\dot{m} = K \cdot A \cdot P^{sat}(T_s) \cdot M / (R_u T_s)$. Pure liquid assumption.
- **Wind effect on K:** higher wind → higher $K$ (exponent $0.78$). Increase in pool radius tends to **reduce** local evaporation flux per unit area but **increase** total evaporation rate.
- **LFL of propane ≈ 2.1%, n-hexane ≈ 1.1%, methanol ≈ 6%, benzene ≈ 1.3%.** Memorise these for KTU numericals.
- **Le Chatelier's rule** for mixtures: $LFL_{mix} = 100 / \sum(y_i / LFL_i)$ — appears in composite fuel scenarios.
- **Pasquill-Gifford dispersion** — remember to include stability class (A–F) — F is most stable (night, low wind), A most unstable (sunny day, strong wind).
- **Flash fire envelope** is the locus where downwind concentration equals LFL.
- **Vapour cloud volume** $V = (\text{total cloud mass})/(\text{cloud density})$, where cloud density uses **average molecular weight** of mixture.
- **Mitigation hierarchy for flash hazards:** Eliminate → Substitute (less volatile) → Reduce inventory → Engineering controls (VRU, inerting, dykes) → Administrative → PPE.
- **Three times-scales comparison** determines outcome: $t_{evap}$ vs $t_{ign}$ vs $t_{disp}$.
- **Connection to next modules:** evaporation output feeds Module 4 (pool fire, BLEVE) and Module 5 (QRA, F-N curves).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dispersion Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dispersion Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Atmospheric Dispersion Models** are mathematical formulations used to predict the spatial and temporal distribution of hazardous chemical vapors, gases, or particulates released accidentally or continuously into the ambient atmosphere. These models quantify the downwind concentration of a pollutant as a function of source strength, release rate, meteorological conditions, and topographic features.

In the KTU 2024 framework, dispersion modeling is positioned as the *quantitative bridge* between the **source model** (release rate) and the **consequence model** (toxic exposure / flammability). The governing equation of state for the atmosphere is the **Continuity Equation**, while the transport behavior is described by the **Advection–Diffusion Equation** (also called the convective diffusion equation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3)**
> Dispersion models are the *backbone* of consequence analysis. The student is expected to know the **Gaussian Plume Model (steady-state, neutrally buoyant)**, **Gaussian Puff Model (transient)**, **Heavy Gas Dispersion Models**, **Pasquill–Gifford Stability Classes (A–F)**, and **Briggs Plume Rise Equations**. Numerical problems involving ground-level concentration $C_{(x,0,0)}$ at the centerline are extremely high-yield in ESE.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Gaussian Plume Model (GPM)**
> The **Gaussian Plume Model** is a steady-state dispersion model in which the concentration of a gas in the atmosphere follows a bivariate normal (Gaussian) distribution in the crosswind and vertical directions. The plume is advected by the mean wind speed $u$ along the x-axis, while turbulent diffusion spreads it according to standard deviations $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing behind a moving train and watching its **smoke plume**. The smoke does not travel in a straight line. Instead, it spreads out in the shape of a **cone** because turbulence in the surrounding air mixes the smoke molecules. The faster the wind blows, the more the plume is *stretched* downwind. On a sunny afternoon with strong solar heating (unstable atmosphere), the plume spreads much wider than on a calm, clear night (stable atmosphere) because warm air rising from the ground creates vigorous vertical mixing.

**Dispersion models simply put mathematical numbers to this "smoke cone."** They tell safety engineers: *"If 100 kg of chlorine leaks from this storage tank, what will be the toxic concentration 500 m downwind at ground level?"*

**Physical constants / Standard metrics (bolded):**
- Standard reference wind speed: $u_{10} = 10\ \text{m/s}$ measured at $z = 10\ \text{m}$ height.
- **Pasquill–Gifford Stability Classes**: **A (very unstable), B (moderately unstable), C (slightly unstable), D (neutral), E (slightly stable), F (very stable)**.
- Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$.
- Standard molecular weight of air: $M_{air} = 28.97\ \text{g/mol}$.
- Typical dispersion coefficients: $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$ expressed as **power-law functions of downwind distance $x$**.
- Concentration unit: **ppm (parts per million)** for toxic gases, or $\text{mg/m}^3$ for particulates.

## 1.3 Classification of Dispersion Models

| Model Category | Governing Physics | Typical Application | KTU Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gaussian Plume** | Steady-state, neutrally buoyant | Continuous release, buoyant gas | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Gaussian Puff** | Transient, neutral buoyancy | Instantaneous release, short duration | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| **Heavy Gas (Britter–McQuaid)** | Density-driven (gravity spreading) | LNG, LPG, dense chlorine clouds | ⭐⭐⭐ High |
| **Box / Slab Models** | Top-hat profile assumption | Initial phase of heavy gas release | ⭐⭐ Medium |
| **3-D CFD Models (Fluent, OpenFOAM)** | Full Navier–Stokes | Complex terrain, building wakes | ⭐ Low (theory only) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Advection–Diffusion Equation (ADE)

The starting point for ALL Gaussian-type models is the steady-state, three-dimensional form of the **Advection–Diffusion Equation**:

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_x \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} + K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

where:
- $C(x,y,z)$ = pollutant concentration $\left[\text{kg/m}^3\right]$
- $u$ = mean wind speed in the x-direction [m/s]
- $K_x, K_y, K_z$ = turbulent eddy diffusivities [m²/s] in $x, y, z$ directions

The **Why** of this equation: A parcel of polluted air is *transported* (advected) by the bulk wind, and *spread* (diffused) by turbulence. The equation is a mass balance on a control volume.

The **How** of getting Gaussian Plume: If we assume (a) wind is along x only, (b) diffusion in x-direction is negligible compared to advection ($K_x \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} \approx 0$), and (c) $K_y$ and $K_z$ are constants, then the ADE reduces to:

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

Solving this with the point-source boundary condition gives the **Gaussian Plume Equation**.

## 2.2 The Gaussian Plume Equation (Centerline)

For a continuous point source at effective height $H$ with emission rate $Q$ [kg/s], the downwind concentration is:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two exponential terms in z?**
> The first term is the **real plume**. The second term is the **"image" plume** representing reflection of the plume at the ground (a no-flux boundary). The ground cannot absorb pollutants upward, so mathematically we *mirror* the source below the ground.

## 2.3 Ground-Level Centerline Concentration ($C_{GL}$)

On the **centerline** ($y = 0$) at **ground level** ($z = 0$), the equation simplifies dramatically because $H^2 = H^2$ cancels the asymmetry:

$$C_{GL}(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is THE most-asked equation in KTU.** Every numerical problem on Gaussian Plume ultimately reduces to this form. Memorize the variables: $Q$ (emission rate), $u$ (wind speed), $\sigma_y$ & $\sigma_z$ (dispersion coefficients as functions of stability class and distance), $H$ (effective stack height).

## 2.4 Effective Stack Height ($H$)

The plume does not travel from the physical stack top. It first **rises** due to buoyancy and momentum. The **effective height** is:

$$H = h_s + \Delta h$$

where $h_s$ = physical stack height [m], and $\Delta h$ = **plume rise** [m].

## 2.5 Briggs Plume Rise Equations (Buoyancy-Dominated, $F_b < 55$)

Briggs (1969/1971) proposed widely-used plume rise formulas. For **buoyancy-dominated** plumes in unstable/neutral conditions:

$$\Delta h = \frac{1.6 \cdot F_b^{1/3} \cdot x_f^{2/3}}{u}$$

where:
- $F_b = g \cdot V_s \cdot d^2 \cdot \frac{(T_s - T_a)}{T_s}$ = buoyancy flux parameter [m⁴/s³]
- $V_s$ = stack gas exit velocity [m/s]
- $d$ = stack exit diameter [m]
- $T_s$ = stack gas temperature [K]
- $T_a$ = ambient air temperature [K]
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ = acceleration due to gravity
- $x_f$ = downwind distance to final rise [m] (depends on stability class)

**Final rise distance $x_f$ values (Briggs):**
- Unstable (A–B): $x_f = 3.5 \cdot x^*$
- Neutral (D): $x_f = 34.5 \cdot F_b^{2/5}$ (when $F_b \geq 55$); $x_f = 14.4 \cdot F_b^{2/5}$ (when $F_b < 55$)
- Stable (E–F): $\Delta h = 2.6 \cdot \left(\frac{F_b}{u \cdot s}\right)^{1/3}$, where $s$ = stability parameter.

## 2.6 Pasquill–Gifford Stability Classes

Stability class is determined by **surface wind speed** and **incoming solar radiation** (day) or **cloud cover** (night):

| Wind Speed (m/s) | Strong Day | Moderate Day | Slight Day | Thin Overcast / 4/8 Cloud | 8/8 Cloud | Clear Night |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| < 2 | **A** | A–B | B | C | D | — |
| 2–3 | A–B | B | C | C | D | F |
| 3–5 | B | B–C | C | D | D | E |
| 5–6 | C | C–D | D | D | D | D |
| > 6 | C | D | D | D | D | D |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students confuse "A" with "F." **A = most unstable (worst dispersion → lowest ground concentration but largest area). F = most stable (worst-case for accumulation, plume travels long distances with little vertical spread).** For emergency planning, both A and F must often be evaluated.

## 2.7 Pasquill–Gifford Dispersion Coefficient Correlations (Briggs Formulas)

The most-used closed-form expressions for $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ are Briggs's fits:

$$\sigma_y(x) = \frac{a \cdot x}{\left(1 + b \cdot x\right)^{c_y}}$$

$$\sigma_z(x) = \frac{d \cdot x}{\left(1 + e \cdot x\right)^{c_z}}$$

| Stability Class | $a$ | $b$ | $c_y$ | $d$ | $e$ | $c_z$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0.22 | 0.0001 | 0.5 | 0.20 | 0 | 0 |
| **B** | 0.16 | 0.0001 | 0.5 | 0.12 | 0 | 0 |
| **C** | 0.11 | 0.0001 | 0.5 | 0.08 | 0.0002 | -0.5 |
| **D** | 0.08 | 0.0001 | 0.5 | 0.06 | 0.0015 | -0.5 |
| **E** | 0.06 | 0.0001 | 0.5 | 0.03 | 0.0003 | -1.0 |
| **F** | 0.04 | 0.0001 | 0.5 | 0.016 | 0.0003 | -1.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Trick:** For class A and B, $\sigma_z$ grows **linearly** (because $e = 0$). For class D, $\sigma_z$ saturates (the "mixing height" limits vertical spread). Always check $z \le L$ where $L$ is the **mixing layer height** (typically 500–2000 m depending on stability).

## 2.8 Heavy Gas Dispersion — Britter–McQuaid Model

For dense gases (e.g., LNG, chlorine, propane), the cloud initially **slumps** under gravity and behaves as a **gravity current**, NOT a Gaussian plume. The **Britter–McQuaid (1978) model** is empirically derived:

$$\frac{C_m}{C_0} = f\!\left(\frac{x}{x_c}\right)$$

where $C_m$ = mean concentration at downwind position, $C_0$ = initial concentration (typically pure vapor), and the **critical distance** is:

$$x_c = \left(\frac{V_0^{1/3}}{u}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{g_0 V_0^{1/3}}{u^2}\right)^{-1/2}$$

with $g_0 = g \cdot \frac{\rho_0 - \rho_a}{\rho_a}$ (reduced gravity).

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{GL} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Ground-level centerline concentration |
| 2 | $H = h_s + \Delta h$ | Effective stack height |
| 3 | $\Delta h = 1.6 \cdot F_b^{1/3} \cdot x_f^{2/3} / u$ | Briggs plume rise (buoyancy) |
| 4 | $F_b = g V_s d^2 (T_s - T_a) / T_s$ | Buoyancy flux |
| 5 | $\sigma_y = a x / (1 + bx)^{c_y}$ | Lateral dispersion |
| 6 | $\sigma_z = d x / (1 + e x)^{c_z}$ | Vertical dispersion |
| 7 | $C_{puff}(x,y,z,t) = \dfrac{Q}{(2\pi)^{3/2} \sigma_x \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-ut)^2}{2\sigma_x^2}-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}-\dfrac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Gaussian Puff concentration |
| 8 | $ERPG / AEGL / IDLH$ | Toxic endpoint reference values |
| 9 | $x_c$ (Britter–McQuaid) | Heavy gas critical distance |
| 10 | $C_m / C_0 = f(x / x_c)$ | Britter–McQuaid correlation graph |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are embedded in software like **ALOHA (EPA), PHAST, TRACE, FRED (Shell), and EFFECTS (TNO)**. As a KTU student, you must be able to derive the equations from first principles and verify software output, not just push buttons.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gaussian Plume Equation (Coordinate System)

### Step 1: Define the coordinate frame

The source is at the origin $(0, 0, 0)$ in a Cartesian frame. The wind blows along the +x direction. The plume spreads in the y (crosswind) and z (vertical) directions due to turbulence.

### Step 2: Apply the steady-state advection–diffusion equation

In steady state, $\partial C / \partial t = 0$. We further assume:
- $K_x = 0$ (longitudinal diffusion is negligible compared to wind advection)
- $K_y$, $K_z$ are constants (homogeneous turbulence)

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

### Step 3: Introduce the substitution $\xi = x/u$

The wind advection just translates time into distance via $x = ut$. Divide both sides by $u$:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \frac{K_y}{u} \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + \frac{K_z}{u} \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

### Step 4: Recognize the form of a diffusion equation

This is mathematically equivalent to the classical heat equation. Its solution in the absence of boundaries is a **Gaussian** in $y$ and $z$:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q^*}{(4\pi K_y x / u)^{1/2}(4\pi K_z x / u)^{1/2}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2 u}{4K_y x} - \frac{z^2 u}{4K_z x}\right)$$

Here $Q^*$ is the *virtual* emission per unit depth. For a 3-D point source of strength $Q$ [kg/s], $Q^* = Q / (2\pi)$ after appropriate integration.

### Step 5: Re-parameterize in terms of $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$

By definition of the Gaussian standard deviation, the diffusion coefficient is related to the variance:

$$\sigma_y^2 = \frac{2 K_y x}{u}, \quad \sigma_z^2 = \frac{2 K_z x}{u}$$

Substituting these back, we get the **ideal Gaussian plume from a ground-level point source**:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

### Step 6: Add the image source for ground reflection

For a source at height $H$ above the ground, place a mirror source at $z = -H$. Superpose both contributions:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

This is the **final Gaussian Plume Equation**.

### Step 7: Reduce to ground-level centerline form

Setting $y = 0$ and $z = 0$:

$$\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right] = 2 \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

The factor of 2 in the numerator cancels the 2 in the denominator of $2\pi$, leaving:

$$\boxed{C_{GL} = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Question)

**Problem:** A point source emits **chlorine gas** continuously at a rate of $Q = 0.10\ \text{kg/s}$. The stack height is $h_s = 50\ \text{m}$, the gas exit velocity is $V_s = 12\ \text{m/s}$, the stack diameter is $d = 1.5\ \text{m}$, and the gas temperature is $T_s = 400\ \text{K}$. The ambient temperature is $T_a = 298\ \text{K}$, and the wind speed at 10 m height is $u = 4\ \text{m/s}$. The atmosphere is in **Pasquill class D (neutral)**.

Calculate the **ground-level centerline concentration** at a downwind distance of $x = 1000\ \text{m}$.

### Step 1: Compute the buoyancy flux $F_b$

$$F_b = g \cdot V_s \cdot d^2 \cdot \frac{(T_s - T_a)}{T_s}$$

$$F_b = 9.81 \cdot 12 \cdot (1.5)^2 \cdot \frac{(400 - 298)}{400}$$

$$F_b = 9.81 \cdot 12 \cdot 2.25 \cdot 0.255$$

$$\boxed{F_b = 67.55\ \text{m}^4/\text{s}^3}$$

### Step 2: Compute the final plume rise $x_f$ (Class D, $F_b < 55$? — No, $F_b = 67.55$)

Since $F_b \geq 55$, use the Class D neutral formula:

$$x_f = 34.5 \cdot F_b^{2/5} = 34.5 \cdot (67.55)^{0.4}$$

Compute $(67.55)^{0.4}$: take $\ln(67.55) = 4.213$, multiply by 0.4 = 1.685, exponentiate: $e^{1.685} = 5.39$.

$$x_f = 34.5 \cdot 5.39 = 186.0\ \text{m}$$

### Step 3: Compute the plume rise $\Delta h$ (buoyancy-dominated)

$$\Delta h = \frac{1.6 \cdot F_b^{1/3} \cdot x_f^{2/3}}{u}$$

Compute $F_b^{1/3} = (67.55)^{1/3}$. $(67.55)^{1/3} \approx 4.066$ (since $4^3 = 64$).

Compute $x_f^{2/3} = (186.0)^{2/3}$. $(186.0)^{1/3} \approx 5.72$, then square: $5.72^2 = 32.7$.

$$\Delta h = \frac{1.6 \cdot 4.066 \cdot 32.7}{4} = \frac{212.8}{4} = 53.2\ \text{m}$$

### Step 4: Effective stack height

$$H = h_s + \Delta h = 50 + 53.2 = 103.2\ \text{m}$$

### Step 5: Dispersion coefficients for Class D at $x = 1000\ \text{m}$

Using Briggs formulas with $a = 0.08$, $b = 0.0001$, $c_y = 0.5$:

$$\sigma_y = \frac{0.08 \cdot 1000}{(1 + 0.0001 \cdot 1000)^{0.5}} = \frac{80}{(1.1)^{0.5}} = \frac{80}{1.0488} = 76.3\ \text{m}$$

For $\sigma_z$ (Class D): $d = 0.06$, $e = 0.0015$, $c_z = -0.5$:

$$\sigma_z = \frac{0.06 \cdot 1000}{(1 + 0.0015 \cdot 1000)^{-0.5}} = 60 \cdot (2.5)^{0.5} = 60 \cdot 1.581 = 94.9\ \text{m}$$

### Step 6: Plug into ground-level centerline equation

$$C_{GL} = \frac{0.10}{\pi \cdot 4 \cdot 76.3 \cdot 94.9} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{(103.2)^2}{2 \cdot (94.9)^2}\right)$$

First, denominator: $\pi \cdot 4 \cdot 76.3 \cdot 94.9 = \pi \cdot 28968 = 91{,}010$.

Exponent: $\dfrac{10650.2}{2 \cdot 9006.0} = \dfrac{10650.2}{18012} = 0.5913$.

$\exp(-0.5913) = 0.5535$.

$$C_{GL} = \frac{0.10}{91010} \cdot 0.5535 = 1.099 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.5535 = 6.08 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

Convert to $\text{mg/m}^3$: $C_{GL} = 6.08 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mg/m}^3 = 0.608\ \mu\text{g/m}^3$.

Convert to ppm (chlorine, $M = 70.9$ g/mol): 

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{C_{mg/m^3} \cdot 24.45}{M} = \frac{0.000608 \cdot 24.45}{70.9} = 2.1 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{ppm}$$

### Step 7: Interpretation

At 1 km downwind, the ground-level chlorine concentration is **0.00021 ppm**, far below the **AEGL-1 (0.5 ppm)**, **AEGL-2 (2.0 ppm)**, and **AEGL-3 (5.0 ppm)**. This means a release of 0.10 kg/s from a 50 m stack under neutral stability poses no off-site toxic hazard at 1 km. However, sensitivity to **stability class** is huge — under **F-class stable** conditions, the plume travels farther with less vertical spread, potentially elevating concentrations dramatically.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget the factor-of-2 from image-source reflection, miss the $\exp(-H^2/2\sigma_z^2)$ ground-decay term, or use wrong Briggs coefficients for the wrong stability class. Always check: which class am I in? Which formula table am I using?

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Atmospheric Dispersion Modeling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Release Scenario] --> B[Estimate Source Emission Rate Q kg/s]
    B --> C{Material Density?}
    C -- Light Neutral Gas --> D[Apply Gaussian Plume Model]
    C -- Dense Gas --> E[Apply Britter-McQuaid or SLAB]
    D --> F[Determine Stability Class A-F]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Get Wind Speed u and Direction]
    G --> H[Compute Plume Rise Delta h via Briggs]
    H --> I[Compute Effective Height H = hs + Delta h]
    I --> J[Calculate Sigma y and Sigma z]
    J --> K[Apply Gaussian Plume Equation for C x,y,z]
    K --> L[Compare with Toxic Endpoints ERPG/AEGL]
    L --> M[Determine Hazard Zones and Consequence Distance]
```

## 4.2 Gaussian Plume 3-D Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stack Source at H] --> B[Plume Centerline along x axis]
    B --> C[Crosswind Spread sigma y]
    B --> D[Vertical Spread sigma z]
    D --> E[Ground Reflection Image Source at -H]
    C --> F[Concentration Iso-surface Gaussian]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Ground Level Concentration C sub GL]
```

## 4.3 Pasquill Stability Class Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Day or Night] --> B{Is it Day?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Determine Incoming Solar Radiation]
    B -- No --> D[Determine Cloud Cover]
    C --> E[Strong / Moderate / Slight]
    E --> F[Cross Reference Wind Speed Table]
    D --> G[Thin Overcast or 8/8 Cloud]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Assign Stability A B C D E F]
    H --> I[Class A = Very Unstable Most Mixing]
    H --> J[Class D = Neutral]
    H --> K[Class F = Very Stable Least Mixing]
```

## 4.4 Comparison of Plume vs Puff Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dispersion Model Selection] --> B{Release Duration}
    B -- Continuous --> C[Gaussian Plume Model]
    B -- Instantaneous --> D[Gaussian Puff Model]
    C --> E[Steady state: dC dt = 0]
    C --> F[Concentration decreases with distance]
    D --> G[Cloud moves with wind at speed u]
    D --> H[Cloud grows in size as sigma_x sigma_y sigma_z increase with t]
    E --> I[Use for flares, vents, pipelines]
    G --> J[Use for vessel rupture, catastrophic failure]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Heavy Gas vs Neutral Dispersion

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Source Release] --> B{Initial Density Relative to Air}
    B -- Lighter than air --> C[Passive Buoyancy]
    B -- Heavier than air --> D[Gravity Slumping]
    C --> E[Gaussian Plume Regime]
    D --> F[Slumping Phase]
    F --> G[Transition Phase]
    G --> H[Passive Dispersion Phase]
    E --> I[Consequence Analysis]
    H --> I
    F --> J[Box Model Volume Conservation]
    J --> H
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**
**Define the Gaussian Plume Model. What are the assumptions made in its derivation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Gaussian Plume Model** is a steady-state mathematical model that predicts the concentration of a pollutant downwind of a continuous point source, assuming the concentration distribution is **bivariate Gaussian** in the crosswind and vertical directions.
**Assumptions (any 3, 1 mark each):**
1. Steady-state conditions (concentration at a point does not vary with time).
2. Wind speed and direction are constant with height and time.
3. Pollutant is conservative (no chemical reaction or deposition).
4. The plume is advected along the x-direction with mean wind speed $u$.
5. Turbulent diffusion coefficients are constant (homogeneous turbulence).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**What is meant by Pasquill–Gifford stability classification? List all six classes with a one-line description each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Pasquill–Gifford stability classification** is a six-category scheme (A–F) used to characterize the **stability of the lower atmosphere** for dispersion calculations, based on surface wind speed and solar radiation (day) or cloud cover (night).

| Class | Description |
|---|---|
| A | Very unstable (strong solar heating, low wind) |
| B | Moderately unstable |
| C | Slightly unstable |
| D | Neutral (overcast, high wind) |
| E | Slightly stable |
| F | Very stable (clear night, light wind) |

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a) [7 marks] Derive the Gaussian Plume Equation for a continuous elevated point source, starting from the advection–diffusion equation. State all assumptions clearly.**

**Model Answer:**

The steady-state advection–diffusion equation in 3-D Cartesian coordinates is:

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_x \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} + K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

**Assumptions (1 mark each, choose any 5):**
1. Steady state: $\partial C/\partial t = 0$.
2. Wind is along x-axis only; $v = w = 0$.
3. Longitudinal diffusion is negligible: $K_x = 0$.
4. $K_y$ and $K_z$ are constants.
5. Source is a point source of mass emission rate $Q$ [kg/s].
6. No chemical reaction or deposition.

With these assumptions, the equation reduces to (1 mark):

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

This is mathematically identical to the 2-D heat equation. Its solution in the unbounded domain is a **bivariate Gaussian** (2 marks):

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

For a source at height $H$ above a non-absorbing ground, an image source at $-H$ is added (2 marks):

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

**Q. (b) [7 marks] Calculate the ground-level centerline concentration 1 km downwind of a stack releasing SO₂ at 5 kg/s. Wind speed = 5 m/s. Stack height = 60 m, gas exit velocity = 10 m/s, stack diameter = 2 m, stack temperature = 350 K, ambient temperature = 300 K. Stability class is D. Use Briggs correlations. Comment on whether the result is hazardous (AEGL-2 for SO₂ = 0.75 ppm).**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Buoyancy flux (1 mark)**

$$F_b = g V_s d^2 \frac{(T_s - T_a)}{T_s} = 9.81 \cdot 10 \cdot 4 \cdot \frac{50}{350} = 56.06\ \text{m}^4/\text{s}^3$$

**Step 2: Final rise distance (Class D, $F_b \geq 55$) (1 mark)**

$$x_f = 34.5 \cdot F_b^{2/5} = 34.5 \cdot (56.06)^{0.4} = 34.5 \cdot 5.30 = 182.9\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3: Plume rise (1 mark)**

$$\Delta h = \frac{1.6 \cdot (56.06)^{1/3} \cdot (182.9)^{2/3}}{5}$$

$$(56.06)^{1/3} \approx 3.83, \quad (182.9)^{1/3} \approx 5.68 \Rightarrow (182.9)^{2/3} \approx 32.3$$

$$\Delta h = \frac{1.6 \cdot 3.83 \cdot 32.3}{5} = \frac{198.0}{5} = 39.6\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Effective height (1 mark)**

$$H = 60 + 39.6 = 99.6\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5: $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$ at $x = 1000$ m, Class D (1 mark)**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{0.08 \cdot 1000}{(1 + 0.1)^{0.5}} = 76.3\ \text{m}$$

$$\sigma_z = \frac{0.06 \cdot 1000}{(1 + 1.5)^{-0.5}} = 60 \cdot (2.5)^{0.5} = 94.9\ \text{m}$$

**Step 6: Ground-level concentration (1 mark)**

$$C_{GL} = \frac{5}{\pi \cdot 5 \cdot 76.3 \cdot 94.9} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{9920.2}{2 \cdot 9006}\right)$$

$$= \frac{5}{113{,}800} \cdot \exp(-0.551) = 4.39 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 0.576 = 2.53 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$= 0.0253\ \text{mg/m}^3$$

Convert to ppm: $\dfrac{0.0253 \cdot 24.45}{64.066} = 0.00965\ \text{ppm} \approx 9.65 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{ppm}$.

**Step 7: Hazard comparison (1 mark)**

Since $0.00965\ \text{ppm} \ll 0.75\ \text{ppm}$ (AEGL-2), the off-site impact at 1 km is **not hazardous** under these conditions. However, the analysis should be repeated for **Class F (stable)** to capture worst-case travel of the plume.

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q. (a) [7 marks] What is the Britter–McQuaid model? When is it preferred over the Gaussian Plume Model? Describe the procedure to determine the downwind concentration of a heavy gas release.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Britter–McQuaid (1978) model** is an **empirical correlation model** for the dispersion of **dense (heavier-than-air) gas clouds**. It relates the **dimensionless concentration** $C_m / C_0$ to the **dimensionless downwind distance** $x / x_c$ via a graphical/nomograph-based relationship. (2 marks)

**Why preferred over Gaussian (1 mark each):**
- Gaussian Plume assumes **passive (neutral buoyancy) dispersion** and **bivariate Gaussian concentration profile**. This is invalid for dense gases where the cloud **slumps** under gravity and forms a **gravity current** with a near-uniform concentration core.
- Heavy gases show **gravity-driven lateral spreading**, **lower vertical mixing**, and a **top-hat velocity profile** rather than Gaussian. The cloud hugs the ground and travels long distances.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Determine **initial release volume $V_0$** and **initial concentration $C_0$** (often pure vapor).
2. Compute **reduced gravity** $g_0 = g \cdot (\rho_0 - \rho_a) / \rho_a$.
3. Compute the **critical distance** $x_c = (V_0^{1/3} / u) \cdot (g_0 V_0^{1/3} / u^2)^{-1/2}$.
4. For a given downwind position $x$, compute the **dimensionless distance** $X = x / x_c$.
5. Read the **dimensionless concentration** $C^* = C_m / C_0$ from the Britter–McQuaid nomograph.
6. Compute the mean concentration $C_m = C^* \cdot C_0$.
7. Compare with toxic endpoints (AEGL/ERPG).

**Q. (b) [7 marks] A 10 m³ storage tank of liquid chlorine ruptures catastrophically. The wind speed is 3 m/s. The ambient temperature is 298 K. Compute the critical distance $x_c$ and the downwind mean concentration at $x = 500\ \text{m}$. Chlorine vapor density at 298 K = 13.2 kg/m³, air density = 1.18 kg/m³.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Initial concentration (1 mark)**

Assume pure chlorine vapor: $C_0 = 13.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$.

**Step 2: Reduced gravity (1 mark)**

$$g_0 = 9.81 \cdot \frac{13.2 - 1.18}{1.18} = 9.81 \cdot 10.19 = 99.97\ \text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 3: Critical distance (2 marks)**

$$V_0^{1/3} = (10)^{1/3} = 2.154\ \text{m}$$

$$\frac{g_0 V_0^{1/3}}{u^2} = \frac{99.97 \cdot 2.154}{9} = 23.92$$

$$x_c = \frac{2.154}{3} \cdot (23.92)^{-1/2} = 0.718 \cdot 0.2045 = 0.147\ \text{m}$$

**Wait — this is unrealistically small.** The Britter–McQuaid correlation requires the **entrainment volume** approach for instantaneous releases. For a finite-duration release we use the **volumetric flow rate $Q_v$** instead. Let us redefine:

**Re-evaluation using volumetric flow rate $Q_v = V_0 / \Delta t$.** Assume release occurs in $\Delta t = 60\ \text{s}$:

$$Q_v = 10/60 = 0.1667\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

The Britter–McQuaid critical distance for continuous release is $x_c = (Q_v / u) \cdot (g_0 Q_v / u^2 D^2)^{-1/2}$ where $D$ is the source dimension. For simplification, the dimensionless distance is $X = x / (Q_v^{2/3} / (g_0^{1/3} \cdot u^{2/3}))$.

**Step 4: Recompute $x_c$ (2 marks)**

$$x_c = \frac{Q_v^{2/3}}{g_0^{1/3} \cdot u^{2/3}} = \frac{(0.1667)^{2/3}}{(99.97)^{1/3} \cdot 3^{2/3}}$$

$$(0.1667)^{2/3} = 0.305, \quad (99.97)^{1/3} = 4.64, \quad 3^{2/3} = 2.08$$

$$x_c = \frac{0.305}{4.64 \cdot 2.08} = \frac{0.305}{9.65} = 0.0316\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5: Dimensionless distance at x = 500 m (1 mark)**

$$X = \frac{x}{x_c} = \frac{500}{0.0316} = 15{,}820$$

This is way off the Britter–McQuaid graph range (valid up to X ≈ 1000). Therefore, at 500 m, the heavy gas has **completely transitioned to passive dispersion**, and the **Gaussian Plume** should be used instead. The Britter–McQuaid graph is valid only in the near-field.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing Gaussian Plume formulas with heavy gas equations.** They are NOT interchangeable. Identify density first.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in image-source reflection, leading to a concentration that is 50% too low.
> 3. **Unit confusion**: $Q$ is in kg/s, but ppm conversion requires dividing by molecular weight and multiplying by 24.45 (molar volume at 25°C, 1 atm).
> 4. **Using wrong Briggs coefficients**: Class A and B have linear $\sigma_z$ (because $e = 0$); students often mistakenly apply Class D coefficients.
> 5. **Neglecting $\Delta h$ (plume rise)**: gives 30–50% overestimation of ground concentration.
> 6. **Forgetting to check $z \le L$ (mixing height)** in the vertical Gaussian term. If plume grows beyond the mixing layer, reflection from the inversion lid changes the equation.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Gaussian Plume is valid only for **passive, neutrally buoyant, continuous** releases.
- ✅ The governing PDE is the **advection–diffusion equation**.
- ✅ Effective stack height $H = h_s + \Delta h$; **Briggs plume rise** depends on $F_b$ and stability.
- ✅ Six stability classes: **A, B, C, D, E, F** (A = most unstable, F = most stable).
- ✅ **Ground-level centerline** formula: $C_{GL} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$.
- ✅ Dispersion coefficients $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ are **functions of $x$** and stability class — use **Briggs or Pasquill–Gifford curves**.
- ✅ For **dense gases** (chlorine, LNG, propane), use **Britter–McQuaid** or box models; Gaussian is invalid.
- ✅ **Gaussian Puff Model** is used for **instantaneous** or **finite-duration** releases.
- ✅ Toxic endpoints to compare with: **AEGL-1, AEGL-2, AEGL-3, ERPG-1, ERPG-2, ERPG-3, IDLH, TLV**.
- ✅ Worst-case stability: **F-class (stable) for nighttime**, **A or B for daytime**.
- ✅ Key constants: $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$, $M_{air} = 28.97\ \text{g/mol}$, $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$.
- ✅ ppm $\leftrightarrow$ mg/m³ conversion: $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{C_{mg/m^3} \cdot 24.45}{M_{gas}}$.
- ✅ Image-source term doubles the result and accounts for **ground reflection** (no-flux boundary).
- ✅ Real-world software tools: **ALOHA, PHAST, EFFECTS, FRED, TRACE**.
- ✅ Engineering utility: design of **safe separation distances**, **emergency response planning**, **occupational health zoning**, **off-site consequence analysis (CSA)**, and **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explosions and Fires - Vapour Cloud Explosions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Explosions and Fires - Vapour Cloud Explosions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

A **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** is a catastrophic combustion event that occurs when a large quantity of flammable vapour, released accidentally into the open atmosphere, mixes with air to form a **flammable cloud** within its lower and upper flammability limits, and is subsequently ignited. The flame front propagates through the pre-mixed cloud, generating a **rapid pressure rise** and a damaging **blast wave** that radiates outward from the combustion zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim expected phrasing):** "A VCE is a combustion of a premixed cloud of flammable vapour with air in which the flame speed accelerates to produce a significant overpressure capable of causing damage to structures and fatality to personnel at considerable distances from the ignition source."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a giant pressure cooker filled with a propane–air mixture that has leaked from a ruptured pipeline. The mixture does not burn instantly because there is **no spark inside the cloud at that moment** (delayed ignition). Now imagine a lit match falling into the cloud far from the release point. The flame races back toward the leak source through the premixed fuel–air, accelerating as it goes because the vapour is being pushed by the expanding combustion products of the burning material behind it. The result is a **shock wave** that radiates outward like a sonic boom, breaking windows hundreds of metres away and knocking down buildings closer in.

A simpler analogy: think of lighting the entire gas-air mixture above a spilled pool of petrol all **at once** instead of at one spot — the resulting expansion is so rapid it behaves like a bomb.

## 1.3 Distinguishing VCE from Related Phenomena

> [!NOTE]
> **Students frequently confuse these events in exams. Memorize the differences:**

| Phenomenon | Fuel State | Mechanism | Characteristic Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VCE** | Premixed vapour + air in cloud | Deflagration / partial detonation | **Blast overpressure** + thermal pulse |
| **Flash Fire** | Vapour cloud (LFL to UFL) | Diffusion flame propagation | **Thermal radiation only**, no significant overpressure |
| **BLEVE** | Superheated liquid in vessel | Catastrophic vessel rupture | **Missile projectiles** + large fireball |
| **Pool Fire** | Liquid pool | Diffusion flame above pool | Steady **thermal radiation** |
| **Dust Explosion** | Suspended solid particles | Combustion of dust cloud | **Blast overpressure** (similar to VCE) |
| **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion** | Two-phase release | Rapid phase change | Fireball + pressure pulse |

## 1.4 Real-World VCE Case Studies (Historical Context)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiners value awareness of landmark incidents. Memorize at least 3:**

- **Flixborough Disaster, UK (1974):** Approximately **30 tonnes of cyclohexane** released; 28 fatalities, 89 injured; the entire plant demolished.
- **Skikda, Algeria (2004):** LNG release ignited; 27 deaths, 74 injuries.
- **Jaipur, India (2009):** Oil terminal explosion with massive VCE contribution.
- **Buncefield, UK (2005):** Gasoline overflow created a **massive unconfined vapour cloud** that did not detonate but burned as a huge flash fire; provides comparison with true VCE.

## 1.5 Why VCE Is Considered a Major Process Industry Hazard

- Requires only a **moderate release quantity** (typically a few hundred kg to a few thousand kg).
- **Delayed ignition** is common (vapour drifts far before finding a spark).
- Generates **overpressures of 1–10 bar** near the source, capable of destroying process equipment.
- **Economic loss** of a single VCE incident routinely exceeds **₹100 crore** in modern refineries.
- Causes the **highest proportion of off-site casualties** among all process accidents.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Necessary Pre-Conditions for a VCE (The "5-C" Model)

A VCE cannot occur unless **all** of the following pre-conditions are satisfied simultaneously:

1. **Containment Failure (C₁):** Loss of containment of a flammable substance (vapour, gas, or volatile liquid).
2. **Confinement (C₂):** Partial or full geometric confinement (vessel, building, congested pipe rack) that promotes flame acceleration.
3. **Conflagration-Ready Mixture (C₃):** The cloud composition lies **between the Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** and the **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)**.
4. **Congestion / Obstacles (C₄):** Process equipment, pipe racks, or structures create **turbulence-generating obstacles** that accelerate the flame.
5. **Delayed Ignition (C₅):** The ignition source is **not immediate** — there is a finite delay (seconds to minutes) during which the cloud disperses and grows.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "presence of oxygen" as a condition. Atmospheric air always contains ~21% O₂, so this is *not* a discriminating pre-condition for VCE in air.

## 2.2 Flammability Limits of Common Substances

| Substance | LFL (% v/v in air) | UFL (% v/v in air) | ΔH_c (kJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Methane (CH₄) | 5.0 | 15.0 | 55,500 |
| Propane (C₃H₈) | 2.1 | 9.5 | 46,000 |
| n-Butane | 1.8 | 8.4 | 45,700 |
| n-Hexane | 1.1 | 7.5 | 45,100 |
| Cyclohexane | 1.3 | 8.0 | 43,900 |
| Hydrogen (H₂) | 4.0 | 75.0 | 142,000 |
| Gasoline | 1.4 | 7.6 | 44,000 |
| Benzene | 1.2 | 7.8 | 40,600 |
| Toluene | 1.2 | 7.1 | 40,600 |
| Acetone | 2.6 | 12.8 | 29,000 |
| Methanol | 6.7 | 36.0 | 20,000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometric concentration** is where complete combustion uses exactly all available O₂. Most hydrocarbons are stoichiometric at ~5–9% v/v. The **most explosive concentration** is slightly rich of stoichiometric (≈1.2–1.5× stoich).

## 2.3 Mechanism of VCE — Step-by-Step

A VCE develops through the following sequential stages:

- **Step 1 — Release:** Piping, vessel, or flange failure releases a flammable fluid.
- **Step 2 — Flash Vaporisation:** Superheated liquid flashes; refrigerated liquids vaporise continuously.
- **Step 3 — Entrainment & Dispersion:** The vapour plume is carried downwind, drawing in air and forming a **larger but leaner cloud**.
- **Step 4 — Flammable Cloud Formation:** The cloud envelope between LFL and UFL is called the **"Flammable Mass Zone"**.
- **Step 5 — Ignition:** A spark, hot surface, or static discharge enters the cloud.
- **Step 6 — Flame Propagation:** A flame front moves outward at the laminar burning velocity (≈0.5–3 m/s for hydrocarbons).
- **Step 7 — Flame Acceleration:** Encountering obstacles and confinement, the flame front **stretches and turbulates**, increasing its surface area. Velocity rises to **100–1000 m/s**.
- **Step 8 — Pressure Generation:** A blast wave forms ahead of the flame front; overpressure may reach **5–10 bar** in highly confined regions.
- **Step 9 — Possible DDT:** In tightly confined geometries with strong obstacles, **Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition (DDT)** can occur, producing near-detonation overpressures (15–20 bar).

## 2.4 The "Yield Factor" η — Why It Exists

In a TNT explosion, **all the chemical energy is released as a shock wave**. In a VCE, only a **fraction** of the fuel's heat of combustion is converted into blast energy because:
- The cloud is not fully stoichiometric.
- Heat is lost to surroundings by radiation during combustion.
- Only the part of the cloud that participates in the explosion contributes.

This fraction is the **yield factor η**, typically:
- **η = 0.01 to 0.05** for open-air unconfined VCE.
- **η = 0.05 to 0.10** for partially confined VCE.
- **η ≈ 0.10 to 0.30** for highly confined VCE (approaches detonation).

> [!CAUTION]
> **TNT method always under-predicts** the overpressure of a confined VCE. The TNO Multi-Energy method is preferred for industrial assessment.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not bare `\vertvert`) in tables. The following table is the exam-ready formula bank.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total fuel combustion energy | $E_{\text{fuel}} = m \cdot \Delta H_c$ | $E$ in kJ; $m$ in kg; $\Delta H_c$ in kJ/kg |
| 2 | Effective explosion energy | $E_{\text{exp}} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c$ | $\eta$ dimensionless (0.01 – 0.10 typical) |
| 3 | TNT-equivalent mass | $W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{c,\text{TNT}}}$ | $\Delta H_{c,\text{TNT}} = 4680$ **kJ/kg**; $W_{\text{TNT}}$ in kg |
| 4 | TNT scaled distance | $Z = \dfrac{r}{W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}}$ | $r$ in m; $Z$ in m·kg⁻¹ᐟ³ |
| 5 | Sachs scaled distance (TNO) | $\bar{r} = r \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_0}{E}\right)^{1/3}$ | $E$ in J; $P_0 = 101325$ **Pa** |
| 6 | Sachs scaled overpressure | $\bar{\Delta P} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{P_0}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Scaled impulse | $\bar{i} = \dfrac{i}{P_0^{2/3} \cdot E^{1/3}}$ | $i$ in Pa·s |
| 8 | Baker-Strehlow flame speed | $M_f = \dfrac{u_{\text{flame}}}{c_0}$ | $M_f$ = Mach number of flame front |
| 9 | Heat radiation from fireball | $q = \dfrac{F \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{4 \pi r^2 \cdot t_b}$ | $q$ in kW/m²; $t_b$ = burn duration in s |
| 10 | Probit for lung rupture (Eisenberg) | $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \cdot \ln(P_s)$ | $P_s$ in Pa |
| 11 | Probit for eardrum rupture | $Y = -15.6 + 1.93 \cdot \ln(P_s)$ | threshold: $Y = 0$ |
| 12 | Probit for fatality (whole body) | $Y = 5.13 + 1.37 \cdot \ln(P_s)$ | 50% fatality at $Y = 5$ |
| 13 | Probit for fatality (TNO, refined) | $Y = -3.05 + 1.18 \cdot \ln(P_s)$ | modern formulation |
| 14 | Probability of injury from probit | $P = 50 \cdot \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ | fraction (0 to 1) |
| 15 | Convective heat release rate | $\dot{Q} = \rho \cdot V \cdot \Delta H_c / t_c$ | W; $t_c$ = combustion time |
| 16 | Flame speed (laminar, hydrocarbon) | $S_L \approx 0.4$ to $0.6$ m/s | basic value, multiplied by 5–10 with obstacles |

## 2.6 Comparison of VCE Modelling Methods

| Feature | TNT Equivalence | TNO Multi-Energy | Baker-Strehlow |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Concept** | Convert VCE to equivalent TNT mass | Decay of blast from a high-energy source | Flame speed + geometry lookup |
| **Input needed** | Fuel mass, η, ΔH_c | Fuel mass, ΔH_c, initial strength | Fuel mass, congestion, confinement |
| **Accuracy for unconfined VCE** | Poor (under-estimates) | Good | Good |
| **Accuracy for confined VCE** | Poor (under-estimates by 10×) | Good | Good |
| **Output** | ΔP vs distance | ΔP vs distance | ΔP vs distance |
| **Best use** | First-pass screening | Detailed QRA | Geometry-specific analysis |
| **KTU Exam frequency** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ |

## 2.7 Damage Criteria — Overpressure Effects on People and Structures

| Side-on Overpressure ΔP | Effect on Structures | Effect on People |
|---|---|---|
| 1 kPa (0.145 psi) | Slight window damage | Loud noise |
| 3 kPa (0.43 psi) | Window breakage, plaster cracks | Minor injuries from flying glass |
| 7 kPa (1.0 psi) | Partial demolition of houses | People knocked down |
| 15 kPa (2.2 psi) | Steel frame buildings distorted, panels blown in | Significant injuries |
| 35 kPa (5.0 psi) | Most buildings collapse | Fatalities common |
| 70 kPa (10 psi) | Reinforced concrete damaged | Lung rupture, many fatalities |
| 100 kPa (15 psi) | Total destruction | High fatality rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the 1 psi / 5 psi / 10 psi thresholds.** Examiners frequently ask for the "overpressure at which reinforced concrete structures fail" → answer is 5–10 psi range.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Application

The KTU syllabus ties VCE to **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** in process plants. Practically:
- Refineries and petrochemical complexes use **TNO Multi-Energy curves** in software like **PHAST, FRED, ALOHA** to map blast contours around process units.
- Occupied buildings are designed so that the **worst-case ΔP at the building face ≤ 7 kPa (≈ 1 psi)** — beyond this, fatalities are likely.
- **Spacing between process units** is dictated by VCE blast-radius calculations, not by fire radiation alone.
- **Off-shore platforms** (e.g., Piper Alpha, 1988) suffered VCE fatalities because blast overpressure propagated through the modular structure faster than evacuation was possible.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the TNT-Equivalent Mass Formula

The TNT-equivalence method reduces a complex deflagration phenomenon to a simple comparison with a TNT charge. The mathematical foundation is built on **conservation of energy** plus an empirical **yield factor**.

### Step 1: Total Chemical Energy Released by Fuel Combustion

When mass $m$ of fuel burns completely, the energy released equals mass times its lower heat of combustion:

$$
E_{\text{fuel}} = m \cdot \Delta H_c
$$

**Why this works:** The lower heat of combustion ($\Delta H_c$) is the heat released per kg of fuel when products of combustion are at 25 °C. For a stoichiometric reaction such as:

$$
\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 46{,}000 \text{ kJ}
$$

the energy per kg of propane is 46,000 kJ/kg. Multiplying by mass $m$ gives total available energy.

### Step 2: Apply the Explosion Yield Factor

In a real VCE, only a fraction $\eta$ of the energy is converted into the blast wave. The remainder is dissipated as thermal radiation, ground heating, and incomplete combustion:

$$
E_{\text{exp}} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c
$$

**Why introduce $\eta$?** A stoichiometric mixture cannot be guaranteed; a fraction of fuel is consumed by diffusion flames that release no overpressure; and the cloud expansion is partially non-adiabatic.

### Step 3: Convert Explosion Energy to TNT-Equivalent Mass

The heat of combustion of TNT (chemical formula $\text{C}_7\text{H}_5\text{N}_3\text{O}_6$) is:

$$
\Delta H_{c,\text{TNT}} = 4680 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

A TNT mass $W_{\text{TNT}}$ releases energy $W_{\text{TNT}} \cdot \Delta H_{c,\text{TNT}}$. Equating this to the effective explosion energy:

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} \cdot \Delta H_{c,\text{TNT}} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c
$$

Solving for $W_{\text{TNT}}$:

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{c,\text{TNT}}}
$$

### Step 4: Define Scaled Distance

To make results from different charge sizes comparable, the **scaled distance** is introduced:

$$
Z = \dfrac{r}{W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}}
$$

where $r$ is the stand-off distance in metres. The cube-root scaling comes from dimensional analysis of a point-source blast (energy scales as $W$, length as $W^{1/3}$).

### Step 5: Read Overpressure from Empirical Charts

Once $Z$ is known, the side-on overpressure $\Delta P$ is read from **Baker's curve** (or the equivalent Wilton's or Sachs formulation). The analytical approximation (Wilton, 1980) is:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{P_0} = \dfrac{1616 \cdot \left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{4.5}\right)^2\right]}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.048}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.32}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{1.35}\right)^2}}
$$

with $Z$ in m·kg⁻¹ᐟ³ and $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (TNT Method — Full Solution)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the most common KTU 14-mark computation. Master it.**

**Problem Statement:** A storage vessel ruptures and **8,000 kg of propane** escapes instantaneously, forming a vapour cloud in an open-air area. Calculate the **side-on overpressure at 250 m** from the cloud centre using the TNT-equivalence method.

**Given Data:**

- Mass of fuel: $m = 8000$ kg
- Yield factor: $\eta = 0.04$ (typical for unconfined VCE)
- Heat of combustion of propane: $\Delta H_c = 46{,}000$ kJ/kg
- Heat of combustion of TNT: $\Delta H_{c,\text{TNT}} = 4680$ kJ/kg
- Stand-off distance: $r = 250$ m
- Atmospheric pressure: $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa

**Step A — Total chemical energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{fuel}} &= m \cdot \Delta H_c \\
&= 8000 \times 46{,}000 \\
&= 3.68 \times 10^{8} \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Effective explosion energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{exp}} &= \eta \cdot E_{\text{fuel}} \\
&= 0.04 \times 3.68 \times 10^{8} \\
&= 1.472 \times 10^{7} \text{ kJ} \\
&= 1.472 \times 10^{10} \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — TNT-equivalent mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{TNT}} &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{c,\text{TNT}}} \\
&= \dfrac{0.04 \times 8000 \times 46{,}000}{4680} \\
&= \dfrac{1.472 \times 10^{7}}{4680} \\
&= 3145.3 \text{ kg TNT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Scaled distance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3} &= (3145.3)^{1/3} \\
&= 14.65 \text{ m·kg}^{-1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \dfrac{r}{W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}} \\
&= \dfrac{250}{14.65} \\
&= 17.06 \text{ m·kg}^{-1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Overpressure from Baker's curve:**

At $Z = 17.06$ m·kg⁻¹ᐟ³, linear interpolation between tabulated points:

- $Z = 15$: $\Delta P \approx 1.1$ psi (7.58 kPa)
- $Z = 20$: $\Delta P \approx 0.9$ psi (6.21 kPa)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{Z=17.06} &\approx 1.1 - \left(\dfrac{17.06 - 15}{20 - 15}\right) \cdot (1.1 - 0.9) \\
&\approx 1.1 - 0.412 \times 0.2 \\
&\approx 1.02 \text{ psi} \\
&\approx 7.03 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result Interpretation:**

A side-on overpressure of **~7 kPa (1 psi)** is sufficient to:
- Break windows within a 250 m radius.
- Cause partial damage to light structures.
- Knock down personnel (significant injury potential).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common valuation error:** Students often forget to convert psi to kPa, or to apply the yield factor. Marks are lost for missing either step.

## 3.3 Derivation of Sachs-Scaled Quantities (TNO Method)

### Step 1: Energy Normalisation by Atmospheric Pressure

The TNO method uses the **atmospheric pressure as a reference** to make scaled quantities dimensionless. For an explosion energy $E$ (in joules), define:

$$
\bar{r} = r \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_0}{E}\right)^{1/3}
$$

This is the **Sachs scaled distance**. Dimensionally, $r$ in m, $P_0$ in Pa, $E$ in J. Verifying units:

$$
\left[\dfrac{P_0}{E}\right]^{1/3} = \left[\dfrac{\text{Pa}}{\text{J}}\right]^{1/3} = \left[\dfrac{\text{N/m}^2}{\text{N·m}}\right]^{1/3} = \text{m}^{-1}
$$

So $\bar{r}$ is dimensionless, as required.

### Step 2: Scaled Overpressure

Similarly, the scaled overpressure is:

$$
\bar{\Delta P} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{P_0}
$$

### Step 3: Initial Strength Selection

The TNO method classifies the initial blast strength on a 1-to-10 scale based on the degree of confinement and congestion:

| Initial Strength | Typical Scenario |
|---|---|
| 1 | Open-air, no obstacles |
| 2 | Open-air, low obstacle density |
| 3 | Low congestion, partial confinement |
| 5 | Moderate congestion, partial confinement |
| 6–7 | Heavy congestion, full confinement |
| 8–10 | Very high confinement (approaches detonation) |

### Step 4: Read $\bar{\Delta P}$ from Multi-Energy Charts

For a chosen initial strength, the curve in the $(\bar{r}, \bar{\Delta P})$ plane gives the overpressure. The empirical correlation for initial strength $n$ (van den Berg, 1985) is:

$$
\bar{\Delta P} = f_n(\bar{r})
$$

These curves are tabulated in the **Green Book (CPR 14E)** and software packages. For an initial strength of 6, a representative fit is:

$$
\bar{\Delta P} = \dfrac{2.5}{\bar{r}^{0.6}} \quad \text{for } 0.3 \le \bar{r} \le 5
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (TNT vs TNO)

| Aspect | TNT Method | TNO Method |
|---|---|---|
| Underlying physics | Single point-source shock | Multi-source blast model |
| Energy scaling | $W_{\text{TNT}}$ (kg TNT) | Sachs scaling $r \cdot (P_0/E)^{1/3}$ |
| Yield factor | Required (η) | Not required — initial strength absorbs it |
| Cost of analysis | Low | Higher (need geometry input) |
| Result accuracy (open air) | Conservative (under-estimates) | Realistic |
| Result accuracy (confined) | Grossly under-estimates | Realistic |
| Regulatory acceptance | Acceptable for screening | Required for detailed QRA |
| Ease of computation | ★★★★★ | ★★★ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — VCE Event Sequence (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Hazardous Material Storage Vessel] --> node2[Loss of Containment Event]
    node2 --> node3[Release of Flammable Vapour or Superheated Liquid]
    node3 --> node4[Flash Evaporation at Atmospheric Conditions]
    node4 --> node5[Atmospheric Dispersion Downwind]
    node5 --> node6{Mixture Concentration in Flammable Range LFL to UFL}
    node6 -- No --> node7[Cloud Dilutes Below LFL - No Explosion]
    node6 -- Yes --> node8[Delayed Ignition Source Contact]
    node8 --> node9[Laminar Flame Propagation]
    node9 --> node10[Flame Acceleration Due to Obstacles and Confinement]
    node10 --> node11[Deflagration or Possible DDT]
    node11 --> node12[Blast Wave Generation]
    node12 --> node13[Fireball and Thermal Radiation]
    node13 --> node14[Structural Damage and Human Casualties]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — TNO Multi-Energy Concept Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nA[Released Mass of Fuel m] --> nB[Total Combustion Energy E equals m times Delta Hc]
    nB --> nC{Select Initial Blast Strength Based on Confinement and Congestion}
    nC -- 1 to 2 --> nD1[Unconfined Open Air]
    nC -- 3 to 4 --> nD2[Low Obstacle Density]
    nC -- 5 to 6 --> nD3[Moderate Confinement]
    nC -- 7 to 8 --> nD4[Heavy Confinement]
    nC -- 9 to 10 --> nD5[Highly Confined Detonation-Like]
    nD1 --> nE[Compute Sachs Scaled Distance r bar]
    nD2 --> nE
    nD3 --> nE
    nD4 --> nE
    nD5 --> nE
    nE --> nF[Read Scaled Overpressure from Multi-Energy Curve]
    nF --> nG[Convert Back to Absolute Overpressure Delta P]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Damage Zones from VCE Centre

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pA[VCE Explosion Centre] --> pB[Near Field Zone Within 50 m]
    pB --> pC[Mid Field Zone 50 m to 200 m]
    pC --> pD[Far Field Zone Beyond 200 m]
    pB --> pE[Heavy Damage Complete Structural Destruction]
    pC --> pF[Moderate Damage Wall and Roof Failure]
    pD --> pG[Light Damage Window Breakage and Glass Injury]
    pE --> pH[Fatality Rate Near 100 percent]
    pF --> pI[Significant Injuries About 50 percent Casualties]
    pG --> pJ[Minor Injuries About 10 percent Casualties]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — VCE Consequence Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    qA[Hazard Identification of Flammable Inventory] --> qB[Release Scenario Definition]
    qB --> qC[Source Modeling Rate and Duration]
    qC --> qD[Dispersion Modeling Using Pasquill Gifford or Integral Models]
    qD --> qE[Flammable Cloud Footprint and Mass]
    qE --> qF[Explosion Modeling TNT or TNO Multi-Energy]
    qF --> qG[Overpressure versus Distance Profile]
    qG --> qH[Damage Estimation Using Probit Equations]
    qH --> qI[Individual Risk and Societal Risk Quantification]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Concept Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rA[Pre-Release Conditions] --> rB1[Containment Available]
    rA --> rB2[Containment Lost]
    rB2 --> rC1[Immediate Ignition]
    rB2 --> rC2[Delayed Ignition]
    rC1 --> rD1[Jet Fire]
    rC2 --> rD2[Flash Fire - No Overpressure]
    rC2 --> rD3[Vapour Cloud Explosion VCE]
    rC2 --> rD4[Possible BLEVE If Liquid Vessel Involved]
    rD3 --> rE1[Overpressure Damage Zone]

```
