# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Part A — Question 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024, Q1.b]**

> *Differentiate between a **flash fire** and a **vapour cloud explosion (VCE)**. State the conditions under which a vapour cloud will explode rather than merely burn as a flash fire.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **flash fire** is the combustion of a vapour cloud in which the flame propagates at a slow, sub-sonic speed through the cloud. Heat is released primarily as **thermal radiation**; **negligible blast overpressure** is generated, and damage is limited to the area of the cloud itself. A **vapour cloud explosion (VCE)**, in contrast, is a high-rate deflagration (or partial detonation) in which the flame front accelerates to **hundreds of metres per second**, generating damaging **blast overpressure** that can destroy structures far beyond the cloud footprint. A vapour cloud explodes rather than burns as a flash fire when (i) the cloud is **partially or fully confined** by surrounding structures, (ii) the cloud contains a **high density of obstacles** (pipe racks, vessels, columns) that promote flame-induced turbulence, and (iii) the cloud composition lies close to **stoichiometric**, ensuring rapid energy release. (3 marks)

### Part A — Question 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2023, Q1.d]**

> *Define the term **"yield factor" (η)** in the context of the TNT-equivalence method for VCE prediction. State typical values for open-air and confined VCEs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **yield factor (η)** is the fraction of the fuel's total chemical energy (heat of combustion × mass) that is effectively converted into the **blast wave** of a vapour cloud explosion. It accounts for incomplete combustion, non-stoichiometric cloud composition, and heat losses to the surroundings during the deflagration. Typical values are **η = 0.01 to 0.05 for unconfined, open-air VCEs** and **η = 0.05 to 0.30 for partially or fully confined VCEs** with high obstacle density. (3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, each Part B question carries 14 marks. Two independent alternative questions are provided below. The student is expected to answer either A or B.**

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024, Module 3, Q2.A]**

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** with a neat sketch. Discuss the **necessary conditions** and the **contributing factors** that determine the severity of a VCE. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A vapour cloud explosion is a major process-industry hazard in which a large mass of flammable vapour, mixed with air within its flammability range, is ignited and the resulting combustion produces a damaging pressure wave. The event proceeds in stages: (i) loss of containment, (ii) flash vaporisation and atmospheric dispersion, (iii) formation of a flammable cloud, (iv) delayed ignition, (v) flame propagation with acceleration due to obstacles and confinement, and (vi) blast-wave generation.

The **necessary conditions** (the "5-C" model) are:

1. **Containment failure** — release of a flammable substance.
2. **Confinement** — partial or full geometric enclosure.
3. **Conflagration-ready mixture** — concentration between LFL and UFL.
4. **Congestion** — obstacles that promote flame-induced turbulence.
5. **Delayed ignition** — finite time between release and ignition.

The **severity-contributing factors** are:

- **Quantity released** — larger mass produces larger blast radius.
- **Yield factor η** — depends on cloud composition and confinement.
- **Degree of confinement** — full confinement can lead to detonation.
- **Density of obstacles** — increases flame speed via turbulence.
- **Cloud composition relative to stoichiometric** — near-stoichiometric clouds release energy most rapidly.
- **Meteorological conditions** — wind speed, stability class affect cloud size and concentration.
- **Ignition strength and location** — strong ignition near the centre of the cloud produces the highest overpressure.

*(Sketch: VCE sequence diagram showing release → dispersion → cloud → ignition → blast.)* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A storage tank ruptures and releases **10,000 kg of cyclohexane** instantaneously, forming an unconfined vapour cloud. Using the **TNT-equivalence method**, determine the **side-on overpressure** at a stand-off distance of **300 m**. Given: $\eta = 0.04$, $\Delta H_c$ (cyclohexane) = 43,900 kJ/kg, $\Delta H_{c,\text{TNT}} = 4680$ kJ/kg, $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — TNT-equivalent mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{TNT}} &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{c,\text{TNT}}} \\
&= \dfrac{0.04 \times 10{,}000 \times 43{,}900}{4680} \\
&= \dfrac{1.756 \times 10^{7}}{4680} \\
&= 3752.1 \text{ kg TNT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Scaled distance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3} &= (3752.1)^{1/3} \approx 15.55 \text{ m·kg}^{-1/3} \\
Z &= \dfrac{300}{15.55} = 19.29 \text{ m·kg}^{-1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Overpressure from Baker's curve:**

At $Z = 19.29$ m·kg⁻¹ᐟ³, by linear interpolation:
- $Z = 15$: $\Delta P \approx 1.1$ psi
- $Z = 20$: $\Delta P \approx 0.9$ psi

$$
\Delta P \approx 1.1 - \left(\dfrac{19.29 - 15}{20 - 15}\right) \times 0.2 = 1.1 - 0.172 = 0.93 \text{ psi} \approx 6.4 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta P \approx 6.4$ kPa (≈ 0.93 psi) — sufficient to break windows and cause injuries at 300 m. **(7 marks)**

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2023, Module 3, Q2.B]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled sketch, explain the **TNO Multi-Energy method** for blast prediction from a VCE. Compare it with the **TNT-equivalence method** in terms of accuracy, required inputs, and applicability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **TNO Multi-Energy method** (van den Berg, 1985) is a blast-prediction technique that treats a VCE as a series of **individual high-energy blast sources** distributed throughout the cloud. The total combustion energy is:

$$
E = m \cdot \Delta H_c
$$

The energy is scaled using **Sachs-scaled distance**:

$$
\bar{r} = r \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_0}{E}\right)^{1/3}
$$

and the scaled overpressure is read from **multi-energy curves** for an assigned **initial blast strength** (1 to 10), chosen based on the degree of confinement and congestion.

**Comparison with the TNT-equivalence method:**

| Feature | TNT Method | TNO Multi-Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy (unconfined) | Under-estimates | Realistic |
| Accuracy (confined) | Grossly under-estimates | Realistic |
| Inputs required | Mass, η, ΔH_c | Mass, ΔH_c, initial strength |
| Geometry effect | Not captured | Captured via initial strength |
| Application | First-pass screening | Detailed QRA / facility design |

The TNO method is **strongly preferred** for industrial assessments because it captures the **physics of flame acceleration** in congested and confined geometries, which is the dominant driver of VCE severity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mass of **6,000 kg of methane** is released and forms a vapour cloud in a **partially confined area with moderate obstacle density** (initial blast strength = 6, TNO method). Calculate the **side-on overpressure at a stand-off distance of 250 m** using the TNO method. Given: $\Delta H_c$ (methane) = 55,500 kJ/kg, $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa, initial strength $n = 6$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total combustion energy:**

$$
E = m \cdot \Delta H_c = 6000 \times 55{,}500 = 3.33 \times 10^{8} \text{ kJ} = 3.33 \times 10^{11} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 2 — Sachs-scaled distance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{r} &= r \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_0}{E}\right)^{1/3} \\
&= 250 \cdot \left(\dfrac{101{,}325}{3.33 \times 10^{11}}\right)^{1/3} \\
&= 250 \cdot (3.043 \times 10^{-7})^{1/3} \\
&= 250 \cdot 0.00672 \\
&= 1.68
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Scaled overpressure from TNO curve for $n = 6$:**

At $\bar{r} = 1.68$, the multi-energy curve for initial strength 6 gives:

$$
\bar{\Delta P} \approx 0.15
$$

**Step 4 — Absolute overpressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \bar{\Delta P} \cdot P_0 \\
&= 0.15 \times 101{,}325 \\
&= 15{,}199 \text{ Pa} \approx 15.2 \text{ kPa} \\
&\approx 2.2 \text{ psi}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta P \approx 15.2$ kPa (≈ 2.2 psi) — sufficient to cause significant structural damage to light buildings and serious injury to people at 250 m. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the yield factor η** — Always multiply $m \cdot \Delta H_c$ by $\eta$ before dividing by $\Delta H_{c,\text{TNT}}$.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency in cube root** — Always use $W$ in kg and $r$ in metres, giving $Z$ in m·kg⁻¹ᐟ³.
> 3. **Using a bare pipe character `\vertvert` in the formula table** — Breaks the markdown table; use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.
> 4. **Confusing TNO's initial strength with yield factor** — TNO does **not** use η; the initial strength is a separate parameter.
> 5. **Skipping the conversion to kPa** — Examiners deduct marks if results are reported only in psi.
> 6. **Neglecting to state assumptions** — Always mention: (a) instantaneous release, (b) uniform cloud, (c) yield factor assumption, (d) Baker's curve validity range.
> 7. **Drawing physical diagrams instead of labelled flowcharts** — For KTU, a clean Mermaid-style block diagram scores higher than a hand-drawn sketch.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. Read it the morning of the exam.**

- ✅ **VCE = deflagration/detonation of a premixed flammable vapour–air cloud that produces damaging blast overpressure.**
- ✅ **Five pre-conditions:** Containment failure + Confinement + Conflagration-ready mixture (LFL–UFL) + Congestion + Delayed ignition.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric concentration** is the most explosive composition (≈ 1.2× stoich is peak flame speed).
- ✅ **LFL and UFL** are substance-specific; memorize the values for methane, propane, gasoline, hydrogen.
- ✅ **Yield factor η** is typically 0.01–0.05 (open air) and 0.05–0.30 (confined).
- ✅ **ΔH_c of TNT** = 4680 kJ/kg.
- ✅ **TNT-equivalent mass formula:** $W_{\text{TNT}} = \eta m \Delta H_c / 4680$.
- ✅ **Scaled distance:** $Z = r / W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}$ (units: m·kg⁻¹ᐟ³).
- ✅ **TNO Sachs scaling:** $\bar{r} = r (P_0/E)^{1/3}$ and $\bar{\Delta P} = \Delta P / P_0$.
- ✅ **TNO initial strength scale:** 1–10, increasing with confinement and congestion.
- ✅ **Critical overpressure thresholds:** 1 psi (window breakage) / 5 psi (building collapse) / 10 psi (fatality zone).
- ✅ **Baker's curve, Wilton's formula, TNO curves** are the three primary ΔP–r correlations.
- ✅ **Eisenberg probit for lung rupture:** $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln P_s$.
- ✅ **Probability of injury from probit:** $P = 50[1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]\%$.
- ✅ **TNT method under-estimates** confined VCE; **use TNO** for industrial QRA.
- ✅ **Flixborough (1974)** is the canonical VCE case study.
- ✅ **Flash fire ≠ VCE** — same cloud, but no overpressure in flash fire.
- ✅ **BLEVE** is a different phenomenon involving vessel rupture, not cloud combustion.
- ✅ **Mitigation:** gas detection, isolation valves, vapour-recovery systems, flare stacks, spacing, congestion control, blast walls.
- ✅ **Effective overpressure output is a function of:** mass released, η (or initial strength), heat of combustion, stand-off distance, geometry.
- ✅ **DDT (Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition)** is possible in highly confined geometries and produces overpressures up to 20 bar.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash Fires
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flash Fires

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Flash Fire** is defined as a non-explosive combustion event in which a flame front propagates rapidly (but **subsonically**, typically at **1–10 m/s**) through a diffuse mixture of a flammable vapor/gas/mist/dust and an oxidizer (usually air), producing a brief, intense, and luminous flame envelope. The defining physics-based criterion, as per the **NFPA 68** and **CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis (CPQRA)**, is the **absence of damaging overpressure** — the hazard is purely **thermal (radiative and convective)**, not shock-driven.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU High-Yield)**
> - **Flash Fire** → Flame speed **subsonic** (< 1 Mach), **no overpressure**, hazard = **thermal radiation only**.
> - **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** → Flame speed **supersonic/detonative**, **damaging overpressure** (> 0.1 bar), hazard = **blast + thermal**.
> - **Pool Fire** → Localized, stationary liquid pool burning with **continuous fuel supply**.
> - **Jet Fire** → Momentum-driven turbulent diffusion flame from a pressurized release.

A flash fire is therefore the **intermediate transient combustion regime** that occurs when a vapor cloud finds a sufficient ignition source but does not have the turbulence/confinement to transition to a deflagration-to-detonation event.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine a room where someone has spilled a large amount of **nail polish remover (acetone)**. You cannot see the vapor because it is colorless, but the room is now filled with a flammable mixture. The moment a person strikes a lighter near the doorway:

- A **blue-orange flame** instantly **races across the entire room** in a fraction of a second.
- The flame is **brief, very hot, but produces no bang** (no window-shattering pressure wave).
- After the flame passes, the temperature spike lasts only **2–5 seconds**, and the fuel is largely consumed.

That instantaneous, room-sweeping "**WHOOOOSH**" — with no explosion, just intense heat and a flash of light — is a **flash fire**. In industry, the "room" is often a **process plant**, a **LPG storage terminal**, or a **refrigerated LNG storage dike**, and the "nail polish remover" is a multi-tonne release of **hydrocarbon vapor** following a pipe rupture or flange gasket failure.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Standard Source |
|---|---|---|
| Flame propagation speed (deflagration) | **1–10 m/s** (subsonic) | NFPA 68 |
| Flash fire duration | **2–10 seconds** (transient) | CCPS / TNO Green Book |
| Peak flame temperature | **1,200–1,900 K** | Combustion thermodynamics |
| Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) | e.g., Methane **5% v/v**, Propane **2.1% v/v** | NFPA 325 |
| Stoichiometric concentration | Fuel-specific (e.g., CH₄ = **9.5% v/v** in air) | Combustion chemistry |
| Thermal radiation hazard threshold | **1–6 kW/m²** for injury | TNO Green Book |
| Autoignition temperature | e.g., Hexane **225 °C** | NFPA 325 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme specifically tests (a) the **distinction between flash fire and VCE**, (b) the role of **flammability limits**, and (c) **radiation damage criteria**. These are the three highest-weight sub-topics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Combustion Triangle and the Pre-Conditions for a Flash Fire

A flash fire cannot occur unless **all three legs of the fire triangle** are simultaneously satisfied within a defined spatial volume:

1. **Fuel** — Flammable vapor/gas/mist/dust at a concentration **between the Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) and the Upper Flammable Limit (UFL)**.
2. **Oxidizer** — Typically atmospheric oxygen at **≥ 21% v/v** (concentration between LFL and UFL is measured in air).
3. **Ignition Source** — Sufficient **activation energy ≥ Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A release **below the LFL** is too lean to burn. A release **above the UFL** is too rich to burn in air. The **flammable envelope** is the spatial region where concentration ∈ [LFL, UFL] — only inside this envelope will a flash fire propagate.

## 2.2 Flammability Limits — The "Operational Boundary" of a Flash Fire

### 2.2.1 Lower Flammable Limit (LFL / LEL)
The minimum fuel concentration in air below which propagation of a flame front does not occur. Below LFL, the heat released by combustion of the available fuel is insufficient to raise the unburned mixture ahead of the flame to its ignition temperature.

### 2.2.2 Upper Flammable Limit (UFL / UEL)
The maximum fuel concentration above which flame propagation does not occur because the oxygen fraction is too low to support combustion.

### 2.2.3 Le Chatelier's Rule (for Multi-Component Fuel Mixtures)
For a flammable gas mixture of components $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$ with mole fractions $y_i$ in the **fuel stream** and individual LFLs $\text{LFL}_i$:

$$
\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} = \frac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{y_i}{\text{LFL}_i}}
$$

**Why this works:** Le Chatelier's rule is empirical, derived from the principle that adiabatic flame temperature is approximately constant at the flammability limit across hydrocarbons. It is **accurate to within ±2%** for most hydrocarbon mixtures and is the **standard tool in KTU numerical problems on flash fire hazard zoning**.

## 2.3 Flame Propagation Mechanism

The flame front propagates by three coupled mechanisms:

- **Thermal conduction** from the burned gas to the unburned gas ahead of the flame.
- **Diffusion of active radicals (H•, O•, OH•)** from the reaction zone to the preheat zone.
- **Pressure-driven expansion** of the hot combustion products, which pushes the unburned mixture forward.

For a flash fire (as opposed to a detonation), the **pressure rise is negligible** (< 0.01 bar), so propagation is governed primarily by **thermal conduction and radical diffusion**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations constitute the high-yield formula bank for KTU 2024 ESE & CE questions on Flash Fires.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Le Chatelier's Rule (mixture LFL) | $\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} = \left( \sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{\text{LFL}_i} \right)^{-1}$ | % v/v | Valid for **multi-component hydrocarbon fuel mixtures in air** |
| 2 | Heat of Combustion (Lower / Net) | $\Delta H_c$ (tabulated; e.g., CH₄ ≈ **50 MJ/kg**) | MJ/kg | **Net** value used when water vapor leaves as vapor (typical for fires) |
| 3 | Adiabatic Flame Temperature | $T_{ad}$ from energy balance $\sum n_i u_i(T_{ad}) = \sum n_p u_p(T_{ad})$ | K | Used to compute peak radiant intensity |
| 4 | Radiative Heat Flux (point source) | $q_r = \dfrac{Q_r}{4\pi r^2}$ | kW/m² | **Far-field** approximation; $Q_r$ = total radiative power |
| 5 | Radiative Heat Flux (solid flame model) | $q_r = \tau \cdot F \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_f^4 - T_a^4)$ | kW/m² | **Near-field**; uses view factor $F$, transmissivity $\tau$, emissivity assumed 1 |
| 6 | Stefan–Boltzmann Law | $E = \sigma T^4$, with $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴ | W/m² | **Blackbody** radiative emission |
| 7 | Thermal Dose (Haber's Law) | $D = t \cdot I$ (sometimes $t \cdot I^{4/3}$) | (kW/m²)·s | Used for **skin pain / second-degree burn** thresholds |
| 8 | Probit Equation (thermal fatality) | $Y = a + b \ln(t \cdot I^{4/3})$ | — | TNO / Eisenberg; $a,b$ tabulated constants |
| 9 | Critical Heat Flux for Ignition of Wood/Paper | **≈ 12.5 kW/m²** for **10 s** exposure | kW/m² | Used in **escalation analysis** |
| 10 | Flash Point | Lowest $T$ at which liquid gives off **enough vapor** to form an ignitable mixture | °C | **NOT** the temperature of the fire — students confuse this |
| 11 | Fire Point | $T$ at which sustained combustion continues after ignition | °C | Fire point ≥ Flash point (typically by **5–10 °C**) |
| 12 | Autoignition Temperature | Minimum $T$ at which fuel self-ignites **without external spark** | °C | Used in **classification of electrical equipment (T-rating)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safety Rule:** In the table above, all absolute-value-type vertical bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking KTU's markdown rendering. Do not write $\vert x \vert$ — write $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Plant Layout (NFPA 30 / IS 15656):** Determines the **separation distance** between hydrocarbon storage and occupied buildings based on thermal flux at the boundary (must be ≤ **12.5 kW/m²** at the property line for non-essential buildings).
- **LNG Terminal Design:** Flash fire envelopes from vapor cloud dispersion are mapped to define the **1%, 50%, 99% fatality contours** for Emergency Planning.
- **Electrical Area Classification (IS/IEC 60079):** The LFL directly drives the **Zone 0 / Zone 1 / Zone 2** classification — equipment selection depends on whether a flammable atmosphere is **continuously, intermittently, or rarely present**.
- **Hazardous Area Ventilation Engineering:** Ventilation rate must be sufficient to dilute any leak to **< 25% of LFL** within the required time.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Radiative Heat Flux at a Receptor Point (Solid Flame Model)

This is the **standard KTU numerical problem**: a flash fire of dimensions $L \times W \times H$ occurs at distance $r$ from a worker/equipment. Compute the thermal flux received.

### Step 1 — Identify the Flame as a Solid Radiating Box

The luminous flame envelope is modeled as a **uniform-temperature rectangular parallelepiped** of dimensions $L$ (length), $W$ (width), $H$ (height), with surface temperature $T_f$ and emissivity $\varepsilon$ (assumed $\varepsilon \approx 1$ for a luminous hydrocarbon flame, i.e., **blackbody approximation**).

### Step 2 — Radiative Power Emitted Per Unit Area

By the Stefan–Boltzmann law, the radiative flux emitted from the surface of the flame is:

$$
E_f = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T_f^{4}
$$

with the **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ $\text{W} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$.

### Step 3 — Total Radiative Power of the Flame

Multiplying by the total flame surface area $A_s$ (sum of all six faces, minus the base touching the ground):

$$
Q_r = E_f \cdot A_s = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T_f^{4} \cdot A_s
$$

### Step 4 — Geometric View Factor Between Flame and Target

The **view factor** $F_{f \to t}$ represents the fraction of radiation leaving the flame that strikes the target (a person or a piece of equipment). For a vertical target at ground level, distance $r$ from the center of a flame of height $H$:

$$
F_{f \to t} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \arctan\left(\frac{H}{r}\right) - \frac{r}{\sqrt{r^2 + H^2}} \cdot \sin\left(\arctan\left(\frac{H}{r}\right)\right) \right]
$$

This expression can be simplified for **far-field** (where $r \gg H$) to the point-source form.

### Step 5 — Atmospheric Transmissivity

Radiation is attenuated by **absorption and scattering** in the humid atmosphere. The transmissivity $\tau_a$ over path length $L_p$ is given by the empirical **Leckner correlation**:

$$
\tau_a = 1.006 - 0.01171 \cdot \log_{10}(X) - 0.02368 \cdot (\log_{10}(X))^2 - 0.01088 \cdot (\log_{10}(X))^3
$$

where $X = L_p \cdot P_w \cdot (T_p / T_a)^{1/2}$ is the **optical path length parameter**, $P_w$ is the **partial pressure of water vapor (Pa)**, and $T_p$, $T_a$ are the path and ambient temperatures in K. For typical engineering approximations in KTU problems, $\tau_a \approx 0.8$ is often used.

### Step 6 — Final Expression for Radiative Heat Flux at the Target

$$
q_r = \tau_a \cdot F_{f \to t} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T_f^{4}
$$

This is the **master equation** for thermal hazard distance calculations in KTU.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Worked Numerical Example (LPG Flash Fire Hazard Distance)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Typical Question:** "An LPG cloud of 50% stoichiometric concentration, occupying a hemispherical volume of radius 10 m, is ignited. Estimate (a) the total heat released, and (b) the thermal flux at a worker standing 25 m from the cloud center. Use the point-source model."

### Given Data

- Fuel: LPG (assumed pure propane, C₃H₈)
- Lower Flammable Limit, LFL = **2.1% v/v**
- Upper Flammable Limit, UFL = **9.5% v/v**
- Stoichiometric concentration = **4.0% v/v**
- Heat of combustion (lower), $\Delta H_c = 46$ **MJ/kg**
- Concentration in cloud = 50% of stoichiometric = 0.5 × 4.0% = **2.0% v/v**
- Cloud radius $R = 10$ m → Cloud volume $V = \frac{2}{3} \pi R^{3} = 2.094 \times 10^{3}$ m³ (hemisphere)
- Ambient temperature $T_a = 298$ K, atmospheric pressure $p = 101.325$ kPa

### Part (a) — Total Heat Released $Q_r$

**Step A1:** Compute moles of air in cloud using ideal gas law:

$$
n_{\text{air}} = \frac{p V}{R_u T_a} = \frac{101325 \times 2094}{8.314 \times 298} = 8.56 \times 10^{4} \text{ mol}
$$

**Step A2:** Compute moles of fuel (propane) at 2.0% v/v:

$$
n_{\text{fuel}} = 0.02 \times n_{\text{air}} = 0.02 \times 8.56 \times 10^{4} = 1.712 \times 10^{3} \text{ mol}
$$

**Step A3:** Compute mass of fuel (molar mass of C₃H₈ = 44.1 g/mol):

$$
m_{\text{fuel}} = n_{\text{fuel}} \times M = 1.712 \times 10^{3} \times 44.1 \times 10^{-3} = 75.5 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step A4:** Total chemical energy released:

$$
Q_{\text{chem}} = m_{\text{fuel}} \times \Delta H_c = 75.5 \times 46 = 3.47 \times 10^{3} \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step A5:** Fraction emitted as radiation (for hydrocarbons, $\chi_r \approx 0.2$ for a flash fire):

$$
Q_r = \chi_r \times Q_{\text{chem}} = 0.2 \times 3.47 \times 10^{3} = 694 \text{ MJ} = 6.94 \times 10^{5} \text{ kJ}
$$

### Part (b) — Radiative Flux at 25 m (Point-Source Model)

**Step B1:** For a point source, the radiative flux at distance $r$ is:

$$
q_r = \frac{Q_r}{4 \pi r^{2}}
$$

**Step B2:** Convert $Q_r$ to kW·s (since 1 W = 1 J/s, and we want flux in kW/m²):

$$
Q_r = 6.94 \times 10^{5} \text{ kJ} = 6.94 \times 10^{8} \text{ J} = 6.94 \times 10^{8} \text{ W·s}
$$

**Step B3:** For a flash fire of duration $\Delta t \approx 3$ s, the **average radiative power** is:

$$
\dot{Q}_r = \frac{Q_r}{\Delta t} = \frac{6.94 \times 10^{8}}{3} = 2.31 \times 10^{8} \text{ W} = 2.31 \times 10^{5} \text{ kW}
$$

**Step B4:** Apply the point-source formula:

$$
q_r = \frac{\dot{Q}_r}{4 \pi r^{2}} = \frac{2.31 \times 10^{5}}{4 \pi \times 25^{2}} = \frac{2.31 \times 10^{5}}{7854} = 29.4 \text{ kW/m}^{2}
$$

### Result Interpretation

A flux of **29.4 kW/m²** at 25 m is **lethal to 100% of exposed personnel** within a few seconds and is **more than sufficient to ignite wood, paper, and clothing**. Recommended evacuation distance (using **1% fatality threshold** ≈ 6 kW/m²):

$$
r_{\text{evac}} = r \times \sqrt{\frac{q_r}{q_{\text{threshold}}}} = 25 \times \sqrt{\frac{29.4}{6.0}} = 25 \times \sqrt{4.9} = 55.4 \text{ m}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Damage vs. Thermal Flux (KTU Standard Reference)

| Thermal Flux $q_r$ (kW/m²) | Effect on Personnel | Effect on Equipment / Process |
|---|---|---|
| **1.0** | Sunburn-equivalent; no significant harm | None |
| **4.0** | Pain in 5–20 s; **first-degree burn** | None |
| **6.0** | Second-degree burn in ~30 s | Plastic deformation of thin metals |
| **12.5** | **Third-degree burn in 10 s**; **1% fatality** (TNO criterion) | **Wood ignites**; major process escalation risk |
| **25.0** | **100% fatality** within 1 min | Steel structural failure begins (after minutes) |
| **37.5** | Instant fatality | Equipment destruction; secondary fires |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Flash Fire Event

The following diagram maps the complete **cause → consequence → mitigation** architecture of a flash fire, following the **bow-tie risk model** used in CCPS and KTU curriculum.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hazard Source: Pressurized Flammable Liquid or Gas] --> B[Loss of Containment: Pipe Rupture / Flange Leak / Vessel Failure]
    B --> C[Liquid or Two-Phase Release into Atmosphere]
    C --> D[Flash Evaporation of Liquid Fraction]
    D --> E[Formation of Vapor Cloud]
    E --> F{Are atmospheric conditions between LFL and UFL inside cloud?}
    F -- NO --> Z1[Cloud disperses; no fire]
    F -- YES --> G[Vapor Cloud Drifts Downwind]
    G --> H{Encounters Ignition Source with Energy greater than MIE?}
    H -- NO --> Z2[Cloud continues to disperse; eventually dilutes below LFL]
    H -- YES --> I[Ignition: Flame Front Initiates]
    I --> J[Subsonic Flame Propagation through cloud]
    J --> K[FLASH FIRE EVENT]
    K --> L[Thermal Radiation to Receptors Personnel and Equipment]
    L --> M[Consequences: Burns, Fatalities, Equipment Damage, Domino Effects]
    M --> N[Mitigation: Emergency Shutdown, Evacuation, Firewater Deluge]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1` is written as `A`, `B`, etc.) to comply with the Mermaid compiler safeguard. All labels are written in **plain uppercase alphanumeric text only** — no bold, italics, or HTML inside quoted labels.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Flash Fire Protection Layers (IPL Mapping)

The **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** in a typical process plant that prevent a flash fire incident are:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Layer 1: Inherent Safe Design] --> P2[Layer 2: Process Control and Alarms]
    P2 --> P3[Layer 3: Safety Instrumented System SIS]
    P3 --> P4[Layer 4: Mechanical Relief Devices PSV and Rupture Disc]
    P4 --> P5[Layer 5: Physical Containment Bunding and Dikes]
    P5 --> P6[Layer 6: Gas Detection and Emergency Shutdown]
    P6 --> P7[Layer 7: Active Fire Protection Deluge and Foam]
    P7 --> P8[Layer 8: Passive Fire Protection Fireproofing]
    P8 --> P9[Layer 9: Emergency Response and Evacuation]
    P9 --> P10[Layer 10: Emergency Planning and Community Awareness]
```

**Each layer must reduce the risk by an independent factor of 10 (i.e., 1 order of magnitude) to satisfy the SIL/PFD requirements of IEC 61511.**

## 4.3 Schematic — Comparison of Flash Fire vs. Other Fire/Explosion Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Ignition of Vapor Cloud] --> F2{Confinement and Turbulence Level}
    F2 -- LOW --> F3[FLASH FIRE - Subsonic - No Overpressure]
    F2 -- MODERATE --> F4[DEFLAGRATION - Subsonic with Pressure 0.1 to 1 bar]
    F2 -- HIGH with Obstacles --> F5[VAPOR CLOUD EXPLOSION - Mach 1 to 5 - Severe Overpressure]
    F2 -- EXTREME --> F6[DETONATION - Supersonic greater than Mach 5 - Catastrophic]
    F1 --> F7{Liquid Pool Present and Continuous}
    F7 -- YES --> F8[POOL FIRE - Stationary - Continuous Thermal Hazard]
    F1 --> F9{Pressurized Release from Orifice}
    F9 -- YES --> F10[JET FIRE - Momentum Driven - High Local Heat Flux]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part A questions are typically 2-mark conceptual or 3-mark short derivations, with **no choice** in the question paper. Answers should fit within **80–120 words**.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Distinguish clearly between a Flash Fire and a Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE). Why is the overpressure absent in a flash fire?**

**Model Answer:**

A **flash fire** is the combustion of a dispersed flammable vapor cloud in which the flame front propagates at a **subsonic velocity (1–10 m/s)**. A **VCE** involves flame propagation at **near-sonic or supersonic speeds**, generating destructive **overpressure (typically > 0.1 bar)**.

In a flash fire, the cloud is **unconfined and un-obstructed**, allowing the combustion products to expand freely upward and outward. The **pressure rise is therefore negligible (< 0.01 bar)**, and the **dominant hazard is thermal radiation**, not blast. In a VCE, the presence of **confinement (e.g., equipment, building walls) and turbulence-generating obstacles** accelerates the flame front, leading to pressure piling and ultimately a damaging blast wave. Hence, the transition from flash fire to VCE is governed by **degree of confinement and obstacle density** in the cloud volume.

*(3 marks — full credit requires explicit mention of flame speed, overpressure, and confinement/obstacle role.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]
**Define Lower Flammable Limit (LFL), Upper Flammable Limit (UFL), and Flash Point. How is each used in industrial hazard zoning?**

**Model Answer:**

- **LFL:** The minimum concentration of a flammable vapor in air below which flame propagation does not occur. Used to size **ventilation systems** and define **hazardous area zones (Zone 0/1/2)**.
- **UFL:** The maximum concentration of a flammable vapor in air above which flame propagation does not occur (oxygen-deficient mixture). Used to ensure that **inerting operations** bring the mixture into a safe region.
- **Flash Point:** The lowest temperature at which a liquid releases enough vapor to form an **ignitable mixture** at its surface. Used to classify liquids into **Class A/B/C** (India: per IS 15656) and to determine **storage temperature limits and electrical equipment T-ratings**.

All three parameters jointly define the **flammable envelope** of a substance; operation outside this envelope eliminates the flash fire hazard.

*(3 marks — definition + one industrial application per parameter.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions are 14-mark problems with **internal choice** (attempt any one out of two). Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Step-by-step working is **mandatory** for full marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. A.** A hexane (C₆H₁₄) storage tank develops a leak, releasing liquid hexane at 25 °C onto a 10 m × 10 m bunded area. The leak continues for 60 s before the emergency isolation valve closes.

**Data Given:**
- Hexane density $\rho_L = 655$ kg/m³
- Hexane LFL = **1.1% v/v**
- Hexane UFL = **7.5% v/v**
- Hexane vapor pressure at 25 °C = **20 kPa**
- Molar mass of hexane = 86.2 g/mol
- Molecular weight of air = 28.97 g/mol
- Total atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa
- Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴
- Flame temperature $T_f = 1500$ K, transmissivity $\tau_a = 0.85$, emissivity $\varepsilon = 1$
- Flash fire duration = 5 s

**Required:**

**(a)** Compute the volume of hexane released and the resulting fuel mass in the vapor cloud. Determine whether the average vapor concentration in the cloud falls within the flammable envelope [LFL, UFL]. (7 marks)

**(b)** Estimate the thermal radiation flux at a worker located 30 m from the cloud center using the point-source model, and comment on whether evacuation is required. (7 marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — PART (a)

**Step 1 — Volumetric Release Rate (assuming a 5 mm diameter hole, leakage rate $Q = 2$ L/s, given):**

Volume released = $Q \times t = 2 \text{ L/s} \times 60 \text{ s} = 120$ L $= 0.12$ m³.

**Step 2 — Mass of Hexane Released:**

$$
m = \rho_L \times V = 655 \times 0.12 = 78.6 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Use Raoult's Law / Vapor Pressure Relation to Find Vapor Mole Fraction at 25 °C:**

$$
y_{\text{hex}} = \frac{p_{\text{vap}}}{p_{\text{total}}} = \frac{20}{101.325} = 0.1974 = 19.74\% \text{ v/v}
$$

**Step 4 — Check Flammability:**

- Calculated concentration: **19.74% v/v**
- Flammable envelope: **[1.1%, 7.5%] v/v**
- Since **19.74% > UFL (7.5%)**, the vapor at the surface is **too rich** to burn.

**Step 5 — Air Entrainment and Dilution:**

As the vapor disperses in air, it dilutes. Assume a hemispherical cloud of radius $R$ (to be determined) such that the average concentration equals the **stoichiometric value of 2.5% v/v** (midpoint approximation for hexane in air).

For a hemispherical cloud of radius $R$, volume:

$$
V_{\text{cloud}} = \frac{2}{3} \pi R^{3}
$$

Total moles in cloud (ideal gas at 298 K):

$$
n_{\text{total}} = \frac{p V_{\text{cloud}}}{R_u T} = \frac{101325 \times \frac{2}{3} \pi R^{3}}{8.314 \times 298}
$$

Moles of hexane in cloud (assuming complete evaporation of 78.6 kg, less fraction lost as liquid pool):

$$
n_{\text{hex}} = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{78.6}{0.0862} = 911.8 \text{ mol}
$$

For average concentration $\bar{y} = 2.5\% = 0.025$:

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{n_{\text{hex}}}{n_{\text{total}}} \Rightarrow n_{\text{total}} = \frac{n_{\text{hex}}}{\bar{y}} = \frac{911.8}{0.025} = 36472 \text{ mol}
$$

Solving for $R$:

$$
R = \left( \frac{n_{\text{total}} \cdot R_u \cdot T}{p \cdot \frac{2}{3} \pi} \right)^{1/3} = \left( \frac{36472 \times 8.314 \times 298}{101325 \times 2.094} \right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
R = \left( \frac{9.034 \times 10^{7}}{2.121 \times 10^{5}} \right)^{1/3} = (425.9)^{1/3} = 7.51 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion of Part (a):** The leak produces **78.6 kg of hexane**, forming a **flammable cloud of radius ~7.5 m** centered above the bund, with the **core too rich to burn** but the **periphery within the flammable envelope**. The inner core burns last as air mixes in — characteristic of a **diffusion-dominated flash fire**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — PART (b)

**Step 1 — Total Energy Released (assuming complete combustion of 78.6 kg hexane, $\Delta H_c = 41.8$ MJ/kg for hexane):**

$$
Q_{\text{chem}} = m \times \Delta H_c = 78.6 \times 41.8 = 3285 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Radiative Fraction (for hydrocarbon flash fire, $\chi_r \approx 0.20$):**

$$
Q_r = \chi_r \times Q_{\text{chem}} = 0.20 \times 3285 = 657 \text{ MJ} = 6.57 \times 10^{8} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3 — Average Radiative Power (over 5 s flash fire duration):**

$$
\dot{Q}_r = \frac{Q_r}{\Delta t} = \frac{6.57 \times 10^{8}}{5} = 1.314 \times 10^{8} \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Point-Source Flux at 30 m:**

$$
q_r = \frac{\dot{Q}_r}{4 \pi r^{2}} = \frac{1.314 \times 10^{8}}{4 \pi \times 30^{2}} = \frac{1.314 \times 10^{8}}{11310} = 1.16 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/m}^{2} = 11.6 \text{ kW/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Transmissivity Correction (if not folded into point-source model):**

$$
q_{r,\text{at target}} = \tau_a \times q_r = 0.85 \times 11.6 = 9.86 \text{ kW/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Hazard Interpretation:**

At **≈ 10 kW/m²**, a worker receives **second-degree burns in <30 s and third-degree burns in 10 s** — well above the **1% fatality threshold of 6 kW/m²**.

**Step 7 — Compute Evacuation Distance (using 1% fatality flux = 6 kW/m²):**

$$
r_{\text{evac}} = 30 \times \sqrt{\frac{9.86}{6.0}} = 30 \times 1.282 = 38.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion of Part (b):** The thermal flux at 30 m is **≈ 10 kW/m² — dangerous**. The **evacuation radius must be extended to at least 38–40 m** to keep personnel below the 1% fatality threshold. The point-source model slightly **over-predicts** the near-field flux; for design purposes, the **solid-flame model with explicit view factor** should be used. *(7 marks)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q. B.** A refinery stores a mixture of **60% methane (CH₄)** and **40% ethane (C₂H₆)** by volume in a pressurized bullet tank. A relief valve opens, releasing a vapor cloud that disperses into the atmosphere.

**Data Given:**
- LFL of CH₄ = **5.0% v/v**; LFL of C₂H₆ = **3.0% v/v**
- Lower heat of combustion: CH₄ = **50.0 MJ/kg**; C₂H₆ = **47.8 MJ/kg**
- Molecular weight: CH₄ = 16.04; C₂H₆ = 30.07 g/mol
- Atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa, $T_a = 298$ K

**Required:**

**(a)** Using **Le Chatelier's rule**, compute the **LFL of the fuel mixture**. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the cloud is ignited and the resulting flash fire has a flame envelope of dimensions $L = 20$ m, $W = 10$ m, $H = 8$ m, flame temperature $T_f = 1600$ K, transmissivity $\tau_a = 0.9$, and the worker is at ground level at horizontal distance $r = 50$ m from the flame center, compute the **radiative heat flux received at the worker** using the **solid-flame model**. State whether the location is safe. (7 marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — PART (a)

**Step 1 — Le Chatelier's Rule Application:**

$$
\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{y_{\text{CH}_4}}{\text{LFL}_{\text{CH}_4}} + \dfrac{y_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6}}{\text{LFL}_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6}}}
$$

with $y_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.60$ and $y_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6} = 0.40$:

$$
\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{0.60}{5.0} + \dfrac{0.40}{3.0}} = \frac{1}{0.120 + 0.133} = \frac{1}{0.253} = 3.95\% \text{ v/v}
$$

**Step 2 — Engineering Interpretation:**

A mixture LFL of **3.95% v/v** is **lower than pure methane (5.0%)** and **higher than pure ethane (3.0%)**, as physically expected. The mixture is therefore **more easily ignitable** than pure methane — a safety concern in mixed-fuel service.

**Step 3 — Industrial Action:**

- Re-evaluate **gas detection set-points** to **25% of LFL = 0.99% v/v** (not 1.25% v/v as for pure methane).
- Re-classify **hazardous area zones** with the lower LFL value.
- Re-evaluate **ventilation requirements** for enclosed process areas.

*(7 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — PART (b)

**Step 1 — Flame Surface Area (Solid Flame Model, all six faces, no base):**

$$
A_s = 2(LW) + 2(LH) + 2(WH) - (LW) = LW + 2(LH) + 2(WH)
$$

Substituting $L = 20$ m, $W = 10$ m, $H = 8$ m:

$$
A_s = (20)(10) + 2(20)(8) + 2(10)(8) = 200 + 320 + 160 = 680 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Radiative Flux Emitted at Flame Surface:**

$$
E_f = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T_f^{4} = 1 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1600)^{4}
$$

Computing $(1600)^4$:

$$
1600^2 = 2.56 \times 10^{6}, \quad 1600^4 = (2.56 \times 10^{6})^{2} = 6.5536 \times 10^{12}
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_f = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.5536 \times 10^{12} = 3.716 \times 10^{5} \text{ W/m}^{2} = 371.6 \text{ kW/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — View Factor from Flame to Vertical Target at 50 m:**

For a vertical target at ground level, with the flame of height $H$ centered horizontally at distance $r$ from the target:

$$
F_{f \to t} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \arctan\left(\frac{H}{r}\right) - \frac{r}{\sqrt{r^{2} + H^{2}}} \cdot \sin\left(\arctan\left(\frac{H}{r}\right)\right) \right]
$$

Substituting $H = 8$, $r = 50$:

- $\dfrac{H}{r} = \dfrac{8}{50} = 0.16$
- $\arctan(0.16) = 0.1587$ rad
- $\sqrt{r^2 + H^2} = \sqrt{2500 + 64} = \sqrt{2564} = 50.64$ m
- $\sin(0.1587) = 0.1581$

Therefore:

$$
F_{f \to t} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ 0.1587 - \frac{50}{50.64} \times 0.1581 \right] = \frac{1}{6.2832} \left[ 0.1587 - 0.1561 \right]
$$

$$
F_{f \to t} = \frac{0.00260}{6.2832} = 4.14 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Radiative Heat Flux at the Target:**

$$
q_r = \tau_a \cdot F_{f \to t} \cdot E_f = 0.9 \times 4.14 \times 10^{-4} \times 371.6 \times 10^{3}
$$

$$
q_r = 0.9 \times 4.14 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.716 \times 10^{5} = 0.9 \times 153.9 = 138.5 \text{ kW/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Hazard Assessment:**

A flux of **138.5 kW/m²** at 50 m is **catastrophically lethal** (well above the 37.5 kW/m² instant-fatality threshold). The worker location is **NOT safe**.

**Step 6 — Compute the Safe Distance (using 1% fatality criterion = 6 kW/m²):**

$$
\frac{q_{r,\text{safe}}}{q_{r,\text{actual}}} = \frac{F_{f \to t,\text{safe}}}{F_{f \to t,\text{actual}}} \cdot \frac{r_{\text{actual}}^{2}}{r_{\text{safe}}^{2}}
$$

Since $F_{f \to t} \approx H/(2\pi r^2)$ for $r \gg H$ (far-field), $F \cdot E \propto 1/r^{2}$. Therefore:

$$
r_{\text{safe}} = r_{\text{actual}} \times \sqrt{\frac{q_{r,\text{actual}}}{q_{r,\text{safe}}}} = 50 \times \sqrt{\frac{138.5}{6.0}} = 50 \times \sqrt{23.08} = 50 \times 4.804 = 240 \text{ m}
$$

**Conclusion of Part (b):** The worker at 50 m receives a **lethal flux of ≈ 138.5 kW/m²**. The **minimum safe distance is ~240 m**, far greater than the 50 m position. The location is **unsafe**; immediate evacuation to at least 250 m is required. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose 2–3 Marks Per Question:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Flash Point with Fire Point and Autoignition Temperature** — All three are distinct; the answer must explicitly contrast them. Conflating them is the **#1 marker-deduction error** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by stoichiometric fraction** when computing mass of fuel in a cloud. The cloud composition is **not** the total release mass — only the vapor fraction between LFL and UFL burns.
> 3. **Using Gross Heat of Combustion (HHV)** instead of **Lower/Nett Heat of Combustion (LHV)**. For fire calculations, **LHV is mandatory** because the water vapor in combustion products does not condense.
> 4. **Forgetting the transmissivity factor** $\tau_a$ in the solid-flame model. Without it, the calculated flux at 50 m+ is **over-predicted by 10–20%**.
> 5. **Mixing units** (mixing kW with kJ, m with cm, % v/v with ppm). **Always normalize to SI** before substituting into equations.
> 6. **Treating a flash fire as a VCE** in numerical questions. If the problem says "flash fire," **no overpressure term** is to be computed — this is a conceptual error worth **3 marks** in KTU valuation.
> 7. **Ignoring the view factor** in the solid-flame model and using a point-source formula. Both models give very different answers; the question wording dictates which one is correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Flash Fires**

- **Definition:** Flash fire = subsonic flame propagation through a vapor cloud; **no damaging overpressure**; **thermal radiation is the sole hazard**.
- **Three Conditions:** Flammable concentration ∈ [LFL, UFL], sufficient oxidizer (air), sufficient ignition energy (> MIE).
- **Le Chatelier's Rule:** $\text{LFL}_{\text{mix}} = \left( \sum y_i / \text{LFL}_i \right)^{-1}$ — must be applied for **multi-component fuel mixtures**; remember to **invert the sum of ratios**.
- **Flash Point vs. Fire Point vs. AIT:** Flash point < Fire point < AIT (for most hydrocarbons). Flash point drives **liquid classification**; AIT drives **electrical equipment T-rating**.
- **Radiative Fraction $\chi_r$:** For hydrocarbon flash fires, $\chi_r \approx 0.2$. Use **LHV** (not HHV) in calculations.
- **Stefan–Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴. Must be used for **blackbody surface emission** in solid-flame model.
- **Damage Thresholds (memorize):** 1% fatality ≈ **6 kW/m²**; 100% fatality ≈ **25 kW/m²**; wood ignition ≈ **12.5 kW/m²** at 10 s.
- **Two Radiative Models:**
  - *Point-source* (far-field, $r \gg H$): $q_r = Q_r / (4 \pi r^2)$.
  - *Solid-flame* (near-field, includes view factor): $q_r = \tau_a \cdot F_{f \to t} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T_f^4$.
- **Atmospheric Transmissivity $\tau_a$:** Use Leckner correlation; typical engineering value **0.8–0.9**.
- **Mitigation Hierarchy:** Inherent safer design → Control → SIS → Relief → Containment → Detection → Active fire protection → Passive fire protection → Emergency response.
- **VCE vs Flash Fire Transition:** Governed by **degree of confinement and obstacle density**; flash fire is the **unconfined, unobstructed** limit of the deflagration spectrum.
- **Past KTU Pattern:** ESE questions on this topic always include **one conceptual Part A** (definition/comparison) and **one numerical Part B** (Le Chatelier or radiation calculation).

---

**End of Module 3 — Flash Fires Notes**
*Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme, Course PBSFT404*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Explosions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physical Explosions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Explosion (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **Physical Explosion** is a sudden, violent release of stored mechanical or thermal energy in a non-reactive manner, resulting from the catastrophic failure of a pressurized containment system (vessel, pipeline, or reactor). Unlike chemical explosions, the energy release is purely physical — driven by **superheated liquid flashing, pressurized gas expansion, vessel over-pressurization, or phase change** — without any rapid oxidation or combustion reaction.

In the framework of **Process Safety and Loss Prevention (CCPS Guidelines)**, physical explosions are categorized as one of the major **fire and explosion hazards** in chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, and LNG industries. The governing physics is rooted in **rapid adiabatic expansion** and the **ideal gas law** combined with the **first law of thermodynamics**.

The general form of a physical explosion energy release is governed by:

$$E = \frac{(P_1 - P_2) \cdot V}{\gamma - 1}$$

where $E$ = energy released, $P_1$ = initial (failure) pressure, $P_2$ = final (atmospheric) pressure, $V$ = volume of gas/vapour, and $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats (Cp/Cv).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Distinction:**
> - **Chemical Explosion** → Combustion/oxidation reaction (e.g., Vapour Cloud Explosion, Dust Explosion)
> - **Physical Explosion** → No chemical reaction; only stored mechanical/thermal energy release (e.g., BLEVE, vessel rupture, runaway polymerisation relief)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker Bomb":**
> Imagine a **pressure cooker** on a stove. Inside, water boils to steam and builds up pressure of ~1.5–2 bar. The lid has a safety whistle to release steam gradually. Now, what happens if you block the whistle, seal the lid completely, and keep heating? The vessel eventually **ruptures catastrophically**, sending the lid rocketing across the kitchen and releasing a massive cloud of scalding steam.
>
> This is **exactly** what a physical explosion is in industry. A reactor, storage tank, or LNG vessel that loses its relief mechanism suddenly fails — and the **stored pressure energy** converts into a blast wave and flying fragments.

**Key Intuitive Takeaway:** Physical explosions are essentially "**mechanical springs releasing**" — energy stored as pressure or phase-bound liquid is liberated in microseconds.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

The following constants are repeatedly used in KTU numerical problems on physical explosions:

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Universal gas constant | R | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Ratio of specific heats (air) | γ | **1.4** | dimensionless |
| Ratio of specific heats (diatomic) | γ | **1.67** | dimensionless |
| Atmospheric pressure | P_atm | **101,325 Pa = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar** | Pa |
| TNT energy of detonation | E_TNT | **4,680 kJ/kg** (≈ 4.6 MJ/kg) | kJ/kg |
| Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (HC) | — | **~15:1** | kg/kg |
| Flame speed (deflagration) | — | **1–100** | m/s |
| Detonation speed | — | **1,800–2,200** | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 — KTU 2024):**
> The five principal types of physical explosion considered are:
> 1. **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** — *chemical* origin but modeled with physical blast methods
> 2. **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion (BLEVE)**
> 3. **Dust Explosion**
> 4. **Confined Explosion** (in buildings/vessels)
> 5. **Unconfined Explosion** (open atmosphere)

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in B.Tech Safety Engineering

A B.Tech engineer in chemical/mechanical safety must be able to:
- **Estimate the blast overpressure** at a given distance from a physical explosion.
- **Determine the TNT equivalent mass** of a vapour cloud or pressurized vessel.
- **Calculate the damage radius** for personnel, structures, and equipment.
- **Design adequate relief systems** (rupture discs, PSVs) to prevent catastrophic failure.

These skills are assessed in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) under Course Outcome **CO3**: *Apply risk assessment methodologies to process plant hazards.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Explosions (Hierarchical Logic)

Per the **CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis (CPQRA)**, explosions are classified as:

```
EXPLOSIONS
│
├── 1. PHYSICAL EXPLOSIONS
│      ├── (a) Pressure Vessel Rupture
│      ├── (b) BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion)
│      └── (c) Mechanical Failure (rotating equipment, flywheels)
│
├── 2. CHEMICAL EXPLOSIONS
│      ├── (a) Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE) — Unconfined/Confined
│      ├── (b) Dust Explosion
│      ├── (c) Condensed Phase Explosion (TNT-like)
│      └── (d) Runaway Reaction Explosion
│
└── 3. NUCLEAR EXPLOSIONS (out of syllabus scope)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is VCE/Dust Explosion considered here?**
> Although chemically driven, KTU's Module 3 groups VCEs and dust explosions **under the "Physical Explosion" study framework** because the **blast modeling methodology** (TNT equivalent, TNO Multi-Energy) treats them as pressure-wave phenomena, not as reaction-kinetics problems.

---

## 2.2 Mechanism of a Physical Explosion — Step-by-Step Logic

### **Step 1: Energy Storage Phase**
Energy is accumulated in a vessel or volume as:
- **Pressure-volume work** of a compressed gas
- **Latent heat** of a superheated liquid
- **Strain energy** of a structural member

### **Step 2: Triggering Event**
- Corrosion, fatigue, over-pressurization, external fire impingement, mechanical impact, or runaway reaction causes **vessel wall failure**.

### **Step 3: Catastrophic Rupture**
- The vessel wall opens in **<10 milliseconds**.
- The gas/vapour expands **adiabatically** (isentropically for an ideal gas).
- Expansion ratio can be **10× to 1000×** the original volume.

### **Step 4: Blast Wave Generation**
- The expanding gas column pushes ambient air outward, creating a **shock wave** (overpressure pulse).
- A **fragment hazard** is generated if the vessel breaks into pieces.

### **Step 5: Damage Consequence**
- Overpressure damages structures (windows shatter at ~7 kPa, buildings collapse at ~70 kPa).
- Thermal radiation from BLEVE fireballs injures personnel.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Physical Explosion Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every equation** a KTU student must memorize for Module 3. These are high-yield for both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2)V}{\gamma - 1}$ | Expansion energy of compressed gas | J | Vessel rupture energy |
| 2 | $E = m \cdot C_v \cdot T_1 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]$ | Adiabatic gas expansion energy | J | Refined vessel energy |
| 3 | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot E}{E_{TNT}}$ | TNT equivalent mass | kg | Converting E to kg TNT |
| 4 | $P_s = P_{atm} \cdot \dfrac{808 \left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{4.871}\right)^2\right]}{\sqrt{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.0482}\right)^2\right]} \cdot \sqrt{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.328\right)\right]^2} \cdot \sqrt{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{1.006\right)^2\right]}}$ | Sadovsky blast overpressure (TNT) | Pa | Side-on overpressure at distance Z |
| 5 | $Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | Scaled distance (Hopkinson's rule) | m/kg^(1/3) | Scaling TNT effects |
| 6 | $E_{BLEVE} = m \cdot \left[C_{pL} \cdot (T_1 - T_{sat}) + \Delta H_{vap}\right]$ | BLEVE energy release | J | Superheated liquid |
| 7 | $D_{fireball} = 5.8 \cdot m^{1/3}$ | Fireball diameter (TNO) | m | BLEVE consequence |
| 8 | $t_{fireball} = 0.45 \cdot m^{1/3}$ | Fireball duration | s | BLEVE thermal dose |
| 9 | $P_{rated} = 2.5 \cdot P_{design}$ | Vessel PSV sizing (ASME) | bar | Relief design |
| 10 | $\text{Fragment range} = R_f$ (from Mannan/correlation) | — | m | Missile hazard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sadovsky Equation Constants (TNT, surface burst):**
> The constants **808, 4.871, 0.0482, 0.328, 1.006** come from empirical fit to TNT blast data. For hemispherical surface burst, the formula gives $P_s$ (side-on overpressure) in **Pa** when $Z$ is in **m/kg^(1/3)** and $W_{TNT}$ in **kg**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas industry**: BLEVE of LPG bullets (e.g., 2008 Mumbai HPCL fire → BLEVE → 40 casualties).
- **LNG terminals**: Designing mounded storage to prevent BLEVE.
- **Pharmaceutical plants**: Dust explosion prevention in API powder handling.
- **Offshore platforms**: Blast wall sizing using TNT-equivalent methodology.
- **Nuclear**: Excluded — handled separately.

---

## 2.5 Damage Criteria (CCPS / KTU Reference Values)

| Overpressure (kPa) | Structural Effect | Personnel Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **2.0** | Loud noise, minor window flex | — |
| **7.0** | Glass shatters | Light injuries |
| **15** | House damage, partial collapse | Eardrum rupture |
| **35** | Heavy damage, walls bow out | Lung injury, fatalities |
| **70** | Most buildings collapse | **50% fatalities** |
| **150+** | Reinforced concrete damage | **Near 100% fatalities** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Energy Released by a Ruptured Pressurized Vessel

### **Starting Point — First Law of Thermodynamics for Open System**

For an adiabatic, reversible (isentropic) expansion of an ideal gas in a rigid vessel that fails catastrophically, the maximum work obtainable (the explosion energy) equals the change in internal energy:

$$E = U_1 - U_2 = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$$

For an **ideal gas**, isentropic relations give:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$

### **Substitute $T_2$ into Energy Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= m \cdot C_v \cdot T_1 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}\right] \\
  &= m \cdot C_v \cdot T_1 \cdot \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Convert Mass to Pressure-Volume Form**

Using ideal gas law: $PV = mRT$, so $m \cdot C_v \cdot T_1 = \dfrac{P_1 V}{\gamma - 1}$ (since $C_p - C_v = R$ and $C_p/C_v = \gamma$, leading to $C_v = R/(\gamma-1)$).

Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{P_1 V}{\gamma - 1} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}\right] \\
  &= \dfrac{V}{\gamma - 1} \left[P_1 - P_2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{-1/\gamma}\right] \\
  &= \dfrac{V}{\gamma - 1} \left[P_1 - P_2^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \cdot P_1^{1/\gamma}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

For $P_2 \ll P_1$ (typical case where $P_2 = P_{atm} \ll P_1$), the second term vanishes, and the formula reduces to the **canonical form**:

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2) \cdot V}{\gamma - 1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** This is the equation you write in the KTU board exam for a "2- or 4-mark short question" on energy released by a failing vessel. Always state $P_2 = 101.325$ kPa if rupture is to atmosphere.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

### **Problem Statement (Model Q):**
> A pressurized air storage tank of volume $V = 2.0$ m³ fails catastrophically. The initial pressure is $P_1 = 10$ bar (gauge) and ambient $P_2 = 1.013$ bar. Air has $\gamma = 1.4$. Calculate:
> (a) The energy released.
> (b) The TNT equivalent mass (efficiency factor $\eta = 10\%$, $E_{TNT} = 4.68$ MJ/kg).
> (c) The scaled distance $Z$ and side-on overpressure at 50 m.

### **Solution — Step (a): Energy Released**

Convert pressures to absolute (Pa):
- $P_1 = (10 + 1.013) \text{ bar} = 11.013 \text{ bar} = 11.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.1013 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_2 = 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $V = 2.0$ m³
- $\gamma = 1.4$

Substitute:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2) \cdot V}{\gamma - 1} \\
  &= \dfrac{(1.1013 \times 10^6 - 1.013 \times 10^5) \times 2.0}{1.4 - 1} \\
  &= \dfrac{(1.0000 \times 10^6) \times 2.0}{0.4} \\
  &= \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^6}{0.4} \\
  &= 5.0 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 5.0 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (a): E = 5.0 MJ**

### **Solution — Step (b): TNT Equivalent Mass**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{TNT} &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot E}{E_{TNT}} \\
        &= \dfrac{0.10 \times 5.0 \times 10^6 \text{ J}}{4.68 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}} \\
        &= \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^5}{4.68 \times 10^6} \text{ kg} \\
        &= 0.1068 \text{ kg TNT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (b): W_TNT ≈ 0.107 kg TNT**

### **Solution — Step (c): Scaled Distance & Overpressure at 50 m**

Scaled distance:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} \\
  &= \dfrac{50}{(0.1068)^{1/3}} \\
  &= \dfrac{50}{0.4747} \\
  &= 105.3 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Z > 10$, the blast is in the **far-field / weak shock regime**. Use the **simplified Sadovsky formula** for $Z > 1$:

$$P_s \approx \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^5}{Z} \text{ Pa (for $Z$ in m/kg}^{1/3}\text{)}$$

Actually, for the rigorous form, the Sadovsky equation is:
$$
P_s = \dfrac{808 \cdot P_{atm} \cdot \left[1 + \left(\frac{Z}{4.871}\right)^2\right]}{\sqrt{\left[1 + \left(\frac{Z}{0.0482}\right)^2\right]} \cdot \sqrt{\left[1 + \left(\frac{Z}{0.328}\right)^2\right]} \cdot \sqrt{\left[1 + \left(\frac{Z}{1.006}\right)^2\right]}}
$$

Plugging in $Z = 105.3$:
- $Z/4.871 = 21.62$ → squared = 467.3
- $Z/0.0482 = 2185$ → squared = 4.77 × 10⁶
- $Z/0.328 = 321$ → squared = 1.03 × 10⁵
- $Z/1.006 = 104.7$ → squared = 1.096 × 10⁴

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_s &= \dfrac{808 \times 101325 \times [1 + 467.3]}{\sqrt{1 + 4.77 \times 10^6} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 1.03 \times 10^5} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 1.096 \times 10^4}} \\
    &= \dfrac{808 \times 101325 \times 468.3}{2184.9 \times 321.2 \times 104.7} \\
    &= \dfrac{3.83 \times 10^{10}}{7.346 \times 10^7} \\
    &= 521.4 \text{ Pa} \approx 5.21 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (c): P_s ≈ 5.21 kPa at 50 m**

> [!TIP]
> **Damage at 5.21 kPa:** Window glass breakage is likely; no serious structural damage. This is why KTU examiners ask for damage interpretation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — TNT vs. TNO Multi-Energy Method

| Aspect | TNT Equivalent Method | TNO Multi-Energy Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Military/condensed-phase | Vapour cloud / process industry |
| **Energy scale** | Mass of TNT (kg) | Energy of vapour cloud (J) |
| **Key assumption** | Point/hemispherical source | Multi-energy source with **initial blast strength** $E_0$ (1–10) |
| **Efficiency** | 1–10% (process) | Variable per obstacle density |
| **Best for** | Condensed explosives, BLEVEs | **VCE in congested/process areas** |
| **Output** | Overpressure vs. distance | Flame speed, blast strength vs. distance |
| **KTU Module 3 weight** | **Higher (numerical questions)** | **Conceptual (Part A)** |

### **TNO Multi-Energy Method — Logic Steps**

1. Identify congested regions in the cloud footprint.
2. Assign blast strength class (1 = weak, 10 = strong detonation).
3. Use TNO look-up tables for flame speed and blast energy fraction.
4. Calculate scaled distance using cloud energy.
5. Read overpressure from multi-energy curves.

The 10-class system:
- **Class 1** → very weak (uncongested, no flame acceleration)
- **Class 6** → moderate (typical process plant congestion)
- **Class 10** → strong detonation equivalent (highly reactive, strongly confined)

---

## 3.4 BLEVE — Energy Calculation Derivation

For a BLEVE, the released energy consists of:
- **Sensible heat** of superheated liquid (above normal boiling point)
- **Latent heat of vaporization** of the flashed fraction

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{BLEVE} &= m \cdot C_{pL} \cdot (T_1 - T_{sat}) + f \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_{vap}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $f$ = fraction of liquid that flashes (typically 0.1–0.3 for hydrocarbons).

**TNO Fireball Correlations** (for $m$ in kg):
$$
D_{fireball} = 5.8 \cdot m^{1/3} \text{ m}, \quad t_{fireball} = 0.45 \cdot m^{1/3} \text{ s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For a BLEVEd propane tank of $m = 20{,}000$ kg:**
> $D_{fireball} = 5.8 \times (20{,}000)^{1/3} = 5.8 \times 27.14 = 157.4$ m
> $t_{fireball} = 0.45 \times 27.14 = 12.2$ s
> This is why BLEVE fatalities occur in **300+ m radius** (from thermal dose, not overpressure).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical Explosion Consequence Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Source] --> B{Energy Type?}
    B -- Physical --> C[Pressurized Vessel]
    B -- Physical --> D[Superheated Liquid]
    B -- Chemical --> E[Flammable Vapour Cloud]
    B -- Chemical --> F[Combustible Dust Cloud]
    C --> C1[Vessel Rupture]
    C1 --> C2[Adiabatic Expansion]
    C2 --> C3[Blast Overpressure Wave]
    D --> D1[BLEVE Phenomenon]
    D1 --> D2[Fireball Formation]
    D2 --> D3[Thermal Radiation]
    E --> E1[Cloud Dispersion]
    E1 --> E2[Ignition in Congestion]
    E2 --> E3[VCE Deflagration]
    E3 --> C3
    F --> F1[Dust Suspension]
    F1 --> F2[Ignition Source Contact]
    F2 --> F3[Dust Explosion]
    F3 --> C3
    C3 --> G[Damage Assessment]
    D3 --> G
    G --> G1[Personnel Injury]
    G --> G2[Structural Damage]
    G --> G3[Equipment Loss]
    G --> G4[Fragment Missiles]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — TNT Method Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Physical Explosion Source] --> B[Calculate Explosion Energy E]
    B --> B1[E = P1-P2 times V over gamma-1]
    B --> B2[E = m times Cv times delta T]
    C[Apply Efficiency Factor eta] --> D[TNT Equivalent Mass]
    D --> D1[W_TNT = eta times E over E_TNT]
    E[Compute Scaled Distance] --> E1[Z = R over W_TNT to the 1/3]
    F[Read Sadovsky Equation] --> F1[Side-on Overpressure P_s]
    G[Apply Damage Criteria Table] --> H[Consequence Report]
    H --> H1[Injury Zones]
    H --> H2[Building Damage Radius]
    H --> H3[Evacuation Distance]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — TNO Multi-Energy Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vapour Cloud Released] --> B[Cloud Footprint Analysis]
    B --> C{Obstruction Density?}
    C -- None / Open --> D[Class 1 to 2 Weak]
    C -- Mild --> E[Class 3 to 4 Moderate]
    C -- Process Piping --> F[Class 5 to 7 Strong]
    C -- Highly Congested --> G[Class 8 to 10 Detonation]
    D --> H[Low Overpressure Damage]
    E --> I[Window Breakage, Light Damage]
    F --> J[Building Damage, Injuries]
    G --> K[Massive Destruction, Fatalities]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — BLEVE Mechanism Phases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Heat Absorption] --> P2[Phase 2: Wall Thinning]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Catastrophic Rupture]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Superheated Liquid Flash]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Fireball Expansion]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Buoyant Rise and Extinction]
    P1 -.Heat from external fire.-> P1
    P2 -.Vessel wall weakens.-> P2
    P3 -.Sudden vessel failure.-> P3
    P4 -.Rapid vaporization.-> P4
    P5 -.Combustion of vapour.-> P5
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define physical explosion. How is it different from a chemical explosion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Physical Explosion (1 mark):** A sudden release of stored mechanical or thermal energy due to failure of a containment vessel, without any chemical reaction. Examples: BLEVE, pressure vessel rupture.
- **Chemical Explosion (1 mark):** A rapid oxidation/combustion reaction producing a blast wave. Examples: VCE, dust explosion.
- **Key difference (1 mark):** In physical explosion, energy is **pre-stored** as pressure or sensible heat; in chemical explosion, energy is **released instantaneously** from a chemical bond rearrangement reaction. The blast modeling is also different — physical explosion energy is calculated from $PV/(\gamma-1)$, while chemical explosion uses combustion enthalpy.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is BLEVE? List the four essential conditions for a BLEVE to occur.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **BLEVE (1 mark):** Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion — a catastrophic failure of a vessel containing a superheated liquid (typically LPG, LNG, propane), resulting in a fireball.
- **Four conditions (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. The vessel contains a **liquid above its atmospheric boiling point** (superheated).
  2. The vessel is **heated externally** (e.g., by fire impingement).
  3. The vessel **fails catastrophically** (sudden rupture, not slow leak).
  4. The released vapour **finds an ignition source** immediately, forming a fireball.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the energy released by the catastrophic failure of a pressurized gas vessel, and list the standard assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spherical propane storage bullet of volume $V = 50$ m³ operates at $P_1 = 8$ bar (gauge) and $T = 30°C$. Calculate:
  (i) Total explosion energy released on catastrophic failure. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) TNT equivalent mass ($\eta = 0.05$, $E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg). **(2 marks)**
  (iii) Side-on overpressure at a distance of 200 m, using the simplified Sadovsky equation. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(a):**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from the first law of thermodynamics for a closed adiabatic system:

$$E = U_1 - U_2 = mC_v(T_1 - T_2)$$

For isentropic expansion of an ideal gas:
$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

Substituting:
$$E = mC_vT_1 \left[1 - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]$$

Using $mC_vT_1 = P_1V/(\gamma-1)$:
$$E = \frac{P_1V}{\gamma-1}\left[1 - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]$$

For $P_2 \ll P_1$, this reduces to:
$$E = \frac{(P_1 - P_2)V}{\gamma-1}$$

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. **Ideal gas** behaviour (valid for air, N₂, natural gas at moderate pressures).
2. **Adiabatic** expansion (no heat loss to surroundings).
3. **Isentropic** (reversible) process — yields maximum work.
4. **Vessel failure is instantaneous** (<10 ms).
5. **$P_2 = P_{atm}$** (rupture to atmosphere).
6. **$\gamma$ is constant** over the temperature range.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(b):**

**Given:** $V = 50$ m³, $P_1 = 8 + 1.013 = 9.013$ bar $= 9.013 \times 10^5$ Pa, $P_2 = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa, $\gamma = 1.13$ (propane, diatomic-like), $\eta = 0.05$, $E_{TNT} = 4.68 \times 10^6$ J/kg.

**(i) Energy released (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{(P_1 - P_2)V}{\gamma - 1} \\
  &= \frac{(9.013 \times 10^5 - 1.013 \times 10^5) \times 50}{1.13 - 1} \\
  &= \frac{(8.0 \times 10^5) \times 50}{0.13} \\
  &= \frac{4.0 \times 10^7}{0.13} \\
  &= 3.077 \times 10^8 \text{ J} = 307.7 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) TNT equivalent (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{TNT} &= \frac{\eta E}{E_{TNT}} = \frac{0.05 \times 3.077 \times 10^8}{4.68 \times 10^6} \\
        &= \frac{1.5385 \times 10^7}{4.68 \times 10^6} = 3.288 \text{ kg TNT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Scaled distance & overpressure (2 marks):**

$$
Z = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \frac{200}{(3.288)^{1/3}} = \frac{200}{1.486} = 134.6 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

For $Z > 30$ (far field), approximate:
$$P_s \approx \frac{P_{atm}}{Z^{1.4}} \cdot \text{correction factor (empirical: } P_s \approx 2.5 \text{ kPa)}$$

Using Sadovsky's full equation gives $P_s \approx 2.7$ kPa. **Damage: window breakage likely at 200 m.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the TNO Multi-Energy method for vapor cloud explosion blast modeling. Compare it with the TNT equivalent method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 30-tonne LPG road tanker is involved in a fire. Assuming BLEVE occurs, calculate the fireball diameter, duration, and the thermal radiation at 200 m using TNO correlations. Given: $m = 30{,}000$ kg. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(a):**

**TNO Multi-Energy Method (4 marks):**
The TNO (Netherlands Organisation for Applied Scientific Research) method, developed by **van den Berg**, treats a VCE as multiple sub-explosions each with its own initial blast strength. The cloud is divided into regions of similar congestion. Each region contributes a blast with strength from **Class 1 (weak) to Class 10 (detonation)**. The blast energy is the combustion energy of the vapour within the congested region, with **100% participation** assumed. The look-up tables then give flame speed and overpressure as functions of scaled distance.

**Key inputs:** cloud energy, congestion class, ignition source location.

**TNT Equivalent Method (1.5 marks):**
Treats the explosion as a point source of TNT. Uses Hopkinson scaled distance $Z = R/W_{TNT}^{1/3}$. The Sadovsky equation gives overpressure. The efficiency factor ($\eta$) is typically 1–10% for process vapours.

**Comparison (1.5 marks):**

| Parameter | TNT | TNO Multi-Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Source geometry | Point | Distributed |
| Energy efficiency | 1–10% | Up to 100% in congested zones |
| Physics | Single point blast | Multiple sub-blasts |
| Best for | Condensed explosives | Vapour clouds in process plants |
| Output | Overpressure vs. distance | Flame speed + overpressure |

**Why TNO is preferred for VCE (1 mark):** TNT underestimates blast damage in highly congested process areas (e.g., pipe racks, vessels) because the actual flame acceleration creates stronger blast overpressures.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(b):**

**Given:** $m = 30{,}000$ kg LPG (mostly propane).

**TNO correlations for hydrocarbon BLEVE fireballs:**

**(i) Fireball diameter (2 marks):**
$$
D = 5.8 \cdot m^{1/3} = 5.8 \times (30{,}000)^{1/3} = 5.8 \times 31.07 = 180.2 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) Fireball duration (2 marks):**
$$
t = 0.45 \cdot m^{1/3} = 0.45 \times 31.07 = 13.98 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Thermal radiation at 200 m (3 marks):**

The view factor $F_{12}$ for a sphere of diameter $D$ to a receiver at distance $r$:
$$
F_{12} = \frac{D^2}{4 r^2} = \frac{(180.2)^2}{4 \times (200)^2} = \frac{32{,}472}{160{,}000} = 0.203
$$

(using geometric approximation: $F_{12} \approx D^2/(4r^2)$ for $r > 2D$)

The surface emissive power of an LPG fireball (TNO): $SEP \approx 350$ kW/m²

Thermal radiation at receiver:
$$
q'' = SEP \cdot \tau \cdot F_{12} \approx 350 \times 1.0 \times 0.203 = 71.0 \text{ kW/m}^2
$$

where $\tau$ = atmospheric transmissivity ≈ 1.0 at short range.

**Damage interpretation:** At 71 kW/m² for ~14 s exposure, **100% fatality** (skin burns, ignition of clothing). The 200 m radius is within the **lethal zone**.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert gauge to absolute pressure** ($P_{absolute} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$). This is a 1-mark deduction in most KTU board valuations.
> 2. **Wrong value of γ**: Students use 1.4 for everything. For hydrocarbons, use γ ≈ 1.1–1.2. Wrong γ → 30–40% error in energy.
> 3. **Ignoring efficiency factor η**: In process explosions, never use 100% efficiency. Typically 1–10%. Setting η = 1 will inflate TNT equivalent 10×.
> 4. **Mixing up scaled distance units**: Hopkinson's $Z$ must be in m/kg^(1/3). Do not use $W_{TNT}$ in tonnes without cube-root conversion.
> 5. **Writing energy in kcal instead of Joules**: All KTU numerical problems use SI units.
> 6. **Confusing BLEVE with VCE**: BLEVE is a physical explosion (superheated liquid); VCE is a chemical explosion (flame propagation). Examiners check for this distinction.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [ ] **Definition**: Physical explosion = sudden release of stored pressure/thermal energy, **no chemical reaction**.
- [ ] **Key formula**: $E = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2)V}{\gamma - 1}$ — must memorize.
- [ ] **TNT equivalent**: $W_{TNT} = \eta E / E_{TNT}$ with $E_{TNT} = 4.68$ MJ/kg.
- [ ] **Scaled distance**: $Z = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ — **Hopkinson's cube-root law**.
- [ ] **Sadovsky constants**: 808, 4.871, 0.0482, 0.328, 1.006 — used in numerical.
- [ ] **BLEVE**: liquid + superheat + vessel failure + ignition = fireball.
- [ ] **TNO fireball**: $D = 5.8 m^{1/3}$, $t = 0.45 m^{1/3}$ — memorize both.
- [ ] **Damage criteria**: 7 kPa (glass), 35 kPa (heavy), 70 kPa (fatal), 150 kPa (concrete damage).
- [ ] **TNO Multi-Energy**: 10 classes — Class 1 = weak, Class 10 = detonation.
- [ ] **TNT vs TNO**: TNT for point source, TNO for distributed VCE.
- [ ] **PSV sizing**: $P_{rated} = 2.5 \times P_{design}$ per ASME.
- [ ] **γ values**: 1.4 (air, N₂), 1.13 (propane), 1.67 (monatomic).
- [ ] **Always convert** $P_{gauge}$ → $P_{absolute}$ before substituting in formulas.
- [ ] **KTU focus areas**: BLEVE, VCE (TNO), TNT equivalent, overpressure calculations.
- [ ] **Past year weightage**: ~12–14 marks per ESE paper on Module 3 topics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BLEVE and Fire Ball
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** BLEVE and Fire Ball ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 BLEVE — Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):** > A **BLEVE** is a catastrophic pressure vessel failure in which a liquid stored at a temperature **above its atmospheric boiling point** undergoes near-instantaneous vaporization upon sudden loss of containment, producing a destructive pressure wave, vessel fragmentation (missiles), and — if the released fuel is flammable — a large buoyant **fireball**. The phenomenon is fundamentally a **thermodynamic event**, not a combustion event in its primary mechanism. The explosion arises because: - A pressurized liquefied gas (LPG, LNG, propane, chlorine, ammonia, CO₂, etc.) is held in a vessel. - The vessel wall is weakened — typically by an external **fire engulfment** or mechanical/ corrosion damage. - A small crack or rupture forms. The liquid inside is superheated relative to atmospheric pressure. - Through the crack, the superheated liquid flashes violently into vapor. Vapor expansion is **~250–300× liquid volume** for hydrocarbons. - The pressure wave shatters the rest of the vessel (rocketing fragments at hundreds of m/s) and the vaporized fuel mixes with air and ignites (if flammable) to form a **fireball**. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Anchor — Three Letters B-L-E-V-E:** > **B**oiling **L**iquid **E**xpanding **V**apor **E**xplosion — think "**B**ursting **L**iquid-vapor **E**xpansion **V**iolent **E**vent." ### 1.2 Fireball — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > A **fireball** is a transient, roughly spherical burning cloud of fuel vapor and air formed by the near-simultaneous release and ignition of a large mass of flammable vapor — most commonly the result of a BLEVE of a pressurized liquefied flammable gas. The fireball is essentially a **rising, burning fire bubble**. It has three main hazards: - **Thermal radiation** (often the dominant fatal hazard, with surface heat fluxes of 200–400 kW/m²) - **Overpressure / blast wave** (minor compared with TNT-type explosions) - **Direct flame contact** (lethal in the engulfed zone) ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" Picture Imagine a sealed pressure cooker on a gas stove.
