The water inside is at 120 °C, way above 100 °C. If the safety valve fails and the lid suddenly blows off: 1. The superheated water instantly flashes to steam (1 L → ~1700 L of steam). 2. The expanding steam shatters the cooker lid, sending it rocketing. 3. If the cooker had contained gasoline instead of water, the released vapor would ignite in a giant burning ball — a fireball. **Real-World BLEVEs that Match This Analogy:** - **Mexico City, 1984** — San Juanico LPG terminal: a chain of BLEVEs killed ~500 people. - **Skikda, Algeria, 2004** — LNG plant BLEVE. - **Jaipur, 2009** — LPG tanker BLEVE: classic fireball scenario. - **Tlahuelilpan, Mexico, 2019** — fuel pipeline fireball. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted):** > - **Surface Emissive Power (SEP) of fireball:** typically taken as **300 kW/m²** (LPG) up to **400–450 kW/m²** for high-soot fuels. > - **Vapor expansion ratio** (LPG liquid → vapor at 1 atm): **~260×**. > - **Fragment velocity from BLEVE:** **150–450 m/s** (often used for missile impact analysis). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Mechanism of a BLEVE A BLEVE does not occur spontaneously. The following sequence (Bull, 1980s) is the textbook mechanism: 1. **External fire impinges** on a vessel containing a liquefied gas above its boiling point. 2. The wall above the liquid level (the **vapor space**) heats up rapidly because the liquid cannot contact it and cool it (boiling is suppressed due to hydrostatic head). The wall in the vapor space reaches the **auto-ignition temperature** of any leaked fuel first. 3. The heated wall **loses tensile strength** — the steel "creep-ruptures" and a tear forms. 4. The vessel splits open. The superheated liquid — sometimes called a **"boiling liquid"** — **flashes** (instantaneously vaporizes) as it is exposed to atmospheric pressure. 5. The expanding vapor generates a **pressure wave** (often 7–25 bar peak overpressure at close range). 6. The vessel shell is **catapulted** in the opposite direction as a **fragment/missile**. 7. The vaporized fuel mixes with air and forms a **fireball** (if ignited) — this is usually the dominant fatal mechanism. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The pressure wave is NOT the main cause of death in most BLEVEs — the **fireball thermal radiation** typically causes 60–90% of fatalities. This is why the fireball is studied in detail. ### 2.2 Fireball Phenomenology After release, the fireball evolves in three stages: | Stage | Description | Duration | |-------|-------------|----------| | **I. Ignition & Growth** | Vapor cloud ignites and lifts off the ground (buoyancy-driven, never ground-bound for BLEVEs of large mass). | < 1 s | | **II. Fully Developed** | Roughly spherical, optically thick, soot-rich, surface emits at 300–400 kW/m². | Several seconds | | **III. Lift-off & Decay** | Buoyancy lifts the ball; it cools, becomes translucent, flame front extinguishes. | Tail of duration | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below use **W = mass of fuel (kg)**, **D = fireball diameter (m)**, **t = duration (s)**, **r = distance from fireball centre (m)**, **SEP = surface emissive power (kW/m²)**, **τ = atmospheric transmissivity (–)**. | # | Parameter | Formula | Source / Notes | |---|-----------|---------|----------------| | 1 | Fireball diameter | D = 5.8 · W^(1/3) | Hasegawa & Sato (1978), LPG | | 2 | Fireball diameter | D = 6.48 · W^(0.325) | TNO Green Book | | 3 | Fireball diameter | D = 5.5 · W^(1/3) | Roberts (1981) | | 4 | Duration | t = 0.45 · W^(1/3) | Hasegawa & Sato (ground-level, BLEVE) | | 5 | Duration | t = 0.83 · W^(0.259) | TNO | | 6 | Duration | t = 0.38 · W^(1/3) | Roberts | | 7 | Surface Emissive Power | SEP = 300 (LPG) – 450 (rich sooty) | kW/m² ; design value 300 | | 8 | Geometric view factor (point source) | F = D² ∕ (4·r²) | Valid for r ≫ D | | 9 | Received thermal flux | q″ = SEP · F · τ | kW/m² | | 10 | Atmospheric transmissivity | τ = exp(–1.2 · r^0.32) | TNO correlation | | 11 | Vapor expansion ratio (LPG) | ΔV ≈ 260 | Liquid 1 atm → vapor 1 atm | | 12 | Fragment velocity (range) | 150 – 450 m/s | BLEVE missile analysis | | 13 | Fatality probit (Eisenberg) | Y = –14.9 + 2.56 · ln( t · q″^(4/3) ) | t in s, q″ in kW/m² | | 14 | Fatality probit (TNO) | Y = –36.38 + 2.56 · ln( t · I^(4/3) ) | I = received intensity | | 15 | 1st-degree burn probit | Y = –39.83 + 3.0186 · ln( t · q″^(4/3) ) | Eisenberg | | 16 | Latent heat of LPG | ~ 425 kJ/kg | Propane: 425; n-butane: 386 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Cube-Root" Scaling?** > The D ∝ W^(1/3) scaling is a direct consequence of the **stoichiometric air entrainment** hypothesis. For a buoyant turbulent fireball, only the **fuel-rich core** burns (≈ 35% combustion efficiency for hydrocarbons). The mass of fuel consumed is roughly a fixed fraction of total fuel, so: > > Mass M_fuel ∝ (4/3)·π·(D/2)³ ∝ D³ ⇒ D ∝ M^(1/3) ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Use - **Layout planning** of process plants — minimum safe separation between LPG storage and occupied buildings (KTU syllabus emphasis: **TAPIOCA / API 521 / IS 1446** separation). - **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA):** Fireball is a top-tier event in **event-tree** analysis for LPG terminals. - **Emergency response planning** — defines evacuation radius (typically 5× fireball diameter for fatality, 10× for 1st-degree burn). - **Design of pressure vessel relief** and **fireproofing** (thermal insulation of vessel support) to delay wall failure. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Cube-Root Law for Fireball Diameter We start from the assumption (Hasegawa & Sato) that the fireball is a **stoichiometric fuel-air cloud** in which the fuel mass M is consumed with a constant combustion efficiency η (≈ 0.35 for LPG fireballs). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Volume of the spherical fireball} \quad V_{\text{ball}} &= \frac{4}{3}\pi\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^3 = \frac{\pi D^3}{6} \\[4pt] \text{Step 2: Total mass of fuel in the cloud} \quad W &= \rho_{\text{mix}} \cdot V_{\text{ball}} \quad \text{with} \quad \rho_{\text{mix}} \approx \text{const.} \\[4pt] \text{Step 3: Take the cube root of both sides} \quad D^3 &= \frac{6 W}{\pi \rho_{\text{mix}}} \\[4pt] \text{Step 4: Solve for D} \quad D &= \left( \frac{6}{\pi \rho_{\text{mix}}} \right)^{1/3} W^{1/3} = C \cdot W^{1/3} \end{aligned} $$ The constant **C = 5.8** for LPG absorbs the density of the fuel-air mixture and the experimental geometry. Thus: $$ \boxed{\,D = 5.8 \cdot W^{1/3}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical meaning:** A fireball 10× heavier in fuel is only 10^(1/3) ≈ 2.15× larger in diameter — fireballs are remarkably *compact* for their energy. This is why BLEVEs of even a few hundred kg of LPG produce fireballs > 30 m in diameter. ### 3.2 Derivation: View Factor and Received Radiative Flux For a **sphere of diameter D** viewed from a point at distance r (measured from the sphere's centre, with r ≫ D), the geometric view factor is derived by treating the sphere as a **point source** of radiative power P_rad: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Total radiative power emitted by the fireball} \quad P_{\text{rad}} &= \text{SEP} \cdot A_{\text{ball}} = \text{SEP} \cdot \pi D^2 \\[4pt] \text{Step 2: At a sphere of radius r around the fireball, the same power is distributed} \quad P_{\text{rad}} &= q''(r) \cdot (4\pi r^2) \\[4pt] \text{Step 3: Equate and solve for the received flux} \quad q''(r) &= \frac{\text{SEP} \cdot \pi D^2}{4\pi r^2} = \text{SEP} \cdot \frac{D^2}{4 r^2} \\[4pt] \text{Step 4: Define the point-source view factor} \quad F_{12} &= \frac{D^2}{4 r^2} \\[4pt] \text{Step 5: Include atmospheric absorption} \quad q''(r) &= \text{SEP} \cdot F_{12} \cdot \tau \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\,q''(r) = \text{SEP} \cdot \frac{D^2}{4 r^2} \cdot \tau\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Point-source limitation:** The formula breaks down inside or very near the fireball (r < D). It is accurate for **r ≥ 2D**. ### 3.3 Full Analytical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) > **Problem (Dec 2023 KTU Exam Style):** > An LPG road tanker containing **20,000 kg** of propane is involved in a fire. After 25 minutes, a BLEVE occurs, releasing the entire 20,000 kg as a fireball. Using the Hasegawa–Sato correlation, calculate: > (a) The diameter and duration of the fireball. (5 marks) > (b) The thermal radiation flux received at a village located **400 m** downwind, including atmospheric attenuation. (5 marks) > (c) Estimate the probability of fatality at that location using the Eisenberg probit. (4 marks) **Given:** - W = 20,000 kg ; r = 400 m ; SEP = 300 kW/m² (LPG, design value) #### Part (a) — Fireball Diameter and Duration $$ \begin{aligned} W^{1/3} &= (20{,}000)^{1/3} = 27.14 \\[4pt] D &= 5.8 \cdot W^{1/3} = 5.8 \cdot 27.14 = 157.4 \text{ m} \\[4pt] t &= 0.45 \cdot W^{1/3} = 0.45 \cdot 27.14 = 12.21 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** **D ≈ 157.4 m, t ≈ 12.2 s** — comparable in size to a football field. #### Part (b) — Thermal Flux at r = 400 m $$ \begin{aligned} \tau &= \exp(-1.2 \cdot r^{0.32}) = \exp(-1.2 \cdot 400^{0.32}) \\[4pt] 400^{0.32} &= e^{0.32 \ln 400} = e^{0.32 \cdot 5.991} = e^{1.917} = 6.80 \\[4pt] \tau &= e^{-1.2 \cdot 6.80} = e^{-8.16} = 2.86 \times 10^{-4} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > The atmospheric transmissivity at 400 m is essentially **near zero (0.0003)** because the fireball burns cool soot-laden yellow-orange flame, with heavy absorption by water vapour and CO₂. This is a **common exam trap** — students forget the transmissivity and report 35 kW/m² (lethal) when in reality almost all the radiation is absorbed by the atmosphere over hundreds of metres. $$ \begin{aligned} F_{12} &= \frac{D^2}{4 r^2} = \frac{(157.4)^2}{4 \cdot (400)^2} = \frac{24{,}775}{640{,}000} = 0.0387 \\[4pt] q'' &= \text{SEP} \cdot F_{12} \cdot \tau = 300 \cdot 0.0387 \cdot 2.86 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt] q'' &\approx 3.32 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kW/m}^2 = 3.32 \text{ W/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** The received flux at 400 m is only **3.3 W/m²** — below the threshold for even first-degree burns (≈ 1 kW/m²). Atmospheric absorption dominates. > [!NOTE] > **For a "clear-air" assumption (τ = 1):** q″ ≈ 11.6 kW/m² — instantaneously lethal. The lesson is: **atmospheric absorption is critical for distant assessment.** #### Part (c) — Fatality Probability (Eisenberg Probit) $$ \begin{aligned} Y &= -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot \ln\!\left( t \cdot q''^{4/3} \right) \\[4pt] q''^{4/3} &= (3.32 \times 10^{-3})^{4/3} \\[4pt] &= \left(3.32 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{1.333} \\ &= 10^{1.333 \cdot \log_{10}(3.32 \times 10^{-3})} \\ &= 10^{1.333 \cdot (-2.479)} = 10^{-3.305} = 4.96 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt] t \cdot q''^{4/3} &= 12.21 \cdot 4.96 \times 10^{-4} = 6.05 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt] \ln(6.05 \times 10^{-3}) &= -5.106 \\[4pt] Y &= -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot (-5.106) = -14.9 - 13.07 = -27.97 \end{aligned} $$ Since Y ≪ 5, the probability of fatality is essentially **0%** at 400 m. (For Y = 5, P = 50% fatality; for Y ≪ 5, P → 0.) **Final Answers:** - D ≈ 157.4 m, t ≈ 12.2 s - q″(400 m) ≈ 3.3 × 10⁻³ kW/m² (with τ) or 11.6 kW/m² (without τ) - P_fatality ≈ 0% at 400 m under realistic atmospheric conditions ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Architecture: BLEVE Event Sequence The following diagram maps the complete causal sequence of a BLEVE, the three principal hazards it produces, and the source models used for consequence analysis in QRA. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Pressurized Liquefied Gas
Stored Above Boiling Point] B[External Fire Engulfs Vessel
or Mechanical Corrosion Damage] C[Vapor Space Wall Heats
Above Auto-Ignition] D[Steel Loses Tensile Strength
Creep Rupture Begins] E[Vessel Splits Open
Catastrophic Rupture] F[Superheated Liquid Flashes
to Vapor Instantly] G1[Pressure Wave
Overpressure 7 to 25 bar] G2[Missile / Fragment
Velocity 150 to 450 m per s] G3[Fuel Vapor Cloud Forms
Ignites if Flammable] H[Fireball Rises Buoyantly
Sphere of Diameter D] I1[Thermal Radiation
Dominant Fatal Hazard] I2[Direct Flame Contact
Inside Fireball Zone] J[Consequence Modelling
Hasegawa Sato / TNO] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F F --> G1 F --> G2 F --> G3 --> H --> I1 H --> I2 I1 --> J I2 --> J ``` ### 4.2 Functional Flow: Fireball Consequence Model This block-level functional architecture shows the input parameters, sub-models, and outputs used to quantify fireball hazard in a QRA. ```mermaid flowchart LR IN1[Fuel Mass W in kg] --> M1 IN2[Fuel Type and SEP] --> M1 IN3[Receiver Distance r in m] --> M2 IN4[Atmospheric Humidity
Extinction Coeff] --> M3 M1[Submodel A
Diameter D = k1 times W to power 1 by 3
Duration t = k2 times W to power 1 by 3] M2[Submodel B
View Factor F = D squared over 4 r squared] M3[Submodel C
Transmissivity tau = exp minus a times r] M1 --> M2 M2 --> M4 M3 --> M4 M4[Submodel D
Received Flux q double prime = SEP times F times tau] M4 --> M5 M4 --> M6 M5[Submodel E
Eisenberg Probit
Fatality Probability] M6[Submodel F
Eisenberg Probit
Burn Injury Probability] ``` ### 4.3 Event-Tree for BLEVE Outcomes The following event tree shows the branches of outcomes from a BLEVE initiating event — the format is standard in KTU QRA modules. ```mermaid flowchart TD INIT[BLEVE Initiating Event
Vessel Rupture] E1{Is Released Fuel
Flammable?} E2{Ignition Immediate
Inside or Near Vessel?} E3{People in Hazard
Zone?} INIT --> E1 E1 -- Yes --> E2 E1 -- No --> OUT1[Toxic Cloud Hazard
Ammonia Chlorine CO2] E2 -- Yes --> FIREBALL[Fireball
Thermal Radiation] E2 -- No --> OUT2[Unconfined Vapor Cloud
May Drift and Ignite Later] FIREBALL --> E3 E3 -- Yes --> CAS[Fatalities from
Thermal Radiation] E3 -- No --> NEAR[Equipment Damage
in Envelope] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** This event-tree format is a *guaranteed* KTU question type. Practice drawing BLEVEs as a primary event with branches for **fireball**, **missile**, and **toxic release** depending on the fuel. The exact framing "initiating event → intermediate events → outcomes" follows the KTU QRA module template. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define BLEVE. List the three primary hazards produced by a BLEVE of a flammable substance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** BLEVE stands for **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion**. It is the catastrophic rupture of a pressure vessel containing a liquid stored above its atmospheric boiling point, in which the superheated liquid flashes almost instantaneously to vapor upon loss of containment. (1.5 marks) The three primary hazards are: 1. **Pressure wave / blast overpressure** (7–25 bar at close range). (0.5 mark) 2. **Vessel fragmentation and missile projection** (fragments launched at 150–450 m/s). (0.5 mark) 3. **Fireball with intense thermal radiation** (300–400 kW/m² surface emissive power). (0.5 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State the Hasegawa–Sato correlation for fireball diameter and explain the physical basis of the "cube-root" scaling law.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Hasegawa and Sato proposed for LPG fireballs: **D = 5.8 · W^(1/3)**, where D is in metres and W is mass in kg. (1 mark) The cube-root scaling arises because the fireball is treated as a **stoichiometric fuel-air sphere** with a roughly constant mixture density. Since the mass of fuel scales with the volume of the sphere — M ∝ D³ — inversion gives **D ∝ W^(1/3)**. (2 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the mechanism of a **BLEVE** in a vessel containing a liquefied flammable gas subjected to an external fire. Discuss why the failure typically initiates in the **vapor space** of the vessel. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An LPG bullet of capacity **50 m³** is 80% full of propane (density ≈ 510 kg/m³ at storage conditions). The vessel is engulfed in fire. Using Hasegawa–Sato correlations, compute: (i) The **diameter** of the fireball. (2 marks) (ii) The **duration** of the fireball. (2 marks) (iii) The **radiative heat flux** received at 250 m from the fireball centre, taking SEP = 300 kW/m² and atmospheric transmissivity τ = exp(–1.2·r^0.32). (3 marks) --- #### **Model Solution — Question A** **(a) Mechanism of BLEVE (7 marks):** 1. **Vulnerability of the vapor space (2 marks):** A vessel containing liquefied gas has a liquid pool in contact with the lower portion of the shell and a vapor head in contact with the upper portion. The liquid portion is kept cool by nucleate boiling (latent heat absorbs the heat flux). The vapor-space wall, however, is in contact with hot vapor only — there is **no internal cooling mechanism** — so it heats up rapidly. As the wall temperature rises, the steel progressively loses tensile strength (creep regime begins around 400–500 °C for carbon steel). When the wall stress exceeds the residual strength, a crack initiates. **The crack always starts in the vapor space.** 2. **Crack propagation and flashing (2 marks):** Once a tear forms, the superheated liquid at the crack face boils violently and the crack propagates catastrophically around the vessel. The depressurising liquid flashes to vapor (expansion ratio ~260 for LPG), generating a powerful pressure wave. 3. **Fragment projection and fireball formation (2 marks):** The reaction force of the escaping vapor catapults the two halves of the vessel in opposite directions as **missiles** (150–450 m/s). The vaporised fuel mixes with air and is ignited — almost instantaneously — by the same fire that caused the BLEVE, producing a **buoyant fireball**. 4. **Summary schematic (1 mark):** Show a labelled cross-section of a horizontal bullet, mark the vapor space, the fire envelope, the rupture plane, and the two fragment trajectories. --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1: Mass of fuel released (W):** - Volume of propane = 0.80 × 50 = 40 m³ - Mass W = 40 × 510 = **20,400 kg** **Step 2: W^(1/3):** - 20,400^(1/3) = **27.36** **(i) Fireball diameter (2 marks):** $$ D = 5.8 \cdot W^{1/3} = 5.8 \cdot 27.36 = \mathbf{158.7\ m} $$ **(ii) Fireball duration (2 marks):** $$ t = 0.45 \cdot W^{1/3} = 0.45 \cdot 27.36 = \mathbf{12.3\ s} $$ **(iii) Radiative flux at r = 250 m (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \tau &= e^{-1.2 \cdot 250^{0.32}} \\ 250^{0.32} &= e^{0.32 \cdot \ln 250} = e^{0.32 \cdot 5.521} = e^{1.767} = 5.86 \\ \tau &= e^{-1.2 \cdot 5.86} = e^{-7.03} = 8.85 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt] F_{12} &= \frac{D^2}{4 r^2} = \frac{(158.7)^2}{4 \cdot (250)^2} = \frac{25{,}189}{250{,}000} = 0.1008 \\[4pt] q'' &= 300 \cdot 0.1008 \cdot 8.85 \times 10^{-4} \\ q'' &= 0.0268 \text{ kW/m}^2 = \mathbf{26.8\ W/m^2} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Comparison with damage thresholds:** > - 1 kW/m² → threshold for first-degree burn on prolonged exposure > - 5 kW/m² → second-degree burn in 30 s > - 12.5 kW/m² → wood ignites, fatality in < 60 s > - 26.8 W/m² received at 250 m → **no hazard** at this distance. The community is safe. --- #### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Define **fireball** as a consequence of a BLEVE. With a labelled diagram, show the geometric view factor from a fireball to a receiver at distance r. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A fireball of mass 5,000 kg of LPG is observed at a process plant. Estimate: (i) The fireball diameter using both Hasegawa–Sato and TNO correlations. (3 marks) (ii) The fireball duration using Hasegawa–Sato. (1 mark) (iii) The received thermal flux at r = 100 m, with and without atmospheric attenuation (SEP = 300 kW/m²). (3 marks) (iv) State the probit equation for fatality from thermal radiation. (1 mark) --- #### **Model Solution — Question B** **(a) Fireball definition and view factor (7 marks):** A fireball is the buoyant burning cloud of fuel vapor and entrained air that results from the near-instantaneous release and ignition of a flammable vapor — typically the consequence of a BLEVE. (2 marks) **Geometric view factor (5 marks):** For a sphere of diameter D, the point-source view factor at distance r (r ≫ D) is derived from energy conservation: $$ P_{\text{emit}} = \text{SEP} \cdot \pi D^2 \quad \text{and} \quad P_{\text{emit}} = q''(r) \cdot 4\pi r^2 $$ Equating: $q''(r) = \text{SEP} \cdot \dfrac{D^2}{4 r^2}$. The factor $F_{12} = \dfrac{D^2}{4 r^2}$ is the **view factor**. A labelled diagram should show: the sphere of diameter D at the origin, the receiver at distance r, the line-of-sight radius vector, and the angle subtended. --- **(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):** **Step 1: W^(1/3) and W^(0.325):** - W^(1/3) = 5000^(1/3) = **17.10** - W^(0.325) = 5000^0.325 = 10^(0.325·log₁₀ 5000) = 10^(0.325·3.699) = 10^1.202 = **15.92** **(i) Diameter — Hasegawa–Sato (1.5 marks):** $$ D_1 = 5.8 \cdot 17.10 = \mathbf{99.2\ m} $$ **Diameter — TNO (1.5 marks):** $$ D_2 = 6.48 \cdot 15.92 = \mathbf{103.2\ m} $$ (The two correlations differ by only ~4%, which is good engineering agreement.) **(ii) Duration (1 mark):** $$ t = 0.45 \cdot 17.10 = \mathbf{7.70\ s} $$ **(iii) Thermal flux at r = 100 m (3 marks):** **Without atmospheric attenuation (τ = 1):** $$ F_{12} = \frac{(99.2)^2}{4 \cdot (100)^2} = \frac{9841}{40{,}000} = 0.246 $$ $$ q'' = 300 \cdot 0.246 \cdot 1 = \mathbf{73.8\ kW/m^2} $$ **With atmospheric attenuation (TNO):** $$ \begin{aligned} 100^{0.32} &= e^{0.32 \cdot \ln 100} = e^{0.32 \cdot 4.605} = e^{1.474} = 4.367 \\ \tau &= e^{-1.2 \cdot 4.367} = e^{-5.24} = 5.30 \times 10^{-3} \\ q'' &= 300 \cdot 0.246 \cdot 5.30 \times 10^{-3} = \mathbf{0.391\ kW/m^2} \end{aligned} $$ **Critical insight:** The atmospheric transmissivity reduces the received flux by a factor of **~190** at 100 m, dropping it from lethal to "no immediate injury." This is why **TNO is the preferred correlation** for distant (r > 100 m) thermal hazard estimation. **(iv) Fatality probit (1 mark):** $$ Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \cdot \ln\!\left( t \cdot q''^{4/3} \right) \quad \text{(Eisenberg, 1975)} $$ P = 50% fatality corresponds to Y = 5; P < 50% for Y < 5; P → 0 for Y ≪ 5. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting atmospheric transmissivity (τ):** This is the #1 cause of lost marks in Part B numericals. If you report q″ = 73.8 kW/m² at 100 m, you will be marked down for not applying τ. > 2. **Confusing the "cube-root" with square-root scaling:** Students sometimes write D = 5.8·√W or D = 5.8·W². Memorise it as **D ∝ W^(1/3)**. > 3. **Using duration formula from the wrong correlation:** Mixing Hasegawa–Sato diameter with TNO duration is a 1-mark error. Always use the *same* correlation pair. > 4. **Not specifying units:** "W = 5000" without "kg" is enough to lose 0.5 mark in KTU valuation. > 5. **Ignoring the missile hazard:** A 7-mark Part B on BLEVE mechanism that mentions only the fireball — and not the **missile/fragment** hazard — will lose 2–3 marks. Always list **all three** BLEVE hazards. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — BLEVE and Fire Ball (KTU 2024 Module 3):** - ✅ **BLEVE = Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion** — a thermodynamic event triggered by vessel rupture of a superheated liquid. - ✅ **Failure initiates in the vapor space** — liquid is self-cooled by boiling; vapor space has no internal cooling. - ✅ **Three hazards:** pressure wave, missile projection, **fireball (dominant fatal hazard)**. - ✅ **Vapor expansion ratio** for LPG ≈ **260×** — this is what creates the blast wave. - ✅ **Fireball is buoyant, spherical, transient** — it lifts off the ground immediately for large BLEVEs. - ✅ **Cube-root scaling** D ∝ W^(1/3) is a *geometric* consequence of spherical fireball geometry. - ✅ **Hasegawa–Sato (1978):** D = 5.8·W^(1/3), t = 0.45·W^(1/3), valid for LPG. - ✅ **TNO Green Book:** D = 6.48·W^(0.325), t = 0.83·W^(0.259), preferred for atmospheric-transmissivity-inclusive QRA. - ✅ **Surface Emissive Power (SEP)** design value = **300 kW/m²** for LPG fireballs. - ✅ **Point-source view factor** F₁₂ = D²/(4r²) — valid for **r ≫ D**. - ✅ **Atmospheric transmissivity** τ = exp(–1.2·r^0.32) [TNO] — **CRITICAL** for r > 100 m. - ✅ **Eisenberg fatality probit:** Y = –14.9 + 2.56·ln(t·q″^(4/3)). - ✅ **Damage thresholds:** 1 kW/m² = 1st-degree burn, 5 kW/m² = 2nd-degree burn, 12.5 kW/m² = fatality in < 60 s. - ✅ **Prevention:** pressure relief valves, fireproofing, water spray cooling, separation distances. - ✅ **Real cases:** Mexico City 1984, Jaipur 2009, Tlahuelilpan 2019 — all LPG fireball disasters. - ✅ **Engineering use:** QRA event-tree analysis, plant layout per **API 521 / IS 1446**, emergency planning. - ✅ **For KTU answer-writing:** always label units, state the correlation you are using, apply τ, and mention all three BLEVE hazards.

topics.title: Confined Explosions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Confined Explosions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Confined Explosion** is defined as the rapid, self-propagating combustion of a flammable mixture (gas, vapor, mist, or dust) contained within an enclosed or partially enclosed vessel, equipment, building, or process enclosure, where the expanding gaseous products of combustion generate a transient, destructive pressure rise before the combustion wave can vent safely to the atmosphere. The process is governed by three simultaneous phenomena: 1. **Heat release** from the exothermic oxidation reaction. 2. **Gas expansion** due to the temperature spike (often **>2000 K**). 3. **Pressure wave generation** that loads the structural walls. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Standard Definition (verbatim style):** > "A confined explosion occurs when a flammable atmosphere, upon ignition, releases energy faster than the enclosing structure can relieve it, resulting in a measurable overpressure (typically **Pmax = 7 to 9 bar(g)** for hydrocarbons) against the confining walls." --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" Principle** > > Imagine boiling a kettle with the lid sealed. As the water turns to steam, the volume of gas tries to expand ~1700×, but the lid blocks it. Pressure builds rapidly. If the lid cannot vent, it eventually bursts catastrophically. > A *confined explosion* is identical, except the "boiling" is **microsecond-scale combustion**, and the expanding medium is the **hot combustion gas** (CO₂, H₂O, N₂) rather than steam. > > The **cage (vessel)** = the kettle. > The **flammable cloud** = the water. > The **ignition spark** = the stove knob. > The **burst** = the explosion. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value (Hydrocarbon) | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Maximum Explosion Pressure | $P_{max}$ | **7 – 9** (gas), **9 – 12** (dust) | bar(g) | | Maximum Rate of Pressure Rise | $\left(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max}$ | **100 – 1000** | bar/s | | Gas Explosion Index | $K_G$ | 10 – 750 | bar·m/s | | Dust Explosion Index | $K_{St}$ | 10 – 600 | bar·m/s | | Laminar Burning Velocity | $S_u$ | **0.3 – 0.8** | m/s | | Auto-Ignition Temperature | AIT | **200 – 600** | °C | | Minimum Ignition Energy | MIE | **0.1 – 1.0** (gas) | mJ | | Lower Flammability Limit | LFL | **1 – 5** | vol % | | Upper Flammability Limit | UFL | **8 – 15** | vol % | | Limiting Oxygen Concentration | LOC | **8 – 12** | vol % O₂ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** The parameter $K_G$ (or $K_{St}$) is the *single most important* classification number in confined explosion analysis. Examiners love asking its derivation from the **Cubic Law**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Stages of a Confined Explosion (Step-by-Step Logic) A confined explosion evolves through **four discrete stages**: - **Stage 1 — Ignition (t = 0):** A point source of energy (spark, hot surface) initiates a thin flame kernel at the center of the flammable mixture. - **Stage 2 — Hemispherical Growth (Sub-sonic):** The flame kernel grows outward as a smooth, hemispherical surface via thermal conduction. This is the **deflagration regime** — flame speed $S_f \approx 1$–$10$ m/s, well below the speed of sound in the unburnt gas. - **Stage 3 — Vessel-Induced Turbulence & Pressure Build-up:** As the flame approaches the walls, the unburnt gas ahead is compressed and *pre-turbulated* by the expanding combustion products. Turbulence dramatically **increases the effective burning velocity** ($S_u$ rises 5–10×). The pressure begins to rise exponentially. - **Stage 4 — Peak Pressure & Possible Detonation Transition:** Pressure peaks at $P_{max}$. If the geometry (long ducts, high $K_G$, strong confinement) is right, the flame front can **accelerate into a detonation** ($S_f > 1000$ m/s). In a *fully* confined vessel without venting, this is the destruction phase. --- ## 2.2 Modes of Combustion Propagation - **Deflagration:** Sub-sonic propagation driven by **thermal diffusion**. - Pressure ratio: $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \approx 6$ to $9$ (isochoric). - Typical in industrial vessels. - **Detonation:** Supersonic propagation driven by a **leading shock wave**. - Pressure ratio: $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} > 15$. - Rare in industry, but extremely destructive when it occurs. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cubic Law (Foundation Equation — Used in ~70% of numerical problems)** > $$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_G \quad \text{(gases, NFPA 68)}$$ $$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_{St} \quad \text{(dusts, ISO 6184)}$$ - $V$ = vessel volume in $\text{m}^3$. - $K_G$ is **size-independent**; it lets you scale data from a 1 m³ lab sphere to a 100 m³ silo. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Adiabatic / Isochoric Combustion Pressure (Theoretical Peak)** > $$P_{max} = \dfrac{P_i \cdot n_b \cdot T_{ad}}{n_u \cdot T_i}$$ > where $n_b, n_u$ = moles of burnt and unburnt gas; $T_{ad}$ = adiabatic flame temperature. > [!IMPORTANT] > **NFPA 68 Vent Sizing — Low-Stress Equation (P_red ≤ 0.5 bar(g))** > $$A_v = A_s \cdot \left[ \frac{K_G}{K_{St,ref}} \right]^{0.5} \quad \text{(simplified Cubic Law vent form)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Enlarged Vessels (NFPA 68, $V > 250$ m³)** > $$A_v = \left(\dfrac{K_G}{P_{red}}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{V^{2/3}}{C}\right)$$ > where $C$ is a vent discharge coefficient (typically 0.5 – 0.9). > [!IMPORTANT] > **TNT Equivalency (Consequence Modeling)** > $$E_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT,unit}}$$ > $E_{TNT,unit} = 4.68$ MJ/kg; $\eta$ = explosion efficiency factor (0.01 – 0.10 typical for vapor cloud). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hooper Equation for TNT Mass Equivalent Blast Damage** > $$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$$ --- ## 2.4 Summary Table — Equations at a Glance | Equation | Application | Variables | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | $K_G = (dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$ | Gas explosion severity | $V$ in m³, dP/dt in bar/s | **NFPA 68** basis | | $K_{St} = (dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$ | Dust explosion severity | Same units | **ISO 6184** | | $P_{max} \approx P_i \cdot E_i$ | Peak overpressure | $E_i$ = expansion ratio (6–8) | Stoichiometric peak | | $A_v \propto V^{2/3}$ | Vent sizing (large vessels) | $A_v$ = vent area | Geometric scaling | | $W_{TNT} = \eta m \Delta H_c / 4.68 \times 10^6$ | Blast damage | $m$ (kg), $\Delta H_c$ (J/kg) | $4.68$ MJ/kg | | $P_{red} = P_{max} \cdot f(A_v/V^{2/3})$ | Reduced pressure post-vent | $f$ from NFPA curves | Vent effectiveness | > **Engineering Utility:** > The $K_G$ / $K_{St}$ system is the **international passport** for explosion protection equipment. It tells the engineer: *Is this gas worse than propane? Do I need explosion isolation? Do I need flameless venting?* Every pressure relief device, every flame arrester, and every isolation valve on a KTU graduate's plant is sized using these equations. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Cubic Law (The KTU Favourite) **Starting premise:** In a spherical vessel of volume $V$, the flame front propagates as an outward-moving sphere. We assume: - Combustion is **isochoric** (no volume change — closed vessel). - Unburnt gas ahead of the flame is **adiabatically compressed**. - The instantaneous burning velocity $S_u$ depends on the local flame radius $r_f$. ### Step 1 — Geometric Burned Volume The radius of the spherical flame kernel at time $t$ is: $$r_f(t) = \int_0^t S_u(\tau) \, d\tau$$ The fraction of volume already burned is: $$X(t) = \frac{V_{burned}}{V} = \left(\frac{r_f}{R}\right)^3$$ where $R$ is the vessel radius. The rate of change of burned volume is: $$\frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{3 \cdot r_f^2}{R^3} \cdot \frac{dr_f}{dt} = \frac{3 \cdot X^{2/3}}{R} \cdot S_u(t)$$ ### Step 2 — Mass Consumption Rate The total mass of unburnt gas is $m_u = \rho_u V (1 - X)$. Mass consumption rate: $$\frac{dm_{burned}}{dt} = \rho_u V \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}$$ ### Step 3 — Heat Release Rate The instantaneous heat release: $$\dot{Q}(t) = \Delta H_c \cdot \rho_u V \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} = \Delta H_c \cdot \rho_u V \cdot \frac{3 X^{2/3} S_u(t)}{R}$$ ### Step 4 — Pressure Rise Rate via Ideal Gas For an isochoric (constant volume) process, $P V = n R_u T$. Differentiating: $$\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{n R_u}{V} \cdot \frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{\rho_u R_u}{M} \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}$$ Substituting energy balance $\dot{Q} = m C_v \dfrac{dT}{dt}$: $$\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{\rho_u R_u}{M} \cdot \frac{\dot{Q}}{m C_v} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{V} \cdot \frac{(\gamma - 1)}{1}$$ where $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ is the ratio of specific heats. ### Step 5 — Peak dP/dt Occurs at X ≈ 0.4 – 0.5 For most hydrocarbons, empirical and analytical studies show the **maximum pressure rise rate** occurs when approximately **half the volume is burned**. At that instant, $X^{2/3} \approx 0.5^{2/3} \approx 0.63$, and $S_u$ is at its turbulence-enhanced value $S_u^{max}$. Therefore: $$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} = \text{const} \cdot \frac{S_u^{max}}{R}$$ ### Step 6 — The Cubic Law Emerges Since $V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^3$, we have $R \propto V^{1/3}$. Therefore: $$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \propto \frac{1}{V^{1/3}}$$ $$\boxed{\;\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_G\;}$$ This is the **Cubic Law**. The constant $K_G$ is purely a property of the fuel–oxidizer mixture and is independent of vessel size. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Pattern) **Problem:** A stoichiometric methane–air mixture explodes in a 2.0 m³ closed test vessel. The recorded $(dP/dt)_{max}$ is **120 bar/s**. Determine: (a) The gas explosion index $K_G$. (b) The expected $(dP/dt)_{max}$ in a 27 m³ industrial hopper handling the same mixture. (c) Comment on the dust-stair classification. ### Solution #### Part (a): $$K_G = \left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = 120 \cdot (2.0)^{1/3}$$ $$(2.0)^{1/3} = 1.2599$$ $$K_G = 120 \times 1.2599 = 151.2 \; \text{bar·m/s}$$ #### Part (b): For the 27 m³ vessel, the same $K_G$ applies: $$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max,27} = \frac{K_G}{V^{1/3}} = \frac{151.2}{(27)^{1/3}} = \frac{151.2}{3.0} = 50.4 \; \text{bar/s}$$ #### Part (c): Looking up the standard $K_G$ reference table: - Methane $K_G \approx 55$ bar·m/s (lab). - The value **151 bar·m/s** corresponds to a **St-1 class gas** (moderately explosive). - Methane itself is normally $K_G = 55$, so the elevated value suggests either a **faster-burning hydrocarbon blend** or **turbulence enhancement**. The hopper would need explosion protection (venting, suppression, or isolation) per **NFPA 68/69**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Indices | Property | $K_G$ (Gas) | $K_{St}$ (Dust) | |---|---|---| | Defined in | **NFPA 68** | **ISO 6184 / EN 14491** | | Typical range | 10 – 750 bar·m/s | 10 – 600 bar·m/s | | Low severity (St-1) | $K_G < 200$ | $K_{St} < 200$ | | Medium severity (St-2) | $200 \le K_G < 300$ | $200 \le K_{St} < 300$ | | High severity (St-3) | $K_G \ge 300$ | $K_{St} \ge 300$ | | Test vessel | 1 m³ sphere (Siwek) | 1 m³ sphere (Siwek) | | Units | bar·m/s | bar·m/s | > **Why the cubic root?** Because the rate-limiting step is the **flame surface area**, which scales as $r^2$, but the volume scales as $r^3$. Hence the empirical exponent 1/3. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Confined Explosion — Event Sequence Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Flammable Mixture
Formed In Vessel"] --> node2["Ignition Source
Spark Or Hot Surface"] node2 --> node3["Flame Kernel
Hemispherical Growth"] node3 --> node4["Turbulence From
Wall Compression"] node4 --> node5["Pressure Build Up
Vessel Walls Loaded"] node5 --> node6{"Vent Opens
Or Vessel Fails"} node6 --> node7["Deflagration
Sub Sonic Release"] node6 --> node8["Detonation
Supersonic Shock"] node7 --> node9["Post Explosion
Damage Assessment"] node8 --> node9 ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Explosion Protection Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Prevention["Layer 1 Prevention"] A1["Inerting
Reduce O2 Below LOC"] A2["Ventilation
Stay Below LFL"] A3["Concentration
Control"] end subgraph Ignition["Layer 2 Ignition Control"] B1["Bonding Grounding
Eliminate Static"] B2["Ex Rated
Electrical Equipment"] B3["Hot Surface
Management"] end subgraph Mitigation["Layer 3 Mitigation"] C1["Explosion Venting
NFPA 68"] C2["Explosion Suppression
NFPA 69"] C3["Explosion Isolation
Flame Arresters"] end subgraph Construction["Layer 4 Construction"] D1["Blast Resistant Walls
NFPA 68"] D2["Deflagration Venting
Panels"] end Prevention --> Ignition Ignition --> Mitigation Mitigation --> Construction ``` ## 4.3 Cubic Law — Process Flow of Derivation ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["Assumption Isochoric
Constant Volume"] --> stepB["Flame Radius Versus Time
rf Of T Equals Integral"] stepB --> stepC["Burned Volume Fraction
X Equals rf Cubed Over R Cubed"] stepC --> stepD["Mass Burn Rate
dX Per dt"] stepD --> stepE["Heat Release Rate
Q Dot"] stepE --> stepF["Pressure Rise Rate
dP Per dt"] stepF --> stepG["Peak At X Equals 0 Point 4 To 0 Point 5"] stepG --> stepH["Cubic Law
dP dt max Times V One Third Equals K"] ``` ## 4.4 Vent Sizing Logic Block (NFPA 68) ```mermaid flowchart TD in1["Identify Fuel
And K St Value"] --> in2["Define Vessel
Volume V"] in2 --> in3["Set Pred
Maximum Allowed Overpressure"] in3 --> in4{"V Greater Than
250 Cubic Meters"} in4 -- "Yes" --> pathA["Use Large Vessel
Enlarged Equation"] in4 -- "No" --> pathB["Use Standard
Cubic Law Vent"] pathA --> out1["Compute Vent Area A Sub v"] pathB --> out1 out1 --> out2["Apply Discharge
Coefficient C"] out2 --> out3["Final A Sub v
In Square Meters"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define the term "Confined Explosion" and list any four important parameters used to characterize its severity.** **Model Answer:** A confined explosion is the rapid combustion of a flammable mixture within an enclosed or partially enclosed space, producing destructive pressure rise due to thermal expansion of combustion products. Four key severity parameters: 1. **$P_{max}$** — Maximum explosion pressure (bar(g)). 2. **$(dP/dt)_{max}$** — Maximum rate of pressure rise (bar/s). 3. **$K_G$** — Gas explosion index (bar·m/s). 4. **$K_{St}$** — Dust explosion index (bar·m/s). --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **State the Cubic Law and explain its physical significance.** **Model Answer:** The Cubic Law states that the product of the maximum rate of pressure rise and the cube root of vessel volume is a constant for a given fuel–oxidizer mixture: $$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_G$$ **Significance:** The $K_G$ value is **size-independent**, allowing engineers to scale explosion data from a 1 m³ laboratory test vessel to industrial-scale equipment (silos, dust collectors, reactors) without re-testing. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]** **(a)** Derive the Cubic Law for confined gas explosions starting from the conservation of energy in an isochoric combustion process. Clearly state all assumptions used. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 5.0 m³ storage tank contains a stoichiometric propane–air mixture. The recorded $(dP/dt)_{max}$ in a 1.0 m³ test sphere is **260 bar/s**.     (i) Calculate the $K_G$ value of propane. **(3 Marks)**     (ii) Predict the $(dP/dt)_{max}$ in the 5.0 m³ tank. **(2 Marks)**     (iii) Recommend a suitable explosion protection strategy. **(2 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Derivation:** Refer to the **Step 1 → Step 6 derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. Key assumptions to explicitly list: - Closed, rigid, spherical vessel of constant volume $V$. - Combustion is isochoric. - Ideal gas behaviour for unburnt mixture. - The flame is a smooth, expanding sphere. - Peak $(dP/dt)$ occurs at $X \approx 0.4$–$0.5$. - The $K_G$ value is fuel-specific and size-independent. Final result: $$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_G$$ **(b)(i):** $$K_G = (260) \cdot (1.0)^{1/3} = 260 \; \text{bar·m/s}$$ **(b)(ii):** $$(5.0)^{1/3} = 1.710$$ $$\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max,5} = \frac{260}{1.710} = 152.05 \; \text{bar/s}$$ **(b)(iii):** A $K_G$ of **260 bar·m/s** places propane in **St-2 (medium severity)** category. Recommended strategy: - **Explosion venting** (NFPA 68) sized using the Cubic Law. - **Flame arresters** on connected piping for explosion isolation. - Consider **inerting** if the process allows it (drop O₂ below LOC ≈ 11% for propane). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]** **(a)** Differentiate between **deflagration** and **detonation** in confined explosions. Explain with neat sketches how a confined explosion evolves through its four stages. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A flour mill silo of volume $V = 250 \; \text{m}^3$ handles wheat flour dust with $K_{St} = 180$ bar·m/s. The maximum allowable reduced explosion pressure is $P_{red} = 0.5$ bar(g). Using the **NFPA 68 enlarged vessel equation**: $$A_v = \left(\frac{K_{St}}{P_{red}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{V^{2/3}}{C}\right)$$ with discharge coefficient $C = 0.65$, calculate the required vent area. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Deflagration vs. Detonation:** | Feature | Deflagration | Detonation | |---|---|---| | Propagation mode | Heat conduction | Shock wave | | Flame speed | 1 – 10 m/s | 1000 – 3500 m/s | | Pressure ratio ($P_2/P_1$) | 6 – 9 | $> 15$ | | Damage mechanism | Quasi-static pressure rise | Impulsive overpressure | Refer to the **event sequence diagram (Section 4.1)** for the four stages. **(b) Vent Area Calculation:** $$V^{2/3} = (250)^{2/3} = (250)^{0.6667} \approx 39.69$$ $$A_v = \left(\frac{180}{0.5}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{39.69}{0.65}\right) = 360 \cdot 61.06$$ $$\boxed{A_v \approx 21{,}982 \; \text{cm}^2 \approx 2.20 \; \text{m}^2}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** The vent is about **2.2 m²** — a large opening, often achieved with **hinged explosion panels** or **fracture diaphragms**. In a real mill, this would be split into **multiple smaller panels** (e.g., four panels of 0.55 m² each) to direct venting safely away from personnel. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers** > > 1. **Wrong exponent in vent equation:** Students often write $V^{1/3}$ instead of $V^{2/3}$ in the NFPA 68 enlarged vessel equation. This causes a **massive numerical error** and full marks lost. > 2. **Forgetting the discharge coefficient $C$:** A bare formula without the $0.5$–$0.9$ factor is *incomplete*. KTU examiners specifically test for this. > 3. **Mixing $K_G$ and $K_{St}$:** Use $K_G$ for **gases/vapors** and $K_{St}$ for **dusts**. Citing the wrong index in a dust problem = −2 marks. > 4. **Not stating assumptions in derivations:** The Cubic Law derivation gets **0 marks** without listing the isochoric / ideal gas / spherical flame assumptions. > 5. **Ignoring units:** KTU answer scripts penalize inconsistent units. Always quote $K_G$ in **bar·m/s** and $A_v$ in **m² or cm²**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] A **confined explosion** = rapid combustion in a closed space producing destructive overpressure. - [x] **$P_{max} \approx 7$–$9$ bar(g)** for gases; **9–12 bar(g)** for dusts. - [x] The **Cubic Law** $K_G = (dP/dt)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3}$ is the **most-tested** equation in this module. - [x] **$K_G$ is size-independent** — it is a material property, not a vessel property. - [x] **Deflagration** = sub-sonic; **Detonation** = supersonic shock-driven. - [x] **NFPA 68** governs explosion venting; **NFPA 69** governs explosion suppression. - [x] Vent area scales as **$A_v \propto V^{2/3}$** for large vessels. - [x] Always include **discharge coefficient $C$** in the vent equation. - [x] **TNT equivalency** is used for blast damage assessment in consequence analysis. - [x] Four stages: **Ignition → Hemispherical Growth → Turbulence & Pressure Rise → Peak / Detonation**. - [x] Explosion protection hierarchy: **Prevention → Ignition Control → Mitigation → Construction**. - [x] LOC, LFL, MIE, AIT are all required to characterize the *likelihood* side; $K_G$, $P_{max}$, $(dP/dt)_{max}$ characterize the *severity* side. - [x] The **Siwek 1 m³ sphere** is the international standard test apparatus. - [x] **St-1 / St-2 / St-3** classification based on $K_{St}$ value thresholds (200, 300 bar·m/s).

topics.title: Pool Fires
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pool Fires # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition A **Pool Fire** is a turbulent diffusion fire burning above a horizontal pool of vaporizing flammable liquid, where the fuel vaporization rate is controlled primarily by **heat feedback** from the flame to the liquid surface. The combustion zone is anchored over a quasi-steady fuel layer of known geometry (typically circular, rectangular, or spill-defined), and the fire is sustained without significant external flow. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 3):** A *pool fire* is a combustion phenomenon in which a liquid fuel, contained in a bund, dike, or unconfined spill, burns in a stationary diffusion flame over the pool surface. The governing parameter is the **mass burning rate per unit area (ṁ″)**, which depends on pool diameter, fuel thermophysical properties, and ambient conditions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Candle in a Saucer Imagine a tea-light candle. The wax melts into a small liquid pool; the flame's heat melts more wax, vaporizes it, and feeds the flame. Now scale this up to a **2000 m² crude oil spill** in a refinery bund. The physics is identical — a flame hovers over a vaporizing liquid, the heat recycling between flame and pool surface is the engine, and the rate at which the liquid disappears is the **burning rate**. **Key Insight for Students:** A pool fire is *self-fuelling*. The flame does not require an external pump, pipe, or jet (unlike a jet fire). Remove the liquid, and the fire goes out. ## 1.3 Why Pool Fires Matter in KTU/Industry Pool fires are the **most frequent loss-of-containment event** in the process industry (oil & gas, petrochemical, tank farms). They initiate cascading failures: - Heat radiation damages adjacent equipment (escalation to BLEVE/VCE). - Smoke plumes reduce visibility for emergency response. - Bund fire scenarios can last for **days** (e.g., 1988 Piper Alpha, 2005 Buncefield). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Values (API RP 521 / TNO Yellow Book):** > - **Surface Emissive Power (SEP)** of large hydrocarbon pools: **150 – 300 kW/m²** > - **Radiative Fraction (χᵣ)** for gasoline: **≈ 0.20** > - **Stoichiometric air-to-fuel ratio** for hydrocarbons: ≈ **14.7 kg air / kg fuel** ## 1.4 Classification of Pool Fires | Type | Fuel | Typical Diameter | Pool Source | |---|---|---|---| | Bund fire | Crude, gasoline | 1 – 50 m | Tank farm secondary containment | | Spill fire | LNG, LPG condensate | 10 – 100 m | Pipeline rupture | | Tray fire | Solvents | 0.1 – 2 m | Process equipment leak | | Marine pool | Bunker fuel | 10 – 500 m | Tanker spill | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Governing Sub-Processes A pool fire is not a single phenomenon — it is the **coupled solution of three sub-problems**: 1. **Vaporization (Pool → Vapor):** Driven by conductive + radiative heat feedback from flame. 2. **Mixing and Combustion (Vapor → Flame):** Buoyancy-driven turbulent diffusion flame. 3. **Radiative Emission (Flame → Target):** Soot-laden luminous flame acts as a grey-body radiator. Each sub-process has its own characteristic length and time scale. ## 2.2 Pool Burning Rate Models ### 2.2.1 The Infinitely-Large Pool Limit (ṁ″∞) For very large pools, the burning rate becomes diameter-independent because the flame is too thick for the rim to drain heat radially: $$\dot{m}''_{\infty} = \frac{k_{c}/c_{p}}{(D/\dot{Q}^*)^{1/3} \cdot \text{(correction)}}$$ In practice, **ṁ″∞ is read from empirical tables** (e.g., Babrauskas 1983): | Fuel | ṁ″∞ (kg/m²s) | ΔHᶜ (MJ/kg) | χᵣ | |---|---|---|---| | Methanol | 0.017 | 20.0 | 0.20 | | Gasoline | 0.055 | 43.7 | 0.20 | | Kerosene | 0.039 | 43.0 | 0.30 | | Diesel | 0.045 | 43.0 | 0.30 | | Crude oil | 0.034 – 0.067 | 42.5 | 0.20 – 0.40 | | LNG (methane) | 0.078 | 50.0 | 0.20 | | LPG (propane) | 0.099 | 46.0 | 0.30 | | Benzene | 0.085 | 40.1 | 0.40 | ### 2.2.2 Finite Pool Correction (Babrauskas Correlation) For finite pool diameter **D**, the effective burning rate is: $$\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \left(1 - e^{-k\beta D}\right)$$ - **k** = absorption-extinction coefficient of the flame (typical: **0.7 – 2.4 m⁻¹**) - **β** = mean beam length correction factor (≈ 1.0 for circular pools) - **D** = pool diameter (m) For small pools (D < critical ≈ 0.2 m for hydrocarbons), the relationship becomes linear: $$\dot{m}'' \approx k\beta D \cdot \dot{m}''_{\infty} \quad \text{(Thomas regime, small pools)}$$ ## 2.3 Total Heat Release Rate The total heat release rate of a circular pool fire is: $$Q = \dot{m}'' \cdot A_p \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \eta_c$$ where: - $A_p = \pi D^2 / 4$ is the pool area (m²) - $\Delta H_c$ is the heat of combustion (J/kg) - $\eta_c$ is the combustion efficiency (≈ **0.95** for open-air pool fires) ## 2.4 Flame Height Correlations ### 2.2.3 Heskestad Plume Correlation (Most Common in KTU Textbooks) For a circular pool of diameter D and heat release rate Q: $$\frac{H_f}{D} = -1.02 + 15.6 \cdot (Q^{*})^{2/3}$$ where the dimensionless heat release rate is: $$Q^* = \frac{Q}{\rho_{\infty} c_p T_{\infty} \sqrt{gD} \cdot D^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Validity:** Valid for **Q* > 1** (buoyancy-driven turbulent diffusion flames). For very small pools or very low Q, this may underpredict. ### 2.2.4 Thomas Correlation (Alternative, KTU-Standard) $$H_f = 42 \cdot D \cdot \left(\frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_{\infty} \sqrt{gD}}\right)^{0.61}$$ Both correlations yield flame heights in the range **2D to 5D** for typical hydrocarbon pool fires (2–20 m for 1–5 m diameter pools). ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Burning rate (finite pool) | $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_\infty (1 - e^{-k\beta D})$ | kg/m²s | Babrauskas | | 2 | Pool area (circular) | $A_p = \pi D^2 / 4$ | m² | Geometry | | 3 | Mass loss rate | $\dot{m} = \dot{m}'' \cdot A_p$ | kg/s | — | | 4 | Total HRR | $Q = \dot{m}'' A_p \Delta H_c \eta_c$ | W (J/s) | ηc ≈ 0.95 | | 5 | Radiative HRR | $Q_r = \chi_r \cdot Q$ | W | χᵣ from tables | | 6 | Flame height (Heskestad) | $H_f/D = -1.02 + 15.6 (Q^*)^{2/3}$ | m | Q* > 1 | | 7 | Flame height (Thomas) | $H_f = 42D(\dot{m}''/(\rho_\infty\sqrt{gD}))^{0.61}$ | m | Empirical | | 8 | Surface emissive power | $E = \dot{m}'' \cdot \Delta H_c \cdot \chi_r$ | kW/m² | SEP, ≈ 150–300 | | 9 | Point-source radiation | $q'' = \tau \cdot Q_r / (4\pi r^2)$ | kW/m² | Simple | | 10 | Solid-flame radiation | $q'' = E \cdot F \cdot \tau$ | kW/m² | View factor F | | 11 | View factor (vertical target) | $F_v = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\arctan\frac{h}{\sqrt{S^2+1}} + \frac{h}{S}\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{S^2+1}}\right)\right]$ | — | h = Hf/r, S = x/r | | 12 | Atmospheric transmissivity | $\tau = 1.006 - 0.0117 \log_{10}(X) - 0.000232 X$ | — | X = r · RH (Pa) | | 13 | Froude number (flame tilt) | $Fr = u_w^2 / (gD)$ | — | Wind correlation | | 14 | Tilted flame height | $H_f(\theta) = H_f \cos\theta + L_f \sin\theta$ | m | L_f ≈ 4D | > **Notation in table above:** $\sqrt{gD}$ = square root of (g × D), not absolute values. $\arctan\frac{h}{\sqrt{S^2+1}}$ uses $\sqrt{\cdot}$ not $\vert\cdot\vert$. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Pool fire calculations feed directly into: - **Thermal radiation contour maps** for layout planning (API RP 521). - **Emergency Impoundment (Bund) Sizing** — bund capacity = 110% of largest tank. - **Fire Protection Engineering** — water deluge demand = $q_{w} = Q_r / (L_v \cdot \eta_{d})$. - **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** — pool fire is one of the canonical reference scenarios (TNO Yellow Book, "Crest" model). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation A: Mass Burning Rate for Finite Pool (Babrauskas Energy Balance) We derive the classical Babrauskas correlation by balancing the **conductive-radiative heat flux to the pool surface** against the **mass flux of vaporization** plus the **conductive heat loss into the sub-cooled liquid layer**. ### Step 1: Energy balance at the pool surface The flame radiates and convects heat back to the liquid; the liquid uses this energy to vaporize and to conduct downward into the bulk liquid: $$q''_{rad,fb} + q''_{conv,fb} = \dot{m}'' \left[\Delta H_v + c_{p,l}(T_s - T_\infty)\right] + q''_{loss}$$ **Reasoning (row by row):** - $q''_{rad,fb}$ = radiative feedback from flame soot zone. - $q''_{conv,fb}$ = convective feedback (small in large pools, neglected here). - $\dot{m}'' \Delta H_v$ = energy absorbed by vaporization. - $\dot{m}'' c_{p,l}(T_s - T_\infty)$ = sensible heating of the fuel. - $q''_{loss}$ = conduction into the bulk liquid below the vaporization front. ### Step 2: Recognize the two limiting cases - **Small pool:** Rim effects dominate. Heat is conducted radially to the rim; the loss term is large, so $\dot{m}''$ scales with D. - **Large pool:** Rim is far away. Quasi-steady conduction zone forms; $\dot{m}''$ saturates at $\dot{m}''_\infty$. ### Step 3: Solve the radial conduction problem (small pool limit) A semi-infinite liquid with a circular heated zone of radius R = D/2 has a mean surface temperature $T_s$ maintained over time. The bulk liquid beneath is initially at $T_\infty$. The transient conduction solution yields, after integrating over the disk: $$q''_{loss} = \frac{2 k_l (T_s - T_\infty)}{\sqrt{\pi \alpha t}}$$ **Logic:** This is the classic 1D semi-infinite slab solution $\text{erfc}(0) = 1$ modified for a finite heated disk. ### Step 4: Combine and integrate For a quasi-steady burning time, integrate the loss term and equate to absorbed energy. Solving with the boundary condition that $\dot{m}'' \to \dot{m}''_\infty$ as $D \to \infty$ yields the canonical form: $$\dot{m}''(D) = \dot{m}''_\infty \left[1 - \exp\left(-k \beta D\right)\right]$$ **Reasoning:** The exponential captures the *saturation* of rim heat loss as pool size grows — beyond a critical $D_c = 3/(k\beta)$, $\dot{m}''$ is within 95% of $\dot{m}''_\infty$. ### Step 5: Numerical evaluation example **Problem:** A gasoline pool of diameter **D = 10 m** burns. Given $k = 1.1 \text{ m}^{-1}$ and $\beta = 1.0$. $$k \beta D = 1.1 \times 1.0 \times 10 = 11$$ $$\dot{m}'' = 0.055 \times (1 - e^{-11}) = 0.055 \times (1 - 1.67 \times 10^{-5}) \approx 0.0550 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$$ **Conclusion:** A 10 m gasoline pool behaves as an *infinitely large* pool — no rim correction needed. ## 3.2 Derivation B: View Factor for a Cylindrical Flame to a Vertical Target A pool fire flame can be modeled as a **vertical cylinder** of diameter D, height $H_f$, with its base at grade. For a small flat vertical target located at horizontal distance $r$ from the cylinder axis, the view factor is given by the **Mudan/Crawford correlation**: $$F_v = \frac{1}{\pi} \arctan\frac{h}{\sqrt{S^2+1}} + \frac{h}{\pi S}\left[1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{S^2+1}}\right] - \frac{\alpha}{\pi}\arctan\frac{h \cdot c}{S} + \frac{h \cdot c}{\pi S} - \frac{c}{\pi}\arctan\frac{h}{S}$$ where: - $h = H_f / r$ (dimensionless flame height) - $S = x / r$ (ratio of target height to distance) - $x$ = vertical height of the target above flame base - $c$ = tilt correction factor For an **untilted flame** ($c \to \infty$, no wind), the formula simplifies to: $$F_v = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\arctan\frac{h}{\sqrt{S^2+1}} + \frac{h}{S}\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{S^2+1}}\right)\right]$$ ### Step-by-step numerical example **Given:** $H_f = 20$ m, $r = 30$ m, $x = 5$ m, no wind. $$h = \frac{H_f}{r} = \frac{20}{30} = 0.667, \quad S = \frac{x}{r} = \frac{5}{30} = 0.167$$ Compute $\sqrt{S^2 + 1} = \sqrt{0.0278 + 1} = \sqrt{1.0278} = 1.0138$. Term 1: $$\frac{1}{\pi}\arctan\frac{0.667}{1.0138} = \frac{1}{\pi}\arctan(0.658) = \frac{1}{\pi}(0.583 \text{ rad}) = 0.1856$$ Term 2: $$\frac{0.667}{\pi \cdot 0.167}\left(1 - \frac{1}{1.0138}\right) = \frac{0.667}{0.5247}\left(1 - 0.9864\right) = 1.271 \times 0.0136 = 0.01728$$ $$F_v = 0.1856 + 0.0173 = 0.2029$$ ## 3.3 Derivation C: Radiative Heat Flux at Target (Point-Source Approximation) For initial estimation, treat the flame as a **point source** of radiative power $Q_r$ located at the center of the flame: $$q'' = \frac{Q_r \cdot \tau}{4 \pi R_s^2}$$ where $R_s$ is the slant distance from flame centroid to target, and $\tau$ is atmospheric transmissivity. ### Numerical example **Given:** $\dot{m}'' = 0.055$ kg/m²s, $D = 10$ m, $\Delta H_c = 43.7$ MJ/kg, $\chi_r = 0.20$, $R_s = 50$ m, $\tau = 0.85$. $$\dot{m} = 0.055 \times \frac{\pi (10)^2}{4} = 4.32 \text{ kg/s}$$ $$Q = 4.32 \times 43.7 \times 10^6 \times 0.95 = 1.79 \times 10^8 \text{ W}$$ $$Q_r = 0.20 \times 1.79 \times 10^8 = 3.58 \times 10^7 \text{ W}$$ $$q'' = \frac{3.58 \times 10^7 \times 0.85}{4 \pi (50)^2} = \frac{3.04 \times 10^7}{31416} = 968 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 1.0 \text{ kW/m}^2$$ **Conclusion:** ~1 kW/m² at 50 m — sufficient to cause second-degree burns in 30 s, and exceed the failure threshold of unprotected steel. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Damage Criteria for Reference | Heat Flux (kW/m²) | Effect on Equipment / People | |---|---| | 1.0 | Sunlight equivalent; no damage | | 4.0 | Pain threshold for skin (20 s exposure) | | 6.3 – 12.5 | Cable damage; wood ignition (with time lag) | | 12.5 – 25 | Unprotected steel structural failure (minutes) | | 25 – 37.5 | Process equipment damage; severe burns | | > 100 | Catastrophic failure, concrete spalling | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Pool Fire Physics — Process Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Flammable Liquid Spill
Pool Formation] --> B[Heat Feedback from Flame
Radiation + Convection] B --> C[Vaporization of Liquid
Fuel Mass Loss Rate] C --> D[Fuel Vapor Mixing with Air
Turbulent Buoyant Plume] D --> E[Combustion in Flame Zone
Soot Formation] E --> F[Flame Geometry
Height and Diameter] F --> G[Thermal Radiation to Targets
View Factor and Transmissivity] G --> H[Damage to Equipment
Escalation Analysis] H --> I{Threshold Exceeded?} I -- Yes --> J[Initiating Event for
BLEVE VCE Domino Effects] I -- No --> K[Safe Separation Maintained] E --> B ``` ## 4.2 Pool Fire Calculation Methodology (KTU-Standard Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[INPUT
Fuel Type and Pool Diameter D] --> B0[Step 1
Burning Rate mdot_inf] B0 --> C0[Step 2
Finite Pool Correction
Babrauskas Equation] C0 --> D0[Step 3
Heat Release Rate Q] D0 --> E0[Step 4
Flame Height Hf
Heskestad or Thomas] E0 --> F0[Step 5
Radiative Heat Release Qr] F0 --> G0[Step 6
View Factor Fv] G0 --> H0[Step 7
Atmospheric Transmissivity tau] H0 --> I0[Step 8
Incident Heat Flux q_doubleprime] I0 --> J0[OUTPUT
Damage Assessment] ``` ## 4.3 Flame Geometry Schematic (Block-Level Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TB P0[Liquid Pool
Diameter D] --> F0A subgraph F0A[Flame Zone] direction TB Z1[Base Region
Continuous Vapor Phase] Z2[Persistent Soot Zone
Luminous Yellow Orange] Z3[Tip Region
Intermittent Plume] Z1 --> Z2 --> Z3 end F0A --> RAD[Radiative Emission
from Soot Zone
Surface Emissive Power E] F0A --> CONV[Convective Plume Rise
Buoyancy Driven] RAD --> T0[Target at Distance r
View Factor F and Transmissivity tau] CONV --> T0 ``` ## 4.4 Pool Fire Tilt Mechanism under Wind ```mermaid flowchart LR W[Wind Velocity uw] --> FR[Froude Number
Fr = uw² over gD] FR --> T1{Fr less than 0.1} FR --> T2{Fr 0.1 to 1.0} FR --> T3{Fr greater than 1.0} T1 --> R1[Vertical Flame
No Tilt] T2 --> R2[Moderate Tilt
theta approx 20 to 40 deg] T3 --> R3[Strong Tilt
Flame Touchdown
Pool Geometry Distorted] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Practical Implication:** Under high wind, the flame *touchdown point* shifts downwind, and the effective view factor to a target upwind can decrease significantly — but the *flame length* increases, and so does the radiation footprint on downwind equipment. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **Define a pool fire. List the four key parameters that govern its thermal hazard.** **Model Answer:** A pool fire is a **turbulent diffusion flame burning above a stationary liquid pool**, sustained by vaporization of the fuel under radiative and convective heat feedback from the flame. Four governing parameters: 1. **Mass burning rate per unit area (ṁ″)** — function of fuel type and pool diameter. 2. **Heat of combustion (ΔHᶜ)** — energy released per kg of fuel. 3. **Radiative fraction (χᵣ)** — fraction of total HRR emitted as thermal radiation. 4. **Flame geometry (D, H_f)** — determines view factor to a target. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Why does the burning rate of a finite hydrocarbon pool fire increase with pool diameter and saturate at large diameters? Use the concept of rim heat loss.** **Model Answer:** In small pools, a significant fraction of the heat received by the liquid is **conducted radially toward the cooler pool rim**, where it is lost. As the diameter increases, the rim becomes geometrically farther away (heat loss scales as 1/D for a thin disk), so more energy becomes available for vaporization. The burning rate $\dot{m}''$ thus grows with D and asymptotically approaches $\dot{m}''_\infty$ — the infinitely-large-pool value governed by 1D vertical conduction and flame radiation feedback. The Babrauskas relation $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_\infty(1 - e^{-k\beta D})$ captures this saturation. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer either the entire Q from Choice-1 OR the entire Q from Choice-2. ### Choice-1 **Question A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Derive the Babrauskas correlation for finite pool burning rate, starting from an energy balance at the pool surface. Clearly state the assumptions. (7 marks) **(b)** A **circular gasoline pool of diameter 15 m** burns in the open. Using the data below, compute: - The steady burning rate per unit area (ṁ″) - The total heat release rate (Q) - The radiative heat release rate (Qᵣ) - The flame height using the Heskestad correlation **Data:** $\dot{m}''_\infty = 0.055$ kg/m²s, $k\beta = 1.1$ m⁻¹, $\Delta H_c = 43.7$ MJ/kg, $\eta_c = 0.95$, $\chi_r = 0.20$, $\rho_\infty = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.0$ kJ/kgK, $T_\infty = 293$ K, $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 marks) **Model Solution (a):** 1. Set up surface energy balance: $q''_{fb} = \dot{m}'' L_v + \dot{m}'' c_{p,l}(T_s - T_\infty) + q''_{loss}$. 2. Identify $q''_{fb}$ as flame radiation (dominant in large pools) = $k\beta E_f$ where $E_f$ is flame emissive power. 3. Recognize two regimes: (i) **1D vertical conduction limit** (large D) giving $\dot{m}''_\infty$, (ii) **radial rim loss regime** (small D) where $q''_{loss} \propto 1/\sqrt{D}$. 4. Combine limits: $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_\infty(1 - e^{-k\beta D})$, where the exponential captures the rim-loss attenuation. **Assumptions stated:** quasi-steady burning; no significant wind effect; constant thermophysical properties; soot-dominated flame radiation; sub-cooled liquid pool. **Model Solution (b):** Step 1 — Burning rate: $$k\beta D = 1.1 \times 15 = 16.5 \implies e^{-16.5} \approx 7 \times 10^{-8} \approx 0$$ $$\dot{m}'' = 0.055(1 - 0) = 0.055 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$$ Step 2 — Pool area and mass loss: $$A_p = \frac{\pi (15)^2}{4} = 176.7 \text{ m}^2$$ $$\dot{m} = 0.055 \times 176.7 = 9.72 \text{ kg/s}$$ Step 3 — Total heat release rate: $$Q = 9.72 \times 43.7 \times 10^6 \times 0.95 = 4.04 \times 10^8 \text{ W} = 404 \text{ MW}$$ Step 4 — Radiative heat release rate: $$Q_r = 0.20 \times 404 \times 10^6 = 80.8 \text{ MW}$$ Step 5 — Flame height via Heskestad: $$Q^* = \frac{Q}{\rho_\infty c_p T_\infty \sqrt{gD} D^2} = \frac{4.04 \times 10^8}{1.2 \times 1000 \times 293 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 15} \times 225}$$ $$Q^* = \frac{4.04 \times 10^8}{1.2 \times 1000 \times 293 \times 12.13 \times 225} = \frac{4.04 \times 10^8}{9.60 \times 10^8} = 0.421$$ Hmm — $Q^* = 0.421 < 1$, so Heskestad validity is marginal. We will use a generalized form valid for all $Q^*$: $$H_f/D = -1.02 + 15.6 (Q^*)^{2/3} = -1.02 + 15.6 (0.421)^{2/3} = -1.02 + 15.6 \times 0.563 = 7.76$$ $$H_f = 7.76 \times 15 = 116.4 \text{ m}$$ This is excessively high. We revert to the **Thomas correlation** which is more reliable for very large $Q$: $$H_f = 42 \times 15 \times \left(\frac{0.055}{1.2\sqrt{9.81 \times 15}}\right)^{0.61} = 630 \times (0.01176)^{0.61} = 630 \times 0.0574 = 36.2 \text{ m}$$ **Conclusion:** Flame height is in the range **30 – 50 m**, dominated by buoyancy. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students forget to convert $\Delta H_c$ from MJ/kg to J/kg (factor of 10⁶). Marks are routinely lost here. > > **Pitfall #2:** Confusion between **heat release rate (W)** and **heat flux (W/m²)**. Q is total power; q″ is flux at a target. --- ### Choice-2 **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]** **(a)** What is a view factor? Derive the expression for the view factor from a cylindrical pool fire flame to a small vertical target, clearly defining all variables. (7 marks) **(b)** A vertical target of height **5 m** is located at a horizontal distance of **25 m** from a kerosene pool fire. The flame height is **18 m**. Calculate: - The view factor $F_v$ (use the simplified Mudan/Crawford formula for an untilted flame). - The incident heat flux on the target, given surface emissive power **E = 150 kW/m²** and atmospheric transmissivity **τ = 0.80**. (7 marks) **Model Solution (a):** The **view factor (configuration factor) F** is the fraction of radiative energy leaving surface 1 that is intercepted by surface 2. For a cylindrical flame (height $H_f$, diameter $D$, base at grade) to a small flat vertical target at distance $r$: $$F_v = \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\arctan\frac{h}{\sqrt{S^2+1}} + \frac{h}{S}\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{S^2+1}}\right)\right]$$ where: - $h = H_f / r$ = dimensionless flame height. - $S = x / r$ = dimensionless target height. - $x$ = target height above ground. The derivation integrates Lambert's cosine law over the flame surface elements and the target surface, then applies the **reciprocity theorem** $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$, exploiting the small-target assumption ($A_2 \to 0$). **Model Solution (b):** Step 1 — Compute dimensionless parameters: $$h = \frac{18}{25} = 0.72, \quad S = \frac{5}{25} = 0.20$$ Step 2 — Compute the geometric term: $$\sqrt{S^2 + 1} = \sqrt{0.04 + 1} = \sqrt{1.04} = 1.0198$$ Step 3 — Evaluate the first term: $$\frac{1}{\pi}\arctan\frac{0.72}{1.0198} = \frac{1}{\pi}\arctan(0.7061) = \frac{1}{\pi}(0.6147) = 0.1957$$ Step 4 — Evaluate the second term: $$\frac{0.72}{\pi \times 0.20}\left(1 - \frac{1}{1.0198}\right) = 1.1459 \times (1 - 0.9806) = 1.1459 \times 0.0194 = 0.02224$$ Step 5 — Total view factor: $$F_v = 0.1957 + 0.0222 = 0.2179$$ Step 6 — Incident heat flux: $$q'' = E \cdot F_v \cdot \tau = 150 \times 0.2179 \times 0.80 = 26.1 \text{ kW/m}^2$$ **Conclusion:** The incident heat flux of **~26 kW/m²** is sufficient to cause **structural failure of unprotected steel in minutes** — well above the 12.5 kW/m² damage threshold. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** Students often confuse the **slant distance** $R_s = \sqrt{r^2 + (H_f/2)^2}$ with the horizontal distance $r$ in the point-source model. Use the correct one based on the chosen model. > > **Pitfall #4:** Atmospheric transmissivity $\tau$ depends on **humidity and distance**; it is *not* a constant 1.0. Use the Wayne/Brotz correlation for a 7-mark question. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Pool fire** = stationary diffusion flame over a vaporizing liquid; self-fuelled by flame heat feedback. - [x] **Burning rate (ṁ″)** depends on fuel type and pool diameter; saturates at $\dot{m}''_\infty$ for large pools. - [x] **Babrauskas equation** $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_\infty(1 - e^{-k\beta D})$ is the KTU standard for finite pools. - [x] **Heat release rate** $Q = \dot{m}'' A_p \Delta H_c \eta_c$; remember unit conversions (MJ to J). - [x] **Radiative fraction χᵣ** is fuel-specific: ~0.20 for gasoline/methane, ~0.30 for diesel/LPG, ~0.40 for benzene. - [x] **Flame height** is typically **2D to 5D** for buoyancy-driven pool fires; use Heskestad for $Q^* > 1$, Thomas as fallback. - [x] **View factor** is the geometric link between the flame and the target; Mudan formula is the KTU benchmark. - [x] **Atmospheric transmissivity** $\tau$ reduces radiation with distance and humidity; Wayne/Brotz formula. - [x] **Solid-flame model** is more accurate than point-source for close targets (< 3D). - [x] **Wind tilt** modifies both flame length and view factor; use the Froude number $Fr = u_w^2 / (gD)$ as the threshold. - [x] **Damage thresholds**: 4 kW/m² (pain), 12.5 kW/m² (steel damage), 25+ kW/m² (catastrophic). - [x] **Common mistakes**: unit errors, misuse of point-source vs solid-flame, ignoring tilt and transmissivity.

topics.title: Jet Fires
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Jet Fires

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jet Fire:** A turbulent diffusion flame produced by the continuous combustion of a fluid (gas, vapor, two-phase, or flashing liquid) issuing with significant momentum from an orifice, rupture, or leak in a pressurized containment system. The flame shape, length, and thermal radiation output are governed primarily by the **mass release rate**, **exit velocity**, **thermodynamic state of the fluid**, and **ambient wind conditions**.

According to the **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** and **API RP 521** classification adopted by KTU 2024 scheme modules, jet fires are categorized as a *non-explosive, sustained, momentum-driven thermal hazard* — distinct from pool fires (gravity-driven) and flash fires (transient cloud combustion).

**Standard Metric Designations Used in KTU Examinations:**
- **Flame Length (L_f)** — meters (m)
- **Mass Release Rate (ṁ)** — kg/s
- **Radiative Heat Flux (q″)** — kW/m²
- **Surface Emissive Power (SEP)** — kW/m²
- **View Factor (F_v)** — dimensionless
- **Atmospheric Transmissivity (τ_a)** — dimensionless

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> 🔥 **Think of a Jet Fire as an Industrial "Blowtorch from a Punctured Gas Cylinder":**

Imagine you have a high-pressure LPG cylinder that develops a small crack. The pressurized gas rushes out at high velocity. If someone nearby has a lit match or there's an ignition source, the escaping gas doesn't just burn like a candle flame — it forms a long, roaring, high-momentum flame that *shoots* several meters away from the leak point, like a giant industrial blowtorch. The flame's tip can be **20–50 times the diameter of the leak hole**, depending on pressure.

This is fundamentally different from a *pool fire* (where liquid accumulates on the ground and burns upward like a campfire). In a jet fire, the **momentum of the escaping fluid drags the flame away from the source**, and the flame's geometry becomes the dominant factor in determining *who or what gets burned* downstream — often equipment, other pipes, or personnel far from the actual leak.

In process plants, jet fires from natural gas, hydrogen, or propane leaks are responsible for **~30% of major hydrocarbon fire incidents** (per the HSE UK Failure Database), making them a critical concern in any Hazard Identification (HAZID) or Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) study.

### 1.3 Why Jet Fires Matter in KTU Context

| Property | Jet Fire | Pool Fire | Flash Fire |
|----------|----------|-----------|------------|
| Driving force | **Momentum** | Gravity | Cloud dispersion |
| Orientation | Horizontal/directed | Vertical/upward | Cloud-shaped |
| Sustained? | Yes (continuous source) | Yes (until fuel exhausted) | Transient |
| Primary damage | Thermal radiation | Thermal radiation | Thermal radiation + asphyxiation |
| Typical length | **5–200 m** | 1–10 m diameter | 10–1000 m cloud |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners specifically test students on the **distinction between jet, pool, and flash fires**, as this distinction determines which consequence model (API, Chamberlain, TNO Multi-Energy) is applicable. Misclassifying a jet fire as a pool fire is a **common 2–3 mark deduction** in valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a Jet Fire

A jet fire evolves through the following structured sequence:

1. **Release Phase**: Pressurized fluid (gas or flashing liquid) escapes through an orifice. Exit velocity approaches sonic conditions if $P_{vessel} / P_{atm} \geq 1.89$ (choked flow condition).
2. **Entrainment Phase**: The high-momentum jet entrains surrounding air via turbulent mixing at the jet boundary. The mixing layer thickness grows linearly with downstream distance.
3. **Combustion Zone**: Combustion occurs where fuel-to-air ratio falls within flammability limits (typically 5–15% vol. for methane).
4. **Lift-Off Region**: The flame lifts off the leak point once exit velocity exceeds a critical threshold, creating the characteristic elongated flame shape.
5. **Radiative Emission**: Hot combustion products (T ≈ 1500–2000 K) emit thermal radiation primarily via soot-mediated continuum emission.

### 2.2 Physical Mechanisms (Why & How)

**Why does the flame lengthen?**
The flame length is essentially the distance required for complete entrainment of stoichiometric air. Higher mass flow rates demand more air → longer flame until stoichiometry is satisfied.

**How is radiation transmitted?**
Hot soot particles (radii ~10–50 nm) within the flame volume act as the primary radiators. Radiation intensity scales with the **4th power of flame temperature** (Stefan-Boltzmann) and is strongly attenuated by atmospheric water vapor and CO₂.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for jet fire calculations. Master these equations — they appear in nearly every Part B question.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $L_f = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{0.0044 \cdot \sqrt{\rho_{rel}}}$ — for horizontal jet | Flame length correlation (Chamberlain/HSE) | m |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot P \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma M}{R T} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$ | Choked (sonic) mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 3 | $q'' = \dfrac{F_v \cdot \tau_a \cdot SEP}{4 \pi r^2}$ | Radiative heat flux (point source model) | kW/m² |
| 4 | $SEP = 0.023 \cdot \dot{m}^{1.18}$ | Surface Emissive Power (gas jet, API correlation) | kW/m² |
| 5 | $\tau_a = 2.02 \cdot (P_w \cdot r)^{-0.09}$ | Atmospheric transmissivity (HSE formula) | – |
| 6 | $P_w = 1013.25 \cdot RH \cdot e^{\left(18.956 - \dfrac{4030}{T_{amb} + 235\right)}} \cdot 10^{-5}$ | Water vapor partial pressure (used in $\tau_a$) | Pa (or mmHg equivalent) |
| 7 | $F_v \approx \dfrac{1}{4\pi}\left(\dfrac{r}{L_f}\right)\left[\text{geometric view factor}\right]$ | View factor for cylindrical flame approximation | – |
| 8 | $r_{safe} = \sqrt{\dfrac{F_v \cdot \tau_a \cdot SEP}{4\pi \cdot q''_{threshold}}}$ | Safe separation distance for given heat flux | m |
| 9 | $t_{pr} = -36.38 + 2.56 \ln\left(V\right)$ | Probit equation for thermal lethality | – |
| 10 | $V = (q'')^{1.33} \cdot t_{exp}$ | Thermal dose (Haber's law, t in seconds) | (kW/m²)^1.33 · s |

**Where:**
- $\dot{m}$ = mass release rate (kg/s)
- $\rho_{rel}$ = relative density of gas (vs air)  
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (≈0.6–0.85)
- $A$ = release area (m²)
- $P$ = upstream pressure (Pa)
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats (≈1.4 for natural gas)
- $M$ = molecular weight (kg/kmol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 kJ/kmol·K**
- $q''_{threshold}$ = critical heat flux (4.7 kW/m² for bare skin 30s, 12.5 kW/m² for 100% lethality in 60s)
- $t_{exp}$ = exposure time (s)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Jet fire modeling is essential for:
- **Onshore/Offshore platform layout design** (API RP 521, ISO 13702)
- **Fire & Gas (F&G) detector placement** — flame detectors must cover jet flame trajectories
- **Firewater deluge system design** — spray density calculations depend on jet fire impingement zones
- **Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valve isolation time** — determines how long a jet fire persists
- **Insurance risk assessment** — quantified radiation zones determine property loss estimates
- **Occupational safety** — escape route design in process modules

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mass Release Rate Through a Rupture (Choked Flow Case)

**Starting Equation:** For isothermal compressible flow through an orifice, applying conservation of mass and energy (steady-state, 1D), the maximum mass flux occurs when flow becomes **choked** (Mach = 1 at the throat).

$$
\dot{m}_{choked} = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R_{sp} T_1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}
$$

**Where:**
- $P_1$ = upstream stagnation pressure (Pa)
- $T_1$ = upstream temperature (K)
- $R_{sp} = R / M$ = specific gas constant (J/kg·K)
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient

**Step-by-step logical derivation:**

**Step 1:** Starting from the 1D energy equation for a compressible gas with no shaft work and adiabatic flow:

$$
h_1 + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2}
$$

For choked flow at the throat, $v_2 = a_2$ (local sonic velocity), and we apply isentropic relations.

**Step 2:** Using $h = c_p T$ and the isentropic relation for choked flow:

$$
