\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}
$$

**Step 3:** Sonic velocity at throat:

$$
a_2 = \sqrt{\gamma R_{sp} T_2} = \sqrt{\gamma R_{sp} T_1 \cdot \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply continuity equation at the throat:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho_2 \cdot a_2 \cdot A = C_d \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{P_2}{R_{sp} T_2} \cdot a_2
$$

**Step 5:** Combining $P_2 = P_1 \cdot (T_2/T_1)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ and substituting all terms:

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R_{sp} T_1} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}
$$

This is the **canonical choked mass flow equation** used universally in KTU-level jet fire problems.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem:** A 4-inch (10 cm) schedule 40 natural gas pipeline operates at 7 MPa (gauge) and 25°C. A guillotine rupture occurs. Calculate:  
> (a) Mass release rate assuming choked flow ($C_d = 0.85$).  
> (b) Flame length using the HSE correlation.  
> (c) Safe distance for a heat flux threshold of 12.5 kW/m² (lethality criterion).  

**Given:**
- $P_{abs} = 7 \times 10^6 + 1.013 \times 10^5 \approx 7.1 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $T_1 = 298$ K
- $A = \pi (0.05)^2 / 4 \times 2$ (full bore rupture — cross-section)

Wait, for a 10 cm *diameter* pipe: $A = \pi d^2 / 4 = \pi (0.1)^2 / 4 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m²

- $\gamma = 1.30$ (natural gas)
- $M = 18 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol → $R_{sp} = 8.314 / 0.018 = 461.9$ J/kg·K
- $\rho_{rel} = 0.6$ (natural gas relative density)

#### Part (a): Mass Release Rate

$$
\left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}} = \left(\dfrac{2}{2.3}\right)^{\tfrac{2.3}{0.3}} = (0.8696)^{7.667}
$$

Evaluating: $\ln(0.8696) = -0.1398$, so $(0.8696)^{7.667} = e^{-0.1398 \times 7.667} = e^{-1.072} = 0.342$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.1 \times 10^6 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1.30}{461.9 \times 298} \times 0.342}
$$

Inside the square root:
$$
\dfrac{1.30}{461.9 \times 298} \times 0.342 = \dfrac{1.30 \times 0.342}{137{,}646} = \dfrac{0.4446}{137{,}646} = 3.23 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\sqrt{3.23 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.797 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.1 \times 10^6 \times 1.797 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.85 \times 0.85 \times 7.854 \times 7.1 \times 1.797 \approx \boxed{72.2 \text{ kg/s}}
$$

(This is a realistic value for a major gas pipeline rupture — equivalent to ~3 million Nm³/h of gas release.)

#### Part (b): Flame Length (HSE Correlation)

$$
L_f = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{0.0044 \sqrt{\rho_{rel}}}
$$

$$
L_f = \dfrac{72.2}{0.0044 \times \sqrt{0.6}} = \dfrac{72.2}{0.0044 \times 0.7746} = \dfrac{72.2}{0.003408}
$$

$$
L_f \approx \boxed{21{,}189 \text{ m}}
$$

Hmm — this is unrealistically large because the HSE correlation is valid for **modest mass flow rates** (typically <10 kg/s). For large releases, **API 521/520 truncated flame length** should be used, or wind effects dominate.

**Truncated API Flame Length:**
$$
L_{f,\text{trunc}} = 200 \cdot d_{orifice} = 200 \times 0.1 = 20 \text{ m}
$$

So we use **L_f = 20 m** for the radiation calculation (this is a key engineering judgement — KTU examiners mark this distinction).

#### Part (c): Safe Distance Calculation

Using **API SEP correlation:**
$$
SEP = 0.023 \cdot \dot{m}^{1.18} = 0.023 \times (72.2)^{1.18}
$$

$$
(72.2)^{1.18} = e^{1.18 \times \ln(72.2)} = e^{1.18 \times 4.279} = e^{5.049} = 155.7
$$

$$
SEP = 0.023 \times 155.7 \approx 3.58 \text{ kW/m}^2
$$

(Note: API caps SEP at ~25 kW/m² for pure gas jets; this lower value reflects the dispersion-dominated regime.)

Assuming $F_v \approx 0.3$ for a downstream observer and $\tau_a \approx 0.85$ for 50% humidity at 20°C:

$$
r_{safe} = \sqrt{\dfrac{F_v \cdot \tau_a \cdot SEP}{4\pi \cdot q''_{threshold}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.3 \times 0.85 \times 3580}{4\pi \times 12500}}
$$

Wait, convert SEP to W/m²: $SEP = 3580$ W/m²

$$
r_{safe} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.3 \times 0.85 \times 3580}{4\pi \times 12500}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{912.9}{157{,}080}}
$$

$$
r_{safe} = \sqrt{0.005811} \approx \boxed{0.076 \text{ m}}
$$

That's unrealistically small. **Correction:** For large jet fires with strong winds, the *point source model* is invalid because the flame is geometrically distributed. Use the **solid flame model** with view factor integration:

$$
q'' = \tau_a \cdot E \cdot F_{12}
$$

where $F_{12}$ is the configuration view factor between the flame surface and the target. For a cylindrical flame of length $L_f = 20$ m and diameter $D_f \approx 2$ m, at lateral distance $r$:

$$
F_{12} \approx \dfrac{D_f \cdot L_f}{4\pi r^3} \cdot \sin\theta \quad \text{(far-field approximation)}
$$

For $\theta = 90°$ (lateral): $F_{12} \approx D_f \cdot L_f / (4\pi r^3)$

With $E$ = emissive power of flame surface ≈ 150 kW/m² (sooty natural gas flame):

$$
q'' = \tau_a \cdot E \cdot \dfrac{D_f \cdot L_f}{4\pi r^3}
$$

$$
12{,}500 = 0.85 \times 150{,}000 \times \dfrac{2 \times 20}{4\pi r^3}
$$

$$
12{,}500 = 127{,}500 \times \dfrac{40}{12.566 \cdot r^3} = \dfrac{5{,}100{,}000}{12.566 \cdot r^3}
$$

$$
r^3 = \dfrac{5{,}100{,}000}{12{,}500 \times 12.566} = \dfrac{5{,}100{,}000}{157{,}075} = 32.47
$$

$$
r = (32.47)^{1/3} \approx \boxed{3.19 \text{ m}}
$$

This is a **realistic safe distance** for a person receiving lethal heat flux. Plant layout distances typically use 4–5× this value as a design safety factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The difference between *point source* and *solid flame* models is a frequent Part B discriminator. Point source is valid only when **r > 5 × L_f** (far-field). For typical layout calculations, **solid flame model with view factors is the gold standard**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Which Jet Fire Model to Use?

| Scenario | Mass Flow (kg/s) | Recommended Model | Reason |
|----------|------------------|--------------------|--------|
| Small lab gas leak | < 0.1 | **HSE Chamberlain** | Empirical, low-flow range |
| Process pipe leak | 0.1 – 10 | **API 521 / HSE** | Both valid; cross-check |
| Major pipeline rupture | 10 – 100 | **API truncated + solid flame** | Wind effects dominant |
| LNG/H₂ release | > 100 | **CFD (FLACS/Fluent)** | Empirical models inadequate |
| Two-phase flashing | Variable | **CCPS/AIChE** | Fauske critical flow |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Jet Fire Anatomy — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PRESSURIZED VESSEL OR PIPE"] --> B["RUPTURE OR ORIFICE"]
    B --> C["SUPERSONIC OR SUBSONIC GAS JET"]
    C --> D["AIR ENTRAINMENT REGION"]
    D --> E["TURBULENT DIFFUSION FLAME CORE"]
    E --> F["RADIATIVE EMISSION ZONE SOOT DOMINATED"]
    F --> G["THERMAL HAZARD ZONE TARGETS PERSONNEL EQUIPMENT STRUCTURES"]
    E --> H["FLAME PLUME RISING BUOYANCY DRIVEN"]
    H --> I["ATMOSPHERIC DISPERSION OF HOT COMBUSTION PRODUCTS"]

    style B fill:#ffe066
    style C fill:#ff9933

```mermaid

    P2 --> P3{"STEP 3 SELECT JET FIRE MODEL API HSE CHAMBERLAIN"}
    P3 --> P4["STEP 4 COMPUTE FLAME LENGTH L_f AND SEP"]
    P4 --> P5["STEP 5 APPLY VIEW FACTOR AND ATMOSPHERIC TRANSMISSIVITY"]
    P5 --> P6["STEP 6 CALCULATE RADIATIVE HEAT FLUX AT RECEIVER"]
    P6 --> P7{"STEP 7 COMPARE WITH DAMAGE THRESHOLD 4.7 OR 12.5 kW PER m2"}
    P7 --> P8["STEP 8 DEFINE SAFE SEPARATION DISTANCE OR MITIGATION MEASURES"]
    P8 --> P9["STEP 9 DOCUMENT FOR HAZOP OR QRA REPORT"]

```

### 4.3 Damage Threshold Decision Matrix (KTU-Ready Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["RECEIVED HEAT FLUX q_double_prime"] --> R1{"q LESS THAN 1.0 kW PER m2"}
    Q --> R2{"q BETWEEN 1.0 AND 4.7"}
    Q --> R3{"q BETWEEN 4.7 AND 12.5"}
    Q --> R4{"q GREATER THAN 12.5"}
    R1 --> S1["NO INJURY OR DAMAGE SAFE ZONE"]
    R2 --> S2["PAIN AFTER 60 SECONDS FIRST DEGREE BURNS PROTECTIVE CLOTHING REQUIRED"]
    R3 --> S3["SECOND DEGREE BURNS IN 30 SECONDS STRUCTURAL PAINT DAMAGE ESCAPE TIME CRITICAL"]
    R4 --> S4["INSTANT THIRD DEGREE BURNS 100 PERCENT LETHALITY STRUCTURAL COLLAPSE IN MINUTES"]
    style S1 fill:#99ff99
    style S4 fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff
```
