# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a toxic gas. Differentiate between a simple asphyxiant and a chemical asphyxiant with one example each. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **toxic gas** is a gaseous substance that, when inhaled, absorbed, or otherwise introduced into the body in sufficient concentration over a defined exposure duration, produces acute or chronic harmful biological effects, including physiological impairment, illness, or death.

**Simple Asphyxiant:** Acts by physically displacing oxygen from breathable air, thereby reducing the partial pressure of O₂ below the physiologically tolerable limit of approximately 19.5 %. The gas itself is essentially non-toxic; the hazard is purely one of oxygen deprivation. **Example:** Nitrogen (N₂), Carbon dioxide (CO₂), Methane (CH₄).

**Chemical Asphyxiant:** Possesses inherent toxicity and actively interferes with the body's oxygen-transport or oxygen-utilization biochemistry, even when present at low concentrations in an otherwise oxygen-sufficient atmosphere. **Example:** Carbon monoxide (CO) — binds haemoglobin with an affinity ~240× that of O₂, forming carboxyhaemoglobin; Hydrogen sulphide (H₂S) — inhibits cytochrome-c oxidase in the mitochondrial electron-transport chain.

### Question 2: State Haber's Law of inhalation toxicology. Why is the exponent n important in QRA? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Haber's Law (1924):** For a defined biological endpoint (e.g., 50 % mortality), the product of exposure concentration raised to an exponent $n$ and exposure duration is constant:

$$
C^n \times t = k
$$

**Importance of the exponent $n$ in QRA:**

- The exponent $n$ quantifies the **degree of nonlinearity** between concentration and toxic effect.
- For $n = 1$ (e.g., HCN), the response is purely dose-driven — doubling C gives twice the effect at half the time.
- For $n > 1$ (e.g., NH₃, where $n = 2$), increasing the concentration has a *disproportionately greater* effect — a small concentration rise produces a large jump in fatality probability.
- The probit equation input variable $V = C^n t$ depends on $n$, so it directly influences the calculated fatality footprint area, which in turn determines land-use planning, shelter-in-place radii, and emergency response zoning under KSPCB and the Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC, 1989 amended 2000).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A: Chlorine Release Consequence Analysis `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the acute toxic effects of chlorine (Cl₂) on the human respiratory system. Explain why the probit equation for chlorine uses the form $Y = -15.33 + 3.13 \ln(C \cdot t)$ and discuss the engineering significance of the coefficient b = 3.13. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50-tonne chlorine railcar ruptures catastrophically at a fertilizer plant. Using the standard Gaussian heavy-gas dispersion approach, the concentration at the plant control room (250 m downwind) is estimated as $C = 60$ ppm, and the time for the cloud to pass is $t = 12$ min. Using the chlorine probit equation, determine the percentage fatality at the control-room location. Comment on the engineering controls required. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Chlorine is a yellow-green pungent gas with high water solubility. Upon inhalation, it reacts with the moist mucosal surfaces of the upper and lower respiratory tract to form **hydrochlorous (HOCl) and hydrochloric (HCl) acids**:

$$
Cl_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons HOCl + HCl
$$

These species are potent oxidizers that produce **chemical burns, inflammation, and pulmonary oedema**. Acute effects include:

- **Mild (1–10 ppm):** Mucous-membrane irritation, lacrimation, throat burning.
- **Moderate (10–30 ppm):** Cough, chest tightness, dyspnoea.
- **Severe (30–60 ppm):** Bronchospasm, chemical pneumonitis, pulmonary oedema with delayed onset (24–48 h).
- **Lethal (>100 ppm):** Massive alveolar damage, ARDS, death.

The probit equation for chlorine is:

$$
Y = -15.33 + 3.13 \ln(C \cdot t)
$$

The form $C^1 \cdot t$ reflects that the toxicological exponent $n$ for Cl₂ is essentially 1, meaning the response is linear in the product C·t (Haber's Law holds in its simplest form for this gas).

**Engineering significance of $b = 3.13$:**  
The slope $b$ governs the *steepness* of the dose-response curve on the probit scale. A larger $b$ implies that a small change in the toxic load produces a large change in fatality probability — i.e., the response is *sensitive* to dose. For chlorine, $b = 3.13$ is moderate, indicating that fatality probability rises sharply within a narrow range of toxic loads. Operationally, this means dispersion modeling must be precise, and buffer distances must be conservatively large.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: $C = 60$ ppm, $t = 12$ min, $C \cdot t = 60 \times 12 = 720$ ppm·min.

**Step 1 — Compute the natural logarithm of the toxic load.**  

$$
\ln(C \cdot t) = \ln(720) = 6.579
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the chlorine probit equation.**  

$$
Y = -15.33 + 3.13 \times 6.579
$$

$$
Y = -15.33 + 20.59 = 5.26
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Probit to percentage.**  

Using the standard normal relationship:

$$
z = Y - 5 = 5.26 - 5 = 0.26
$$

$$
P\% = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{0.26}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}(0.1838)\right]
$$

From the error-function table, $\text{erf}(0.1838) \approx 0.2057$:

$$
P\% = 50 \times (1 + 0.2057) = 50 \times 1.2057 = 60.3\%
$$

**Result:** Approximately **60 % of exposed personnel** at the control room are predicted to suffer fatality.

**Engineering controls required:**

- **Detection & Alarm:** Toxic-gas detectors with thresholds at 0.5 ppm (TLV) and 5 ppm (alarm), with redundancy and coverage to the prevailing wind direction.
- **Containment:** Double-walled storage with nitrogen padding; emergency isolation valves (EIVs) actuated within 60 s.
- **Ventilation & Scrubbing:** Emergency scrubbers (NaOH absorption) and water curtains; the plant should have a 100 % capacity caustic scrubber train.
- **Shelter-in-place & Evacuation:** Predefined evacuation routes; SCBA provision for all personnel; at least 30-min self-contained breathing apparatus for the control-room operators.
- **Land-use planning:** A 1.5- to 3-km safety buffer for populations in the prevailing downwind direction as per the AEGL-3 and ERPG-3 levels.

---

### Question B: Haber's Law and Hydrogen Sulphide `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive Haber's Law of inhalation toxicology. State the assumptions and limitations of the law. Discuss why the law is invalid for highly reactive or highly soluble gases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For hydrogen sulphide (H₂S), the probit equation is given by:

$$
Y = -31.42 + 3.008 \ln(C^{1.43} \cdot t)
$$

where $C$ is in ppm and $t$ in minutes. If a refinery releases H₂S such that a worker 100 m downwind is exposed to $C = 200$ ppm for $t = 8$ min, determine: (i) the probit value, (ii) the percentage fatality, and (iii) the toxic load (V) and discuss whether doubling the concentration halves the time required to produce the same fatality. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Derivation of Haber's Law:**

Haber proposed that the biological response $R$ to an inhaled gas is a function of the *dose* — the product of concentration and exposure duration. For an exposure rate of $C$ (ppm) over time $t$ (min), the cumulative dose $D$ delivered to the alveolar region is proportional to $C \times t$. For a constant biological effect:

$$
C \times t = k \quad \text{(simplified form, } n = 1\text{)}
$$

In the generalized form, concentration may act nonlinearly on the receptor, so:

$$
C^n \times t = k
$$

Taking natural logarithms:

$$
n \ln C + \ln t = \ln k
$$

**Assumptions of Haber's Law:**

1. The toxicant follows a linear dose-response relationship at the receptor.
2. There is no threshold concentration below which no effect occurs.
3. Exposure is continuous and constant over time $t$.
4. The toxicant does not bioaccumulate, metabolize, or detoxify significantly during the exposure period.
5. The biological endpoint is well-defined and reproducible.

**Limitations:**

- Fails for **highly soluble gases** (e.g., NH₃, HCl) where the upper respiratory tract absorbs the bulk of the dose, and a plateau effect occurs at high concentrations.
- Fails for **highly reactive gases** (e.g., Cl₂, O₃) that cause local tissue damage; doubling the concentration does not simply double the dose at the alveoli.
- Fails for **chronic, low-level exposures** where time-dependent bioaccumulation or repair kinetics dominate.
- Fails for **sensitizers** (e.g., TDI, MDI) where immune-mediated response is non-monotonic.

**Why the law is invalid for highly reactive/soluble gases:**  
For these gases, the dose is not simply $C \times t$ because the gas is *consumed* at the point of contact, and the receptor (lung tissue) saturates. Increasing C beyond a certain value produces diminishing additional damage because the tissue is already maximally affected. Conversely, for highly reactive gases, doubling the concentration may *more than* double the effect — hence $n > 1$.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: $C = 200$ ppm, $t = 8$ min, $n = 1.43$, $a = -31.42$, $b = 3.008$.

**Step 1 — Compute the toxic load $V$.**  

$$
V = C^{1.43} \cdot t = 200^{1.43} \times 8
$$

We compute $200^{1.43}$:

$$
\ln(200^{1.43}) = 1.43 \times \ln(200) = 1.43 \times 5.298 = 7.576
$$

$$
200^{1.43} = e^{7.576} = 1949.6
$$

Therefore:

$$
V = 1949.6 \times 8 = 15{,}597 \quad \text{(ppm}^{1.43}\text{·min)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the probit value.**  

$$
\ln(V) = \ln(15597) = 9.656
$$

$$
Y = -31.42 + 3.008 \times 9.656 = -31.42 + 29.045 = -2.375
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage fatality.**  

$$
z = Y - 5 = -2.375 - 5 = -7.375
$$

This $z$ value is far in the negative tail of the standard normal distribution, corresponding to essentially **0 % fatality** at the given exposure. This indicates that at $C = 200$ ppm for $t = 8$ min, the H₂S cloud produces a sub-lethal toxic load for the population.

**Step 4 — Doubling concentration analysis.**  

If $C$ is doubled from 200 to 400 ppm while keeping the toxic load the same:

$$
400^{1.43} \cdot t_2 = 200^{1.43} \cdot 8
$$

$$
t_2 = \frac{200^{1.43} \times 8}{400^{1.43}} = \frac{1949.6 \times 8}{400^{1.43}}
$$

Computing $400^{1.43}$:

$$
\ln(400^{1.43}) = 1.43 \times \ln(400) = 1.43 \times 5.991 = 8.567
$$

$$
400^{1.43} = e^{8.567} = 5242.4
$$

Therefore:

$$
t_2 = \frac{15597}{5242.4} = 2.975 \text{ min}
$$

So the time required is reduced from 8 min to ~2.98 min, a reduction of about 2.69× — **not** the factor of 2 that simple linear Haber's Law (n=1) would predict. The exponent $n = 1.43$ makes the effect of concentration disproportionately stronger, meaning doubling C more than halves the time required for the same biological effect.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Pitfalls in Toxic Gas Effect Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the units** of concentration (ppm) and time (min) when using probit equations — loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Conflating ppm with mg/m³** in calculations; you must use the form of $C$ that matches the probit coefficients (always check the table).
> 3. **Using the wrong exponent $n$** when computing toxic load $V = C^n t$. The $n$ value is gas-specific and not always 1.
> 4. **Skipping the Probit-to-Percentage conversion step.** KTU examiners require both the probit value $Y$ *and* the final percentage; showing only $Y$ is incomplete.
> 5. **Omitting the engineering interpretation.** A pure mathematical answer without the engineering mitigation discussion loses at least 2 marks in the 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Confusing TLV with IDLH.** TLV is for chronic/occupational exposure (8-h TWA); IDLH is the 30-min escape limit. Examiners catch this repeatedly.
> 7. **Writing the probit equation with the wrong base logarithm.** All KTU probit equations use the natural logarithm (ln), not log₁₀.
> 8. **Ignoring the population density** in the consequence footprint. The footprint area alone is meaningless without specifying the population at risk.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Module 3 Recap — Toxic Gas Effects:**

- **Definition:** A toxic gas is any gaseous substance that produces a harmful biological response at sufficient C × t.
- **Classification axes:** (1) Mechanism of action, (2) Target organ, (3) Exposure duration (acute vs chronic).
- **Simple asphyxiant** = displaces O₂ (N₂, CO₂, CH₄). **Chemical asphyxiant** = interferes with O₂ transport/utilization (CO, H₂S, HCN).
- **Haber's Law:** $C^n \times t = k$. For $n = 1$, simple C·t dose-response.
- **Probit equation:** $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$; $Y = 5$ ⇒ 50 % affected; $Y = 4$ ⇒ ~16 %; $Y = 6$ ⇒ ~84 %.
- **Probit-to-percentage formula:** $P\% = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$.
- **Toxic Load $V$:** $V = C^n \cdot t$ is the probit input variable.
- **Key limit values (always know at least 3 for each gas):** TLV-TWA, TLV-STEL, TLV-C, PEL, IDLH, ERPG-1/2/3, AEGL-1/2/3.
- **Common industrial gases:** CO, H₂S, HCN, Cl₂, NH₃, SO₂, NO₂, HF, Phosgene, MIC, Benzene, Formaldehyde.
- **Highly reactive gases have n > 1** (e.g., NH₃: n = 2; H₂S, HCN: n = 1.43).
- **Consequence pathway:** Source → Release → Dispersion → C(t) → Toxic Load → Probit → % affected → Footprint → Risk.
- **Mitigation hierarchy (in order):** Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering controls (containment, scrubbers, BPCS, SIS, ESD) → Administrative controls (procedures, training) → PPE (SCBA, escape masks).
- **KTU software tools** for QRA: ALOHA, SAFETI, PHAST, CANARY.
- **Regulatory anchors in India:** MSIHC Rules 1989 (amended 2000), Factories Act 1948, Manufacture Use Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, KSPCB consent orders, MoEF&CC EIA Notification 2006.
- **Concentration conversion:** $C_{ppm} = \frac{24.45 \times C_{mg/L}}{M}$ (at 25 °C, 1 atm).
- **Endpoint severity hierarchy:** TLV < ERPG-1 < ERPG-2 < ERPG-3 < AEGL-3 ≈ IDLH.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any 14-marker, structure the answer as **(1) Definition & classification**, **(2) Probit/Haber math with all steps shown**, **(3) Numerical substitution with units**, **(4) Probit-to-percentage conversion**, **(5) Engineering mitigation**. Skipping any of these five blocks costs 2–3 marks.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Thermal Effects # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are "Thermal Effects" in Process Safety? In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: PBSFT404), **Thermal Effects** refer to the complete family of phenomena by which a chemical or physical process releases, absorbs, or transports heat in such a way that it can initiate, accelerate, or escalate a hazardous event. In the context of Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment, thermal effects bridge the gap between a *chemical instability* and a *physical consequence* such as fire, explosion, BLEVE, or runaway reaction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Thermal effects* are the temperature-driven physical and chemical changes — including heat generation, heat accumulation, phase change, decomposition, and runaway — that alter the energy balance of a process and contribute to loss of containment, fire, or explosion. The governing quantity is the **heat balance** of a reacting mass: $$ q_{generated} \;=\; q_{accumulated} \;+\; q_{removed} $$ Any imbalance where heat generation outpaces heat removal is a **thermal hazard**. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker That Forgot Its Whistle" Imagine a pressure cooker on a stove. Normally, the burner (energy input) and the whistle (energy output) are balanced — heat enters, steam exits, the cooker is safe. Now imagine the whistle gets blocked: steam pressure and temperature keep climbing. The pot does not "know" that the safety valve has failed; it simply continues to accept heat from the burner. Eventually, the pot ruptures violently. A **thermal runaway** is exactly this — but instead of a blocked whistle, the "blockage" is created by the reaction itself: the hotter the system becomes, the faster the reaction proceeds, the more heat is released, and the temperature climbs further. This is called **positive feedback**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students:** > Almost every major industrial disaster — Bhopal (MIC), Seveso (dioxin), Texas City (ammonium nitrate) — had a *thermal* trigger hidden inside a *chemical* event. Identifying thermal effects means identifying the *upstream cause* of the consequence. --- ## 1.3 Core Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics The following constants and metrics are *non-negotiable* for KTU examinations: - **Adiabatic Temperature Rise (ΔTad)** — the temperature increase if *all* the reaction heat is retained. Units: **K** or **°C**. - **Activation Energy (Ea)** — minimum energy barrier to start a reaction. Units: **kJ/mol** or **J/mol**. - **Universal Gas Constant (R)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**. - **Time to Maximum Rate (TMRad)** — time for an adiabatic runaway to reach its peak rate. Units: **hours, days, or years**. - **Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature (SADT)** — the lowest ambient temperature at which a substance self-heats by ≥ 6 °C in 7 days. Units: **°C**. - **Heat of Reaction (ΔHr)** — enthalpy change per mole reacted. Units: **kJ/mol**. **Negative for exothermic**. - **Heat Release Rate (q̇)** — power per unit volume. Units: **W/m³** or **kW/L**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > The Module 3 outcomes demand that the student be able to (a) recognise thermal runaway, (b) apply Semenov and Frank-Kamenetskii criteria, and (c) interpret TGA/DSC data for hazard assessment. Memorise the metrics above — they appear every examination cycle. --- ## 1.4 The Three "Faces" of Thermal Hazard | Face | Description | Example | |------|-------------|---------| | **Thermal Runaway** | Self-accelerating exothermic reaction | Styrene polymerisation in a tank | | **Thermal Explosion** | Sudden, violent release of accumulated heat | Dust cloud deflagration, condensed-phase explosion | | **Thermal Decomposition** | Break-down of a stable molecule into reactive fragments | Decomposition of organic peroxides | These three faces share the *same root cause*: a violated energy balance. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Energy Balance: The Heart of Every Thermal Problem Consider a reacting mass of volume $V$, density $\rho$, specific heat $C_p$, with an exothermic reaction generating heat at rate $q_G$ (W/m³) and losing heat to the surroundings at rate $q_R$ (W/m³). The transient energy balance is: $$ \rho \, C_p \, \frac{dT}{dt} \;=\; q_G(T) \;-\; q_R(T) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this equation matters:** > This is the *master equation* of thermal effects. The left side is the rate of temperature change (sensible heat storage). The right side is the *competition* between heat generation and heat removal. If the right side becomes positive and *grows with T*, runaway occurs. ### 2.1.1 Heat Generation Term (Arrhenius Behaviour) For a single first-order reaction: $$ q_G(T) \;=\; \Delta H_r \, k_0 \, C^n \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) $$ where $k_0$ is the pre-exponential factor, $C$ is the concentration, and $n$ is the reaction order. ### 2.1.2 Heat Removal Term (Newton's Cooling) $$ q_R(T) \;=\; U \, \frac{A}{V} \, \bigl(T - T_a\bigr) $$ where $U$ is the overall heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $A$ is the heat-transfer area, and $T_a$ is the ambient temperature. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Observation:** $q_G$ is **exponential** in $T$ (Arrhenius), while $q_R$ is **linear** in $T$ (Newtonian cooling). The exponential always wins at high enough temperature — this is the *mathematical origin of runaway*. --- ## 2.2 Thermal Explosion Theories — The Three Classical Models ### 2.2.1 Semenov Theory (Idealised: Zero Internal Temperature Gradient) **Assumption:** The reactant mass is *uniformly mixed*; temperature is constant throughout; only the *boundary* removes heat. - **Heat generation:** $q_G = V \, \Delta H_r \, k_0 \, C^n \, \exp(-E_a / RT)$ - **Heat removal:** $q_R = U \, A \, (T - T_a)$ **Critical condition (Semenov criterion):** At the critical ambient temperature $T_{a,cr}$, the heat-generation curve is *tangent* to the heat-removal line: $$ q_G(T_{cr}) \;=\; q_R(T_{cr}) \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dq_G}{dT}\bigg|_{T_{cr}} \;=\; \frac{dq_R}{dT}\bigg|_{T_{cr}} $$ The resulting critical ambient temperature is given by: $$ T_{a,cr} \;=\; T_{cr} \;-\; \frac{R T_{cr}^2}{E_a} $$ where $T_{cr}$ is the critical reactant temperature. ### 2.2.2 Frank-Kamenetskii Theory (No Mixing: Internal Gradient Exists) **Assumption:** Heat is generated *internally*; no internal convection; heat flows only by conduction to the vessel wall. The governing equation in non-dimensional form: $$ \nabla^2 \theta \;=\; -\delta \, \exp(\theta) $$ where $\theta$ is the non-dimensional temperature and $\delta$ is the **Frank-Kamenetskii parameter**: $$ \delta \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_r \, k_0 \, C^n \, E_a \, L^2}{\lambda \, R \, T_a^2} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T_a}\right) $$ $L$ is the characteristic dimension (half-thickness for slab, radius for cylinder/sphere), and $\lambda$ is the thermal conductivity. **Criticality criterion:** Runaway occurs when $\delta > \delta_{cr}$, where $\delta_{cr}$ depends on geometry: | Geometry | $\delta_{cr}$ | |----------|---------------| | Infinite slab (half-thickness $L$) | 0.878 | | Infinite cylinder (radius $r_0$) | 2.000 | | Sphere (radius $r_0$) | 3.322 | ### 2.2.3 Thomas Theory (Intermediate: Finite Internal Convection) **Assumption:** Internal convection is finite (described by a Biot-like number); neither Semenov nor Frank-Kamenetskii alone is sufficient. The Thomas parameter $\beta$ accounts for both internal conduction and surface cooling: $$ \beta \;=\; \frac{\delta \, \text{Bi}}{1 + \text{Bi}} $$ where $\text{Bi} = U L / \lambda$ is the Biot number. - $\text{Bi} \to 0$: Thomas $\to$ Semenov (no internal resistance). - $\text{Bi} \to \infty$: Thomas $\to$ Frank-Kamenetskii (no surface resistance). --- ## 2.3 Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature (SADT) SADT is the lowest ambient temperature at which the self-accelerating decomposition of a substance occurs in a standard package (typically 50 kg) within 7 days. **Above SADT, the substance will self-heat catastrophically.** For practical screening, the **United Nations SADT test** (UN Test H.4 / H.6) is the reference. A useful theoretical approximation (proposed by Wilberforce, used as a screening tool only): $$ \text{SADT} \;\approx\; T_{a,cr} \;\; \text{(from Semenov analysis)} $$ --- ## 2.4 Time to Maximum Rate (TMRad) TMRad is the adiabatic time required for the reaction rate to reach its maximum (i.e., for the runaway to peak). It is the **operational metric** that tells a process engineer *how much time* they have to intervene. The classical Townsend–Tou equation: $$ \text{TMR}_{ad} \;=\; \frac{C_p \, R \, T^2}{\Delta H_r \, k_0 \, E_a} \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb for KTU:** > - TMRad ≥ 24 h → reaction is "well-behaved"; standard cooling is enough. > - TMRad between 1 h and 24 h → caution; need active cooling or venting. > - TMRad < 1 h → **inherently unstable**; emergency relief design mandatory. --- ## 2.5 Adiabatic Temperature Rise (ΔTad) If *all* the reaction heat is retained (perfect insulation, no losses), the temperature rise is: $$ \Delta T_{ad} \;=\; \frac{-\Delta H_r \, C_{A0}}{\rho \, C_p} $$ where $C_{A0}$ is the initial concentration of reactant A. The factor of 1 (i.e., reaction goes to completion) is implied. For dilute solutions, the molar heat capacity of the entire mass $C_p^{tot}$ is used: $$ \Delta T_{ad} \;=\; \frac{-\Delta H_r \, n_{reacted}}{C_p^{tot}} $$ --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | $\rho C_p (dT/dt) = q_G - q_R$ | Master energy balance | W/m³ | | 2 | $q_G = \Delta H_r \, k_0 \, C^n \exp(-E_a / RT)$ | Arrhenius heat generation | W/m³ | | 3 | $q_R = U (A/V) (T - T_a)$ | Newtonian cooling | W/m³ | | 4 | $T_{a,cr} = T_{cr} - R T_{cr}^2 / E_a$ | Semenov critical ambient | K | | 5 | $\delta = (\Delta H_r k_0 C^n E_a L^2) / (\lambda R T_a^2) \cdot \exp(-E_a / R T_a)$ | Frank-Kamenetskii parameter | dimensionless | | 6 | $\delta_{cr}$ (slab) = 0.878; (cylinder) = 2.000; (sphere) = 3.322 | Criticality thresholds | dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{TMR}_{ad} = (C_p R T^2) / (\Delta H_r k_0 E_a) \cdot \exp(E_a / R T)$ | Time to max rate | s | | 8 | $\Delta T_{ad} = (-\Delta H_r \, n_{reacted}) / C_p^{tot}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise | K or °C | | 9 | $\text{Bi} = U L / \lambda$ | Biot number (Thomas parameter) | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** > - **Reactor design:** Equations 1–4 set the operating-temperature ceiling. > - **Storage/transport:** Equation 9 (with UN H.4) gives SADT for classification of dangerous goods. > - **Emergency relief:** Equation 7 sets the *time window* for rupture-disk sizing (per DIERS methodology). --- ## 2.7 The "Runaway Window" — Combining ΔTad and TMRad A compact two-parameter plot is used in industry: - **x-axis:** TMRad (log scale, hours) - **y-axis:** ΔTad (°C or K) - **Quadrant I (high TMR, low ΔT):** safe. - **Quadrant II (low TMR, low ΔT):** reactive but manageable. - **Quadrant III (low TMR, high ΔT):** **HIGH HAZARD**. - **Quadrant IV (high TMR, high ΔT):** energetic but slow — still requires mitigation. > [!NOTE] > KTU often asks students to *classify* a reaction in this quadrant. The classification is the *first step* in selecting a mitigation strategy (inherent safety → passive → active → procedural). # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Semenov Criticality Criterion We start with the steady-state form of the energy balance (no accumulation, $dT/dt = 0$): $$ q_G(T) \;=\; q_R(T) $$ For an $n$-th order reaction in a well-mixed reactor, with constant concentration $C$: $$ q_G(T) \;=\; V \, \Delta H_r \, k_0 \, C^n \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) $$ $$ q_R(T) \;=\; U \, A \, \bigl(T - T_a\bigr) $$ **Step 1.** At the critical ambient temperature $T_{a,cr}$, the two curves just *touch* — they are tangent. This means both the values *and* the slopes match: $$ q_G(T_{cr}) \;=\; q_R(T_{cr}) $$ $$ \frac{dq_G}{dT}\bigg|_{T_{cr}} \;=\; \frac{dq_R}{dT}\bigg|_{T_{cr}} $$ **Step 2.** Differentiate $q_R$ with respect to $T$: $$ \frac{dq_R}{dT} \;=\; U A \;=\; \text{constant} $$ **Step 3.** Differentiate $q_G$ with respect to $T$. Using $\frac{d}{dT}\exp(-E_a/RT) = \exp(-E_a/RT) \cdot (E_a / R T^2)$: $$ \frac{dq_G}{dT} \;=\; V \Delta H_r k_0 C^n \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) \cdot \frac{E_a}{R T^2} $$ $$ \frac{dq_G}{dT} \;=\; q_G \cdot \frac{E_a}{R T^2} $$ **Step 4.** Set the slopes equal using Step 1 to eliminate $q_G$: $$ q_R(T_{cr}) \cdot \frac{E_a}{R T_{cr}^2} \;=\; U A $$ But $q_R(T_{cr}) = U A (T_{cr} - T_{a,cr})$, so: $$ U A (T_{cr} - T_{a,cr}) \cdot \frac{E_a}{R T_{cr}^2} \;=\; U A $$ **Step 5.** Cancel $U A$ from both sides: $$ (T_{cr} - T_{a,cr}) \cdot \frac{E_a}{R T_{cr}^2} \;=\; 1 $$ **Step 6.** Solve for $T_{a,cr}$: $$ T_{cr} - T_{a,cr} \;=\; \frac{R T_{cr}^2}{E_a} $$ $$ T_{a,cr} \;=\; T_{cr} \;-\; \frac{R T_{cr}^2}{E_a} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** The critical ambient temperature is *lower* than the critical reactant temperature by an amount $R T_{cr}^2 / E_a$, which is small when $E_a$ is large (sharp Arrhenius). For "lazy" reactions (low $E_a$), the difference is bigger — meaning cooling margin is wider. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Semenov Critical Temperature **Problem:** A liquid reactant decomposes exothermically with $\Delta H_r = -150$ kJ/mol, $E_a = 80$ kJ/mol, $k_0 = 10^{12}$ /s, $C = 2$ kmol/m³. The vessel volume is $V = 0.5$ m³, area $A = 3$ m², $U = 250$ W/(m²·K), and $T_a = 300$ K. Is the reactor safe? Find $T_{a,cr}$. **Step 1.** Compute the heat generation function $q_G(T) = V \cdot \Delta H_r \cdot k_0 \cdot C \cdot \exp(-E_a/RT)$ at the *current* $T_a = 300$ K. $$ \frac{E_a}{R T_a} \;=\; \frac{80{,}000}{8.314 \times 300} \;\approx\; 32.07 $$ $$ \exp(-32.07) \;\approx\; 1.27 \times 10^{-14} $$ $$ q_G(300) \;=\; 0.5 \times 150{,}000 \times 10^{12} \times 2 \times 1.27 \times 10^{-14} $$ $$ q_G(300) \;\approx\; 1.905 \times 10^{4} \;\text{W} \;=\; 19.05 \;\text{kW} $$ **Step 2.** Compute the heat removal at $T_a = 300$ K (assuming the reactant temperature equals $T_a$): $$ q_R \;=\; U A (T - T_a) \;=\; 250 \times 3 \times 0 \;=\; 0 \;\text{W} $$ **Step 3.** At $T = 300$ K there is no driving force for cooling, so the reactant will warm up. Repeat at $T = 310$ K: $$ \frac{E_a}{R T} \;=\; \frac{80{,}000}{8.314 \times 310} \;\approx\; 31.04 $$ $$ \exp(-31.04) \;\approx\; 3.39 \times 10^{-14} $$ $$ q_G(310) \;\approx\; 0.5 \times 150{,}000 \times 10^{12} \times 2 \times 3.39 \times 10^{-14} \;\approx\; 5.09 \times 10^{4} \;\text{W} $$ $$ q_R(310) \;=\; 250 \times 3 \times 10 \;=\; 7{,}500 \;\text{W} $$ > At 310 K, $q_G \approx 50.9$ kW vs $q_R = 7.5$ kW — runaway is already happening. **Step 4.** Find the critical $T_{cr}$ where $q_G = q_R$ *and* the slopes match. A numerical iteration (acceptable in KTU answer) gives $T_{cr} \approx 305$ K. **Step 5.** Apply the Semenov criterion: $$ T_{a,cr} \;=\; T_{cr} \;-\; \frac{R T_{cr}^2}{E_a} $$ $$ T_{a,cr} \;=\; 305 \;-\; \frac{8.314 \times 305^2}{80{,}000} $$ $$ T_{a,cr} \;=\; 305 \;-\; \frac{8.314 \times 93{,}025}{80{,}000} $$ $$ T_{a,cr} \;=\; 305 \;-\; 9.66 \;\approx\; 295.3 \;\text{K} $$ **Conclusion:** Since the operating ambient is $T_a = 300$ K > $T_{a,cr} = 295.3$ K, the reactor is **NOT safe** — it is operating above the Semenov limit and will runaway. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Adiabatic Temperature Rise (ΔTad) **Step 1.** In an adiabatic system, $q_R = 0$. The energy balance reduces to: $$ \rho \, C_p \, \frac{dT}{dt} \;=\; q_G(T) $$ **Step 2.** For a complete reaction, integrate over the full conversion. The total heat released per unit mass of reactant is $-\Delta H_r \, C_{A0} / \rho$. This heat raises the temperature by: $$ \Delta T_{ad} \;=\; \frac{q_{total}}{C_p} \;=\; \frac{-\Delta H_r \, C_{A0}}{\rho \, C_p} $$ **Step 3.** Re-cast in molar terms (per mole of limiting reactant): $$ \Delta T_{ad} \;=\; \frac{-\Delta H_r \, n_{limiting}}{C_p^{tot}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Use:** If $\Delta T_{ad}$ > the boiling point of the reactant minus the operating temperature, the system will *self-boil* before stabilising — a precursor to BLEVE. This is why vent sizing (DIERS) starts from $\Delta T_{ad}$. --- ## 3.4 Derivation of TMRad — Townsend–Tou Equation **Step 1.** At low conversion, the concentration $C$ is approximately constant ($C \approx C_0$). The reaction rate is: $$ -r_A \;\approx\; k_0 \, C_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) $$ **Step 2.** The adiabatic energy balance becomes: $$ \rho \, C_p \, \frac{dT}{dt} \;=\; -\Delta H_r \, k_0 \, C_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) $$ **Step 3.** Separate variables: $$ dt \;=\; \frac{\rho \, C_p}{-\Delta H_r \, k_0 \, C_0} \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) dT $$ **Step 4.** The integrand $\exp(E_a/RT)$ is *sharply peaked* near the maximum-rate temperature $T_m$. Use Laplace's method (the steepest-descent approximation): $$ \int_{T_0}^{T_m} \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) dT \;\approx\; \frac{R T_m^2}{E_a} \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R T_m}\right) $$ **Step 5.** Substitute and simplify using $\rho C_p / (-\Delta H_r C_0) = 1/\Delta T_{ad}$: $$ \text{TMR}_{ad} \;\approx\; \frac{R T_m^2}{\Delta T_{ad} \cdot E_a \cdot k_0} \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R T_m}\right) $$ In many textbooks, this is rewritten using the quasi-adiabatic "apparent" rate constant at temperature $T$: $$ \text{TMR}_{ad} \;=\; \frac{C_p \, R \, T^2}{\Delta H_r \, k_0 \, E_a} \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters operationally:** A process engineer measures the *current* temperature $T$ and uses TMRad as the "countdown clock" for emergency response. TMRad is *exponentially* sensitive to $T$ — a 10 K rise can cut TMRad by a factor of 2 to 10. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — When to Use Which Model | Question being asked | Use Semenov | Use Frank-Kamenetskii | Use Thomas | |----------------------|-------------|----------------------|------------| | Stirred tank reactor (forced mixing) | ✔ Best | ✗ | acceptable | | Large storage tank (natural convection only) | ✗ | ✔ Best | acceptable | | Packed bed with conduction only | ✗ | ✔ Best | ✗ | | Intermediate (some mixing, finite Bi) | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ Best | | Quick screening of SADT | ✔ | ✗ | ✗ | | Detailed DIERS vent sizing | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Trap (KTU 2024):** > Students often pick Semenov for *all* problems. Semenov assumes **uniform temperature** — true only for well-stirred, low-viscosity, small reactors. For a 50-tonne warehouse drum, Frank-Kamenetskii is the only correct model. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Thermal Hazard Assessment Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Identify Reacting Substance] --> nodeB[Retrieve Thermochemical Data
Delta H, Ea, k0, Cp] nodeB --> nodeC[Run Calorimetric Tests
TGA and DSC] nodeC --> nodeD{Energy Balance
QG versus QR} nodeD -- QG greater than QR --> nodeE[Classify Severity
Delta Tad and TMRad] nodeD -- QG less than QR --> nodeF[Design Cooling
Margin to Tacr] nodeE --> nodeG{High TMR Low Delta T
Quadrant I or II} nodeE --> nodeH{Low TMR High Delta T
Quadrant III HIGH HAZARD} nodeE --> nodeI[Select Mitigation
Inherent to Procedural] nodeI --> nodeJ[Emergency Relief
DIERS Sizing] nodeI --> nodeK[Inventory Control
and Segregation] nodeI --> nodeL[Active Cooling
and Quench] nodeF --> nodeM[Document SADT
and TMRad for SOP] nodeH --> nodeN[Re-evaluate Process
or Substitute Chemistry] ``` --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Three Classical Thermal Explosion Models Compared ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Reacting Mass] --> nodeB{Type of Mixing
and Geometry} nodeB -- Well mixed uniform T --> nodeC[Semenov Model] nodeB -- No internal mixing --> nodeD[Frank Kamenetskii Model] nodeB -- Finite internal convection --> nodeE[Thomas Model] nodeC --> nodeF[QG exponential in T
QR linear in T
Tangency gives Tacr] nodeD --> nodeG[Heat equation
Del squared theta equals minus delta exp theta
Delta cr depends on geometry] nodeE --> nodeH[Biot number Bi
Interpolates between Semenov and FK
Beta equals delta Bi over 1 plus Bi] nodeF --> nodeI[Critical Ambient Tacr] nodeG --> nodeJ[Critical Delta cr
0.878 slab, 2.0 cylinder, 3.322 sphere] nodeH --> nodeK[Critical Beta cr
Thomas chart] ``` --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Positive Feedback Loop of Thermal Runaway ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA[Initial Disturbance
Cooling Failure or Contamination] --> stepB[Temperature Rises] stepB --> stepC[Reaction Rate Increases
Arrhenius Law] stepC --> stepD[Heat Generation QG Increases] stepD --> stepE[Temperature Rises Further
Positive Feedback] stepE --> stepF{Does Cooling Catch Up?} stepF -- No --> stepG[Runaway Accelerates] stepF -- Yes --> stepH[New Steady State at Higher T] stepG --> stepI[Decomposition or Boiling or Rupture] stepI --> stepJ[Loss of Containment
Fire or Explosion] stepH --> stepK[Continue Operation with Alarm] ``` --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram: The "Runaway Window" — ΔTad vs TMRad ```mermaid flowchart TD q1[Plot TMRad on X axis Log scale hours] --> q2[Plot Delta Tad on Y axis Celsius] q2 --> q3[Quadrant I High TMR Low Delta T SAFE] q2 --> q4[Quadrant II Low TMR Low Delta T MANAGEABLE] q2 --> q5[Quadrant III Low TMR High Delta T HIGH HAZARD] q2 --> q6[Quadrant IV High TMR High Delta T ENERGETIC BUT SLOW] q5 --> q7[Action Substitute or Inherent Safer Design] q6 --> q8[Action Strong Procedural Controls] q4 --> q9[Action Active Cooling and Venting] q3 --> q10[Action Standard Monitoring] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams in KTU exams:** > Examiners often show a Mermaid-like flow diagram in the question paper and ask: *"Identify the missing block."* Knowing the four quadrants of the runaway window and the three explosion models will let you answer in 2–3 lines and earn full marks. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define **thermal runaway** and list the *three* minimum conditions that must be satisfied simultaneously for it to occur in a reactor. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Thermal runaway is a self-accelerating, uncontrolled temperature rise in a reacting system where the rate of heat generation by the exothermic reaction exceeds the rate of heat removal to the surroundings, causing the temperature to climb without external intervention. The three necessary conditions are: 1. **Exothermicity:** The reaction must release heat (i.e., $\Delta H_r < 0$). 2. **Positive temperature coefficient of rate:** The reaction rate must increase with temperature (i.e., Arrhenius behaviour with appreciable $E_a$). 3. **Inadequate heat removal:** The cooling system must be unable to absorb the generated heat — i.e., $q_R < q_G$ at the operating temperature. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "exothermic" with "runaway." An exothermic reaction by itself is *not* a runaway; it becomes a runaway only when the *system response* is positive. Mention all three conditions to score full marks. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **SADT** and **TMRad**. State the typical operational decision based on each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | SADT | TMRad | |-----------|------|---------------| | Full form | Self-Accelerating Decomposition Temperature | Time to Maximum Rate (adiabatic) | | Quantity | Temperature (°C or K) | Time (s, min, h, day) | | Purpose | Sets the **storage and transport** limit | Sets the **emergency response** window | | Test basis | UN H.4 / H.6 (7-day, 50 kg package) | Adiabatic calorimetry (e.g., ARC, VSP) | | Decision | "Do not store above this ambient T" | "Intervene within this time, or design vent" | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often treat SADT and TMRad as interchangeable. They are not. SADT is a *temperature* threshold; TMRad is a *time*. Always quote units and the *decision context*. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student attempts **either** the whole of Question A *or* the whole of Question B (internal choice). --- ### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified for Module 3]** **(a)** With the help of a clearly labelled **energy balance diagram**, derive the **Semenov criticality criterion** for thermal explosion. Define each term and state the underlying assumption. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 1 m³ stirred-tank reactor contains a reacting mixture with the following data: - $\Delta H_r = -200$ kJ/mol - $E_a = 90$ kJ/mol - $k_0 = 4 \times 10^{13}$ /s - $C = 3$ kmol/m³ - $U A = 800$ W/K - $T_a = 320$ K Find the **Semenov critical reactant temperature $T_{cr}$** numerically and comment on whether the operating ambient is safe. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) — Semenov Derivation (7 marks):** Start with the energy balance for a well-mixed reacting mass of volume $V$ and heat-transfer area $A$: $$ \rho \, C_p \, V \, \frac{dT}{dt} \;=\; V \Delta H_r k_0 C^n \exp(-E_a/RT) \;-\; U A (T - T_a) $$ At steady state ($dT/dt = 0$), the *heat generation* curve $q_G(T) = V \Delta H_r k_0 C^n \exp(-E_a/RT)$ and the *heat removal* line $q_R(T) = U A (T - T_a)$ intersect. The critical condition is *tangency*: $$ q_G(T_{cr}) \;=\; q_R(T_{cr}) \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dq_G}{dT}\bigg|_{T_{cr}} \;=\; \frac{dq_R}{dT}\bigg|_{T_{cr}} $$ Differentiate $q_G$ with respect to $T$ (using the chain rule on the Arrhenius term): $$ \frac{dq_G}{dT} \;=\; q_G \cdot \frac{E_a}{R T^2} $$ Setting $dq_G/dT = dq_R/dT = U A$, and using $q_G = U A (T_{cr} - T_{a,cr})$: $$ U A (T_{cr} - T_{a,cr}) \cdot \frac{E_a}{R T_{cr}^2} \;=\; U A $$ Cancel $U A$ and solve: $$ T_{a,cr} \;=\; T_{cr} \;-\; \frac{R T_{cr}^2}{E_a} $$ **Assumption:** Temperature is spatially uniform within the reactor (perfect mixing); heat is removed only at the vessel boundary; physical properties are constant. **(1 mark)** A clearly labelled diagram should show $q_G$ as an exponential curve and $q_R$ as a straight line, with the tangent point at $(T_{cr}, q_{cr})$ and the three regimes: sub-critical, critical, and super-critical. **(2 marks for diagram)** --- **(b) — Numerical Evaluation (7 marks):** The critical condition is: $$ q_G(T_{cr}) \;=\; q_R(T_{cr}) $$ $$ V \Delta H_r k_0 C \exp(-E_a / R T_{cr}) \;=\; U A (T_{cr} - T_a) $$ Plug in values (note $V = 1$ m³, so $V \Delta H_r k_0 C$ becomes $1 \times 200{,}000 \times 4 \times 10^{13} \times 3000 = 2.4 \times 10^{22}$ W): $$ 2.4 \times 10^{22} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{90{,}000}{8.314 \, T_{cr}}\right) \;=\; 800 \, (T_{cr} - 320) $$ **Iterate** (tabular method, each row 1 mark): | Trial $T_{cr}$ (K) | $E_a / R T_{cr}$ | $\exp(-E_a/RT_{cr})$ | LHS (W) | RHS (W) | |----------------|----------|----------|---------|---------| | 330 | 32.81 | 5.55 × 10⁻¹⁵ | 1.33 × 10⁸ | 8,000 | | 340 | 31.84 | 1.43 × 10⁻¹⁴ | 3.44 × 10⁸ | 16,000 | | 350 | 30.93 | 3.51 × 10⁻¹⁴ | 8.42 × 10⁸ | 24,000 | The LHS grows far faster than the RHS; tangency occurs *below* the operating point. A more refined estimate gives $T_{cr} \approx 325$ K. **Apply Semenov criterion:** $$ T_{a,cr} \;=\; T_{cr} \;-\; \frac{R T_{cr}^2}{E_a} $$ $$ T_{a,cr} \;=\; 325 \;-\; \frac{8.314 \times 325^2}{90{,}000} $$ $$ T_{a,cr} \;\approx\; 325 \;-\; 9.76 \;\approx\; 315.2 \;\text{K} $$ **Conclusion:** The operating ambient $T_a = 320$ K is **greater** than $T_{a,cr} = 315.2$ K, so the reactor is **NOT safe**. Runaway is expected. Immediate mitigation: increase cooling capacity, reduce inventory, or substitute chemistry. --- ### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for Module 3]** **(a)** Define the **Frank-Kamenetskii parameter $\delta$** and explain how the criticality condition $\delta = \delta_{cr}$ is used to determine the **safe radius of a stored reactive solid**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A reactive solid is stored in a cylindrical drum of radius $r_0$ and length $L \gg r_0$. Data: - $E_a = 100$ kJ/mol - $\Delta H_r = -180$ kJ/mol - $k_0 = 10^{10}$ /s - $\rho = 1500$ kg/m³ - $\lambda = 0.3$ W/(m·K) - $C_p = 1800$ J/(kg·K) - $T_a = 350$ K Determine the **maximum safe radius** of the drum. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) — Frank-Kamenetskii Framework (7 marks):** The Frank-Kamenetskii (FK) parameter $\delta$ is a dimensionless number that compares the rate of heat generation to the rate of heat conduction within a *non-stirred* mass: $$ \delta \;=\; \frac{Q \, E_a \, L^2}{\lambda \, R \, T_a^2} $$ where $Q = \Delta H_r \, k_0 \, C^n \, \exp(-E_a / R T_a)$ is the volumetric heat-release rate at the ambient temperature and $L$ is the characteristic dimension (half-thickness for slab, radius for cylinder/sphere). **Criticality condition:** If $\delta \leq \delta_{cr}$, heat is conducted away faster than it is generated, and the system reaches a sub-critical temperature profile. If $\delta > \delta_{cr}$, the centreline temperature diverges and runaway occurs. The **safe size** for a given geometry is obtained by setting $\delta = \delta_{cr}$ and solving for $L$: $$ L_{max} \;=\; \sqrt{ \frac{\delta_{cr} \, \lambda \, R \, T_a^2}{Q \, E_a} } $$ For an infinite cylinder, $\delta_{cr} = 2.000$. **Marks breakdown:** definition of $\delta$ (2 marks), explanation of $\delta_{cr}$ geometry dependence (2 marks), safe-radius derivation (2 marks), diagram or worked formula (1 mark). --- **(b) — Maximum Safe Radius (7 marks):** For an infinite cylinder, $\delta_{cr} = 2.000$. Compute $Q$ at $T_a = 350$ K. Approximating $C^n \approx 1$ (pure substance, decay dominated by Arrhenius), the effective reaction rate constant at ambient is: $$ k(T_a) \;=\; 10^{10} \exp\!\left(-\frac{100{,}000}{8.314 \times 350}\right) $$ $$ \frac{E_a}{R T_a} \;=\; \frac{100{,}000}{2909.9} \;\approx\; 34.37 $$ $$ k(T_a) \;\approx\; 10^{10} \times 1.27 \times 10^{-15} \;\approx\; 1.27 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{/s} $$ Volumetric heat release: $$ Q \;\approx\; \Delta H_r \, k(T_a) \, \rho / M $$ Treating the solid as the reactant itself with molar mass $M$ embedded in the rate constant (an alternative form, common in FK treatments): $$ Q \;\approx\; \vert \Delta H_r \vert \, k_0 \, \exp(-E_a/RT_a) \;\approx\; 1.8 \times 10^{5} \times 1.27 \times 10^{-5} \;\approx\; 2.29 \;\text{W/m}^3 $$ Now solve for $L = r_0$: $$ r_{0,max} \;=\; \sqrt{ \frac{\delta_{cr} \, \lambda \, R \, T_a^2}{Q \, E_a} } $$ $$ r_{0,max} \;=\; \sqrt{ \frac{2.000 \times 0.3 \times 8.314 \times 350^2}{2.29 \times 100{,}000} } $$ **Numerator:** $2.000 \times 0.3 \times 8.314 \times 122{,}500 \;\approx\; 6.11 \times 10^{5}$ **Denominator:** $2.29 \times 100{,}000 \;=\; 2.29 \times 10^{5}$ $$ \text{Ratio} \;\approx\; 2.67 $$ $$ r_{0,max} \;\approx\; \sqrt{2.67} \;\approx\; 1.63 \;\text{m} $$ **Conclusion:** The drum radius must not exceed approximately **1.6 m** at $T_a = 350$ K. If the actual radius is larger, the centreline will run away. **Marks:** Q calculation (2 marks), setup of FK equation (2 marks), arithmetic (2 marks), conclusion (1 mark). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the assumption.** Semenov and FK are *not* interchangeable. Always state the assumption (uniform T vs. conduction only) before writing the formula. **(Loses 1–2 marks)** > 2. **Mixing units.** $E_a$ must be in **J/mol** (not kJ/mol) when used in $E_a/RT$ where $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). **(Loses 2–3 marks)** > 3. **Confusing ΔTad with TMRad.** ΔTad is a *temperature*, TMRad is a *time*. Do not equate them. **(Loses 2 marks)** > 4. **Ignoring geometry in FK.** A slab, cylinder, and sphere have *different* $\delta_{cr}$. Picking the wrong one gives the wrong safe size. **(Loses 2–3 marks)** > 5. **Skipping the iteration step.** The Semenov critical temperature cannot be solved analytically in general; a tabular iteration is expected and rewarded. **(Loses 1 mark)** --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - **Master equation** of thermal effects: $\rho C_p \, dT/dt = q_G - q_R$. - **Runaway** = $q_G > q_R$ *and* $d q_G / dT > d q_R / dT$ (positive feedback). - **Semenov** assumes well-mixed, uniform T; gives $T_{a,cr} = T_{cr} - R T_{cr}^2 / E_a$. - **Frank-Kamenetskii** assumes no mixing, conduction only; uses $\delta$ parameter. - $\delta_{cr}$: slab **0.878**, cylinder **2.000**, sphere **3.322**. - **Thomas** interpolates via Biot number: $\beta = \delta \, \text{Bi} / (1 + \text{Bi})$. - **ΔTad** = $-\Delta H_r \, n / C_p^{tot}$; total temperature rise under perfect insulation. - **TMRad** = $(C_p R T^2)/(\Delta H_r k_0 E_a) \cdot \exp(E_a/RT)$ — operational "countdown." - **SADT** = lowest ambient for self-heating of ≥ 6 °C in 7 days; sets storage/transport rules. - **Quadrant III** of the runaway window (low TMR, high ΔTad) is the **HIGH-HAZARD** zone. - **Universal gas constant** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) — keep $E_a$ in J/mol when using it. - **TGA** measures mass loss vs. T (decomposition); **DSC** measures heat flow vs. T (exo/endotherm). - **TMR thresholds:** ≥ 24 h safe; 1–24 h caution; < 1 h unstable — emergency relief mandatory. - **DIERS methodology** uses TMRad + ΔTad for vent sizing. - **Engineering motto:** "If you cannot measure the heat, you cannot control the reaction." Always request calorimetric data for new chemistries.

topics.title: Explosion Effects - Software Application for Effect and Damage Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Explosion Effects - Software Application for Effect and Damage Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Explosion Effect Calculation** is the quantitative engineering methodology used to determine the physical consequences (blast overpressure, impulse, thermal radiation, fragment projection) of an uncontrolled energy release event, and to translate these physical effects into tangible damage metrics to people, structures, and equipment. In KTU 2024 Process Safety parlance, this discipline combines **thermodynamics of combustion**, **shock wave physics**, and **empirical damage correlations** to estimate Loss of Containment (LOC) consequences.

The two principal *physical* outputs of an explosion calculation are:
1. **Peak Side-on Overpressure ($\Delta P_s$)** — the maximum transient pressure above ambient experienced by a target.
2. **Positive Phase Impulse ($i_s$)** — the time-integrated pressure above ambient, $\int \Delta P(t)\,dt$, which governs momentum transfer and structural translation damage.

The standard industry damage metrics translate these into:
- **Structural Damage Criteria** (in % destruction or USD/m² rebuild cost)
- **Personnel Injury Criteria** (1st-degree burns, eardrum rupture, lung hemorrhage, lethality)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Must Memorize):** An **explosion** is a sudden, rapid, and violent release of energy accompanied by a shock wave and high-velocity expanding gases that produces overpressure, thermal radiation, and fragmentation effects on the surroundings.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "TNT Bomb" Mental Model
Imagine dropping a stone into a perfectly still pond. The impact creates a circular wave (overpressure) that travels outward, losing amplitude as it expands (geometric and thermodynamic attenuation). The water behind the wave is given a momentary push (impulse). A blast wave is essentially this — but in 3D air, moving at **supersonic** speeds (up to Mach 5 for the leading front) and carrying orders of magnitude more energy.

**The "Kitchen Stove" Analogy for TNT Equivalency:**
Think of a gas cylinder leak in your kitchen. The leaked propane has a stored chemical energy roughly equivalent to a certain mass of the explosive **TNT (Trinitrotoluene)**. Engineers calculate *"how many kilograms of TNT would release the same blast?"* and then use a century of military blast-test data (curves of overpressure vs. distance) to predict damage. This is the **TNT Equivalency Method** — the most widely taught, simplest KTU tool.

### 1.3 Classification of Explosions Relevant to KTU Syllabus

| Explosion Class | KTU Definition | Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Deflagration** | Subsonic flame propagation ($M < 1$) | Propane-air cloud in open field |
| **Detonation** | Supersonic flame with shock-coupled front ($M > 1$) | High-pressure gas pipe rupture |
| **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** | Non-confined flammable cloud ignition | Flixborough, UK 1974 disaster |
| **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE)** | Catastrophic vessel failure with superheated liquid | LPG railcar rupture |
| **Dust Explosion** | Combustion of suspended combustible particulates | Grain silo, coal handling |
| **Confined Explosion** | Combustion inside process equipment/structure | Reactor vessel, pipe rack |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked to "classify" an explosion, students frequently confuse **deflagration** with **detonation**. The discriminator is the **flame Mach number relative to the unburned gas**, NOT the absolute flame speed. A deflagration can still produce severe overpressure (up to ~8 bar in a VCE) without ever becoming a detonation.

### 1.4 Why Software? The Manual-Calculation Bottleneck
The blast equations (TNO, BST, TNT) require iterative graphical lookups, multi-variable interpolation, and atmospheric corrections. **Effect-modelling software packages** (ALOHA, PHAST, FRED, EFFECTS) automate these steps using:
- Built-in **atmospheric dispersion sub-models** (Pasquill-Gifford, Gaussian puff)
- **Consequence footprint generation** (threat zones: Red, Orange, Yellow)
- GIS mapping and **population density overlays**
- Direct **probit-equation coupling** for fatality probability

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The TNT Equivalency Method (TNT EM)

This is the simplest, most exam-frequent method. It converts any hydrocarbon mass into an equivalent **TNT mass** and then uses military blast curves to compute overpressure.

**Step-by-step Logic:**

1. **Determine fuel mass available for explosion** ($M_{fuel}$, kg) — typically 10% of the LFL cloud mass in an unconfined VCE assumption.
2. **Compute energy released** by combustion:
   $E = M_{fuel} \times \eta \times H_c$
   where $\eta$ = empirical yield factor (0.01 – 0.10 for unconfined VCE; often taken as **0.05** or 5%), $H_c$ = heat of combustion (J/kg).
3. **Convert to equivalent TNT mass**:
   $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{E}{E_{TNT}} = \dfrac{M_{fuel} \times \eta \times H_c}{4.52 \times 10^6}$
   where $E_{TNT} = 4.52$ **MJ/kg** is the heat of detonation of TNT.
4. **Compute scaled distance** (Hopkinson's cube-root scaling law):
   $Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$
5. **Read peak overpressure** $\Delta P_s$ from the **Braun / Mannan / Mills correlation chart** or use the polynomial fit.

### 2.2 The TNO Multi-Energy Method (TNO MEM)

The TNO method (developed by the Dutch Organisation for Applied Scientific Research) is the most theoretically rigorous KTU method because it explicitly accounts for **congestion and confinement**.

- Uses **10 blast strength categories** (level 1 = weak; level 10 = detonation-strength).
- An unconfined, uncongested open-air cloud is classified as **level 1** (often the assumed default for KTU problems).
- A heavily congested pipe rack is **level 6 – 8**.
- A fully confined, congested region with strong turbulence is **level 9 – 10**.

**Scaled Distance:**
$\bar{R} = \dfrac{R}{\left(\dfrac{E_0}{P_0}\right)^{1/3}}$

where $E_0$ = total released combustion energy (J), $P_0$ = ambient pressure (Pa).

**Scaled Peak Overpressure** $\Delta \bar{P}_s$ is read from the **van den Berg blast curve families** (one per energy level).

### 2.3 The Baker–Strehlow–Tang (BST) Method

The BST method correlates the **peak overpressure** with three parameters:
1. **Flame speed Mach number** ($M_f$)
2. **Congestion** (number of obstacles per unit volume, $A/V$)
3. **Fuel reactivity** (low / medium / high, e.g., methane vs. hydrogen)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "M-A-V":** BST = **M**ach × **A**rea/**V**olume × reactivity. The flame-speed Mach number and the $A/V$ congestion ratio jointly determine which **flame-expansion-strength family** of blast curves to use.

### 2.4 Damage Criteria — Overpressure Thresholds for Structures

| Peak Overpressure $\Delta P_s$ | Damage Effect (Industry Standard — CCPS / TNO) |
|---|---|
| **0.5 kPa (0.07 psi)** | Loud bang, no structural damage |
| **2.0 kPa (0.29 psi)** | Window glass breakage (typical shatter threshold) |
| **10 kPa (1.45 psi)** | Minor structural damage, 10% window breakage |
| **30 kPa (4.35 psi)** | Steel-framed buildings distorted, 50% window breakage |
| **70 kPa (10.15 psi)** | Heavy damage to process equipment, panel walls blown in |
| **100 kPa (14.5 psi)** | Reinforced concrete severely damaged |
| **200 kPa (29 psi)** | Most buildings collapse |
| **700 kPa (101.5 psi)** | Total destruction of equipment / cratering |

### 2.5 Probit Equations for Personnel Injury (KTU High-Yield)

The **Probit (Probability unit) model** converts a physical effect (overpressure, thermal dose) into a **% fatality probability**.

**General form:**
$Y = a + b \ln(V)$
$P = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$
where $V$ = effective dose (e.g., peak overpressure in Pa), $a,b$ = empirical constants.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TNT Equivalency | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{M \cdot \eta \cdot H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ | kg TNT | Mass-energy conversion |
| 2 | Hopkinson Scaled Distance | $Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | m/kg$^{1/3}$ | Non-dimensional distance |
| 3 | Scaled Overpressure (TNT) | $\Delta \bar{P}_s = f(Z)$ | dimensionless | Use Braun/Mannan chart |
| 4 | TNO Scaled Distance | $\bar{R} = R \left(\dfrac{P_0}{E_0}\right)^{1/3}$ | dimensionless | Multi-energy blast |
| 5 | Positive Impulse (scaled) | $\bar{i}_s = f(\bar{R})$ | Pa·s/m | TNO curves |
| 6 | Lung-Hemorrhage Probit (Eisenberg) | $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(\Delta P_s)$ | — | Lethality from overpressure |
| 7 | Eardrum-Rupture Probit | $Y = -15.6 + 1.93 \ln(\Delta P_s)$ | — | 50% rupture threshold |
| 8 | Whole-Body Translation Probit | $Y = 5.13 - 5.41 \ln(V)$ | — | Velocity-based fatality |
| 9 | TNT Energy Density | $E_{TNT} = 4.52 \times 10^6$ | J/kg | Conversion constant |
| 10 | Sachs Scaled Pressure | $\bar{P} = \dfrac{\Delta P_s}{P_0}$ | dimensionless | Shock-wave analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Almost every 14-mark numerical in Module 3 expects the student to (i) compute $W_{TNT}$, (ii) compute $Z$, (iii) read $\Delta P_s$ from the chart, and (iv) apply a probit to obtain fatality %.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA):** Explosion effect calculations are the *consequence* leg of the risk equation $R = f \times C$. A LOPA / QRA study for an LNG terminal or a refinery FCCU is incomplete without these calculations.
- **Land-Use Planning (LUP):** Indian Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation (PESO) / Offshore Safety Directorate (OSD) regulations use effect distances to define safety buffer zones around bulk hydrocarbon storage.
- **Emergency Response Planning (ERP):** Yellow/Red threat zones from ALOHA/EFFECTS software are used to set evacuation radii.
- **Insurance & Reinsurance:** Major industrial insurers (e.g., Lloyds of London syndicates) require quantified blast footprints for premium calculation of refineries and petrochemical plants.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: TNT-Equivalent Overpressure at a Given Distance

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
*10,000 kg of propane accidentally releases from a bullet tank and forms a vapor cloud. The cloud is ignited as an unconfined VCE. Assuming an explosion yield factor of $\eta = 5\%$ and complete combustion, calculate the peak side-on overpressure at a distance of **500 m** downwind.*

**Given Data:**
- $M_{propane} = 10{,}000$ kg
- $H_c$ of propane = $46 \times 10^6$ J/kg
- $\eta = 0.05$ (5% yield factor — unconfined VCE assumption)
- $E_{TNT} = 4.52 \times 10^6$ J/kg
- $R = 500$ m
- $P_0 = 101{,}325$ Pa (standard atmosphere)

**Step 1 — Total Combustion Energy Released:**

$$E = M_{fuel} \times \eta \times H_c$$

$$E = 10{,}000 \times 0.05 \times 46 \times 10^6$$

$$E = 2.3 \times 10^{10} \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to TNT-Equivalent Mass:**

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{E}{E_{TNT}} = \frac{2.3 \times 10^{10}}{4.52 \times 10^6}$$

$$W_{TNT} = 5088.5 \text{ kg} \approx 5.09 \text{ tonnes TNT}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Hopkinson Scaled Distance:**

$$Z = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \frac{500}{(5088.5)^{1/3}}$$

$$W_{TNT}^{1/3} = 17.18$$

$$Z = \frac{500}{17.18} = 29.10 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Step 4 — Use the Mills/Mannan Polynomial Fit (valid for $Z$ in m/kg$^{1/3}$):**

$$\Delta P_s = \frac{101.325 \times 808 \left[1 + \left(\frac{Z}{4.5}\right)^2 \right]}{\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{Z}{0.048}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{Z}{0.32}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{Z}{1.35}\right)^2}\right]}$$

Substituting $Z = 29.10$:

$$\left(\frac{Z}{4.5}\right)^2 = 41.82, \quad \left(\frac{Z}{0.048}\right)^2 = 367{,}378, \quad \left(\frac{Z}{0.32}\right)^2 = 8266, \quad \left(\frac{Z}{1.35}\right)^2 = 464.5$$

$$\Delta P_s = \frac{101.325 \times 808 \times 42.82}{\sqrt{367{,}380} \cdot \sqrt{8267} \cdot \sqrt{465.5}}$$

$$\Delta P_s = \frac{3{,}504{,}000}{606.1 \times 90.93 \times 21.57}$$

$$\Delta P_s = \frac{3{,}504{,}000}{1{,}189{,}000} = 2.946 \text{ kPa} \approx 3.0 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Damage Interpretation:**
From the damage table, **3.0 kPa (≈0.43 psi)** lies in the **"minor structural damage, some window breakage"** zone. At this pressure, light injuries (eardrum rupture possible) but no mass-casualty building damage.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Probit Calculation for Fatality Probability

**Problem Statement (Continuation):**
*Using the Eisenberg lung-hemorrhage probit, estimate the fatality percentage for an exposed person at 500 m.*

**Step 1 — Convert overpressure to Pa:**

$$\Delta P_s = 2.946 \text{ kPa} = 2946 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Probit Equation:**

$$Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(\Delta P_s)$$

$$Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \times \ln(2946)$$

$$\ln(2946) = 7.989$$

$$Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \times 7.989 = -77.1 + 55.20 = -21.90$$

**Step 3 — Convert Probit to Percentage:**

$$P = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

$$\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{-21.90 - 5}{1.4142} = \frac{-26.90}{1.4142} = -19.02$$

$$\text{erf}(-19.02) \approx -1.0$$

$$P = 50 \left[1 + (-1)\right] = 0\%$$

**Interpretation:** A probit of $-21.9$ is *vastly* below the 50% lethality threshold (probit = 5). Fatality from lung hemorrhage at this distance is **statistically negligible**. However, **secondary effects** (flying glass, building collapse, translation) must be checked using the eardrum and translation probits.

**Alternative Check — Eardrum Rupture Probit:**

$$Y_{ear} = -15.6 + 1.93 \ln(\Delta P_s) = -15.6 + 1.93 \times 7.989 = -15.6 + 15.42 = -0.18$$

$$P_{ear} = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{-0.18 - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}(-3.665)\right]$$

$$\text{erf}(-3.665) \approx -1.0 \Rightarrow P_{ear} \approx 0\%$$

At 500 m, eardrum rupture is also negligible. **Conclusion:** Beyond 500 m the propane VCE is **not life-threatening** in the open.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Methods

| Parameter | TNT Equivalency | TNO Multi-Energy | Baker-Strehlow-Tang |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Military blast data | Dutch TNO laboratory | Baker, Strehlow, Tang (1980s) |
| **Inputs** | Fuel mass, $\eta$, $H_c$ | Energy, confinement/congestion, blast level | Flame Mach, $A/V$ ratio, fuel reactivity |
| **Output** | $\Delta P_s$, impulse | $\Delta \bar{P}_s$, blast duration | $\Delta P_s$ (family-specific) |
| **Assumes** | Point-source, hemispherical surface burst | Hemispherical/turbulent partial confinement | Hemispherical, varying flame speed |
| **Best for** | Open-air VCEs, conservative KTU estimates | Congested process plants, pipe racks | Detailed CFD-validated studies |
| **Limitations** | Ignores geometry/congestion | Requires expert judgement on level 1-10 | Limited fuel reactivity categories |
| **KTU Weightage** | ★★★★★ (most numerical) | ★★★★ (theory heavy) | ★★★ (conceptual) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Explosion-Effect Software Tool (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a typical consequence-modelling package (such as **ALOHA, PHAST, EFFECTS, or FRED**) is architected internally:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Inputs: Fuel type, Mass, Storage conditions, Wind, T, P, RH] --> B[Source Model: Discharge rate, Pool evaporation, Jet]
    B --> C[Dispersion Model: Gaussian Plume / Puff]
    C --> D[Cloud Mass within LFL-UFL]
    D --> E{Explosion Type?}
    E -- Unconfined VCE --> F[TNT Equivalency Sub-Model]
    E -- Congested VCE --> G[TNO Multi-Energy Sub-Model]
    E -- Confined --> H[Baker-Strehlow-Tang Sub-Model]
    F --> I[Blast Curve Lookup: Overpressure vs Distance]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Effect Calculator: Peak OP, Impulse, Duration]
    J --> K[Damage Module: Probit Y -> Fatality %]
    J --> L[Damage Module: Structure destruction level]
    K --> M[Output: Red / Orange / Yellow threat zones on GIS map]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Decision Support: Evacuation radius, siting distance, PPE]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Inputs flow top-down from the user interface (A) through physical sub-models (B–H), are unified by a common blast-curve lookup engine (I), and emerge as human-decision artifacts (M, N). The "Explosion Type?" decision node is the **engineer's cognitive decision** — software packages do not auto-select the method; the user must.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: TNO Multi-Energy Level Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Flammable cloud ignited] --> Q1{Fully confined?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> L9[Energy Level 9-10: Detonation-like]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Heavy congestion, A/V high?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Pipe rack / process module?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> L7[Level 7-8: Strong VCE]
    Q3 -- No --> L5[Level 5-6: Moderate congestion]
    Q2 -- No --> L1[Energy Level 1-3: Open-air, weak deflagration]
    L9 --> RESULT[Use Level 9 blast curve]
    L7 --> RESULT
    L5 --> RESULT
    L1 --> RESULT
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Damage-Effect Decision Tree from Overpressure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Peak Side-on Overpressure Pa] --> C1{Delta P_s less than 7000 Pa?}
    C1 -- Yes --> C2{Delta P_s less than 2000 Pa?}
    C2 -- Yes --> OUT1[Window glass breakage only]
    C2 -- No --> OUT2[Minor structural damage]
    C1 -- No --> C3{Delta P_s less than 35000 Pa?}
    C3 -- Yes --> OUT3[50% window breakage, light injuries]
    C3 -- No --> C4{Delta P_s less than 100000 Pa?}
    C4 -- Yes --> OUT4[Heavy structural damage, casualties]
    C4 -- No --> OUT5[Total destruction, mass casualties]
```

### 4.4 Process-Stage Reference: Where Explosion Calculations Sit in a QRA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hazard Identification: HAZOP / FMEA] --> B[Scenario Selection]
    B --> C[Frequency Analysis: Event Tree]
    C --> D[Consequence Analysis: Explosion Effect]
    D --> E[Risk = Frequency x Consequence]
    E --> F[Risk Matrix / f-N Curve]
    F --> G[Risk Treatment: ALARP Demonstration]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define "Peak Side-on Overpressure" and "Positive Phase Impulse" as applied to blast-wave damage assessment. State the units of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Peak side-on overpressure ($\Delta P_s$):** The maximum transient pressure, above local ambient, experienced by a surface oriented parallel to the direction of wave propagation. Measured in **Pascal (Pa)** or **psi**. (1.5 marks)
- **Positive phase impulse ($i_s$):** The time integral of overpressure over the positive (compression) phase of the blast wave, $i_s = \int_0^{t_+} \Delta P_s(t) \, dt$. Measured in **Pa·s** or **psi·ms**. (1.5 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List the three principal methods used in industry for explosion effect estimation. State one limitation of the TNT equivalency method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three methods are:
1. **TNT Equivalency Method** (1 mark)
2. **TNO Multi-Energy Method** (1 mark)
3. **Baker-Strehlow-Tang (BST) Method** (1 mark — for the third, even saying "BST" suffices)

> Limitation to add if asked: "TNT method ignores the actual geometry, congestion, and confinement of the cloud, often leading to over-conservative overpressure estimates for partially confined VCEs." (Bonus mark)

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (Answer ANY ONE)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full Numerical on TNT Equivalency + Probit

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

*An unconfined vapor cloud of methane ($\text{CH}_4$) of mass 5,000 kg is accidentally released and ignited in an open process area. The yield factor is $\eta = 0.04$ and the heat of combustion is $55 \times 10^6$ J/kg.*

**(a)** Calculate the **equivalent TNT mass** of the explosion and the **Hopkinson scaled distance** at a receptor located **400 m** from the cloud center. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Mannan polynomial correlation given below, compute the **peak side-on overpressure** at the receptor. Then, using the **Eisenberg lung-hemorrhage probit**, estimate the **fatality probability** of a person at the receptor. **(7 marks)**

$$\Delta P_s = \frac{101.325 \times 808 \left[1 + (Z/4.5)^2\right]}{\sqrt{1 + (Z/0.048)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (Z/0.32)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (Z/1.35)^2}} \text{ kPa}$$

$$Y_{lung} = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(\Delta P_s) \text{ with } \Delta P_s \text{ in Pa}$$

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
- Energy released: $E = 5000 \times 0.04 \times 55 \times 10^6 = 1.1 \times 10^{10}$ J (2 marks)
- $W_{TNT} = 1.1 \times 10^{10} / 4.52 \times 10^6 = 2433.6$ kg TNT (3 marks)
- $Z = 400 / (2433.6)^{1/3} = 400 / 13.43 = 29.78$ m/kg$^{1/3}$ (2 marks)

**Part (b):**
- Substituting $Z = 29.78$ into the polynomial: $(Z/4.5)^2 = 43.79$, $(Z/0.048)^2 = 384{,}617$, $(Z/0.32)^2 = 8657$, $(Z/1.35)^2 = 486.3$ (2 marks)
- $\Delta P_s = (101.325 \times 808 \times 44.79) / (\sqrt{384618} \times \sqrt{8658} \times \sqrt{487.3}) = 3.665 \times 10^6 / (620.2 \times 93.05 \times 22.07) = 3.665 \times 10^6 / 1.273 \times 10^6 = 2.88$ kPa (3 marks)
- $\Delta P_s$ in Pa = 2880 Pa; $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \times \ln(2880) = -77.1 + 6.91 \times 7.965 = -77.1 + 55.04 = -22.06$ (1 mark)
- $P = 50[1 + \text{erf}((-22.06-5)/\sqrt{2})] = 50[1 + \text{erf}(-19.14)] \approx 0\%$ (1 mark)

> **Interpretation:** At 400 m, the peak overpressure is ~2.9 kPa (window damage range). Lung-hemorrhage fatality is essentially zero, but eardrum rupture and glass-fragment injury need separate probit checks. (Optional half-mark for good interpretation)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — TNO Multi-Energy + Software Knowledge

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **TNO Multi-Energy Method** in detail. With the aid of a sketch in your answer booklet, describe how the **energy level (1 to 10)** is selected for a given explosion scenario. Give **two example scenarios**, one corresponding to energy level 1–2 and one to level 7–8. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **TNO Multi-Energy** and **Baker-Strehlow-Tang (BST)** methods in terms of (i) input parameters, (ii) handling of confinement, and (iii) typical industrial applicability. List **any two software packages** used for explosion effect modelling and state **one strength** of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
- TNO method assumes that the explosion strength depends primarily on the **partial confinement and congestion** of the flammable cloud. The energy is released from each "explosive" sub-volume (acoustic source) and produces a hemispherical blast. (2 marks)
- Ten blast strength categories are used; the user picks a level based on visible geometry. Open-air VCEs default to level 1–2; congested pipe racks default to level 7–8. (2 marks)
- **Sketch description** (must include a labelled diagram showing 3 cloud regions, e.g., a fully open region + a partially congested region + a fully congested region, each assigned a different level). (1 mark)
- Example L1–L2: Methane leak in an open agricultural field ignited in summer. Example L7–L8: Ethylene release inside an ethylene plant's pipe-rack module with multiple obstacles. (2 marks)

**Part (b):**

| Aspect | TNO MEM | BST Method |
|---|---|---|
| Input parameters | Total energy, blast level (1–10) | Flame Mach, $A/V$, reactivity |
| Confinement handling | Implicit via blast-level selection | Implicit via flame-Mach + congestion |
| Industrial use | Open/congested VCE screening | Refined VCE & gas-detonation analysis |

(3 marks for the table, 1 mark per correct point on software)

- **ALOHA (EPA):** Strength — open-source, free, integrates directly with Google Earth for plume/blast footprint visualization. (1.5 marks)
- **PHAST (DNV):** Strength — comprehensive (release → dispersion → fire → explosion) with full probit-coupling; industry standard for offshore QRA. (1.5 marks)

(Acceptable alternatives: EFFECTS, FRED, EXOPLUS, TRACE, CANARY.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take cube root correctly** in the Hopkinson scaled-distance step. Marks lost in seconds.
> 2. **Mixing units** — using $\Delta P_s$ in **kPa** in a probit that demands **Pa** (or vice versa). The 10³ error wipes out the entire probit calculation.
> 3. **Confusing side-on overpressure with reflected overpressure.** Reflected $\approx 2 \Delta P_s + ...$ for normal incidence on a rigid wall — a frequent trap.
> 4. **Choosing an arbitrary yield factor** in the TNT method. The KTU answer key typically expects $\eta = 0.05$ (5%) for unconfined VCEs unless stated otherwise.
> 5. **Skipping the cube root in $(W_{TNT})^{1/3}$** — a classic 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Probit mis-spelling** — examiners do not penalize this, but a wrong numerical value (e.g., using $-77.1$ for eardrum instead of $-15.6$) is fatal.

---

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition of Explosion:** Sudden, rapid, violent energy release producing blast wave + thermal + fragment effects.
- **TNT Equivalency:** $W_{TNT} = (M \cdot \eta \cdot H_c) / 4.52 \times 10^6$ — simple, conservative, KTU default.
- **Hopkinson Scaled Distance:** $Z = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ — the **single most important equation** in Module 3.
- **TNO Multi-Energy:** 10 levels; level = function of confinement and congestion.
- **BST Method:** uses flame Mach + $A/V$ ratio + fuel reactivity.
- **Software:** ALOHA, PHAST, FRED, EFFECTS, EXOPLUS, TRACE.
- **Damage Thresholds (memorize 4–5):** 0.5 kPa (window), 10 kPa (minor), 35 kPa (heavy), 100 kPa (total), 200 kPa (collapse).
- **Probits (memorize 2):** Lung $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(\Delta P_s)$ and Eardrum $Y = -15.6 + 1.93 \ln(\Delta P_s)$ — both with $\Delta P_s$ in **Pa**.
- **Software output** = Red / Orange / Yellow threat zones, plotted on GIS.
- **Real-world anchor:** Flixborough 1974, Buncefield 2005, Jaipur 2009 — all VCE incidents where these calculations are now standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Mnemonic — "PEST-SET":** For any explosion problem, follow **P**roblem statement → **E**nergy of combustion → **S**caled distance → **T**NT equivalent (or TNO level) → **S**caled overpressure from chart → **E**valuate probit → **T**hreat zone. This sequence wins 12+ of the 14 marks in any KTU numerical.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vulnerability Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vulnerability Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Vulnerability** is formally defined by the **United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR)** as:

> *"The conditions determined by physical, social, economic and environmental factors or processes which increase the susceptibility of an individual, a community, assets, or systems to the impacts of hazards."*

In the KTU 2024 framework for **PBSFT404**, Vulnerability Analysis is the **systematic, scientific process of identifying, quantifying, and characterizing the susceptibility of exposed elements (people, property, infrastructure, ecosystems, or systems) to a given hazard**. It is the **third pillar** of the disaster risk equation and forms a critical input to **Risk Assessment** alongside Hazard Identification and Exposure Analysis.

The classical **UNDRR Risk Equation** that frames this entire module is:

$$R = H \times V \times E$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk** (expected loss per unit time)
- $H$ = **Hazard** (probability of a threatening event)
- $V$ = **Vulnerability** (degree of damage susceptibility, $0 \le V \le 1$)
- $E$ = **Exposure** (value of elements in the hazard zone)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Vulnerability is **NOT** a property of the hazard alone. It is a **property of the element-at-risk interacting with the hazard**. A building is not vulnerable in isolation — it is *vulnerable to a given hazard of a given intensity*. This distinction is a high-frequency exam point.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of vulnerability like a person's **resistance to a common cold (the hazard)**:
- A person with a strong immune system, good nutrition, proper clothing, and a clean home is **less vulnerable** — they have high **coping capacity** and **adaptive capacity**.
- A malnourished child in a cold, damp house is **highly vulnerable** — even a mild cold (low hazard) can be fatal.

In the same way, an **earthquake-resistant skyscraper** (low vulnerability) and a **weak unreinforced masonry hut** (high vulnerability) sit in the *same seismic zone* (same hazard, same exposure), but the outcomes of an earthquake will be drastically different because of their **differential vulnerability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Vulnerability answers the question — *"If the hazard strikes, HOW BAD will it hurt this particular thing?"* It is the **"weakness dial"** of the element-at-risk.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Standard Metric | Symbol | Range / Unit | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Damage Factor** | $D_f$ | $0$ to $1$ (dimensionless) | Structural Engineering |
| **Mean Damage Ratio** | $MDR$ | $0$ to $1$ (dimensionless) | Catastrophe Modeling |
| **Annual Loss Expectancy** | $ALE$ | Currency/Year (e.g., ₹/yr) | Risk Engineering |
| **Vulnerability Index** | $V_i$ | $0$ to $1$ (dimensionless) | UNDRR / INFORM |
| **Probability of Damage State** | $P_{ds}$ | $0$ to $1$ (dimensionless) | HAZUS-MH |

## 1.4 The Three Core Components of Vulnerability

1. **Sensitivity** — the degree to which a system is affected by a hazard stimulus.
2. **Adaptive Capacity** — the ability of a system to adjust to, modify, or change characteristics to cope with hazards.
3. **Coping Capacity** — the ability of a system to use available resources and abilities to face adverse consequences.

$$V = f(S, AC, CC)$$

Where $S$ = Sensitivity, $AC$ = Adaptive Capacity, $CC$ = Coping Capacity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Vulnerability

Vulnerability is multi-dimensional. For a KTU 14-mark question, you must classify vulnerability along **four standard axes**:

### 2.1.1 Physical / Structural Vulnerability
- Applies to buildings, infrastructure, lifeline systems.
- Driven by: construction material, age, design code adherence, maintenance state, building typology.
