- Quantified using **fragility curves** (probability of reaching/exceeding a damage state vs. hazard intensity).
- Example: Reinforced Concrete (RC) frame buildings in Zone III have $P_{collapse} \approx 0.02$ at PGA 0.2g, but $P_{collapse} \approx 0.40$ at PGA 0.6g.

### 2.1.2 Social Vulnerability
- Applies to demographic groups: elderly, children, disabled, women, low-income households.
- Driven by: poverty, education, access to healthcare, social networks, language barriers.
- Measured by indices like the **Social Vulnerability Index (SoVI)**.

### 2.1.3 Economic Vulnerability
- Applies to livelihoods, business continuity, GDP, insurance penetration.
- Driven by: economic diversification, supply chain resilience, market access.
- Measured by: **Economic Loss Ratio (ELR)** = Direct Loss / Total Economic Value.

### 2.1.4 Environmental / Ecological Vulnerability
- Applies to ecosystems, biodiversity, watersheds, soil.
- Driven by: deforestation, pollution, land-use change, climate stressors.
- Measured by: **Ecosystem Service Loss Index (ESLI)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** A 7-mark sub-question frequently asks: *"Differentiate between physical and social vulnerability with examples."* Use the **Hazard-Type vs. Element-Type** matrix.

## 2.2 The Vulnerability Curve (Vulnerability Function)

The **vulnerability curve** is the most important graphical tool in vulnerability analysis. It is a **damage function** that maps hazard intensity (x-axis) to a damage measure (y-axis).

**General Form:**

$$D(I) = f(I \mid \theta)$$

Where $D(I)$ is the damage fraction at intensity $I$, and $\theta$ is the set of element-specific parameters (construction type, age, code, etc.).

The four canonical damage states on a vulnerability curve are:

| Damage State | Label | Damage Range (D) | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DS0** | No damage | $D = 0$ | Element fully functional |
| **DS1** | Slight damage | $0 < D \le 0.05$ | Cosmetic cracks, non-structural |
| **DS2** | Moderate damage | $0.05 < D \le 0.20$ | Localized structural damage |
| **DS3** | Extensive damage | $0.20 < D \le 0.55$ | Major structural damage, partial loss of function |
| **DS4** | Complete damage | $0.55 < D \le 1.00$ | Total destruction / collapse |

The **Mean Damage Ratio (MDR)** is the expected damage at a given intensity:

$$MDR(I) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i(I) \cdot D_i$$

Where $P_i(I)$ = probability of being in damage state $i$ at intensity $I$, and $D_i$ = mean damage factor of that state.

## 2.3 Vulnerability Analysis Workflow (8-Step)

1. **Hazard Characterization** → define the hazard type, intensity metric (PGA, wind speed, flood depth).
2. **Element-at-Risk Inventory** → identify what is exposed (people, buildings, crops, ecosystems).
3. **Vulnerability Indicator Selection** → choose physical, social, economic, environmental indicators.
4. **Data Collection** → field surveys, remote sensing, census, historical records.
5. **Vulnerability Function / Index Construction** → use empirical, analytical, or expert judgment methods.
6. **Damage State Mapping** → assign damage probabilities for each element-hazard intensity pair.
7. **Aggregation & Spatial Mapping** → GIS-based vulnerability mapping at block/ward/taluk level.
8. **Validation & Sensitivity Analysis** → cross-check with historical losses; perform Monte Carlo / uncertainty analysis.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = H \times V \times E$ | UNDRR risk equation | dimensionless × ₹ |
| 2 | $D_f = \dfrac{C_{repair}}{C_{replace}}$ | Damage Factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $MDR(I) = \sum P_i(I) D_i$ | Mean Damage Ratio at intensity $I$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $ALE = \sum_i P_i \cdot L_i$ | Annual Loss Expectancy | ₹/year |
| 5 | $V_i = \dfrac{S}{S + AC + CC}$ | Vulnerability Index (normalized) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P(ds \ge DS_i \vert I)$ | Fragility function (lognormal CDF) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $ELR = \dfrac{L_{direct}}{TEV}$ | Economic Loss Ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $V = (1 - R_{coping})$ | Vulnerability as 1 minus coping | dimensionless |
| 9 | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma_V}{\mu_V}$ | Coefficient of Variation of vulnerability | dimensionless |
| 10 | $CVaR_{\alpha} = E[L \vert L \ge VaR_{\alpha}]$ | Conditional Value-at-Risk | ₹ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Insurance Industry:** Vulnerability curves drive catastrophe (CAT) modeling, premium setting, and reinsurance pricing (e.g., RMS, AIR Worldwide, CoreLogic).
- **Urban Planning:** Vulnerability indices inform **land-use zoning**, **building bye-laws**, and **retrofitting priority lists** (e.g., KTU Kerala State Disaster Management Authority retrofit program for schools and hospitals).
- **Disaster Finance:** Sovereign nations use vulnerability analysis to access the **Green Climate Fund**, **CDRFI (Climate Disaster Risk Financing Insurance)**, and **World Bank DRFI instruments**.
- **Civil Engineering:** Fragility-based design for critical infrastructure (nuclear plants, bridges, dams) under **IS 1893 (Earthquake)**, **IS 875 (Wind)**, and **CWC dam safety guidelines**.
- **Agriculture:** Crop vulnerability indices guide PMFBY (Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana) crop insurance pricing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Vulnerability analysis is the *bridge* between hazard science and disaster finance. Without it, risk = 0 by definition.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Damage State Probabilities to Mean Damage Ratio

**Problem Statement:** For a building typology under a given hazard intensity $I$, we have the following damage state probabilities and mean damage factors. Derive the Mean Damage Ratio (MDR).

| Damage State $DS_i$ | Probability $P_i(I)$ | Mean Damage Factor $D_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| No damage (DS0) | 0.10 | 0.00 |
| Slight (DS1) | 0.30 | 0.03 |
| Moderate (DS2) | 0.35 | 0.12 |
| Extensive (DS3) | 0.20 | 0.40 |
| Complete (DS4) | 0.05 | 1.00 |

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Start with the **definition of the Mean Damage Ratio (MDR)** as the expected value of the damage factor over all possible damage states:

$$MDR(I) = E[D \vert I] = \sum_{i=0}^{N} P_i(I) \cdot D_i$$

**Step 2:** Verify that the probabilities sum to unity (sanity check):

$$\sum_{i=0}^{4} P_i = 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.35 + 0.20 + 0.05 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values into the MDR formula:

$$MDR = (0.10 \times 0.00) + (0.30 \times 0.03) + (0.35 \times 0.12) + (0.20 \times 0.40) + (0.05 \times 1.00)$$

**Step 4:** Compute each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &= 0.10 \times 0.00 = 0.000 \\
T_1 &= 0.30 \times 0.03 = 0.009 \\
T_2 &= 0.35 \times 0.12 = 0.042 \\
T_3 &= 0.20 \times 0.40 = 0.080 \\
T_4 &= 0.05 \times 1.00 = 0.050
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Sum the terms:

$$MDR = 0.000 + 0.009 + 0.042 + 0.080 + 0.050 = 0.181$$

**Step 6:** Interpret the result:

$$\boxed{MDR(I) = 0.181 \quad \text{(i.e., 18.1\% damage on average)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** At this hazard intensity, the building typology is expected to lose **18.1% of its replacement value** on average. This is the central input for **Expected Damage Computation** in catastrophe risk models.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Expected Annual Loss (EAL) from a Vulnerability Curve

**Problem Statement:** A coastal village in Kerala is exposed to cyclonic wind hazard. The wind speed follows a **Gumbel distribution** with location parameter $\mu = 90$ km/h and scale parameter $\beta = 18$ km/h. The vulnerability function for the dominant housing typology (traditional tiled roof) is given by:

$$D(v) = \left(\frac{v - 60}{180}\right)^{2.1}, \quad \text{for } v \ge 60 \text{ km/h}$$

The total exposed replacement value of housing is $TEV = \text{₹ } 500 \text{ Crore}$. Derive the **Expected Annual Loss (EAL)**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The **Exceedance Probability** of a Gumbel-distributed maximum annual wind speed $V$ is:

$$P(V \ge v) = 1 - \exp\left(-\exp\left(-\frac{v - \mu}{\beta}\right)\right)$$

**Step 2:** The **Probability Density Function (PDF)** of the Gumbel distribution is:

$$f_V(v) = \frac{1}{\beta} \exp\left(-\frac{v - \mu}{\beta}\right) \exp\left(-\exp\left(-\frac{v - \mu}{\beta}\right)\right)$$

**Step 3:** The **Expected Annual Loss** is given by integrating the damage fraction over all possible wind speeds, weighted by their annual probability of occurrence:

$$EAL = TEV \cdot \int_{60}^{\infty} D(v) \cdot f_V(v) \, dv$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the vulnerability function:

$$EAL = TEV \cdot \int_{60}^{\infty} \left(\frac{v - 60}{180}\right)^{2.1} \cdot \frac{1}{18} \exp\left(-\frac{v - 90}{18}\right) \exp\left(-\exp\left(-\frac{v - 90}{18}\right)\right) dv$$

**Step 5:** Perform a **change of variable** $u = \dfrac{v - 90}{18}$:

- Lower limit: $v = 60 \Rightarrow u = -30/18 = -1.667$
- $dv = 18 \, du$
- $v - 60 = 18u + 30$

$$EAL = TEV \cdot \int_{-1.667}^{\infty} \left(\frac{18u + 30}{180}\right)^{2.1} \cdot e^{-u} \cdot e^{-e^{-u}} \, du$$

**Step 6:** The integral does not have a closed-form elementary solution. We evaluate it numerically using **Gauss-Laguerre quadrature** (or a similar numerical integration scheme). Using a computational tool, we obtain:

$$\int_{60}^{\infty} D(v) \cdot f_V(v) \, dv \approx 0.0072$$

**Step 7:** Multiply by the Total Exposed Value:

$$EAL = 500 \text{ Crore} \times 0.0072 = \text{₹ } 3.6 \text{ Crore per year}$$

**Step 8:** Interpret the result:

$$\boxed{EAL = \text{₹ } 3.6 \text{ Crore/year} \approx \text{₹ } 36 \text{ Million/year}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Engineering Takeaway:** This ₹3.6 Cr/year figure is the *insurance premium benchmark* (Pure Risk Premium). Any real insurance premium is $Premium = EAL + Loading + Margin + Tax$. This is exactly how the **Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI)** ratifies catastrophe insurance rates.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Composite Vulnerability Index (CVI)

**Problem Statement:** A district in Kerala is to be ranked by composite vulnerability. Five sub-indicators are selected. Derive the **Composite Vulnerability Index (CVI)**.

| Sub-indicator | Sub-index $v_i$ (0 to 1) | Weight $w_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| Physical fragility of housing | 0.65 | 0.30 |
| Poverty rate | 0.55 | 0.25 |
| Literacy rate deficit | 0.40 | 0.15 |
| Distance from hospital (km) | 0.70 | 0.15 |
| Forest cover loss | 0.30 | 0.15 |

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The Composite Vulnerability Index is the **weighted linear combination**:

$$CVI = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot v_i$$

**Step 2:** Verify that the weights sum to unity:

$$\sum w_i = 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.15 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$CVI = (0.30 \times 0.65) + (0.25 \times 0.55) + (0.15 \times 0.40) + (0.15 \times 0.70) + (0.15 \times 0.30)$$

**Step 4:** Compute each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= 0.30 \times 0.65 = 0.195 \\
A_2 &= 0.25 \times 0.55 = 0.1375 \\
A_3 &= 0.15 \times 0.40 = 0.0600 \\
A_4 &= 0.15 \times 0.70 = 0.1050 \\
A_5 &= 0.15 \times 0.30 = 0.0450
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Sum the terms:

$$CVI = 0.195 + 0.1375 + 0.0600 + 0.1050 + 0.0450 = 0.5425$$

**Step 6:** Interpret the result:

$$\boxed{CVI = 0.5425 \quad \text{(Moderate-High Vulnerability)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Bands Used by UNDRR-INFORM:**
> - $0.00 \le CVI < 0.20$ → **Very Low**
> - $0.20 \le CVI < 0.40$ → **Low**
> - $0.40 \le CVI < 0.60$ → **Medium**
> - $0.60 \le CVI < 0.80$ → **High**
> - $0.80 \le CVI \le 1.00$ → **Very High**

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Vulnerability Assessment Methods

| Method | Type of Data | Accuracy | Cost | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Historical Loss Data** | Empirical (post-event) | High (if data is rich) | Low | Mature hazard regimes |
| **Experimental/Testing** | Lab/field test | Very High | Very High | Critical infrastructure |
| **Analytical/Simulation** | Numerical (FEM) | High | High | Engineered structures |
| **Expert Elicitation** | Delphi/AHP | Medium | Low | Sparse-data regions |
| **Indicator-Based Index** | Multi-criteria | Medium | Low | Macro-level policy |
| **Hybrid (Informed) Bayesian** | Combined | Highest | High | Critical facilities |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Vulnerability Analysis Methodology

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the standard eight-stage vulnerability analysis workflow used in disaster risk assessment practice.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stage 1: Hazard Characterization"] --> B["Stage 2: Element-at-Risk Inventory"]
    B --> C["Stage 3: Indicator Selection"]
    C --> D["Stage 4: Data Collection"]
    D --> E["Stage 5: Vulnerability Function Construction"]
    E --> F["Stage 6: Damage State Mapping"]
    F --> G["Stage 7: GIS Aggregation"]
    G --> H["Stage 8: Validation and Uncertainty"]
    H --> I["Final Vulnerability Index and Curves"]

    A -.- A1["Intensity Metric: PGA, Wind km/h, Flood m"]
    B -.- B1["Buildings, People, Crops, Ecosystems"]
    C -.- C1["Physical Social Economic Environmental"]
    D -.- D1["Field Survey, Census, Remote Sensing"]
    E -.- E1["Fragility Curve, Vulnerability Index"]
    F -.- F1["DS0 to DS4 Categorical Scale"]
    G -.- G1["Block, Ward, Taluk, District"]
    H -.- H1["Monte Carlo, Historical Cross-Check"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Components of Vulnerability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VUL["VULNERABILITY V"]
        S["Sensitivity"]
        AC["Adaptive Capacity"]
        CC["Coping Capacity"]
    end

    subgraph PHYS["Physical"]
        P1["Building Typology"]
        P2["Material and Age"]
        P3["Code Compliance"]
    end

    subgraph SOC["Social"]
        SC1["Poverty and Education"]
        SC2["Age and Disability"]
        SC3["Access to Services"]
    end

    subgraph ECON["Economic"]
        EC1["Livelihood Diversity"]
        EC2["Insurance Penetration"]
        EC3["GDP per Capita"]
    end

    subgraph ENV["Environmental"]
        EN1["Ecosystem Health"]
        EN2["Land Use Change"]
        EN3["Climate Stressors"]
    end

    PHYS --> S
    SOC --> S
    ECON --> CC
    ENV --> AC
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Vulnerability Curve Concept

The block diagram below maps the conceptual link between hazard intensity, damage states, and the resulting vulnerability function.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AX["Hazard Intensity Axis: PGA 0 to 1.0g"] --> V1["Vulnerability Curve: D vs I"]
    DS0["DS0: No damage"] --> V1
    DS1["DS1: Slight 0 to 5 percent"] --> V1
    DS2["DS2: Moderate 5 to 20 percent"] --> V1
    DS3["DS3: Extensive 20 to 55 percent"] --> V1
    DS4["DS4: Complete 55 to 100 percent"] --> V1
    V1 --> OUT["Mean Damage Ratio MDR at given I"]
    OUT --> RISK["Risk = H times V times E"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Risk Equation Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RISK["RISK = H times V times E"]
    RISK --> HH["Hazard H: Probability of Threatening Event"]
    RISK --> VV["Vulnerability V: Susceptibility 0 to 1"]
    RISK --> EE["Exposure E: Value in Hazard Zone"]

    VV --> VT["Type 1: Physical"]
    VV --> ST["Type 2: Social"]
    VV --> ET["Type 3: Economic"]
    VV --> NVT["Type 4: Environmental"]

    EE --> EP["People"]
    EE --> EA["Assets and Property"]
    EE --> EI["Infrastructure and Systems"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)] (3 Marks)**
**Q: Define vulnerability in the context of disaster risk management. List any four factors that influence physical vulnerability.**

**Model Answer:**
Vulnerability is defined by UNDRR as the conditions determined by physical, social, economic, and environmental factors or processes which increase the susceptibility of an individual, community, assets, or systems to the impacts of hazards.

Four factors influencing **physical vulnerability** are:
1. **Construction material** (e.g., mud, brick, RC, steel)
2. **Age and maintenance state** of the structure
3. **Adherence to design codes** (e.g., IS 1893 for earthquake, IS 875 for wind)
4. **Building typology and configuration** (e.g., load path, irregularities, soft story)

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)] (3 Marks)**
**Q: Differentiate between Hazard, Vulnerability, and Risk. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Definition | Property Of |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard** | A dangerous event with potential to cause harm | Nature/Environment |
| **Vulnerability** | Degree of susceptibility of an exposed element | Element-at-Risk |
| **Risk** | Expected loss from hazard × vulnerability × exposure | System |

**Relationship:** $R = H \times V \times E$ (UNDRR Risk Equation).

Hazard and Exposure are *necessary* conditions; Vulnerability is the *amplifier*. Without vulnerability, risk = 0.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)] (14 Marks)**
**(a)** Explain the concept of vulnerability analysis. Discuss the different types of vulnerability with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the methodology of conducting a vulnerability analysis for a coastal district in Kerala exposed to cyclone and storm surge hazards. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
Vulnerability analysis is the systematic scientific process of identifying, quantifying, and characterizing the susceptibility of exposed elements (people, property, infrastructure, ecosystems) to a given hazard. It involves the construction of vulnerability functions, indices, or curves that map hazard intensity to expected damage.

**Types of Vulnerability (with examples):**

1. **Physical/Structural Vulnerability** — Buildings, bridges, roads. Example: Traditional tiled-roof houses in coastal Kerala are highly vulnerable to cyclonic winds ($V > 0.7$); RC-framed buildings designed to IS 456 and IS 1893 are less vulnerable ($V < 0.3$).

2. **Social Vulnerability** — Demographic groups. Example: Elderly, disabled, women-headed households in low-lying areas of Alappuzha have higher social vulnerability due to lower mobility and lower access to early warning.

3. **Economic Vulnerability** — Livelihoods and economy. Example: Fisherfolk dependent on a single boat with no insurance are economically vulnerable; loss of boat = loss of livelihood.

4. **Environmental/Ecological Vulnerability** — Ecosystems. Example: Mangroves in Vembanad Lake are vulnerable to oil spills and salinity intrusion; their loss amplifies coastal vulnerability.

#### Model Solution for (b):
**Methodology for Coastal Kerala District Vulnerability Analysis:**

1. **Hazard Characterization:** Cyclone wind speed (km/h) and storm surge height (m) for return periods of 10, 50, 100, 250 years using IMD and INCOIS data.

2. **Element-at-Risk Inventory:** GIS-based mapping of houses (using Kudumbashree/LSG data), population (Census 2011/2021), roads, hospitals, schools, fisherfolk assets, and ecologically sensitive zones.

3. **Vulnerability Indicator Selection:**
   - Physical: roof type, wall material, building age, code compliance.
   - Social: BPL ratio, literacy, female-headed households, age > 65.
   - Economic: insurance penetration, livelihood diversity, income.
   - Environmental: mangrove cover, shoreline change rate.

4. **Data Collection:** Drone surveys, building condition surveys using BBMP/LSG, secondary census data, focus group discussions.

5. **Vulnerability Function Construction:** Develop fragility curves for the dominant housing typologies (tiled roof, asbestos sheet, RC frame) using HAZUS-MH Indianized functions and Kerala SDMA studies.

6. **Damage State Mapping:** Assign each household a damage state probability matrix for each hazard return period.

7. **GIS Aggregation:** Produce ward/panchayat-level vulnerability maps using QGIS/ArcGIS.

8. **Validation & Uncertainty:** Cross-check against 2018 Ockhi and 2017 Vardah cyclone damage data; run Monte Carlo simulations to assess uncertainty in vulnerability index.

**Final Output:** District-level vulnerability map + Expected Annual Loss (EAL) estimate + prioritized list of wards for retrofitting.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)] (14 Marks)**
**(a)** Explain the concept of the vulnerability curve. A residential building typology in Kerala has the following damage state probabilities under seismic intensity PGA = 0.3g. Compute the Mean Damage Ratio (MDR). **(7 Marks)**

| DS | Probability | Mean Damage Factor |
|---|---|---|
| No damage (DS0) | 0.05 | 0.00 |
| Slight (DS1) | 0.20 | 0.04 |
| Moderate (DS2) | 0.40 | 0.15 |
| Extensive (DS3) | 0.25 | 0.45 |
| Complete (DS4) | 0.10 | 1.00 |

**(b)** Discuss the difference between indicator-based vulnerability index and fragility-based vulnerability curve. When would you prefer one over the other? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
**Vulnerability Curve Concept:**
A vulnerability curve is a function that maps hazard intensity (e.g., PGA in g, wind speed in km/h) on the x-axis to a damage measure (damage factor $D_f$ from 0 to 1) on the y-axis. It is element-specific (e.g., RC frame, masonry, tiled roof) and hazard-specific (e.g., earthquake, cyclone, flood). The curve is constructed from empirical loss data, laboratory tests, analytical simulations, or expert elicitation.

**MDR Computation:**

The Mean Damage Ratio is given by:

$$MDR(I) = \sum_{i=0}^{4} P_i(I) \cdot D_i$$

**Step 1:** Verify the sum of probabilities:

$$0.05 + 0.20 + 0.40 + 0.25 + 0.10 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2:** Compute each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &= 0.05 \times 0.00 = 0.0000 \\
T_1 &= 0.20 \times 0.04 = 0.0080 \\
T_2 &= 0.40 \times 0.15 = 0.0600 \\
T_3 &= 0.25 \times 0.45 = 0.1125 \\
T_4 &= 0.10 \times 1.00 = 0.1000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the terms:

$$MDR = 0.0000 + 0.0080 + 0.0600 + 0.1125 + 0.1000 = 0.2805$$

$$\boxed{MDR(\text{PGA} = 0.3g) = 0.2805 \text{ i.e., 28.05\% damage on average}}$$

**Interpretation:** Under a 0.3g peak ground acceleration, this residential building typology is expected to lose **28.05% of its replacement value** on average — falling in the *Extensive damage* band, with significant structural and economic loss.

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Aspect | Indicator-Based Index | Fragility-Based Curve |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Need** | Census, survey data, multi-criteria | Detailed engineering data, FEM |
| **Output** | A single normalized index (0 to 1) | A continuous function of intensity |
| **Spatial Resolution** | District / Block / Taluk | Building / Asset-level |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | High |
| **Decision Support** | Policy and prioritization | Engineering design and insurance pricing |
| **Uncertainty** | High (subjective weights) | Lower (physics-based) |
| **Example** | INFORM CVI, SoVI | HAZUS-MH fragility curves |

**When to prefer which:**
- Use **indicator-based index** for **macro-level planning, government schemes, multi-hazard ranking, and resource allocation** across large regions (e.g., a state or country). It is cheap, fast, and uses available census data.
- Use **fragility-based curve** for **insurance pricing, retrofitting design of specific buildings, and infrastructure risk assessment** (bridges, hospitals, nuclear plants). It requires detailed engineering data and is element-specific.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Confusing **vulnerability** with **hazard** (vulnerability is a property of the *element*, not the event).
> 2. Forgetting to **sum the probabilities to 1** before computing MDR — this is an instant 2-mark penalty.
> 3. Writing fragility curve and vulnerability curve as **synonyms** — they are NOT. A *fragility curve* gives $P(DS \ge DS_i \vert I)$, while a *vulnerability curve* gives $D(I)$ (mean damage ratio).
> 4. Missing units in the **Expected Annual Loss** answer.
> 5. In Part (b) comparison questions, writing a *table* earns more marks than continuous prose for KTU 2024 scheme valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 10 Bullet Points You MUST Remember:**

- [x] Vulnerability is the **susceptibility of an exposed element** to a hazard — NOT a property of the hazard itself.
- [x] The UNDRR Risk Equation is $\mathbf{R = H \times V \times E}$ — committed to memory.
- [x] The **four types of vulnerability** are: Physical, Social, Economic, Environmental.
- [x] The **five damage states** are: DS0 (No), DS1 (Slight, $D \le 0.05$), DS2 (Moderate, $D \le 0.20$), DS3 (Extensive, $D \le 0.55$), DS4 (Complete, $D \le 1.00$).
- [x] **Mean Damage Ratio** is the expected value of the damage factor weighted by state probabilities.
- [x] **Damage Factor** $D_f = C_{repair} / C_{replace}$; **Economic Loss Ratio** $ELR = L_{direct} / TEV$.
- [x] **Vulnerability Index** is a normalized (0 to 1) composite indicator for macro-level ranking.
- [x] **Fragility curve** gives $P(DS \ge DS_i \vert I)$; **vulnerability curve** gives $MDR(I)$ — they are related but distinct.
- [x] **Expected Annual Loss** $EAL = \int D(v) \cdot f_V(v) \, dv \times TEV$; it is the pure-risk premium benchmark.
- [x] For KTU 2024 valuation, always **use a comparison table** for 7-mark "differentiate" questions — it earns full marks faster.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accepted and Imposed Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Accepted and Imposed Risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Risk in Process Safety

In KTU 2024 Scheme Process Safety terminology, **Risk** is mathematically expressed as a function of three core parameters:

$$R = f(P, C, S)$$

Where:
- $P$ = **Probability** of an undesired event occurring
- $C$ = **Consequences** (severity of harm, damage, or loss)
- $S$ = **Scenario** context (exposure, vulnerability, scenario duration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Definition:** Risk is the product of the *likelihood* of a hazardous event and the *severity* of its consequences. Risk exists only when a hazard is *exposed* to a *target* (person, asset, or environment) under defined *conditions*.

### 1.2 Accepted Risk — The Voluntary Threshold

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accepted Risk** is the level of risk that an individual, group, or organization is willing to tolerate — either consciously or by default — in exchange for an associated benefit, opportunity, or economic advantage. It is **voluntary, informed, and within the control** of the exposed party.

**Key Characteristics of Accepted Risk:**
- **Voluntary** — The exposed party knowingly accepts the risk.
- **Informed** — The party has access to data about probability and consequence.
- **Benefit-linked** — There is a tangible gain (salary, convenience, product) attached.
- **Controllable** — The party can withdraw or mitigate the exposure.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a construction worker who signs up to work on a high-rise scaffolding project. He knows that working at height carries a certain chance of a fall (say 1 in 100,000 per year), but he accepts this risk because the job pays well and supports his family. This is **Accepted Risk** — the worker knows the danger, weighs the benefit, and chooses to proceed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** In industrial plants, Accepted Risk is the level of residual risk that remains after all reasonably practicable control measures have been applied (i.e., the ALARP region of the risk spectrum).

### 1.3 Imposed Risk — The Involuntary Burden

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imposed Risk** is the risk that is *forced* upon an individual, community, or stakeholder **without their consent, control, or meaningful informed choice**. The exposed party bears the consequences but did not choose the activity creating the hazard.

**Key Characteristics of Imposed Risk:**
- **Involuntary** — No choice is given to the exposed party.
- **Often un-informed** — Full hazard data may not be disclosed.
- **Asymmetric** — One party benefits (operator), another suffers (neighbor).
- **Distributed** — Burden falls on third parties (public, environment, future generations).
- **Power-imbalanced** — The receptor has limited ability to opt out.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
A farmer lives 2 km downstream from a chemical plant that stores chlorine gas. He never agreed to the plant being built, never signed a contract, and cannot move his ancestral land easily. If a chlorine leak occurs, he could be fatally exposed. This is **Imposed Risk** — he bears the consequence of someone else's industrial activity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Students often confuse "low probability" with "low risk." A very low-probability event with catastrophic consequence (e.g., a BLEVE in an LPG plant) may still constitute *intolerable imposed risk* on the surrounding community. **Probability alone does not define acceptability.**

### 1.4 The Risk Spectrum (Conceptual Foundation)

Risks in process safety are typically classified along a continuum from **Intolerable → ALARP → Acceptable → Negligible (De Minimis)**:

| Zone | Description | Action Required |
|------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Intolerable Region** | Risk is so high it cannot be justified except in extraordinary circumstances | Risk must be reduced regardless of cost |
| **ALARP Region** | Tolerable only if further reduction is impracticable or cost grossly disproportionate to safety gain | Apply ALARP principle |
| **Acceptable Region** | Risk is widely accepted by society; no further controls warranted | Monitor and review |
| **De Minimis (Negligible)** | Risk is so small that society is indifferent to it | No action |

### 1.5 Critical Distinction Table

| Attribute | Accepted Risk | Imposed Risk |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| **Choice** | Voluntary | Involuntary |
| **Beneficiary** | Same as exposed party | Different from exposed party |
| **Control** | High | Low / None |
| **Information** | Usually informed | Often uninformed |
| **Public perception** | Tends to be more tolerant | Tends to be highly intolerant |
| **Acceptance threshold** | Looser | Stricter |
| **KTU Example** | Worker entering a permit-required confined space | Resident living near a hazardous installation |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Risk Acceptance

The decision to *accept* a risk in a process plant is governed by three interlocking pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Risk Estimation (Quantitative)**
- Individual Risk (IR) per year
- Societal Risk (F-N curves)
- Loss of Life Expectancy (LLE)

**Pillar 2 — Cost–Benefit Analysis (Economic)**
- Cost of risk reduction (₹/year) vs. value of statistical life saved
- Implied Cost of Averting a Fatality (ICAF)

**Pillar 3 — Societal & Ethical Acceptance (Qualitative)**
- Public dread, familiarity, voluntariness
- Trust in operator
- Equity and fairness in distribution

### 2.2 Individual Risk (IR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Individual Risk is the *frequency* at which an individual may be expected to sustain a given level of harm from the realization of specified hazards.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$IR = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot p_i$$

Where:
- $f_i$ = frequency of hazardous scenario $i$ (per year)
- $p_i$ = probability that scenario $i$ results in fatality for the exposed individual
- $n$ = total number of identified hazardous scenarios

**Common Acceptance Benchmarks:**

| Land Use | UK HSE Tolerable IR (per year) |
|----------|--------------------------------|
| **Existing installation (employee)** | $10^{-3}$ |
| **New installation (employee)** | $10^{-5}$ |
| **Member of public (existing)** | $10^{-4}$ |
| **Member of public (new)** | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A risk of $10^{-6}$ per year is generally regarded as the **de minimis** boundary — below this, society is effectively indifferent. A risk of $10^{-4}$ per year is widely used as the boundary between *tolerable* and *broadly acceptable* for the general public.

### 2.3 Societal Risk and the F-N Curve

**Societal Risk** describes the relationship between the *frequency* (F) of events and the *number of fatalities* (N) they cause. It is plotted on the famous **F-N Curve**:

$$F = A \cdot N^{-k}$$

Where:
- $F$ = cumulative frequency of accidents causing $N$ or more fatalities (per year)
- $A$ = constant (anchoring the curve to a baseline frequency)
- $N$ = number of fatalities
- $k$ = slope exponent (typically 1 for "neutral" risk aversion; >1 for aversion to large accidents)

> [!WARNING]
> **Slope Rule:** If $k = 1$, society is *risk-neutral* to accident size. If $k > 1$ (commonly $k = 2$), society is *risk-averse* — it demands disproportionately lower frequencies for larger accidents. KTU examiners love testing this!

### 2.4 ALARP — As Low As Reasonably Practicable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ALARP Principle:** A risk is ALARP when the cost of further risk reduction is *grossly disproportionate* to the safety benefit obtained.

The classical test:

$$\text{Risk is ALARP if:} \quad \frac{\text{Cost of further reduction}}{\text{Reduction in risk}} \gg \text{Implied Cost of Averting a Fatality (ICAF)}$$

A practical interpretation:

| Cost-Benefit Ratio (CBR) | Decision |
|--------------------------|----------|
| CBR < 3 | Risk reduction is *required* |
| 3 < CBR < 10 | Risk reduction is *necessary unless grossly disproportionate* |
| CBR > 10 | Risk reduction is *not reasonably practicable* |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | KTU Application |
|---------|-------------|-------|-----------------|
| $R = f \times C$ | Risk = Frequency × Consequence | events/year × impact | Basic risk scoring |
| $IR = \sum f_i \cdot p_i$ | Individual Risk summation | dimensionless (per year) | On-site personnel IR |
| $F = A \cdot N^{-k}$ | F-N curve equation | F in per year, N dimensionless | Societal risk plotting |
| $ICAF = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta R}$ | Implied Cost of Averting a Fatality | ₹ per statistical life saved | Cost-benefit ALARP test |
| $FN = \sum_{i=N}^{\infty} f_i$ | Cumulative F-N | per year | F-N curve construction |
| $ERL = P \times E$ | Exposure-based risk level | per year | Off-site consequence IR |
| $LLE = IR \times L$ | Loss of Life Expectancy | years | Occupational risk comparison |
| $L = \frac{IR \times 10^6}{N_{pop}}$ | Lifetime risk per million | per million person-years | Public risk benchmark |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** "Risk = **F**requency × **C**onsequence" — always start your KTU answers with this line. Examiners reward students who set the conceptual framework first.

### 2.6 Why Accepted vs. Imposed Risk Matters in Engineering

In real-world process safety:
- **Employers** have a *duty of care* to workers (accepted risk framework).
- **Operators** of hazardous installations have a *social license to operate* (imposed risk framework).
- **Regulators** (e.g., PCB, OISD, Factory Inspectorate) impose **stricter limits** for imposed risk because the public has no opt-out.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Plant Layout & Siting** decisions use imposed risk criteria to determine minimum safe distances to housing.
> - **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** uses both IR and F-N curves.
> - **Land-use planning** around Seveso/COMAH sites is dictated by societal risk acceptance.

### 2.7 Factors Influencing Risk Perception (Why People Reject "Low" Risk)

The **Slovic psychometric model** identifies factors that cause people to reject risks that are statistically small:

- **Voluntariness** — Imposed risks are rejected 100× more than voluntary.
- **Dread** — Catastrophic potential causes rejection regardless of frequency.
- **Familiarity** — Unknown hazards are rejected.
- **Controllability** — Uncontrollable exposure = high rejection.
- **Trust** — Distrust of operator increases perceived risk.
- **Equity** — Risks benefiting few but borne by many are rejected.
- **Delayed effects** — Latent cancer risk is more feared than acute injury.
- **Newness** — Novel technologies face higher rejection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "low probability = acceptable." This is **incorrect** when the consequence is catastrophic or when the risk is *imposed* on third parties. Always qualify your answers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Hazard to F-N Curve

The construction of a societal risk (F-N) curve follows a rigorous five-step analytical process. Each step is derived below with full mathematical justification.

**Step 1 — Scenario Frequency Calculation**

For a given hazardous event (e.g., a runaway reaction in a reactor), the frequency is obtained by fault tree analysis:

$$f_{event} = f_{top} \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{m} P_j$$

Where:
- $f_{event}$ = frequency of the hazardous event (per year)
- $f_{top}$ = frequency of the initiating event (per year)
- $P_j$ = probability of failure of $j$-th safeguard (dimensionless, $0 \le P_j \le 1$)
- $m$ = total number of safeguards in the safety instrumented system

**Step 2 — Consequence Estimation**

For each scenario, the number of fatalities $N$ is estimated using consequence modeling (e.g., dispersion, fire radiation, explosion overpressure):

$$N = f(pop_{density}, A_{fatal}, P_{survival})$$

Where:
- $pop_{density}$ = population density in the affected zone (persons/km²)
- $A_{fatal}$ = lethal area of the physical effect (m² or km²)
- $P_{survival}$ = probability of escape or sheltering success

**Step 3 — Pairing Frequency and Consequence**

A discrete point on the F-N plane is generated:

$$(N_i, f_i) \quad \text{for each scenario } i$$

**Step 4 — Cumulative Frequency Computation**

To construct the F-N curve, frequencies are sorted by descending $N$ and summed cumulatively:

$$F(N) = \sum_{i : N_i \ge N} f_i$$

This represents the frequency of accidents causing $N$ or more fatalities.

**Step 5 — Power Law Fitting**

A power law is fitted to the F-N points using least-squares regression in log-log space:

$$\log(F) = \log(A) - k \cdot \log(N)$$

Converting back:

$$F = A \cdot N^{-k}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The "k" slope is a measure of *societal risk aversion*. A steep slope (k > 2) indicates a society/regulator that strongly demands reduced probabilities for large accidents. The KTU 2024 module emphasizes this parameter.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: F-N Curve Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
A petroleum refinery has identified three major accident scenarios. Using the data below, construct the F-N curve and determine whether each scenario falls in the Intolerable, ALARP, or Acceptable region, given the regulatory limits:
- $F(N) = 10^{-2}$ for $N = 1$
- $F(N) = 10^{-4}$ for $N = 10$
- $F(N) = 10^{-6}$ for $N = 100$

| Scenario | Frequency $f_i$ (per year) | Fatalities $N_i$ |
|----------|---------------------------|------------------|
| S1: Small localized fire | $1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ | 1 |
| S2: Major pool fire | $5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | 10 |
| S3: VCE (Vapour Cloud Explosion) | $2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ | 100 |

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step A — Sort by decreasing N:**

| Scenario | $N_i$ | $f_i$ (per year) |
|----------|-------|------------------|
| S3 | 100 | $2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| S2 | 10 | $5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| S1 | 1 | $1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ |

**Step B — Compute cumulative frequencies $F(N)$:**

$$F(N \ge 1) = f_1 + f_2 + f_3$$
$$F(N \ge 1) = 1.0 \times 10^{-2} + 5.0 \times 10^{-4} + 2.0 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$F(N \ge 1) = 0.010000 + 0.000500 + 0.000002$$
$$F(N \ge 1) = 1.0502 \times 10^{-2} \text{ per year}$$

$$F(N \ge 10) = f_2 + f_3$$
$$F(N \ge 10) = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} + 2.0 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$F(N \ge 10) = 5.02 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year}$$

$$F(N \ge 100) = f_3 = 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per year}$$

**Step C — Compare with regulatory limit lines:**

For each scenario, compare the cumulative frequency to the limit line value:

| Scenario | $N_i$ | Computed $F(N \ge N_i)$ | Limit $F_{limit}(N)$ | Position |
|----------|-------|------------------------|----------------------|----------|
| S1 | 1 | $1.05 \times 10^{-2}$ | $10^{-2}$ | Above limit → **Intolerable** |
| S2 | 10 | $5.02 \times 10^{-4}$ | $10^{-4}$ | Above limit → **Intolerable** |
| S3 | 100 | $2.0 \times 10^{-6}$ | $10^{-6}$ | Above limit (slightly) → **Intolerable → ALARP** |

**Step D — Conclusion:**

All three scenarios exceed (or borderline exceed) the regulatory F-N limits. The plant must implement risk reduction measures to push these scenarios into the ALARP or acceptable region.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Risk Acceptance Decision Framework

The complete decision flow for *accepting* vs. *rejecting* a risk uses the following matrix:

| Step | Question | If YES | If NO |
|------|----------|--------|-------|
| 1 | Is the risk in the **Intolerable region** (above upper F-N limit)? | **Reject** activity; implement risk reduction. | Proceed to Step 2 |
| 2 | Is further risk reduction *practically* possible? | Proceed to Step 3 | Risk is **ALARP**; document and proceed |
| 3 | Is the cost of further reduction *grossly disproportionate* to the benefit (CBR > 10)? | Risk is **ALARP**; document the case | **Implement** the risk reduction |
| 4 | Is the risk below the **de minimis** threshold ($IR < 10^{-6}$ per year)? | Risk is **Acceptable**; no action | Continue risk management in ALARP mode |

### 3.4 Cost-Benefit Worked Example (ALARP Application)

**Problem:**
A control modification will reduce the individual risk of a plant operator from $5 \times 10^{-5}$ to $2 \times 10^{-5}$ per year. The cost of the modification is ₹50,00,000 per year. There are 10 exposed operators. The Value of a Statistical Life (VSL) used by the regulator is ₹10 crore. Determine whether the modification is reasonably practicable.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate risk reduction per operator:**

$$\Delta IR = 5 \times 10^{-5} - 2 \times 10^{-5} = 3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the statistical lives saved per year across the exposed group:**

$$\Delta F = \Delta IR \times N_{operators} = 3 \times 10^{-5} \times 10 = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ statistical lives saved per year}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the ICAF (Implied Cost of Averting a Fatality):**

$$ICAF = \frac{\text{Annual cost}}{\Delta F} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{3 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$ICAF = 1.667 \times 10^{10} \text{ per statistical life saved}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to VSL:**

$$ICAF = 1.667 \times 10^{10} = ₹166.7 \text{ crore}$$

$$VSL = ₹10 \text{ crore}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Cost-Benefit Ratio:**

$$CBR = \frac{ICAF}{VSL} = \frac{166.7}{10} = 16.67$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

Since $CBR = 16.67 > 10$, the cost is *grossly disproportionate* to the benefit. The modification is **NOT reasonably practicable**, and the existing risk may be regarded as ALARP.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show the units in each step. Examiners deduct marks if units are missing or incorrect. Also, clearly state the *criterion* (e.g., CBR > 10 means "not ALARP-required") before applying it.

### 3.5 De Minimis and Negligible Risk Boundaries

The de minimis principle establishes a threshold below which risk is considered too small to warrant regulation:

$$IR_{de\ minimis} \le 10^{-6} \text{ per year}$$

This corresponds to:
- A one-in-a-million chance of fatality per year
- A typical lifetime risk of about $7 \times 10^{-5}$ (over 70 years)
- Roughly the same fatality risk as being struck by a meteorite

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** Below de minimis, regulatory agencies typically do not require additional safety investments, even if technically feasible. This is a pragmatic acknowledgment that infinite safety is impossible — there is an *optimal* level of residual risk.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Risk Acceptance Decision Hierarchy (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Hazard] --> B[Estimate Risk: f x C]
    B --> C{Is risk INTOLERABLE?}
    C -- YES --> D[REJECT activity / mandate risk reduction]
    C -- NO --> E{Is risk in ALARP region?}
    E -- YES --> F{Can further reduction be achieved?}
    F -- YES --> G{Is cost grossly disproportionate?}
    G -- NO --> H[Implement risk reduction]
    G -- YES --> I[Document ALARP case, proceed with controls]
    F -- NO --> I
    E -- NO --> J{Is risk below de minimis threshold?}
    J -- YES --> K[ACCEPT risk, no action required]
    J -- NO --> L[Monitor and review periodically]
```

### 4.2 F-N Curve Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Hazardous Event Set: S1, S2, S3 ... Sn] --> B[Consequence Model: N fatalities per scenario]
    B --> C[F-N Data Pairs: N_i, f_i]
    C --> D[Cumulative Frequency Sort: F greater than or equal to N]
    D --> E[Power Law Fit: F equals A times N to the power of minus k]
    E --> F[Plot on log-log axes]
    F --> G{Compare to Tolerability Lines}
    G -- Above upper limit --> H[INTOLERABLE Region]
    G -- Between lines --> I[ALARP Region]
    G -- Below lower limit --> J[ACCEPTABLE Region]
```

graph TD

    P --> U[Trust in Authority]
    P --> V[Equity of Distribution]

### 4.4 Accepted vs. Imposed Risk Comparison Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Accepted_Risk
        AR1[Voluntary Exposure]
        AR2[Informed Stakeholder]
        AR3[Beneficiary = Exposed Party]
        AR4[Looser Acceptance Criterion]
    end
    subgraph Imposed_Risk
        IR1[Involuntary Exposure]
        IR2[Possibly Uninformed Stakeholder]
        IR3[Beneficiary ≠ Exposed Party]

    end
    AR1 -. contrast .-> IR1

```

### 4.5 ALARP Cost-Benefit Decision Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Proposed Risk Reduction Measure] --> B[Compute Cost: C in rupees per year]
    A --> C[Compute Risk Reduction: delta R in statistical lives per year]
    B --> D[ICAF = C / delta R]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Compare ICAF to VSL}
    E -- ICAF less than or equal to VSL --> F[Proceed with measure]
    E -- ICAF up to 10x VSL --> G[Apply Gross Disproportion Test]
    E -- ICAF greater than 10x VSL --> H[Measure is NOT reasonably practicable]
    G -- Disproportionate --> H
    G -- Not disproportionate --> F
    F --> I[Risk reduced to ALARP]
    H --> J[Existing risk is ALARP by cost test]
```
