# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Direct Conceptual Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Define the Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI). What is the threshold value of F&EI that classifies a process unit as "Severe"?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)** is a dimensionless numerical rating used to quantify the **relative magnitude of fire and explosion hazard** of a process unit. It is calculated as the product of the **Material Factor (MF)** and the **Process Unit Hazards Factor (F₃)**, where F₃ is the product of General Process Hazards (F₁) and Special Process Hazards (F₂).
>
> $$\text{F\&EI} = MF \times F_1 \times F_2$$
>
> The threshold F&EI value for classification as a **"Severe"** unit is **128**. Units with F&EI > 158 are classified as **"Very Severe"**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**What is the Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)? State its formula and the threshold value that triggers mandatory mitigation.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)** is a Dow Chemical index used to quantify the **acute toxic airborne hazard** of a worst-case chemical release, especially for siting decisions.
>
> $$CEI = \frac{Q \times H_c}{D}$$
>
> where Q = quantity released, H<sub>c</sub> = health factor, D = reference distance (100 m).
>
> The threshold value that triggers **mandatory mitigation** is **CEI = 1**. Values between 1 and 10 indicate a "concern", 10 to 100 indicate a "major concern", and CEI > 100 indicates a "critical" toxic hazard.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part B questions carry 14 marks each, with **internal choice**. Each question has sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. The full step-by-step model solution is provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **step-by-step procedure** for the determination of the **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)**. List the **categories of General Process Hazards (F₁)** and **Special Process Hazards (F₂)**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A hexane storage unit has the following data:
- **Material Factor (MF) = 16**
- **General Process Hazards:** Exothermic reaction (P₁ = 0.5), Material handling penalty (P₃ = 0.4), Indoor enclosed unit (P₄ = 0.25). All others zero. → **F₁ = ?**
- **Special Process Hazards:** Operates in flammable range (S₃ = 0.5), Quantity of flammable material (S₇ = 1.5), Corrosion (S₈ = 0.2). All others zero. → **F₂ = ?**
- The replacement value of equipment within the unit = **₹ 2 Crore**.
- Damage Factor (DF) corresponding to computed F&EI = **0.45**.
- Location Factor = 1.0.

Compute the **(i) F&EI, (ii) Actual Maximum Probable Property Damage (Actual MPPD), and (iii) Business Interruption Loss**. **[7 marks]**

> **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Procedure (7 marks)

The Dow F&EI is determined in **five sequential steps**:

| Step | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Identify the material and look up (or compute) the **Material Factor (MF)**. | MF (1–40) |
| **2** | Evaluate **General Process Hazards** (F₁) by adding the **six penalty categories** (P₁–P₆). | F₁ |
| **3** | Evaluate **Special Process Hazards** (F₂) by adding the **twelve penalty categories** (S₁–S₁₂). | F₂ |
| **4** | Compute **F₃ = F₁ × F₂** and then **F&EI = MF × F₃**. | F&EI (1–300) |
| **5** | Read **Damage Factor (DF)** from the DF-vs-F&EI curve. Compute **Actual MPPD = Base MPPD × DF**. | $ Loss |

**Categories of General Process Hazards (F₁):**
1. **P₁** — Exothermic chemical reactions
2. **P₂** — Endothermic reactions
3. **P₃** — Material handling and transfer
4. **P₄** — Enclosed or indoor process units
5. **P₅** — Access of emergency response
6. **P₆** — Drainage and spill control

**Categories of Special Process Hazards (F₂):**
1. **S₁** — Toxic materials
2. **S₂** — Sub-atmospheric pressure
3. **S₃** — Operation in/near flammable range
4. **S₄** — Dust explosion
5. **S₅** — Relief pressure
6. **S₆** — Low temperature
7. **S₇** — Quantity of flammable material
8. **S₈** — Corrosion and erosion
9. **S₉** — Leakage at joints/packing
10. **S₁₀** — Fired heaters
11. **S₁₁** — Hot oil heat exchange
12. **S₁₂** — Large rotating equipment

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute F₁:**

$$F_1 = 1.0 + P_1 + P_3 + P_4 = 1.0 + 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.25 = 2.15$$

**Step 2 — Compute F₂:**

$$F_2 = 1.0 + S_3 + S_7 + S_8 = 1.0 + 0.5 + 1.5 + 0.2 = 3.20$$

**Step 3 — Compute F₃:**

$$F_3 = F_1 \times F_2 = 2.15 \times 3.20 = 6.88$$

**Step 4 — Compute F&EI:**

$$\text{F\&EI} = MF \times F_3 = 16 \times 6.88 = 110.08$$

**Step 5 — Classification:**

F&EI = 110.08 falls in the **"Heavy"** range (97 – 127) → significant mitigation required.

**Step 6 — Actual MPPD:**

$$\text{Actual MPPD} = \text{Base MPPD} \times DF = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.45 = \text{₹ } 90{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 7 — Business Interruption Loss:**

$$\text{BI Loss} = 0.70 \times \text{Actual MPPD} = 0.70 \times 90{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 63{,}00{,}000$$

> **Final Answer:** **F&EI = 110.08 (Heavy); Actual MPPD = ₹ 90,00,000; BI Loss = ₹ 63,00,000.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** What is the **Chemical Exposure Index (CEI)**? Explain the methodology of CEI calculation with the help of a worked example involving **chlorine release from a 50-tonne storage vessel at a distance of 200 m**. Health factor H<sub>c</sub> for chlorine = **6**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A refinery has **1,500 employees** working **2,000 hours per year** for **5 years**. During this period, **3 fatalities** occurred. Calculate the **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)** and the **Average Rate of Death (ARD)**. Comment on the risk level. **[7 marks]**

> **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — CEI Calculation (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** The CEI is a Dow Chemical index that quantifies the **acute airborne toxic hazard** of a chemical release for **siting and emergency response** planning. It is defined as:

$$CEI = \frac{Q \times H_c}{D}$$

**Given data:** Q = 50,000 kg; H<sub>c</sub> = 6; D = 200 m.

**Calculation (5 marks):**

$$CEI = \frac{50{,}000 \times 6}{200} = \frac{300{,}000}{200} = 1500$$

**Interpretation:** CEI = 1500 → **far exceeds the "Critical" threshold of 100** → This storage vessel **must be relocated** or must have **major engineering controls** (water-spray curtain, vapor recovery, double-walled tank, gas detection).

#### Part (b) — FAR and ARD (7 marks)

**Given data:** F = 3 fatalities; N = 1,500 workers; T = 2,000 hr/yr × 5 yr = 10,000 hr/worker; total person-hours = 1,500 × 10,000 = 1.5 × 10⁷ hr.

**Step 1 — FAR (3 marks):**

$$FAR = \frac{F \times 10^8}{N \times T} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^7} = 20 \text{ fatalities per } 10^8 \text{ person-hours}$$

**Step 2 — ARD (2 marks):**

Total exposure time = 5 years. Average exposed persons = 1,500.

$$ARD = \frac{3 \text{ fatalities} / 5 \text{ yr}}{1500 \text{ persons}} = \frac{0.6 \text{ fat/yr}}{1500} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3 — Risk Comment (2 marks):**

- **FAR = 20** is **3–4× higher** than the chemical industry average (5–10) and is comparable to **deep-sea fishing** (industry FAR ≈ 30). → **Risk is unacceptable**; immediate corrective action required.
- **ARD = 4 × 10⁻⁴/yr** is **40× the ALARP target** (10⁻⁵/yr for workers; 10⁻⁶/yr for public) → risk is in the **"Unacceptable" region** of the F-N diagram.

> **Final Answer:** **FAR = 20; ARD = 4 × 10⁻⁴/yr → Risk is Unacceptable.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Risk Index problems (KTU 2024 ESE pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the +1.0 baseline** in F₁ and F₂ calculations. Always write `F₁ = 1.0 + ΣP` — the +1.0 is the no-penalty baseline.
> 2. **Confusing MF (F&EI) with H<sub>c</sub> (CEI).** MF is for **fire/explosion energy**; H<sub>c</sub> is for **toxicity**. They are NOT interchangeable.
> 3. **Mixing units.** CEI uses kg/m or lb/ft — pick **one system** and stick to it. KTU 2024 allows both SI and US customary; mixing them inside a single formula is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Skipping the interpretation.** Examiners award **2 marks** for "interpretation and conclusion" (e.g., "CEI = 200 → Critical → Relocation required"). Don't end the problem with just a number.
> 5. **Confusing F&EI categories.** Light (1–60), Moderate (61–96), Heavy (97–127), Severe (128–158), Very Severe (>158). Memorize these.
> 6. **Wrong DF lookup.** DF is read from a **published curve**, not calculated. In the KTU exam, the **DF value is given** in the question — don't waste time re-deriving it.
> 7. **Forgetting the Location Factor** when computing Actual MPPD. The complete form is: `Actual MPPD = Base MPPD × DF × Location Factor`.
> 8. **Writing `|x|` with keyboard pipe in the answer sheet.** It is read as a table separator. Use parentheses or write "absolute value of x".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Risk Indices (Module 4)**

- **Risk Index** = numerical score to rank and screen process hazards; NOT an absolute risk predictor.
- **Three canonical indices:** Dow **F&EI** (fire/explosion), Dow **CEI** (toxic), **Mond** (European combined).
- **MF** is looked up from a table for ~270 substances; the formula is empirical.
- **F₁** = 1 + sum of 6 general process penalty categories (P₁ to P₆).
- **F₂** = 1 + sum of 12 special process penalty categories (S₁ to S₁₂).
- **F₃ = F₁ × F₂** captures *non-linear compounding* of independent failure modes.
- **F&EI = MF × F₃**; the magic thresholds are 96, 128, 158.
- **Damage Factor (DF)** is read from a curve; multiply by **Base MPPD × Location Factor** to get Actual MPPD.
- **Business Interruption Loss = 0.70 × Actual MPPD** (Dow's empirical 70% rule).
- **CEI = Q × H<sub>c</sub> / D**, threshold of concern = 1, critical = 100.
- **FAR = (Fatalities × 10⁸) / Person-hours**; industry avg ≈ 5, ALARP ≈ 4.
- **ARD = (Fatalities/yr) / Exposed Persons**; public limit ≈ 10⁻⁶/yr, worker limit ≈ 10⁻⁵/yr.
- **Mond Index** adds toxicity, layout, and quantity compensation to the F&EI structure.
- **F-N Curve** is the standard way to display societal risk on a log-log plot.
- **LTIFR** and **TRIR** are *workforce safety KPIs*, not fatality-risk indices — do not confuse them with FAR/ARD.
- **Risk indices are screening tools**; for absolute risk, use full **QRA with probit equations** (Y = a + b·ln(V)), **E₀₅₀** (50% fatality probit), and **ALARP principles**.
- **Always interpret the final number** — KTU awards marks for conclusion, not just calculation.
- **Memorize the formula sheet** (Section 2.5) — it covers ~90% of KTU numerical questions on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Individual Risk and Societal Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Individual Risk and Societal Risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Individual Risk (IR) — Formal Definition

**Individual Risk (IR)** is formally defined as the *frequency* (or probability per unit time, typically per year) at which an individual person, assumed to be continuously present at a given location, is expected to sustain a specified level of harm (commonly fatality) from the realization of all identified hazardous events associated with the activity under consideration.

Mathematically, it is the increment of risk imposed on an individual by being present in the vicinity of a hazard, irrespective of whether the person remains there for 24 hours per day or only for a fraction of the time.

$$IR_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j \cdot p_{ij}$$

Where:
- $f_j$ = frequency of occurrence of hazardous event $j$ (per year)
- $p_{ij}$ = probability that individual $i$ is fatally injured given event $j$
- $n$ = total number of identified hazardous events

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Individual Risk is a **scalar quantity** (single value per location) and is **time-independent** — it represents a rate (risk per year), not a probability of dying over a lifetime.

### 1.2 Societal Risk (SR) — Formal Definition

**Societal Risk** is the relationship between the **frequency** (F) of an accident and the **number of people** (N) suffering a specified level of harm (typically fatality) from that accident. It is an aggregate, population-level metric.

$$SR = \{F(N) \mid N = 1, 2, 3, \dots\}$$

Where $F(N)$ is the cumulative frequency of all accidents causing $N$ or more fatalities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction:** Individual Risk answers *"How likely is it that **I** die?"* — Societal Risk answers *"How likely is it that **many of us** die at once?"* — the two are NOT the same and BOTH must be evaluated per the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

**Analogy 1 — Lottery vs. Plane Crash:**
Imagine a town of 10,000 people living near a chemical plant.
- **Individual Risk** is like saying: *"Each person in the town has a 1 in 10,000 chance (10⁻⁴/year) of being killed by a plant accident in any given year."* This is a personal, per-head metric — the same for every resident regardless of population.
- **Societal Risk** is like saying: *"There is a 1 in 1,000 chance per year that an accident kills 50 or more people at once."* This is a *consequence-based* metric that captures the dread of a single catastrophic event.

**Analogy 2 — Road Travel:**
- IR ≈ Your personal chance of dying in a road accident per year (say 1×10⁻⁴).
- SR ≈ The chance of a multi-vehicle bus crash killing 20+ people in a single event (very low frequency, but high consequence).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** Standards like UK HSE's *"Reducing Risks, Protecting People"* (R2P2), TNO's *Purple Book*, and Singapore's *Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) Guidelines* all use IR + SR as the two pillars of risk acceptance criteria.

---

### 1.4 Standard Risk Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Quantity Measured | Typical Unit | What It Captures |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Individual Risk (IR)** | Risk to one person | per year (yr⁻¹) | Personal exposure |
| **Societal Risk (SR)** | Risk to a group | F-N curve | Catastrophic potential |
| **Location-Specific IR** | Risk at a fixed point | per year (yr⁻¹) | Spatial risk mapping (isopleths) |
| **Fatal Accident Rate (FAR)** | Deaths per 10⁸ person-hours | — | Industrial workforce safety |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Individual Risk

The concept of Individual Risk rests on three pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Hazard Identification & Frequency**
- A thorough Hazard Identification (HAZID) study identifies all credible initiating events (vessel rupture, pipe break, fire, explosion, toxic release, etc.).
- For each event, a **frequency** $f_j$ must be quantified — typically derived from generic failure-rate databases (e.g., OREDA, HSE Offshore, EGIG for gas pipelines).
- Frequencies are usually expressed in **events per year** (yr⁻¹).

**Pillar 2 — Consequence Modeling & Probability of Harm**
- Given that event $j$ occurs, the conditional probability $p_{ij}$ of fatality for person at location $i$ is computed using:
  - *Fire/Explosion models:* TNT-equivalent, TNO Multi-Energy, Shell BLEVE correlations.
  - *Toxic dispersion models:* Gaussian plume, heavy-gas (SLAB, DEGADIS), probit equations.
  - *Dose-response:* Probit equations $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$ converted to probability $P = 50\% \cdot [1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$.

**Pillar 3 — Exposure Modeling**
- The individual may not be at the location 100% of the time. An **exposure fraction** $E_i$ (0 ≤ E ≤ 1) is introduced:
$$IR_i = E_i \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n} f_j \cdot p_{ij}$$

For a continuously exposed person (residents, on-site workers) $E_i = 1$.

### 2.2 Theoretical Foundations of Societal Risk

Societal Risk does **not** simply equal $N \times IR$ — that would be a naive population scaling. Instead, SR accounts for the **multiplicity of fatalities per single event**:

**Step 1 — Group People by Zone**
The study area is divided into zones (outdoor, indoor, day/night occupancy), each containing a known number of persons $N_k$.

**Step 2 — Event Tree / Consequence Tree**
For each hazardous event, the consequence model predicts a number of fatalities in each zone, $n_{jk}$, using:
$$n_{jk} = N_k \cdot p_{jk}$$

**Step 3 — Build the F-N Curve**
Group all events (across all scenarios and weather conditions) into bins of fatality count $N$, and sum their frequencies:

$$F(N) = \sum_{j} f_j \quad \text{where} \quad N_j \geq N$$

This **cumulative F-N curve** is the graphical representation of Societal Risk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cumulative?** Because society tolerates "1 in 10,000 years of 10 deaths" and "1 in 1,000,000 years of 100 deaths" as roughly comparable levels of dread — the cumulative form captures this properly.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $IR_i = E_i \sum_j f_j \cdot p_{ij}$ | Individual Risk at location *i* | yr⁻¹ | Time-independent rate |
| 2 | $IR = \sum_k f_k \cdot p_k$ | IR (no exposure factor) | yr⁻¹ | For continuously exposed persons |
| 3 | $p_{ij} = \Phi\!\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | Probit-to-probability conversion | dimensionless | $\Phi$ is standard normal CDF |
| 4 | $Y = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$ | Probit equation | dimensionless | C in ppm, t in minutes (toxic) |
| 5 | $F(N) = \sum_{j : N_j \geq N} f_j$ | Societal Risk (cumulative) | yr⁻¹ | $F$ decreases as $N$ increases |
| 6 | $f(N) = F(N) - F(N+1)$ | Non-cumulative frequency (differential) | yr⁻¹ | Used in some F-N representations |
| 7 | $FN\text{-curve slope} = -\frac{\log(F_1/F_2)}{\log(N_1/N_2)}$ | Risk aversion slope (α) | dimensionless | TNO Purple Book: α = 1 (neutral), α = 2 (averse) |
| 8 | $IR_{tolerable} \leq 10^{-4}$ per year | HSE criterion — workers | yr⁻¹ | Maximum tolerable for employees |
| 9 | $IR_{tolerable} \leq 10^{-5}$ per year | HSE criterion — public | yr⁻¹ | Maximum tolerable for general public |
| 10 | $FAR = \frac{N_{fatalities} \times 10^8}{\text{person-hours worked}}$ | Fatal Accident Rate | dimensionless | 1 FAR = 1 death per 10⁸ person-hours |
| 11 | $ALARP$: Risk must be reduced *"as low as reasonably practicable"* | — | — | Gross disproportion test |
| 12 | $E_i = \frac{t_{\text{exposed}}}{8760}$ | Exposure fraction | dimensionless | 8760 = hours per year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** The boundary conditions matter in valuation. Always state units explicitly. A missing "per year" in an IR answer is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in KTU 2024 ESE.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Land-use planning (LUP):** Individual risk isopleths (contours of equal IR) are drawn on maps to define safety zones around hazardous installations (HSE PADHI + COMAH regulations in the UK).
- **Emergency planning:** Societal risk identifies whether an event could cause mass casualties, dictating evacuation route design and shelter-in-place protocols.
- **Insurance & regulatory licensing:** Both IR and SR are inputs to ALARP demonstrations required for major hazard facility licensing (e.g., EU Seveso III Directive, Indian Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989).
- **Process safety design:** Comparing alternative layouts/processes via Expected Loss of Life (ELL) and FN curves is standard QRA practice.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Event Frequencies to Individual Risk

**Given:** A pressurized chlorine storage vessel at a chemical plant.

**Assumed data** (illustrative, typical values from OREDA & Chlorine Institute):

| Event | Frequency $f_j$ (yr⁻¹) | Probit (Toxic Dose) | $p_{ij}$ (fatality) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small leak (10 mm) | $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ | $Y = -5.3 + 0.5 \ln(C^2 t)$ at 100 m | 0.05 |
| Medium leak (50 mm) | $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | $Y$ at 100 m | 0.30 |
| Catastrophic rupture | $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ | $Y$ at 100 m | 0.85 |

For an operator working outdoors at 100 m, continuously exposed ($E_i = 1$).

**Step 1 — Compute $p_{ij}$ using Probit equation for chlorine exposure (Toxic load = $C^2 t$, with $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min, constants $a = -10.6$, $b = 3.0$, $n = 2$ for lethality).**

The Probit equation for chlorine fatality at toxic load $C^2 t$ is:
$$Y = -10.6 + 3.0 \ln(C^2 t)$$

Probability of fatality:
$$p = 0.5 \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Individual Risk formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR_{100\,\text{m}} &= E \cdot \sum_j f_j \cdot p_{ij} \\
&= 1.0 \cdot \left[(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(0.05) + (1.0 \times 10^{-4})(0.30) + (1.0 \times 10^{-6})(0.85)\right] \\
&= 1.0 \cdot \left[5.0 \times 10^{-5} + 3.0 \times 10^{-5} + 8.5 \times 10^{-7}\right] \\
&= 1.0 \cdot \left[8.0 \times 10^{-5} + 0.85 \times 10^{-6}\right] \\
&\approx 8.0085 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with HSE Criterion.**
$IR_{100\,\text{m}} \approx 8.0 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{yr}^{-1}$

This is **less than the worker tolerable limit of $10^{-4}$ yr⁻¹** → IR is **acceptable** for the worker.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Constructing a Societal Risk F-N Curve

**Given:** The same chlorine facility, but now with 4 zones of population (residential blocks at varying distances).

**Zone data** (illustrative):

| Zone | Distance (m) | Population $N_k$ (night) | $p_{jk}$ (fatality) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Z1 (Workforce) | 100 | 20 | 0.05, 0.30, 0.85 |
| Z2 (Village A) | 500 | 200 | 0.001, 0.02, 0.40 |
| Z3 (Village B) | 1000 | 500 | 0.0001, 0.005, 0.10 |
| Z4 (Town) | 2000 | 5000 | 1e-6, 0.0005, 0.02 |

**Step 1 — Compute fatalities per event per zone** $n_{jk} = N_k \cdot p_{jk}$.

| Event | Z1 | Z2 | Z3 | Z4 | **Total $N_j$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small leak | 1 | 0.2 | 0.05 | 0.005 | **≈ 1.26 → bin N=1** |
| Medium leak | 6 | 4 | 2.5 | 2.5 | **≈ 15 → bin N=10** |
| Catastrophic | 17 | 80 | 50 | 100 | **≈ 247 → bin N=100** |

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, **binning** is done at standard cutoffs: 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, 300, 1000 fatalities.

**Step 2 — Compute cumulative frequencies.**

| Bin $\geq N$ | $F(N)$ (yr⁻¹) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\geq 1$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-3} + 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \approx 1.001 \times 10^{-3}$ | All events have ≥ 1 fatality |
| $\geq 10$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-6} = 1.01 \times 10^{-4}$ | Medium + Catastrophic |
| $\geq 100$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ | Catastrophic only |

**Step 3 — Plot the F-N curve.**

The F-N curve is drawn on a **log-log plot** with:
- X-axis: Number of fatalities $N$ (log scale)
- Y-axis: Cumulative frequency $F$ (log scale)

A typical F-N diagram (log-log):

```
   F (yr⁻¹)
   1e-1 │●
        │
   1e-2 │ ●
        │  ●
   1e-3 │   ●───── (≥1 fatalities)
        │
   1e-4 │        ●── (≥10 fatalities)
        │
   1e-5 │
        │
   1e-6 │             ●── (≥100 fatalities)
        │   ╱
   1e-7 │  ╱  ALARP region
        │ ╱
   1e-8 │╱
        └────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬────
             1   10   100  300  1000  N
```

**Step 4 — Compute the slope of the F-N curve (risk aversion index).**

Using the two extreme points $(N_1, F_1) = (1, 1.001 \times 10^{-3})$ and $(N_2, F_2) = (100, 10^{-6})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= -\frac{\log(F_2 / F_1)}{\log(N_2 / N_1)} \\
&= -\frac{\log(10^{-6} / 1.001 \times 10^{-3})}{\log(100 / 1)} \\
&= -\frac{\log(9.99 \times 10^{-4})}{\log(100)} \\
&= -\frac{-3.0}{2.0} \\
&= 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation (TNO Purple Book):** $\alpha = 1$ → "risk neutral" society; $\alpha = 2$ → "risk averse" society (steeper penalty for large accidents). Our value $\alpha = 1.5$ indicates **moderately risk-averse** societal risk profile.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: IR vs SR — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Aspect | Individual Risk (IR) | Societal Risk (SR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Perspective** | One person | Group of people |
| **Quantity** | Scalar (number per year) | Curve (F vs N) |
| **Captures** | Personal safety | Mass-casualty dread |
| **Plot** | Iso-risk contours on map | F-N curve (log-log) |
| **Key variable** | Frequency × conditional probability of death | Frequency of N+ fatalities |
| **Criterion type** | Numerical threshold (10⁻⁴, 10⁻⁵) | F-N curve position + slope |
| **Sensitive to population density?** | No | **Yes — strongly** |
| **Accounts for multi-fatality events?** | No (treats each person independently) | **Yes — explicitly** |
| **Application** | Land-use planning, occupational safety | Emergency planning, licensing |
| **KTU 2024 Weight** | ~50% of Module 4 marks | ~50% of Module 4 marks |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: End-to-End Risk Calculation

**Problem:** A natural gas transmission pipeline passes 200 m from a residential colony of 800 people. Pipeline failure frequency = $5 \times 10^{-5}$ per km·year. Length through the hazard zone = 2 km. Jet-fire fatality probability for a person at 200 m = 0.02. Calculate (a) the Individual Risk at 200 m, and (b) the expected number of fatalities per year.

**Solution:**

**(a) Individual Risk:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{pipeline} &= 5 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{yr}^{-1}\,\text{km}^{-1} \times 2\,\text{km} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{yr}^{-1} \\
IR_{200\,\text{m}} &= f \cdot p = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.02 = 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Result: $IR = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ yr⁻¹. This is **below the public tolerable limit of $10^{-5}$ yr⁻¹** → IR **acceptable**.

**(b) Expected Fatalities per Year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[N] &= N_{pop} \cdot f \cdot p = 800 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.02 \\
&= 800 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fatalities/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Result: $E[N] = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ fatalities/year, or roughly **1 fatality every 625 years**.

This is the **Expected Loss of Life (ELL)** — a key component of societal risk evaluation.

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **end-to-end risk quantification workflow** from hazard identification to risk acceptance decision, integrating both IR and SR.

```mermaid

    B --> B1[Component Failure Database OREDA/HSE]
    B --> B2[Historical Incident Records]
    B --> B3[Expert Judgment Delphi]
    A --> C[Consequence Modeling]
    C --> C1[Fire Explosion TNT TNO]
    C --> C2[Toxic Dispersion Gaussian SLAB]
    C --> C3[BLEVE Jet Fire]
    B --> D[Event Tree Synthesis]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Compute Conditional Probability p sub ij]
    E --> F[Risk Computation Engine]
    F --> F1[Individual Risk IR i]
    F --> F2[Societal Risk F-N Curve]
    F --> F3[Expected Loss of Life ELL]
    F1 --> G[IR Iso-risk Contours on Map]
    F2 --> H[F-N Curve Plot log-log]
    F1 --> I[Compare with HSE Criteria]
    F2 --> I
    I --> J{Risk Acceptable?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Proceed to ALARP Demonstration]
    J -->|No| L[Risk Reduction Measures]

    K --> M[Licensing and Land-use Approval]
```
