## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**  
Define Individual Risk (IR). State the standard HSE tolerable individual risk criteria for (i) workers and (ii) members of the public, with units.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Individual Risk (IR) is the frequency at which an individual, assumed to be continuously present at a given location, is expected to sustain a specified level of harm (typically fatality) from the realization of hazardous events associated with the activity. Mathematically, $IR = \sum_j f_j \cdot p_{ij}$ in units of **per year (yr⁻¹)**.  
- **Workers (employees):** tolerable IR ≤ **1 × 10⁻⁴ per year** (1 mark)  
- **Public (general public):** tolerable IR ≤ **1 × 10⁻⁵ per year** (1 mark)  
- Definition (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
What is an F-N curve? Why is it plotted on a log-log scale, and what is the significance of its slope?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An F-N curve is a graphical representation of **Societal Risk**, plotting the **cumulative frequency F (yr⁻¹)** of accidents causing **N or more fatalities** against **N**.  
- **Log-log scale** (1 mark): Used because the frequency span (e.g., 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻³) and fatality span (1 to 10,000) both span many orders of magnitude; log-log converts them to a manageable linear form.  
- **Slope significance** (1 mark): The slope α indicates society's **risk aversion** — α = 1 (neutral), α = 2 (averse). A steeper (more negative) curve means society penalizes large accidents more heavily.  
- F-N definition (1 mark)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Module 4 Internal Choice — Question A (14 Marks)**  
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model | Module 4]**

> *An ammonia storage facility is located near a township. QRA gives the following data:*
> *- Catastrophic vessel rupture: frequency = 1 × 10⁻⁶ per year, expected fatalities = 80*
> *- Major pipe rupture: frequency = 5 × 10⁻⁵ per year, expected fatalities = 12*
> *- Minor leak: frequency = 1 × 10⁻³ per year, expected fatalities = 1*
>
> *(a) Compute the Individual Risk at the boundary of the facility (assume continuous outdoor exposure, p = 0.4 for the dominant catastrophic event) and state whether it meets HSE criteria for the public. (7 marks)*
>
> *(b) Construct the cumulative F-N curve, determine the slope of the curve, and comment on whether the societal risk is acceptable (TNO Purple Book criterion: neutral slope α = 1, averse α = 2). (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Individual Risk at facility boundary (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IR &= f_{catastrophic} \cdot p_{fatality} \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.4 \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-7} \,\text{yr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Computation: 2 marks; units: 1 mark; interpretation: 2 marks; comparison: 2 marks)

**Comparison:** $IR = 4 \times 10^{-7}$ yr⁻¹ is **far below** the public criterion of $10^{-5}$ yr⁻¹ (factor of 25 lower). Hence, IR is **acceptable** for the public at the facility boundary.

> (Note: The minor and major events do not contribute to IR at the facility boundary in this simplified problem; if they do, total IR = $\sum f_j p_j$ — full credit given for showing this step.)

---

**(b) F-N Curve & Slope (7 marks):**

| Bin $\geq N$ | Events included | $F(N)$ (yr⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|
| $\geq 1$ | All three | $1.0 \times 10^{-3} + 5.0 \times 10^{-5} + 1.0 \times 10^{-6} = 1.051 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| $\geq 10$ | Major + Catastrophic | $5.0 \times 10^{-5} + 1.0 \times 10^{-6} = 5.1 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| $\geq 50$ | Catastrophic | $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ |

**F-N Points:** (1, 1.05×10⁻³), (10, 5.1×10⁻⁵), (50, 1.0×10⁻⁶)

**Slope calculation (using endpoints):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= -\frac{\log(F_2 / F_1)}{\log(N_2 / N_1)} \\
&= -\frac{\log(1.0 \times 10^{-6} / 1.051 \times 10^{-3})}{\log(50 / 1)} \\
&= -\frac{\log(9.51 \times 10^{-4})}{\log(50)} \\
&= -\frac{-3.022}{1.699} \\
&\approx 1.78
\end{aligned}
$$

(F-N table: 3 marks; slope formula & calculation: 2 marks; interpretation: 2 marks)

**Interpretation:** $\alpha \approx 1.78$ is between neutral (1) and averse (2). The facility shows **moderately risk-averse** societal risk. Since the curve lies below the TNO averse criterion line, the societal risk is **acceptable**, but ALARP demonstration is still required.

---

**Module 4 Internal Choice — Question B (14 Marks)**  
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model | Module 4]**

> *(a) Explain the difference between Individual Risk and Societal Risk with suitable examples. Why is it insufficient to evaluate only Individual Risk in major hazard installations? (7 marks)*
>
> *(b) For a refinery storage area, three accident scenarios have been identified. Using the data below, calculate the Expected Loss of Life (ELL) and plot the F-N curve. Comment on the risk aversion profile. (7 marks)*
> *Scenario 1: $f_1 = 1 \times 10^{-3}$/yr, $N_1 = 2$ fatalities*  
> *Scenario 2: $f_2 = 1 \times 10^{-4}$/yr, $N_2 = 15$ fatalities*  
> *Scenario 3: $f_3 = 1 \times 10^{-6}$/yr, $N_3 = 200$ fatalities*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IR vs SR — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Individual Risk (IR) | Societal Risk (SR) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Risk to a single person | Risk to a group |
| Unit | per year (yr⁻¹) | F-N curve |
| Example | Worker fatality per year on offshore platform | Mass-casualty event near stadium |

- **IR definition + example**: (2 marks)
- **SR definition + example**: (2 marks)
- **Why IR alone is insufficient**: (3 marks)  
  - IR treats every individual independently and **does not capture the dread of mass-casualty events** (e.g., Bhopal 1984, Texas City 1947).  
  - A facility with low IR but high population density (school, stadium nearby) can have **unacceptable SR**.  
  - Regulatory frameworks (HSE R2P2, TNO Purple Book) require BOTH IR and SR assessment; failure to evaluate SR violates ALARP demonstration.

---

**(b) ELL and F-N Curve (7 marks):**

**Expected Loss of Life (ELL):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ELL &= \sum_j f_j \cdot N_j \\
&= (1 \times 10^{-3})(2) + (1 \times 10^{-4})(15) + (1 \times 10^{-6})(200) \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-3} + 1.5 \times 10^{-3} + 2 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 3.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fatalities/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Formula: 1 mark; substitution: 2 marks; result: 1 mark; units: 1 mark)

**Cumulative F-N Curve:**

| $\geq N$ | $F(N)$ (yr⁻¹) |
|---|---|
| $\geq 1$ | $1.001 \times 10^{-3}$ (all three) |
| $\geq 10$ | $1.1 \times 10^{-4}$ (S2 + S3) |
| $\geq 100$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ (S3 only) |

**Slope:**

$$
\alpha = -\frac{\log(10^{-6}/1.001 \times 10^{-3})}{\log(100/1)} = -\frac{-3.0}{2.0} = 1.5
$$

**Risk Aversion Comment:** $\alpha = 1.5$ indicates **moderately risk-averse** — the facility penalizes large accidents but not as steeply as a fully averse society (α = 2). This is **typical for refinery operations** and is **generally acceptable** under TNO Purple Book, provided ALARP is demonstrated.

(ELL: 3 marks; F-N table: 2 marks; slope: 1 mark; comment: 1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units (yr⁻¹)** in IR answers → −1 mark guaranteed
> 2. **Using non-cumulative frequency** instead of cumulative F → −2 marks in SR questions
> 3. **Confusing F(N) and f(N)** — F is cumulative (≥ N), f is differential (= N)
> 4. **Skipping the Probit-to-probability conversion step** in IR calculations → −1 to −2 marks
> 5. **Forgetting to compare with HSE criteria** explicitly → −1 mark (just calculation is not enough)
> 6. **In slope calculation, not stating "risk aversion" interpretation** → −1 mark
> 7. **Mixing up frequency units** (per km·year vs per year) — for pipelines, must multiply by length → −1 mark
> 8. **Omitting "ALARP demonstration is still required"** even when risk is acceptable → −1 mark
> 9. **Using $10^{-3}$ for public** instead of $10^{-5}$ — mixing worker and public criteria → −1 to −2 marks

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Individual Risk (IR)** = frequency (per year) at which an **individual** at a fixed location is fatally harmed. Units: **yr⁻¹**.
- ✅ **IR formula:** $IR_i = E_i \sum_j f_j \cdot p_{ij}$ — always include exposure fraction for non-continuous exposure.
- ✅ **HSE Worker criterion:** $IR \leq 10^{-4}$ yr⁻¹; **HSE Public criterion:** $IR \leq 10^{-5}$ yr⁻¹.
- ✅ **Societal Risk (SR)** = relationship between accident frequency F and number of fatalities N. Graphical form = **F-N curve**.
- ✅ **F-N curve is always CUMULATIVE** — $F(N) = \sum_{j : N_j \geq N} f_j$, plotted on **log-log paper**.
- ✅ **Slope α (risk aversion):** α = 1 neutral, α = 2 averse (TNO Purple Book convention).
- ✅ **Probit equation:** $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$; **Conversion to probability:** $P = 0.5[1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$.
- ✅ **Expected Loss of Life (ELL):** $ELL = \sum f_j N_j$ — a scalar summary of societal risk.
- ✅ **ALARP principle:** Risk must be reduced *"as low as reasonably practicable"* — applies even when IR/SR are below tolerable limits.
- ✅ **Isopleths / Iso-risk contours** = lines of equal IR drawn on a map for land-use planning.
- ✅ **FAR (Fatal Accident Rate):** deaths per 10⁸ person-hours; 1 FAR corresponds to ~$10^{-3}$ IR for full-time workers.
- ✅ **Both IR and SR must be evaluated** — IR alone misses mass-casualty events; SR alone misses personal risk.
- ✅ **F-N curve points to remember:** (1, 10⁻³), (10, 10⁻⁵), (100, 10⁻⁷) represent typical HSE tolerability anchor points.
- ✅ **For pipelines**, always multiply frequency (per km·yr) by **pipeline length within hazard zone**.
- ✅ **For ALARP test:** "Gross disproportion" — only when cost of risk reduction is **grossly disproportionate** to safety benefit can additional measures be foregone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When the question asks *"compare IR and SR"*, ALWAYS present them in a **comparison table** with definitions, units, criteria, applications, and a closing statement on why both are needed. This single answer structure fetches 7/7 marks reliably.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceptance Criteria for Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Acceptance Criteria for Risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Acceptance Criteria for Risk** is the predefined, systematic, and quantitative/qualitative benchmark or threshold of risk against which the estimated level of risk from a hazardous installation, process, operation, or activity is evaluated to determine whether the risk is **broadly acceptable**, **tolerable** (with controls), or **unacceptable** (requiring immediate mitigation or rejection of the activity).

According to the **Health and Safety Executive (HSE)** UK framework — which forms the basis of KTU 2024 syllabus treatment — acceptance criteria for risk are formally expressed through:

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Consequence Severity}, \text{Likelihood of Occurrence})$$

The risk acceptance criterion is the cutoff value of this function above which the risk is deemed intolerable and below which the activity may proceed subject to the **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) requirement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):**
> Acceptance criteria for risk is a decision-support tool that classifies risks into three zones — *Broadly Acceptable*, *Tolerable (ALARP Region)*, and *Intolerable/Unacceptable*. The criteria convert subjective engineering judgement into objective, defensible go/no-go decisions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a cliff and deciding how close to the edge is "safe" to stand:

- **Standing 10 meters away** → Risk is *broadly acceptable* (negligible concern)
- **Standing 1 meter away** → Risk is *tolerable* (you accept it because moving further back costs effort, but you take precautions)
- **Standing at the very edge** → Risk is *unacceptable* (must retreat immediately)

The **"acceptance criteria for risk"** are the invisible lines drawn on the ground that distinguish these three zones. In process safety, these lines are not arbitrary — they are derived from fatality statistics, societal expectations, regulatory mandates (e.g., **1 × 10⁻⁶ per year individual fatality risk** for workers near major hazard installations), and cost-benefit reasoning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:**
> "Acceptable risk" does **not** mean "zero risk." It is the level of risk society/industry is willing to live with given the benefits of the activity. Risk is the currency of industrial progress — it must be *managed*, not *eliminated*.

### 1.3 Fundamental Risk Magnitudes Used in Acceptance Criteria

| Risk Metric | Symbol | Typical Acceptable Threshold | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Individual Risk (Fatality) | IR | **1 × 10⁻⁶** | per year |
| Individual Risk (HSE Upper Limit) | IR | **1 × 10⁻⁴** | per year |
| Societal Risk (F-N curve) | F(N) | Slope **−1** (UK HSE) | dimensionless |
| Failure Rate | λ | **1 × 10⁻⁵** | per year |
| Probability of fatality on-site | P | **1 × 10⁻³** | per year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These thresholds are the **anchor values** examiners expect students to recall in KTU board examinations. Memorize **1 × 10⁻⁶** (broadly acceptable individual risk) and **1 × 10⁻⁴** (maximum tolerable individual risk).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Zone Risk Acceptance Framework (HSE UK Model)

The HSE (UK) tolerability of risk framework divides the risk space into three distinct zones:

**Zone 1 — Intolerable Region (RED ZONE)**
- Risk is so high that the activity **must not** be permitted, regardless of benefits.
- Action: Reject the activity, redesign, or relocate.

**Zone 2 — ALARP / Tolerable Region (YELLOW ZONE)**
- Risk is tolerable only if **all reasonably practicable measures** have been taken to reduce it further.
- This is the operational zone for most industrial activities.
- Action: Apply ALARP reduction until "gross disproportion" cost-benefit barrier is reached.

**Zone 3 — Broadly Acceptable Region (GREEN ZONE)**
- Risk is negligible compared to everyday risks.
- No further risk reduction is required (cost would outweigh marginal benefit).
- Action: Routine monitoring suffices.

### 2.2 The ALARP Principle (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)

ALARP is the **operational decision rule** used within the yellow (tolerable) zone. It requires that:

$$\text{Risk Reduction Effort} \propto \text{Inverse of Cost-Disproportion}$$

In economic terms, a risk reduction measure must be implemented **unless** the cost is "grossly disproportionate" to the safety benefit achieved:

$$\frac{\text{Cost of Risk Reduction Measure}}{\text{Risk Reduction Benefit}} \gg 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Measure may be waived}$$

The **Gross Disproportion Factor (GDF)** is typically:
- **GDF = 2 to 10** for low-risk scenarios
- **GDF = 10** (standard industry rule of thumb)
- **GDF = Up to 100** for risks involving large numbers of people

### 2.3 Cost-Benefit Analysis Equation (ALARP Quantitative Form)

The economic limit of risk reduction is determined by:

$$C_{risk\,reduction} \leq \text{GDF} \times B_{risk\,reduction}$$

Where the **monetized benefit of risk reduction** is calculated as:

$$B = N \times P_f \times V_{stat}$$

- $N$ = Number of exposed people
- $P_f$ = Probability of fatality
- $V_{stat}$ = **Statistical Value of Life (VSL)** ≈ ₹8 Crore (India context) or £1.8 Million (UK HSE 2020)

### 2.4 Individual Risk Calculation

The Individual Risk (IR) to a person at a location is the **sum of products of consequence and frequency** across all accident scenarios affecting that location:

$$IR = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \times p_{i,f}$$

- $f_i$ = Frequency of initiating event *i* (per year)
- $p_{i,f}$ = Probability of fatality at the location given event *i*

### 2.5 Societal Risk — The F-N Curve

Societal risk addresses multiple fatality events. It is plotted on a log-log graph:
- **X-axis (N):** Number of fatalities per event
- **Y-axis (F):** Cumulative frequency of events with N or more fatalities

The **F-N curve criterion** requires that the F-N curve of the facility lies **below** the limit line:

$$F \times N^k = C \quad \text{(Limit Line Equation)}$$

For the **HSE UK limit line**: $k = 1$ (slope of −1)

$$F = \frac{C}{N}$$

A typical value: $C = 10^{-2}$ fatalities/year (this places a 1-fatality event frequency ceiling at $10^{-2}$ per year).

### 2.6 Risk Matrix for Acceptance Criteria

The classical **5×5 Risk Matrix** assigns acceptance based on Severity × Likelihood:

| Severity \ Likelihood | L1: Rare | L2: Unlikely | L3: Possible | L4: Likely | L5: Almost Certain |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S5: Catastrophic** | Medium | High | Very High | Extreme | Extreme |
| **S4: Major** | Medium | Medium | High | Very High | Extreme |
| **S3: Moderate** | Low | Medium | High | Very High | Very High |
| **S2: Minor** | Low | Low | Medium | High | High |
| **S1: Negligible** | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | High |

**Acceptance Bands:**
- **Low / Medium → ACCEPTABLE** (proceed with routine controls)
- **High → TOLERABLE** (ALARP applies)
- **Very High / Extreme → UNACCEPTABLE** (mandate redesign or shutdown)

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $IR = \sum f_i \cdot p_{i,f}$ | Individual fatality risk | yr⁻¹ |
| 2 | $B = N \cdot P_f \cdot V_{stat}$ | Monetized benefit of safety | ₹ or £ |
| 3 | $C_{RR} \leq GDF \times B_{RR}$ | ALARP cost-benefit test | currency |
| 4 | $F \cdot N^k = C$ | F-N curve societal limit | events/yr |
| 5 | $GDF = \frac{\text{Cost of Measure}}{\text{Risk Reduction Benefit}}$ | Gross disproportion test | dimensionless |
| 6 | $VSL \approx ₹8\,Cr \approx £1.8\,M$ | Statistical Value of Life | ₹ / £ |
| 7 | $k_{safety} = 1 - P_{accident}$ | Engineering safety factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $R_{residual} = R_{initial} \times (1 - E_{reduction})$ | Residual after mitigation | yr⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> Acceptance criteria for risk form the *legal and ethical backbone* of COMAH (Control of Major Accident Hazards) regulations in the UK, OSHA PSM (Process Safety Management) in the USA, and the **Indian Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC, 1989)**. Every quantitative risk assessment (QRA) report submitted to regulatory authorities must explicitly map estimated risks against these criteria.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Individual Risk from a Single Hazardous Source

**Problem Setup:**
A chlorine storage vessel has a critical failure frequency of $f = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ per year. The probability that an individual at 200 m downwind will be fatally affected is $p_f = 0.4$. The exposure time at the location is 50% of the year. Determine the **Individual Risk** and compare with acceptance criteria.

**Step 1 — Identify frequency of fatality-causing event at the point of interest.**

The frequency of fatality-causing incidents reaching the exposed individual is:

$$f_{eff} = f_{failure} \times p_{fatality \vert failure}$$

$$f_{eff} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.4$$

$$f_{eff} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{per year}$$

**Step 2 — Apply exposure time correction.**

Since the individual is present only 50% of the year:

$$f_{exposed} = f_{eff} \times T_{exposure}$$

$$f_{exposed} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.5 = 1 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{per year}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with acceptance criteria.**

- HSE Tolerable upper limit: $IR_{tol} = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ per year
- Broadly acceptable limit: $IR_{acc} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ per year

Our computed value: $IR = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ per year

**Step 4 — Classification:**

$$\text{Result: } 1 \times 10^{-6} < 1 \times 10^{-5} < 1 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\therefore \text{Risk is in the TOLERABLE (ALARP) zone}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Decision: Risk acceptable subject to ALARP demonstration.}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: ALARP Cost-Benefit Test for a Proposed Mitigation Measure

**Problem Setup:**
A plant employs 50 workers in a high-risk area. The current individual fatality probability is $P_f = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ per year. A proposed engineered safeguard costs **₹50 Lakh** to install and would reduce $P_f$ to $5 \times 10^{-6}$ per year. The Statistical Value of Life (VSL) is ₹8 Crore. Apply the **Gross Disproportion Factor (GDF) test** with GDF = 10.

**Step 1 — Calculate current societal risk exposure (annual expected fatalities).**

$$AF_{current} = N \times P_f$$

$$AF_{current} = 50 \times 4 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$AF_{current} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{fatalities/year}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate reduced risk after measure.**

$$AF_{reduced} = 50 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{fatalities/year}$$

**Step 3 — Compute fatalities averted per year.**

$$\Delta F = AF_{current} - AF_{reduced}$$

$$\Delta F = 2 \times 10^{-3} - 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta F = 1.75 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{fatalities/year}$$

**Step 4 — Compute monetized benefit over 10-year asset life.**

$$B = \Delta F \times VSL \times T_{life}$$

$$B = 1.75 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^{7} \times 10$$

$$B = 1.75 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^{8}$$

$$B = 1.4 \times 10^{6} \, \text{₹ (i.e., ₹1.4 Crore)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute gross-disproportion cost threshold.**

$$C_{threshold} = B \times GDF$$

$$C_{threshold} = 1.4 \times 10^{6} \times 10 = 1.4 \times 10^{7} \, \text{₹ (i.e., ₹1.4 Crore)}$$

**Step 6 — Decision.**

- Actual cost of measure: $C = ₹50\,Lakh = 5 \times 10^{6}$ ₹
- Allowed threshold: $C_{threshold} = 1.4 \times 10^{7}$ ₹

$$C = 5 \times 10^{6} < C_{threshold} = 1.4 \times 10^{7}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Decision: Cost is NOT grossly disproportionate. ALARP requires implementation of the safeguard.}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: F-N Curve Plotting for a Refinery Installation

**Problem Setup:**
A QRA identifies the following accident scenarios at a refinery:

| Scenario | No. of Fatalities (N) | Frequency (F, per year) |
|---|---|---|
| S1: Pump seal leak (toxic) | 1 | $1 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| S2: Storage tank rupture (vapour cloud) | 5 | $5 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| S3: Major distillation column BLEVE | 25 | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| S4: Catastrophic plant-wide explosion | 100 | $5 \times 10^{-8}$ |

The HSE limit line is $F \times N = 10^{-2}$. Determine acceptance.

**Step 1 — Compute cumulative frequency $F_c(N)$ for each N.**

Cumulative frequency includes all events causing ≥ N fatalities:

$$F_c(N=1) = 1 \times 10^{-3} + 5 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-6} + 5 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$F_c(N=1) \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_c(N=5) = 5 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-6} + 5 \times 10^{-8} \approx 5.1 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$F_c(N=25) = 1 \times 10^{-6} + 5 \times 10^{-8} \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$F_c(N=100) = 5 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 2 — Compare each pair $(N, F_c)$ with the limit line $F_c \cdot N = 10^{-2}$.**

| N | $F_c$ (actual) | $F_c \cdot N$ | Limit (10⁻²) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1.05 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.05 \times 10^{-3}$ | $10^{-2}$ | **ACCEPTABLE** (below limit) |
| 5 | $5.1 \times 10^{-5}$ | $2.55 \times 10^{-4}$ | $10^{-2}$ | **ACCEPTABLE** (below limit) |
| 25 | $1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ | $2.63 \times 10^{-5}$ | $10^{-2}$ | **ACCEPTABLE** |
| 100 | $5 \times 10^{-8}$ | $5 \times 10^{-6}$ | $10^{-2}$ | **ACCEPTABLE** |

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**

Since $F_c \cdot N < 10^{-2}$ for all scenarios, the societal risk is **within the broadly acceptable region**.

$$\boxed{\text{Decision: No further risk reduction mandatory; routine ALARP monitoring suffices.}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Risk Acceptance Decision Flowchart (HSE Tolerability Framework)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Risk Assessment Completed] --> B[Compute Individual Risk IR and Societal Risk F-N]
    B --> C{IR greater than 1e-4 per year?}
    C -- YES --> D[RED ZONE: INTOLERABLE - Reject Activity]
    C -- NO --> E{IR less than 1e-6 per year?}
    E -- YES --> F[GREEN ZONE: BROADLY ACCEPTABLE - Routine Monitoring]
    E -- NO --> G[YELLOW ZONE: TOLERABLE - Apply ALARP]
    G --> H[Identify Risk Reduction Measures]
    H --> I{Cost grossly disproportionate to benefit?}
    I -- YES --> J[STOP: ALARP Demonstrated - Proceed]
    I -- NO --> K[Implement Measure]
    K --> L[Recompute Residual Risk]
    L --> M{Residual IR less than 1e-6?}
    M -- YES --> F
    M -- NO --> G
    D --> N[END: Activity Prohibited]
    F --> O[END: Activity Approved]
    J --> O
```

### 4.2 ALARP Triangle (Hierarchy of Risk Reduction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ALARP Demonstration] --> B[Inherent Safety Design]
    B --> C[Engineering Controls SIS ESD Systems]
    C --> D[Procedural Controls SOPs Permits]
    D --> E[PPE Last Resort]
    E --> F[Cost Benefit GDF Test]
    F --> G{Residual Risk Below Acceptable Threshold?}
    G -- YES --> H[ALARP ACHIEVED]
    G -- NO --> I[Return to Higher Level of Hierarchy]
```

### 4.3 F-N Curve Structure (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Accident Scenario Database] --> B[Consequence Modeling PHAST ALOHA]
    A --> C[Frequency Modeling Fault Tree Event Tree]
    B --> D[Fatality N Estimation]
    C --> E[Frequency F per Year]
    D --> F[Compute F N Product]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Compare with HSE Limit Line}
    G -- Below --> H[SOCIETALLY ACCEPTABLE]
    G -- Above --> I[ALARP Region - Mitigation Required]
    G -- Far Above --> J[UNACCEPTABLE - Reject or Relocate]
```

### 4.4 Three-Zone Risk Acceptance Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Z1 [INTOLERABLE ZONE - RED]
        Z1A[IR greater than 1e-4]
        Z1B[F N product greater than 1e-2]
    end
    subgraph Z2 [TOLERABLE ALARP ZONE - YELLOW]
        Z2A[IR between 1e-6 and 1e-4]
        Z2B[Apply Gross Disproportion Test]
        Z2C[Demonstrate ALARP Reduction]
    end
    subgraph Z3 [BROADLY ACCEPTABLE ZONE - GREEN]
        Z3A[IR less than 1e-6]
        Z3B[Routine Monitoring Only]
    end
    Z1 -- Risk Reduction --> Z2
    Z2 -- Further ALARP --> Z3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for Examiners:**
> When asked to "explain the HSE tolerability framework" in exams, the recommended answer structure is to draw a graph with **Log(IR) on Y-axis** and three horizontal bands corresponding to the three zones, marking the threshold lines at $10^{-4}$ and $10^{-6}$. This is the highest-scoring answer approach.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1 (Module 4 – Acceptance Criteria for Risk)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Define acceptance criteria for risk. List the three zones of risk acceptance as per the HSE framework."**

**Model Answer:**

Acceptance criteria for risk are predefined benchmarks used to determine whether the estimated level of risk from an activity is acceptable, tolerable, or unacceptable. These criteria enable objective, quantitative decision-making in risk management.

The three zones of risk acceptance per the **Health and Safety Executive (HSE) framework** are:

1. **Intolerable Zone (Red):** Risk is so high that the activity must be prohibited regardless of benefits.
2. **Tolerable / ALARP Zone (Yellow):** Risk is tolerated only if all reasonably practicable measures have been taken to reduce it further.
3. **Broadly Acceptable Zone (Green):** Risk is negligible compared to daily-life risks; no further risk reduction is mandated.

The corresponding individual risk thresholds are **$1 \times 10^{-4}$/year** (intolerable ceiling) and **$1 \times 10^{-6}$/year** (broadly acceptable limit).

---

#### **Question A2 (Module 4 – Acceptance Criteria for Risk)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"What is the ALARP principle? State its significance in process safety management."**

**Model Answer:**

ALARP stands for **"As Low As Reasonably Practicable."** It is the operational decision rule applied within the tolerable risk zone, requiring that all risk reduction measures be implemented unless the cost of doing so is **grossly disproportionate** to the safety benefit achieved.

**Significance in Process Safety Management:**

1. **Practical Limit:** Acknowledges that zero risk is unattainable and provides an economically rational stopping rule.
2. **Legal Defence:** Demonstrating ALARP is a legal defence under COMAH / OSHA PSM regulations.
3. **Continuous Improvement:** Drives iterative risk reduction through engineering and procedural controls.
4. **Societal Equity:** Balances industrial benefits against worker and public safety expectations.
5. **Cost Optimization:** Prevents infinite expenditure on negligible marginal risk reduction.

The mathematical test is given by: $C_{measure} \leq GDF \times B_{risk\,reduction}$, where GDF is typically **2 to 10** (up to 100 for high-fatality scenarios).

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** — `Option Set 1`
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Adapted]`

**"Discuss in detail the various acceptance criteria used for risk evaluation in process industries. Include the ALARP principle, individual risk criteria, societal risk criteria (F-N curve), and cost-benefit analysis in your answer."**

**(a) ALARP Principle and Cost-Benefit Analysis** — **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **ALARP principle** is a structured risk reduction framework applied within the tolerable risk zone. Its formal mathematical statement is:

$$C_{measure} \leq GDF \times B_{risk\,reduction}$$

The **Cost-Benefit Analysis** procedure follows these steps:

**Step 1:** Quantify the initial risk (in fatalities/year) using frequency-consequence modeling.

**Step 2:** Identify candidate risk reduction measures and their installation costs.

**Step 3:** Calculate the **Statistical Value of Life (VSL)** — typically ₹8 Crore in India or £1.8 Million in UK HSE.

**Step 4:** Compute monetized benefit:
$$B = N \times \Delta P_f \times VSL \times T_{asset\,life}$$

**Step 5:** Apply **Gross Disproportion Factor (GDF)**:
- GDF = 2 for trivial risks
- GDF = 10 (standard industry value)
- GDF = 100 for catastrophic scenarios

**Step 6:** Compare cost vs threshold; if $C \leq GDF \times B$, implement the measure; if not, ALARP is demonstrated at the existing level.

**Step 7:** Recompute residual risk and verify it falls into the broadly acceptable zone.

---

**(b) Individual Risk and Societal Risk Criteria** — **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Individual Risk (IR)** is the frequency at which an individual at a specific location is expected to sustain a fatal injury. It is calculated as:

$$IR = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \times p_{i,f}$$

**Acceptance Thresholds (HSE UK):**

| Criterion | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Intolerable limit | $1 \times 10^{-4}$/year | Maximum tolerable IR |
| ALARP region | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ to $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | Tolerable with ALARP |
| Broadly acceptable | $< 1 \times 10^{-6}$/year | Negligible risk |

**Societal Risk (SR)** captures the concern for events causing multiple fatalities simultaneously. It is represented graphically as an **F-N Curve** on log-log axes, where:
- F = cumulative frequency (per year)
- N = number of fatalities per event

The **HSE limit line** is given by:
$$F \times N = 10^{-2} \quad \text{(slope −1)}$$

The facility's F-N curve must lie **below** this limit line for societal acceptance. Events involving larger N receive stricter scrutiny because society's aversion to multiple-fatality accidents is greater than the numerical fatality count suggests — a phenomenon known as **risk aversion**.

The combined use of IR (localized risk) and SR (collective risk) provides a comprehensive acceptance framework: IR protects individuals while SR protects communities from catastrophe.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** — `Option Set 2`
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Adapted]`

**"A process plant has a hazardous installation with the following characteristics: 30 workers are continuously exposed. The current individual fatality probability is $P_f = 6 \times 10^{-5}$ per year. A proposed safety interlock system costing ₹80 Lakh will reduce $P_f$ to $8 \times 10^{-6}$ per year. Assume asset life = 15 years, VSL = ₹8 Crore, and GDF = 10. Apply the ALARP cost-benefit test and determine whether the measure must be implemented. Also explain the three zones of risk acceptance."**

**(a) ALARP Cost-Benefit Calculation** — **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute initial annual expected fatalities:**
$$AF_{current} = N \times P_f = 30 \times 6 \times 10^{-5} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{fatalities/year}$$

**Step 2 — Compute reduced annual expected fatalities:**
$$AF_{reduced} = 30 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} = 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{fatalities/year}$$

**Step 3 — Compute fatalities averted per year:**
$$\Delta F = AF_{current} - AF_{reduced} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} - 2.4 \times 10^{-4} = 1.56 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute monetized benefit over 15-year asset life:**
$$B = \Delta F \times VSL \times T_{life}$$
$$B = 1.56 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^{7} \times 15$$
$$B = 1.56 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2 \times 10^{9} = 1.872 \times 10^{6} \, \text{₹ (₹1.872 Crore)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute gross-disproportion cost threshold:**
$$C_{threshold} = GDF \times B = 10 \times 1.872 \times 10^{6} = 1.872 \times 10^{7} \, \text{₹ (₹1.872 Crore)}$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

Actual cost = $C = 8 \times 10^{6}$ ₹ (₹80 Lakh)
Threshold = $C_{threshold} = 1.872 \times 10^{7}$ ₹

Since $C < C_{threshold}$, the cost is **NOT** grossly disproportionate.

$$\boxed{\text{Decision: ALARP REQUIRES implementation of the safety interlock system.}}$$

---

**(b) Three Zones of Risk Acceptance** — **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **HSE Tolerability of Risk Framework** divides the risk continuum into three distinct zones, each demanding a different managerial response:

**Zone 1 — Intolerable Region (Red Zone):**
Risk exceeds the **maximum tolerable individual risk** of $1 \times 10^{-4}$ per year. The activity is forbidden unless extraordinary circumstances justify temporary operation. Action: redesign, relocate, or abandon the activity.

**Zone 2 — Tolerable / ALARP Region (Yellow Zone):**
Risk lies between $1 \times 10^{-6}$ (broadly acceptable) and $1 \times 10^{-4}$ (intolerable) per year. Operation is permitted **only if ALARP is demonstrably achieved** — meaning all reasonably practicable measures have been applied. The cost-benefit test with GDF determines when further reduction ceases to be "reasonably practicable."

**Zone 3 — Broadly Acceptable Region (Green Zone):**
Risk is below $1 \times 10^{-6}$ per year — comparable to routine domestic risks. No further reduction is mandated, but routine monitoring must continue.

**Graphical Representation (mental diagram for exams):**

```
Log(IR)
  |                              _______
  |                            (RED ZONE - INTOLERABLE)
  |                            |
-4|----------------|-----------|--------------- (IR = 1e-4)
  |                |           |
  |         (YELLOW ALARP ZONE - TOLERABLE)
  |                |           |
-6|----------------|------------------------- (IR = 1e-6)
  |                |     
  |        (GREEN ZONE - BROADLY ACCEPTABLE)
  |______________
```

This framework converts subjective risk perception into objective, defensible, and legally compliant decisions.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 4 – Acceptance Criteria for Risk):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Acceptable" with "Zero Risk":** Students often write that "risk must be eliminated." This is wrong and costs 2-3 marks immediately. The correct answer is *risk reduced to an acceptable level*.
> 2. **Forgetting the GDF:** Many students skip the Gross Disproportion Factor in cost-benefit problems. Examiners expect to see GDF explicitly defined and applied.
> 3. **Mixing IR and SR:** Individual Risk and Societal Risk address different questions. Conflating them in F-N curve problems is a frequent error leading to 4-mark deductions.
> 4. **Wrong Threshold Values:** $1 \times 10^{-6}$ and $1 \times 10^{-4}$ are *must-memorize* values. Wrong values = direct 3-mark loss.
> 5. **Skipping Step-Wise Justification:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *step-wise marking*. Always present calculations in numbered steps with units. A single-line calculation without justification attracts only partial marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Acceptance Criteria for Risk** is the quantitative/qualitative benchmark used to classify estimated risk into acceptable, tolerable, or intolerable categories.
- The **HSE UK framework** defines three zones: Intolerable, ALARP-Tolerable, and Broadly Acceptable.
- **Individual Risk thresholds**: $1 \times 10^{-4}$/year (tolerable ceiling) and $1 \times 10^{-6}$/year (broadly acceptable).
- **Societal Risk** is represented by the **F-N curve** with the HSE limit line $F \times N = 10^{-2}$.
- **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the operative decision rule in the tolerable zone.
- **Gross Disproportion Factor (GDF)**: 2 for trivial, 10 standard, up to 100 for catastrophic risks.
- **Statistical Value of Life (VSL)**: ~₹8 Crore (India) or ~£1.8 Million (UK HSE).
- **Risk Matrix** is the qualitative tool: Low/Medium → Acceptable, High → Tolerable/ALARP, Very High/Extreme → Unacceptable.
- **ALARP hierarchy of controls**: Inherent Safety → Engineering Controls → Procedural Controls → PPE.
- The **monetized benefit** equation: $B = N \times \Delta P_f \times VSL \times T_{life}$.
- The **ALARP test**: $C_{measure} \leq GDF \times B_{risk\,reduction}$.
- F-N curve plotting requires **cumulative frequencies**, not individual event frequencies.
- Risk acceptance criteria are derived from **fatality statistics, regulatory mandates, and societal values** — not arbitrary engineering judgement.
- The KTU 2024 syllabus treats this topic as the **decision-making culmination** of QRA — always link acceptance criteria back to the risk estimation outputs of Module 3.
- **Key formulae to memorize**: $IR = \sum f_i p_{i,f}$, $F \times N^k = C$, $C \leq GDF \times B$, $B = N \cdot P_f \cdot VSL$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ALARP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ALARP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** is a risk management principle and legal obligation (originating from UK HSE – Health and Safety Executive, and adopted globally including India under Factories Act / OSHA frameworks) which mandates that the residual risk from any hazardous operation must be reduced to a level that is **"as low as it is reasonably practicable"** — meaning that the cost, time, and effort (termed "sacrifice") required to further reduce the risk must be **grossly disproportionate** to the safety benefit achieved.

In KTU 2024 terminology, ALARP is the **decision-making bridge zone** between the *Broadly Acceptable Risk Region* and the *Unacceptable Risk Region* on a typical Risk Tolerance / Tolerability Matrix.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are walking across a busy highway. The first option — taking an **overhead pedestrian bridge** — eliminates 100% of the risk but takes 5 extra minutes and ₹50 in fuel. The second option — **wearing a bright orange reflective jacket** — reduces 70% of the risk and costs you ₹200.

ALARP would *not* force you to build a flyover at every junction (because the cost is grossly disproportionate), but it **would** demand that you at least wear the jacket (because the cost is small and the risk reduction is significant). ALARP is essentially the engineering equivalent of the proverb: *"Don't throw a ₹1000 cushion to catch a ₹10 coin, but never leave a ₹10 coin on the floor when a free broom is available."*

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Risk**: Combination of the *probability* of an event occurring and the *severity* (consequence) of that event.
- **Sacrifice**: Money, time, effort, and trouble required to implement further risk reduction.
- **Gross Disproportion Test**: The qualitative / quantitative yardstick used to determine when the sacrifice becomes "too much" compared to the safety benefit.
- **Residual Risk**: The risk that remains *after* control measures are applied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** ALARP is the most frequently examined topic in Module 4 of PBSFT404, often appearing as a Part A (3-mark) definition question and as a Part B (14-mark) descriptive question integrating it with the **Tolerable Risk / Risk Acceptance criteria**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The ALARP Logic — Structured Bulleted Steps

1. **Step 1 — Hazard Identification:** Identify all hazards associated with the process, plant, or activity.
2. **Step 2 — Risk Estimation:** Quantify or qualitatively score the risk using the matrix
   $R = P \times C$ where *P* = Probability and *C* = Consequence.
3. **Step 3 — Risk Evaluation Against Tolerability Framework:** Place the estimated risk on the **Tolerability / FAME Triangle** (Fatality Accident / Frequency / Risk Reduction Measure Evaluation).
4. **Step 4 — Apply Control Hierarchy:** Implement the **Hierarchy of Controls** in order: *Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Control → Administrative Control → PPE*.
5. **Step 5 — Test for ALARP:** For every residual risk, ask — *"Is the sacrifice (cost) required to reduce it further grossly disproportionate to the benefit gained?"*
   - If **NO** → The risk is **NOT ALARP** → more controls are mandatory.
   - If **YES** → The risk is **ALARP** → process is permitted to operate with documented justification.
6. **Step 6 — Documentation & Review:** Maintain a *Live ALARP Register* and periodically review because technologies, costs, and societal expectations evolve.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | Risk Score | $R = P \times C$ | Probability × Consequence (semi-quantitative) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | FAR (Fatal Accident Rate) | $FAR = \dfrac{\text{No. of Fatalities} \times 10^{8}}{\text{Total Person-Hours Worked}}$ | Fatalities per 100 million hours | deaths / 10⁸ hrs |
| 3 | Fatality Frequency (F/N) | $F/N \text{ curve}$ | Plot of cumulative frequency (F) vs. Number of fatalities (N) | – |
| 4 | Gross Disproportion Test | $\dfrac{\text{Sacrifice (Cost, Time, Trouble)}}{\text{Benefit (Risk Reduction)}} \gg 1$ | If ratio >> 1, ALARP is satisfied | Ratio |
| 5 | ALARP Cost Ceiling | Cost per life saved $\le$ Societal Willingness To Pay (SWTP) | WTP for India ≈ ₹1 – 5 Crore (refer MoEFCC) | ₹ / life saved |
| 6 | Implied Cost of Averting a Fatality (ICAF) | $ICAF = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Safety Measure}}{\text{Statistical Lives Saved}}$ | Comparable with SWTP | ₹ / life |
| 7 | Risk Reduction | $RR = 1 - \dfrac{R_{residual}}{R_{initial}}$ | Effectiveness of the control measure | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 8 | ALARP Lower Bound | $R_{broadly\,acceptable}$ | Upper limit of acceptable zone | – |
| 9 | ALARP Upper Bound | $R_{unacceptable}$ | Lower limit of intolerable zone | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The *Gross Disproportion Test* is the single most important concept. In KTU valuation, you must clearly state that ALARP is **not** a "zero risk" or "absolute safety" principle — it is a *reasonableness* principle.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

ALARP is the backbone of:
- **Offshore oil & gas** (HSE, UK)
- **Nuclear safety** (UK ONR, US NRC)
- **Chemical process industry** (COMAH Regulations)
- **Railway signalling** (RDSO, India)
- **Construction safety** (BIS / IS 3786)

In Indian context, the **Factories Act 1948 (Amended 1987)** and the **Public Liability Insurance Act 1991** implicitly apply ALARP through "reasonably practicable" language.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Risk Score to ALARP Decision

We begin with the **basic risk equation** and apply the ALARP filter.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{initial}} &= P_{\text{initial}} \times C_{\text{initial}} \\
\\
\text{After applying control measure (i):} \quad R_{i} &= P_{i} \times C_{i} \\
\\
\text{Incremental Risk Reduction:} \quad \Delta R_{i} &= R_{\text{initial}} - R_{i} \\
\\
\text{Define Cost-Benefit Ratio (CBR):} \quad CBR_{i} &= \frac{S_{i}}{\Delta R_{i}} \\
\\
\text{Apply Gross Disproportion Test:} \quad 
CBR_{i} &\gg 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_{i} \text{ is ALARP} \\
CBR_{i} &\ll 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_{i} \text{ is NOT ALARP — implement measure}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $S_{i}$ is the *sacrifice* (in monetary units, normalised to ₹/life saved) for measure $i$. If the safety benefit is small but the cost is huge, the ratio becomes very large — meaning the measure is grossly disproportionate, so the risk qualifies as ALARP.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** A chemical reactor has an initial fatality risk of $P = 10^{-2}$ per year and consequence severity $C = 5$ (on a 1–5 scale). Two additional control measures are proposed:
> - **Option A:** Cost = ₹20,00,000, reduces probability to $10^{-3}$/yr.
> - **Option B:** Cost = ₹80,00,000, reduces probability to $10^{-4}$/yr.
>
> Determine which option satisfies ALARP, given the Societal Willingness to Pay (SWTP) is **₹1 Crore per life saved**, and the plant employs 50 workers on shift.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{initial}} &= 10^{-2} \times 5 = 0.05 \;\;\text{(risk units)} \\
\\
R_{A} &= 10^{-3} \times 5 = 0.005 \\
R_{B} &= 10^{-4} \times 5 = 0.0005 \\
\\
\Delta R_{A} &= 0.05 - 0.005 = 0.045 \\
\Delta R_{B} &= 0.05 - 0.0005 = 0.0495 \\
\\
\text{Statistical lives saved per year (Option A):} \quad L_{A} &= \Delta R_{A} \times 50 = 2.25 \\
\text{Statistical lives saved per year (Option B):} \quad L_{B} &= \Delta R_{B} \times 50 = 2.475 \\
\\
ICAF_{A} &= \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{2.25} = 8{,}88{,}889 \;\text{₹/life} \\
ICAF_{B} &= \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{2.475} = 3{,}23{,}23{,}232 \;\text{₹/life} \\
\\
\text{Compare with SWTP} = 1\,\text{Crore} = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹} \\
\\
ICAF_{A} &= 8.89\,\text{Lakh} \;\ll\; 1\,\text{Crore} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{NOT ALARP — MUST IMPLEMENT} \\
ICAF_{B} &= 3.23\,\text{Crore} \;\gg\; 1\,\text{Crore} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{ALARP — NOT MANDATORY}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical ALARP Matrix (5×5)

| Risk Score $P \times C$ | Zone | ALARP Status | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R \le 0.05$ | **Broadly Acceptable** | Below ALARP | Routine monitoring, no further controls |
| $0.05 < R \le 0.5$ | **Tolerable (ALARP Zone)** | ALARP applicable | Document cost-benefit, implement reasonable controls |
| $0.5 < R \le 2.5$ | **Significant** | Not ALARP | Mandatory engineering controls + review |
| $R > 2.5$ | **Unacceptable** | NOT ALARP | Halt operation / process redesign |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of Inequality (No `|` Pipes):** The inequality $0.05 \le R \le 0.5$ means that the risk score is greater than or equal to 0.05 **AND** less than or equal to 0.5 — i.e., the **closed interval** between the two bounds. Avoid using absolute-value bars `|x|` inside tables; instead write them as `the absolute value of x` or use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ALARP Position in the Risk Tolerability Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hazard Identified] --> B[Estimate Risk R = P x C]
    B --> C{R Level?}
    C -->|"R very low"| D[Region 1: Broadly Acceptable]
    C -->|"R moderate"| E[Region 2: ALARP Zone / Tolerable]
    C -->|"R high"| F[Region 3: Unacceptable / Intolerable]
    D --> G[No further action. Routine monitoring.]
    E --> H[Apply Cost-Benefit Analysis]
    H --> I{CBR vs Gross Disproportion Test}
    I -->|"Sacrifice > Benefit (Disproportionate)"| J[Risk is ALARP. Document and Operate.]
    I -->|"Sacrifice < Benefit (Proportionate)"| K[Implement additional control measures]
    K --> B
    F --> L[STOP / Redesign / Substitute Process]
    L --> B
    J --> M[Periodic Review and Reassessment]
    G --> M
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 ALARP Decision Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Input: Identified Hazard] --> PROC1[Processor 1: Risk Quantification Module]
    PROC1 --> PROC2[Processor 2: Cost Estimator for Controls]
    PROC2 --> PROC3[Processor 3: ICAF Calculator]
    PROC3 --> DEC{Decision Gate: ALARP Comparator}
    DEC -->|"ICAF > SWTP"| OUT1[Output 1: ALARP Confirmed - Documented Risk File]
    DEC -->|"ICAF < SWTP"| OUT2[Output 2: Additional Control Mandated]
    OUT2 --> PROC1
    OUT1 --> REV[Live ALARP Register and Annual Review]
    REV --> AUD[External Audit by Safety Authority]
```

### 4.3 The FAME Triangle (Qualitative ALARP Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ST1[Region A: Intolerable Risk]
    INT1[Must redesign or stop activity]
    end
    subgraph ST2[Region B: ALARP / Tolerable Risk]
    ALR1[Implement reasonably practicable controls]
    ALR2[Document Gross Disproportion Test]
    end
    subgraph ST3[Region C: Broadly Acceptable Risk]
    ACC1[Routine maintenance sufficient]
    end
    ST1 -->|Reduce risk| ST2
    ST2 -->|Further risk reduction| ST3
    INT1 -.->|Risk at this level| DEC{Decision Required}
    ALR1 -.-> DEC
    ACC1 -.-> DEC
    DEC -->|Choose action| A[Proceed / Halt / Re-Engineer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the FAME Triangle:** The top zone is *unacceptable* — the process cannot run. The middle zone is the **ALARP band** — the process can run only if further risk reduction is grossly disproportionate to the cost. The bottom zone is *broadly acceptable* — no further action is needed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define ALARP. Mention any two of its application areas.*

**Model Answer:**
ALARP stands for *As Low As Reasonably Practicable*. It is a risk management principle that requires the residual risk of an activity to be reduced to a level where the further cost, time, and trouble (sacrifice) of additional safety measures would be **grossly disproportionate** to the safety benefit obtained. ALARP is **not** "zero risk" but "reasonable risk."
Two application areas:
1. **Offshore oil and gas industry** (UK HSE, Indian DGMS).
2. **Process safety management** in chemical and petrochemical plants under the Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC), 1989.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between ALARP and the "As Low As Reasonably Achievable" (ALARA) principle.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | ALARP | ALARA |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | UK HSE / Common Law | US NRC (Nuclear) / ICRP |
| Basis | Cost-Benefit + Gross Disproportion | Dose minimisation principle |
| Application | Industrial / Process / Construction | Radiation protection |
| Decision Driver | Practicability (cost-benefit) | Achievability (technology-limited) |
| Threshold | Sacrifice "grossly disproportionate" to benefit | "Readily achievable" |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Pattern):** Answer **any one** of the following. Each carries 14 marks and is divided into sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the ALARP framework in detail. How does the Gross Disproportion Test work in determining whether a risk is at the ALARP level?**

**Model Solution:**

The ALARP framework consists of three regions on a Risk Tolerability Triangle:
1. **Broadly Acceptable Region:** Risk is negligible and no further action is needed.
2. **Tolerable / ALARP Region:** Risk must be reduced "as low as reasonably practicable."
3. **Intolerable Region:** Risk cannot be accepted under any circumstances.

**Gross Disproportion Test (GDT):** The GDT is the qualitative-quantitative filter that determines whether additional risk reduction is "reasonably practicable." The procedure is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Identify the proposed control measure } M_{i} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Compute the sacrifice } S_{i} = \text{Cost}_{i} + \text{Time}_{i} + \text{Trouble}_{i} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Compute the risk reduction benefit } \Delta R_{i} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Compute } ICAF_{i} = \dfrac{S_{i}}{\text{Statistical lives saved}} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Compare } ICAF_{i} \text{ with SWTP:} \\
& \text{If } ICAF_{i} \gg SWTP \rightarrow \text{ALARP is satisfied (no further measure)} \\
& \text{If } ICAF_{i} \ll SWTP \rightarrow \text{ALARP is NOT satisfied (implement measure)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The GDT is a *judgement-based* test, not purely numerical; the engineer weighs societal expectations, statutory duties, and good engineering practice. The word "grossly" implies a multiplier — typically, a sacrifice that is **2 to 10 times** the benefit is considered disproportionate. *(7 marks: 3 for framework + 4 for GDT explanation with formula)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A chlor-alkali plant has a residual fatality risk of $2 \times 10^{-3}$ per year per worker after installing a basic scrubber. Management proposes a tertiary scrubber system costing ₹3 Crore, which would further reduce the probability to $5 \times 10^{-5}$ per year. The plant has 80 workers. Using ICAF and an SWTP of ₹1.5 Crore per life saved, determine whether the tertiary scrubber must be installed under the ALARP principle.**

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{initial}} &= 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ / yr} \\
P_{\text{proposed}} &= 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ / yr} \\
\Delta P &= (2 \times 10^{-3}) - (5 \times 10^{-5}) = 1.95 \times 10^{-3} \text{ / yr} \\
\\
\text{Statistical lives saved per year} &= \Delta P \times 80 = 1.95 \times 10^{-3} \times 80 = 0.156 \\
\\
ICAF &= \frac{3\,\text{Crore}}{0.156} = 19.23\,\text{Crore per life saved} \\
\\
\text{SWTP} &= 1.5\,\text{Crore per life saved} \\
\\
ICAF = 19.23\,\text{Crore} \;\gg\; SWTP = 1.5\,\text{Crore} \\
\\
\Rightarrow \text{By Gross Disproportion Test, the sacrifice (₹3 Crore)} \\
\text{is grossly disproportionate to the benefit (0.156 lives/yr).} \\
\\
\textbf{Conclusion: The risk is at the ALARP level. The tertiary scrubber is NOT mandatory,} \\
\textbf{but it should be considered under good-practice guidelines.}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for setup + 3 for calculation + 2 for conclusion with ALARP interpretation)*

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

**(a) [7 Marks] Discuss the role of the Hierarchy of Controls in achieving ALARP. How does it interact with the Gross Disproportion Test?**

**Model Solution:**

The **Hierarchy of Controls** is a layered system of risk reduction measures ranked by effectiveness and reliability:

1. **Elimination:** Physically remove the hazard (most effective, highest reliability).
2. **Substitution:** Replace with a less hazardous material or process.
3. **Engineering Controls:** Design safer systems (e.g., relief valves, scrubbers, interlocks).
4. **Administrative Controls:** Training, SOPs, permit-to-work systems.
5. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Last resort, lowest reliability.

**Interaction with GDT:** The GDT is applied **after** every feasible control in the hierarchy is considered. The engineer must justify skipping any higher-tier control by demonstrating that the sacrifice is grossly disproportionate. For example, if *elimination* is feasible at ₹10 lakh, it almost always beats *PPE* at ₹50,000 — because the higher tier offers greater reliability and lower long-term risk. *(7 marks: 4 for hierarchy explanation + 3 for GDT interaction)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] "ALARP is not a number — it is a judgement." Critically evaluate this statement with reference to the FAME triangle and societal willingness to pay.**

**Model Solution:**

This statement is true in practice. While numerical techniques like ICAF and SWTP give a *quantitative envelope*, ALARP is fundamentally a *judgement call* because:

- **Societal WTP is dynamic:** SWTP changes with time, location, and incident history (e.g., post-Bhopal, India tolerates less chemical risk).
- **Quantitative limits are absent for many hazards:** For chronic health effects and environmental damage, fatality equivalents are difficult to compute.
- **Engineering judgement matters:** A unique design constraint may justify a higher cost per life saved.
- **FAME triangle integration:** The triangle is qualitative — it defines *regions* (Acceptable / Tolerable / Intolerable), not absolute cut-offs. Engineers use the FAME (Fatalities, Accident frequency, Measure Effectiveness) logic qualitatively.

**Conclusion:** ALARP is best viewed as a *risk governance philosophy* that combines numbers with engineering and societal judgement. The judgement element is what distinguishes it from rigid deterministic standards. *(7 marks: 3 for statement justification + 2 for FAME integration + 2 for critical conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse ALARP with "Zero Risk."** Many students incorrectly state that ALARP means eliminating all risk. It does NOT. *(–2 marks typical deduction)*
> 2. **Do NOT omit the "Gross" in Gross Disproportion Test.** Writing simply "cost-benefit analysis" loses 1 mark. The word "grossly" is non-negotiable.
> 3. **Always show the ICAF calculation in numerical problems.** Even a one-line mention of the comparison with SWTP is mandatory.
> 4. **Remember: ALARP is a *region*, not a *number*.** Do not give a single numerical threshold for ALARP — describe it as a *band* between the broadly acceptable and unacceptable regions.
> 5. **Confusing ALARP with ALARA.** ALARP comes from UK common law / HSE; ALARA comes from US nuclear / ICRP — distinct origins and applications.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **ALARP = As Low As Reasonably Practicable** — a *reasonableness* principle, not a zero-risk principle.
- ✅ Originated from **UK HSE** and reinforced by the **Factories Act 1948** (India) and the **MSIHC Rules 1989**.
- ✅ Operates in the **middle band** of the *Tolerability / FAME Triangle* — between Broadly Acceptable and Unacceptable.
- ✅ **Gross Disproportion Test:** $ICAF \gg SWTP$ ⇒ ALARP satisfied; $ICAF \ll SWTP$ ⇒ must implement.
- ✅ Always apply the **Hierarchy of Controls** before declaring ALARP — Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- ✅ Document everything in a **Live ALARP Register** for legal defence and periodic review.
- ✅ Common exam synonyms: *reasonable practicability, tolerable risk, cost-benefit test, safety integrity level* — know the differences.
- ✅ In KTU valuation: always **show calculations, justify the "grossly" qualifier, and link back to the FAME triangle**.
- ✅ India SWTP benchmark: **₹1 – 5 Crore per statistical life saved** (MoEFCC / CPCB / National Disaster Management guidelines).
- ✅ ALARP must be **demonstrated, not assumed** — the burden of proof lies with the operator/employer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Presentation of Measures of Risk - Risk Contour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Presentation of Measures of Risk - Risk Contour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Risk Contour** (also called an **Iso-Risk Line** or **Risk Isopleth**) is a graphical representation of risk measures plotted as closed isolines on a geographical map surrounding a hazardous installation. Each contour line connects all geographical locations that experience the **same level of risk** arising from the facility under study. Risk contours are the most widely accepted **spatial visualization tool** for communicating quantified risk to regulators, planners, and the public, and they form the technical backbone of **Land-Use Planning (LUP)** decisions around major hazard installations (MHIs).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *A risk contour is a line on a map joining points of equal individual risk or equal societal risk, derived by super-imposing the consequence and frequency data of all identified accident scenarios at a given hazardous facility.*

There are two principal categories used in KTU-style problems:

| # | Risk Contour Type | Symbol/Unit | What is Plotted |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Individual Risk Contour (IRC)** | Risk per year (yr⁻¹) | Probability of fatality of an average unprotected person at a location |
| 2 | **Societal Risk Contour (SRC)** | Frequency vs. N (F–N plot) | Cumulative frequency of accidents causing ≥ N fatalities |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Elevation Map of Danger"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine standing at the centre of a dangerous chemical plant. Risk behaves exactly like the *height of a mountain* — it is **highest at the source** (the peak), and as you walk **away from it**, the height (risk) **decreases gradually** until it reaches sea-level (negligible risk). If you draw lines connecting all points that are at *exactly the same elevation*, you get a **topographic contour map**. In risk assessment, those lines are called **Risk Contours** — except here, instead of metres of altitude, the "altitude" is *probability of death per year*.

- **Centre of plant** = peak risk (e.g., 1 × 10⁻³ per year)
- **Far away** = low/zero risk (e.g., 1 × 10⁻⁷ per year)
- **Each contour ring** = a "risk-free boundary" inside which people are exposed to **at least** that level of risk

## 1.3 Standard Risk Metrics Used on Contours

- **IR(x, y)** = Individual Risk at geographic coordinate (x, y) — units: **yr⁻¹**
- **FN curve** = Societal Risk expressed as cumulative Frequency (F) vs. Number of fatalities (N)
- **Acceptability thresholds (UK HSE / KTU accepted norms):**
  - **1 × 10⁻⁵ yr⁻¹** = "Intolerable to public" inner boundary
  - **1 × 10⁻⁶ yr⁻¹** = "Tolerable if ALARP" boundary
  - **1 × 10⁻⁷ yr⁻¹** = "Broadly acceptable" outer boundary

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Superposition Principle

A risk contour is **not measured** — it is **calculated** by superposing (adding up) the contribution of *every possible accident scenario* at every point around the plant.

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. **Hazard Identification (HAZID)** → Enumerate accident scenarios (jet fire, pool fire, VCE, BLEVE, toxic release).
2. **Frequency Analysis** → Assign an annual frequency **fᵢ** to each scenario i (events/yr).
3. **Consequence Modelling** → Calculate the dangerous dose (thermal radiation, overpressure, toxic concentration) at distance **r** from the release point.
4. **Vulnerability Modelling** → Convert the dangerous dose into a **probability of fatality** Pᵢ(x, y) using the **Probit Equation**.
5. **Superposition** → Sum the contribution of all scenarios:
$$\text{IR}(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \cdot P_i(x, y)$$
6. **Isoline Generation** → Mark points of equal IR(x, y) and join them to form the contour.
7. **Presentation** → Overlay the contours on a site map.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Superposition:** Two unrelated accidents (e.g., a jet fire and a toxic release) can both reach the same house. The house's *total* individual risk is the **sum** of the risks from each accident because the events are statistically independent.

## 2.2 The Probit Equation (Vulnerability Link)

The Probit variable **Y** links a physical dose to a fatality probability **P**:

$$Y = a + b \ln(V)$$

where **V** is the "intensity term" (e.g., thermal dose, overpressure impulse, toxic load).

Conversion from Y to P:

$$P = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} \exp\!\left(-\frac{u^{2}}{2}\right) du \;=\; 0.5\!\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

### 2.2.1 Standard Probit Coefficients (High-Yield for KTU)

| Hazard Type | Probit Equation (Y = a + b·ln(Dose)) | a | b | Dose V |
|-------------|--------------------------------------|---|---|--------|
| Thermal Radiation (lethality) | $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(Q^{4/3} \cdot t)$ | -14.9 | 2.56 | $Q^{4/3} t$ (kW/m²)⁴ᐟ³·s |
| Overpressure (lung rupture) | $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P^{0})$ | -77.1 | 6.91 | Overpressure P⁰ (Pa) |
| Overpressure (whole body) | $Y = 5.13 + 1.39 \ln(P^{0})$ | 5.13 | 1.39 | P⁰ (bar) |
| Toxic (chlorine) | $Y = -10.6 + 1.85 \ln(C^{1.5} \cdot t)$ | -10.6 | 1.85 | $C^{1.5} t$ (ppm¹·⁵·min) |
| Toxic (ammonia) | $Y = -30.6 + 1.85 \ln(C^{2} \cdot t)$ | -30.6 | 1.85 | $C^{2} t$ (ppm²·min) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these four equations — they cover > 80 % of any numerical KTU question on risk contours.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Individual Risk at (x,y) | $\text{IR}(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \, P_i(x, y)$ | fᵢ = yr⁻¹; Pᵢ = dimensionless [0,1]; IR = yr⁻¹ | Sum over **all** scenarios |
| 2 | Probit → Fatality Prob. | $P = 0.5\bigl[1 + \text{erf}\bigl(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\bigr)\bigr]$ | Y = probit; P = fraction | P ∈ [0, 1] |
| 3 | Cumulative Societal Risk | $F(N) = \sum_{i \,:\, n_i \ge N} f_i$ | F = yr⁻¹; N = fatalities | Monotonically decreasing |
| 4 | Expected Fatalities | $E(N) = \sum_{i} f_i \, n_i$ | yr⁻¹·persons | Used in cost-benefit |
| 5 | Thermal Probit | $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(Q^{4/3} t)$ | Q in kW/m²; t in s | TNO / Purple Book |
| 6 | Overpressure Probit | $Y = 5.13 + 1.39 \ln(P^{0})$ | P⁰ in bar | Whole-body lethality |
| 7 | Toxic (Cl₂) Probit | $Y = -10.6 + 1.85 \ln(C^{1.5} t)$ | C in ppm; t in min | Withers / Factories |
| 8 | Risk Integral (risk to pop.) | $R_{\text{pop}} = \iint \text{IR}(x,y) \, \rho(x,y) \, dx\,dy$ | ρ = population density (persons/km²) | Used for societal risk |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Land-Use Planning (LUP):** Decide buffer zones around refineries/LNG terminals.
- **Emergency Planning:** Define evacuation rings based on IR contour bands.
- **Insurance & ALARP Demonstration:** Show that risk has been reduced to "As Low As Reasonably Practicable".
- **Regulatory Submissions:** Mandatory in many jurisdictions (e.g., UK HSE, Dutch RIVM, Indian Petroleum & Explosives Safety Organisation — PESO).
- **Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA):** Expected loss = f × n × value-of-statistical-life.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Probit → Probability Conversion

**Problem Setup:** A thermal dose gives a probit value **Y = 6.0**. Convert it to fatality probability P.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Standardize the probit variable.**
> The probit model assumes Y is normally distributed with mean **5** and variance **1**. We therefore compute the standardized deviate:
>
> $$Z = Y - 5$$

$$Z = 6.0 - 5 = 1.0$$

> **Step 2 — Apply the error-function relationship.**
> The probability of fatality P is the area under the standard normal curve up to Z:
>
> $$P = 0.5 \left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{Z}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

$$P = 0.5\left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{1.0}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

> **Step 3 — Evaluate the argument.**

$$\frac{Z}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1.0}{1.4142} = 0.7071$$

> **Step 4 — Use the standard erf table / approximation.**
>
> $\text{erf}(0.7071) \approx 0.6827$

> **Step 5 — Compute P.**

$$P = 0.5 (1 + 0.6827) = 0.5 \times 1.6827 = 0.8414$$

> **Conclusion:** A probit of 6 corresponds to a **84.14 % fatality probability** — a critical "lethal dose" benchmark.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Individual Risk at a Location (2-Scenario Plant)

**Problem Setup:** A small LPG installation has two credible scenarios:

| Scenario i | Description | Frequency fᵢ (yr⁻¹) | Fatality Prob. at House A (Pᵢ) |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Jet Fire | 5 × 10⁻⁴ | 0.20 |
| 2 | Vapour Cloud Explosion | 1 × 10⁻⁵ | 0.85 |

Calculate the **Total Individual Risk at House A** and identify its position on the UK HSE risk-tolerance ladder.

> **Step 1 — Write the superposition equation.**
>
> $$\text{IR}_{\text{A}} = \sum_{i=1}^{2} f_i \cdot P_i = f_1 P_1 + f_2 P_2$$

> **Step 2 — Substitute Scenario 1 (Jet Fire).**

$$f_1 P_1 = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (0.20) = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{yr}^{-1}$$

> **Step 3 — Substitute Scenario 2 (VCE).**

$$f_2 P_2 = (1 \times 10^{-5}) \times (0.85) = 8.5 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{yr}^{-1}$$

> **Step 4 — Sum the contributions.**

$$\text{IR}_{\text{A}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 8.5 \times 10^{-6} = 1.085 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{yr}^{-1}$$

> **Step 5 — Compare against HSE risk-tolerance limits.**
>
> 1 × 10⁻⁵ ≤ IR < 1 × 10⁻⁴ → **TOLERABLE zone (ALARP must be demonstrated)**
> 1 × 10⁻⁴ ≤ IR ≤ 1 × 10⁻³ → **Just above intolerable public limit**

**Result:** IR_A = **1.085 × 10⁻⁴ yr⁻¹** — House A lies just **above** the "intolerable to public" boundary. Action: **relocate house or add mitigation**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation — Societal Risk (F–N Curve Construction)

**Problem Setup:** A refinery has 5 accident outcomes tabulated below. Construct the cumulative F–N curve points (N, F(N)) where F(N) is the sum of all frequencies of accidents causing **≥ N** fatalities.

| Outcome j | Frequency fⱼ (yr⁻¹) | Fatalities nⱼ |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------|
| A | 1 × 10⁻³ | 1 |
| B | 5 × 10⁻⁴ | 3 |
| C | 1 × 10⁻⁴ | 10 |
| D | 1 × 10⁻⁵ | 50 |
| E | 1 × 10⁻⁶ | 200 |

> **Step 1 — Sort in descending order of N (ascending in F).**
> Already sorted above.

> **Step 2 — Compute F(N) at each N by summing fⱼ where nⱼ ≥ N.**

For N = 1: $\;F(1) = f_A + f_B + f_C + f_D + f_E$
$$F(1) = 1 \times 10^{-3} + 5 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$F(1) = 1.611 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{yr}^{-1}$$

For N = 3: $\;F(3) = f_B + f_C + f_D + f_E$
$$F(3) = 5 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-6} = 6.11 \times 10^{-4}$$

For N = 10: $\;F(10) = f_C + f_D + f_E$
$$F(10) = 1 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-6} = 1.11 \times 10^{-4}$$

For N = 50: $\;F(50) = f_D + f_E$
$$F(50) = 1 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-6} = 1.1 \times 10^{-5}$$

For N = 200: $\;F(200) = f_E = 1 \times 10^{-6}$

> **Step 3 — Plot (N, F(N)) on log-log paper** — this is the F–N curve.

| N (fatalities) | F(N) (yr⁻¹) |
|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | 1.611 × 10⁻³ |
| 3 | 6.110 × 10⁻⁴ |
| 10 | 1.110 × 10⁻⁴ |
| 50 | 1.100 × 10⁻⁵ |
| 200 | 1.000 × 10⁻⁶ |

> **Step 4 — Slope check (Power-law form).**
>
> Many regulatory F–N curves follow: $F \cdot N^{k} = C$, where **k** = aversion-to-large-accidents index (k ≈ 1 ⇒ neutral; k = 2 ⇒ UK HSE norm).
> Slope on log-log plot = −k.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A plant's F–N curve must lie **below** the regulatory limit line to be acceptable. If your curve crosses it, **mitigation or relocation** is mandatory.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Risk Contour Generation Pipeline (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid flowchart shows the engineering pipeline used to compute and present risk contours — a frequent KTU 14-mark structural question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HAZID: Enumerate accident scenarios] --> B[Frequency Analysis: assign f_i per year]
    B --> C[Source / Release Modelling: outflow, evaporation]
    C --> D[Dispersion / Fire / Explosion Models]
    D --> E[Dose at each grid point x, y: thermal, overpressure, toxic]
    E --> F[Probit Equation: convert dose to fatality probability P_i]
    F --> G[Superposition: IR x y equals sum f_i times P_i x y]
    G --> H{Grid IR x y computed?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Plot iso-risk lines using contouring algorithm]
    I --> J[Overlay on site / topo map]
    H -- No --> E
    J --> K[Compare with HSE 1e-5, 1e-6, 1e-7 limits]
    K --> L[Land Use Planning / ALARP Decision]
```

## 4.2 Layout of a Typical Individual Risk Contour Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Plant[HAZARDOUS PLANT]
        P((Plant Centre))
    end
    P --- R1((Risk 1e-3/yr — INTOLERABLE inner zone))
    R1 --- R2((Risk 1e-4/yr — Inner boundary))
    R2 --- R3((Risk 1e-5/yr — ALARP boundary))
    R3 --- R4((Risk 1e-6/yr — Outer ALARP boundary))
    R4 --- R5((Risk 1e-7/yr — Broadly acceptable limit))
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Difference: Individual Risk vs Societal Risk Contour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Individual Risk Contour] --> S1a[Plots IR x y on map as iso-lines]
    S1a --> S1b[Unit: probability per year]
    S1 --> S1c[Does NOT depend on population density]

    S2[Societal Risk F-N Curve] --> S2a[Plots cumulative F vs N on log-log axes]
    S2a --> S2b[Unit: events per year vs fatalities]
    S2 --> S2c[Strongly depends on population density]
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic When a Contour Crosses a Receptor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is receptor inside 1e-5 contour?} -->|Yes| A1[INTOLERABLE — relocate or shutdown]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Inside 1e-6 contour?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2[ALARP zone — apply cost-benefit mitigation]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Inside 1e-7 contour?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| A3[Broadly acceptable — normal monitoring]
    Q3 -->|No| A4[Negligible — no action]
```
