# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a "Risk Contour". Mention its two main types.

> **[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**
>
> A **risk contour** is a line drawn on a geographical map that joins all points of equal level of risk (individual or societal) arising from a hazardous facility. It is generated by superimposing the consequence and frequency data of all credible accident scenarios and converting the dangerous dose at each location into a fatality probability using the probit model.
>
> **Two main types** *(1 Mark each)*:
> 1. **Individual Risk Contour (IRC)** — plots the probability of fatality of an unprotected person continuously present at a location (units: yr⁻¹).
> 2. **Societal Risk Contour / F–N Curve** — plots the cumulative frequency (F) of accidents causing N or more fatalities, on log–log axes.

---

### Q2. List the four UK HSE individual risk tolerance limits and explain the ALARP principle.

> **[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**
>
> **HSE individual risk limits** *(1 Mark)*:
> 1. **≥ 1 × 10⁻⁴ yr⁻¹** → **Intolerable** to any worker or public
> 2. **1 × 10⁻⁵ to 1 × 10⁻⁴ yr⁻¹** → **Tolerable only if ALARP demonstrated**
> 3. **1 × 10⁻⁶ to 1 × 10⁻⁵ yr⁻¹** → **Tolerable if ALARP demonstrated**
> 4. **≤ 1 × 10⁻⁶ yr⁻¹** → **Broadly acceptable**
>
> **ALARP** *(2 Marks)*: "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" — a risk-management principle requiring that operators reduce risk further unless the **cost (in money, time, effort)** of additional mitigation is **grossly disproportionate** to the safety benefit achieved. It applies in the zone between broadly acceptable and intolerable limits.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> *(1) Students commonly lose marks by forgetting to multiply fᵢ by Pᵢ — the **frequency alone** is not the risk. (2) The probit equation and conversion to probability are mandatory steps — skipping them costs 2 marks instantly. (3) Always state units of IR (yr⁻¹). (4) Always overlay contour interpretation (ALARP/Intolerable) in the conclusion — without it, examiners deduct a full mark.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Individual Risk Contour Computation*

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* A petrochemical complex has three credible accident scenarios. The fatality probabilities at a residential colony located 500 m downwind are tabulated below. Compute the **Total Individual Risk (IR)** at the colony and comment on its acceptability using UK HSE limits.

| Scenario | Description | Frequency fᵢ (yr⁻¹) | Fatality Probability at colony Pᵢ |
|----------|-------------|---------------------|----------------------------------|
| S1 | Pool fire | 8 × 10⁻⁴ | 0.10 |
| S2 | VCE (Vapour Cloud Explosion) | 3 × 10⁻⁵ | 0.50 |
| S3 | Toxic chlorine release | 5 × 10⁻⁵ | 0.60 |

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* The thermal radiation intensity at a distance of 200 m from a BLEVE is **Q = 25 kW/m²** for a duration of **t = 20 s**. Calculate the **probit Y** and the **probability of fatality** at that location. Use the thermal probit equation $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(Q^{4/3} t)$ and $P = 0.5\bigl[1 + \text{erf}\bigl(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\bigr)\bigr]$. State whether the location falls inside the lethal zone.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Step-by-step** *(7 marks distributed: 1 mark formula, 4 marks computation, 2 marks conclusion)*
>
> Step 1 — Apply superposition:
> $$\text{IR}_{\text{colony}} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} f_i \, P_i = f_1 P_1 + f_2 P_2 + f_3 P_3$$
>
> Step 2 — Pool Fire contribution:
> $$f_1 P_1 = (8 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.10 = 8.0 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{yr}^{-1}$$
>
> Step 3 — VCE contribution:
> $$f_2 P_2 = (3 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.50 = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{yr}^{-1}$$
>
> Step 4 — Toxic release contribution:
> $$f_3 P_3 = (5 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.60 = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{yr}^{-1}$$
>
> Step 5 — Sum:
> $$\text{IR}_{\text{colony}} = 8.0 \times 10^{-5} + 1.5 \times 10^{-5} + 3.0 \times 10^{-5} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{yr}^{-1}$$
>
> Step 6 — HSE comparison:
> $1.25 \times 10^{-4} > 1 \times 10^{-4}$ ⇒ **INTOLERABLE for public exposure.**
> **Conclusion** *(2 marks)*: The colony is in the **intolerable zone**. Recommended actions: (i) increase separation distance to ≥ 1 km, (ii) install mitigation (water spray, blast walls), (iii) emergency planning / shelter-in-place.
>
> ---
>
> **(b) Step-by-step** *(7 marks: 2 marks probit formula, 2 marks numeric substitution, 2 marks erf/P calculation, 1 mark conclusion)*
>
> Step 1 — Compute the dose argument $V = Q^{4/3} \cdot t$:
> $$Q^{4/3} = 25^{4/3} = 25^{1.333} = 25 \times 25^{0.333} = 25 \times 2.924 = 73.1 \; (\text{kW/m}^{2})^{4/3}$$
>
> Step 2 — Multiply by t = 20 s:
> $$V = 73.1 \times 20 = 1462$$
>
> Step 3 — Take natural logarithm:
> $$\ln(1462) = 7.288$$
>
> Step 4 — Compute probit Y:
> $$Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \times 7.288 = -14.9 + 18.66 = 3.76$$
>
> Step 5 — Compute (Y − 5)/√2:
> $$\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3.76 - 5}{1.4142} = \frac{-1.24}{1.4142} = -0.877$$
>
> Step 6 — Read erf(−0.877) ≈ −0.786 (from standard normal/error tables).
>
> Step 7 — Compute P:
> $$P = 0.5\bigl[1 + (-0.786)\bigr] = 0.5 \times 0.214 = 0.107$$
>
> Step 8 — Conclusion: **P ≈ 10.7 % fatality probability** — the location is **NOT yet in the lethal zone**, but mitigation (water deluge, protective clothing, evacuation route) is recommended since P > 1 % is a regulatory trigger.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Societal Risk F–N Curve*

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and interpretation of a **Societal Risk F–N curve**. Discuss why the y-axis and x-axis are plotted on **logarithmic scales**.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* A chemical plant has the following accident outcome data. Plot the cumulative F–N points and determine the slope of the best-fit straight line on a log–log chart. Comment on whether the slope satisfies the UK HSE aversion criterion (k ≈ 1 to 2).

| Outcome | fⱼ (yr⁻¹) | nⱼ (fatalities) |
|---------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | 1 × 10⁻² | 1 |
| 2 | 1 × 10⁻³ | 5 |
| 3 | 1 × 10⁻⁴ | 25 |
| 4 | 1 × 10⁻⁵ | 100 |
| 5 | 1 × 10⁻⁶ | 500 |

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a)** *(7 marks: 2 marks definition, 2 marks construction, 2 marks interpretation, 1 mark log-scale justification)*
>
> **Definition** *(2 marks)*: A Societal Risk F–N curve is a graphical representation showing the cumulative frequency **F(N)** of all accident scenarios that result in **N or more fatalities**, plotted as F (y-axis) versus N (x-axis), on **log–log axes**.
>
> **Construction steps** *(2 marks)*:
> 1. Tabulate each scenario's frequency fⱼ and fatality count nⱼ.
> 2. Sort scenarios in descending order of nⱼ.
> 3. Compute cumulative frequency: $F(N) = \sum_{j:\,n_j \ge N} f_j$ at each unique N.
> 4. Plot points (N, F(N)) on log–log paper; join to form the F–N curve.
> 5. Overlay regulatory limit line for comparison.
>
> **Interpretation** *(2 marks)*: A point on the curve says "accidents killing ≥ N people occur with cumulative frequency F(N)". A **steeper** curve indicates stronger aversion to large-scale disasters. Points **above** the regulatory limit line ⇒ unacceptable risk.
>
> **Why log–log** *(1 mark)*: Risk values span 8–10 orders of magnitude (10⁻⁶ to 10⁰), and fatality counts also span 3–4 orders of magnitude. Log scales compress the dynamic range and reveal the underlying **power-law** relationship $F \cdot N^{k} = C$ as a straight line whose slope = −k.
>
> ---
>
> **(b) Step-by-step** *(7 marks: 3 marks F(N) computation, 2 marks slope, 2 marks conclusion)*
>
> Step 1 — Compute cumulative F(N) at each N (since the data is already sorted with unique N values):
>
> | N | F(N) (yr⁻¹) |
> |---|-------------|
> | 1 | $1 \times 10^{-2} + 1 \times 10^{-3} + 1 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-6} = 1.111 \times 10^{-2}$ |
> | 5 | $1 \times 10^{-3} + 1 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-6} = 1.111 \times 10^{-3}$ |
> | 25 | $1 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-6} = 1.111 \times 10^{-4}$ |
> | 100 | $1 \times 10^{-5} + 1 \times 10^{-6} = 1.100 \times 10^{-5}$ |
> | 500 | $1 \times 10^{-6}$ |
>
> Step 2 — Take logs of N and F(N):
>
> | log₁₀(N) | log₁₀(F) |
> |----------|----------|
> | 0.000 | −1.954 |
> | 0.699 | −2.954 |
> | 1.398 | −3.954 |
> | 2.000 | −4.959 |
> | 2.699 | −6.000 |
>
> Step 3 — Compute slope k (using endpoints for simplicity):
>
> $$k = -\frac{\Delta \log_{10}(F)}{\Delta \log_{10}(N)} = -\frac{(-1.954) - (-6.000)}{0.000 - 2.699} = -\frac{4.046}{-2.699} \approx 1.50$$
>
> Step 4 — Conclusion *(2 marks)*: The slope **k ≈ 1.5** lies **between 1 and 2**, which **satisfies the UK HSE aversion criterion**. The plant is socially acceptable, but the regulator will still require that the curve lies **entirely below** the limit line — this must be verified by overlaying the F–N curve on the HSE limit chart.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin these 12 bullets in your memory before the exam.**

- **Risk Contour** = iso-line of equal risk (IR or FN) on a geographic map around a hazardous installation.
- **Two families**: Individual Risk Contour (spatial, units yr⁻¹) and Societal Risk F–N Curve (cumulative frequency vs fatalities).
- **Master equation** for IRC: $\text{IR}(x, y) = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot P_i(x, y)$ — frequency × probability **at each location**.
- **Probit** links physical dose (thermal, overpressure, toxic) to fatality probability — must convert Y to P via error function.
- **Thermal probit**: $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(Q^{4/3} t)$.
- **Overpressure probit** (whole body): $Y = 5.13 + 1.39 \ln(P^{0})$ with P⁰ in bar.
- **Toxic probit (Cl₂)**: $Y = -10.6 + 1.85 \ln(C^{1.5} t)$.
- **F(N) for F–N curve**: cumulative frequency — **sum of fᵢ for all accidents with nᵢ ≥ N**.
- **F–N curve is on log–log axes** because data spans many orders of magnitude and follows a power law $F \cdot N^{k} = C$.
- **UK HSE risk tolerance limits**: 1 × 10⁻⁴ (intolerable) → 1 × 10⁻⁵ (ALARP) → 1 × 10⁻⁶ (ALARP) → 1 × 10⁻⁷ (broadly acceptable).
- **ALARP** = "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" — cost-benefit optimization in the middle zone.
- **Societal risk** depends on **population density**; **individual risk** does NOT — this is the conceptual difference examiners love to test.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: F-N Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** F-N Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **F-N Curve** (Frequency–Number of Fatalities Curve) is a graphical and mathematical tool used in **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** to represent the **societal risk** posed by an industrial installation or a process plant. It plots the cumulative frequency (F) of incidents that result in **N or more fatalities** on the ordinate (y-axis) against the number of fatalities (N) on the abscissa (x-axis), typically using **log-log axes**.

Formally, the F-N Curve expresses the relationship:

> **"The expected frequency F (per year) of accidents causing N or more fatalities at a hazardous installation."**

This curve integrates the contributions of all identified accident scenarios (top events) into a single, compact risk metric that captures both the **likelihood** and the **magnitude of consequence** of major accidents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The F-N curve is the universal benchmark for communicating **societal risk** to regulators, the public, and corporate management. It is a mandatory deliverable in any QRA report submitted under the **Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC, 1989)** and the **Chemical Accident (Emergency Planning, Preparedness and Response) Rules, 1996**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are the captain of a ship sailing through a busy shipping lane. You don't just want to know "will there be a storm?" — you want to know "**how big a storm, and how often**?" A small drizzle might happen every week (high frequency, low severity), while a Category-5 cyclone might happen once in 100 years (low frequency, very high severity).

The **F-N Curve** does exactly this for industrial accidents:
- The **horizontal axis (N)** tells you **"how deadly is the accident?"** (number of people it could kill).
- The **vertical axis (F)** tells you **"how often does such an accident happen (per year)?"**
- Every point on the curve is a "weather report" of risk: a small bump in fatalities might happen once every 10 years (F = 0.1/yr), while a catastrophic mass-fatality event might happen once every 10,000 years (F = 10⁻⁴/yr).

So the F-N curve is essentially the **"risk fingerprint"** of a plant — a single signature graph that regulators and engineers use to ask: *"Is this plant safe enough for society?"*

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **F** | Cumulative frequency of events with N or more fatalities (year⁻¹) |
| **N** | Number of fatalities |
| **Societal Risk** | Risk experienced by a group of people (contrasted with **Individual Risk**) |
| **Individual Risk (IR)** | Probability that an average person at a location will die per year |
| **ALARP** | As Low As Reasonably Practicable (UK HSE concept) |
| **Tolerability Limit** | A regulatory line on the F-N curve above which risk is **not acceptable** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Standard F-N Lines (used globally for risk governance):**
> - **HSE (UK):** F = 10⁻² for N = 1, slope = −1
> - **VROM (Netherlands):** F = 10⁻³ for N = 1, slope = −2
> - **Hong Kong EPD:** F = 10⁻³ for N = 1, slope = −1.5
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of the F-N Curve

The cumulative F-N curve is typically modeled as a **power-law relationship** between frequency and the number of fatalities:

$$F(N) = C \cdot N^{-k}$$

Where:
- $F(N)$ = cumulative frequency of incidents causing **N or more fatalities** (year⁻¹)
- $C$ = constant (intercept of the curve at N = 1)
- $N$ = number of fatalities
- $k$ = slope exponent (negative slope on log-log plot)

### 2.1.1 Logarithmic Transformation

Taking logarithms of both sides:

$$\log_{10}(F) = \log_{10}(C) - k \cdot \log_{10}(N)$$

This is the equation of a **straight line** on log-log paper, with:
- **Y-intercept** = $\log_{10}(C)$
- **Slope** = $-k$

## 2.2 Why F-N Curve is Almost Always Log-Log

Because risk values span many orders of magnitude — from 10⁻⁶ /year (very rare mass fatality) to 10⁻¹ /year (relatively common single fatality) — a linear plot cannot capture both ends. The **log-log transformation** compresses the wide dynamic range into a readable chart.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power-law F-N model | $F = C \cdot N^{-k}$ | year⁻¹ | Baseline risk curve |
| Log-log linear form | $\log_{10} F = \log_{10} C - k \log_{10} N$ | dimensionless | Curve fitting & plotting |
| HSE Tolerability Line | $F \cdot N = 10^{-2}$ | year⁻¹ | UK societal risk benchmark |
| VROM Tolerability Line | $F \cdot N^{2} = 10^{-3}$ | year⁻¹ | Netherlands benchmark |
| Hong Kong Line | $F \cdot N^{1.5} = 10^{-3}$ | year⁻¹ | Hong Kong EPD benchmark |
| Cumulative vs Specific | $F_{cum}(N) = \sum_{n \geq N} F_{spec}(n)$ | year⁻¹ | Risk integration step |
| Individual Risk (IR) | $IR = F_{cum}(1)$ | year⁻¹ | Single-person risk |
| Expected Fatalities | $E(N) = \sum_i F_i \cdot N_i$ | fatalities/year | Cumulative societal burden |
| FN Integral (Risk Integral) | $RI = \sum_i F_i \cdot \sqrt{N_i}$ | dimensionless | Dutch $RI$ metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Notation:** Absolute values (such as in $\vert \log_{10} F \vert$) **MUST NOT** use the vertical pipe symbol `\|` inside markdown tables. Use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table renderer.

## 2.4 Why Different Slopes Are Used?

Different regulatory jurisdictions choose different values of $k$ based on **public risk aversion**:
- A **steeper slope (k = 2)** means society is *strongly averse* to large-scale disasters — even a small chance of many fatalities is unacceptable.
- A **gentler slope (k = 1)** is more lenient — risk is treated as proportional to expected loss only.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The F-N curve is the **backbone of land-use planning around hazardous installations**. In India, it informs the **"Buffer Zone"** requirement under the **National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA)** guidelines, where installations with steeper F-N curves (higher societal risk) must maintain larger green belts around them.

> [!NOTE]
> In the **Canvey Island Study (1978)** and **Rijnmond Study (1982)** — landmark QRA exercises in Europe — the F-N curve was first used comprehensively to compare the safety profile of an entire petrochemical hub against regulatory tolerability lines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Power Law to Linear (Log-Log) Form

**Step 1 — Start with the cumulative risk model:**

$$F(N) = C \cdot N^{-k}$$

**Step 2 — Take the base-10 logarithm of both sides:**

$$\log_{10}[F(N)] = \log_{10}\left[C \cdot N^{-k}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the logarithm product rule:** $\log_{10}(A \cdot B) = \log_{10}(A) + \log_{10}(B)$

$$\log_{10}[F(N)] = \log_{10}(C) + \log_{10}\left(N^{-k}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the power rule:** $\log_{10}(x^p) = p \cdot \log_{10}(x)$

$$\log_{10}[F(N)] = \log_{10}(C) - k \cdot \log_{10}(N)$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the standard line equation** $y = mx + b$:
- Let $y = \log_{10}(F)$
- Let $x = \log_{10}(N)$
- Slope $m = -k$
- Intercept $b = \log_{10}(C)$

$$\boxed{\log_{10} F = -k \cdot \log_{10} N + \log_{10} C}$$

This confirms that on log-log paper, the F-N curve is a straight line.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Slope Calculation Between Two Known Points

Suppose two accident scenarios on the F-N curve are known:

- Scenario A: $N_1 = 1$, $F_1 = 10^{-2}$
- Scenario B: $N_2 = 10$, $F_2 = 10^{-4}$

**Step 1 — Write the log-log equations:**

$$\log_{10}(F_1) = \log_{10}(C) - k \cdot \log_{10}(N_1)$$
$$\log_{10}(F_2) = \log_{10}(C) - k \cdot \log_{10}(N_2)$$

**Step 2 — Subtract equation (1) from equation (2):**

$$\log_{10}(F_2) - \log_{10}(F_1) = -k \cdot \left[\log_{10}(N_2) - \log_{10}(N_1)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Use the logarithm difference rule:**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{F_2}{F_1}\right) = -k \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{10^{-4}}{10^{-2}}\right) = -k \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{10}{1}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the logarithms:**

$$\log_{10}(10^{-2}) = -k \cdot \log_{10}(10)$$

$$-2 = -k \cdot 1$$

**Step 6 — Solve for k:**

$$k = 2$$

**Step 7 — Interpret:** The slope $k = 2$ corresponds exactly to the **Dutch (VROM) tolerability criterion**, meaning this hypothetical plant sits right on the Dutch acceptability line.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

A LPG storage facility has the following QRA-derived incident data:

| Scenario | Number of Fatalities (N) | Cumulative Frequency F (year⁻¹) |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------------------|
| 1        | 1                      | $10^{-1}$                      |
| 2        | 2                      | $10^{-1.7}$                    |
| 3        | 5                      | $10^{-2.5}$                    |
| 4        | 10                     | $10^{-3.3}$                    |
| 5        | 50                     | $10^{-4.7}$                    |

**Task:** Determine the slope $k$ and intercept $C$ of the best-fit F-N curve, and compare against the HSE and VROM criteria.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Use the endpoints to estimate slope k:**

From Scenario 1 to Scenario 5:

$$k = \frac{\log_{10}(F_1) - \log_{10}(F_5)}{\log_{10}(N_5) - \log_{10}(N_1)}$$

$$k = \frac{(-1) - (-4.7)}{\log_{10}(50) - \log_{10}(1)}$$

$$k = \frac{3.7}{1.699}$$

$$k \approx 2.18$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the constant C using Scenario 1 ($N = 1, F = 10^{-1}$):**

$$10^{-1} = C \cdot (1)^{-2.18}$$

$$C = 10^{-1} = 0.1$$

**Step 3 — Final F-N equation:**

$$\boxed{F = 0.1 \cdot N^{-2.18}}$$

**Step 4 — Compare against HSE criterion (slope = 1):**
Since $k = 2.18 > 1$, the curve is **steeper** than the HSE line. For large N, the facility has **lower frequency than the HSE limit**.

**Step 5 — Compare against VROM criterion (slope = 2):**
Since $k = 2.18 > 2$, the curve is **steeper** than the VROM line. The facility is **more acceptable** than the VROM tolerability criterion.

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** This LPG facility satisfies both HSE and VROM societal risk limits; it would be considered **socially acceptable** under both UK and Dutch regulatory frameworks, but the actual risk integral $RI = \sum F_i \sqrt{N_i}$ should still be evaluated.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: How to Interpret the F-N Curve

| Curve Position | Engineering Interpretation |
|----------------|----------------------------|
| Curve **above** tolerability line | **Not Acceptable** — design must be modified; risk reduction mandatory |
| Curve **between** tolerability & negligible lines | **ALARP region** — apply risk reduction as far as reasonably practicable |
| Curve **below** negligible line | **Acceptable** — risk broadly tolerable, no further action needed |
| **Steeper curve** (higher k) | Society less exposed to mass-fatality events |
| **Shallower curve** (lower k) | Higher risk of large disasters → regulatory scrutiny required |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of F-N Curve Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Hazard Identification: HAZOP, FMEA, Checklists"] --> B["Scenario Selection: Top Events from Fault Tree"]
    B --> C["Consequence Modelling: Pool Fire, VCE, BLEVE"]
    C --> D["Estimate Number of Fatalities: N"]
    D --> E["Estimate Frequency of Each Scenario: F"]
    E --> F["Build Cumulative Frequency: Fcum greater than equal to N"]
    F --> G["Plot F vs N on Log Log Axes"]
    G --> H["Overlay Regulatory Tolerability Lines: HSE, VROM"]
    H --> I{"Is Curve Above Tolerability Limit?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["NOT ACCEPTABLE: Risk Reduction Mandatory"]
    I -- No --> K{"Is Curve in ALARP Region?"}
    K -- Yes --> L["ALARP: Apply Cost Benefit Analysis"]
    K -- No --> M["ACCEPTABLE: Risk Broadly Tolerable"]
    J --> N["Add Safety Layers: SIS, Bunds, Ventilation"]
    L --> N
    N --> B
```

## 4.2 Interpretation Diagram — Societal Risk Acceptance Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Z1["UNACCEPTABLE ZONE"]
        Z1A["Curve Above Tolerability Line"]
    end

    subgraph Z2["ALARP ZONE"]
        Z2A["Between Tolerability and Negligible Lines"]
    end

    subgraph Z3["ACCEPTABLE ZONE"]
        Z3A["Curve Below Negligible Line"]
    end

    L1["Tolerability Line: F greater than or equal to 10powerneg2 divided by N"] -. "Upper Boundary" .-> Z1
    L2["Negligible Line: F greater than or equal to 10powerneg4 divided by N"] -. "Lower Boundary" .-> Z3

    Z1 -->|"Risk Reduction Mandatory"| Z2
    Z2 -->|"Risk Tolerable Subject to ALARP"| Z3
```

## 4.3 F-N Curve Schematic (Log-Log Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Point A: N = 1, F = 10powerneg1 Single fatality, once per 10 years"]
    P2["Point B: N = 10, F = 10powerneg3 Ten fatalities, once per 1000 years"]
    P3["Point C: N = 100, F = 10powerneg5 Mass fatality, once per 100000 years"]

    P1 -->|"Slope -2 Power Law"| P2
    P2 -->|"Slope -2 Power Law"| P3

    P1 -. "On Plant Curve" .-> X["Societal Risk Profile of Plant"]
    P2 -. "On Plant Curve" .-> X
    P3 -. "On Plant Curve" .-> X
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above map the **functional flow** of risk data through a F-N curve construction pipeline. In actual QRA software (e.g., **SAFETI, PHAST, ALOHA**), the consequence modelling step uses dispersion equations (Pasquill-Gifford) and explosion models (TNT equivalent / TNO Multi-Energy) to feed into the F-N aggregation engine.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

Define the F-N curve. What is the significance of the slope parameter $k$ in the F-N curve equation $F = C \cdot N^{-k}$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The F-N curve is a graphical representation of **societal risk**, plotting the cumulative frequency (F) of accidents causing **N or more fatalities** against the number of fatalities (N), typically on log-log axes. (1 mark)

The mathematical form is $F = C \cdot N^{-k}$, where $C$ is a constant (intercept) and $k$ is the **slope exponent**. (1 mark)

The significance of $k$: It indicates the **rate at which frequency decreases** with increasing severity. A **larger $k$** (steeper curve) implies stronger public risk aversion — society is more protected against mass-fatality events. Regulatory criteria use $k = 1$ (HSE), $k = 2$ (VROM), and $k = 1.5$ (Hong Kong). (1 mark)

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

Distinguish between **Individual Risk (IR)** and **Societal Risk (SR)**. Which one is represented by the F-N curve?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Individual Risk (IR)** is the probability that an unprotected person, present 24/7 at a fixed location near a hazard source, will die due to that hazard per year. It is a **location-specific** risk, expressed as $IR = \sum_i f_i \cdot p_i$ where $f_i$ is the frequency and $p_i$ is the probability of fatality. (1.5 marks)

- **Societal Risk (SR)** is the risk experienced by a **group of people** simultaneously. It depends not only on frequency and consequence but also on the **spatial distribution** of the population. (1 mark)

- The **F-N curve represents Societal Risk**. (0.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the methodology for constructing the **F-N curve** for a major hazard installation. List the four key steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process plant has the following incident data:

| Event | N (Fatalities) | F (year⁻¹) |
|-------|---------------|-----------|
| 1     | 1             | $10^{-1.0}$ |
| 2     | 5             | $10^{-2.2}$ |
| 3     | 20            | $10^{-3.5}$ |
| 4     | 100           | $10^{-5.0}$ |

(i) Estimate the slope $k$ and constant $C$ of the F-N curve.  
(ii) Compare the curve with the **VROM criterion** ($k = 2$, anchor $10^{-3}$ at N = 1) and state whether the plant meets the Dutch tolerability standard. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

The methodology for constructing the F-N curve consists of the following four steps:

**Step 1: Hazard Identification (1.5 marks)**
Use techniques such as **HAZOP, FMEA, What-If Analysis, and Checklist Review** to identify all credible accident scenarios (top events) at the installation. Examples include pool fires, vapour cloud explosions (VCE), BLEVE, and toxic gas releases.

**Step 2: Consequence Analysis (1.5 marks)**
For each top event, use consequence models (e.g., Pasquill-Gifford dispersion, TNT-equivalent explosion model, TNO Multi-Energy model) to estimate the **number of fatalities (N)**. This requires population density data around the plant during day and night conditions.

**Step 3: Frequency Estimation (1.5 marks)**
Using **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** or **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)**, compute the frequency (per year) of each top event. Account for failure rates of safety barriers (relief valves, ESD systems, foam systems, etc.).

**Step 4: Plotting the Cumulative F-N Curve (2.5 marks)**
- Sort scenarios by descending N.
- Compute the **cumulative frequency** $F_{cum}(N) = \sum_{n \geq N} F(n)$.
- Plot $F_{cum}$ vs $N$ on log-log axes.
- Overlay regulatory tolerability lines (HSE, VROM) to interpret the result.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Slope calculation using endpoints (Event 1 and Event 4): (2 marks)**

$$k = \frac{\log_{10}(F_1) - \log_{10}(F_4)}{\log_{10}(N_4) - \log_{10}(N_1)}$$

$$k = \frac{(-1.0) - (-5.0)}{\log_{10}(100) - \log_{10}(1)} = \frac{4.0}{2.0} = 2.0$$

**Step 2 — Calculate constant C (2 marks):**

Using Event 1: $F_1 = C \cdot N_1^{-k}$

$$10^{-1} = C \cdot (1)^{-2.0}$$

$$C = 0.1$$

**Step 3 — Final F-N equation: (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{F = 0.1 \cdot N^{-2.0}}$$

**Step 4 — VROM comparison: (2 marks)**

VROM criterion: $F \cdot N^{2} = 10^{-3}$, or $F = 10^{-3} \cdot N^{-2}$.

At $N = 1$: $F_{VROM} = 10^{-3}$, but plant has $F_{plant} = 10^{-1}$ → plant is **above** VROM line at low N.

At $N = 100$: $F_{VROM} = 10^{-3} \cdot 10^{-4} = 10^{-7}$, plant has $F_{plant} = 10^{-5}$ → plant is **below** VROM line at high N.

The two lines **intersect** somewhere between N = 1 and N = 100. The plant does **not fully meet** the VROM criterion at all N values. **(Plant is acceptable for high-fatality events but problematic for low-fatality, higher-frequency events — risk reduction measures recommended.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **ALARP** as applied to societal risk. How is the F-N curve used to define the three risk regions (Unacceptable, ALARP, Acceptable)? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using a graphical sketch, illustrate the **HSE, VROM, and Hong Kong EPD** tolerability criteria on a single log-log F-N diagram. Discuss how a safety engineer would use such a composite plot in decision-making. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**

**ALARP** stands for **As Low As Reasonably Practicable** — a UK Health and Safety Executive (HSE) principle for risk management. (1 mark)

Under ALARP, a risk must be reduced **unless the cost of further reduction is grossly disproportionate** to the safety benefit achieved. (1 mark)

The F-N curve is divided into three regions (3 marks):

1. **Unacceptable Region (above the Upper Tolerability Line):** Risk is too high under any circumstances. The plant must not operate as-is; major design changes are mandatory.
2. **ALARP Region (between Upper and Lower Lines):** Risk is tolerable only if all reasonably practicable measures to reduce it have been implemented. Cost-benefit analysis is used.
3. **Acceptable (Broadly Tolerable) Region (below the Lower Line):** Risk is considered negligible; further risk reduction is not required, although good-practice measures continue.

The two boundary lines (Upper and Lower) are typically anchored at (N = 1, F = 10⁻²) and (N = 1, F = 10⁻⁴) with slope −1. (2 marks)

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**

A composite F-N diagram would have **three lines** with the following equations (3 marks):

| Criterion | Equation | Anchor Point |
|-----------|----------|--------------|
| HSE (UK)  | $F = 10^{-2} \cdot N^{-1}$ | $(1, 10^{-2})$ |
| VROM (NL) | $F = 10^{-3} \cdot N^{-2}$ | $(1, 10^{-3})$ |
| HK EPD    | $F = 10^{-3} \cdot N^{-1.5}$ | $(1, 10^{-3})$ |

Graphically, VROM has the **steepest slope**, HSE has the **gentlest**, and HK EPD lies in between. (2 marks)

A safety engineer uses such a plot by: (2 marks)
1. **Plotting the plant's actual F-N curve** on the same axes.
2. **Checking against the strictest criterion** (usually VROM for offshore plants, HSE for onshore UK plants).
3. If the curve lies in the ALARP region, the engineer conducts a **cost-benefit analysis** to justify additional safety investments.
4. The composite plot helps in **selecting which regulatory framework to adopt** for a multi-national company.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting "cumulative" frequency:** Students often plot the *specific* (exact-N) frequency instead of the *cumulative* (N-or-more) frequency. The F-N curve by KTU convention is **always cumulative**.
> 2. **Confusing axes:** F goes on the y-axis, N on the x-axis. Marks deducted for inversion.
> 3. **Linear plot:** The F-N curve is a **log-log plot**. Plotting on linear axes is a 1-mark penalty.
> 4. **Wrong anchor for VROM/HSE:** VROM is $F \cdot N^2 = 10^{-3}$, not $10^{-2}$. HSE is $F \cdot N = 10^{-2}$, not $10^{-3}$. Memorization errors cost up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the "or more"** in the F-N definition. If event with N = 5 occurs at $10^{-3}$/year, then $F_{cum}(1)$ must include it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ F-N Curve represents **Societal Risk** (not Individual Risk).
- ✅ F = cumulative frequency of events with **N or more** fatalities.
- ✅ Standard form: $F = C \cdot N^{-k}$ — a **power-law** (straight line on log-log).
- ✅ **Log-log axes** are mandatory; both axes span multiple decades.
- ✅ **HSE** criterion: slope $k = 1$, anchor $10^{-2}$ at $N = 1$.
- ✅ **VROM** criterion: slope $k = 2$, anchor $10^{-3}$ at $N = 1$ (steepest).
- ✅ **Hong Kong EPD** criterion: slope $k = 1.5$, anchor $10^{-3}$ at $N = 1$.
- ✅ Three zones: **Unacceptable (above)**, **ALARP (middle)**, **Acceptable (below)**.
- ✅ Construction sequence: Hazard ID → Consequence → Frequency → Cumulative Plot.
- ✅ Used in **QRA**, **land-use planning**, and **regulatory licensing** of hazardous installations.
- ✅ The F-N curve is the **bridge between engineering risk analysis and public policy**.
- ✅ Slope $k$ is the **public's risk-aversion index** — higher k means society strongly rejects mass-fatality events.
- ✅ In India, F-N curves are referenced in **NDMA guidelines** and **MoEFCC EIA Notifications (2006)** for chemical and petrochemical industries.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Individual Risk and Societal Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Calculation of Individual Risk and Societal Risk # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Individual Risk (IR) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > **Individual Risk (IR)** is defined as the **frequency (or probability per year)** at which an individual at a specific location is expected to suffer fatality due to exposure to all hazardous events originating from a given activity or installation. It is expressed in **dimensions of fatalities per person per year (yr⁻¹)** and is a purely *location-based* scalar metric — it does **not** depend on whether a person is actually present. ### Mathematical Definition (Rigorous Form) For a given location *x* occupied by a hypothetical continuously-present individual: $$IR(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j \cdot p_{f,j}(x)$$ Where: - $f_j$ = frequency of occurrence of hazardous event *j* (per year, **yr⁻¹**) - $p_{f,j}(x)$ = probability of fatality of the individual at location *x* given event *j* (dimensionless, 0 to 1) - $N$ = total number of independent hazardous scenarios considered > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The "Lightning vs Pipeline" Thought Experiment** > Imagine standing in an open field during a thunderstorm. The *individual risk* is the chance **you** will be struck — regardless of whether you are actually there. If you are not there, the risk still exists in the abstract sense. The same applies to a process plant: IR at a fence-line tells us the chance of fatality for a *virtual* person standing there **24 × 7 × 365** — a worst-case, ever-present receiver. This is why IR is plotted as **contour lines (isolines)** on a map of the plant, much like elevation contours on a topographic map. ### 1.2 Societal Risk (SR) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > **Societal Risk (SR)** is the relationship between the **frequency of occurrence** of hazardous events and the **magnitude (number of fatalities)** they produce. It accounts for the *cumulative* impact on a population and is conventionally expressed as an **F–N curve** (also called a Farmer's curve) — a plot of cumulative frequency $F$ (yr⁻¹) versus number of fatalities $N$ on logarithmic scales. ### Mathematical Definition (Rigorous Form) The cumulative frequency $F(N)$ represents the expected frequency of all accidents causing **at least N** fatalities: $$F(N) = \sum_{j=1}^{N_{events}} f_j \quad \text{for all } j \text{ such that } n_j \geq N$$ Where: - $n_j$ = number of fatalities produced by hazardous event *j* - $f_j$ = frequency of that specific event (per year, **yr⁻¹**) > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The "Airline Crash vs Car Crash" Example** > A single car crash killing 1 person (high frequency, low consequence) versus a rare aircraft accident killing 200 people (low frequency, high consequence) are *not* equivalent from a societal perspective. Societal risk quantifies this by plotting **how often** (frequency) each level of *group* fatality occurs. The shape of the F–N curve conveys *risk aversion*: a steeper slope means society demands much lower frequencies for high-fatality events than the "neutral" line would predict. ### 1.3 The "Two-Axis" Framework in KTU Risk Assessment | Metric | Axis Measured | Question Answered | Output Form | |---|---|---|---| | **Individual Risk (IR)** | Frequency per person (yr⁻¹) | *"How likely is **one specific person** to die?"* | Contour map (isolines) | | **Societal Risk (SR)** | Frequency vs. Number of fatalities | *"How often do accidents killing **N or more** people occur?"* | F–N curve (log–log plot) | > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Memory Hook** > - **IR** → "Me, Here, Now" → **single** victim perspective → **contours** > - **SR** → "The Group, Cumulative" → **multiple** victims perspective → **F–N curve** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Risk Calculation (The "Risk Triangle") The calculation of IR and SR is built upon three independent pillars. Each pillar must be quantified for **every** hazardous scenario in the inventory: ### Pillar 1 — Frequency Analysis ($f_j$) - Estimates **how often** an initiating event occurs. - Sources: **generic failure databases** (e.g., OREDA, CCPS PERD), plant-specific operational data, fault tree analysis (FTA), event tree analysis (ETA). - Typical units: **events per year (yr⁻¹)**. ### Pillar 2 — Consequence Analysis ($C_j$) - Determines the **physical outcome** of the event: fire radiation, vapor cloud explosion overpressure, toxic dispersion concentration. - Tools: source models, dispersion models (Gaussian puff/plume), fire models (pool fire, jet fire, fireball), explosion models (TNT equivalent, TNO Multi-Energy). - Output: intensity of physical effect at various distances. ### Pillar 3 — Vulnerability (Probit) Analysis ($p_f$ or $V$) - Translates physical effect **intensity** into **probability of fatality** for a person exposed. - Uses **Probit equations** (Probabilistic Unit Toxicity/Damage relationships). - Input: physical intensity (e.g., thermal dose, overpressure, toxic dose). - Output: probability between 0 and 1. ## 2.2 The Core Risk Equation The **single most important equation** in KTU Module 4 is the *combined* risk formulation: $$R_{scenario} = f_j \times p_{f,j}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Multi-Scenario Aggregation Principle** > Risk is **scalar-additive** only on the *frequency* side. When multiple independent scenarios exist at the same location, their **frequencies add** (because they are mutually exclusive in time, assuming first-event-dominance). The probabilities of fatality from each scenario are then **weighted by their respective frequencies** and summed to give the **total individual risk** at that location. ## 2.3 Probit Equations — The Vulnerability Translator > [!NOTE] > A **probit variable** $Y$ is a transformed probability. The relationship between probit and probability of fatality is: > $$Y = 5 + \frac{p_f - 0.5}{0.5} \cdot \text{logit}^{-1} \text{ formulation}, \quad p_f = 0.5 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$ > In practice, $Y$ is given by a **linear function of ln(dose)** for the relevant physical effect. ### Standard Probit Equations (CCPS / TNO Purple Book) | Hazard Type | Probit Equation (Y) | Dose Variable | Lethal Threshold (50%) | |---|---|---|---| | **Thermal radiation** (fire) | $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(V)$ | $V = t \cdot I^{4/3}$ (kW/m²)$^{4/3}$·s | $V \approx 1085$ (kW/m²)$^{4/3}$·s | | **Explosion overpressure** | $Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P_s)$ | $P_s$ = peak overpressure (Pa) | $P_s \approx 121$ kPa | | **Toxic load (chlorine, CO, H₂S, etc.)** | $Y = a + b \ln(C^n t)$ | $C$ = concentration (ppm), $t$ = exposure time (min) | Varies by chemical | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse the *probit variable* $Y$ with the *probability of fatality* $p_f$. You must convert: $p_f = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} e^{-u^2/2}\, du$. For exam answers, the table of $p_f$ vs $Y$ is acceptable. ## 2.4 The F–N Curve Construction Procedure **Step 1 — Scenario Enumeration:** List all hazardous scenarios with $(f_j, n_j)$ pairs. **Step 2 — Sort by Fatalities:** Order scenarios in **descending** order of $n_j$. **Step 3 — Cumulative Summation:** For each integer $N$ from 1 to max, compute: $$F(N) = \sum_{j: n_j \geq N} f_j$$ **Step 4 — Plot:** Draw $F$ (y-axis, log scale) vs $N$ (x-axis, log scale). Connect with a stepped or smoothed curve. **Step 5 — Compare to Criteria:** Overlay the applicable tolerability criteria (HSE UK, VROM, etc.). ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > **Print this table — it is the exam lifeline.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $IR(x) = \sum_j f_j \cdot p_{f,j}(x)$ | Individual risk at location $x$ | yr⁻¹ | Location-based, hypothetical continuous occupancy | | 2 | $F(N) = \sum_{j: n_j \geq N} f_j$ | Societal risk cumulative frequency | yr⁻¹ | At-least-N fatalities | | 3 | $f \cdot N$ (single scenario) | Risk contribution (single point on F–N) | fatalities·yr⁻¹ | "Pair" of an F–N curve | | 4 | $E(N) = \sum_j n_j \cdot f_j$ | Expected number of fatalities per year | fatalities/yr | "Risk integral" or expectation | | 5 | $f \propto N^{-n}$ | Slope criterion (F–N power law) | dimensionless | $n=1$ neutral, $n=2$ aversion, $n=3$ strong aversion | | 6 | $Y = a + b \ln(\text{dose})$ | Probit equation (general form) | dimensionless | Convert to $p_f$ via normal CDF | | 7 | $p_f = 0.5\,[1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$ | Probit → probability | dimensionless | Standard normal transformation | | 8 | $V = t \cdot I^{4/3}$ | Thermal dose (fire lethality) | (kW/m²)$^{4/3}$·s | Eisenberg/CCPS formulation | | 9 | $\text{ALARP} \iff \text{cost} \leq \text{benefit}$ | As Low As Reasonably Practicable | monetary units | Risk must be reduced "until" cost is grossly disproportionate | | 10 | $R_{IR} < 10^{-4}$ (upper), $R_{IR} < 10^{-6}$ (broadly acceptable) | HSE UK IR limits | yr⁻¹ | Worker vs public thresholds | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Land-use planning:** IR isolines are used by regulators (e.g., HSE, EPA, Dutch VROM) to set **safety distances** between hazardous installations and residential zones. - **Emergency planning:** F–N curves inform the sizing of shelters, evacuation routes, and on-site fire services. - **Insurance underwriting:** Insurers use Expected Value $E(N)$ to compute **annualized loss expectancy (ALE)** for premium calculation. - **ALARP demonstration:** Both IR and SR must be reduced to "ALARP" before a plant can be commissioned — the F–N curve is the primary document presented to safety regulators. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Individual Risk Formula from First Principles ### Step 1 — Start with the Frequency-Probability Foundation The risk of fatality to a person at location $x$ from a single hazardous event $j$ is the **joint probability** of the event occurring **and** the person being fatally affected. For a **single** event $j$ in a 1-year period: $$R_{j}(x) = P(\text{event } j \text{ occurs in 1 yr}) \times P(\text{fatality} \mid \text{event } j, \text{ location } x)$$ The first term is the **frequency per year** $f_j$. The second term is the **conditional probability of fatality** $p_{f,j}(x)$ (from probit analysis). Therefore: $$R_j(x) = f_j \cdot p_{f,j}(x) \quad \text{(units: yr}^{-1}\text{)}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Additivity of Frequencies for Independent Events For $N$ *independent* hazardous events that could each cause fatality at location $x$, the **total individual risk** is the sum of the individual scenario risks. This is because the events are *mutually exclusive in the small time intervals* relevant to fatality (a person cannot die twice in the same accident): $$IR(x) = R_1(x) + R_2(x) + \cdots + R_N(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{N_{events}} f_j \cdot p_{f,j}(x)$$ ### Step 3 — Formal Statement of the IR Contour An **IR isoline** is the locus of points where $IR(x) = C$ (constant). For a single accident scenario, the locus of *equal* lethality is a closed curve around the source, defined implicitly by: $$f_j \cdot p_{f,j}(x) = C \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{f,j}(x) = \frac{C}{f_j}$$ For thermal events, this corresponds to a **constant thermal dose contour** $V = t \cdot I^{4/3}$ at a fixed exposure time $t$, which is itself a closed curve around the fire source. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Because $p_f$ depends on distance, IR contours are nearly always *concentric* closed curves around each hazard source, displaced along the prevailing wind direction for dispersing scenarios. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Societal Risk F–N Curve ### Step 1 — Define the Population Distribution For a scenario $j$ causing $n_j$ fatalities, the **frequency-density pair** is $(f_j, n_j)$. The F–N curve uses *cumulative* frequency, meaning we want to know "how often accidents killing **at least N** people occur". ### Step 2 — Construct the Cumulative Distribution For a discrete set of $M$ scenarios, define the *cumulative frequency function*: $$F(N) = \sum_{j=1}^{M} f_j \cdot \mathbb{1}(n_j \geq N)$$ where $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the **indicator function** (1 if true, 0 if false). This produces a **step function** of $N$ on the log–log plot. ### Step 3 — Smooth Approximation (Analytical Form) For a *continuous* distribution, the F–N curve is often approximated by a **power law**: $$F(N) = A \cdot N^{-n}$$ Taking logarithms: $$\log F(N) = \log A - n \cdot \log N$$ This is a straight line on the log–log plot with slope $-n$. The slope $n$ encodes **risk aversion**: - $n = 1$: **Risk neutral** — frequency scales inversely with magnitude (10× more fatalities → 10× less frequent) - $n = 2$: **Risk averse** — typical societal expectation (10× fatalities → 100× less frequent) - $n = 3$: **Strongly risk averse** — used for highly catastrophic events (e.g., nuclear) ### Step 4 — Compute the Expected Value The **expected number of fatalities per year** (also called the "risk integral"): $$E(N) = \sum_{j=1}^{M} n_j \cdot f_j$$ This is the **area under the F–N curve** when plotted in linear-linear axes (with $f$ on the y-axis, $N$ on the x-axis, interpreting the curve as a probability density on $N$): $$E(N) = \int_{0}^{\infty} N \cdot dF(N) \approx \int_{0}^{\infty} N \cdot \left(-\frac{dF}{dN}\right) dN$$ > [!TIP] > **Exam Trick:** For a power-law $F(N) = A N^{-n}$, the expected value integral is: > $$E(N) = \int_{1}^{N_{max}} N \cdot A n N^{-(n+1)} dN = A n \int_{1}^{N_{max}} N^{-n} dN$$ > For $n \neq 1$, this evaluates to $E(N) = \frac{A n}{1-n} \left[N_{max}^{1-n} - 1\right]$. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A refinery has three identified hazardous scenarios: > - Scenario A: $f_A = 10^{-3}$ yr⁻¹, $n_A = 1$ fatality, at location L1: $p_{f,A} = 0.8$ > - Scenario B: $f_B = 10^{-4}$ yr⁻¹, $n_B = 5$ fatalities, at location L1: $p_{f,B} = 0.6$ > - Scenario C: $f_C = 10^{-5}$ yr⁻¹, $n_C = 25$ fatalities, at location L1: $p_{f,C} = 0.4$ > > Calculate: (a) Individual Risk at L1, (b) Cumulative Societal Risk F(1), F(5), F(10), F(25), (c) Expected fatalities per year, (d) Plot F–N curve. ### Part (a) — Individual Risk at L1 Apply the fundamental IR formula: $$IR(L_1) = \sum_{j=A,B,C} f_j \cdot p_{f,j}(L_1)$$ Substituting the numerical values: $$IR(L_1) = (10^{-3} \times 0.8) + (10^{-4} \times 0.6) + (10^{-5} \times 0.4)$$ Computing each term step-by-step: $$10^{-3} \times 0.8 = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ $$10^{-4} \times 0.6 = 0.6 \times 10^{-4} = 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ $$10^{-5} \times 0.4 = 0.4 \times 10^{-5} = 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ Adding (note careful decimal alignment of exponents): $$IR(L_1) = 800 \times 10^{-6} + 60 \times 10^{-6} + 4 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\boxed{IR(L_1) = 864 \times 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1} \approx 8.64 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A person at L1 has approximately an **8.64 × 10⁻⁴ chance of fatality per year** (≈ 1 in 1,157 per year). This is **above** the HSE upper IR limit of $10^{-4}$ yr⁻¹ → **NOT TOLERABLE** under HSE UK criteria. ### Part (b) — Cumulative Societal Risk F(N) For each integer threshold N, sum the frequencies of all scenarios with $n_j \geq N$: **For N = 1:** All three scenarios have $n_j \geq 1$, so: $$F(1) = f_A + f_B + f_C = 10^{-3} + 10^{-4} + 10^{-5}$$ $$F(1) = 1000 \times 10^{-6} + 100 \times 10^{-6} + 10 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\boxed{F(1) = 1110 \times 10^{-6} = 1.11 \times 10^{-3} \text{ yr}^{-1}}$$ **For N = 5:** Scenarios B and C have $n_j \geq 5$: $$F(5) = f_B + f_C = 10^{-4} + 10^{-5} = 1.1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ **For N = 10:** Only Scenario C has $n_j \geq 10$: $$F(10) = f_C = 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ **For N = 25:** Only Scenario C has $n_j \geq 25$: $$F(25) = f_C = 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ ### Part (c) — Expected Fatalities per Year $$E(N) = \sum_{j} n_j \cdot f_j = (1)(10^{-3}) + (5)(10^{-4}) + (25)(10^{-5})$$ Computing each term: $$(1)(10^{-3}) = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$(5)(10^{-4}) = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$(25)(10^{-5}) = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$$ Adding: $$E(N) = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} + 0.5 \times 10^{-3} + 0.25 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\boxed{E(N) = 1.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fatalities per year}}$$ ### Part (d) — F–N Curve Construction Summary | Threshold N | Cumulative Frequency F(N) (yr⁻¹) | log₁₀(N) | log₁₀(F) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $1.11 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.00 | -2.955 | | 5 | $1.10 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.70 | -3.959 | | 10 | $1.00 \times 10^{-5}$ | 1.00 | -5.000 | | 25 | $1.00 \times 10^{-5}$ | 1.40 | -5.000 | The slope of the segment from $N=5$ to $N=25$ is: $$\text{slope} = \frac{\log(10^{-5}) - \log(1.1 \times 10^{-4})}{\log(25) - \log(5)} = \frac{-5 - (-3.96)}{1.40 - 0.70} = \frac{-1.04}{0.70} \approx -1.49$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The fitted slope of $\approx -1.49$ is **steeper than -1** (risk-neutral) but **shallower than -2** (risk-averse). Under HSE UK F–N criteria, the steepest acceptable slope is $n = 1$, so this installation **fails** the steepness requirement for catastrophic events — a typical KTU 14-mark problem outcome. --- # 4.
