KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define Individual Risk. Differentiate it from Societal Risk. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - **Individual Risk (1.5 marks):** The frequency at which a hypothetical individual, continuously present at a specific location, is expected to suffer fatality from all hazardous events at an installation. Mathematically: $IR(x) = \sum_j f_j \cdot p_{f,j}(x)$, expressed in **yr⁻¹**. > - **Societal Risk (1 mark):** The relationship between frequency of hazardous events and the number of fatalities produced, expressed as an **F–N curve**. > - **Key difference (0.5 mark):** IR concerns *one* hypothetical person at a *fixed location* (location-based, scalar); SR concerns the *cumulative* impact on a *population* and is a *function* $F(N)$ of fatality count. ### Q2. State and explain the Probit equation for thermal radiation fatality. What is the thermal dose variable $V$? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - **Probit equation (1.5 marks):** $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(V)$, where $Y$ is the probit variable and $V$ is the thermal dose. > - **Thermal dose definition (1 mark):** $V = t \cdot I^{4/3}$, with units of (kW/m²)$^{4/3}$·s, where $I$ = thermal radiation intensity (kW/m²) and $t$ = exposure time (s). > - **Conversion (0.5 mark):** Probability of fatality $p_f$ is obtained from $Y$ using the normal distribution: $p_f = 0.5\,[1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Individual Risk Dominant > `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` > > **(a) [7 Marks]** A chlorine storage facility has two major release scenarios. Scenario 1: a 50 mm pipe rupture releases 10 kg/s of chlorine with frequency $f_1 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ yr⁻¹. The concentration at a control room 200 m downwind is computed to give a probit value $Y_1 = 4.2$. Scenario 2: a 25 mm flange leak releases 1 kg/s with frequency $f_2 = 10^{-3}$ yr⁻¹ and probit $Y_2 = 3.5$ at the same control room. > > Calculate the **total individual risk at the control room** and comment on its tolerability with respect to HSE criteria. Show all probit-to-probability conversions. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** A second location (residential boundary, 500 m downwind) has probit values $Y_1' = 2.8$ and $Y_2' = 2.0$ for the same two scenarios. Calculate the IR at the residential boundary, and determine the **minimum individual risk isoline** that should be drawn on the plant layout to protect the public. Comment on the applicability of the **as low as reasonably practicable (ALARP)** principle. ### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Control Room IR:** **Step 1:** Convert probit values to probabilities of fatality. For Scenario 1: $Y_1 = 4.2$. Using standard probit tables or the normal CDF: $$p_{f,1} = 0.5 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{4.2 - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] = 0.5 \left[1 + \text{erf}(-0.566)\right]$$ $$\text{erf}(-0.566) \approx -0.538 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{f,1} \approx 0.5(1 - 0.538) = 0.231$$ For Scenario 2: $Y_2 = 3.5$. Similarly: $$p_{f,2} = 0.5 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{3.5 - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] = 0.5 \left[1 + \text{erf}(-1.061)\right]$$ $$\text{erf}(-1.061) \approx -0.787 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{f,2} \approx 0.5(1 - 0.787) = 0.107$$ **Step 2:** Calculate per-scenario risk contributions. $$R_1 = f_1 \cdot p_{f,1} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.231 = 1.155 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ $$R_2 = f_2 \cdot p_{f,2} = 10^{-3} \times 0.107 = 1.07 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ **Step 3:** Sum for total IR at control room. $$IR_{CR} = R_1 + R_2 = 1.155 \times 10^{-4} + 1.07 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$IR_{CR} = 2.225 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1} \approx 2.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Tolerability Verdict:** HSE UK upper limit for workers is $10^{-3}$ yr⁻¹ (intolerable) and $10^{-4}$ yr⁻¹ (ALARP zone). At $\mathbf{2.2 \times 10^{-4}}$ yr⁻¹, the control room is in the **ALARP region** for workers — risk reduction must be implemented unless the cost is grossly disproportionate. **Part (b) — Residential Boundary IR:** **Step 1:** Convert probit values at residential boundary. For Scenario 1: $Y_1' = 2.8$: $$p_{f,1}' = 0.5 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{2.8 - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] = 0.5 \left[1 + \text{erf}(-1.556)\right]$$ $$\text{erf}(-1.556) \approx -0.928 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{f,1}' \approx 0.5(1 - 0.928) = 0.036$$ For Scenario 2: $Y_2' = 2.0$: $$p_{f,2}' = 0.5 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{2.0 - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] = 0.5 \left[1 + \text{erf}(-2.121)\right]$$ $$\text{erf}(-2.121) \approx -0.997 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{f,2}' \approx 0.5(1 - 0.997) = 0.0015$$ **Step 2:** Calculate per-scenario risks at residential boundary. $$R_1' = f_1 \cdot p_{f,1}' = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.036 = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ $$R_2' = f_2 \cdot p_{f,2}' = 10^{-3} \times 0.0015 = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ **Step 3:** Sum for total IR at residential boundary. $$IR_{RB} = R_1' + R_2' = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} + 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$IR_{RB} = 1.95 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Isoline & ALARP Verdict:** HSE UK public limit is $10^{-4}$ yr⁻¹ (ALARP upper bound) and $10^{-6}$ yr⁻¹ (broadly acceptable). At $\mathbf{2.0 \times 10^{-5}}$ yr⁻¹, the residential boundary is in the **ALARP region for the public**. The **minimum isoline** to be drawn on the plant layout is the $10^{-6}$ yr⁻¹ contour (broadly acceptable), and all residences must lie **outside** this contour. ALARP applies — additional protection (e.g., water curtains, leak detection, isolation valves) must be implemented unless the cost is grossly disproportionate to the marginal safety benefit. ### Question B (14 Marks) — Societal Risk Dominant > `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` > > **(a) [7 Marks]** A process plant has the following catastrophic event inventory from a quantified risk assessment (QRA): > > | Scenario | Frequency $f_j$ (yr⁻¹) | Fatalities $n_j$ | > |---|---|---| > | VCE-1 | $10^{-2}$ | 1 | > | VCE-2 | $10^{-3}$ | 3 | > | FBL-1 | $5 \times 10^{-4}$ | 8 | > | FBL-2 | $10^{-4}$ | 20 | > | TLD-1 | $10^{-5}$ | 50 | > | TLD-2 | $10^{-6}$ | 100 | > > Construct the **F–N curve** (table of cumulative frequency $F(N)$ for $N = 1, 3, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100$) and calculate the **expected number of fatalities per year** $E(N)$. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the constructed F–N curve to the **HSE UK tolerability criteria** (slope = -1 in ALARP region, anchor point $F = 10^{-2}$ yr⁻¹ at $N = 1$). Recommend whether the plant passes the societal risk test, and if not, specify the most cost-effective *layer of protection* (LOPA-style) to bring the curve into compliance. Justify your choice using the **ALARP principle** and the **inverse-square** slope criterion $f \propto N^{-2}$. ### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — F–N Curve Construction:** The cumulative frequency is computed by summing all scenario frequencies whose fatality count is **at least** the threshold $N$: **For N = 1:** All six scenarios qualify (lowest is VCE-1 with $n=1$): $$F(1) = 10^{-2} + 10^{-3} + 5 \times 10^{-4} + 10^{-4} + 10^{-5} + 10^{-6}$$ $$F(1) = 10{,}000 \times 10^{-6} + 1{,}000 \times 10^{-6} + 500 \times 10^{-6} + 100 \times 10^{-6} + 10 \times 10^{-6} + 1 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\boxed{F(1) = 1.1611 \times 10^{-2} \text{ yr}^{-1}}$$ **For N = 3:** Scenarios with $n_j \geq 3$ are VCE-2, FBL-1, FBL-2, TLD-1, TLD-2: $$F(3) = 10^{-3} + 5 \times 10^{-4} + 10^{-4} + 10^{-5} + 10^{-6} = 1.611 \times 10^{-3} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ **For N = 5:** FBL-1, FBL-2, TLD-1, TLD-2: $$F(5) = 5 \times 10^{-4} + 10^{-4} + 10^{-5} + 10^{-6} = 6.11 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ **For N = 8:** FBL-1, FBL-2, TLD-1, TLD-2: $$F(8) = 5 \times 10^{-4} + 10^{-4} + 10^{-5} + 10^{-6} = 6.11 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ **For N = 10:** FBL-1, FBL-2, TLD-1, TLD-2 (same as N=5 since FBL-1 has n=8, FBL-2 has n=20): $$F(10) = 6.11 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ **For N = 20:** FBL-2, TLD-1, TLD-2: $$F(20) = 10^{-4} + 10^{-5} + 10^{-6} = 1.11 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ **For N = 50:** TLD-1, TLD-2: $$F(50) = 10^{-5} + 10^{-6} = 1.1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$ **For N = 100:** Only TLD-2: $$\boxed{F(100) = 10^{-6} \text{ yr}^{-1}}$$ **Expected Fatalities:** $$E(N) = \sum_j n_j f_j = (1)(10^{-2}) + (3)(10^{-3}) + (8)(5 \times 10^{-4}) + (20)(10^{-4}) + (50)(10^{-5}) + (100)(10^{-6})$$ Computing each term: $$(1)(10^{-2}) = 1.0 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$(3)(10^{-3}) = 3.0 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$(8)(5 \times 10^{-4}) = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$(20)(10^{-4}) = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$(50)(10^{-5}) = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$(100)(10^{-6}) = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$$ Adding: $$E(N) = 10 \times 10^{-3} + 3 \times 10^{-3} + 4 \times 10^{-3} + 2 \times 10^{-3} + 0.5 \times 10^{-3} + 0.1 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\boxed{E(N) = 1.96 \times 10^{-2} \text{ fatalities per year}}$$ **Part (b) — HSE Comparison & LOPA Recommendation:** HSE UK criterion: at $N=1$, $F = 10^{-2}$ yr⁻¹, slope = -1 in ALARP region. The line equation: $$\log F = \log(10^{-2}) - 1 \cdot \log N = -2 - \log N$$ So the HSE dividing line (in log–log space) is: $$F_{HSE}(N) = 10^{-2} \cdot N^{-1} = \frac{10^{-2}}{N}$$ Computing the ratio $F_{plant}(N) / F_{HSE}(N)$ for the high-fatality scenarios: | N | $F_{plant}$ (yr⁻¹) | $F_{HSE}$ (yr⁻¹) | Ratio $F_{plant}/F_{HSE}$ | Status | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $1.16 \times 10^{-2}$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ | 1.16 | Just above | | 3 | $1.61 \times 10^{-3}$ | $3.33 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.48 | Below | | 8 | $6.11 \times 10^{-4}$ | $1.25 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.49 | Below | | 50 | $1.1 \times 10^{-5}$ | $2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.055 | Well below | | 100 | $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.01 | Well below | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verdict:** The plant F–N curve lies **entirely below** the HSE line for $N \geq 3$, and marginally above for $N=1$. This means the plant **passes** the societal risk test for catastrophic (multi-fatality) events. The only borderline point is $N=1$ (occupational fatalities), but this is within the ALARP region and is typically managed through standard safety procedures. > [!NOTE] > **If the curve were ABOVE the line** (typical KTU extension question), the most cost-effective **LOPA-style** reduction would target the **highest-contribution scenarios** — in this case **TLD-1** (toxic load, 50 fatalities, $10^{-5}$ yr⁻¹) — because the inverse-square slope criterion $f \propto N^{-2}$ means *high-N* scenarios are weighted more heavily in societal risk perception. A single additional safety instrumented function (SIF) reducing $f_{TLD-1}$ by a factor of 10 to $10^{-6}$ yr⁻¹ would lower $F(50)$ to $2 \times 10^{-6}$ yr⁻¹ — well within the ALARP envelope — at relatively modest cost compared to fundamental re-engineering. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > 1. **Unit mismatch error (2 marks lost):** Never write IR in units of "per person" alone; it is **per person per year (yr⁻¹)**. Same for $F(N)$ — must be yr⁻¹. > 2. **Probit-to-probability conversion skipped (3 marks lost):** The probit $Y$ is *not* the probability. Always convert via the normal CDF or a published probit table. KTU examiners will deduct marks if you just state $p_f = Y/5$ or similar shortcuts. > 3. **Cumulative F(N) summation error (2 marks lost):** Students often take $F(N) = f$ (the *single scenario* frequency). $F(N)$ is **cumulative** — sum over all scenarios with $n_j \geq N$. > 4. **Forgetting that $n=1$ scenario in F–N curve (1 mark lost):** The $N=1$ point must be computed and plotted. Many students start the curve at $N=3$ or $N=5$, losing the anchor point. > 5. **Conflating "ALARP" with "acceptable" (1 mark lost):** ALARP is *not* "safe" — it is "tolerable only if further reduction is impractically costly". Always state the cost-vs-benefit trade-off. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Night Survival Sheet)** > > ✅ **IR Definition:** Frequency of fatality for a hypothetical individual continuously at a location; units = **yr⁻¹**; output = **contour map**. > ✅ **SR Definition:** Relationship between cumulative frequency $F(N)$ and number of fatalities $N$; output = **F–N curve** (log–log). > ✅ **Master Formula IR:** $IR(x) = \sum_j f_j \cdot p_{f,j}(x)$ — additive over independent scenarios. > ✅ **Master Formula SR:** $F(N) = \sum_{j: n_j \geq N} f_j$ — cumulative over all scenarios meeting threshold. > ✅ **Three Pillars:** Frequency ($f_j$), Consequence (physical effect), Vulnerability (probit → $p_f$). > ✅ **Probit conversion:** $p_f = 0.5\,[1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$ — must convert probit $Y$ to probability. > ✅ **Thermal probit:** $Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \ln(V)$ where $V = t \cdot I^{4/3}$ in (kW/m²)$^{4/3}$·s. > ✅ **Slope criteria:** $n=1$ neutral, $n=2$ risk-averse (HSE), $n=3$ strong aversion (nuclear). > ✅ **HSE UK limits:** IR worker upper $10^{-3}$ yr⁻¹, ALARP $10^{-4}$ yr⁻¹, broadly acceptable $10^{-6}$ yr⁻¹. Public one order stricter. > ✅ **ALARP = "tolerable only if cost of further reduction is grossly disproportionate to benefit".** > ✅ **Expected fatalities:** $E(N) = \sum_j n_j f_j$ — the "risk integral" used in insurance and ALARP cost-benefit. > ✅ **F–N curve log–log plot:** $F$ on y-axis, $N$ on x-axis, both logarithmic; a power law $F = A N^{-n}$ becomes a straight line. > ✅ **Scenarios are independent:** Frequencies add, not the probabilities. The $p_f$ values are weighted by their $f_j$ before summation. > ✅ **LOPA reduction principle:** Target the *largest contribution* to the F–N curve at the *highest N* values — this is where the inverse-square slope criterion imposes the strictest tolerability. > ✅ **Individual Risk contour = constant $f \cdot p_f$ locus** = essentially a constant physical-effect (dose) contour. > ✅ **F–N curve is NOT a probability distribution** — it is a *cumulative* exceedance curve. The $y$-axis is frequency, not probability. > ✅ **Public vs worker:** Public IR tolerability limits are **10× stricter** than worker limits (1 in 10,000 per year vs 1 in 1,000 per year). > ✅ **VROM (Netherlands) criteria:** Anchor at $F = 10^{-5}$ yr⁻¹ at $N = 10$, slope $-2$ — used as an alternative to HSE UK in international QRA.

topics.title: Human Reliability Analysis (HRA): Factors Leading to Human Error
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Human Reliability Analysis (HRA): Factors Leading to Human Error

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Human Reliability Analysis (HRA)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Human Reliability Analysis (HRA)** is a structured, systematic methodology used within Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA) / Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) to identify, evaluate, and reduce the probability of **human errors** that could lead to accidents, system failures, or hazardous events in complex industrial systems (e.g., nuclear power plants, chemical process plants, oil & gas refineries, aviation).

Mathematically, HRA seeks to quantify the **Human Error Probability (HEP)** — the likelihood that a person performing a task will fail to execute that task correctly within a specified time window.

$$\text{HEP} \in [0, 1]$$

where **HEP = 0** represents perfect human reliability (no error), and **HEP = 1** represents certain failure (error guaranteed). Typical industry values for routine tasks fall in the range **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻²** (i.e., 1 in 10,000 to 1 in 100).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Air Traffic Controller" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine an **air traffic controller** guiding a plane toward the runway during a thunderstorm. Even with perfect radar and instruments, the controller can:
> - **Misread** the altitude (a **perception error**)
> - **Forget** to issue a descent command (a **memory lapse**)
> - **Choose the wrong runway** approach (a **decision mistake**)
> - **Skip** a checklist step because of fatigue (a **violation due to PSFs**)

HRA is the scientific discipline of predicting *how often* such human failures will occur, identifying *why* they occur, and designing **defenses** (alarms, procedures, training, ergonomics) to lower their probability. Just as a structural engineer calculates the load-bearing capacity of a beam, a safety engineer calculates the "error-bearing capacity" of the human operator.

---

## 1.3 Why HRA Matters in Process Safety

In the modern process industry, **60–80% of industrial accidents** have a human factor as a contributing root cause (e.g., Bhopal 1984, Texas City 2005, Deepwater Horizon 2010). HRA bridges the gap between:

| Hardware Reliability | Human Reliability |
|:---:|:---:|
| Predictable via Weibull/exponential distributions | Variable, context-dependent, non-stationary |
| Component-based | Operator/team-based |
| Failure rate ≈ constant in service life | Failure rate varies with stress, fatigue, training |

HRA integrates the **"human" component** into a Quantitative Risk Assessment, ensuring that the calculated risk of a plant truly reflects operational reality.

---

## 1.4 The Two Pillars of HRA — Error vs. Violation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Distinguish Clearly:**
> - **Human Error** = *Unintended* deviation from intent (slip, lapse, mistake).
> - **Violation** = *Intentional* deviation from rules/procedures (routine, situational, optimizing, exceptional).

This distinction is critical because **errors are mitigated by training & ergonomics**, while **violations are mitigated by safety culture & supervision**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Human Error Taxonomy — The Three Cognitive Failures

Reason's classification (1990) — the *gold standard* referenced in nearly all HRA methodologies:

### (A) Slips & Lapses — **Execution Failures** (Skill-Based Level)
- The **plan is correct**, but the **action fails** in execution.
- Occur during highly practiced, automatic behaviors.
- Triggered by **attention capture** (distraction, interruption).
- **Examples:**
  - Pressing the *STOP* button instead of *START* (motor program error).
  - Forgetting to close a valve that was just opened (memory lapse).

### (B) Mistakes — **Intentional Failures** (Rule-/Knowledge-Based Level)
- The **plan is wrong**, but executed flawlessly.
- Result from faulty reasoning, misdiagnosis, or rule misapplication.
- **Sub-types:**
  - *Rule-based mistake*: Applying the right rule under wrong conditions.
  - *Knowledge-based mistake*: No rule available; reasoning from first principles — high failure rate.
- **Example:** Operator diagnosing a pump vibration as cavitation when it is actually bearing failure.

### (C) Violations — **Motivational Failures**
- Deliberate deviation from procedure.
- Reflect **organizational culture**, not cognition.
- **Three sub-types (Reason & Parker, 1997):**
  - *Routine*: "We always do it this way" — habitual.
  - *Situational*: Necessary due to time/workload pressure.
  - *Optimizing*: Personal convenience over procedure.

---

## 2.2 Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs) — The Root Causes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PSFs** are the situational, environmental, personal, and organizational conditions that influence human performance and therefore modify the **base human error probability**.

PSFs are the **multipliers** in every HRA calculation. Common classification:

### Internal / Individual PSFs
- Physical condition: **fatigue, illness, intoxication, sleep deprivation**
- Psychological state: **stress, boredom, complacency, over-confidence**
- Competence: **training, experience, qualification, mental model**
- Motivation: **morale, job satisfaction, personal problems**

### External / Situational PSFs
- **Ergonomics**: display legibility, control layout, Fitts' Law compliance
- **Procedures**: clarity, accuracy, currency of documentation
- **Tools & equipment**: reliability, calibration, availability
- **Workload**: too high → errors of commission; too low → errors of omission (vigilance decrement)
- **Time pressure / shift schedule**: circadian effects (night shift → 30% higher error rate)

### Organizational / Latent PSFs
- **Safety culture**, management commitment
- **Communication** (shift handover quality)
- **Supervision quality**
- **Maintenance programs**

---

## 2.3 Overview of Major HRA Methodologies

| Method | Year | Approach | Strength | Weakness |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **THERP** (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction) | 1983 | Decomposition + event tree | Quantitatively rigorous, taxonomy-rich | Data-intensive, complex |
| **HEART** (Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique) | 1986 | Nominal × PSF multipliers | Simple, rapid screening | Less detailed |
| **SLIM-MAUD** | 1982 | Success Likelihood Index | Captures expert judgment | Subjective scaling |
| **ATHEANA** | 1994 | Context-driven, qualitative | Captures error-forcing contexts | Resource-heavy |
| **CREAM** (Cognitive Reliability and Error Analysis) | 1998 | Cognitive model + CPCs | Distinguishes control modes | Less mature data |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are examinable in KTU ESE — memorize the structure, not just the symbols.**

| Formula | Equation | Parameters / Use |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **HEP (Generic)** | $HEP = P(\text{failure} \mid \text{task, context})$ | Probability of task failure given context |
| **THERP Composite** | $HEP_{task} = \sum_i E_i \cdot \prod_{j} PSF_{ij}$ | $E_i$ = basic error probability; $PSF_{ij}$ = performance shaping factor multiplier |
| **HEART Equation** | $HEP = K_0 \cdot \prod_{i} (APLC_i \cdot EPC_i)$ | $K_0$ = nominal unreliability; $APLC$ = Assessed Proportion of Affect on Performance; $EPC$ = Error Producing Condition |
| **HEART Generic Task Types (GTT)** | 9 generic tasks defined (A–I) | Each has a nominal $K_0$ value (e.g., GTT-A: $5 \times 10^{-5}$, GTT-C: $2 \times 10^{-2}$) |
| **SLIM-MAUD** | $SLI = \sum w_i \cdot r_i$ | $w_i$ = weight of PSF $i$; $r_i$ = rating (0–1); then map SLI → HEP via calibration |
| **CREAM (CPCs)** | Control Mode depends on 4 CPCs | "Opportunity", "Working conditions", "Adequacy of MMI", "Support" |
| **Human Error Rate (HER)** | $HER = \dfrac{\text{Number of errors}}{\text{Observations} \times \text{Time}}$ | Empirical field measurement, e.g., errors per 1000 hours |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Mnemonic — "K × A × E":** In HEART, you ALWAYS multiply the **Nominal ($K_0$) × Assessed Proportions × Error-Producing Conditions**. If you forget to include an EPC, you have under-estimated the HEP — examiners award full marks only when ALL three terms appear.

---

## 2.5 The Swiss Cheese Model (James Reason, 1990)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A mandatory KTU diagram-question topic.**
> Accidents occur when **holes (latent failures + active errors)** in **multiple defensive layers** (engineering controls, administrative controls, PPE, supervision) align momentarily — like slices of Swiss cheese aligning their holes. HRA aims to **reduce hole diameter** (PSF improvement) and **increase the number of slices** (defenses).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — HEART Method (Hammond, 1988)

The HEART method is the **most commonly tested HRA technique in KTU** because of its elegant multiplicative form.

### Step 1 — Identify the Generic Task Type (GTT)

Each task belongs to one of **9 Generic Task Types**, each with a tabulated nominal unreliability $K_0$.

| GTT | Description | Nominal $K_0$ |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| A | Totally unfamiliar, performed at speed with no real idea of likely consequences | $5 \times 10^{-1}$ |
| B | Shift or restore a system to a new or original state on a single attempt without supervision | $2 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| C | Complex task requiring high level of comprehension and skill | $2 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| D | Fairly simple task performed rapidly | $9 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| E | Routine, highly practiced, rapid task involving relatively low level of skill | $1 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| F | Restore or shift a system to an original or new state with some checking | $3 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| G | Completely familiar, well-designed, highly-practiced routine task | $4 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| H | Respond correctly to system command even when there is an augmented or automated supervisory system | $9 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| I | Others | – |

### Step 2 — Identify Applicable Error-Producing Conditions (EPCs)

EPCs are conditions that **increase the probability of error**. HEART identifies **38 EPCs**. Selected examples:

| EPC | Description | Max Effect $W$ |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Unfamiliarity with a situation, no awareness of consequences | 17 |
| 2 | Time shortage / time pressure | 11 |
| 3 | Low signal-to-noise ratio | 10 |
| 4 | Misperception of information | 8 |
| 5 | Distraction | 7 |
| 6 | Short-term memory overload | 6 |
| 9 | Need for transfer of knowledge | 6 |
| 11 | Ambiguity in procedures | 5 |

### Step 3 — Determine Assessed Proportion of Affect (APLC)

For each applicable EPC, the analyst assigns a proportion $APLC_i \in [0.05, 0.95]$ representing **how much** that EPC affects the task's reliability, **relative to its maximum effect**.

$$APLC_i = \frac{\text{Actual Effect of EPC}_i}{\text{Maximum Possible Effect } (W_i)}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the HEART Equation

$$\boxed{HEP = K_0 \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( W_i \times APLC_i + 1 \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "+1"?** The "+1" ensures that if an EPC has zero assessed proportion ($APLC = 0$), the multiplier reduces to **1** (no effect), not **0** (which would be mathematically incorrect — it would imply the EPC eliminates all error). This is the most commonly forgotten term in KTU answer sheets.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A reactor operator must rapidly close an emergency cooling valve during a power failure. Use HEART to estimate the HEP.
>
> **Given:**
> - GTT = **B** (Shift system on single attempt) → $K_0 = 2 \times 10^{-2}$
> - EPC-1 (Unfamiliarity) applies, $APLC_1 = 0.5$, $W_1 = 17$
> - EPC-2 (Time pressure) applies, $APLC_2 = 0.8$, $W_2 = 11$
> - EPC-5 (Distraction from alarms) applies, $APLC_3 = 0.6$, $W_3 = 7$

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Compute each multiplier term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term}_1 &= (W_1 \times APLC_1) + 1 \\
&= (17 \times 0.5) + 1 \\
&= 8.5 + 1 = 9.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term}_2 &= (W_2 \times APLC_2) + 1 \\
&= (11 \times 0.8) + 1 \\
&= 8.8 + 1 = 9.8
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term}_3 &= (W_3 \times APLC_3) + 1 \\
&= (7 \times 0.6) + 1 \\
&= 4.2 + 1 = 5.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Multiply all terms with $K_0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HEP &= K_0 \times \text{Term}_1 \times \text{Term}_2 \times \text{Term}_3 \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 9.5 \times 9.8 \times 5.2 \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 484.18 \\
&\approx 9.6836
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical Interpretation Step (mandatory in KTU valuation):**

Since $HEP \approx 9.68 > 1$, this is **mathematically impossible** because probability cannot exceed 1. The result indicates that the **combined EPC effect is so severe** that the nominal task structure cannot absorb it. Practical HRA interpretation:

- Either the task must be **re-engineered** (add automatic shutdown)
- Or the EPCs must be **reduced** (improved training, reduce time pressure)
- Re-apply HEART with revised EPCs until $HEP \leq 1$ (typically target $HEP < 10^{-1}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Examiners explicitly test whether the student **recognizes and discusses the HEP > 1 anomaly**. If your answer simply states "HEP = 9.68" without commenting on the absurdity, you lose 2 marks automatically.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Error Categories

| Dimension | Slip / Lapse | Mistake | Violation |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Cognitive Level | Skill-Based | Rule/Knowledge-Based | Motivational |
| Intent | Correct | Correct | Wrong (deliberate) |
| Execution | Fails | Succeeds (at wrong action) | Succeeds (at wrong action) |
| Trigger | Distraction, interruption | Misdiagnosis, faulty rule | Culture, pressure, convenience |
| Frequency | Most common | Less frequent | Frequency depends on culture |
| Countermeasure | Alarms, checklists, ergonomics | Training, decision aids | Safety culture, supervision |
| KTU Cue Word | "Forgot to…" | "Misinterpreted…" | "Decided to skip…" |

---

## 3.3 Computational Continuity — From HEP to System Unreliability

In a QRA, individual HEPs feed into a **fault/event tree**. The top-event probability $P_{top}$ is computed as the **product of independent human and hardware failures**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{top} &= \prod_{i} HEP_i \times \prod_{j} U_{hardware,j} \\
&= HEP_{total} \times U_{equipment}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $U_{equipment}$ = unavailability of hardware (from reliability block diagram).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Threshold:** For Safety Instrumented Functions (SIF), the typical target **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** is between $10^{-5}$ and $10^{-2}$ depending on the SIL (Safety Integrity Level, IEC 61508). Human error contributions must keep the total PFD within these bounds.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The HRA Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Define Scope and Task Inventory"] --> B["Decompose Tasks into Sub-tasks"]
    B --> C["Identify Potential Human Errors per Sub-task"]
    C --> D["Screen for Error-Forcing Contexts and PSFs"]
    D --> E{"Qualitative or Quantitative Method?"}
    E -->|Qualitative| F["Apply HEART or SLIM-MAUD for HEP"]
    E -->|Quantitative| G["Apply THERP with Event Tree"]
    F --> H["Compute Nominal HEP times PSF Multipliers"]
    G --> I["Combine with Hardware Failure Data"]
    H --> J["Sensitivity Analysis"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["Recommend PSF Reduction Measures"]
    K --> L["Re-evaluate HEP and Document"]
```

## 4.2 The Swiss Cheese Model — Multi-Layer Defense

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Layer1["Layer 1: Engineering Controls - Inherent Safety"]
        A1["Hazard Elimination"]
        A2["Process Containment"]
        A3["Alarms and Trips"]
    end
    subgraph Layer2["Layer 2: Administrative Controls"]
        B1["Standard Operating Procedures"]
        B2["Permit to Work Systems"]
        B3["Training Programs"]
    end
    subgraph Layer3["Layer 3: Supervisory Controls"]
        C1["Shift Supervision"]
        C2["Independent Verification"]
        C3["Management of Change"]
    end
    subgraph Layer4["Layer 4: PPE and Human Action"]
        D1["Operator Response"]
        D2["Emergency PPE"]
        D3["Evacuation Drill"]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A3
    B1 --> B2 --> B3
    C1 --> C2 --> C3
    D1 --> D2 --> D3
    X["Latent Failure + Active Error Path"] -.-> A1
    X -.-> B1
    X -.-> C1
    X -.-> D1
```

## 4.3 Human Error Taxonomy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Human Failure Detected"] --> Q1{"Was the intended action correct?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{"Did execution match the plan?"}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{"Was the wrong action deliberate?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4["NO HUMAN ERROR - System or hardware failure"]
    Q2 -->|No| R1["SLIP OR LAPSE - Skill Based Error"]
    Q3 -->|No| R2["MISTAKE - Rule or Knowledge Based"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3["VIOLATION - Motivational Failure"]
    R1 --> S1["Countermeasure: Ergonomics, Checklists, Alarms"]
    R2 --> S2["Countermeasure: Training, Decision Aids, Procedures"]
    R3 --> S3["Countermeasure: Safety Culture, Supervision, Motivation"]
```

## 4.4 HEART Method Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Select Generic Task Type GTT"] --> B["Look up Nominal K0 from HEART Table"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Identify applicable EPCs"]
    C --> D["For each EPC, assign APLC value"]
    D --> E["Compute Multiplier = W times APLC plus 1"]
    E --> F["Step 3: HEP equals K0 times product of all multipliers"]
    F --> G{"HEP greater than 1?"}
    G -->|Yes| H["Re-engineer task or reduce EPCs"]
    G -->|No| I["HEP is acceptable - Document and Monitor"]
    H --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All four Mermaid diagrams follow the KTU-PREMIER alphanumeric node-ID rule. They depict the *process architecture* rather than physical drawings — this is intentional, per the protocol for complex industrial-safety topics.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Human Reliability Analysis (HRA). List any four Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Human Reliability Analysis (HRA) is a structured, quantitative and qualitative methodology used to evaluate the probability of human error in tasks that impact the safety and productivity of complex industrial systems. It identifies the conditions under which operators may fail, quantifies that failure probability (HEP), and recommends error-reduction measures.

> **Four PSFs (1 mark — 0.25 each):**
> 1. **Time pressure** (EPC-2 in HEART)
> 2. **Fatigue / sleep deprivation**
> 3. **Procedural clarity / ambiguity**
> 4. **Ergonomics / display design**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Slip and a Mistake in human error taxonomy. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Slip | Mistake |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Cognitive level** | Skill-based | Rule/knowledge-based |
| **Intent** | Correct intent, wrong execution | Wrong intent, executed correctly |
| **Cause** | Attention failure, distraction | Misdiagnosis, faulty reasoning |
| **Example** | Operator presses **STOP** instead of **START** on a pump (same location, adjacent button) | Operator interprets a low flow reading as **valve failure** and opens bypass, when the real cause is a **blocked strainer** |

> **Industrial context (1 mark):** Both error types appear in **Buncefield (2005)** and **Texas City (2005)** incident reports — slips dominate routine operations, mistakes dominate emergencies.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In HRA calculation questions, marks are awarded **only if** the student:
> 1. Names the chosen method (e.g., HEART or THERP).
> 2. Identifies the **Generic Task Type (GTT)** with its specific $K_0$ value.
> 3. Lists **each EPC** with both its $W$ value AND the APLC assigned.
> 4. Shows the **multiplication step explicitly** (no skipping).
> 5. Comments on **whether $HEP \leq 1$** and what to do if not.
>
> Omitting any one of these five elements typically costs **3–4 marks** per sub-question. Examiners specifically look for the **"+1"** in the HEART equation — forgetting it costs 1 full mark.

---

### Question A — Module Internal Choice 1 (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the HEART method for Human Reliability Analysis with its generic equation. Apply HEART to estimate the HEP for an operator resetting a firewater pump on a single attempt during low visibility conditions. Assume GTT-B ($K_0 = 2 \times 10^{-2}$), EPC-1 (Unfamiliarity, $W = 17$, APLC = 0.3), EPC-3 (Low signal-to-noise ratio, $W = 10$, APLC = 0.4), and EPC-2 (Time pressure, $W = 11$, APLC = 0.5).**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the HEART equation (2 marks):**

$$HEP = K_0 \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \times APLC_i + 1)$$

where:
- $K_0$ = nominal unreliability of the Generic Task Type (GTT)
- $W_i$ = maximum effect weight of EPC-$i$ (from HEART tables)
- $APLC_i$ = Assessed Proportion of the Effect of EPC-$i$

**Step 2 — Identify GTT and $K_0$ (1 mark):**
GTT = B (Restore system on single attempt) → $K_0 = 2 \times 10^{-2}$

**Step 3 — Compute each multiplier (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term}_1 &= (W_1 \times APLC_1) + 1 \\
&= (17 \times 0.3) + 1 \\
&= 5.1 + 1 = 6.1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term}_2 &= (W_2 \times APLC_2) + 1 \\
&= (10 \times 0.4) + 1 \\
&= 4.0 + 1 = 5.0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term}_3 &= (W_3 \times APLC_3) + 1 \\
&= (11 \times 0.5) + 1 \\
&= 5.5 + 1 = 6.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HEP &= 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 6.1 \times 5.0 \times 6.5 \\
&= 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 198.25 \\
&\approx 3.965
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion (mandatory interpretation):** Since $HEP \approx 3.97 > 1$, the result is not a valid probability. The combined EPC effects exceed what the nominal task structure can absorb. Recommended risk-control measures: **(i)** reduce time pressure by pre-staging the procedure, **(ii)** improve visibility by adding task lighting, **(iii)** increase operator familiarity through simulation drills. After mitigation, HEP must be re-evaluated.

---

#### Part (b) (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Discuss the classification of human errors based on Reason's taxonomy. With a suitable diagram, explain the Swiss Cheese Model of accident causation.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Reason's Error Taxonomy (4 marks):**

James Reason (1990) classified human error into three cognitive categories:

1. **Slips and Lapses (Skill-Based Errors):**
   - Occur during highly practiced, automatic behavior.
   - Plan is correct; execution fails.
   - Example: Pressing wrong button due to inattention.

2. **Mistakes (Rule-Based / Knowledge-Based Errors):**
   - Plan itself is incorrect; execution is flawless.
   - Subtypes: Rule-based (wrong rule applied) and Knowledge-based (no rule, reasoning fails).
   - Example: Misdiagnosing alarm cause.

3. **Violations (Motivational Failures):**
   - Deliberate deviation from procedure.
   - Routine, situational, or optimizing.
   - Example: Bypassing a lockout for speed.

**Swiss Cheese Model (3 marks):**

According to Reason, organizational accidents occur when the "holes" (latent failures, active errors, and PSF degradations) in **multiple defensive layers** momentarily align. The layers include:

- **Layer 1:** Engineering controls (alarms, trips, containment)
- **Layer 2:** Administrative controls (procedures, permits, training)
- **Layer 3:** Supervisory controls (oversight, verification, MOC)
- **Layer 4:** Operator response and PPE

Each slice has holes of varying size. When the holes align, the accident trajectory passes through all defenses. HRA aims to **shrink holes** (improve PSFs) and **add more slices** (defenses).

---

### Question B — Module Internal Choice 2 (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**List and explain any six Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs) that influence human error in process industries. State their direction of influence on HEP.**

**Model Solution (7 marks — 1 mark each + 1 for table):**

| # | PSF | Direction of Influence on HEP |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Fatigue / Sleep Deprivation** | ↑ HEP (errors of omission rise sharply after 12 h shift) |
| 2 | **High Workload** | ↑ HEP (cognition overload → slips) |
| 3 | **Low Workload / Vigilance Decrement** | ↑ HEP (errors from inattention during long monitoring) |
| 4 | **Poor Ergonomics (display/control layout)** | ↑ HEP (Fitts' Law violations, misread gauges) |
| 5 | **Time Pressure** | ↑ HEP (Haste → slip-and-rule-based errors) |
| 6 | **Inadequate Procedures (ambiguity)** | ↑ HEP (wrong rule application) |
| 7 | **Good Training and Experience** | ↓ HEP (better mental model) |
| 8 | **Effective Supervision** | ↓ HEP (catches errors early) |
| 9 | **Positive Safety Culture** | ↓ HEP (violations reduce) |

> **KTU mnemonic:** "FATIGUE, WORKLOAD, ERGO, TIME, PROCEDURE, TRAINING" — the six most-frequently asked PSFs in KTU exams.

---

#### Part (b) (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Compare THERP and CREAM methods of HRA. State their year of development, approach, and key strength.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Parameter | THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction) | CREAM (Cognitive Reliability and Error Analysis) |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Developer / Year** | Swain & Guttmann, 1983 (Sandia National Lab) | Erik Hollnagel, 1998 |
| **Type** | Decomposition-based, quantitative | Context-driven, cognitive |
| **Core Concept** | Task → Sub-task → HEP × PSF multiplier | Common Performance Conditions (CPCs) determine control mode |
| **Strength** | Large empirical database; integrated with event-tree analysis | Captures cognitive failure modes explicitly |
| **Weakness** | Data-intensive; weak on knowledge-based errors | Less mature empirical data |
| **Output** | Numerical HEP + dependency model | Control mode + qualitative HEP band |
| **Best Use Case** | Nuclear, aerospace QRA | Complex socio-technical systems, healthcare |

**Conclusion:** THERP remains the **industry benchmark for probabilistic HRA**, while CREAM excels in **cognitive/socio-technical** applications. Modern practice often uses **both** — THERP for quantification, CREAM for context verification.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this in your answer sheet memory):**

- ✅ **HRA = Human Reliability Analysis** — quantifies *Human Error Probability (HEP)* in QRA.
- ✅ **HEP ∈ [0, 1]** — typical range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ for routine tasks.
- ✅ **Three error types:** Slip (execution), Mistake (planning), Violation (motivational).
- ✅ **PSFs** modify the **base HEP** — internal, external, organizational.
- ✅ **HEART equation:** $HEP = K_0 \times \prod (W_i \times APLC_i + 1)$ — **never forget the +1**.
- ✅ **GTT-B** has $K_0 = 2 \times 10^{-2}$ — the most tested nominal value.
- ✅ **EPCs:** 38 listed in HEART — know at least 5 (Unfamiliarity, Time pressure, Distraction, Memory overload, Procedure ambiguity).
- ✅ **THERP (1983)** = quantification + event tree; **HEART (1986)** = screening + multiplicative.
- ✅ **Swiss Cheese Model** (Reason, 1990): accidents = alignment of holes in multiple defensive layers.
- ✅ **If computed HEP > 1** — *interpret*, don't ignore. Recommend PSF reduction + task re-engineering.
- ✅ **Human error contributes to 60–80%** of major industrial accidents — HRA is non-optional in any serious QRA.
- ✅ **Cognitive level determines countermeasure:** slips → ergonomics; mistakes → training; violations → culture.
- ✅ **Industrial case studies to mention** (impresses the examiner): **Bhopal 1984, Chernobyl 1986, Texas City 2005, Buncefield 2005, Deepwater Horizon 2010** — all have human-factors root causes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of HRA Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of HRA Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hazard and Risk Assessment (HRA) Techniques** are systematic, structured, and documented methodologies employed in Process Safety Management (PSM) and Safety Engineering to **identify, evaluate, prioritize, and control** potential hazards and their associated risks in industrial systems, processes, equipment, and human activities. The "characteristics" of these techniques refer to the defining attributes, scope, methodology type, applicability domain, and performance metrics that distinguish one technique from another, thereby guiding safety engineers in selecting the most appropriate method for a given scenario.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Characteristics of HRA techniques form the foundation for **technique selection** in Module 4. You must master the *selection criteria* — qualitative/quantitative, inductive/deductive, team-based/solitary — as these directly appear in KTU ESE Part B questions (14-markers).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the **"Characteristics"** of a technique are typically classified along **seven axis vectors**:

1. **Methodological Nature** — Qualitative, Semi-Quantitative, or Quantitative
2. **Logic Direction** — Inductive (Forward) or Deductive (Backward)
3. **Failure Analysis Depth** — Single-failure or Combined/Multiple-failure
4. **Team Configuration** — Individual-driven or Team-based
5. **Application Lifecycle Stage** — Design, Operation, Decommissioning, or Emergency
6. **Data & Resource Intensity** — Low, Medium, or High
7. **Output Type** — Risk ranking, Logic diagram, Risk register, or Numerical probability

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Standard Phrasing):** *"Characteristics of HRA techniques are the inherent analytical and procedural properties — such as methodological approach, logical direction, data requirements, and output format — that define how each technique identifies, analyzes, and evaluates hazards and risks, thereby enabling the selection of the most suitable technique for a specific industrial context."*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. The doctor has several tools at their disposal: a **stethoscope** (listens to heart sounds — quick, qualitative), an **X-ray** (reveals bone structure — detailed, requires specialist interpretation), and a **blood test** (gives numerical values — quantitative, requires lab). 

Similarly, a safety engineer has multiple HRA tools:

- **Checklist** = Stethoscope (Quick check, qualitative, pre-existing list)
- **HAZOP** = Full body examination by a team of specialists (Thorough, team-based, qualitative)
- **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** = MRI Scan (Shows logical relationships, quantitative, requires expertise)
- **FMEA** = X-ray of components (Examines individual parts, semi-quantitative)

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Box:** Choosing the right HRA technique is like choosing the right camera lens — **wide-angle (What-If Analysis)** for broad views, **macro lens (FMEA)** for component details, and **thermal imaging (FTA)** for hidden cause-effect chains. A skilled safety engineer knows when to switch lenses.

### Standard Metrics & Boundaries (KTU 2024 Standards)

- **Risk Acceptability Threshold (Fatal Accident Rate, FAR):** $FAR = 10^{-3}$ per person-year (intolerable), $10^{-6}$ per person-year (broadly acceptable per **ILO/UK HSE Tolerability of Risk framework**).
- **Risk Matrix Categories:** Typically **5×5** grids with severity rated from **1 (Negligible)** to **5 (Catastrophic)** and likelihood from **1 (Rare)** to **5 (Frequent)**.
- **Standard RPN (Risk Priority Number) in FMEA:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$, where each factor ranges **1–10**.
- **Bow-Tie Elements:** A **single Top Event** flanked by a **Threat-side (Causes)** fault tree and a **Consequence-side (Effects)** event tree.
- **Team Size Norm (HAZOP):** **4–8 members** (including independent chairman, scribe, and process experts).

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Student Misconception:** Students often confuse *"What-If"* (broad, brainstorming) with *"Checklist"* (narrow, item-based). Remember — **Checklists are static & confirmatory**; **What-If is dynamic & exploratory**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Defining Characteristics of HRA Techniques — Expanded Analysis

#### Characteristic 1: Methodological Nature (Qualitative / Semi-Quantitative / Quantitative)

This characteristic defines the **output type** of the analysis.

- **Qualitative Techniques:** Use descriptive categories (Low/Medium/High) for risk ranking.
  - Examples: **HAZOP, What-If Analysis, Checklists**.
  - Output: Risk register with categorical risk levels.
  - **Why use them?** Fast, low-cost, no numerical data required.

- **Semi-Quantitative Techniques:** Assign numerical scores but not absolute probabilities.
  - Examples: **FMEA (RPN method)**, **Risk Matrix Scoring**.
  - Output: Numerical priority scores (e.g., RPN = 120).

- **Quantitative Techniques:** Calculate actual failure frequencies or probabilities.
  - Examples: **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**, **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)**, **QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment)**.
  - Output: Numerical probability values (e.g., $P = 2.3 \times 10^{-4}$/year).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In the **petrochemical industry**, qualitative methods (HAZOP) are used during **design phase** for hazard identification, while quantitative methods (FTA/ETA) are used to comply with **regulatory standards** like the **CIMAH Regulations 1984** (UK) or **OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119** (USA) for high-consequence facilities.

#### Characteristic 2: Logic Direction (Inductive vs. Deductive)

- **Inductive (Forward / Bottom-Up):**
  - Starts from a **deviation/cause** → works forward to identify all **consequences**.
  - **Logic Flow:** "If this fails, what happens?"
  - Examples: **HAZOP, FMEA, What-If, ETA** (event tree branches from initiating event).
  - **Best for:** Identifying *all possible outcomes* of a known initiating event.

- **Deductive (Backward / Top-Down):**
  - Starts from a **top event (unwanted outcome)** → works backward to identify all **causes**.
  - **Logic Flow:** "How could this happen?"
  - Examples: **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**.
  - **Best for:** Identifying *all combinations of failures* that could lead to a known disaster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A typical 7-mark question asks *"Justify whether FTA is suitable for analyzing multiple simultaneous failures."* The answer hinges on its **deductive** nature and use of **AND/OR logic gates**.

#### Characteristic 3: Failure Analysis Depth

- **Single-Failure Mode Analysis:** Assumes one component fails at a time.
  - Example: **FMEA** traditionally assumes one failure per component analysis.
- **Combined/Multiple-Failure Mode Analysis:** Considers simultaneous or cascading failures.
  - Example: **FTA** uses **AND gates** to model simultaneous failures.
  - **ETA** models sequences following a single initiating event.
  - **Bow-Tie** combines both for comprehensive cause-consequence analysis.

#### Characteristic 4: Team Configuration

- **Team-Based (Collaborative):** Requires multidisciplinary team for brainstorming.
  - HAZOP: 4–8 experts (process engineer, control engineer, safety engineer, operator, maintenance).
  - What-If: 3–6 experts for structured questioning.
  - **Why team?** Diverse perspectives uncover blind spots; reduces individual cognitive bias.

- **Individual-Driven (Expert-Only):** Can be executed by a single analyst.
  - Checklists: Pre-prepared by experts, executed by operators/inspectors.
  - FMEA: Often executed by design engineer (though best with team input).
  - **Why individual?** Standardized, repeatable, low-cost for routine assessments.

#### Characteristic 5: Application Lifecycle Stage

- **Design Phase:** HAZOP, FMEA, What-If (prospective hazard identification).
- **Operational Phase:** Checklists (routine inspection), ETA (incident scenario analysis).
- **Decommissioning Phase:** What-If, Checklists (identify dismantling hazards).
- **Emergency Planning:** Bow-Tie, ETA (map escalation pathways).
- **Continuous/Periodic:** Checklists, routine HAZOP reviews after modifications.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **Greenfield projects** (new plant design), **HAZOP + FMEA** is mandatory. In **Brownfield projects** (existing plants), **What-If + Checklists** are commonly used for **Management of Change (MOC)** assessments.

#### Characteristic 6: Data & Resource Intensity

| Resource Type | Low | Medium | High |
|---------------|-----|--------|------|
| Time Required | 1–2 days | 1–4 weeks | 1–6 months |
| Cost (Approx.) | <$10K | $10K–$100K | >$100K |
| Expertise | Operator-level | Engineer-level | Specialist + modeling software |
| Examples | Checklists, What-If | FMEA, HAZOP | QRA, FTA, Bow-Tie |

#### Characteristic 7: Output Type

- **Risk Register:** HAZOP, FMEA, Checklists (tabular hazard list).
- **Logic Diagram:** FTA (tree), ETA (branching), Bow-Tie (threats + consequences).
- **Categorical Risk Score:** Risk Matrix, What-If (High/Medium/Low).
- **Numerical Probability:** FTA, ETA, QRA (with failure rate data).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **board exam-ready reference** for quantitative HRA characteristics. Memorize these for the Part A 3-mark questions.

| S.No. | Formula / Symbol | Definition | Typical Use | Units / Boundary |
|-------|------------------|------------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number (FMEA) | Component-level risk ranking | Dimensionless; range 1–1000 |
| 2 | $P_{TOP} = \prod_{i} P_{i}$ (AND gate) | Probability of Top Event (all components must fail) | FTA — parallel system | Probability (0–1) |
| 3 | $P_{TOP} = 1 - \prod_{i} (1 - P_{i})$ (OR gate) | Probability of Top Event (at least one fails) | FTA — series system | Probability (0–1) |
| 4 | $FAR = \frac{N_{fatalities}}{N_{workers} \times T_{exposure}}$ | Fatal Accident Rate | Risk tolerability check | fatalities per $10^{8}$ person-hours |
| 5 | $Risk = Severity \times Likelihood$ | Generic Risk Score | Risk matrix | Dimensionless score |
| 6 | $P_{event/year} = \lambda \times t$ | Event frequency (rare event approximation) | ETA initiating event | Events per year |
| 7 | $MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Reliability data for FTA/ETA | Hours / cycles |
| 8 | $Unavailability = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \approx \lambda t$ | Component unavailability | FTA basic events | Probability (0–1) |
| 9 | $R_{acceptable} < R_{tolerable}$ | Risk acceptance criterion | Decision-making | Per regulatory standard |
| 10 | $ALARP$: $Cost \propto Risk Reduction$ | As Low As Reasonably Practicable | Risk mitigation economic test | Cost-effectiveness ratio |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **Offshore Oil & Gas industry** (e.g., ONGC, Shell), the standard pipeline is:
1. **HAZID (Hazard Identification)** at conceptual stage — qualitative team-based.
2. **HAZOP** at detailed design — qualitative team-based, comprehensive.
3. **FMEA** for critical equipment (e.g., Emergency Shutdown Valves) — semi-quantitative.
4. **FTA** for major accident hazards (e.g., gas release, explosion) — quantitative.
5. **ETA** for consequence modeling (fire, explosion, toxic dispersion) — quantitative.
6. **QRA** for siting, layout, and emergency planning — fully quantitative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industrial Connection:** The **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)** directly influenced the development of **HRA characteristics standardization** worldwide. Post-Bhopal, the **Methyl Isocyanate (MIC) release** highlighted the need for *deductive* (FTA) and *team-based* (HAZOP) techniques — characteristics now standard in **CIMAH/Seveso/COMAH** regulations.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — Top Event Probability for AND Gate

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **mandatory for 7-mark Part B sub-questions** in KTU ESE. Practice writing the aligned equations with text justification.

**Problem Setup:** A safety system has **three independent barriers** (B1, B2, B3) that must *all* fail for a top event to occur (modeled as an **AND gate**). Given:
- $P(B1) = 10^{-2}$
- $P(B2) = 10^{-3}$
- $P(B3) = 10^{-4}$

**Derive the probability of the Top Event.**

**Step 1: Identify the Logic Structure**

For an **AND gate**, the top event occurs *only* if **all input events occur simultaneously**. Since the three barriers are stated to be **independent**, the joint probability is the product of individual probabilities.

**Step 2: Apply the AND-Gate Probability Formula**

For independent events $A$, $B$, $C$:

$$P(A \cap B \cap C) = P(A) \times P(B) \times P(C)$$

**Step 3: Substitute the Values**

$$P_{TOP} = P(B1) \times P(B2) \times P(B3)$$

$$P_{TOP} = 10^{-2} \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Result**

$$P_{TOP} = 10^{-2-3-4} = 10^{-9}$$

**Step 5: Interpret the Result**

$$P_{TOP} = 1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ per demand}$$

**Interpretation:** The probability of the top event is one-in-a-billion per demand, which is **well below the broadly acceptable threshold of $10^{-6}$** per UK HSE standards. Therefore, the three-barrier system provides **sufficient risk reduction** for the analyzed hazard.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — OR Gate Probability

**Problem Setup:** A system has **two independent pumps** (P1, P2). The top event (no flow) occurs if **either pump fails** (OR gate). Given:
- $P(P1 \text{ fails}) = 0.01$
- $P(P2 \text{ fails}) = 0.02$

**Derive the probability of the Top Event (system failure).**

**Step 1: Identify the Logic Structure**

For an **OR gate**, the top event occurs if **at least one** of the input events occurs. The direct approach is the **inclusion-exclusion principle** for two events:

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

For independent events, $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \times P(B)$.

**Step 2: Apply the OR-Gate Probability Formula (Complement Form)**

The complement approach is often simpler:

$$P(A \cup B) = 1 - P(\bar{A} \cap \bar{B}) = 1 - P(\bar{A}) \times P(\bar{B})$$

**Step 3: Compute the Complement Probabilities**

$$P(\bar{P1}) = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

$$P(\bar{P2}) = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the OR-Gate Formula**

$$P_{TOP} = 1 - (0.99 \times 0.98)$$

**Step 5: Compute the Product**

$$0.99 \times 0.98 = 0.9702$$

**Step 6: Final Calculation**

$$P_{TOP} = 1 - 0.9702 = 0.0298$$

**Step 7: Interpretation**

$$P_{TOP} \approx 2.98 \times 10^{-2} = 2.98\%$$

**Interpretation:** The probability of system failure (no flow) is approximately **2.98% per demand**, which is **unacceptably high** for a critical safety system. This indicates a **single-point-of-vulnerability** and demands **redundancy improvement** (e.g., adding a third pump or installing a backup power supply).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: FMEA — Risk Priority Number (RPN) Calculation

**Problem Setup:** Analyzing a **pressure relief valve** in a chemical reactor. The failure mode is *"Valve fails to open on demand."*
- **Severity (S):** Catastrophic — rated **9** (out of 10)
- **Occurrence (O):** Occasional — rated **4** (out of 10)
- **Detection (D):** Moderate — rated **5** (out of 10)

**Calculate the RPN and recommend action.**

**Step 1: Recall the RPN Formula**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Values**

$$RPN = 9 \times 4 \times 5$$

**Step 3: Compute the Result**

$$RPN = 9 \times 20 = 180$$

**Step 4: Interpret the RPN Value**

Standard RPN thresholds (industry convention):
- $RPN < 100$: **Acceptable** — no action required.
- $100 \leq RPN < 200$: **Caution** — consider mitigation.
- $RPN \geq 200$: **Unacceptable** — mandatory corrective action.

**Conclusion:** RPN = 180 falls in the **Caution** zone. Recommended actions:
- Increase inspection frequency (reduce $O$).
- Install detection instrumentation (reduce $D$).
- Add redundant relief path (reduce $S$ effect).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of HRA Techniques by Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison matrix is the **single most important** revision tool for KTU ESE Part B (14-mark) questions. It will appear in some form in nearly every exam.

| Technique | Nature | Logic | Failure Depth | Team-Based? | Lifecycle Stage | Data Intensity | Output Type |
|-----------|--------|-------|---------------|-------------|-----------------|----------------|-------------|
| **HAZOP** | Qualitative | Inductive | Single & Combined | Yes (4–8) | Design, Modification | Medium | Risk register + recommendations |
| **FMEA** | Semi-Quantitative | Inductive | Primarily Single | No (individual) | Design, Maintenance | Medium | RPN rankings |
| **FTA** | Quantitative | Deductive | Combined (AND/OR) | No (analyst) | Design, Incident investigation | High | Logic tree + probability |
| **ETA** | Quantitative | Inductive (forward from init. event) | Cascading sequences | No (analyst) | Design, Emergency planning | High | Event tree + frequency |
| **What-If** | Qualitative | Inductive | Single & Combined | Yes (3–6) | Any stage | Low–Medium | Question-answer list |
| **Checklist** | Qualitative | Confirmatory | Single | No (operator) | Operations, Inspection | Low | Pass/Fail list |
| **Bow-Tie** | Semi-Quant./Quant. | Both (combined) | Combined | Yes (small team) | Operations, MOC | High | Visual diagram |
| **LOPA** | Semi-Quantitative | Both | Combined | Yes (small team) | Design, Operations | Medium–High | Risk gap analysis |
| **Hazard Index (Dow/Mond)** | Quantitative | Scoring | N/A | No (individual) | Early design, Siting | Medium | Fire/explosion index |

---

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Logic: How to Select an HRA Technique (Decision Tree Logic)

**Step 1:** Define the purpose — *Hazard identification? Risk evaluation? Both?*
- If only **identification** → **What-If, Checklist, HAZOP, HAZID**.
- If **evaluation with ranking** → **FMEA, Risk Matrix, LOPA**.
- If **probabilistic quantification** → **FTA, ETA, QRA**.

**Step 2:** Define the project stage.
- **Conceptual/Feasibility:** What-If, Hazard Index, Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA).
- **Detailed Design:** HAZOP, FMEA, FTA.
- **Operations:** Checklists, Bow-Tie, ETA.
- **Decommissioning:** What-If, Checklists.

**Step 3:** Assess data availability.
- **Limited data:** Qualitative methods (HAZOP, What-If).
- **Reliable failure rate databases** (e.g., OREDA, IEEE 500): FTA, ETA.

**Step 4:** Consider resources and timeline.
- **Tight timeline, low budget:** Checklists, What-If.
- **Adequate time and resources:** HAZOP, FMEA.
- **Long-term, critical hazard:** FTA, ETA, QRA.

**Step 5:** Match to regulatory requirements.
- **PSM, Seveso, COMAH compliance:** HAZOP + LOPA + QRA (mandatory combination).

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part A carries 3 marks, typically 1–2 sentence answers, testing direct recall. Each question below is followed by a **precise model answer** matching KTU board valuation key length.

---

#### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: List any three characteristics of HRA techniques used in process safety management.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three characteristics of HRA techniques are:

1. **Methodological Nature** — Techniques can be qualitative (e.g., HAZOP, What-If), semi-quantitative (e.g., FMEA, LOPA), or quantitative (e.g., FTA, ETA) based on the type of output produced.

2. **Logic Direction** — Techniques follow either an **inductive (forward)** approach (e.g., HAZOP — start from deviation and find consequences) or a **deductive (backward)** approach (e.g., FTA — start from top event and find causes).

3. **Team Configuration** — Some techniques are team-based requiring multidisciplinary experts (e.g., HAZOP with 4–8 members), while others are individual-driven (e.g., Checklists executed by a single operator).

*(1 mark per characteristic with correct example — Total 3 marks)*

---

#### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]

**Q: Differentiate between inductive and deductive reasoning in Hazard and Risk Assessment techniques. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Inductive Reasoning | Deductive Reasoning |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Direction** | Forward / Bottom-up | Backward / Top-down |
| **Starting Point** | Deviation or cause | Top event (undesired outcome) |
| **Question Asked** | "If this fails, what happens?" | "How could this happen?" |
| **Example Technique** | HAZOP, FMEA, What-If | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) |
| **Output** | List of possible consequences | Combination of root causes |

*(1.5 marks for distinction + 1.5 marks for correct example pair — Total 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice**. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Below are TWO complete alternative questions (A and B). Choose ONE to attempt.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: As a process safety engineer, you are tasked with selecting the most appropriate HRA technique for a new offshore drilling platform project. Answer the following:**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **seven key characteristics** used to differentiate HRA techniques. For each characteristic, provide **one example technique** that exemplifies it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the offshore drilling platform project, justify the selection of **HAZOP as the primary technique** for design-phase hazard identification, citing at least **four specific characteristics** of HAZOP that make it suitable. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

The seven key characteristics of HRA techniques are:

**1. Methodological Nature (1 mark)**

HRA techniques can be **qualitative** (HAZOP, What-If, Checklist) where risks are categorized descriptively (High/Medium/Low), **semi-quantitative** (FMEA, LOPA) where numerical scores like RPN are assigned, or **quantitative** (FTA, ETA, QRA) where actual failure probabilities are calculated.

**2. Logic Direction (1 mark)**

Techniques follow either an **inductive (forward) approach** (e.g., HAZOP — starts from a process deviation like "no flow" and explores consequences) or a **deductive (backward) approach** (e.g., FTA — starts from a top event like "blowout" and identifies all causes).

**3. Failure Analysis Depth (1 mark)**

Some techniques analyze **single failure modes** at a time (FMEA traditionally examines one component failure), while others analyze **combined/multiple failures** simultaneously (FTA uses AND gates to model simultaneous barrier failures).

**4. Team Configuration (1 mark)**

**Team-based techniques** (HAZOP with 4–8 multidisciplinary experts, What-If with 3–6 experts) leverage diverse perspectives for comprehensive hazard identification. **Individual-driven techniques** (Checklists, FMEA by design engineer) are standardized and repeatable.

**5. Application Lifecycle Stage (1 mark)**

Techniques are applied at different stages: **Design phase** (HAZOP, FMEA for prospective hazard identification), **Operational phase** (Checklists for routine inspection, Bow-Tie for barrier management), and **Decommissioning phase** (What-If, Checklists).

**6. Data & Resource Intensity (1 mark)**

**Low-intensity** techniques (Checklists, What-If) require minimal time, cost, and expertise. **Medium-intensity** techniques (HAZOP, FMEA) require 1–4 weeks and trained engineers. **High-intensity** techniques (FTA, ETA, QRA) require specialized software, failure rate databases (e.g., OREDA), and 1–6 months.

**7. Output Type (1 mark)**

Outputs vary from **risk registers** (HAZOP, FMEA, Checklists — tabular lists) to **logic diagrams** (FTA, ETA, Bow-Tie — visual trees), **categorical scores** (Risk Matrix — High/Medium/Low), and **numerical probabilities** (QRA — $P = 2.3 \times 10^{-4}$/year).

---

##### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Justification for HAZOP Selection in Offshore Drilling Platform Design Phase:**

HAZOP is the optimal primary technique for this application based on the following **four characteristics**:

**1. Inductive Logic Direction (1.75 marks)**

HAZOP follows an **inductive (forward) reasoning** approach, starting from process deviations (e.g., "no flow," "high pressure," "reverse flow") and systematically identifying all possible consequences. For a **complex offshore platform** with interconnected drilling, processing, and safety systems, this forward exploration ensures **all potential hazard pathways** are discovered — critical for a facility where missed hazards can lead to **catastrophic blowouts or explosions** (e.g., Deepwater Horizon 2010 incident).

**2. Team-Based Configuration (1.75 marks)**

HAZOP requires a **multidisciplinary team of 4–8 experts** including process engineers, drilling engineers, control/instrumentation engineers, safety engineers, experienced offshore operators, and HSE managers. Offshore platforms have **complex, integrated systems** (BOP stack, mud circulation, wellhead, separators) requiring diverse expertise to identify subtle interaction hazards that no single analyst could detect.

**3. Qualitative Output Suitable for Design Decisions (1.75 marks)**

HAZOP produces a **qualitative risk register** with categorized risk levels and **action items** (e.g., "install additional high-pressure alarm," "review ESD logic"). During the design phase, **detailed failure rate data is often unavailable**, making quantitative techniques (FTA, ETA) impractical. The qualitative HAZOP output directly supports **design modification decisions** before construction.

**4. Application at Design Lifecycle Stage (1.75 marks)**

HAZOP is **specifically designed for the detailed design phase** of process plants. It examines **nodes (sections of piping/equipment)** using **guide words** (No/Not, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than) applied to **process parameters** (flow, pressure, temperature, level). For an offshore platform's **wellhead and processing train**, this structured node-by-node analysis ensures **comprehensive coverage** of all process sections before procurement and construction begins.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Alternative]

**Q: A chemical manufacturing plant has experienced a series of near-miss incidents. The safety manager wants to deploy appropriate HRA techniques. Answer the following:**

**(a)** Compare **HAZOP, FMEA, and FTA** based on the characteristics: **methodological nature, logic direction, team configuration, and output type**. Present your answer in a comparative table format with justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reactor system has **two independent safety barriers** that must both fail for an explosion to occur. The failure probability of Barrier 1 is $5 \times 10^{-3}$ and Barrier 2 is $8 \times 10^{-3}$. **Calculate the explosion probability** and comment on its acceptability against the **UK HSE broadly acceptable risk threshold of $10^{-6}$ per year**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Comparative Analysis of HAZOP, FMEA, and FTA:**

| Characteristic | HAZOP | FMEA | FTA |
|----------------|-------|------|-----|
| **Methodological Nature** | Qualitative — uses descriptive risk categories (High/Medium/Low) | Semi-Quantitative — uses numerical RPN scores | Quantitative — calculates actual failure probabilities |
| **Logic Direction** | Inductive (forward) — starts from process deviations, explores consequences | Inductive (forward) — starts from component failure modes, identifies effects | Deductive (backward) — starts from top event, identifies root causes |
| **Team Configuration** | Team-based (4–8 multidisciplinary experts) | Can be individual (design engineer) or small team (2–3) | Individual (specialist analyst with modeling software) |
| **Output Type** | Tabular risk register with node-by-node findings and recommendations | RPN-ranked list of component failure modes with severity/occurrence/detection scores | Logic tree diagram with AND/OR gates and top event probability |

**Justification:**

- **HAZOP** is best suited for **process industries** (chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas) where systematic exploration of process deviations is needed. Its team-based approach ensures comprehensive coverage but is **time-intensive (1–4 weeks)**.

- **FMEA** is best suited for **equipment-level reliability analysis** (e.g., valves, pumps, instruments). Its semi-quantitative RPN enables **prioritization of maintenance resources** but may miss **system-level interactions**.

- **FTA** is best suited for **complex systems with multiple failure combinations** (e.g., reactor protection systems, aircraft, nuclear plants). It requires **reliable failure rate data** (e.g., OREDA, IEEE Std 500) and produces **probabilistic outputs** for regulatory compliance.

*(1.75 marks per row with correct comparison — Total 7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Calculation of Explosion Probability:**

**Step 1: Identify the Logic Structure (1 mark)**

The problem states that **both barriers must fail** for the explosion to occur. This is an **AND gate** configuration in Fault Tree Analysis terminology.

**Step 2: Recall the AND-Gate Formula for Independent Events (1 mark)**

$$P_{TOP} = P(B1) \times P(B2)$$

**Step 3: Apply the Independence Assumption (1 mark)**

Since the two barriers are stated to be **independent**, the joint probability is the product of individual probabilities.

**Step 4: Substitute the Given Values (1 mark)**

$$P_{TOP} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5: Multiply the Coefficients (1 mark)**

$$P_{TOP} = (5 \times 8) \times 10^{-3-3}$$

$$P_{TOP} = 40 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 6: Express in Standard Scientific Notation (1 mark)**

$$P_{TOP} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

**Step 7: Compare with HSE Threshold and Comment (1 mark)**

The calculated explosion probability is $4.0 \times 10^{-5}$ per year, which is **40 times higher** than the **UK HSE broadly acceptable risk threshold of $10^{-6}$ per year**.

**Conclusion:** The current two-barrier system does **NOT meet** the broadly acceptable risk standard. The explosion risk falls in the **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) region** between $10^{-6}$ and $10^{-4}$ per year, meaning:

- Risk reduction measures are **mandatory**.
- Cost-benefit analysis must be performed to implement additional safeguards.
- Possible recommendations: add a **third independent barrier**, install a **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** with high Safety Integrity Level (SIL-3), or improve **barrier testing frequency** to reduce unavailability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Marks lost for missing units:** Always write the answer in **per year** or **per demand** — examiners deduct 0.5 mark if units are omitted.
> 2. **Marks lost for non-standard notation:** Write as $4.0 \times 10^{-5}$ per year, NOT $4 \times 10^{-5}$ (KTU expects explicit power-of-10 notation).
> 3. **Marks lost for not commenting on acceptability:** Numerical answer alone is worth 5/7 marks. The **comparison with HSE threshold and ALARP discussion** is worth the remaining 2 marks — this is where most students lose marks.
> 4. **Marks lost for assuming dependence:** If students incorrectly assume dependence, the answer will be wrong. Always state the **independence assumption explicitly** to earn full marks.
> 5. **Marks lost for missing recommendation:** The question asks to "comment on acceptability" — a complete answer must include a **concrete recommendation** (e.g., add third barrier, install SIF).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist for last-minute KTU exam revision. Each bullet is a guaranteed potential 3–7 mark question.**

**Core Definitions:**
- ✅ HRA = **Hazard and Risk Assessment** — systematic identification, evaluation, and control of hazards.
- ✅ Characteristics = the **defining properties** (7 axes) that distinguish one HRA technique from another.
- ✅ Inductive = **forward (cause → effect)**; Deductive = **backward (effect → cause)**.

**Seven Characteristics — Quick Memory Aid (Mnemonic: "MLF-T-LD-RO"):**
- ✅ **M**ethodological nature (Qual / Semi-Quant / Quant)
- ✅ **L**ogic direction (Inductive / Deductive)
- ✅ **F**ailure depth (Single / Combined)
- ✅ **T**eam configuration (Team / Individual)
- ✅ **L**ifecycle stage (Design / Operations / Decommissioning)
- ✅ **D**ata intensity (Low / Medium / High)
- ✅ **R**esource & **O**utput type (Diagram / Register / Score / Probability)

**Technique Matching (Master This!):**
- ✅ **HAZOP** = Qualitative + Inductive + Team (4–8) + Design + Risk Register
- ✅ **FMEA** = Semi-Quantitative + Inductive + Individual + Design + RPN
- ✅ **FTA** = Quantitative + Deductive + Individual + Design/Investigation + Logic Tree + Probability
- ✅ **ETA** = Quantitative + Inductive (forward) + Individual + Operations + Event Tree
- ✅ **What-If** = Qualitative + Inductive + Team (3–6) + Any stage + Q&A list
- ✅ **Checklist** = Qualitative + Confirmatory + Individual + Operations + Pass/Fail
- ✅ **Bow-Tie** = Semi-Quant/Quant + Both + Small team + Operations + Diagram
- ✅ **LOPA** = Semi-Quantitative + Both + Small team + Design/Operations + Risk gap

**Critical Formulas (Memorize These 3!):**
- ✅ $RPN = S \times O \times D$ (FMEA)
- ✅ $P_{AND} = \prod P_i$ (FTA AND gate)
- ✅ $P_{OR} = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$ (FTA OR gate)

**Industry Standards (Examiner's Favorites!):**
- ✅ UK HSE broadly acceptable risk = $10^{-6}$ per year
- ✅ UK HSE intolerable risk = $10^{-4}$ per year
- ✅ ALARP region = $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$ per year
- ✅ HAZOP team size = 4–8 members
- ✅ RPN threshold: <100 OK, 100–200 caution, >200 unacceptable
- ✅ Standard guide words: No, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than
- ✅ RPN scale: 1–10 for each of S, O, D (max RPN = 1000)

**Historical Connection (Frequently Asked!):**
- ✅ **Bhopal 1984 (MIC release)** → drove HRA standardization worldwide
- ✅ **Flixborough 1974 (Nypro caprolactam plant)** → led to mandatory HAZOP in UK (CIMAH 1984)
- ✅ **Piper Alpha 1988 (North Sea)** → led to offshore safety directives
- ✅ **Deepwater Horizon 2010** → highlighted BOP and well integrity HRA gaps

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ❌ Don't confuse **What-If** (exploratory) with **Checklist** (confirmatory).
- ❌ Don't assume **FMEA** is fully quantitative — it is **semi-quantitative** (RPN only).
- ❌ Don't forget that **FTA is deductive** and **ETA is inductive** — common mix-up.
- ❌ Don't write $P_{OR} = P_1 + P_2$ — this is **only valid** for mutually exclusive events.
- ❌ Don't forget the **independence assumption** in FTA calculations.

**Selection Logic (When in Doubt, Use This):**
- ✅ Need quick qualitative check → **Checklist**
- ✅ Need design-phase team analysis → **HAZOP**
- ✅ Need component reliability ranking → **FMEA**
- ✅ Need root cause analysis of known incident → **FTA**
- ✅ Need consequence modeling from initiating event → **ETA**
- ✅ Need integrated cause-consequence with barriers → **Bow-Tie**
- ✅ Need risk gap analysis for SIL determination → **LOPA**

**Final Exam Tip:**
> ✅ In **Part B 14-mark questions**, always structure your answer as: (1) Define key terms, (2) List characteristics with examples, (3) Justify selection using the **7-axis framework**, (4) If calculation is involved, show **all algebraic steps** with **units** and **commentary on acceptability**.

**Confidence Check — Can You Answer These Without Looking?**
- ✅ What is the RPN formula and its three components?
- ✅ What is the difference between inductive and deductive HRA techniques? Give examples.
- ✅ What are the three risk regions defined by UK HSE? What is ALARP?
