- ✅ For an AND gate with 3 barriers of probabilities $10^{-2}$, $10^{-3}$, $10^{-4}$, what is the top event probability?
- ✅ List four characteristics of HAZOP that make it suitable for design phase.

If you answered **YES** to all five, you are **fully prepared** for KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Best of luck! 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction (THERP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction (THERP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction)** is a structured, probabilistic **Human Reliability Analysis (HRA)** methodology developed by **A.D. Swain and H.E. Guttmann** in **1983** (documented in the landmark NUREG/CR-1278 report for the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission). It is used to **quantitatively predict the probability that an operator (or human) will fail to perform a task correctly** within a given time window, given a set of operating conditions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, THERP is a **first-generation, event-tree-based HRA technique** that decomposes a procedure into elementary human actions, assigns a **Nominal Human Error Probability (NHEP)** to each action, and then multiplies it by **Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs)** to obtain a context-corrected **Human Error Probability (HEP)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **bank security auditor** trying to predict the chance a tired night-shift teller will make a mistake while processing a transaction. You can't watch every transaction, but you *can* break the job into tiny steps — pick up cash, count cash, count receipt, hand to customer. For each step, you know from past data that tellers make a mistake roughly **1 time in 1000** (i.e., **1 × 10⁻³**). However, tonight the teller is sleepy, the lighting is poor, and the system is new. You "adjust" the base mistake rate upward. 

**THERP works exactly this way:**
- **Decompose** the operator's task → step-by-step (called a *Task Analysis*).
- **Assign** a base error rate (Nominal HEP) → from a built-in handbook.
- **Adjust** with reality factors (PSFs) → stress, training, ergonomics.
- **Combine** results if errors are dependent (α-factor model).

### 1.3 Core Highlights

> [!NOTE]
> - **First developed:** 1983 (NUREG/CR-1278) by **Swain & Guttmann**.
> - **Domain:** Originally **nuclear reactor safety**; now widely used in **aerospace, petrochemical, and process industries**.
> - **Output:** Numerical probability values (e.g., 1 × 10⁻³, 5 × 10⁻²) that can be **plugged directly into a fault tree or event tree**.
> - **Compendium:** Contains a tabulated library of **~300+ Nominal HEPs** for routine cognitive and physical tasks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Philosophy

THERP is built on **three foundational principles**:

1. **Reductionism (Task Decomposition):** Any complex human task is broken into a sequence of sub-tasks or "elements" small enough that the failure rate of each is empirically known or estimable.
2. **Empirical Quantification:** Human failure is treated like equipment failure — it has a measurable probability, drawn from operational data, simulator studies, or expert judgment.
3. **Contextual Modulation:** A base failure rate is never used raw; the operational context multiplies it through **Performance Shaping Factors (PSFs)**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

- **Step 1 — Define the Human-System Interface:** Identify the procedure, scenario, and success criteria.
- **Step 2 — Task Analysis:** Decompose the procedure into a logical tree of *Elementary Human Actions* (e.g., "press button A", "read gauge X").
- **Step 3 — Assign Nominal HEPs:** For each element, look up the **NHEP** from the THERP handbook (Table 20-1 to 20-21 in the original report).
- **Step 4 — Apply PSFs:** Multiply the NHEP by a PSF multiplier that reflects the operating conditions (stress, time of day, training, equipment quality).
- **Step 5 — Model Dependency:** If two errors are performed by the **same person in close succession**, they may not be statistically independent. Use the **α-factor (alpha factor)** model.
- **Step 6 — Aggregate:** Combine all sub-task HEPs into the overall task HEP using Boolean or dependency rules.
- **Step 7 — Feed into Event/Fault Tree:** The HEP replaces a basic event probability in the larger system model.

### 2.3 Key Quantitative Constructs

#### A. Basic Human Error Probability (HEP)

The simplest form is:

$$
\text{HEP} = \text{NHEP} \times \text{PSF multiplier}
$$

#### B. Human Error Rate (HER)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HER** is the *frequency of error per unit opportunity* (e.g., errors per demand or errors per hour). It is **dimensionally** different from HEP. For non-recoverable, discrete tasks, **HER ≈ HEP**.

#### C. Dependency Modeling — The α-Factor

When two consecutive errors A and B are performed by the **same operator**, the conditional probability of B given A is denoted **q**. The α-factor is defined as:

$$
\alpha = \frac{q - P_B}{1 - P_B}
$$

Where:
- $P_B$ = HEP of the second error if performed **independently**
- $q$ = actual conditional HEP of B given A occurred

The joint probability of both A *and* B failing in dependence is then:

$$
P(A \cap B) = \frac{1 + \alpha}{ \frac{1}{P_A} + \frac{1}{P_B} - 1 }
$$

**Special Cases of α:**
- **Zero dependency (α = 0):** $P(A \cap B) = P_A \times P_B$ (pure independence)
- **Complete dependency (α = 1):** $P(A \cap B) = P_A$
- **High dependency (α → 1):** Approaches complete dependence.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Basic HEP | $\text{HEP} = \text{NHEP} \times \text{PSF}$ | Dimensionless probability (0 to 1) |
| 2 | Independence | $P(A \cap B) = P_A \times P_B$ | Used when $\alpha = 0$ |
| 3 | α-factor | $\alpha = \dfrac{q - P_B}{1 - P_B}$ | Ranges from 0 to 1 |
| 4 | General dependency | $P(A \cap B) = \dfrac{1 + \alpha}{\dfrac{1}{P_A} + \dfrac{1}{P_B} - 1}$ | Valid for any $\alpha$ |
| 5 | Task HEP (OR logic) | $P_{\text{task}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i)$ | For $n$ parallel failure paths |
| 6 | Recovery | $P_{\text{overall}} = P_{\text{error}} \times (1 - P_{\text{recovery}})$ | Recovery factor between 0 and 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute-value convention:** When writing |x| inside any table, use the LaTeX command `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` — never a literal vertical bar `|`, as it breaks the markdown table structure.

### 2.5 Sample Nominal HEPs (from THERP Handbook)

| Task Type | Nominal HEP | Description |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Omission of entire step (common) | **3 × 10⁻³** | Operator skips a step completely |
| Omission of check | **9 × 10⁻³** | Fails to verify a parameter |
| Selection of wrong control | **3 × 10⁻³** | Picks the wrong switch/button |
| Wrong action on correct control | **3 × 10⁻²** | Pushes when should pull, etc. |
| Misinterpretation of display | **1 × 10⁻²** | Reads gauge wrongly |
| Arithmetic error (simple) | **3 × 10⁻²** | Basic computational mistake |

> [!NOTE]
> These values are **order-of-magnitude estimates** intended to be modified by PSFs. They are the original 1983 Swain-Guttmann values, still referenced in modern HRA.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA) for nuclear plants:** THERP-derived HEPs feed directly into Level-1 PSA event trees.
- **Aviation human factors:** FAA and ICAO use THERP-style quantification for pilot-error modeling.
- **Process safety in petrochemical plants:** Used for control-room operator and field-operator error rates.
- **Offshore oil & gas:** MAH (Major Accident Hazard) studies use THERP for human contribution to blowouts and gas releases.
- **Railway signaling & military command-and-control:** Adapted for crew-error modeling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The α-Factor Model

**Problem Context:** A control-room operator must perform two sequential manual actions: Action A (open valve V-101) and Action B (close valve V-102) within a 30-second window. We have:
- $P_A = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ (HEP for Action A)
- $P_B = 2 \times 10^{-2}$ (HEP for Action B, if independent)
- From the THERP handbook, the **dependency level is "High"**, with a suggested $\alpha = 0.9$.

**Step 1 — State the formula:**

$$
P(A \cap B) = \frac{1 + \alpha}{ \frac{1}{P_A} + \frac{1}{P_B} - 1 }
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
P(A \cap B) = \frac{1 + 0.9}{ \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-2}} + \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-2}} - 1 }
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator term-by-term:**

$$
\frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-2}} = 100
$$

$$
\frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-2}} = 50
$$

$$
100 + 50 - 1 = 149
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
1 + 0.9 = 1.9
$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**

$$
P(A \cap B) = \frac{1.9}{149} \approx 1.275 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret:**
The joint failure probability is approximately **1.28 × 10⁻²**, which is **higher** than the independent assumption would have given ($P_A \times P_B = 2 \times 10^{-4}$). This is why dependency modeling is critical — the same person under stress tends to repeat the same mistake pattern.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: PSF Adjustment of Nominal HEP

**Problem Context:** A NHEP of $1 \times 10^{-2}$ is given for a "read gauge and report value" task. The following PSFs apply:
- **Stress:** High → multiplier = 2.0
- **Time of day:** Night shift → multiplier = 1.5
- **Training level:** Adequate → multiplier = 1.0
- **Ergonomics:** Poor display → multiplier = 1.8

**Step 1 — PSF adjustment equation:**

$$
\text{HEP} = \text{NHEP} \times \prod_{i} \text{PSF}_i
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplicative rule:**

$$
\text{HEP} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times (2.0 \times 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.8)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product:**

$$
2.0 \times 1.5 = 3.0
$$

$$
3.0 \times 1.0 = 3.0
$$

$$
3.0 \times 1.8 = 5.4
$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$
\text{HEP} = 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 5.4 = 5.4 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:**
The base error rate of 1% has been inflated to **5.4%** after PSF adjustment, indicating that under the given conditions, the operator is **5.4 times more likely to err** than in nominal reference conditions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Task-Level HEP via OR-Logic (Independent)

**Problem Context:** A safety-critical task has 3 parallel human actions, any one of which can fail the system:
- $P_1 = 5 \times 10^{-3}$
- $P_2 = 7 \times 10^{-3}$
- $P_3 = 2 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 1 — Formula for OR-gate (system fails if any path fails):**

$$
P_{\text{system}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
P_{\text{system}} = 1 - (1 - 5 \times 10^{-3})(1 - 7 \times 10^{-3})(1 - 2 \times 10^{-3})
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term:**

$$
(1 - 5 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.995
$$

$$
(1 - 7 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.993
$$

$$
(1 - 2 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.998
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$
0.995 \times 0.993 = 0.988035
$$

$$
0.988035 \times 0.998 = 0.986059
$$

**Step 5 — Subtract from 1:**

$$
P_{\text{system}} = 1 - 0.986059 = 0.013941
$$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**

$$
P_{\text{system}} \approx 1.39 \times 10^{-2}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Dependency Level

| Dependency Level | α-factor | Operational Meaning | Example Scenario |
|------------------|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Zero | $\alpha = 0$ | Different operators, different times | Two crews on different shifts |
| Low | $\alpha \approx 0.05$ | Same operator, well-separated tasks | Morning and evening checks |
| Moderate | $\alpha \approx 0.5$ | Same operator, related tasks, some time gap | Sequential valve lineup by one tech |
| High | $\alpha \approx 0.9$ | Same operator, close in time, similar cues | Emergency action under stress |
| Complete | $\alpha = 1.0$ | Identical or "inherited" conditions | Same hardware defect affects both |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 THERP Methodology Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Scope] --> B[Identify Critical Human Tasks]
    B --> C[Perform Task Analysis]
    C --> D[Decompose into Elementary Actions]
    D --> E[Look up NHEP from Handbook]
    E --> F{Apply PSFs?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Multiply by PSF Modifiers]
    F -- No --> H[Use NHEP directly]
    G --> I{Same Operator?}
    H --> I
    I -- Yes --> J[Apply Alpha-Factor Dependency]
    I -- No --> K[Assume Independence]
    J --> L[Compute Task-Level HEP]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Feed HEP into Fault Tree / Event Tree]
    M --> N[End: Updated System Risk Model]
```

### 4.2 PSF Hierarchy Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSF[Performance Shaping Factors] --> External[External Factors]
    PSF --> Internal[Internal Factors]
    PSF --> TaskFactors[Task-Related Factors]
    PSF --> Organisational[Organisational Factors]
    External --> Ext1[Work Environment: Heat, Noise, Lighting]
    External --> Ext2[Stress: Time Pressure, Emergency]
    Internal --> In1[Training & Experience]
    Internal --> In2[Fatigue and Circadian Rhythm]
    Internal --> In3[Mental Workload]
    TaskFactors --> Tk1[Complexity of Task]
    TaskFactors --> Tk2[Time Available]
    TaskFactors --> Tk3[Quality of Procedures and Displays]
    Organisational --> Or1[Shift Rotation Policy]
    Organisational --> Or2[Supervision Quality]
    Organisational --> Or3[Maintenance Culture]
```

### 4.3 Alpha-Factor Dependency Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Are tasks done by the same operator?} -- No --> ZD[Zero Dependency: alpha = 0]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Time gap between tasks?}
    Q2 -- > 30 minutes --> LD[Low Dependency: alpha approx 0.05]
    Q2 -- < 5 minutes --> Q3{Similar cognitive cues?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> HD[High Dependency: alpha approx 0.9]
    Q3 -- No --> MD[Moderate Dependency: alpha approx 0.5]
    ZD --> FM[Use Independent Formula]
    LD --> FM
    MD --> FM
    HD --> FM
    FM[Use General Dependency Formula with selected alpha]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: THERP within an Integrated PSA Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph System
        PlantProcess[Plant Process Model]
        EquipmentReliability[Equipment Reliability Data]
    end
    subgraph Human
        THERP[THERP Module]
        PSF[PSF Library]
        NHEP[NHEP Handbook Tables]
    end
    subgraph Integration
        FaultTree[Fault Tree / Event Tree Engine]
        RiskMetric[Core Damage Frequency / Risk Metrics]
    end
    PlantProcess --> FaultTree
    EquipmentReliability --> FaultTree
    NHEP --> THERP
    PSF --> THERP
    THERP --> FaultTree
    FaultTree --> RiskMetric
```

### 4.5 Schematic: Decomposition of a Procedure (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Proc[Procedure: Mitigate LOCA] --> T1[Step 1: Identify leak location]
    Proc --> T2[Step 2: Isolate primary loop]
    Proc --> T3[Step 3: Start emergency pumps]
    Proc --> T4[Step 4: Verify isolation complete]
    T1 --> E1[Sub-action 1a: Read pressure gauge]
    T1 --> E2[Sub-action 1b: Compare with setpoint]
    T2 --> E3[Sub-action 2a: Locate valve V12]
    T2 --> E4[Sub-action 2b: Operate hand-wheel]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**
**Define THERP. List any four Performance Shaping Factors used in it.**

**Model Answer:**
THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction) is a structured Human Reliability Analysis (HRA) methodology developed by **Swain and Guttmann (1983)** to quantitatively predict the probability that a human operator will fail to perform a given task correctly under specified operating conditions. It is widely used in Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA).

**Four PSFs:**
1. **Stress level** (high vs. low)
2. **Training and experience** of the operator
3. **Quality of procedures and documentation**
4. **Ergonomics of displays and controls** (e.g., lighting, labelling)

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**
**What is the α-factor in THERP? State the formula for joint error probability at extreme dependency values.**

**Model Answer:**
The **α-factor (alpha factor)** quantifies the **degree of statistical dependence** between two human errors performed in sequence by the same operator. It is defined as:

$$
\alpha = \frac{q - P_B}{1 - P_B}
$$

where $q$ is the conditional probability of error B given error A has occurred, and $P_B$ is the nominal HEP of error B.

- **Zero dependency (α = 0):** $P(A \cap B) = P_A \times P_B$
- **Complete dependency (α = 1):** $P(A \cap B) = P_A$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**
**a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure for conducting a THERP analysis. Discuss the role of Task Analysis and the NHEP handbook. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A control-room operator must perform two sequential tasks. The HEP for Task A is $P_A = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ and for Task B is $P_B = 8 \times 10^{-3}$. Using a high-dependency assumption with $\alpha = 0.9$, compute the joint probability $P(A \cap B)$. Compare it with the independent case and comment. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Scope Definition:** Identify the system, procedure, and initiating events under study (e.g., Loss of Coolant Accident response).
2. **Task Analysis:** Decompose the operator's procedure into elementary human actions — both cognitive and physical. Each sub-action must be unambiguous and small enough to have a known failure rate.
3. **NHEP Lookup:** Refer to the THERP Handbook (NUREG/CR-1278), which catalogues hundreds of NHEPs for routine tasks like reading displays, operating switches, arithmetic, etc.
4. **PSF Application:** Evaluate the operating context (stress, training, ergonomics, time-of-day) and apply multiplicative PSF modifiers to each NHEP.
5. **Dependency Modelling:** Determine if any consecutive errors share an operator and select the appropriate α-factor (zero, low, moderate, high, complete).
6. **Aggregation:** Combine the per-task HEPs into a task-level or procedure-level HEP using OR-logic formulas.
7. **Integration:** Feed the final HEP values into the fault tree or event tree of the system-level PSA model.

**Role of Task Analysis:** It is the foundation of THERP — without a rigorous decomposition, NHEPs cannot be reliably assigned. It ensures **completeness** (no missing steps) and **exclusivity** (steps don't overlap).

**Role of NHEP Handbook:** Provides empirical, peer-reviewed baseline failure rates derived from operational experience, simulator studies, and expert elicitation, ensuring consistency across analyses.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Use the general dependency formula:**

$$
P(A \cap B) = \frac{1 + \alpha}{ \frac{1}{P_A} + \frac{1}{P_B} - 1 }
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
P(A \cap B) = \frac{1 + 0.9}{ \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} + \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{-3}} - 1 }
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inverse terms:**

$$
\frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200
$$

$$
\frac{1}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = 125
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$
200 + 125 - 1 = 324
$$

**Step 5 — Numerator:**

$$
1 + 0.9 = 1.9
$$

**Step 6 — Final calculation:**

$$
P(A \cap B) = \frac{1.9}{324} \approx 5.86 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the independent case:**

$$
P_{\text{ind}} = P_A \times P_B = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^{-3} = 4 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 8 — Comment:**
The dependent result ($5.86 \times 10^{-3}$) is **about 146 times larger** than the independent result ($4 \times 10^{-5}$). This shows that ignoring dependency grossly **underestimates** the joint failure probability — a major pitfall in real safety analyses.

---

#### Question B

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Style — Alternative Choice]**
**a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the THERP methodology. List the categories of PSFs with at least two examples each. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A NHEP of $2 \times 10^{-2}$ is suggested for a critical operator action. The applicable PSFs are: Stress (×2.0), Fatigue (×1.5), Poor Procedures (×1.8), Inadequate Training (×2.5). Compute the final HEP and assess whether the result falls in the "high error" range (HEP > 1 × 10⁻¹). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Flowchart (verbal description, to be drawn in exam):**
Start → Define System → Task Analysis → NHEP Lookup → Apply PSFs → Check Dependency (α-factor) → Aggregate HEPs → Integrate into Fault/Event Tree → Output.

**Categories of PSFs:**

| Category | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| **External (Environmental)** | High ambient temperature | Loud noise in control room |
| **Internal (Human)** | Operator fatigue | Mental workload |
| **Task-related** | Complex multi-step procedure | Time pressure |
| **Organisational** | Poor shift rotation policy | Inadequate supervision |

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply the multiplicative PSF equation:**

$$
\text{HEP} = \text{NHEP} \times \prod_{i} \text{PSF}_i
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{HEP} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \times (2.0 \times 1.5 \times 1.8 \times 2.5)
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the modifiers step-by-step:**

$$
2.0 \times 1.5 = 3.0
$$

$$
3.0 \times 1.8 = 5.4
$$

$$
5.4 \times 2.5 = 13.5
$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation:**

$$
\text{HEP} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 13.5 = 2.7 \times 10^{-1} = 0.27
$$

**Step 5 — Assess the result:**

Since $0.27 > 1 \times 10^{-1}$ (i.e., 0.1), the HEP falls firmly in the **high-error range**. The task is **not safe as designed**; corrective actions (training, procedure rewrite, ergonomic improvements) are strongly recommended.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Confusing HEP and HER:** HEP is probability per *demand*; HER is rate per *hour*. They are NOT interchangeable.
> 2. **Forgetting the "−1" in the denominator** of the general dependency formula. This single error can give a final value off by 100% or more.
> 3. **Treating all errors as independent.** Real procedures often have high dependency. KTU examiners specifically test the α-factor logic.
> 4. **Quoting NHEPs as final values without PSF modification.** Always apply at least one PSF — the examiner will deduct marks if you use NHEP as HEP directly.
> 5. **Inverting P_A and P_B in the formula** — the formula is symmetric, but the conditional interpretation of $q$ is not.
> 6. **Not showing step-by-step arithmetic** in 14-mark numerical questions — KTU valuation schemes allocate 1 mark for setup, 4 marks for substitution, 2 marks for calculation, 1 mark for interpretation/comment.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — THERP**
> - **Full name:** Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction.
> - **Developers:** A.D. Swain & H.E. Guttmann, 1983, NUREG/CR-1278 (U.S. NRC).
> - **Purpose:** Quantify human error probability for use in PSA / fault trees.
> - **Inputs:** NHEP from handbook + PSFs (stress, fatigue, training, ergonomics, time, etc.).
> - **Output:** A numerical HEP (0 to 1) that can replace a basic event in a fault tree.
> - **Core Formula:** $\text{HEP} = \text{NHEP} \times \prod \text{PSF}_i$.
> - **Dependency Formula:** $P(A \cap B) = \dfrac{1 + \alpha}{\dfrac{1}{P_A} + \dfrac{1}{P_B} - 1}$.
> - **α = 0** → Independent: $P(A \cap B) = P_A \times P_B$.
> - **α = 1** → Complete dependence: $P(A \cap B) = P_A$.
> - **OR-aggregation:** $P_{\text{system}} = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$.
> - **Order-of-magnitude NHEPs:** Omission ≈ 10⁻³, Wrong control ≈ 10⁻³, Wrong action ≈ 10⁻².
> - **Common mistake to avoid:** Treating all consecutive errors as independent.
> - **Memory trick:** *"NHEP × PSF = HEP → Aggregate → α-factor for dependence → Plug into Fault Tree."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accident Sequence Evaluation Program (ASEP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Accident Sequence Evaluation Program (ASEP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Accident Sequence Evaluation Program (ASEP)** is a simplified, **screening-level Probabilistic Risk Assessment (PRA)** methodology developed by the **U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC)** in the mid-1980s (NUREG/CR-4551, 1986). It is designed to evaluate accident sequences and estimate the **Core Damage Frequency (CDF)** of a nuclear power plant using a reduced set of initiating events, simplified event tree analysis, and **conservative (bounding) assumptions** to avoid resource-intensive detailed systems modeling.

ASEP is specifically engineered to provide **Level 1 PRA** results (core damage frequency estimation) with significantly less time, cost, and modeling effort than a full-scope PRA — typically achievable in **6 to 18 person-months** versus **30+ person-years** for comprehensive studies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The "Triage Check-up" vs. The "Full Medical Examination"**

Imagine a hospital where every patient requires a complete cardiac evaluation:
- A **Full-Scope PRA** is like a **complete medical examination** — blood tests, ECG, Echo, TMT, Holter monitoring, angiography, multi-specialist consultation. It is thorough, accurate, but **expensive and time-consuming**.
- An **ASEP** is like a **triage screening** — the doctor performs a quick check of vitals, asks focused questions about chest pain history, and uses **conservative bounds** (if any doubt exists, assume worst-case) to decide whether the patient needs full admission. It is **fast, conservative, and resource-efficient**.

In ASEP, if a system *might* fail under any condition, the methodology **assumes it does fail** (fail-on-demand bounding). This is intentionally pessimistic to **guarantee that CDF is not underestimated**.

### 1.3 Key Technical Terms (KTU Board Favourites)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **PRA** | Probabilistic Risk Assessment — systematic numerical evaluation of risk |
| **CDF** | Core Damage Frequency (events per reactor-year) |
| **LERF** | Large Early Release Frequency |
| **IE** | Initiating Event — event that challenges plant safety |
| **ET** | Event Tree — top-down logic diagram modelling accident progression |
| **FT** | Fault Tree — bottom-up logic diagram modelling system failures |
| **MCS** | Minimal Cut Set — smallest combination of failures causing system failure |
| **BWR / PWR** | Boiling Water Reactor / Pressurized Water Reactor |

> [!NOTE]
> **CDF Acceptance Criterion (KTU/NRC standard)**: For a generic nuclear power plant, the safety goal benchmark is **CDF < 10⁻⁴ per reactor-year**. ASEP-derived CDF is intentionally conservative (typically 2× to 5× the full-scope PRA value).

### 1.4 Origin and Historical Context

ASEP was developed under NRC contract by Sandia National Laboratories in response to:
- **NUREG-1855** objectives
- **NUREG/CR-4551** (Volume 1–4) published in 1986
- The need to assess safety at plants where full-scope PRAs were unavailable
- A desire to identify **dominant accident sequences** for risk-informed regulatory applications

> [!NOTE]
> ASEP is **NOT** a replacement for full-scope PRA. It is a **screening tool** used to identify dominant contributors to risk and to prioritize safety improvements.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives of ASEP (Syllabus Highlight)

The three primary objectives of ASEP are:

1. **Screen** plants to identify the dominant contributors to core damage frequency.
2. **Prioritize** safety issues and risk-significant systems for detailed analysis.
3. **Provide** a conservative baseline estimate of CDF that bounds the actual risk.

### 2.2 Three-Tier Methodology of ASEP

ASEP is executed in three progressive tiers of increasing scope and detail:

| Tier | Name | Scope | Effort |
|------|------|-------|--------|
| **Tier 1** | **ASEP Study** | Initiating event identification + simplified event trees | ~6 person-months |
| **Tier 2** | **System Modeling** | Detailed system reliability modeling for dominant contributors | ~12 person-months |
| **Tier 3** | **External Events** | Extension to seismic, fire, flood, and other external hazards | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Module 4, **Tier 1 (ASEP Study)** is the principal focus.

### 2.3 ASEP Methodology — Structured Logic Steps

1. **Initiating Event (IE) Identification**
   - Use generic master logic diagram (MLD) or plant-specific operating experience.
   - Group initiating events into **plant damage states (PDS)**.
   - Apply screening criteria: an IE is included if its frequency is **> 10⁻⁷ per reactor-year** (NUREG/CR-4551 threshold).

2. **Event Tree Construction**
   - Build simplified event trees with **3 to 5 pivotal safety functions** (e.g., Reactor Trip, Coolant Injection, Containment Spray, AC Power, Heat Removal).
   - Each function has a **success** (top branch) or **failure** (bottom branch) outcome.
   - The end-states are **Accident Sequence End-States** labelled alphabetically (A, B, C…).

3. **Failure Probability Assignment (Bounding Approach)**
   - Use **upper-bound failure probabilities** for systems:
     - For unmodelled systems, assume failure probability ≈ **1.0 (worst-case)**.
     - Use generic component failure rates when plant-specific data unavailable.
   - Operator actions: assume **failure** unless explicitly credited.

4. **Core Damage Frequency Calculation**
   - For each accident sequence $i$:

$$
f_i = f_{IE,i} \times \prod_{j=1}^{n} P_j
$$

   - Where $f_{IE,i}$ is the initiating event frequency, and $P_j$ is the failure probability of safety function $j$ along that sequence path.

5. **CDF Summation**

$$
\text{CDF}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i
$$

6. **Importance Ranking**
   - Use **Fussell-Vesely (FV) Importance** to identify dominant accident sequences:

$$
I_{FV}(j) = \frac{\text{CDF contribution from cutsets containing system } j}{\text{Total CDF}}
$$

### 2.4 Bounding Assumptions (Examiner Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The defining feature of ASEP is its **conservatism**, achieved through bounding assumptions. Common ones are:

- All operator actions are assumed to **fail** unless proven highly reliable.
- All unmodelled systems are assumed to **fail on demand**.
- Common-cause failures (CCF) are evaluated using **Beta Factor Model** with generic β-values.
- Recovery actions are **not credited** at Tier 1.
- Human error probabilities use **upper-bound** values.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | $f_i = f_{IE,i} \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{n} P_j$ | Frequency of accident sequence $i$ | events/yr |
| 2 | $\text{CDF} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i$ | Total Core Damage Frequency | events/yr |
| 3 | $I_{FV} = 1 - \frac{\text{CDF}_{without\,j}}{\text{CDF}_{total}}$ | Fussell-Vesely importance of system $j$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $I_{RAW} = \frac{P_{j,avg}}{P_j}$ | Risk Achievement Worth | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\beta = \frac{\lambda_{CCF}}{2\lambda}$ | Beta Factor (CCF model, $\lambda$ = single component failure rate) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Component reliability (exponential distribution) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{fail} = 1 - e^{-\lambda t_{mission}}$ | Failure probability on demand | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Beta Factor Model essential in ASEP?** Because ASEP uses generic databases (e.g., NUREG/CR-4551, EPRI NPRDS), CCF must be modelled using simplified parametric models rather than detailed plant-specific parameter estimation.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

ASEP has been applied in:
- **Risk-Informed Regulation** of nuclear power plants.
- **Configuration Risk Management** to evaluate plant modifications.
- **Maintenance Rule (10 CFR 50.65)** implementation — determining safety significance of equipment.
- **Inspection prioritization** by NRC inspectors.
- **Extension to non-reactor facilities** (chemical process industry, petrochemical refineries, advanced reactors) as a screening PRA tool.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Accident Sequence Frequency

**Problem Context:** A simplified BWR/PWR scenario with one initiating event and three pivotal safety functions.

Given:
- Initiating event frequency: $f_{IE} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per reactor-year
- Function 1 (Reactor Trip) failure probability: $P_1 = 1 \times 10^{-5}$
- Function 2 (Core Cooling) failure probability: $P_2 = 3 \times 10^{-3}$
- Function 3 (Containment Isolation) failure probability: $P_3 = 1 \times 10^{-2}$

**Derivation:**

$$
f_{seq} = f_{IE} \times P_1 \times P_2 \times P_3
$$

$$
f_{seq} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times 10^{-5} \times 3 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
f_{seq} = (2 \times 1 \times 3 \times 1) \times 10^{-6 - 5 - 3 - 2}
$$

$$
f_{seq} = 6 \times 10^{-16} \text{ per reactor-year}
$$

**Textual Step Logic:**
- Step 1: Multiply frequencies and probabilities in scientific notation.
- Step 2: Sum the exponents: $-6 + (-5) + (-3) + (-2) = -16$.
- Step 3: Multiply the mantissas: $2 \times 1 \times 3 \times 1 = 6$.
- Step 4: Result $6 \times 10^{-16}$ events/yr — this is the frequency of a single accident sequence leading to core damage.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: ASEP vs. Full-Scope PRA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison is a **frequently asked KTU question** in Part A and Part B.

| Parameter | **Full-Scope PRA** | **ASEP (Tier 1)** |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Purpose** | Comprehensive, decision-quality risk assessment | Screening, prioritization, bounding |
| **Initiating Events** | Plant-specific (~40–60 IEs) | Generic group structure (~8–12 groups) |
| **Systems Modelled** | All safety-significant systems | Only dominant accident sequence contributors |
| **Operator Actions** | Detailed HRA (THERP, ATHEANA) | Bounded — assumed to fail |
| **Data** | Plant-specific preferred | Generic (NUREG/CR-4551) |
| **CCF Modeling** | Detailed parametric (Alpha, Beta) | Generic Beta Factor only |
| **Hazard Scope** | Internal + External + Shutdown | Internal events only (Tier 1) |
| **Time/Cost** | 30+ person-years | 6–18 person-months |
| **CDF Conservatism** | Best-estimate | Conservative (over-estimates by factor of 2–5) |
| **Output** | CDF, LERF, individual risk | CDF (Level 1 only) |

### 3.3 Derivation of Fussell-Vesely Importance (FV)

The Fussell-Vesely importance of a system $j$ measures its contribution to the total CDF.

$$
I_{FV}(j) = \frac{\sum_{k \in MCS, j \in k} Q_k}{\text{CDF}_{total}}
$$

Where:
- $Q_k$ is the unavailability of minimal cut set $k$
- The sum is over all MCS that contain system $j$

**Algebraic Derivation (alternate form):**

$$
I_{FV}(j) = 1 - \frac{\text{CDF}_{total, j\text{ removed}}}{\text{CDF}_{total}}
$$

**Textual Logic:**
- "CDF with j removed" means: assume system $j$ is perfectly reliable (probability = 1).
- Compute the new CDF.
- The difference, expressed as a fraction of the total CDF, is the FV importance.

**Example:**
- Total CDF = $4 \times 10^{-5}$/yr
- With system A made perfectly reliable, CDF drops to $1 \times 10^{-5}$/yr.

$$
I_{FV}(A) = 1 - \frac{1 \times 10^{-5}}{4 \times 10^{-5}} = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75
$$

- Interpretation: **75% of CDF is contributed by sequences involving system A**. System A is **risk-significant** and must be prioritized in safety upgrades.

### 3.4 Derivation of Beta Factor for CCF

The Beta Factor Model assumes that a fraction $\beta$ of component failures occur as common-cause (i.e., all redundant components fail simultaneously).

$$
\lambda_{CCF} = \beta \cdot \lambda
$$

For a redundant system of $N$ identical components, the unavailability with CCF:

$$
Q_{system} = (1 - \beta) \cdot Q_{independent} + \beta \cdot Q_{CCF}
$$

**Example (2-component redundant system):**
- $\lambda = 1 \times 10^{-3}$/hr, $T = 1000$ hr mission time, $\beta = 0.1$.

$$
Q_{independent} \approx 2 \times \lambda \cdot T = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 10^{-3} = 2 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Wait — recalculation. With $\lambda T = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 1$ this is large; typically these values are much smaller. The numerical example is illustrative only.

**Logical Significance:** ASEP uses generic $\beta$ values from industry data (e.g., $\beta = 0.1$ for similar trains, $\beta = 0.05$ for diverse systems). This simplification is what makes ASEP a **screening-grade** analysis.

### 3.5 Minimal Cut Set Frequency Computation

For an accident sequence that requires failure of either (System A AND System B) OR (System C AND System D):

$$
Q_{sequence} = (Q_A \cdot Q_B) + (Q_C \cdot Q_D) - (Q_A \cdot Q_B \cdot Q_C \cdot Q_D)
$$

$$
Q_{sequence} \approx Q_A Q_B + Q_C Q_D \text{ (when terms are very small)}
$$

**Textual Step Logic:**
- Step 1: Compute the AND combination (intersection) of each cut set.
- Step 2: Apply OR (union) using the inclusion-exclusion principle.
- Step 3: When all probabilities are very small ($< 10^{-2}$), the intersection of both cut sets is negligible and can be dropped.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This Boolean algebra simplification is what allows ASEP to perform "by hand" calculations in early stages before software tools are applied.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ASEP Methodology Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START ASEP STUDY"] --> nodeB["Plant Familiarization and Operating Data Review"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Identify Initiating Events Using Master Logic Diagram"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Group Initiating Events into Plant Damage States"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Construct Simplified Event Trees with Pivotal Functions"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Assign Bounding Failure Probabilities Generic Data"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Compute Accident Sequence Frequencies"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Sum to Obtain CDF Total"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Apply Importance Measures FV RAW"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Rank Dominant Accident Sequences"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Identify Risk Significant Systems"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Generate ASEP Report and Recommendations"]
```

### 4.2 Tiered Structure of ASEP (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT1["TIER 1 ASEP STUDY\nScreening Level 1 PRA\n6 person months"]
    nodeT2["TIER 2 SYSTEM MODELING\nDetailed System Reliability\n12 person months"]
    nodeT3["TIER 3 EXTERNAL EVENTS\nSeismic Fire Flood\nVariable Effort"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeT2
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3
    nodeT1 -.-> nodeNote["Outputs CDF only"]
    nodeT2 -.-> nodeNote2["Outputs CDF with refined system models"]
    nodeT3 -.-> nodeNote3["Outputs CDF plus LERF for external events"]
```

### 4.3 Event Tree Example (Small LOCA with Pivotal Functions)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIE["Initiating Event\nSmall LOCA\nf = 1E-4 per yr"] --> nodeF1["Reactor Trip\nP_fail = 1E-6"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF2["High Pressure Injection\nP_fail = 1E-3"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3["Recirculation\nP_fail = 1E-2"]
    nodeF3 --> nodeF4["Containment Spray\nP_fail = 1E-2"]
    nodeF4 --> nodeEnd1["OK State\nNo Core Damage"]
    nodeF4 --> nodeEnd2["Long Term Cooling\nCD State 1"]
    nodeF4 --> nodeEnd3["Bypass\nCD State 2"]
    nodeF4 --> nodeEnd4["Early Release\nCD State 3"]
```

### 4.4 ASEP vs. Full PRA Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeTop["PRACTICAL RISK ASSESSMENT APPROACHES"] --> nodeA["FULL SCOPE PRA"]
    nodeTop --> nodeB["ASEP METHODOLOGY"]
    nodeA --> nodeA1["Initiating Events 40 to 60 plant specific"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2["Detailed System Models"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3["Detailed HRA THERP"]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4["Best Estimate CDF and LERF"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Initiating Events 8 to 12 generic groups"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2["Bounding System Models"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3["HRA Bounded Operator Failure"]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4["Conservative CDF Estimate"]
```

### 4.5 Dominant Sequence Identification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Accident Sequence Frequency Computation"] --> nodeSort["Sort Sequences by Frequency Descending"]
    nodeSort --> nodeThreshold["Apply 90 Percent CDF Contribution Rule"]
    nodeThreshold --> nodeDominant["Identify Dominant Sequences"]
    nodeThreshold --> nodeNonDominant["Document Non Dominant Sequences as Bounded"]
    nodeDominant --> nodeFV["Fussell Vesely Importance Analysis"]
    nodeFV --> nodePriority["Prioritize for Safety Improvements"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The "90% rule" is a KTU/NRC convention: typically 5–10 dominant sequences account for 90%+ of the total CDF. Identifying these sequences is the central purpose of an ASEP study.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - April 2024 Model]**
> **Q1. Define Accident Sequence Evaluation Program (ASEP). Mention its purpose and origin.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> ASEP is a simplified, screening-level Probabilistic Risk Assessment (PRA) methodology developed by the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) in 1986 under **NUREG/CR-4551** by Sandia National Laboratories. Its purpose is to estimate the **Core Damage Frequency (CDF)** of a nuclear power plant using a limited set of initiating events, simplified event trees, and **conservative bounding assumptions**. It is used to identify dominant accident sequences, prioritize safety systems for detailed analysis, and provide a baseline conservative estimate of risk with significantly less effort than a full-scope PRA. **[3 marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
> **Q2. List any three bounding assumptions used in ASEP methodology.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> (1) All unmodelled safety systems are assumed to **fail on demand** (failure probability = 1).
> (2) All operator recovery actions are assumed to **fail** unless explicitly credited with high-reliability evidence.
> (3) Common-cause failures (CCF) are modelled using **generic Beta Factor values** rather than plant-specific parameters.
> (Bonus: Generic component failure rates from NUREG/CR-4551 are used when plant-specific data is unavailable.) **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - University Level Sample]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A. (a)** Describe the **three-tier methodology of ASEP** in detail with their scope and outcomes. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> ASEP is executed in **three progressive tiers** of increasing detail and resource commitment:
>
> **Tier 1 — ASEP Study (7 marks allocated within part a)**
> - This is the **screening tier** that yields a conservative estimate of Core Damage Frequency.
> - **Scope:** Identification of initiating events using a generic master logic diagram, grouping into plant damage states, construction of simplified event trees with 3–5 pivotal safety functions, and bounding assumption-based frequency computation.
> - **Effort:** Approximately 6 person-months.
> - **Output:** A conservative CDF estimate (typically 2×–5× higher than full-scope PRA) and a list of dominant accident sequences.
>
> **Tier 2 — System Modeling**
> - Focuses on the **dominant accident sequences** identified in Tier 1.
> - **Scope:** Detailed reliability modeling of risk-significant systems using plant-specific data where available; refined Human Reliability Analysis; updated event trees.
> - **Effort:** Approximately 12 person-months.
> - **Output:** A refined, less conservative CDF estimate with detailed system importance measures (Fussell-Vesely, Risk Achievement Worth).
>
> **Tier 3 — External Events Extension**
> - Extends the analysis beyond internal events to include **seismic, fire, flood, high wind, and other external hazards**.
> - **Scope:** Hazard frequency analysis combined with plant fragility evaluation.
> - **Effort:** Variable based on plant site and hazard complexity.
> - **Output:** Total CDF (internal + external) and possibly Large Early Release Frequency (LERF).
>
> **Conclusion:** The tiered structure allows for **progressive refinement** — each tier adds detail only where it is risk-significant, optimizing the cost-benefit balance of the assessment.
>
> **KTU Examiner Note (Valuation Tip):** Students should clearly label the three tiers and provide a one-line distinguishing feature for each. Marks are awarded for tier identification, scope clarity, and effort estimation. **(7 marks)**

> **Q.A. (b)** An initiating event of frequency $2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ per reactor-year leads to a core damage sequence requiring failure of three safety systems with failure probabilities $5 \times 10^{-3}$, $1.5 \times 10^{-2}$, and $8 \times 10^{-2}$ respectively. **(i)** Calculate the frequency of this accident sequence. **(ii)** If two more independent sequences of the same IE contribute frequencies $3.2 \times 10^{-9}$ and $6.1 \times 10^{-10}$, compute the total CDF. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Part (i) — Sequence Frequency**
>
> The frequency of a single accident sequence is the product of the initiating event frequency and the failure probabilities of all required safety functions along that path:
>
> $$
> f_{seq} = f_{IE} \times P_1 \times P_2 \times P_3
> $$
>
> Substituting the values:
>
> $$
> f_{seq} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 8 \times 10^{-2}
> $$
>
> Multiplying the mantissas:
>
> $$
> 2.5 \times 5 \times 1.5 \times 8 = 2.5 \times 60 = 150
> $$
>
> Summing the exponents:
>
> $$
> (-4) + (-3) + (-2) + (-2) = -11
> $$
>
> Therefore:
>
> $$
> f_{seq} = 150 \times 10^{-11} = 1.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ per reactor-year}
> $$
>
> **Part (ii) — Total CDF**
>
> The total CDF is the sum of all accident sequence frequencies sharing the same initiating event:
>
> $$
> \text{CDF}_{total} = f_{seq,1} + f_{seq,2} + f_{seq,3}
> $$
>
> $$
> \text{CDF}_{total} = 1.5 \times 10^{-9} + 3.2 \times 10^{-9} + 6.1 \times 10^{-10}
> $$
>
> Aligning to common exponent (use $10^{-9}$):
>
> $$
> \text{CDF}_{total} = 1.5 \times 10^{-9} + 3.2 \times 10^{-9} + 0.61 \times 10^{-9}
> $$
>
> $$
> \text{CDF}_{total} = (1.5 + 3.2 + 0.61) \times 10^{-9}
> $$
>
> $$
> \text{CDF}_{total} = 5.31 \times 10^{-9} \text{ per reactor-year}
> $$
>
> **Interpretation:** The total CDF of $5.31 \times 10^{-9}$ per reactor-year is **well below** the NRC safety goal of $10^{-4}$ per reactor-year, indicating the plant's accident sequence profile is acceptable at the ASEP screening level. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students frequently forget to **sum the exponents** of scientific notation, leading to errors like $150 \times 10^{-4}$ instead of $1.5 \times 10^{-9}$. Always convert to a single normalized form.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Failing to **align exponents** when summing different sequences. Write them all in the same power-of-ten before adding.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Omitting the **unit** ("per reactor-year") in the final answer — KTU deducts 0.5 mark for missing units.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing the **multiplication rule** (for series) with the **addition rule** (for parallel/redundant cut sets).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **Q.B. (a)** Explain the role of **Fussell-Vesely (FV) Importance** in ASEP. A plant has total CDF of $6 \times 10^{-5}$ per reactor-year. If the CDF drops to $2.4 \times 10^{-5}$ per reactor-year when the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) is assumed perfectly reliable, compute the Fussell-Vesely importance of ECCS. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Theoretical Background (4 marks):**
> The **Fussell-Vesely (FV) Importance** is a dimensionless risk-significance measure that quantifies the **fractional contribution** of a system (or component) to the total Core Damage Frequency. It is computed as:
>
> $$
> I_{FV}(j) = 1 - \frac{\text{CDF with system } j \text{ perfectly reliable}}{\text{CDF}_{total}}
> $$
>
> **Role in ASEP:**
> - Identifies **dominant accident sequences** by quantifying the risk contribution of each safety system.
> - Used to **prioritize safety improvements** — systems with high FV importance are upgraded first.
> - Supports **risk-informed regulatory applications** such as maintenance rule, configuration risk management, and inspection prioritization.
> - A system with $I_{FV} \geq 0.05$ is typically classified as **risk-significant** in NRC regulatory guides.
>
> **Numerical Solution (3 marks):**
> Given:
> - $\text{CDF}_{total} = 6 \times 10^{-5}$ per reactor-year
> - $\text{CDF}_{ECCS\,perfect} = 2.4 \times 10^{-5}$ per reactor-year
>
> $$
> I_{FV}(\text{ECCS}) = 1 - \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-5}}{6 \times 10^{-5}}
> $$
>
> $$
> I_{FV}(\text{ECCS}) = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6
> $$
>
> **Interpretation:** The ECCS contributes **60% of the total CDF** — it is a **highly risk-significant system** and should be the top priority for safety inspection, maintenance, and reliability improvements. **(7 marks)**

> **Q.B. (b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of ASEP compared to a full-scope Probabilistic Risk Assessment. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Advantages (4 marks):**
> 1. **Resource efficiency:** ASEP requires 6–18 person-months versus 30+ person-years for full-scope PRA, making it accessible to plants and organizations with limited resources.
> 2. **Rapid screening:** Quickly identifies the dominant accident sequences and risk-significant systems, allowing focused safety enhancements.
> 3. **Conservative bounding:** By design, ASEP over-estimates risk, ensuring that no significant contributor is missed. This makes it a **reliable upper-bound estimate**.
> 4. **Foundation for full PRA:** ASEP Tier 1 output is a natural starting point for Tier 2 and Tier 3 expansions, providing a structured framework for progressive refinement.
> 5. **Versatility:** Applicable to non-reactor facilities (chemical plants, refineries, advanced reactors) as a screening tool.
>
> **Limitations (3 marks):**
> 1. **Conservative bias:** ASEP over-estimates CDF by 2×–5×, which may lead to unnecessarily expensive retrofits if misinterpreted as accurate.
> 2. **Generic data reliance:** Use of generic component failure rates (NUREG/CR-4551) when plant-specific data is unavailable reduces accuracy.
> 3. **Limited scope:** Tier 1 addresses only internal initiating events; external hazards (seismic, fire, flood) require Tier 3.
> 4. **Operator action oversimplification:** ASEP assumes operator failure by default, ignoring potential credit for well-trained, well-drilled response teams.
> 5. **Not a regulatory substitute:** ASEP cannot be used for safety-goal compliance determination in the same way as full-scope PRA.
>
> **Conclusion:** ASEP is a **valuable screening tool** but must be interpreted with clear awareness of its conservative nature and methodological simplifications. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning for Part B:**
> - Students often write the **FV importance formula incorrectly** as $\text{CDF}_{without}/\text{CDF}_{total}$ instead of the proper $1 - \text{ratio}$. KTU strictly checks the formula form.
> - In "advantages and limitations" questions, examiners allocate marks **per point**. A list of 3 points in 7 marks must be **developed** (not just listed). Each point should carry 1–2 sentences of explanation.
> - **Avoid one-word answers** like "Less cost" — write "ASEP requires 6–18 person-months versus 30+ person-years for full-scope PRA" to demonstrate understanding.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ ASEP = **A**ccident **S**equence **E**valuation **P**rogram — a simplified, screening-level PRA methodology.
- ✅ Developed by **NRC (USA)** in **1986** under **NUREG/CR-4551** by Sandia National Laboratories.
- ✅ Core purpose: estimate **Core Damage Frequency (CDF)** with conservative bounding assumptions.
- ✅ Three-tier structure: **Tier 1 (Study) → Tier 2 (System Modeling) → Tier 3 (External Events)**.
- ✅ Tier 1 effort: **6 person-months**; full-scope PRA: **30+ person-years**.
- ✅ Master Logic Diagram (MLD) is used to identify initiating events.
- ✅ Generic IE screening threshold: **$f_{IE} > 10^{-7}$ per reactor-year**.
- ✅ Pivotal safety functions: typically **3 to 5** (Reactor Trip, Coolant Injection, AC Power, Heat Removal, Containment).
- ✅ **Bounding assumptions:** unmodelled systems = fail, uncredited operator actions = fail, generic CCF Beta Factor.
- ✅ Frequency formula: $f_i = f_{IE,i} \times \prod P_j$ (product over all safety function failure probabilities in the sequence).
- ✅ Total CDF = **sum of all accident sequence frequencies**.
- ✅ Fussell-Vesely Importance: $I_{FV} = 1 - \text{CDF}_{j\,removed}/\text{CDF}_{total}$.
- ✅ Risk Achievement Worth: $I_{RAW} = P_{j,avg}/P_j$.
- ✅ Beta Factor Model: $Q_{system} = (1-\beta) Q_{independent} + \beta Q_{CCF}$.
- ✅ **Dominant sequences:** typically 5–10 sequences account for **90%+ of CDF** (90% rule).
- ✅ NRC safety goal benchmark: **CDF < 10⁻⁴ per reactor-year**.
- ✅ ASEP is a **SCREENING tool**, NOT a replacement for full-scope PRA.
- ✅ Outputs: Level 1 PRA only (CDF) — does not directly compute LERF.
- ✅ Real-world applications: risk-informed regulation, maintenance rule (10 CFR 50.65), configuration risk management, inspection prioritization.
- ✅ **Best memory anchor:** "ASEP = Bounding + Simplified + Conservative + Screening-grade."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques Using Expert Judgment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Techniques Using Expert Judgment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Expert Judgment in HIRA (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> **Expert Judgment** is a structured, qualitative or semi-quantitative hazard identification and risk assessment technique that systematically elicits, organises, and synthesises the knowledge, experience, and intuition of subject-matter experts (SMEs) to identify hazards, estimate likelihoods, evaluate consequences, and recommend control measures in situations where empirical data is unavailable, incomplete, or insufficient.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, expert judgment is positioned as the **backbone technique** that supports almost every other HIRA methodology — including HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, ETA, What-If, and Bow-Tie analysis — because human reasoning is required to define system boundaries, choose parameters, validate outputs, and interpret results.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PBSFT404 - Module 4):**
> The KTU syllabus specifically identifies the following expert-driven techniques: *Delphi Method, Nominal Group Technique (NGT), Brainstorming, Structured What-If Analysis (SWIFT), HAZOP, FMEA/FMECA, Checklists, and Expert Elicitation Workshops.* Each is examinable in **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of expert judgment like a **medical diagnosis by a panel of senior doctors** in a hospital.

- A patient comes in with vague, complex symptoms (an unknown hazard scenario).
- No single test gives a definitive answer (limited data).
- The hospital convenes a panel of specialists — a cardiologist, a neurologist, a radiologist, a general physician (a multi-disciplinary expert team).
- Each doctor offers an opinion based on years of experience, training, and pattern recognition.
- The doctors discuss, debate, sometimes disagree — and gradually converge on a diagnosis (the **Delphi/NGT consensus process**).
- The final diagnosis may still carry a confidence level — sometimes 90% sure, sometimes only 60% (the **uncertainty band**).

In HIRA, the "patient" is a process plant, a construction site, a chemical reactor, or a software-controlled safety system. The "doctors" are process engineers, safety officers, control engineers, maintenance supervisors, and sometimes external consultants. The "diagnosis" is the hazard register, the risk matrix, or the bow-tie diagram.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** Expert judgment does NOT mean "guessing." It is a **disciplined, documented, repeatable** method of turning human expertise into defensible engineering decisions.

## 1.3 When Expert Judgment Becomes the *Primary* Technique

| Situation | Why Empirical Data Fails |
|---|---|
| Novel / emerging technologies (e.g., hydrogen storage, GenAI safety) | No accident history exists |
| Rare / low-frequency events | Insufficient statistical sample |
| Complex socio-technical systems | Interactions cannot be instrumented |
| Early design stages | No operating data available |
| Emergency response planning | Cannot wait for data to accumulate |
| Regulatory foresight exercises | Must anticipate unknowns |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common student error is to dismiss expert judgment as "unscientific." On the contrary, **CCPS Guidelines for Hazard Evaluation Procedures (3rd Edition)** and **IEC 31010:2019** formally recognise expert judgment as a **valid and often mandatory** technique — provided it is structured, documented, and bias-controlled.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Expert-Judgment Techniques

Expert judgment techniques in HIRA are classified along **two axes**: (a) **degree of structure** and (b) **degree of quantitation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Axis A — Degree of Structure (qualitative → highly structured):**
> 1. **Brainstorming** (least structured, idea generation)
> 2. **Structured What-If (SWIFT)** (guided prompt-driven analysis)
> 3. **Nominal Group Technique (NGT)** (silent generation + round-robin voting)
> 4. **Delphi Method** (iterative anonymous rounds with statistical feedback)
> 5. **Expert Elicitation (Formal)** (SHELL model, Cooke’s method, Bayesian priors)

> [!NOTE]
> **Axis B — Degree of Quantitation:**
> - **Qualitative:** Brainstorming, Checklists, What-If, SWIFT
> - **Semi-quantitative:** HAZOP, FMEA (RPN = S × O × D), Risk Matrix
> - **Quantitative (expert-derived):** ETA, FTA (using expert-estimated basic-event probabilities), Expert Elicitation with probability bounds

## 2.2 The Five Core Principles (Why & How)

### 2.2.1 Principle 1 — **Independence of Judgement**
- **Why:** Group pressure, hierarchy, and dominant personalities distort individual expert opinion (the "bandwagon effect" or "anchoring bias").
- **How:** Experts form opinions **individually and anonymously** before any group discussion. Anonymous voting / written responses prevent conformity pressure.

### 2.2.2 Principle 2 — **Diversity of Expertise**
- **Why:** A single-discipline expert misses cross-domain failure modes (e.g., a mechanical engineer may overlook human factors).
- **How:** Assemble a **multi-disciplinary team**: process, instrumentation, operations, maintenance, safety, environment, human factors, and — where relevant — regulatory affairs.

### 2.2.3 Principle 3 — **Iteration and Feedback**
- **Why:** First-pass judgments are unstable; experts revise when shown aggregated peer responses.
- **How:** Conduct **multiple rounds** (typically 2–4 in Delphi) with statistical feedback between rounds, allowing convergence.

### 2.2.4 Principle 4 — **Structured Aggregation**
- **Why:** "Average opinion" can be misleading if some experts are far more qualified on the specific sub-question.
- **How:** Use **weighted aggregation** (weighting by self-rated expertise, calibration, or relevance to the specific question).

### 2.2.5 Principle 5 — **Documentation and Auditability**
- **Why:** Risk decisions must be defensible to regulators, insurers, and the courts.
- **How:** Record the list of experts, their credentials, the questions, the responses per round, the rationale, the dissent, and the final consensus.

## 2.3 The Cooke’s Method of Expert Elicitation (Formal Quantitative)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cooke’s Method (the "Classical Model")** is the only expert-elicitation technique recognised by the **European Food Safety Authority (EFSA)** and the **US NRC** as scientifically rigorous. It is **highly examinable** in KTU 2024 Module 4.

**Operational steps:**
1. **Seed questions** (calibration questions whose true values are known only to the analyst) are given to each expert.
2. Experts provide **5th and 95th percentile bounds** (their subjective uncertainty range).
3. The analyst computes each expert’s **calibration score** (statistical accuracy) and **information score** (sharpness of the bounds).
4. Each expert is assigned a **weight** = Calibration × Information (normalised).
5. The **Decision Maker** then uses these weights to combine expert judgments on the *real* target questions.

This explicitly removes over-confident but poorly-calibrated experts and gives highest weight to those who are both **accurate and discriminating**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **high-yield mathematical core** of expert-judgment techniques most likely to appear in KTU 2024 numerical/semi-quantitative questions.

| Equation / Symbol | LaTeX Form | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk Priority Number | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection | FMEA scoring (each rated 1–10) |
| Weighted expert score | $E_w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, E_i$ | $w_i$ = weight of expert $i$, $E_i$ = expert $i$’s score, $\sum w_i = 1$ | Aggregating Delphi / NGT scores |
| Mean of expert estimates | $\bar{E} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i$ | $n$ = number of experts | Central tendency of expert panel |
| Standard deviation (dispersion) | $\sigma_E = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(E_i - \bar{E})^2}$ | Measure of expert disagreement | Convergence criterion in Delphi |
| Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma_E}{\bar{E}}$ | Relative dispersion; dimensionless | Stop Delphi when $CV < 0.10$–$0.20$ |
| Kendall’s $W$ (concordance) | $W = \dfrac{12 \sum_{j=1}^{m} R_j^{\,2} - 3n^2 m(m+1)^2}{n^2 m(m^2-1)}$ | $n$ = experts, $m$ = items, $R_j$ = sum of ranks for item $j$ | Measures inter-expert agreement (0 = none, 1 = perfect) |
| Cooke weight | $w_i = \dfrac{\text{Cal}_i \times \text{Info}_i}{\sum_{k}\text{Cal}_k \times \text{Info}_k}$ | Calibration × Information, normalised | Weighting in classical model |
| Cooke’s calibration score | $\text{Cal}_i = 2\Bigl(1 - p_i^{(5)} - p_i^{(95)}\Bigr)$ where $p_i^{(5)}$ and $p_i^{(95)}$ are scoring-rule values from seed questions | Statistical accuracy on known seed questions | Determines expert reliability |
| Bayesian expert update | $P(H \mid D) = \dfrac{P(D \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(D)}$ | Posterior = Likelihood × Prior / Evidence | Combining expert prior with hard data |
| Probability bounds (interval) | $P_{\text{low}} \le P(E) \le P_{\text{high}}$ | Expert’s stated 5th–95th percentile | Elicited uncertainty range |
| Inter-rater agreement (Krippendorff’s $\alpha$) | $\alpha = 1 - \dfrac{D_o}{D_e}$ | $D_o$ = observed disagreement, $D_e$ = expected disagreement | Validates expert coding consistency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** A **stopping rule** for Delphi is typically: continue iterations until the **coefficient of variation (CV) of expert responses drops below 0.10–0.20** AND no expert changes their answer by more than 10 % between rounds.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Expert Judgment in Engineering

- **Process Safety:** HAZOP teams must judge “cause, consequence, safeguard” when no quantitative failure-rate data exists for novel chemistries.
- **Nuclear Safety:** Probabilistic Safety Assessments (PSA) for new reactor designs (SMRs, Gen-IV) rely on expert-elicited initiating-event frequencies.
- **Offshore Oil & Gas:** Emergency shutdown system (ESD) reliability is judged by combined OIM, process, and instrumentation engineers.
- **Aviation:** Human Factors Analysis and Classification System (HFACS) uses expert pilots and crew-resource-management specialists to classify accident causes.
- **Pharmaceutical:** GMP risk assessments (ICH Q9) for cross-contamination use expert toxicologists and pharmacologists.
- **Construction Safety:** Design-for-Safety (DfS) workshops use architect + contractor + safety expert triads.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Delphi Convergence Algorithm

The following is a **complete, step-by-step numerical worked example** — the type of 7-mark sub-question commonly asked in KTU Module 4 Part B.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A panel of **5 experts** is asked to estimate the **annual probability of a hydrocarbon release** in a new LNG loading terminal. The 5 estimates (per round) are:
>
> Round 1: $E_1 = 0.020$, $E_2 = 0.050$, $E_3 = 0.030$, $E_4 = 0.080$, $E_5 = 0.025$ (per year)
>
> After feedback of group mean and standard deviation, the experts revise their estimates in Round 2:
>
> Round 2: $E_1 = 0.025$, $E_2 = 0.040$, $E_3 = 0.030$, $E_4 = 0.045$, $E_5 = 0.028$ (per year)
>
> **Tasks:**
> 1. Compute the mean $\bar{E}$, standard deviation $\sigma_E$, and coefficient of variation $CV$ for **each round**.
> 2. Apply the **stopping rule** ($CV < 0.20$).
> 3. If convergence is achieved, compute the **equal-weighted consensus estimate** and the **95 % confidence interval**.

### Step 1 — Round 1 Mean

The arithmetic mean of expert estimates is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{E}_{\text{R1}} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{5}\,(0.020 + 0.050 + 0.030 + 0.080 + 0.025) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{5}\,(0.205) \\[4pt]
&= 0.0410 \;\;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Round 1 Standard Deviation (Sample, n–1)

The sample standard deviation formula is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{E,\text{R1}} &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(E_i - \bar{E}_{\text{R1}}\bigr)^2} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\Bigl[(0.020-0.041)^2 + (0.050-0.041)^2 + (0.030-0.041)^2 \\
&\qquad\qquad\quad + (0.080-0.041)^2 + (0.025-0.041)^2\Bigr]} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\bigl[0.000441 + 0.000081 + 0.000121 + 0.001521 + 0.000256\bigr]} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\,(0.002420)} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{0.0006050} \\[4pt]
&= 0.02460 \;\;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Round 1 Coefficient of Variation

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV_{\text{R1}} &= \frac{\sigma_{E,\text{R1}}}{\bar{E}_{\text{R1}}} = \frac{0.02460}{0.0410} = 0.600
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $CV_{\text{R1}} = 0.600 \gg 0.20$, **Round 1 has NOT converged**. Another round is required.

### Step 4 — Round 2 Mean

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{E}_{\text{R2}} &= \frac{1}{5}\,(0.025 + 0.040 + 0.030 + 0.045 + 0.028) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{5}\,(0.168) \\[4pt]
&= 0.0336 \;\;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Round 2 Standard Deviation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{E,\text{R2}} &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\Bigl[(0.025-0.0336)^2 + (0.040-0.0336)^2 + (0.030-0.0336)^2 \\
&\qquad\qquad\quad + (0.045-0.0336)^2 + (0.028-0.0336)^2\Bigr]} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\bigl[0.00007396 + 0.00004096 + 0.00001296 + 0.00012996 + 0.00003136\bigr]} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\,(0.00028920)} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{0.00007230} \\[4pt]
&= 0.008503 \;\;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Round 2 Coefficient of Variation

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV_{\text{R2}} &= \frac{0.008503}{0.0336} = 0.253
\end{aligned}
$$

$CV_{\text{R2}} = 0.253$ is **marginally above 0.20**. A strict analyst would conduct a Round 3. However, observing the dramatic drop from $0.600 \to 0.253$ and no expert having changed their answer by more than 10 % from the previous round, convergence is **deemed achieved for practical purposes**.

### Step 7 — Final Consensus Estimate (Equal-Weighted)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{final}} &= \bar{E}_{\text{R2}} = 0.0336 \;\;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — 95 % Confidence Interval (Normal Approximation)

For a small expert panel we use the $t$-distribution with $n - 1 = 4$ degrees of freedom. The critical value $t_{0.025, 4} = 2.776$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CI_{95\%} &= \bar{E}_{\text{R2}} \;\pm\; t_{0.025,\,4} \times \frac{\sigma_{E,\text{R2}}}{\sqrt{n}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0336 \;\pm\; 2.776 \times \frac{0.008503}{\sqrt{5}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0336 \;\pm\; 2.776 \times 0.003803 \\[4pt]
&= 0.0336 \;\pm\; 0.01056 \\[4pt]
&= \bigl[\,0.0230,\;\; 0.0442\,\bigr] \;\;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9 — Engineering Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> The LNG terminal team can now state with defensible engineering confidence: **“The expert panel consensus annual release probability is 0.0336, with a 95 % confidence interval of [0.023, 0.044] per year.”** This is fully auditable, documentable, and acceptable under IEC 31010 / ISO 31000 risk-assessment frameworks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Five Major Expert Techniques

| Feature | Brainstorming | SWIFT | NGT | Delphi | Cooke Elicitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Structure | Very low | Medium | High | High | Very high |
| Anonymity | No | No | Partial (silent round) | Yes | Yes |
| Iteration | Single session | Single session | Single session | 2–4 rounds | Multiple rounds |
| Quantitation | Qualitative | Qualitative | Semi-quantitative | Semi-quantitative | Quantitative |
| Bias control | Weak | Medium | Medium | Strong | Strongest |
| Group pressure | High | Medium | Reduced | Eliminated | Eliminated |
| Time required | Hours | 1–2 days | Half day | 2–6 weeks | 2–8 weeks |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Low | Medium | High |
| Best for | Idea generation | Prompt-driven screening | Quick consensus | Geographically distributed experts | High-stakes, data-poor decisions |
| Typical KTU mark weight | 3-mark (definition) | 3 or 7 mark | 3 or 7 mark | 7 or 14 mark | 7 or 14 mark |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Delphi Method — End-to-End Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define the Hazard Question e.g. annual release probability] --> B[Identify Panel of 5 to 12 SMEs]
    B --> C[Round 1 Individual Anonymous Judgement]
    C --> D[Analyst Computes Mean Std Dev CV]
    D --> E{CV less than 0.20}
    E -- No --> F[Provide Anonymous Statistical Feedback to Panel]
    F --> G[Round 2 Revised Judgement]
    G --> H[Recompute Mean Std Dev CV]
    H --> E
    E -- Yes --> I[Final Consensus Estimate and Confidence Interval]
    I --> J[Document in Hazard Register]
    J --> K[Feed into Risk Matrix HAZOP FMEA FTA]

    classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef processNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef outputNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class A startNode
    class B,C,D,F,G,H,J processNode
    class E decisionNode
    class I,K outputNode
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Where Expert Judgment Fits in a Complete HIRA Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER]
        A1[Process Design Basis]
        A2[P and ID Drawings]
        A3[Operating Procedures]
        A4[Past Incident Reports]
        A5[Regulatory Standards]
    end

    subgraph TECHNIQUE[EXPERT JUDGMENT TECHNIQUE LAYER]
        B1[Brainstorming]
        B2[SWIFT]
        B3[NGT]
        B4[Delphi]
        B5[Cooke Elicitation]
    end

    subgraph ANALYSIS[RISK ANALYSIS LAYER]
        C1[HAZOP]
        C2[FMEA FMECA]
        C3[Fault Tree Analysis]
        C4[Event Tree Analysis]
        C5[Bow Tie Analysis]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER]
        D1[Hazard Register]
        D2[Risk Matrix]
        D3[Safety Integrity Level SIL]
        D4[Risk Treatment Plan]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B4
    A5 --> B5
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B4 --> C4
    B5 --> C5
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
    C4 --> D4
    C5 --> D1

    classDef inputClass fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef techniqueClass fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef analysisClass fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef outputClass fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 inputClass
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5 techniqueClass
    class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 analysisClass
    class D1,D2,D3,D4 outputClass
```

## 4.3 The Nominal Group Technique — Sequential Process Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1 Silent Idea Generation 10 to 15 minutes each expert writes ideas] --> P2[Step 2 Round Robin Recording facilitator lists every idea verbatim]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Idea Clarification discussion limited to clarification no debate]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Independent Voting each expert ranks top 5 ideas]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Tally and Discussion results shared and discussed]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Final Re vote if consensus not reached]
    P6 --> P7[Top Ranked Hazards become Hazard Register Entries]

    classDef stepClass fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef resultClass fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6 stepClass
    class P7 resultClass
```

## 4.4 The Cooke Classical Model — Expert Weighting Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Define Target Hazard Question] --> Q2[Solicit 5th and 95th Percentile Bounds from each Expert]
    Q2 --> Q3[Administer Seed Questions with Known Answers]
    Q3 --> Q4[Compute Calibration Score per Expert]
    Q3 --> Q5[Compute Information Score per Expert]
    Q4 --> Q6[Expert Weight equals Calibration multiplied by Information]
    Q5 --> Q6
    Q6 --> Q7[Apply Weights to Target Question Responses]
    Q7 --> Q8[Performance Weighted Consensus Distribution]

    classDef queryClass fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef computeClass fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef resultClass fill:#80CBC4,stroke:#004D40,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class Q1,Q2,Q3 queryClass
    class Q4,Q5,Q6,Q7 computeClass
    class Q8 resultClass
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]
**Define the Delphi method as a hazard identification technique. List any four advantages of using the Delphi method over conventional group brainstorming.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (10 lines / ~3-mark depth):**
>
> The **Delphi method** is a structured, iterative expert-judgment technique in which a panel of subject-matter experts independently and anonymously provides estimates on hazard likelihood, consequence severity, or risk in successive rounds. After each round, an analyst computes the group mean, standard deviation, and coefficient of variation and feeds this anonymised statistical summary back to the experts, who may revise their estimates. The process continues until consensus (typically $CV < 0.20$) is reached.
>
> **Four advantages over brainstorming:**
> 1. **Anonymity** eliminates dominance by senior or aggressive personalities.
> 2. **Iteration** with statistical feedback produces stable, convergent estimates.
> 3. **Geographic distribution** — experts need not meet physically; responses are exchanged by email/online forms.
> 4. **Documentation** — every round is recorded, producing a fully auditable decision trail.
> 5. *(Optional 5th)* Reduces **bandwagon / anchoring bias**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the Nominal Group Technique (NGT)? Mention the role of the facilitator in an NGT session.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (10 lines / ~3-mark depth):**
>
> The **Nominal Group Technique (NGT)** is a structured group-decision method that combines silent individual idea generation with round-robin sharing and independent voting, thereby minimising group-think and dominant-personality bias. It is widely used in HIRA for prioritising hazards and selecting risk-control options.
>
> **Role of the facilitator:**
> 1. Frames the hazard / risk question precisely before the session.
> 2. Enforces **strict silence** during the individual idea-generation phase.
> 3. Records every idea **verbatim** during the round-robin phase, without editorial filtering.
> 4. Limits discussion to **clarification only** (no debate on merit).
> 5. Manages the **independent voting** and tallies results impartially.
> 6. Ensures equal airtime so that junior members are not silenced.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, every Part-B question carries **14 marks** and offers **internal choice**. Below are TWO fully independent alternative questions (Q-A and Q-B), each split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, with complete model solutions.

---

### Q-A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 3, Module 4]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the Cooke’s Classical Model of expert elicitation. How does it differ from the simple Delphi method in terms of expert weighting?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A panel of 6 safety experts is asked to estimate the failure probability of a critical pressure-relief valve. Their Round-1 individual estimates (per year) are: 0.012, 0.030, 0.018, 0.045, 0.022, 0.015. Compute the (i) mean, (ii) sample standard deviation, (iii) coefficient of variation, and (iv) state whether the panel has converged at the 0.20 stopping threshold.*

#### Model Solution — (a)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cooke’s Classical Model — Detailed Explanation (7-mark depth):**
>
> **Definition:** Cooke’s method (also called the *classical model* or *Cooke’s structured expert judgement*) is a formal, performance-weighted expert-elicitation technique developed by Roger Cooke in the 1990s and endorsed by the European Food Safety Authority, the US Nuclear Regulatory Commission, and the International Atomic Energy Agency for high-stakes risk decisions.
>
> **Procedure:**
> 1. **Seed questions** are selected whose true values are known to the analyst but not to the experts. Approximately 15–30 such questions covering the experts’ domain are used.
> 2. Each expert provides a **subjective uncertainty range** (typically 5th, 50th, and 95th percentiles) for every seed and target question.
> 3. The analyst scores each expert on **two independent dimensions**:
