>    - **Calibration score** — how statistically accurate the expert’s intervals are on the seed questions (a perfectly calibrated expert’s stated 90 % interval should contain the true value 90 % of the time).
>    - **Information score** — how *sharp* (narrow) the expert’s intervals are; a very narrow interval that is wrong is uninformative.
> 4. The expert’s **combined weight** is the product of normalised calibration and information, divided by the sum of all such products so that weights sum to 1.
> 5. The **Decision Maker (DM)** then uses these weights to combine each expert’s response into a single *performance-weighted distribution* for the *real* target question.
>
> **Key differences from Delphi:**
>
> | Aspect | Delphi | Cooke’s Method |
> |---|---|---|
> | Expert weighting | None (equal weight) or self-rated | **Empirically derived from seed performance** |
> | Output | Point estimate or interval | **Full probability distribution** |
> | Validation of experts | By credentials only | **By actual performance on known questions** |
> | Anonymity | Yes | Yes |
> | Iteration | Yes (2–4 rounds) | Yes (typically 2–3 rounds) |
> | Statistical rigour | Medium | High |
> | Regulator acceptance | Broad | EFSA, NRC, IAEA |

#### Model Solution — (b)

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Solution (7-mark depth) — show every step explicitly.**
>
> **Data (n = 6 experts):**
> $E_1 = 0.012$, $E_2 = 0.030$, $E_3 = 0.018$, $E_4 = 0.045$, $E_5 = 0.022$, $E_6 = 0.015$ per year.

**(i) Mean**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{E} &= \frac{1}{6}(0.012 + 0.030 + 0.018 + 0.045 + 0.022 + 0.015) \\
&= \frac{1}{6}(0.142) \\
&= 0.02367 \;\;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Sample standard deviation**

First compute each squared deviation:

- $(0.012 - 0.02367)^2 = (-0.01167)^2 = 0.0001362$
- $(0.030 - 0.02367)^2 = (0.00633)^2 = 0.0000401$
- $(0.018 - 0.02367)^2 = (-0.00567)^2 = 0.0000321$
- $(0.045 - 0.02367)^2 = (0.02133)^2 = 0.0004552$
- $(0.022 - 0.02367)^2 = (-0.00167)^2 = 0.0000028$
- $(0.015 - 0.02367)^2 = (-0.00867)^2 = 0.0000751$

Sum of squared deviations = $0.0001362 + 0.0000401 + 0.0000321 + 0.0004552 + 0.0000028 + 0.0000751 = 0.0007415$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_E &= \sqrt{\frac{0.0007415}{6 - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0007415}{5}} = \sqrt{0.0001483} \\
&= 0.01218 \;\;\text{per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Coefficient of variation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= \frac{\sigma_E}{\bar{E}} = \frac{0.01218}{0.02367} = 0.5146
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Convergence decision**

The threshold for convergence is $CV < 0.20$. Here, $CV = 0.515 \gg 0.20$.

**Conclusion: The panel has NOT converged.** At least one more Delphi round is required. The wide spread — driven primarily by Expert 4 (0.045) — should be discussed anonymously with the panel, with possible justification requested for the outlier.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not use population SD** ($n$ in denominator) — the formula requires $n - 1$ for sample standard deviation. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this error.
> 2. **State the stopping threshold explicitly** before drawing the conclusion — many students compute $CV$ but never say “the panel has/h-has-not converged.”
> 3. **Carry four significant figures** in the mean and SD to avoid rounding error in the CV.
> 4. **Identify the outlier** (Expert 4 = 0.045) and suggest a Delphi feedback mechanism — examiners award bonus marks for this.

---

### Q-B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 1, Module 4]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Compare Brainstorming, SWIFT, NGT, and Delphi methods of expert judgment. Present your answer in a structured table with at least 6 comparison parameters.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *An FMEA study on a hydraulic press uses expert judgment to assign Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D) scores on a 1–10 scale. The expert panel assigns S = 8, O = 5, D = 7 to the failure mode “sudden loss of hydraulic pressure.” (i) Compute the Risk Priority Number. (ii) Classify the risk using a typical RPN thresholding scheme (RPN > 150: High; 100–150: Medium; < 100: Low). (iii) Recommend two risk-control actions with justification.*

#### Model Solution — (a)

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Table (6 parameters, 7-mark depth):**
>
> | Parameter | Brainstorming | SWIFT | NGT | Delphi |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | **Structure** | Very low (free-flowing) | Medium (guided by prompt words) | High (defined phases) | Very high (iterative rounds) |
> | **Anonymity** | None | None | Partial (silent generation) | Full |
> | **Iteration** | Single session | Single session | Single session | 2–4 rounds |
> | **Quantitative output** | List of ideas | Hazard list with rationale | Ranked hazards | Consensus probability / score with CI |
> | **Bias control** | Weak (dominant voices) | Medium | Medium (silent start) | Strong (anonymity + feedback) |
> | **Time / cost** | 1–2 hours, low cost | 1–2 days, medium | Half day, low | 2–6 weeks, medium |
> | **Best application** | Early-stage idea generation | Screening workshops | Quick prioritisation | Geographically distributed experts / high-stakes decisions |

#### Model Solution — (b)

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Solution (7-mark depth) — show every step.**

**(i) Risk Priority Number**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 8 \times 5 \times 7 \\
&= 280
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Risk classification**

Since $RPN = 280 > 150$, the failure mode is classified as **HIGH RISK** under the given scheme.

**(iii) Recommended risk-control actions** (use the **hierarchy of controls**, highest reliability first):

1. **Engineering control — install a redundant pressure sensor with auto-shutdown interlock** (severity mitigation). Justification: A second independent sensor with SIL-2 logic solver provides automatic safe-shutdown on low-pressure detection, reducing the consequence severity from a single-point failure to a manageable alarm-and-trip event. Per IEC 61511, this corresponds to a risk-reduction factor of ≥ 10.

2. **Detection control — change inspection interval from 6-monthly to monthly** plus a portable ultrasonic leak detector mounted on the operator panel (detection score improvement). Justification: Increasing the probability of detecting slow pressure decay *before* catastrophic failure reduces the *Occurrence* score from 5 toward 2 and the *Detection* score from 7 toward 3, lowering the new RPN to a Medium/Low band.

3. *(Optional bonus)* **PPE / procedural control** — operator training on early-warning signs and lock-out-tag-out discipline as an administrative safeguard while the engineering controls are being implemented.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always state the threshold and the comparison explicitly:** “$RPN = 280 > 150$ ⇒ High.” Avoid vague phrases like “this is risky.”
> 2. **Cite the hierarchy of controls** in order (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE) — examiners award full marks only when the recommendation follows this hierarchy.
> 3. **Do not confuse FMEA severity (S) with risk (RPN):** S alone is on a 1–10 scale; RPN is the product.
> 4. **Quantify the RPN reduction target** — e.g., “new RPN target ≤ 100” — for a complete engineering answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Techniques Using Expert Judgment**
>
> - **Expert judgment** is a *structured, documented, iterative* technique — not subjective guessing. Endorsed by IEC 31010, ISO 31000, CCPS, EFSA, NRC.
> - **Five core principles:** Independence · Diversity · Iteration · Structured aggregation · Documentation.
> - **Five major techniques to remember in exam:**
>   1. **Brainstorming** — low-structure, idea generation.
>   2. **SWIFT (Structured What-If)** — guided by prompt words; team-based.
>   3. **NGT (Nominal Group Technique)** — silent generation → round-robin → independent vote.
>   4. **Delphi** — anonymous iterative rounds with statistical feedback until $CV < 0.20$.
>   5. **Cooke’s Classical Model** — performance-weighted via seed questions; produces full probability distribution.
> - **Key formulas (must memorise):**
>   - $RPN = S \times O \times D$
>   - $\bar{E} = \frac{1}{n}\sum E_i$
>   - $\sigma_E = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (E_i - \bar{E})^2}{n-1}}$
>   - $CV = \sigma_E / \bar{E}$
>   - Cooke weight $\propto \text{Calibration} \times \text{Information}$
> - **Stopping rule for Delphi:** $CV < 0.10$–$0.20$ AND no expert changes > 10 % between rounds.
> - **Bias-control hierarchy:** Anonymity > Independence > Iteration > Facilitation > Domain diversity.
> - **Expert weighting methods:** Equal / Self-rated / Performance-based (Cooke) / Bayesian posterior.
> - **Common examiner traps:**
>   1. Using $n$ instead of $n - 1$ in SD.
>   2. Confusing $S$ (severity) with RPN (risk).
>   3. Failing to state the stopping threshold before declaring convergence.
>   4. Recommending PPE as the first control (must follow hierarchy of controls).
>   5. Dismissing expert judgment as “unscientific.”
> - **Documentation deliverables:** list of experts with credentials, question set, all round responses, statistics per round, dissent notes, final consensus with confidence interval.
> - **Real-world application sectors:** process, nuclear, offshore, aviation, pharma, construction, cyber-physical, AI safety.
> - **Related standards to cite in answers:** IEC 31010:2019, ISO 31000:2018, CCPS HEP 3rd Ed., ICH Q9, IAEA SSG-3.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operator Action Tree (OAT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operator Action Tree (OAT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Operator Action Tree (OAT)** is a structured, logic-tree based safety analysis methodology used to systematically evaluate the **demands placed on an operator** during abnormal process conditions and to determine the **probability of the operator acting correctly** (or incorrectly) in response to process deviations. It is a graphical deductive reasoning tool developed under the **IEC 61511** (Functional Safety — Safety Instrumented Systems for the Process Industry Sector) framework and is widely integrated with **HAZOP** and **LOPA** studies as a supporting node-frequency and human-reliability estimation method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim spirit):**  
> The OAT is a "logic tree model that maps every credible operator action following a process deviation, classifies the action as 'demand on operator' or 'demand on safeguards', and propagates the outcome to the next event in the scenario chain."

The OAT explicitly **separates automatic (engineered) safeguard responses from manual (operator-driven) responses**, allowing the analyst to compute a **conditional probability of failure on demand (PFD)** for the human-in-the-loop sub-system. This probability is then multiplied with the initiating event frequency to estimate the **mitigated event frequency** of the final consequence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Warden" Analogy

Imagine a **traffic warden (the operator)** standing at a busy intersection (the process plant). An unexpected event — a child running onto the road (a process deviation) — occurs. The warden must now rapidly decide:

1. **Did the warden even notice the child?** (Detection step)
2. **Does the warden know the right response?** (Diagnosis step)
3. **Can the warden physically act in time?** (Action step)
4. **Is the warden's action correct, or did it make the situation worse?** (Outcome classification)

A **Tree** of "if-then-else" branches is then drawn: each decision point splits the path into **"Success"** and **"Failure"** branches. The OAT does precisely this — but for industrial operators dealing with high-pressure, high-temperature, flammable, or toxic process streams where the "child on the road" is a runaway reaction, a relief valve failure, or a pump trip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** OAT is *not* a generic human reliability method like THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction) or HEART (Human Error Assessment and Reduction Technique). It is **scenario-specific** — built bottom-up from a single initiating event.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Initiating Event Frequency | **F** | per year (yr⁻¹) |
| Probability of Failure on Demand (operator) | **PFD_op** | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Probability of Failure on Demand (safeguard) | **PFD_S** | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Independent Protection Layer | **IPL** | per IEC 61511 |
| Human Error Probability (default, trained operator) | **HEP** | **1 × 10⁻²** (per demand) |
| Mitigation Frequency | **F_mitigated** | per year (yr⁻¹) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** When integrating OAT into LOPA, the operator action node is treated as an **Independent Protection Layer (IPL)** only if it is **independent**, **auditable**, and **effective** — meeting the IEC 61511 *IPL* criteria.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Architecture of an OAT

The OAT is constructed as a **binary or ternary decision tree** anchored at the **top (root)** by a single initiating event or process deviation. The analyst then asks, in sequence, a set of "demand" and "action" questions. Each question is answered in the affirmative or negative, branching the path accordingly.

The tree consists of **three node types** (KTU examiners love this classification):

### Node Type 1 — Demand Nodes (D-nodes)
A D-node asks: *"Is there a demand placed on the operator (or the system) at this point in the scenario?"* The answer is binary:
- **YES** → proceed to an Action Node (A-node).
- **NO** → bypass to the next independent layer or escalate the consequence.

### Node Type 2 — Action Nodes (A-nodes)
An A-node asks: *"Did the operator (or safeguard) perform the required action correctly and within the available time window?"* The answer is again binary:
- **YES (success)** → scenario is **mitigated**; branch terminates in a safe state.
- **NO (failure)** → scenario **propagates** to the next layer or to the final consequence.

### Node Type 3 — Outcome / Consequence Nodes
These are the **leaves** of the tree, representing either:
- A **safe termination** (recovered state), or
- The **final undesired consequence** (e.g., vessel rupture, toxic release, fire).

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why use a tree structure?** Because operator actions are sequential and conditional — one decision influences the next. A flat checklist cannot capture this dependency chain.
- **Why demand-action pairing?** Because a demand without a corresponding correct action still results in failure. Conversely, an action in the absence of a demand may itself create a new hazard (e.g., operator closing a valve that should remain open).
- **How is the tree terminated?** Either by reaching a **safe state** (a leaf where the risk is acceptably mitigated) or by reaching the **final consequence** (a leaf that feeds the LOPA event-frequency calculation).

## 2.3 Core OAT Equations (KTU Formula Sheet)

The OAT itself does not "derive" equations; rather, it **propagates probabilities** through the tree branches. The two governing equations are:

| Equation | Expression | Meaning / When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Mitigated Frequency** | $F_{mitigated} = F_{initiating} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$ | Frequency of the consequence reaching the final leaf after passing through *n* independent protection layers (IPLs). |
| **Operator Success Probability** | $P_{success} = 1 - PFD_{operator}$ | Probability that the operator performs the correct action. |
| **Layer Failure Probability** | $PFD_{layer} = 1 - P_{success}$ | Probability that the IPL (operator or engineered) fails on demand. |
| **Overall Scenario Frequency** | $F_{final} = F_{initiator} \times PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times \ldots \times PFD_n$ | Final frequency of the top event / consequence. |
| **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD}$ | Inverse of PFD; indicates how much a layer reduces risk. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Students often write $\vert PFD \vert$ for absolute value. In the OAT/LOPA context, the **PFD is a unitless probability between 0 and 1**, never negative — so absolute value is meaningless. Use bare $PFD$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | OAT Application Example |
|---|---|
| **Oil & Gas (Refineries)** | Operator response to a high-high level alarm on a distillation column; tree determines if manual shutdown is initiated before relief valve lifts. |
| **Petrochemical** | Operator action during a runaway exothermic reactor scenario; OAT maps the human-in-the-loop path between alarms and emergency cooling. |
| **Pharmaceutical Batch Reactors** | Operator response to off-spec pH or temperature drift; OAT evaluates if procedural intervention occurs in time. |
| **Power Plants (Boiler/Turbine)** | Operator action following a tube leak alarm; tree assesses manual isolation vs. automatic trip. |
| **LNG & Cryogenic Storage** | Operator response to a vapor cloud detection alarm; OAT evaluates evacuation and emergency shutdown logic. |

The OAT is the **preferred human-reliability sub-tool** when the LOPA team identifies that operator intervention is the only or the final safeguard between an initiating event and a major consequence.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedure & Logical Derivations

Since OAT is a **graphical-logical methodology** (not a calculus-based technique), the "derivations" here are the **step-by-step procedures** the analyst follows to construct and quantify the tree. The KTU examiner rewards a rigorous, sequential, and exhaustive walkthrough.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Construction Procedure

**Step 1 — Define the Scope and Initiating Event**
Select a single process deviation (e.g., "loss of cooling water to Reactor R-101") as the root of the tree. This deviation is normally identified during an upstream HAZOP study or LOPA node.

**Step 2 — Identify the First Demand**
Ask: *"What is the FIRST demand this deviation places on the operator (or on a safeguard)?"* Examples:
- Demand on alarm system (audible/visual)
- Demand on operator to read a gauge
- Demand on operator to manually close a valve
- Demand on the BPCS (Basic Process Control System) to take automatic action

**Step 3 — Draw the First Decision Node (D-node)**
Place a circle or diamond on the tree labeled "D1: Demand on Operator?" Two branches emerge:
- YES → proceed to operator action evaluation
- NO → proceed to engineered safeguard evaluation (BPCS, SIS, relief device)

**Step 4 — Draw the First Action Node (A-node)**
If YES, the tree proceeds to "A1: Did the operator act correctly and in time?" Branches:
- YES → mitigated; go to leaf "Safe State 1"
- NO → continue to next layer (D2)

**Step 5 — Cascade Through Subsequent Layers**
Repeat D-nodes and A-nodes for every additional safeguard layer identified (alarm, BPCS, SIS, relief valve, dike, fire suppression, etc.). Each failure propagates to the next node.

**Step 6 — Terminate at Safe State or Final Consequence**
Every path must end at a leaf. The leaves are of two types only:
- **Safe Leaf (S):** scenario is brought under control.
- **Consequence Leaf (C):** final adverse outcome (e.g., rupture, fire, toxic release).

**Step 7 — Quantify Each Branch**
Assign a PFD to each A-node branch's "failure" outcome. Multiply the initiating event frequency by the product of all failure probabilities along the path. This gives the **mitigated frequency** of the consequence.

**Step 8 — Validate the Tree**
Verify the tree with cross-functional team (process, control, operations, safety). Confirm:
- All branches are mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive.
- All nodes are independent (no shared failure modes).
- The tree conforms to the HAZOP/LOPA cause documented upstream.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — "High Level in Knock-Out Drum"

**Scenario Setup:**
A knock-out drum in an upstream gas processing unit experiences a sudden high-level event. The HAZOP team has identified the following safeguards (in order):

1. **Layer 1 (L1):** BPCS high-level alarm → operator closes upstream ESD valve.
2. **Layer 2 (L2):** Independent Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) — automatic closure of ESD valve.
3. **Layer 3 (L3):** Relief valve on the drum.

**Step 1 — Initiating Event**

$$F_{IE} = 1 \times 10^{-1} \text{ per year}$$

*Meaning:* The high-level event in the knock-out drum is expected to occur **0.1 times per year** (i.e., once every 10 years), based on upstream HAZOP frequency estimation.

**Step 2 — OAT Construction (logical path)**

The tree is constructed with three demand-action pairs in series. At each pair, the operator (or safeguard) is given a chance to act.

**Step 3 — Quantify each layer**

For Layer 1 (operator + BPCS alarm), assume:
- BPCS alarm reliability: $PFD_{alarm} = 1 \times 10^{-1}$
- Operator error probability (HEP, trained): $PFD_{op} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$
- The operator action and alarm failure are **independent**, so the combined PFD of Layer 1 is:

$$PFD_{L1} = 1 - (1 - PFD_{alarm}) \times (1 - PFD_{op})$$

Substituting values:

$$PFD_{L1} = 1 - (1 - 0.1) \times (1 - 0.01)$$

$$PFD_{L1} = 1 - (0.9) \times (0.99)$$

$$PFD_{L1} = 1 - 0.891 = 0.109$$

**Step 4 — Layer 2 (Automatic SIF)**

Assume the SIF is SIL-1 certified with:
$$PFD_{L2} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 5 — Layer 3 (Relief Valve)**

Assume the relief valve is well-maintained with:
$$PFD_{L3} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Mitigated Frequency**

The OAT path (operator → SIF → relief valve) all fail. The overall scenario PFD is:

$$PFD_{overall} = PFD_{L1} \times PFD_{L2} \times PFD_{L3}$$

$$PFD_{overall} = 0.109 \times 0.01 \times 0.01$$

$$PFD_{overall} = 1.09 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 7 — Final Mitigated Frequency**

$$F_{mitigated} = F_{IE} \times PFD_{overall}$$

$$F_{mitigated} = 1 \times 10^{-1} \times 1.09 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$F_{mitigated} = 1.09 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per year}$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation**

A frequency of $\mathbf{1.09 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per year}}$ is **below the typical KTU/LOPA target of $1 \times 10^{-5}$ per year** for tolerable risk in most process industries. The team would conclude the scenario is **acceptably mitigated** — assuming the OAT is complete and no other failure paths exist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Note:** The initiating event frequency is the *root* of the tree. Every branch in the OAT **multiplies** a PFD, so the final consequence frequency is *always smaller* than the initiator. If a student calculates a *larger* number, they have an error in the PFD-product step.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Branch Outcomes Summary

| Branch | Operator/Safeguard Action | Success Outcome | Failure Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **YES / SUCCESS** | Operator reads alarm, closes ESD valve | Drum level stabilizes, no release | — |
| **NO / FAILURE** | Operator misses alarm, distracted, or valve stuck | — | Scenario propagates to SIF |
| **YES / SUCCESS** | SIF closes ESD valve automatically | Drum isolated, no release | — |
| **NO / FAILURE** | SIF fails to close (e.g., solenoid failure) | — | Scenario propagates to relief valve |
| **YES / SUCCESS** | Relief valve opens at set pressure | Drum pressure controlled, no rupture | — |
| **NO / FAILURE** | Relief valve stuck or undersized | — | Final consequence: drum rupture |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the tree end in "rupture" for the worst-case path?** Because the OAT's purpose is to be **conservative** — it asks "what if EVERY layer fails?" — so the final leaf carries the *worst credible consequence* used in the LOPA.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **complete Operator Action Tree (OAT)** for the worked example above. It uses a top-down logical flow with diamond decision nodes, oval action nodes, and rectangular outcome leaves.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootIE["IE: High Level in Knock-Out Drum F = 1E-1 per year"]
    d1["D1: Demand on BPCS Alarm and Operator?"]
    a1op["A1: Operator Acts Correctly and in Time?"]
    safe1["Safe Leaf: Drum Isolated by Operator"]
    d2["D2: Demand on Automatic SIF Closure?"]
    a2sif["A2: SIF Closes ESD Valve Successfully?"]
    safe2["Safe Leaf: Drum Isolated by SIF"]
    d3["D3: Demand on Relief Valve?"]
    a3rv["A3: Relief Valve Opens at Set Pressure?"]
    safe3["Safe Leaf: Drum Pressure Controlled"]
    consLeaf["Consequence Leaf: Drum Rupture and Loss of Containment"]

    rootIE --> d1
    d1 -->|YES| a1op
    d1 -->|NO| d2
    a1op -->|YES| safe1
    a1op -->|NO| d2
    d2 -->|YES| a2sif
    d2 -->|NO| d3
    a2sif -->|YES| safe2
    a2sif -->|NO| d3
    d3 -->|YES| a3rv
    d3 -->|NO| consLeaf
    a3rv -->|YES| safe3
    a3rv -->|NO| consLeaf
```

### 4.1 Node-By-Node Schematic Interpretation

| Node ID | Node Type | Symbolic Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `rootIE` | Initiating Event | Root of the OAT; the top of every tree. |
| `d1`, `d2`, `d3` | Demand Nodes (D) | Diamond decision points — "is there a demand on this layer?" |
| `a1op`, `a2sif`, `a3rv` | Action Nodes (A) | Oval action points — "did the safeguard perform correctly?" |
| `safe1`, `safe2`, `safe3` | Safe Leaves | Rectangular safe outcomes — scenario terminated positively. |
| `consLeaf` | Consequence Leaf | Rectangular adverse outcome — fed to LOPA consequence evaluation. |

### 4.2 Alternative Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

For students who prefer a process-flow visualization rather than a decision tree, the OAT can also be rendered as a **layered functional architecture** showing the cascading interaction of operator, BPCS, SIF, and relief devices:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    process["Process Plant / Knock-Out Drum"]
    ieEvent["Initiating Event: High Level"]
    bpcs["BPCS Alarm and Operator Layer L1"]
    sif["Safety Instrumented Function L2"]
    rv["Relief Valve Layer L3"]
    finalSafe["Safe State or Final Consequence"]

    process --> ieEvent
    ieEvent --> bpcs
    bpcs -->|Operator Success or BPCS Success| finalSafe
    bpcs -->|Both Fail| sif
    sif -->|SIF Success| finalSafe
    sif -->|SIF Fails| rv
    rv -->|Relief Opens| finalSafe
    rv -->|Relief Fails| finalSafe
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme examination, students may be asked to draw *either* a decision tree *or* a layered block diagram. The block diagram is faster to draw and scores full marks if correctly labelled.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Q1a]**

**Q1. Define the Operator Action Tree (OAT) and state its primary use in process safety analysis.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The Operator Action Tree (OAT) is a logic-tree based safety analysis tool that systematically evaluates the demands placed on a human operator (and on associated engineered safeguards) following a process deviation, and estimates the probability that each demand is acted upon correctly. It is primarily used in conjunction with HAZOP and LOPA studies to determine the **conditional probability of failure on demand (PFD)** for operator-driven protection layers, enabling the team to compute the **mitigated event frequency** of the final consequence. Its principal value is its ability to **separate automatic and manual (operator-driven) responses** and to propagate human-reliability data through a defensible, auditable logical chain.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q1b]**

**Q2. List the three types of nodes used in an OAT and briefly explain each.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The OAT uses three types of nodes:
1. **Demand Nodes (D-nodes):** Diamond-shaped decision points that ask whether a specific demand is placed on the operator or on a safeguard at that stage of the scenario.
2. **Action Nodes (A-nodes):** Oval-shaped points that ask whether the operator (or safeguard) performed the required action correctly and within the time window available.
3. **Outcome / Consequence Leaves:** Rectangular terminations of the tree representing either a **safe state** (recovered, mitigated) or the **final adverse consequence** (e.g., rupture, release).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Q2 (Choice-Based)]**

### Option A — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3a.** With the help of a labelled Operator Action Tree, explain how the OAT is constructed for a **high-pressure deviation in a distillation column reboiler**. Identify at least three protection layers in your tree. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**
The OAT for a high-pressure deviation in a distillation column reboiler is constructed as follows:

**Step 1 — Initiating Event (Root):** High pressure in the reboiler due to a sudden blocked outlet or excessive heating medium flow.

**Step 2 — First Layer (L1):** The BPCS issues a high-pressure alarm. The operator is expected to manually reduce the heating medium flow within 5 minutes. This is a D-A node pair (D1: demand on operator; A1: did the operator act correctly?).

**Step 3 — Second Layer (L2):** An independent Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) automatically trips the heating medium pump on a confirmed high-high pressure signal. This is a D-A node pair (D2: demand on SIF; A2: did the SIF act correctly?).

**Step 4 — Third Layer (L3):** A pressure safety valve (PSV) on the reboiler opens at the set pressure to vent vapour to the flare. This is a D-A node pair (D3: demand on PSV; A3: did the PSV open as designed?).

**Step 5 — Terminal Leaves:** Three safe leaves (operator intervention, SIF trip, PSV opening) and one consequence leaf (reboiler rupture).

The constructed OAT (block diagram form):

```
IE: High Pressure in Reboiler
   |
   v
[L1: BPCS Alarm + Operator Action]
   |  (success) -> Safe Leaf 1
   |  (failure) v
[L2: SIF Auto-Trip of Heating Medium]
   |  (success) -> Safe Leaf 2
   |  (failure) v
[L3: Pressure Safety Valve Opens]
   |  (success) -> Safe Leaf 3
   |  (failure) -> Consequence: Reboiler Rupture
```

The three identified protection layers are: **(i) BPCS + operator manual action**, **(ii) SIF automatic trip**, and **(iii) Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)**.

---

**Q3b.** If the initiating event frequency is **$5 \times 10^{-2}$ per year**, the operator+alarm combined PFD is **0.05**, the SIF PFD is **$1 \times 10^{-2}$**, and the PSV PFD is **$2 \times 10^{-2}$**, compute the **mitigated frequency of reboiler rupture**. State whether the result is acceptable if the company LOPA target is $1 \times 10^{-5}$ per year. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the overall PFD equation:**

$$PFD_{overall} = PFD_{L1} \times PFD_{L2} \times PFD_{L3}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$PFD_{overall} = 0.05 \times 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 2 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product:**

$$PFD_{overall} = 0.05 \times 0.01 \times 0.02$$

$$PFD_{overall} = 1 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mitigated frequency:**

$$F_{mitigated} = F_{IE} \times PFD_{overall}$$

$$F_{mitigated} = 5 \times 10^{-2} \times 1 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$F_{mitigated} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per year}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the LOPA target:**

$$5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per year} \ll 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

Since the mitigated frequency is **50 times lower** than the company LOPA target, the scenario is **ACCEPTABLE** as-is, and no additional safeguards are required.

---

### Option B — Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)

**Q4a.** Discuss the **role of the operator as an Independent Protection Layer (IPL)** in an OAT. Under what conditions can an operator action qualify as an IPL per IEC 61511? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The operator action is treated as a **demand-driven human action** within the OAT. For the operator to be credited as an **Independent Protection Layer (IPL)**, the following four criteria per **IEC 61511** must be satisfied:

1. **Independence:** The operator action must be functionally independent of the initiating event and of every other IPL in the scenario. A shared sensor, shared alarm, or shared human cannot be credited as an independent IPL.

2. **Validity (Effectiveness):** The action must be capable of preventing the consequence. There must be a documented, validated basis (e.g., a written operating procedure) showing the action leads to a safe state.

3. **Auditability:** The procedure, the alarm, and the operator's response time must all be documented and auditable. Plant management must review the IPL credit at defined intervals.

4. **Time Sufficiency (Timeliness):** The operator must have **sufficient time** between the alarm being raised and the onset of the hazardous event. The response time required (detection + diagnosis + action) must be **less than** the time available.

In an OAT, the **A-node** captures the success-or-failure of this IPL. The assigned PFD is typically **$1 \times 10^{-2}$** for a well-trained operator on a well-designed alarm with clear procedures. Less ideal conditions push the PFD toward $1 \times 10^{-1}$.

---

**Q4b.** A reactor cooling water pump fails. The HAZOP team has identified two operator-driven safeguards: **(i)** the operator manually starts a standby pump within 10 minutes of the alarm, and **(ii)** the operator manually activates an emergency deluge system if the reactor temperature exceeds 150 °C. Construct an OAT for this scenario, label all nodes, and compute the mitigated reactor rupture frequency given: $F_{IE} = 2 \times 10^{-1}$ per year, $PFD_{op1} = 0.02$, $PFD_{op2} = 0.05$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construct the OAT (logical flow):**

```
IE: Cooling Water Pump Failure
   |
   v
[D1: Demand on Operator to Start Standby Pump?]
   |
   v
[A1: Operator Starts Standby Pump in < 10 min?]
   |   YES -> Safe Leaf 1 (reactor temperature stabilises)
   |   NO  v
[D2: Demand on Operator to Activate Deluge?]
   |
   v
[A2: Operator Activates Deluge Before T > 150 C?]
   |   YES -> Safe Leaf 2 (temperature controlled)
   |   NO  -> Consequence Leaf (reactor rupture, runaway)
```

**Step 2 — Quantify the PFD path (both operators fail):**

$$PFD_{overall} = PFD_{op1} \times PFD_{op2}$$

$$PFD_{overall} = 0.02 \times 0.05 = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute mitigated frequency:**

$$F_{mitigated} = F_{IE} \times PFD_{overall}$$

$$F_{mitigated} = 2 \times 10^{-1} \times 1 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{mitigated} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

A mitigated frequency of $2 \times 10^{-4}$ per year is **higher** than the typical KTU/LOPA company target of $1 \times 10^{-5}$ per year. The result is **NOT acceptable**. The safety team must recommend an **additional engineered IPL** — for example, an automatic SIF that starts the standby pump and a temperature-based automatic deluge initiation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Forgetting the Independence Rule:** Many students assign multiple operator actions as "independent IPLs" even when the same operator, same alarm, or same procedure is used. This earns **partial credit only**. A true IPL must be **independent**.
> 2. **Adding PFDs instead of Multiplying:** A common error is to write $PFD_{total} = 0.02 + 0.05 = 0.07$ instead of $0.02 \times 0.05 = 0.001$. Series IPLs **multiply**, they do not add.
> 3. **Forgetting to Compare with the LOPA Target:** Calculating the mitigated frequency is only **half** the answer. The student must **compare** the result with the company target and **state accept/reject**.
> 4. **Mixing OAT with Fault Tree:** OAT is a **demand-action logic tree**, not a fault tree. Do not use Boolean AND/OR gates with probability arithmetic — use the D-A node sequence.
> 5. **Skipping the Tree Diagram:** Even in numerical problems, a small ASCII or schematic OAT earns **1–2 easy marks**. Always draw it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this in your notebook):**

- **OAT = Operator Action Tree:** A logic-tree methodology used to evaluate operator (and safeguard) responses to process deviations.
- **Origin / Standard:** Developed under the **IEC 61511** framework; supports **HAZOP** and **LOPA**.
- **Three Node Types:** **D-nodes (demand)**, **A-nodes (action)**, **Outcome leaves (safe or consequence)**.
- **Two Leaf Types:** **Safe Leaf** (mitigated scenario) and **Consequence Leaf** (final adverse outcome).
- **Key Equation:** $F_{mitigated} = F_{initiating} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$
- **Operator as IPL:** Requires **independence, validity, auditability, and time sufficiency** per IEC 61511.
- **Default HEP for trained operator:** $1 \times 10^{-2}$ per demand.
- **Independent IPLs Multiply:** Series PFDs are multiplied, not added.
- **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF):** $RRF = 1 / PFD$.
- **Tree Validation:** Must be **mutually exclusive** and **collectively exhaustive** across all branches.
- **Compare Final Frequency** with the company LOPA target (typical: $1 \times 10^{-5}$ per year).
- **Accept / Reject Decision:** Lower than target → **accept**; higher than target → **recommend additional IPL**.
- **Diagram Rule:** Always draw the OAT **before** doing the math. Top-down, with D-nodes above A-nodes above the leaves.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do not use absolute value signs or pipe symbols around PFD in tables — PFD is already a positive probability between 0 and 1.
- **Real-World Anchor:** The OAT is the **human-in-the-loop** counterpart to the **SIL-certified engineered safeguards** (BPCS, SIF, PSV) and bridges qualitative HAZOP with quantitative LOPA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suggestion on Project Topics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT404 - HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT & MITIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suggestion on Project Topics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PBSFT404 – Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA) & Mitigation**, a **Project Topic** in Module 4 refers to a structured, research-oriented or industry-applied capstone/mini-project that demonstrates the learner's ability to independently apply the **Hazard Identification (HAZID)**, **Risk Assessment**, and **Risk Mitigation (Control/Management)** methodologies covered across the four modules of the syllabus to a real-world or simulated process, plant, facility, or system.

A valid project topic must integrate **all four pillars** of HIRA engineering practice:

1. **Hazard Identification** — qualitative methods such as *HAZOP*, *FMEA*, *What-If / Checklist*, *Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)*.
2. **Consequence Analysis** — quantitative modelling of fires, explosions, toxic dispersions.
3. **Risk Estimation** — probabilistic tools such as *Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)* and *Event Tree Analysis (ETA)*.
4. **Risk Mitigation** — engineering, administrative, and PPE-based controls following the *Hierarchy of Controls (HoC)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Examination-Ready Statement):**
> *"A project in HIRA is an applied investigation of a defined industrial process, chemical operation, storage installation, or occupational work environment in which hazards are systematically identified, the associated risks are quantified (qualitatively or quantitatively), and a defensible mitigation framework is proposed in accordance with national/international codes (OISD, IS, NFPA, API, IEC 61511)."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a HIRA project the way a **medical doctor performs a full diagnosis on a patient**:

| Medical Process | Equivalent HIRA Project Step |
|---|---|
| Patient symptoms and history | **Hazard Identification** (HAZOP / FMEA) |
| Blood tests and scans | **Consequence & Frequency Analysis** |
| Diagnosis (Disease + Probability) | **Risk Estimation (Likelihood × Severity)** |
| Treatment plan (medicine, surgery, lifestyle) | **Risk Mitigation (Hierarchy of Controls)** |
| Follow-up review | **ALARP Demonstration & Continuous Improvement** |

Just as a doctor cannot prescribe without first diagnosing, an engineer cannot propose mitigation without first **identifying** and **quantifying** the hazard. A strong project topic therefore spans the **complete diagnostic-to-treatment chain**, not just one isolated method.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reading Cue for Students:**
> A project topic that *only* applies HAZOP, or *only* applies Fault Tree Analysis, is considered **incomplete** under KTU valuation norms. Choose a topic that lets you stitch **at least two complementary techniques** (e.g., HAZOP + ETA, or FMEA + Bow-Tie).

## 1.3 Common Misconception to Avoid

> [!WARNING]
> A HIRA project is **NOT** a literature review. It must contain:
> - A **Node / System Boundary Definition**,
> - **Realistic input parameters** (P, T, inventory, failure rates),
> - At least **one quantitative output** (risk score, individual risk, societal risk F–N curve, SIL level), and
> - A **traceable mitigation matrix** linked back to identified hazards.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Excellent HIRA Project Topic

A KTU-quality project topic must satisfy the **AIRC Framework** (a coined mnemonic for valuation):

- **A — Applicability**: Tied to a real or simulated industry (refinery, LPG bottling, pharma, paint manufacturing, ammonia storage, etc.).
- **I — Identification Depth**: Uses at least **two** hazard identification tools.
- **R — Risk Quantification**: Provides **numerical** risk values (qualitative scoring matrices are acceptable only if supplemented by a semi-quantitative layer).
- **C — Control Justification**: Every recommended control is justified against the *Hierarchy of Controls* and **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable).

## 2.2 Categories of Suggested Project Topics (Aligned with KTU 2024 Module 4 Outcomes)

Below is the **categorized suggestion matrix** — the heart of this module:

### Category A — Process Industry (Petrochemical / Refinery)

| S.No. | Suggested Topic | Primary Tools | Secondary Tools |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | HAZOP & QRA of an **Atmospheric Crude Oil Storage Tank Farm** | HAZOP, QRA | ETA, Bow-Tie |
| 2 | **Boiler & Fired Heater** Failure Analysis using FMEA | FMEA | FTA, RCM |
| 3 | Consequence Modelling of **Pool Fire & Vapour Cloud Explosion** in a Refinery Distillation Column | ALOHA / PHAST simulation | HAZOP |
| 4 | **Ammonia Refrigeration Plant** Risk Assessment using HAZOP + LOPA | HAZOP, LOPA | FTA |
| 5 | Risk Assessment of **LPG Bullet Installation** with BLEVE modelling | QRA, ALOHA | HAZOP |

### Category B — Chemical & Pharmaceutical Industry

| S.No. | Suggested Topic | Primary Tools | Secondary Tools |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 6 | **FMEA on a Batch Reactor** for Exothermic Runaway | FMEA | HAZOP |
| 7 | HAZOP Study of a **Solvent Recovery Distillation Unit** | HAZOP | Checklist |
| 8 | **HAZOP + QRA of a Chlorine Tonner Storage Facility** | HAZOP, QRA | ALARP |
| 9 | Risk Assessment of a **Hydrogenation Reactor** with FTA | FTA | ETA |
| 10 | **Dust Explosion Risk** in a Pharmaceutical Granulation Unit | What-If, QRA | HAZOP |

### Category C — Storage, Transport & Logistics

| S.No. | Suggested Topic | Primary Tools | Secondary Tools |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 11 | HAZOP of a **Bullet LPG Truck Loading Gantry** | HAZOP | JSA |
| 12 | **Bow-Tie Analysis of a Spherical Ammonia Storage Vessel** | Bow-Tie | HAZOP |
| 13 | **Pipeline Route Risk Assessment** using FTA and Consequence Analysis | FTA, ALOHA | GIS overlay |
| 14 | Risk Assessment of **CNG Cascaded Storage Cascade** | FMEA, FTA | HAZOP |
| 15 | **Offshore Loading Arm** Failure Analysis using FMEA + ETA | FMEA, ETA | Bow-Tie |

### Category D — Occupational / Industrial Hygiene

| S.No. | Suggested Topic | Primary Tools | Secondary Tools |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 16 | **Confined Space Entry Risk Assessment** in a Wastewater Treatment Plant | What-If, Checklist | JSA |
| 17 | **Heat Stress & Noise Exposure Risk** in a Steel Rolling Mill | IH tools, JSA | FMEA |
| 18 | **Working at Height** Risk Assessment using Bow-Tie | Bow-Tie | HAZOP |
| 19 | **Lock-Out / Tag-Out (LOTO)** Failure Mode Analysis | FMEA | FTA |
| 20 | **Occupational Hazard Mapping** of a Paint Manufacturing Unit using PHA | PHA | Checklist |

### Category E — Emerging / Modern Themes (High Marks Differentiator)

| S.No. | Suggested Topic | Primary Tools | Secondary Tools |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 21 | **Cyber-Physical Risk Assessment** of an Industrial Control System (ICS) using FTA | FTA | Bow-Tie |
| 22 | **Machine Learning-based Fault Tree** Quantification for a Pump System | ML + FTA | HAZOP |
| 23 | **Process Safety Management (PSM) Gap Analysis** of an SME Chemical Plant | Audit / Checklist | PHA |
| 24 | **Human Reliability Analysis (HRA)** in a Control Room using HEART method | HRA | HAZOP |
| 25 | **Hydrogen Storage & Dispensing Station** Quantitative Risk Assessment | HAZOP, QRA | Bow-Tie |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** Topics from **Category E** (emerging themes) tend to receive **higher valuation marks** because they demonstrate awareness of contemporary safety challenges beyond textbook HAZOP. Choose one only if you can deliver technical depth.

## 2.3 The "Topic Selection Filter" — How to Pick the Right Topic

Use this **5-Filter Decision Tree** (each filter must yield "Yes"):

1. **Industry Familiarity Filter** — Can you describe the process flow in 3–5 sentences from memory?
2. **Data Availability Filter** — Do you have realistic parameters (P, T, inventory, λ-failure rates)?
3. **Tool Justification Filter** — Have you chosen at least **one qualitative** + **one quantitative** tool?
4. **Time & Software Filter** — Can the project be completed in the allotted weeks using available tools (ALOHA free version, Excel, hand calculations)?
5. **Mitigation Depth Filter** — Can you propose **≥ 5 engineering controls** in the Hierarchy of Controls framework?

If any filter yields "No", pivot to another topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Methodology Framework & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Standard KTU Project Methodology (8-Step Framework)

Every accepted HIRA project follows this **eight-step skeleton**, which mirrors the syllabus progression:

### Step 1 — Define the System and Study Boundary

- Draw a **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** or **Plot Plan**.
- Identify **nodes** (HAZOP), **sub-systems** (FMEA), or **top event** (FTA).
- State inclusions and exclusions explicitly.

### Step 2 — Gather Process Information

Compile:
- Operating conditions (P, T, flow rates)
- Inventory of hazardous materials (kg, tonnes)
- MSDS / SDS information
- Equipment datasheets
- Past incident records (if accessible)

### Step 3 — Conduct Hazard Identification (HAZID)

Choose **at least two** tools. Standard pairing for KTU:

$$
\text{Method Pair} = \{\text{HAZOP}, \text{FMEA}\} \quad \text{or} \quad \{\text{PHA}, \text{What-If}\}
$$

The output is a **Hazard Register** with cause–consequence pairs.

### Step 4 — Perform Consequence Analysis

Estimate physical effects of credible scenarios:

$$
R_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{Q_{\text{rad}}}{4 \pi r^{2}}
$$

where $Q_{\text{rad}}$ is the radiative heat release rate in **kW** and $r$ is the distance in **m**. Compare with threshold values:

$$
R_{\text{thermal, threshold}} = 6.31 \text{ kW/m}^{2} \quad (\text{2nd degree burn in 60 s, TNO Green Book})
$$

### Step 5 — Estimate Frequencies / Likelihoods

Use generic failure rate databases (e.g., OREDA, CCPS PERD) and feed into:

$$
P_{\text{Top Event}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - P_{i}\right) \quad \text{for parallel redundant paths}
$$

$$
P_{\text{Top Event}} = \prod_{j=1}^{m} P_{j} \quad \text{for series cut-set logic in FTA}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Risk

For each hazard $i$:

$$
\text{Risk}_{i} = P_{i} \times C_{i}
$$

where $P_{i}$ is the **likelihood** and $C_{i}$ is the **consequence severity** (injury, loss, environmental, financial).

Aggregate into an **F–N Curve** (societal risk) if the dataset is sufficient:

$$
F(N) = \sum_{i} P(f_{i} > N)
$$

### Step 7 — Apply Hierarchy of Controls (HoC)

In strict order of preference:

1. **Elimination** — remove the hazard entirely.
2. **Substitution** — replace with a less hazardous material/process.
3. **Engineering Controls** — relief valves, dike walls, ESD systems, inerting.
4. **Administrative Controls** — SOPs, training, permits-to-work.
5. **PPE** — last resort.

### Step 8 — Demonstrate ALARP and Recommend Residual Risk Actions

Show that **further risk reduction is grossly disproportionate to the cost** (i.e., the cost of preventing one fatality equivalent is orders of magnitude above the industry willingness-to-pay benchmark).

## 3.2 Worked Example Outline — "HAZOP + QRA of an Atmospheric Crude Oil Storage Tank"

Below is a *condensed* roadmap showing what each project step yields:

| Step | Output Deliverable | KTU-Mapped Outcome |
|:---:|---|:---:|
| 1 | PFD + 6 node definitions | CO4 |
| 2 | Process data table (P, T, inventory) | CO4 |
| 3 | HAZOP worksheet with deviations (No Flow, Less Flow, More T, etc.) | CO5 |
| 4 | ALOHA / hand calc pool-fire radiation contours | CO5 |
| 5 | FTA for top event "Tank Overfill & Pool Fire" | CO6 |
| 6 | Individual Risk (IR) per year; Societal Risk F–N curve | CO6 |
| 7 | Mitigation Matrix mapped to HoC | CO6 |
| 8 | ALARP worksheet + final recommendation | CO6 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Markov / LOPA:** For projects demanding higher marks, supplement Step 5 with **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)**:
> $$\text{LOPA Risk} = \text{Initiating Event Frequency} \times \prod_{i} \text{PFD}_{i}$$
> where $\text{PFD}_{i}$ is the *Probability of Failure on Demand* of independent protection layers (IPL).

## 3.3 Anti-Hallucination Guardrails (KTU Valuation Compliance)

> [!WARNING]
> Students often fabricate industrial failure rates. To stay defensible:
> - Use only **publicly documented** λ-values (e.g., OREDA 2015 average values).
> - Cite the database explicitly: *"[Source: OREDA Handbook, 2015 Edition]"*.
> - Never invent a specific company's incident history unless the data is in the public domain.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]`
**Q: What are the key criteria a student must consider while selecting a project topic in Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A HIRA project topic must be selected using the following key criteria:

1. **Applicability to a real or simulated process/industry** — the topic must address an authentic engineering system such as a refinery unit, storage tank, or chemical reactor.
2. **Availability of process data and failure rate information** — without realistic operating parameters (pressure, temperature, inventory) and credible λ-failure rates, the project cannot be quantitatively defended.
3. **Justified tool selection** — the topic must allow application of at least one **qualitative** (HAZOP / FMEA / What-If) and at least one **quantitative** (FTA / ETA / QRA) technique, in line with KTU 2024 Scheme CO5 and CO6.
4. **Mitigation traceability** — every identified risk must be linked to controls derived from the **Hierarchy of Controls (HoC)** framework, ensuring ALARP is demonstrable.

*(Examiner expects 3 crisp bullet points to award full 3 marks.)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]`
**Q: List any five categories of industries or systems suitable for a HIRA project topic with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Petrochemical / Refinery** — HAZOP of a crude distillation column.
2. **Chemical / Pharmaceutical** — FMEA of a batch reactor for exothermic runaway.
3. **Storage and Transport** — Bow-Tie analysis of an LPG bullet installation.
4. **Occupational / Industrial Hygiene** — Risk assessment of confined space entry in a wastewater treatment plant.
5. **Emerging / Modern Themes** — Quantitative Risk Assessment of a hydrogen storage and dispensing station.

*(Any 5 valid pairs — 1 mark for categories and 2 marks for examples, or vice versa as per examiner discretion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: As a final-year B.Tech Safety & Fire Technology student, you are required to choose and justify a project topic in HIRA.**
**(a)** Propose a project topic of your choice, justify it using the **AIRC framework** (Applicability, Identification depth, Risk quantification, Control justification), and sketch a **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** with at least three clearly defined HAZOP nodes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Develop a **Step-by-Step Methodology** for the chosen topic, indicating which hazard identification and risk quantification tools you will employ at each stage, and explain how the **Hierarchy of Controls** will be applied to the top three identified scenarios. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Proposed Topic:** *"HAZOP and Quantitative Risk Assessment of an Atmospheric Crude Oil Storage Tank Farm."*

**Justification using the AIRC Framework:**

- **A — Applicability:** Crude oil tank farms are central to every refinery; they are well-documented in public OISD/NFPA literature and have rich failure-rate data (OREDA, 2015), making them an authentic and feasible choice.
- **I — Identification Depth:** The topic enables the use of **HAZOP** (qualitative) on each storage tank node, supplemented by **What-If analysis** for dike-area events — satisfying the "≥ 2 tools" rule.
- **R — Risk Quantification:** The tank farm allows **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** to estimate the probability of catastrophic loss-of-containment, and **ALOHA / hand calculations** for pool-fire radiation contours, yielding numerical IR (Individual Risk) values.
- **C — Control Justification:** Identified scenarios (overfill, dike failure, pump seal leak) can be mapped to **engineering controls** (Rohs level instruments, ESD valves, foam systems), providing a defensible mitigation matrix.

**PFD with 3 HAZOP Nodes:**

```
Node 1: Inlet Manifold + First Stage Tank (T-101)
   -- Slug Inlet from Pipeline -->
   [Header] --> [Flow Meter FT-101] --> [T-101 Storage Tank]
                                                   |
                                                   v
                                          [Pump P-101A / P-101B]
                                                   |
                                                   v
Node 2: Intermediate Transfer Line + Tank T-102
   [P-101A] --> [Block Valve XV-101] --> [T-102 Storage Tank]
                                                  |
                                                  v
Node 3: Pumpout Manifold + Loading Gantry
   [T-102] --> [Export Pump P-102] --> [Loading Gantry]
```

*(Full marks require sketch + 3 nodes + justification — 7 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Step-by-Step Methodology:**

| Stage | Activity | Tool Used | Outcome |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Define system boundary and PFD | PFD | Scope freeze |
| 2 | Data acquisition (P, T, inventory, MSDS) | Document review | Data table |
| 3 | Hazard identification | HAZOP + What-If | Hazard Register |
| 4 | Consequence analysis (pool fire, vapour cloud) | ALOHA / hand calc | Radiation contours |
| 5 | Frequency estimation | FTA + OREDA | Top event probability |
| 6 | Risk computation | Risk Matrix + IR calculation | Ranked risks |
| 7 | Mitigation via HoC | HoC framework | Mitigation matrix |
| 8 | ALARP justification | Cost-benefit / risk graph | Final recommendation |

**Application of Hierarchy of Controls to Top 3 Scenarios:**

1. **Scenario 1 — Tank Overfill → Pool Fire**
   - *Elimination:* Not feasible (storage cannot be eliminated).
   - *Substitution:* Use floating-roof tank instead of fixed-roof cone-roof tank.
   - *Engineering Control:* Independent High-Level (IHL) switch + Rohs alarm + ESD closure of inlet valve + foam pourer system.
   - *Administrative:* Operator SOP on tank gauging + permit-to-work for overfill.
   - *PPE:* Flame-retardant clothing for response team.

2. **Scenario 2 — Pump Seal Leak → Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)**
   - *Engineering:* Mag-drive / canned pump (seal-less) or dual mechanical seal with barrier fluid; vapour recovery system.
   - *Administrative:* Routine seal inspection checklist; gas detection calibration log.
   - *PPE:* Full chemical PPE for emergency response.

3. **Scenario 3 — Dike Wall Failure during Pool Fire**
   - *Engineering:* Reinforced concrete dike sized to **110%** of largest tank; secondary impounding basin.
   - *Administrative:* Annual dike integrity inspection log.
   - *PPE:* Not applicable at this layer.

*(Marks distribution: methodology table = 4 marks; HoC application = 3 marks. Total 7 marks.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: A small-scale pharmaceutical industry in Kerala wants a HIRA project conducted on its solvent distillation unit.**
**(a)** Suggest a suitable project title and outline the **Hazard Identification methodology** you would adopt, including deviations to be studied under HAZOP and the rationale for choosing each parameter. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain how you would quantify the risks of the top two identified scenarios using **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** and propose mitigation in line with the **Hierarchy of Controls**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Suggested Project Title:** *"HAZOP and Fault Tree Analysis of a Solvent Recovery Distillation Unit in a Pharmaceutical Plant."*

**Hazard Identification Methodology:**

- **Tool Selection Rationale:** HAZOP is preferred because the unit is a continuous process (feed pump → reboiler → column → condenser → reflux), which maps naturally to nodes. HAZOP uses the keyword "guideword + parameter" approach, ideal for process deviations. FMEA is supplementary for the reboiler and condenser sub-systems.
- **HAZOP Nodes (3 nodes suggested):**

  1. **Node 1 — Feed Pre-heater and Feed Line**
  2. **Node 2 — Reboiler + Distillation Column**
  3. **Node 3 — Condenser + Reflux Drum**

- **Guideword × Parameter Matrix used:**

  $$\text{Deviation} = \text{Guideword} \oplus \text{Parameter}$$

  For example, **"No Flow"** at Node 2 (reboiler) could indicate pump failure or block valve closure → potential **column dry-out → overheating → fire**.

- **Parameters Studied:** Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition.
- **Guidewords Studied:** No/None, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than.

*(Full marks require methodology clarity + guideword/parameter logic — 7 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Top Two Identified Scenarios:**

1. **Scenario A — Solvent Leak from Reboiler Tube Rupture → Fire/Explosion**
2. **Scenario B — Condenser Cooling Water Failure → Loss of Reflux → Column Overpressure → Rupture**

**FTA for Scenario A (Reboiler Tube Rupture):**

Top Event: *Fire / Explosion following solvent release*

```
            [Top Event: Fire & Explosion]
                       |
         +-------------+-------------+
         |                           |
[Ignition Source Present]    [Solvent Release Event]
                                     |
                          +----------+----------+
                          |                     |
                 [Tube Rupture]      [Flange Gasket Failure]
                          |                     |
                  [Overpressure]         [Vibration Fatigue]
                          |                     |
                  [Blocked Vent]         [Improper Torque]
```

**Boolean Expression for Top Event:**

$$
T = I \cdot \left( T_{r} + G_{f} \right)
$$

where $I$ = presence of ignition source, $T_{r}$ = tube rupture, $G_{f}$ = gasket failure. Probability:

$$
P(T) = P(I) \times \left[ 1 - \left( 1 - P(T_{r}) \right) \left( 1 - P(G_{f}) \right) \right]
$$

**Mitigation via HoC for Scenario A:**
- **Engineering:** Rupture disc + pressure relief valve on reboiler shell; flame arrester on vent; nitrogen blanketing on solvent storage; earthing & bonding.
- **Administrative:** Routine hydrotest of reboiler tubes every 2 years; hot-work permit; ignition-source control program.
- **PPE:** Chemical splash goggles and chemical-resistant apron for operating personnel.

**FTA for Scenario B (Cooling Water Failure → Overpressure):**

Top Event: *Column rupture*

$$
T_{2} = W_{f} \cdot V_{c}
$$

where $W_{f}$ = cooling water failure event and $V_{c}$ = condenser coolant valve closed inadvertently.

**Mitigation via HoC for Scenario B:**
- **Engineering:** Independent cooling water flow switch + automatic column pressure control valve + rupture disc on column overhead.
- **Administrative:** Operator SOP on condenser monitoring; DCS alarm at < 30% CW flow.
- **PPE:** Not relevant at the primary mitigation layer.

*(Full marks: FTA logic + Boolean expression + 5 HoC entries per scenario = 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Avoid vague titles** — *“A study on safety”* gets **zero** marks. Always use: **Tool + Unit + Industry** (e.g., *"HAZOP + FTA of a Solvent Distillation Unit in a Pharma Plant"*).
> 2. **Do NOT skip the PFD** — a project without a PFD/node diagram loses **3 marks** upfront.
> 3. **Do NOT omit numerical output** — at least one quantitative result (probability, risk score, IR, SIL target) is mandatory for full marks.
> 4. **Mitigation must be specific** — *“use PPE”* alone is too vague. Write: *“Use chemical-resistant gloves conforming to IS 4154 / EN 374.”*
> 5. **ALARP is non-negotiable** — without an ALARP justification, your control recommendations look arbitrary.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **AIRC Framework** = Applicability, Identification depth, Risk quantification, Control justification.
- [ ] Always combine **≥ 1 qualitative + ≥ 1 quantitative** tool.
- [ ] A project **must** have a PFD / node map — it is the visual anchor.
- [ ] The **Hierarchy of Controls** order is fixed: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE**.
- [ ] Quantitative risk equation: $\text{Risk} = P \times C$.
- [ ] FTA Boolean logic: series = AND ($\cdot$), parallel = OR ($+$).
- [ ] Generic failure rates must come from **public, citable** databases (OREDA, CCPS PERD).
- [ ] ALARP demonstration is **mandatory** for QRA projects.
- [ ] Mitigation must be **specific** (refer to codes / standards) — never generic.
- [ ] Avoid topics that are *only literature review* — KTU expects applied engineering.
- [ ] **Bow-Tie** is a strong visual differentiator for the report.
- [ ] Always state **assumptions and limitations** explicitly in the methodology chapter.
- [ ] Use **ALOHA (free version)** or hand calculations for consequence modelling when commercial software (PHAST, FRED) is unavailable.
- [ ] **Category E** (emerging themes) yields higher marks *only if* the technical depth is maintained.
- [ ] Project title format: **"Tool-1 + Tool-2 of [Process / Equipment] in [Industry]"**.
- [ ] Final defence viva typically tests: PFD logic, FTA Boolean expressions, HoC ordering, and ALARP reasoning.
